topics.title: Concept of Property Vis-a-Vis Intellectual Property
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Property Vis-a-Vis Intellectual Property

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Property"? — The Foundational Legal Notion

In jurisprudence and property law, the term **"property"** does **not** mean a physical object itself. It refers to a **legally enforceable bundle of rights** that a person holds *over* an object (whether tangible or intangible) of value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (Memorise this verbatim):**
> *"Property is a legal right or interest that entitles its owner to possess, use, enjoy, and dispose of a thing, and to exclude all others from interfering with that thing, subject to the limits fixed by law."*
> — Restated from the Indian Easements Act, 1882 (Section 4) and Blackstone's Commentaries on Laws of England.

In simpler terms, when we say "Riya owns this pen," we technically mean that Riya has a *legally protected right* to:
1. **Possess** it physically,
2. **Use** it for permitted purposes,
3. **Enjoy** any benefits from it (renting, lending),
4. **Dispose** of it (sell, gift, destroy, will it to heirs), and
5. **Exclude** everyone else from it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Answers:**
> Property is therefore **NOT the thing itself** — it is the *relationship* between a person and a thing, recognised and protected by the State. The pen is merely the *object* of the property right.

---

## 1.2 What is "Intellectual Property" (IP)?

**Intellectual Property (IP)** is a specialised sub-category of property in which the *object* of the right is the **product of human intellect, creativity, or mental labour** — rather than a physical, material thing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (WIPO-Standard Formulation):**
> *"Intellectual Property refers to the legal rights that result from intellectual activity in the industrial, scientific, literary, and artistic fields, covering inventions, designs, artistic works, trademarks, and confidential information."*
> — Adapted from Article 2(viii) of the **WIPO Convention, 1967**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Why IP is the 'Invisible Land' of the Mind"

> [!NOTE]
> **🍎 Intuition Builder (Use this analogy in your KTU answer to score examiner goodwill):**
>
> Imagine a farmer, **Mr. Kuttan**, who owns a **mango orchard** in Kerala. He has a fence, a title deed, and a legally defined boundary. If a neighbour plucks his mangoes, the police and the court can physically remove the intruder from the land. The land is **scarce, visible, and rivalrous** — only one person can stand on a given square metre at a time.
>
> Now imagine a **software engineer, Ms. Lakshmi**, who designs a brilliant new algorithm in Bengaluru. The algorithm lives inside her laptop. If someone copies it onto a USB drive, the original is **not diminished**. The "object" cannot be fenced. The idea can be shared by millions simultaneously. The challenge of law here is: **How do we give Ms. Lakshmi "ownership" over something that is invisible, non-rivalrous, and infinitely reproducible?**
>
> This is precisely what **Intellectual Property Law** solves — it extends the *concept* of property (a bundle of exclusionary rights granted and protected by the State) into the **realm of the mind**.

---

## 1.4 The Two Faces of Property — A Working Taxonomy

| **Category** | **Object of Right** | **Examples** | **Governing Law in India** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tangible (Corporeal) Property** | Physical, material things | Land, house, car, smartphone | Transfer of Property Act, 1882 |
| **Intangible (Incorporeal) Property** | Non-physical rights/interests | Patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade secrets | Patents Act 1970, Copyright Act 1957, Trade Marks Act 1999 |

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Intellectual Property sits in the **Intangible** branch. It is a **hybrid** — its *object* is intangible (an idea, a brand, a song), but its *enforcement* often requires the State to control the *tangible carriers* of that idea (CD-ROMs, factories, packaging).

---

## 1.5 The "Vis-a-Vis" — Meaning for the KTU Examiner

The phrase *"Concept of Property **vis-à-vis** Intellectual Property"* simply means: **"A comparative study of Traditional Property concepts and how Intellectual Property relates to, differs from, and extends those concepts."**

The examiner expects the student to demonstrate:
- A clear **understanding of the classical concept of property**,
- Recognition of **how IP fits into, yet departs from, the classical model**, and
- The ability to **justify why IP deserves legal protection at all**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Classical Concept of Property — A "Bundle of Rights"

Following the **bundle-of-rights theory** (popularised by American legal scholar Wesley Hohfeld, 1913), ownership of property is not one monolithic right but a *cluster* of separable rights. For classical property, this bundle typically includes:

1. **Right to Possess** — physical control over the object.
2. **Right to Use** (usus) — to deploy the object for lawful purposes.
3. **Right to Enjoy / Benefit** (fructus) — to collect income, rent, royalties.
4. **Right to Dispose / Alienate** (abusus) — to sell, mortgage, gift, or destroy.
5. **Right to Exclude** — the *most fundamental* right, preventing others from interfering.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Right to Exclude" is the spine of property law.** The famous US case *International News Service v. Associated Press (1918)*, 248 U.S. 215, established that the essence of property is the **right to exclude**. The same logic extends to IP — e.g., a patent holder has the right to exclude others from making, using, or selling the invention without licence.

---

## 2.2 How IP "Borrowed" the Property Framework — And Where It Diverges

IP law essentially **translates** the bundle-of-rights concept into a creative, industrial, or commercial setting. But the *object* of those rights is fundamentally different from a mango orchard.

### 2.2.1 Economic Characteristics of the IP Object

| **Property Trait** | **Tangible Property (Orchard)** | **Intellectual Property (Algorithm)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Rivalry** | Rival — one person's use *reduces* what is left for others | Non-rival — one person's use does *not* reduce availability for others |
| **Excludability** | Easily excludable (fence, lock, title) | Inherently hard to exclude (copies are cheap, fast, and identical) |
| **Scarcity** | Naturally scarce (finite land) | Artificially scarce (the idea is abundant, but the law *creates* scarcity via legal monopoly) |
| **Durability** | Subject to physical wear and tear | Indestructible — copying does not "use up" the original |
| **Transferability** | Transferred by physical delivery or registration | Transferred by licence or assignment, often territorially limited |
| **Cost of Replication** | High (must buy/construct another) | Near-zero (a song can be copied 10 million times at the cost of electricity) |

---

## 2.3 Three Classical Justifications for Granting IP Protection (KTU Favourite!)

The KTU examiner often asks: *"Why is IP treated as property at all?"* The following three theories form the standard answer:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theory 1 — John Locke's Labour Theory (Natural Rights)**
> *"Every man has a property in his own person. The labour of his body and the work of his hands are properly his."* — *Second Treatise on Government, 1689.*
> Since a creator *labours* mentally to produce an invention or a book, society ought to grant them a property right in the *fruits* of that labour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theory 2 — Utilitarian / Economic Incentive Theory**
> If creators were not rewarded, they would not invest time, money, and risk in producing innovations. A *temporary legal monopoly* (e.g., 20-year patent) incentivises innovation, ultimately benefiting the public when the IP enters the public domain. — Foundational principle of the **Paris Convention (1883)** and the **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theory 3 — Personality Theory (Hegel & Kant)**
> Property is an extension of the *personality* of the individual. A painting, an invention, or a brand carries the imprint of its creator's mind and identity; to deny them property in it is to deny the protection of their personhood. — Articulated by G.W.F. Hegel, *Philosophy of Right (1820)*.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| **Basis of Comparison** | **Traditional (Physical) Property** | **Intellectual Property (IP)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Object** | Tangible, material thing | Intangible creation of the mind |
| **Basis of Right** | Possession, use, occupation, labour on land | Creation, invention, authorship, use in commerce |
| **Scarcity** | Natural | Created artificially by law |
| **Rival or Non-rival** | Rival | Non-rival (inherently) |
| **Excludability** | Easy (fence, lock) | Difficult — needs legal machinery |
| **Duration** | Potentially infinite (heirs) | Limited by statute (e.g., patents 20 yrs, copyright life+60 yrs) |
| **Mode of Transfer** | Sale deed, gift, inheritance | Assignment, licence (written & registered) |
| **Source of Right** | State recognition of de facto possession / contract | Statute (Patents Act, Copyright Act, TM Act) |
| **Universality** | Generally recognised by all legal systems | Territorial — must be secured in each country |
| **Infringement** | Trespass, theft (physical interference) | Infringement (copying, imitating, importing) |
| **Remedy** | Recovery of possession, damages, injunction | Injunction, damages or account of profits, delivery up, criminal prosecution |
| **Public Domain** | Generally not relevant; ownership persists | Time-bound — eventually falls into public domain |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

For a B.Tech student, the *practical* relevance of distinguishing property from IP is immediate:

- **In startups**: A founder's *physical laptop* is personal property; the *source code written on it* is IP that must be assigned to the company.
- **In research**: A research paper is the **author's** IP (copyright); a patented process discovered in a funded lab is typically **assigned** to the funding institution.
- **In employment**: The *salary* you receive is consideration; the *inventions you make on the job* may belong to the employer under Section 39 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Tip:** Always read the **IP assignment clause** in your employment agreement. Under Indian law, employee inventions during the course of employment *typically* vest in the employer (subject to "adequate remuneration" exceptions).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — From Property to IP

This section builds the *logical bridge* between the two concepts in a step-by-step analytical framework, as the KTU examiner expects.

---

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of Why IP is a Form of Property

**Step 1 — Identify the Universal Feature of Property.**
$$\text{Property} = \text{Legal Right (Possess + Use + Enjoy + Dispose + Exclude)}$$

> **Logic:** Across all legal systems, what we call "property" is always a State-protected *right*, not a *thing*. The "thing" is merely the *object* to which the right attaches.

**Step 2 — Identify the Universal Feature of the "Object".**
$$\text{Object} \in \{\text{Physical Thing},\; \text{Intellectual Creation}\}$$

> **Logic:** Legal systems historically granted property rights primarily over physical things (land, cattle, gold). Over time, society recognised that creations of the mind (songs, inventions, brand names) also have economic and moral value.

**Step 3 — Apply the Bundle-of-Rights Test to a Creative Work.**
A novelist writes a novel. Does she have:
- **Right to possess** the manuscript? ✅ Yes.
- **Right to use** (publish, reproduce)? ✅ Yes.
- **Right to enjoy** (collect royalties)? ✅ Yes.
- **Right to dispose** (sell copyright, license to a publisher)? ✅ Yes.
- **Right to exclude** (prevent plagiarism)? ✅ Yes — enforceable under the **Copyright Act, 1957**.

$$\therefore \text{By the bundle-of-rights test, the novel is the object of a property right.}$$

**Step 4 — Confirm the Economic Function.**
The novel has *value* (a market exists for books). Without legal recognition, the author could not capture that value, because copying is cheap and undetectable. Therefore the State *intervenes* to create **artificial scarcity** through a **time-limited monopoly**.

> **Logic:** This artificial scarcity is the *very mechanism* by which IP becomes a *functional* equivalent of physical property.

**Step 5 — Limit the Right for Public Welfare.**
$$\text{IP Right} = f(\text{Creator Reward},\; \text{Public Access}) = \text{Optimal Balance}$$

> **Logic:** A pure perpetual property right over an idea would create a *perpetual monopoly*, harming innovation. Hence IP rights are **statutorily time-limited**:
> - **Patent:** 20 years from filing date.
> - **Copyright:** Life of the author + 60 years.
> - **Trademark:** 10 years, renewable indefinitely (as long as used).
> - **Design:** 10 years, extendable by 5 years.
>
> After expiry, the work enters the **public domain** — society's collective property.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Step-by-Step Differentiation)

| **Stage of Analysis** | **Traditional Property** | **Intellectual Property** | **Examiner's Cue** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Origin of Right** | Possession, occupation, labour, contract, succession | Statute (Patent Act, Copyright Act, Trade Marks Act) | Cite the specific statute |
| **2. Object Identified** | Res (thing) in physical space | Res incorporalis (idea, expression, sign) in conceptual space | Use Latin terms — impresses examiner |
| **3. Mode of Acquisition** | Purchase, gift, inheritance, adverse possession | Registration (patent, TM) or creation (copyright auto) | Highlight the auto-acquisition of copyright |
| **4. Scope of Right** | Generally absolute (subject to public law limits) | Limited, time-bound, subject to public-interest exceptions | Cite Section 52 of Copyright Act for fair use |
| **5. Conflict Resolution** | Possessory remedies, trespass law | Infringement suits, anti-piracy statutes | Distinguish civil from criminal remedies |
| **6. End of Right** | Abandonment or destruction of object | Expiry of statutory term — automatic entry into public domain | Use "public domain" prominently |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical / Conceptual Example (KTU-Style)

> **Question:** *A startup, "KeralaBotics Pvt. Ltd.," develops an AI algorithm. The CEO claims the algorithm is "the company's property." Identify the precise legal character of this claim and the statutory basis.*

**Solution (Model Answer Framework):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Object:**
The algorithm is an *intangible* — it qualifies as a **computer program / technical know-how**, and potentially as a **patentable invention** if it meets the criteria of novelty, inventive step, and industrial application under **Section 2(1)(j) of the Patents Act, 1970**.

**Step 2 — Identify the Type of IP Rights Available:**
- **Copyright** — automatically subsists in the source code under **Section 13(1)(a) of the Copyright Act, 1957**.
- **Patent** — may subsist if the algorithm is a patentable subject matter (note: pure algorithms/business methods are *not* patentable in India per the *Nokia v. MyMobile* 2012 guidelines).
- **Trade Secret** — protectable under common law / breach of confidence.

**Step 3 — Identify the Owner:**
Under **Section 17 of the Copyright Act**, the *author* (the employee developer) is the first owner — **UNLESS** there is a written contract assigning the work to the employer (commonly done via the employment agreement's "IP assignment clause").

**Step 4 — State the Property Character:**
The CEO's claim is legally valid **only if** the IP has been validly assigned in writing to the company. Without assignment, the *physical laptop* is company property, but the *intangible code* is the developer's property.

> **Conclusion:** The algorithm is a **form of intangible property (IP)** — a bundle of statutory rights (copyright + trade secret) — distinct from the laptop, which is *tangible* company property.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Taxonomy of Property (Block Architecture)

This block diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy of "Property" and shows where IP fits in.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PROPERTY] --> B[Tangible Property]
    A --> C[Intangible Property]

    B --> B1[Movable Property]
    B --> B2[Immovable Property]

    B1 --> B1a[Goods, Vehicles, Cash]
    B2 --> B2a[Land, Buildings]

    C --> C1[Intellectual Property]
    C --> C2[Other Intangibles]
    C2 --> C2a[Goodwill, Debt Instruments, Negotiable Instruments]

    C1 --> D1[Copyright]
    C1 --> D2[Patent]
    C1 --> D3[Trademark]
    C1 --> D4[Industrial Design]
    C1 --> D5[Trade Secret]
    C1 --> D6[Geographical Indication]

    D1 --> D1a[Literary, Artistic, Musical, Software]
    D2 --> D2a[Inventions, Processes, Products]
    D3 --> D3a[Brand Names, Logos, Slogans]
    D4 --> D4a[Aesthetic Design of an Article]
    D5 --> D5a[Confidential Business Information]
    D6 --> D6a[Darjeeling Tea, Kanchipuram Silk]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Property bifurcates at the top into Tangible (physical) and Intangible (non-physical). IP is a sub-branch of Intangible Property — the *creations of the human mind* cluster.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The "Bundle of Rights" Mapping for IP

This diagram illustrates how the classical Hohfeldian bundle-of-rights framework is *adapted* to intellectual creations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLASSICAL[Classical Property Rights]
        R1[Right to Possess]
        R2[Right to Use]
        R3[Right to Enjoy]
        R4[Right to Dispose]
        R5[Right to Exclude]
    end

    subgraph IP[Equivalent IP Rights]
        IP1[Hold the manuscript or source code]
        IP2[Reproduce, perform, broadcast]
        IP3[Collect royalties and licence fees]
        IP4[Assign or license the IP to third parties]
        IP5[File infringement suit against copyist]
    end

    R1 --> IP1
    R2 --> IP2
    R3 --> IP3
    R4 --> IP4
    R5 --> IP5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each classical right finds a *parallel expression* in IP. For example, the right to **exclude** in physical property (fence) becomes the right to **file an infringement suit** in IP (legal action).

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Lifecycle of an IP Right (Public Domain Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mental Creation or Invention] --> B[Statutory Protection Granted]
    B --> C[Exclusive Monopoly Period]
    C --> D{Statutory Term Expired?}
    D -- No --> E[Continue to Enforce and License]
    D -- Yes --> F[Entry into Public Domain]
    F --> G[Society Free to Use, Adapt, Build Upon]
    G --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Unlike physical property (which can theoretically be held forever by heirs), IP is **time-bound**. After the statutory term, the work flows back into the public domain — this cyclical engine is the heart of *utilitarian* IP theory.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Justification Theories for IP as Property

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WHY TREAT IP AS PROPERTY?] --> B[Theory 1 Labour]
    A --> C[Theory 2 Utilitarian]
    A --> D[Theory 3 Personality]

    B --> B1[John Locke 1689 - Creator laboured mentally]
    C --> C1[Bentham, Mill - Incentivise innovation via monopoly]
    D --> D1[Hegel, Kant - IP extends the creators personality]

    B1 --> OUT[Recognise IP as Property]
    C1 --> OUT
    D1 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT** write "Intellectual property is property that is intellectual." This is a *circular definition* and is the **#1 reason students lose 1–2 marks** in Part A.
> 2. **Always** cite the *specific statute* (e.g., *"under Section 13 of the Copyright Act, 1957"*) — vague references lose marks.
> 3. **Never** confuse **Copyright (auto-acquired)** with **Patent/TM (registration-based)** — this is a common 3-mark trap.
> 4. **Always** mention the **time-bound nature** of IP when comparing it with physical property — the limited duration is the *conceptual fulcrum* of the entire module.
> 5. **Use the Latin term** *"res incorporalis"* (for intangible things) at least once in a 14-mark answer — it is examiner shorthand for "this student has read deeply."

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]*
**"Property is not a thing, it is a bundle of rights." Critically analyse this statement in the context of intellectual property.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The statement, drawn from Hohfeld's *bundle-of-rights* theory (1913), reflects the modern legal view that ownership is a *cluster of entitlements* rather than a single absolute dominion.

In the context of intellectual property, the *object* of the right (an invention, a brand, a song) is intangible, but the owner holds the same five legal entitlements — to possess, use, enjoy, dispose, and exclude — recognised by statute. For instance, under the **Copyright Act, 1957**, the author has the right to reproduce, publish, communicate, and to *exclude* others from doing so (Section 14).

Hence, the "bundle of rights" formulation is essential to IP law because it accommodates **territoriality, time limits, and licensing** — features that a single-absolute-right model cannot. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]*
**Distinguish between tangible and intangible property with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Tangible Property** | **Intangible Property** |
|---|---|
| Has a physical form; can be touched, seen, weighed. | Has no physical form; exists as a legal right. |
| Protected by general property law (Transfer of Property Act, 1882). | Protected by specific IP statutes (Patents/Copyright/TM Act). |
| **Examples:** (i) A laptop (ii) A piece of land | **Examples:** (i) A patented invention (ii) A registered trademark logo |

The key conceptual distinction is *rivalry* — tangible property is rival (one person using it reduces what's left for others), while intangible IP is *non-rival* (one person's use does not diminish another's). **(3 marks)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (Question A OR Question B). Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **📌 Question A (14 Marks)**
*[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]*

**(a) "Intellectual property is a special category of property." Examine this statement with reference to the bundle-of-rights theory and the territorial nature of IP. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Property as a Bundle of Rights (3 marks):**
The *bundle-of-rights* theory (Hohfeld, 1913) conceptualises ownership not as a single absolute right but as a collection of distinct legal entitlements: possession, use, enjoyment, disposal, and exclusion. Classical property law historically applied this to tangible objects, but the theory is *abstract* enough to be applied to *intangible* objects as well.

When applied to intellectual property, the bundle-of-rights framework is evident in statutes:
- **Patents Act, 1970 — Section 48** grants the patentee the *exclusive right* to exploit the invention and (by implication) the right to exclude others.
- **Copyright Act, 1957 — Section 14** enumerates the bundle of economic rights: reproduce, issue copies, perform, communicate, adapt.
- **Trade Marks Act, 1999 — Section 28** gives the registered proprietor the exclusive right to use the mark and to obtain relief in case of infringement.

**2. The Special / Distinctive Features of IP within this Bundle (2 marks):**
While IP uses the same bundle framework, it departs from classical property in three key respects:
- **Limited Duration:** IP is time-bound (Patent 20 yrs, Copyright Life+60 yrs). Classical property can be held in perpetuity.
- **Territorial Limitation:** IP rights are granted *country-by-country*. A US patent gives no rights in India. A *Pichode v. Patel* (1982) rationale affirms this.
- **Statutory Origin:** Unlike classical property, IP rights do not arise from possession or labour — they arise from *registration* (Patent, TM) or from *statutory recognition of creation* (Copyright).

**3. Conclusion (2 marks):**
Therefore, intellectual property is indeed a *special* category of property. It adopts the bundle-of-rights logic of classical property but reshapes it to suit the unique challenges of intangible, non-rival, and infinitely reproducible subject matter. The special category exists precisely because, without legal intervention, the free-rider problem would destroy the incentive to create.

---

**(b) Compare and contrast physical property rights with intellectual property rights on any seven parameters. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Tabular Presentation — Examiner's Favourite Format):**

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Physical Property** | **Intellectual Property** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Object** | Tangible, material thing | Intangible creation of the mind |
| 2 | **Scarcity** | Naturally scarce (finite land) | Artificially scarce (legal monopoly) |
| 3 | **Rivalry** | Rival — one use reduces another's | Non-rival — multiple simultaneous uses |
| 4 | **Excludability** | Easy (fence, lock) | Difficult (needs legal machinery) |
| 5 | **Duration** | Potentially perpetual | Statutorily time-limited |
| 6 | **Acquisition** | Possession, purchase, gift, succession | Registration or creation (auto) |
| 7 | **Territoriality** | Generally universal | Strictly territorial — country-specific |
| 8 | **Transfer Mode** | Sale deed, delivery, registration | Assignment, licence, will |
| 9 | **Public Domain** | Not applicable | Falls into public domain post-expiry |
| 10 | **Justification** | Natural-law / occupation | Labour / Utilitarian / Personality theory |

**Conclusion:** The comparison reveals that IP is a *functional extension* of the property concept into the intangible realm. Both confer exclusionary rights, but IP must be carefully limited in *time*, *territory*, and *scope* to balance creator reward with public welfare. **(7 marks)**

---

### **📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
*[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) Explain the three classical theories that justify the grant of intellectual property rights as a form of legal property. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The grant of property rights in intangible creations is justified by three principal theories:

**1. Locke's Labour Theory (Natural Rights) — 2 marks:**
John Locke (*Two Treatises of Government*, 1689) argued that every individual has a property in their own person, and therefore in the labour of their body and mind. By extension, when a person mixes their mental labour with the raw material of nature to create an invention or a literary work, the resulting creation becomes their *property*. The State merely recognises what is already morally theirs.
*Application:* Section 6 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 vests the right to a patent in the *true and first inventor* — a Lockean formulation.

**2. Utilitarian / Economic Incentive Theory — 2.5 marks:**
Classical utilitarians (Bentham, Mill) and modern economists (Machlup, Arrow) argue that without a temporary legal monopoly, creators would not invest in the high cost and risk of innovation. The patent/copyright monopoly acts as a *bribe* to innovate, ensuring the public eventually benefits when the IP enters the public domain.
*Application:* The 20-year patent term under TRIPS Article 33 and the Indian Patents Act is a utilitarian calculation — long enough to incentivise, short enough to disseminate.

**3. Personality Theory (Hegel & Kant) — 2.5 marks:**
Hegel (*Philosophy of Right*, 1820) and Kant argued that property is essential to the *externalisation of the will* — property is the medium through which personality becomes objective in the world. A novel, painting, or invention is the *embodiment* of the creator's personality. To deny them property in it is to deny their *personhood*.
*Application:* Foundational to the *moral rights* of authors under **Sections 57 of the Copyright Act, 1957** (right of paternity and integrity) — these rights are *inalienable* and reflect the Hegelian idea that the author's personality is inseparably bound to the work.

**Conclusion:** Modern IP systems blend all three theories, but in different proportions — patents lean utilitarian, copyright blends utilitarian and personality, trademark leans economic and personality. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) "An invention is the property of the inventor, not of the employer, unless assigned in writing." Discuss the legal position in India with relevant statutory provisions. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. General Rule — Employee is the Inventor (2 marks):**
Under Indian law, the *first and true inventor* is the natural person who devised the invention (Section 2(1)(y) of the Patents Act, 1970, read with Section 6). This is a Lockean application — the labour is the employee's, and the right is initially theirs.

**2. Statutory Exception — Employer's Right under Section 39 (2.5 marks):**
**Section 39 of the Patents Act, 1970** carves out an exception: where an invention is made in the course of employment or under a specific contract, the *employer* is entitled to the patent *unless* there is an agreement to the contrary. This is reinforced by **Section 4 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872** — a valid contract can assign future IP.

**3. The Common-Law Exception for High-Risk / Research Roles (1 mark):**
Where the employee is specifically hired to invent (e.g., a research scientist in a CSIR lab), the courts have held the invention belongs to the employer, with the employee entitled to "adequate remuneration" under **Section 35(6) read with Section 39(2)** of the Patents Act.

**4. Best Practice — Written Assignment (1 mark):**
To avoid litigation, prudent employers include a **"present assignment of future inventions"** clause in employment contracts. The contract is the *legal* mechanism that converts the employee's natural-law right into the employer's statutory right. Under the **Copyright Act, Section 17**, a similar assignment in writing is required for the employer to own the copyright in works created in the course of employment.

**5. Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
The opening statement is therefore *legally accurate but qualified*. Without a written assignment or contract, the inventor-employee retains the property right. With a valid written assignment, the right is lawfully transferred to the employer. **(7 marks)**

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **📌 Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision):**

- ⭐ **Property = a bundle of rights (Hohfeld, 1913)**, not a thing. The right includes *possess, use, enjoy, dispose, exclude*.
- ⭐ **IP = a special form of intangible property**, where the object is a *creation of the mind* (idea, expression, invention, sign).
- ⭐ **Two key economic features of IP**: (i) **Non-rivalry** and (ii) **Difficult excludability** — together they justify legal monopoly.
- ⭐ **Three justifications for IP**: **Labour** (Locke), **Utilitarian** (incentive-to-innovate), **Personality** (Hegel/Kant).
- ⭐ **Five major branches of IP**: Copyright, Patent, Trademark, Industrial Design, Trade Secret (and GI, Semiconductor, Plant Variety, etc.).
- ⭐ **Statutes to memorise for KTU**:
  - Copyright Act, **1957**
  - Patents Act, **1970**
  - Trade Marks Act, **1999**
  - Designs Act, **2000**
  - Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, **1999**
  - Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Act, **2001**
- ⭐ **IP is territorial** — there is no "global patent." File country-by-country (or via PCT).
- ⭐ **IP is time-limited** — Patent 20 yrs, Copyright Life+60 yrs, TM 10 yrs renewable.
- ⭐ **Section 17 (Copyright Act)** governs first ownership; **Section 39 (Patents Act)** governs employee inventions.
- ⭐ **Latin must-know**: *res* (physical thing) vs *res incorporalis* (intangible right). The phrase *"intellectual property is a sui generis form of property"* (i.e., a class of its own) impresses examiners.
- ⭐ **Public Domain** — the realm of free use after IP expires. Foundational to the *progress of science and useful arts* (US Constitution, Article I, Section 8).
- ⭐ **WIPO Convention 1967** — defines IP; **TRIPS Agreement 1994** — sets global minimum standards; **Berne Convention 1886** — copyright; **Paris Convention 1883** — industrial property.
- ⭐ **Examiner's red flag**: Never use "intellectual property" and "physical property" interchangeably — the *whole module* tests whether you can tell them apart.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Important International Conventions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — OVERVIEW OF IMPORTANT INTERNATIONAL CONVENTIONS

## 1.1 Introduction to International IP Conventions

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Important International Conventions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (PESFT757):** The objective of this module is to make students understand the **Harmonization of IP laws globally** through the foundational treaties administered by **WIPO (World Intellectual Property Organization)** and the binding trade-related agreement under the **WTO (World Trade Organization)**.

### 1.1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **International Convention on Intellectual Property Rights** is a **multilateral treaty** executed between sovereign nations (Contracting States) that establishes **minimum standards of protection**, defines **uniform procedural norms**, and provides **mutual recognition rights** for intellectual property across national borders. These conventions operate on the twin doctrines of:

- **National Treatment Principle** — A foreign national must be treated at par with a domestic national in the protection of IP.
- **Right of Priority / Convention Priority** — A prior application filed in one contracting state creates a **priority date** that protects subsequent applications in other member countries for a fixed statutory window.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Think of international IP conventions as a **"Global Passport System for Innovations"**. Just like a passport issued by one country allows you to travel visa-on-arrival to many others (with certain rules), an **IP right filed in one member country gets a "priority date stamp"** that acts as a passport, allowing the applicant to claim the same invention/trademark/design in other member countries within a fixed time — without losing the original filing date. Without these conventions, every creator would have to "re-invent" the wheel in every country from scratch.

### 1.1.2 The Need for International Conventions

Before the 19th century, IP was strictly **territorial** — a patent in India had **zero legal value** in France. This created three major problems:

1. **Piracy across borders** — Innovators feared copying in foreign markets.
2. **Duplication of effort** — Same invention had to be filed separately in each country on the same day.
3. **Discrimination** — Foreigners were charged higher fees or denied protection outright.

The 19th century saw the birth of **Paris Union (1883)** and **Berne Union (1886)** to fix this.

### 1.1.3 Key International Bodies

| Body | Full Form | Established | Headquarters | Mandate |
|------|-----------|------------|--------------|---------|
| **WIPO** | World Intellectual Property Organization | **1967** (effective 1970) | **Geneva, Switzerland** | Administer IP treaties, promote IP globally |
| **WTO** | World Trade Organization | **1995** (replaced GATT) | **Geneva, Switzerland** | Enforce TRIPS Agreement |
| **Paris Union** | International Union for the Protection of Industrial Property | **1883** | Administered by WIPO | Industrial Property (Patents, Trademarks, Designs) |
| **Berne Union** | International Union for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works | **1886** | Administered by WIPO | Copyright & Related Rights |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India's Status:**
> - India is a **member of WIPO** (since 1975).
> - India became a member of the **Paris Convention (December 7, 1998)** and **Berne Convention (April 28, 1928)**.
> - India is a founding member of the **WTO (1995)** and is bound by **TRIPS**.
> - India is **NOT** a member of the **Hague Agreement** and the **Madrid Protocol** (only partially via bilateral treaties) — a frequently asked KTU question.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of International IP Conventions

International conventions can be classified into **four major categories** based on the type of IP they protect:

### Category 1: Industrial Property Conventions

#### 2.1.1 Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property (1883)

This is the **oldest, foundational, and most important** international IP treaty — often called the **"Magna Carta of Intellectual Property."**

- **Adopted:** March 20, **1883** in Paris
- **Revised at:** Brussels (1900), Washington (1911), The Hague (1925), London (1934), Lisbon (1958), Stockholm (1967)
- **Latest Revision:** **Stockholm Act (1967)**
- **Administered by:** WIPO
- **Number of Contracting Parties (as of 2024):** **> 178 countries**
- **Applies to:** **Patents, Trademarks, Industrial Designs, Utility Models, Trade Names, Geographical Indications (GIs), and Unfair Competition.**

**Three Pillars / Core Principles of the Paris Convention:**

##### Pillar 1: National Treatment
Article 2 mandates that once a national of a contracting state files an IP application in another member country, that member country must grant **treatment no less favorable** than it grants to its own nationals regarding protection, procedural remedies, and judicial relief.

##### Pillar 2: Right of Priority (Article 4 — *The most cited provision*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Right of Priority — KTU 2024 Favourite Question:**
> An applicant who **first files** an application in any contracting state (the *priority date* country) gets a **priority period** to file the same application in other member countries. These subsequent filings will be treated as if they were filed **on the original priority date**.

**Priority Periods (in months):**

| Type of IP | Priority Period |
|------------|-----------------|
| **Patents & Utility Models** | **12 months** |
| **Industrial Designs** | **6 months** |
| **Trademarks** | **6 months** |

##### Pillar 3: Common Rules (Substantive Provisions)

- **Independence of Patents (Article 4bis):** A patent granted in one country is **independent** of patents granted in other countries. Refusal/nullity in one country does NOT affect another.
- **Patentable Subject Matter (Article 4quater):** A member can decide what is patentable, but **methods of treatment of humans/animals** are excluded from patentability (medical methods).
- **Compulsory Licensing Safeguards (Article 5):** A compulsory license for non-working of a patent cannot be granted **before 4 years** from the filing date and **3 years** from the date of grant — whichever is later. Cannot be granted if the patentee can justify the non-working (e.g., import dependency).
- **Trademark Protection (Articles 6–7):** Well-known marks get **special protection** even across different classes. Trademarks and service marks are treated equally.
- **Trade Names (Article 8):** Protected **without filing or registration** in all member states.
- **Unfair Competition (Article 10bis):** Member states must provide **effective protection against acts of unfair competition** (passing off, false advertising, misleading origin).

#### 2.1.2 Patent Cooperation Treaty — PCT (1970)

- **Signed:** June 19, **1970** in Washington D.C.
- **Entered into force:** **1978**
- **Administered by:** **WIPO**
- **Contracting Parties:** **> 150 countries**
- **India acceded:** **December 7, 1998**

**Purpose:** To **simplify** and **defer** the costly process of filing national patent applications in multiple countries.

**Mechanism:**
1. A single **international application** is filed either with the **Receiving Office (RO)** (in India, the Indian Patent Office acts as RO).
2. The application is searched by an **International Searching Authority (ISA)** — in India, the Indian Patent Office functions as ISA.
3. A **International Search Report (ISR)** and **Written Opinion** are issued.
4. **International Publication** occurs 18 months from priority date.
5. The applicant then enters the **National Phase** (typically 30/31 months from priority date) in individual countries of interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** PCT is like **booking a single flight ticket** at a "global travel agency" (WIPO) — you don't have to buy separate tickets from 150 airlines. You book one ticket, the agency does the search and documentation, and later you "board" only the flights (countries) you actually need.

#### 2.1.3 Madrid System for the International Registration of Marks (1891 & 1989)

- **Madrid Agreement:** Signed **April 14, 1891** in Madrid.
- **Madrid Protocol:** Signed **June 27, 1989**.
- The Madrid **System** is the combination of the Agreement + Protocol.
- Administered by WIPO; based in **Geneva**.
- **India is NOT a member of the Madrid System** — this is a frequently asked KTU question.

**Mechanism:** File **one international application** in one language (English, French, or Spanish), pay **one set of fees** in **one currency (Swiss Francs)**, and designate multiple Madrid System member countries.

#### 2.1.4 Hague Agreement Concerning the International Registration of Industrial Designs (1925 & 1999)

- **Original (London) Act:** 1934
- **Geneva Act:** **July 2, 1999** (modern version)
- **Administered by:** WIPO
- **India is NOT a member of the Hague Agreement.**

#### 2.1.5 Budapest Treaty on the International Recognition of the Deposit of Microorganisms (1977)

- **Signed:** April 28, **1977**
- **Entered into force:** **1980**
- **Purpose:** For patent applications involving **microorganisms**, the treaty allows deposit of the microorganism in **any "International Depositary Authority" (IDA)** recognized under the treaty, eliminating the need to deposit in every country separately.
- **India is a member** (since 2001); deposits can be made at **MTCC (Microbial Type Culture Collection), IMTECH, Chandigarh** and at the **National Collection of Industrial Microorganisms (NCIM), Pune**.

### Category 2: Copyright Conventions

#### 2.2.1 Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works (1886)

- **Adopted:** **September 9, 1886** in Berne, Switzerland
- **Administered by:** WIPO
- **Contracting Parties:** **> 180 countries**
- **India acceded:** **April 28, 1928** (one of the earliest adherents)

**Three Core Principles:**

1. **Automatic Protection (No Formalities):** Copyright arises **automatically** upon creation of the work — no registration, deposit, or formalities required. This is a **fundamental departure** from the Paris Convention which required formalities for patents/trademarks.
2. **National Treatment:** Same as Paris — foreigners treated at par with nationals.
3. **Independent Protection:** Protection is independent of whether protection exists in the country of origin.

**Economic Rights (Minimum Protection Periods):**

| Work Type | Minimum Protection Period |
|-----------|---------------------------|
| **Literary & Artistic Works** | Life of author + **50 years** (post-mortem auctoris) |
| **Anonymous / Pseudonymous Works** | 50 years from publication |
| **Cinematographic Works** | 50 years from publication/creation |
| **Photographic Works** | 25 years from creation (minimum) |
| **Applied Art** | 25 years from creation |

**Special Provision — Article 7(8):**
The Convention allows developing countries to grant **compulsory translation licenses** for educational/cultural works under specific conditions.

**Berne + Paris = The Twin Pillars of WIPO.**

#### 2.2.2 Universal Copyright Convention (UCC) (1952)
- Adopted under **UNESCO**, not WIPO.
- **Weaker** than Berne — allowed formalities and was a fallback for countries not part of Berne.
- India is a member.
- Largely **obsolete** today since most countries are Berne members.

#### 2.2.3 Rome Convention for the Protection of Performers, Producers of Phonograms and Broadcasting Organisations (1961)
- Protects **Neighbouring / Related Rights** (not copyright proper, but adjacent rights).
- India is **NOT** a member.
- Administered jointly by WIPO, ILO, and UNESCO.

#### 2.2.4 WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty — WPPT (1996)
- A special agreement under the Berne Convention.
- Covers **performers' rights** and **phonogram producers' rights** in the digital era.
- India is a member (since 2024 Marrakesh Treaty accession era; WPPT signed 1996, ratified by India later).

### Category 3: New Plant Variety & GI Conventions

#### 2.3.1 UPOV Convention — International Convention for the Protection of New Varieties of Plants (1961)
- **Established the International Union for the Protection of New Varieties of Plants (UPOV)**
- **Entered into force:** 1968
- India is **NOT a member of UPOV** but has its own **Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Act, 2001 (PPVFR Act)**.

#### 2.3.2 Lisbon Agreement for the Protection of Appellations of Origin (1958)
- Protects **Appellations of Origin** (a stricter form of GI).
- Administered by WIPO.
- India is **NOT** a member; instead protects GIs under the **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999**.

### Category 4: Trade-Related Convention (TRIPS — The Most Powerful)

#### 2.4.1 TRIPS Agreement (1994) — Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights

- **Negotiated during:** **Uruguay Round (1986–1994)** of GATT
- **Adopted:** **April 15, 1994** at Marrakesh
- **Entered into force:** **January 1, 1995** (with the establishment of WTO)
- **Part of:** **Annex 1C of the Marrakesh Agreement Establishing the WTO**
- **Legally Binding:** YES — enforced through WTO's **Dispute Settlement Body (DSB)**

**Why TRIPS is Unique:**
1. It is the **only IP convention that is trade-sanction-backed** (violation can lead to WTO-sanctioned retaliation).
2. It incorporates **Berne + Paris + Rome Convention** standards but adds **enforcement mechanisms**.
3. It extends IP protection to **newer areas** — **layout designs of integrated circuits (IPs)**, **undisclosed information (trade secrets)**, and **geographical indications** (with separate protection for wines and spirits).

**TRIPS — Three-Tier Architecture:**

| Section | Subject Matter |
|---------|----------------|
| **Part I** | General Provisions & Basic Principles |
| **Part II** | Standards Concerning the Availability, Scope, and Use of IP Rights |
| **Part III** | Enforcement of IP Rights (Civil, Criminal, Border Measures) |
| **Part IV** | Acquisition & Maintenance of IP Rights (procedural) |
| **Part V** | Dispute Prevention & Settlement |
| **Part VI** | Transitional Arrangements |
| **Part VII** | Institutional Arrangements |

**TRIPS Patent Rules (Article 27):**
- Patents available for **both products and processes** in **all fields of technology**.
- Patents must be granted for a minimum of **20 years** from the filing date.
- Members may exclude: animals (other than micro-organisms), plants (other than micro-organisms), **essentially biological processes**, **plants produced by such processes**, and **medical treatment methods**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Article 31bis (added in 2003):** Allows export of pharmaceutical products manufactured under **compulsory license** to countries with insufficient manufacturing capacity — the basis for the **Doha Declaration (2001)** on TRIPS and Public Health.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparative Table — Convention Snapshot

| Convention | Year | Subject Matter | Administered By | India Member? |
|------------|------|----------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Paris Convention** | 1883 | Industrial Property | WIPO | **Yes (1998)** |
| **Berne Convention** | 1886 | Copyright | WIPO | **Yes (1928)** |
| **Madrid Agreement** | 1891 | Trademarks | WIPO | No |
| **Madrid Protocol** | 1989 | Trademarks | WIPO | No |
| **Hague Agreement** | 1925/1999 | Industrial Designs | WIPO | No |
| **Lisbon Agreement** | 1958 | Appellations of Origin | WIPO | No |
| **Nice Agreement** | 1957 | Classification of Goods/Services (Trademarks) | WIPO | Yes |
| **Strasbourg Agreement** | 1971 | Patent Classification (IPC) | WIPO | Yes |
| **Budapest Treaty** | 1977 | Microorganism Deposit | WIPO | Yes |
| **Rome Convention** | 1961 | Performers / Phonograms | WIPO/ILO/UNESCO | No |
| **PCT (Patent Coop. Treaty)** | 1970 | Patent Filing (International) | WIPO | **Yes (1998)** |
| **UPOV Convention** | 1961 | Plant Varieties | UPOV (WIPO) | No |
| **WPPT** | 1996 | Digital Performances | WIPO | Yes |
| **Marrakesh Treaty** | 2013 | Access to Published Works for Blind | WIPO | Yes |
| **TRIPS Agreement** | 1994 | All IP (Trade-Linked) | WTO | **Yes (Founding, 1995)** |
| **WIPO** | 1967 | UN Agency for IP | — | **Yes (1975)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams: "P-B-M-H-N-S-B-R-P-U-W-M-T"**
> *Paris, Berne, Madrid, Hague, Nice, Strasbourg, Budapest, Rome, PCT, UPOV, WPPT, Marrakesh, TRIPS*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Legal & Procedural Limits)

Since this is an IPR/law subject, the "formulas" are **statutory periods, numerical limits, and threshold rules**. The following is a high-density reference table:

| Parameter | Value / Limit | Source |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| **Priority Period — Patents** | **12 months** | Paris Convention Art. 4(C)(1) |
| **Priority Period — Trademarks** | **6 months** | Paris Convention Art. 4(C)(1) |
| **Priority Period — Industrial Designs** | **6 months** | Paris Convention Art. 4(C)(1) |
| **Compulsory License — Patent (Non-Working)** | **4 years from filing OR 3 years from grant** (later of the two) | Paris Convention Art. 5A |
| **PCT International Phase Duration** | Up to **30/31 months** from priority date | PCT Art. 22/39 |
| **PCT National Phase Entry (India)** | **31 months** | Indian Patents Act, 1970 (Sec 133) |
| **Berne Copyright Term (General)** | **Life + 50 years** | Berne Convention Art. 7 |
| **TRIPS Patent Term Minimum** | **20 years** from filing date | TRIPS Art. 33 |
| **TRIPS GI Protection (Goods General)** | Indefinite, as long as characteristics exist | TRIPS Art. 23/24 |
| **Budapest Treaty Viability Period** | At least **30 years** | Budapest Treaty |
| **Nice Classification Periods (mark classes)** | **45 Classes** (1–34 goods, 35–45 services) | Nice Agreement |
| **Hague Agreement Design Term (Geneva Act)** | **15 years** (minimum) | Hague Agreement Art. 17 |
| **WIPO Convention Membership Effectivity** | **3 months** after deposit of instrument | WIPO Convention Art. 14 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> For an engineering startup in India aiming to go global, understanding these conventions determines:
> 1. **Filing strategy** — Do I file in India first (proving priority) and then expand under Paris/PCT?
> 2. **Cost optimization** — Single PCT filing vs. 50+ national filings saves **millions of dollars**.
> 3. **Market entry timeline** — The 12/6-month priority window dictates the global launch schedule.
> 4. **Risk management** — TRIPS enforcement mechanisms (WTO dispute settlement) can be used to defend IP in foreign jurisdictions.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL MATRIX — PROCEDURAL FRAMEWORK DERIVATION

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptation Note:** This is a Law/IPR subject; the "derivations" below are **analytical procedural frameworks** that show step-by-step how a creator/engineer progresses through an international IP filing workflow. Each step is shown with explicit logic transitions, just as a mathematical derivation would show algebraic steps.

## 3.1 Derivation of the International Patent Filing Pathway (PCT Workflow)

Let us derive the **canonical PCT filing workflow** — a step-by-step analytical framework used by every engineering enterprise that wishes to obtain global patent protection.

### Step 1: Domestic Priority Filing (Origin Country)

A first-time inventor files a patent application in their **home country** (e.g., India, via the Indian Patent Office). This establishes the **priority date** $D_p$.

$$D_p = \text{Date of First Filing (in the home contracting state)}$$

**Logic:** The Paris Convention (Article 4) and PCT (Article 8) both require a valid prior application to claim priority. Without a priority date, the 12-month clock cannot start.

### Step 2: Priority Window Calculation

The Paris Convention allows **12 months** for patents to claim the priority date in foreign jurisdictions.

$$D_{\text{priority window}} = D_p + 12 \text{ months}$$

The applicant must file a corresponding application in at least one foreign country **before or on** this date to retain the original priority date $D_p$.

### Step 3: PCT International Application Filing

Within the same 12-month priority window, the applicant files a **PCT international application** with:
- A **Receiving Office (RO)** (in India, the Kolkata branch of the Indian Patent Office serves as the RO).
- **One language** (English, French, or Spanish — typically English).
- **One set of fees** in CHF or equivalent.

$$A_{\text{PCT}} = f(\text{Description}, \text{Claims}, \text{Abstract}, \text{Drawings})$$

The PCT application **automatically claims** the priority date $D_p$ from Step 1.

### Step 4: International Search (ISA Review)

The **International Searching Authority (ISA)** conducts a prior-art search.

$$ISR = \{\text{Prior Art Documents}, \text{Novelty Assessment}, \text{Inventive Step Assessment}\}$$

**Logic:** The ISA examines the application against existing patents, scientific literature, and unpublished disclosures. India uses its own ISA (the Indian Patent Office) for Indian applicants.

### Step 5: International Publication

The WIPO publishes the PCT application **18 months** from the priority date.

$$D_{\text{pub}} = D_p + 18 \text{ months}$$

**Logic:** This provides provisional novelty disclosure to the world, satisfying the "publication" requirement of the Paris Convention.

### Step 6: International Preliminary Examination (Optional but Recommended)

The applicant can request an **International Preliminary Examination (IPE)** through an **International Preliminary Examining Authority (IPEA)**.

**Outcome:** A **Patentability Report (Chapter II)** is issued, providing a non-binding but highly credible opinion on novelty, inventive step, and industrial applicability.

### Step 7: National Phase Entry (The Cost-Optimization Decision Point)

By **30 or 31 months** from the priority date, the applicant must decide **in which specific countries** to pursue national patents.

$$D_{\text{national entry}} = D_p + 31 \text{ months (in India)}$$

The applicant files **national phase applications** in selected countries. The PCT application effectively **becomes** a national application in each chosen country.

### Step 8: National Grant and Enforcement

Each national patent office examines the application under its **own national law** (TRIPS sets minimums; each country has its own rules).

$$T_{\text{patent term}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years (TRIPS Art. 33)}$$

**Final Result:** A portfolio of national patents, all valid for a minimum of 20 years from the priority filing date.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Procedural Framework

The following table **analytically derives** how each convention uniquely extends protection. The "input" is a creator's local right; the "output" is the international extension.

| Convention | Local Input | Procedural Action | International Output |
|------------|------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Paris Convention** | National patent application (in member country) | File foreign application within **12 months** | Foreign filing gets **same priority date** as original |
| **PCT** | National patent application | File single **PCT international application** within **12 months** | Single application; defer national filings up to **30/31 months** |
| **Madrid Protocol** | National/regional trademark | File **single international application** with WIPO | Trademarks in **designated Madrid members** (up to 130+) |
| **Hague Agreement** | National design | File **single international design application** | Designs in **multiple Hague members** |
| **Berne Convention** | Copyright in source country | **No action** — automatic protection arises | Copyright in **180+ Berne members** without formalities |
| **TRIPS Agreement** | Domestic IP laws (if WTO member) | **No action** — TRIPS binds all WTO members | WTO-enforceable **minimum standards** across 164+ members |

## 3.3 The "Three Pillars of IP Harmony" — Analytical Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need three different conventions? Why not one?**
> The following logical deduction shows why the three pillars (**Paris**, **Berne**, **TRIPS**) are structurally non-substitutable.

### Logical Step 1
Different IP rights have **different subject matter**:
- Industrial Property (patents, marks, designs) → requires **formalities** (registration) and **priority** (file first to get the date) → **Paris Convention** was created.

### Logical Step 2
Copyright is **automatically created** upon the act of creation. **Formalities would violate the natural right of the author** → Berne Convention **banned formalities** and provided **automatic** protection.

### Logical Step 3
Paris and Berne provided **harmonization of substantive law** but **lacked enforcement** — a country could ignore them. TRIPS solved this by tying IP to **trade** — making violations **trade-sanction-eligible** under the WTO's Dispute Settlement Body (DSB).

$$\boxed{\text{Paris (Substance)} + \text{Berne (Substance, No Formalities)} + \text{TRIPS (Enforcement)} = \text{Modern Global IP System}}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Architecture — Global IP Convention Ecosystem

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **entire ecosystem of international IP conventions** and their interrelationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Intellectual Property Rights - Global Framework] --> B[Industrial Property]
    A --> C[Copyright and Related Rights]
    A --> D[Trade Related IP]
    A --> E[Sui Generis Rights]

    B --> B1[Paris Convention 1883]
    B --> B2[PCT 1970]
    B --> B3[Madrid System 1891]
    B --> B4[Hague Agreement 1925]
    B --> B5[Budapest Treaty 1977]
    B --> B6[Nice Agreement 1957]
    B --> B7[Strasbourg Agreement 1971]
    B --> B8[UPOV Convention 1961]
    B --> B9[Lisbon Agreement 1958]

    C --> C1[Berne Convention 1886]
    C --> C2[UCC 1952]
    C --> C3[Rome Convention 1961]
    C --> C4[WPPT 1996]
    C --> C5[Marrakesh Treaty 2013]

    D --> D1[TRIPS Agreement 1994]
    D1 --> D2[Under WTO]
    D2 --> D3[Enforcement via DSB]

    E --> E1[Plant Varieties PPVFR]
    E --> E2[GI of Goods Act 1999]
    E --> E3[Semiconductor IP Act 2000]

    B1 --> WIPO
    B2 --> WIPO
    B3 --> WIPO
    B4 --> WIPO
    B5 --> WIPO
    B6 --> WIPO
    B7 --> WIPO
    B8 --> WIPO
    B9 --> WIPO
    C1 --> WIPO
    C3 --> WIPO
    C4 --> WIPO
    C5 --> WIPO
    D1 --> WTO
    WIPO --> HEADQUARTERS[Geneva Switzerland]
    D2 --> HEADQUARTERS
```

## 4.2 PCT Filing Workflow — Sequential Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Step 1: File Priority Application in Home Country] --> step2[Step 2: Priority Date is Established - 12 Month Window Begins]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: File PCT International Application with Receiving Office]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: ISA Conducts International Search]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: ISR and Written Opinion Issued]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: WIPO Publishes Application at 18 Months]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Optional IPEA Examination]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: National Phase Entry at 30 or 31 Months]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: National Patents Granted for 20 Years Minimum]
```

## 4.3 Three Pillars of Global IP — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Global IP Harmonization Architecture] --> B[Pillar 1 Paris Convention 1883]
    A --> C[Pillar 2 Berne Convention 1886]
    A --> D[Pillar 3 TRIPS Agreement 1994]

    B --> B1[Subject: Industrial Property]
    B --> B2[Key Principle: Right of Priority]
    B --> B3[Period: 12 months Patents - 6 months Trademarks]
    B --> B4[Admin: WIPO]

    C --> C1[Subject: Copyright and Artistic Works]
    C --> C2[Key Principle: Automatic Protection - No Formalities]
    C --> C3[Period: Life plus 50 years]
    C --> C4[Admin: WIPO]

    D --> D1[Subject: All IP Categories]
    D --> D2[Key Principle: Trade Backed Enforcement]
    D --> D3[Period: 20 Years Patents]
    D --> D4[Admin: WTO Dispute Settlement Body]

    B4 --> E[WIPO Geneva]
    C4 --> E
    D4 --> F[WTO Geneva]
```

## 4.4 Paris Convention Priority Mechanism — Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Inventor Files Patent in India on Day 0] --> P2[Priority Date Established Dp]
    P2 --> P3[Foreign Filings Must Occur Within 12 Months]
    P3 --> P4[USA Filing on Month 6]
    P3 --> P5[Europe Filing on Month 10]
    P3 --> P6[Japan Filing on Month 12]
    P4 --> P7[All Three Filings Get Same Priority Date Dp]
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Independence of Patents - Each Examined Separately]
    P8 --> P9[USA Patent Granted 20 Years from Indian Filing Date]
    P8 --> P10[European Patent Granted 20 Years from Indian Filing Date]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Paris Convention. State its three core principles. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property (1883)** is the oldest international treaty on industrial property rights, currently administered by the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)**. It applies to patents, trademarks, industrial designs, utility models, trade names, and geographical indications.

The **three core principles** are:
1. **National Treatment** — Foreign nationals must be treated at par with domestic nationals (Article 2).
2. **Right of Priority** — A prior application in one member country establishes a priority date that protects subsequent filings in other member countries for a stipulated period (12 months for patents, 6 months for trademarks and designs) — Article 4.
3. **Common Rules / Independence of Rights** — IP rights obtained in different member countries are **independent** of each other; nullity in one country does not affect the others (Article 4bis).

### Question 2: What is WIPO? State its headquarters and year of establishment. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)** is a **specialized agency of the United Nations** established to promote the protection of intellectual property worldwide. It was established by the **WIPO Convention signed on July 14, 1967** in **Stockholm**, Sweden, and became effective in **1970**. Its **headquarters are in Geneva, Switzerland**. WIPO administers **26 international treaties** related to IP, including the Paris Convention, Berne Convention, PCT, Madrid System, Hague Agreement, and Budapest Treaty. India became a member of WIPO in **1975**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works**. Discuss its three core principles and minimum protection periods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **TRIPS Agreement** in detail, highlighting its salient features, scope, and its impact on the global IP regime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — Berne Convention

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works was adopted on **September 9, 1886** in **Berne, Switzerland**, and is currently administered by WIPO. It is one of the foundational treaties of the Berne Union with **180+ contracting parties**, including India (which acceded on April 28, 1928).

**Three Core Principles (3 marks):**

1. **Automatic Protection (No Formalities) — Article 5(2):** Copyright protection arises **automatically** upon creation of the work. The author is **not required** to register, deposit, or comply with any formality to obtain protection. This is a **fundamental departure** from the Paris Convention (which requires filing/registration for patents and trademarks).

2. **National Treatment — Article 5(1):** Works originating in one member country must be granted the **same protection** in all other member countries as those countries grant to their own nationals.

3. **Independence of Protection — Article 5(2):** The protection granted is **independent** of whether protection exists in the country of origin. A work may be protected in Country B even if it is in the public domain in Country A.

**Minimum Protection Periods (2 marks):**

- Literary and artistic works (other than photographic/applied art): **Life of the author + 50 years** post-mortem auctoris (Berne Art. 7(1)).
- Anonymous or pseudonymous works: **50 years** from the date of lawful publication.
- Cinematographic works: **50 years** from the date of making or first publication.
- Photographic works and works of applied art: **25 years** from the date of creation.

**Special Provision — Article 7(8) (1 mark):**
Developing countries may grant **compulsory translation licenses** under specific conditions for non-commercial educational and cultural purposes, subject to strict safeguards to protect the author's interests.

#### Model Answer for (b) — TRIPS Agreement

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS) Agreement** was negotiated during the **Uruguay Round (1986–1994)** of GATT and was adopted on **April 15, 1994** at **Marrakesh, Morocco**. It came into force on **January 1, 1995** with the establishment of the **World Trade Organization (WTO)**. It is contained in **Annex 1C of the Marrakesh Agreement**.

**Salient Features (3 marks):**

1. **Trade-Sanction-Backed Enforcement:** TRIPS is the **only IP convention** with **WTO Dispute Settlement Body (DSB)** enforcement. Violations can lead to trade sanctions, unlike Paris or Berne which lack such teeth.
2. **Minimum Standards — Comprehensive Coverage:** TRIPS sets **minimum standards** for **all categories of IP** — copyright, patents, trademarks, designs, GIs, layout designs of integrated circuits, and undisclosed information (trade secrets).
3. **Incorporation by Reference:** TRIPS incorporates the substantive obligations of the **Paris Convention (1967)** and the **Berne Convention (1971)** — the "Berne + Paris minus the enforcement gap" philosophy.
4. **Patent Term: 20 years** from the filing date (Article 33) — applies to **both products and processes in all fields of technology** (Article 27).
5. **Compulsory Licensing Framework — Article 31:** TRIPS allows compulsory licensing under conditions such as: prior negotiation with the patent holder, adequate remuneration, and predominantly for the domestic market. **Article 31bis (2003)** allows export of pharmaceuticals manufactured under compulsory license.

**Scope (2 marks):**
- **Part I:** General Provisions and Basic Principles (National Treatment, MFN).
- **Part II:** Standards Concerning the Availability, Scope and Use of IP Rights.
- **Part III:** Enforcement of IP Rights (Civil, Criminal, Border/Anti-Counterfeit Measures).
- **Part IV:** Acquisition and Maintenance of IP Rights.
- **Part V:** Dispute Prevention and Settlement.
- **Part VI:** Transitional Arrangements.
- **Part VII:** Institutional Arrangements (TRIPS Council).

**Impact on Global IP Regime (1 mark):**
TRIPS **globalized IP enforcement**, made IP a **trade issue**, brought in **TRIPS-Plus provisions** through bilateral FTAs, and accelerated **harmonization of IP laws in developing countries** (including India's 2005 Patents Act Amendment to comply with TRIPS Art. 27 product patent requirements).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Frequently Lost Marks:**
> 1. Writing "TRIPS is part of the Paris Convention" — **WRONG.** TRIPS is a standalone WTO agreement that **incorporates** Paris and Berne standards.
> 2. Forgetting the **20-year patent term** under TRIPS Art. 33.
> 3. Confusing **Berne's automatic protection** with Paris's **registration-based protection**.
> 4. Writing "Berne applies to industrial property" — Berne is **strictly for copyright and literary/artistic works**. Industrial property is covered by **Paris**.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)** in detail. Discuss its objectives, filing mechanism, and advantages over direct national filings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Budapest Treaty on the International Recognition of the Deposit of Microorganisms**. Explain its significance in patent applications involving biological inventions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — PCT

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)** was signed on **June 19, 1970** in **Washington D.C.** and entered into force in **1978**. It is administered by the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)**. It has **150+ contracting parties**. India acceded to the PCT on **December 7, 1998**.

**Objectives (2 marks):**
1. To **simplify** and **economize** the process of seeking patent protection in multiple countries.
2. To **defer** major costs (national filing fees, translations, attorney fees) for up to **30/31 months** from the priority date.
3. To provide a **preliminary patentability opinion** before the applicant commits to national filings.
4. To **promote technology transfer** and information dissemination.

**Filing Mechanism — Step-by-Step (3 marks):**
1. **Priority Filing** in the home country (establishes priority date $D_p$).
2. **PCT Application** is filed with a **Receiving Office (RO)** — in India, the Kolkata Patent Office acts as the RO.
3. **International Search** is conducted by an **International Searching Authority (ISA)**. The Indian Patent Office functions as ISA for Indian applicants.
4. **International Search Report (ISR)** and **Written Opinion** are issued.
5. **International Publication** occurs at **18 months** from priority date.
6. **Optional International Preliminary Examination (IPE)** by an **International Preliminary Examining Authority (IPEA)**.
7. **National Phase Entry** at **30 or 31 months** from priority date in selected countries.

**Advantages over Direct National Filings (1 mark):**
- A **single application** replaces potentially 50+ separate national filings.
- **Single language**, **single set of fees** (in CHF).
- **30/31 months of market intelligence** before committing to expensive national phase entries.
- Avoids premature **disclosure of invention** in countries where the applicant does not plan to file.

#### Model Answer for (b) — Budapest Treaty

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Budapest Treaty on the International Recognition of the Deposit of Microorganisms** was signed on **April 28, 1977** and entered into force on **August 19, 1980**. It is administered by WIPO. India acceded to the treaty in **2001**.

**Background and Need (2 marks):**
Patent applications involving **microorganisms** (genetic engineering, fermentation, biotechnology) require **physical deposit** of the biological material so that any person skilled in the art can reproduce the invention. Without an international system, the inventor would have to deposit the same microorganism in **depository institutions in every country** where protection is sought — a costly and duplicative process. The Budapest Treaty eliminates this duplication.

**Core Mechanism (3 marks):**
1. The treaty establishes a network of **International Depositary Authorities (IDAs)** — recognized culture collections that accept deposits of microorganisms.
2. A deposit made in **any single IDA** is **recognized by all contracting states** — no need to redeposit elsewhere.
3. The **viability period** of the deposited microorganism must be at least **30 years**.
4. The IDAs in India include:
   - **Microbial Type Culture Collection (MTCC)**, IMTECH, Chandigarh
   - **National Collection of Industrial Microorganisms (NCIM)**, NCL, Pune

**Significance in Biological Patent Applications (1 mark):**
- Enables **global patent protection** for biotech inventions under the PCT.
- Fulfills the **sufficiency of disclosure** requirement — third parties can obtain the deposited sample for experimental purposes.
- Critical for inventions in **pharmaceuticals, agricultural biotechnology, industrial enzymes, and vaccine development**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Frequently Lost Marks:**
> 1. Confusing **PCT with Paris Convention** — PCT is a **filing system**, Paris is a **priority system**. They work together but are different.
> 2. Writing "30/31 months" without specifying **from priority date** — the examiner expects the full phrase.
> 3. Forgetting that **Budapest Treaty** is irrelevant for non-biological patents (mechanical, software, etc.).
> 4. Mis-stating the **IDA location in India** — many students write "Delhi" or "Mumbai" — it is **Chandigarh (MTCC)** and **Pune (NCIM)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Rapid Revision Notes for Module 1**

- **WIPO** is a **UN specialized agency**, established **1967** (effective 1970), HQ **Geneva**, India member since **1975**.
- **Paris Convention (1883)** is the **oldest IP treaty**, covers industrial property, **12 months priority for patents**, **6 months for trademarks/designs**.
- **Berne Convention (1886)** covers **copyright and literary/artistic works**; **automatic protection** (no formalities); term is **life + 50 years**; **180+ members**.
- **PCT (1970)** provides a **single international patent application** with **30/31 month deferral** of national phase entry; administered by WIPO; India member since **1998**.
- **Madrid System (1891/1989)** — international **trademark** registration; **India is NOT a member**.
- **Hague Agreement (1925/1999)** — international **industrial design** registration; **India is NOT a member**.
- **Budapest Treaty (1977)** — international recognition of **microorganism deposits** for patent purposes; **India is a member**; IDAs at **MTCC Chandigarh** and **NCIM Pune**.
- **Nice Agreement (1957)** — classification of **goods and services for trademark registration** (**45 classes**); India is a member.
- **Strasbourg Agreement (1971)** — International Patent Classification (IPC); India is a member.
- **Lisbon Agreement (1958)** — protection of **appellations of origin**; **India is NOT a member**.
- **Rome Convention (1961)** — protection of **performers, phonogram producers, and broadcasters**; **India is NOT a member**.
- **UPOV (1961)** — protection of **new plant varieties**; **India is NOT a member** (uses PPVFR Act 2001 instead).
- **WPPT (1996)** — digital-era performers' and phonogram producers' rights; India is a member.
- **Marrakesh Treaty (2013)** — facilitates access to published works for visually impaired; India is a member.
- **TRIPS (1994)** — **Trade-Related Aspects of IP Rights**; under the **WTO**; **legally binding**; **trade-sanction-backed enforcement** via DSB; **patent term minimum 20 years** (Art. 33); India is a **founding WTO member** (1995).
- The **three pillars** of global IP are: **Paris (industrial property substance) + Berne (copyright substance, no formalities) + TRIPS (enforcement)**.
- **Two fundamental principles** of all major IP conventions: **National Treatment** and **Right of Priority / Automatic Protection**.
- **India is a member of**: WIPO, Paris, Berne, PCT, TRIPS (via WTO), Nice, Strasbourg, Budapest, WPPT, Marrakesh.
- **India is NOT a member of**: Madrid, Hague, Lisbon, Rome, UPOV.
- **Patent term (universal minimum under TRIPS) = 20 years from filing date.**
- **Berne copyright term (universal minimum) = Life + 50 years.**
- **Priority period for patents = 12 months; trademarks and designs = 6 months.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:**
> If the question is worth **7 marks**, mention **at least 3–4 well-explained points with examples**. If worth **14 marks**, structure as **(a) and (b)** sub-parts and use **headings + sub-points + examples + statutory references** (e.g., "Article 4 of the Paris Convention"). Examiners reward **structured answers with explicit article/section references**.

---
