## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**"What is the WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT) 1996? State its significance in the digital age."**

**Model Answer:**
The WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT) is a special international treaty under the Berne Convention, adopted at the **WIPO Diplomatic Conference in Geneva on December 20, 1996**, and entered into force on **March 6, 2002**. It is administered by the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)** and consists of 25 substantive articles.

Its **significance in the digital age** is to extend and clarify copyright protection in the digital and networked environment. The WCT explicitly addresses the protection of **computer programs** (as literary works), **databases**, the **right of distribution**, the **right of rental**, and crucially the **right of communication to the public** (including on-demand "making available" right). It also mandates protection against the **circumvention of technological measures** (TPMs) and unauthorized tampering with **rights management information (RMI)**. Thus, WCT is rightly called the "Internet Treaty" because it adapts the 1886 Berne framework to the realities of the 21st-century digital economy.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**"Explain the relationship between the WCT 1996 and the Berne Convention 1886."**

**Model Answer:**
The WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT) 1996 is explicitly structured as a **"special agreement"** under the Berne Convention 1886, as declared in its **Article 1**. This relationship means:

1. **Building, not Replacing:** WCT does not replace Berne. It supplements and strengthens Berne for the digital era.
2. **Mandatory Compliance:** Any contracting party to the WCT must comply with the **substantive provisions of Articles 1 to 21 and the Appendix of the Berne Convention**, in addition to the obligations in the WCT itself.
3. **No Prejudicial Effect:** WCT does not reduce the existing obligations of contracting parties under Berne, TRIPS, or the WPPT.
4. **Inter-Treaty Consistency:** Where WCT and Berne use the same term (e.g., "reproduction," "communication"), they must be interpreted consistently.

In short, WCT operates as a **digital-era upgrade of Berne**, layered on top of the older framework to address new technological challenges that the 1886 drafters could not have foreseen.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **substantive copyright protection standards** introduced by Articles 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8 of the WIPO Copyright Treaty 1996. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The substantive protection standards of WCT (Articles 4 to 8) are the operational heart of the treaty:

**Article 4 – Computer Programs:**
Computer programs are protected as **"literary works"** within the meaning of **Article 2 of the Berne Convention**. The protection applies regardless of the mode or form of expression — covering **source code, object code, microcode, and any intermediate form**. This article settled the long-standing debate over whether software qualifies for copyright.

**Article 5 – Compilations of Data (Databases):**
The WCT explicitly extends copyright to **compilations of data or other material**, in any form, that constitute intellectual creations by virtue of their **selection or arrangement**. However, this protection covers **only the structure, selection, and arrangement** — not the underlying data itself. This was critical for the emerging digital database industry (e.g., search engines, online libraries).

**Article 6 – Right of Distribution:**
Authors are granted the **exclusive right to authorize the making available of the original and copies of their works to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership**. This right is subject to the **right of exhaustion (first sale doctrine)** — once a copy is sold with the author's authorization, the buyer may resell or otherwise dispose of that specific copy.

**Article 7 – Right of Rental:**
Authors of computer programs, cinematographic works, and works embodied in phonograms have the **exclusive right to authorize commercial rental** of their works. This is significant because the rental market for software, movies, and music cassettes/CDs had become a major source of revenue (and a target for piracy).

**Article 8 – Right of Communication to the Public:**
This is the **most important article for the digital age**. Authors have the **exclusive right to authorize any communication of their works to the public, by wire or wireless means**, including the **"making available" of works in such a way that members of the public may access them from a place and at a time individually chosen by them**. This on-demand access right is the legal foundation of **streaming services, app stores, online libraries, and digital content platforms**.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **enforcement provisions** under Articles 11 and 12 of the WCT 1996. Why are they important for digital rights management? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Articles 11 and 12 of the WCT are the **enforcement backbone** of the treaty in the digital era. They are necessary because copyright law alone was insufficient to protect works in a world where users could break encryption or strip metadata.

**Article 11 – Protection of Technological Measures (TPMs):**
Contracting parties must provide **adequate legal protection and effective remedies** against the circumvention of **effective technological measures** used by authors in connection with the exercise of their rights, and against the **manufacture and distribution of devices** primarily designed to circumvent such measures.

Examples of technological measures include:
- Encryption of DVDs (CSS, AACS).
- DRM locks on e-books (Adobe Digital Editions).
- Access control systems on streaming platforms.

The legal protection of TPMs ensures that breaking a digital lock to access a copyrighted work is **not just a technical violation** but a **legally actionable offense**.

**Article 12 – Rights Management Information (RMI):**
Contracting parties must provide adequate and effective remedies against persons who knowingly **remove or alter electronic rights management information** or **distribute works knowing that RMI has been removed or altered** without authorization.

RMI includes metadata such as:
- The author's name.
- Copyright notice and year.
- Terms of license (e.g., "All Rights Reserved" or "Creative Commons BY-NC").
- Identifying numbers or codes linking to licensing databases.

**Importance for Digital Rights Management (DRM):**
1. **Anti-Piracy Deterrent:** Articles 11 and 12 create legal teeth for DRM systems. Without them, technical locks could be broken with no legal consequence.
2. **License Integrity:** RMI protection ensures that users cannot strip licensing information to misrepresent authorized uses.
3. **Foundation for National Laws:** These articles formed the basis of the **DMCA (USA, 1998)**, the **EU Copyright Directive (2001/29/EC)**, the **Information Technology Act (India, 2000, amended 2012)**, and similar laws worldwide.
4. **Commercial Incentive:** By protecting TPMs and RMI, the treaty incentivizes copyright holders to invest in digital distribution channels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Part B Question A:**
> 1. **Article 8 phrasing is critical.** Do not write "right to broadcast" or "right to stream" — the precise WCT phrase is "right of communication to the public, including the making available of works." Marks are deducted for imprecise terminology.
> 2. **Do not confuse Article 6 (Distribution) with Article 7 (Rental).** Distribution is about selling/transferring ownership; rental is about temporary commercial use for money.
> 3. **TPM and RMI are different.** TPM (Art. 11) protects the lock; RMI (Art. 12) protects the label. Confusing these is a common error.
> 4. **Do not claim WCT replaced Berne.** WCT is a "special agreement" under Berne — it supplements, not substitutes.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2022]`

**(a)** Describe the **background, adoption, and entry into force** of the WIPO Copyright Treaty 1996. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Background:**
The 1980s and 1990s witnessed a technological revolution with the rise of the personal computer, the internet, and digital media. Existing international copyright treaties — primarily the **Berne Convention (1886)** for substantive protection and the **TRIPS Agreement (1994)** for enforcement — were drafted in an era before digital technology. As a result, several gaps emerged:
- The status of **computer programs** as literary works was debated.
- **Databases** had uncertain protection.
- The act of **"making available" works on the internet** was not explicitly covered.
- Digital rights management (DRM) had no international legal framework.

**Adoption:**
To address these gaps, WIPO convened a **Diplomatic Conference on Copyright and Neighboring Rights** in **Geneva, Switzerland**, from **December 2 to December 20, 1996**. After three weeks of intense negotiation among delegates from over 160 countries, two treaties were adopted:
1. **WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT)** — for authors of literary and artistic works.
2. **WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty (WPPT)** — for performers and producers of phonograms.

The WCT was formally adopted on **December 20, 1996**, and was opened for signature.

**Entry into Force:**
Under **Article 18 of the WCT**, the treaty enters into force **three months after 30 instruments of ratification or accession** are deposited with the Director General of WIPO. This threshold was met, and the WCT entered into force on **March 6, 2002**.

Since then, the treaty has been acceded to by **over 100 contracting parties**, including the European Union, the United States, Japan, India, China, and the United Kingdom.

---

**(b)** Critically analyze the **rights of distribution, rental, and communication to the public** under WCT 1996, with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The economic rights of distribution, rental, and communication are central to the WCT's role in the digital economy. A critical analysis reveals their interplay and practical significance.

**Right of Distribution (Article 6):**
- Authors have the exclusive right to authorize the **making available of the original and copies of their works to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership**.
- This right is **subject to the principle of exhaustion (first-sale doctrine)** — once a copy is sold with the author's authorization, the right holder cannot object to its subsequent resale.
- *Example:* A bookstore that lawfully purchases a novel from a publisher can resell it without seeking the publisher's permission. However, the publisher controls the initial distribution channel.

**Right of Rental (Article 7):**
- Authors of **(a) computer programs, (b) cinematographic works, and (c) works embodied in phonograms** have the exclusive right to authorize **commercial rental** of their works.
- For other categories of works (e.g., books), the rental right applies only when the commercial rental has led to widespread copying that materially impairs the author's reproduction right.
- *Example:* A video rental store (e.g., the now-defunct Blockbuster) needs authorization from the copyright owner of a movie to legally rent it.

**Right of Communication to the Public (Article 8):**
- The most consequential right in the digital era, it grants authors the **exclusive right to authorize any communication of their works to the public by wire or wireless means**, including the **on-demand "making available" right**.
- The phrase "from a place and at a time individually chosen by them" is the **operative definition of "on-demand" services** like streaming.
- *Example:* A music streaming service like Spotify must obtain a license under Article 8 to make a song "available" to subscribers who can play it whenever they choose.

**Critical Analysis:**
- The three rights are **complementary, not exclusive**. A single act (e.g., downloading a movie) can implicate the reproduction right, the distribution right, and the communication right simultaneously.
- The **first-sale doctrine (exhaustion)** is a major limitation on the distribution right, balancing the author's economic interests with the free movement of lawfully acquired goods.
- The **on-demand making available clause** in Article 8 is the most often litigated provision, with cases like **Viacom v. YouTube (USA)** and **Svensson v. Retriever (EU)** revolving around its interpretation.
- Critics argue that the WCT's strong protection of TPMs (Article 11) may **override the three-step test (Article 10)** in practice, but the Agreed Statements clarify that exceptions should remain consistent with the test.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Part B Question B:**
> 1. **Do not state that the WCT entered into force on December 20, 1996.** That is the date of *adoption*; it entered into force on **March 6, 2002**.
> 2. **Diplomatic Conference location:** Must be specified as **Geneva, Switzerland**. Do not write "WIPO headquarters" alone — be precise.
> 3. **Article 6 is NOT absolute.** Always mention the **right of exhaustion (first-sale doctrine)**. Marks are deducted for ignoring this limitation.
> 4. **Article 7 applies to specific categories:** (a) computer programs, (b) cinematographic works, (c) phonograms. Do not generalize it to all works.
> 5. **Article 8 "making available"** is *included within* the broader right of communication — do not treat them as separate rights.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

#### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Full Name:** WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT) — *not* "World Copyright Treaty."
- **Date of Adoption:** **December 20, 1996**, at the Diplomatic Conference in **Geneva, Switzerland**.
- **Date of Entry into Force:** **March 6, 2002** (after 30 ratifications).
- **Number of Articles:** **25 substantive articles** + Preamble.
- **Nature:** A **special agreement** under the **Berne Convention 1886** (Article 1).
- **Administering Body:** **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)**, Geneva.
- **Companion Treaty:** **WPPT 1996** (WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty). Together they are the **"WIPO Internet Treaties."**

#### Key Article-Wise Memory Points

- **Art. 1:** WCT is a special agreement under Berne.
- **Art. 4:** Computer programs = literary works (any form of expression).
- **Art. 5:** Databases protected for selection/arrangement, not underlying data.
- **Art. 6:** Distribution right + first-sale doctrine.
- **Art. 7:** Rental right for software, films, and music.
- **Art. 8:** "Making available" right for on-demand access — the digital heart of WCT.
- **Art. 9:** Software protected for at least 50 years.
- **Art. 10:** Three-step test for limitations and exceptions.
- **Art. 11:** Anti-circumvention of TPMs.
- **Art. 12:** Protection of RMI (metadata).

#### Two Agreed Statements to Remember

1. **On Article 6/7:** The "first-sale doctrine" exhaustion applies to physical copies; the right of distribution via transmission is not exhausted by online transmission.
2. **On Article 10:** Limitations and exceptions in the digital environment should remain consistent with the three-step test.

#### Common Confusions to Avoid

- **WCT ≠ Berne Convention.** WCT is a *supplement*, not a replacement.
- **WCT ≠ WPPT.** WCT is for authors; WPPT is for performers and phonogram producers.
- **"Internet Treaties"** = WCT + WPPT, not WCT alone.
- **Article 8** is about *communication to the public* (including making available) — not just "broadcasting."
- **Article 11** protects the *lock* (TPM); **Article 12** protects the *label* (RMI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, always structure your answer as: (1) Background/Context (2 marks), (2) Key Article-by-Article Explanation (8 marks), (3) Significance/Critique/Examples (2 marks), (4) Conclusion (2 marks). Use **underlined headings** and **bullet points** for readability — KTU examiners reward clear presentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hague Agreement 1925
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hague Agreement 1925

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hague Agreement Concerning the International Registration of Industrial Designs** is a multilateral international treaty originally signed in **The Hague, Netherlands, on 6 November 1925**. It establishes a unified procedural mechanism whereby an applicant can obtain protection for an **industrial design** in multiple countries (Contracting Parties) through a **single international application** filed with the **International Bureau of the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)** in Geneva, Switzerland.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *The Hague Agreement is the international legal framework that allows for the simultaneous protection of industrial designs in multiple jurisdictions through a centralized registration procedure administered by WIPO, eliminating the need to file separate national applications in each country.*

The original 1925 text has undergone **three substantive revisions**:
- **London Act (1934)**
- **Lisbon Act (1958)** *(declared a closed Act in 2016)*
- **Geneva Act (1999)** *(the current, sole operative Act)*

| Act Name | Year | Status (as of 2024) |
|---|---|---|
| The Hague Act | **1925** | Replaced |
| London Act | **1934** | Replaced |
| Lisbon Act | **1958** | Closed (new accessions ceased) |
| Geneva Act | **1999** | **Currently Operative** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a designer based in India who has created a beautiful ergonomic chair. Without the Hague Agreement, to protect your chair's design in 30 countries, you would have to:
- Hire 30 different lawyers,
- File 30 separate applications,
- Translate documents into 30 different languages,
- Pay 30 different fees,
- Track 30 separate renewal dates.

**The Hague Agreement acts like a "global umbrella."** Instead of opening 30 bank accounts in 30 countries, you open **one international account** at WIPO. You submit one design, pay one fee, and WIPO "forwards" your application to each member country you designate, just as a post office forwards a single letter to multiple addresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy:** Think of the Hague Agreement like a **multi-utility payment app** (e.g., Paytm or Google Pay) — one app, one login, but you can pay electricity bills in multiple states or even international vendors. The Hague Agreement = one international application paying design "bills" in multiple countries.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statutory Metrics of the Hague System:**
> - **Initial Protection Period:** **5 years**
> - **Maximum Total Protection:** Up to **15 years** (renewable in 5-year tranches, depending on national law)
> - **Deferment of Publication Period:** Up to **30 months** (under the Geneva Act)
> - **Application Languages:** English, French, or Spanish
> - **Minimum Number of Designs per Application:** **1**
> - **Maximum Number of Designs per Application:** **100** (under Geneva Act)
> - **International Bureau Headquarters:** **Geneva, Switzerland (WIPO)**

---

## 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Alignment

| KTU Bloom Level | Mapping |
|---|---|
| **CO 1 / K2 (Understand)** | Explain the origin, purpose, and structure of the Hague Agreement |
| **CO 1 / K3 (Apply)** | Identify the procedural steps for filing international design applications |
| **CO 2 / K4 (Analyze)** | Compare the 1925 Original Act with the Geneva Act 1999 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution and Historical Timeline

The Hague Agreement was born out of the **post-WWI industrial harmonization movement**. European nations recognized that fragmented design laws were a barrier to international trade.

### 2.1.1 Phase 1 — The 1925 Original Act
- Conceived at the **Paris Industrial Property Conference** (1883, Paris Convention provided the parent framework).
- Drafted and finalized in **The Hague in 1925**.
- Limited scope: required a prior national or trademark registration in the country of origin before international filing.
- Cumbersome: maximum 6 reproductions required.

### 2.1.2 Phase 2 — The 1934 London Act
- Loosened the **"prior registration"** requirement.
- Extended deferment of publication to **6 months**.
- Increased number of reproductions.

### 2.1.3 Phase 3 — The 1958 Lisbon Act
- Introduced a **15-year** term of protection (renewable in 5-year periods).
- Allowed applications without prior national filing.
- Enlarged membership (Portugal, Spain, France, Germany, Italy, etc.).
- Now a **closed Act** — no new countries can join.

### 2.1.4 Phase 4 — The 1999 Geneva Act *(CURRENT)*
- Major overhaul to align with **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**.
- Allows electronic filing.
- Permits **deferment of publication up to 30 months**.
- Eliminates mandatory prior national filing completely.
- Broadened to **100 designs per application**.
- **India is NOT yet a member**, but the Madrid Protocol model suggests future accession.

---

## 2.2 Salient Features (Geneva Act, 1999)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operational Features:**
> 1. **International Bureau (WIPO)** — Centralized registry and administrator.
> 2. **Common Regulations** — Standardized procedural rules for all Contracting Parties.
> 3. **Designated Contracting Parties** — Applicant chooses which member states to seek protection in.
> 4. **Independent National Protection** — Each designated state grants/refuses protection according to its own substantive law.
> 5. **Renewal Mechanism** — Centralized renewal through WIPO.

---

## 2.3 Conditions for Filing a Hague Application

Under the Geneva Act, the applicant must satisfy **one** of the following:

| Condition | Description |
|---|---|
| **Article 3(1)(i)** | Habitual residence in a Contracting Party |
| **Article 3(1)(ii)** | Industrial/commercial establishment in a Contracting Party |
| **Article 3(1)(iii)** | National of a Contracting Party |
| **Article 3(1)(iv)** | Habitual residence in a member of the Paris Union (1883) |
| **Article 3(1)(v)** | Industrial/commercial establishment in a Paris Union member |
| **Article 3(1)(vi)** | National of a Paris Union member |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Recall:** The qualifying criteria cascade is **Habitation → Business → Nationality**, applicable either to the Hague Union OR the Paris Union.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

While the Hague Agreement is not a "mathematical" topic, KTU board papers often test the **"Period × Fee × Country"** parameters. The following tables summarize the **operational equation**.

### Table 2.A — Period & Renewal Math

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{renewal}} \quad \text{where} \quad T_{\text{renewal}} = 5 \text{ years}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Initial registration period | $T_{\text{init}}$ | **5 years** |
| Each renewal block | $T_{r}$ | **5 years** |
| Maximum permitted renewals (Geneva Act) | $n_{\text{max}}$ | **2** (max total 15 yrs) |
| Maximum deferment of publication | $D_{\max}$ | **30 months** |

### Table 2.B — Fee Computation

$$
\text{Total Fee} = \underbrace{F_{\text{basic}}}_{\text{Single Design}} + \underbrace{F_{\text{std-design}} \times (N-1)}_{\text{Additional Designs}} + \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{k} F_{\text{designation},j}}_{\text{Designated States}} + F_{\text{pub-deferment}}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $F_{\text{basic}}$ | Base fee for first design |
| $F_{\text{std-design}}$ | Standard fee for each additional design |
| $N$ | Total number of designs in application |
| $F_{\text{designation},j}$ | Designation fee for $j$-th designated state |
| $k$ | Number of designated states |
| $F_{\text{pub-deferment}}$ | Optional 30-month publication deferment fee |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid pipe character!** When indicating "or", write $\vert$ or "OR" — never use bare `|` inside a markdown table.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

For B.Tech students, the Hague Agreement is highly relevant for:

1. **Industrial Design Protection** in consumer electronics, automotive, furniture, and consumer goods.
2. **Startups and Innovation** — designers can protect designs in **EU, Japan, USA, Switzerland, and the UK** through a single filing.
3. **Cost Reduction** — saves **40–70%** in legal costs compared to parallel national filings.
4. **Strategic IP Portfolio Management** — centralizes renewal tracking.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Note:** The Hague Agreement handles only the **formalities (procedure)** of registration. **Substantive examination (novelty, originality)** is conducted by each **national office independently**. A Hague registration can be **refused** by individual national offices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Procedural Workflow (Geneva Act, 1999)

The Hague filing process is a **multi-stage pipeline**. Each step has a defined legal effect.

### Step 1 — Determine Eligibility (Article 3)

The applicant must establish a **qualifying criterion**:

$$
\text{Qualify} \iff \big[ (\text{Residency} \lor \text{Business} \lor \text{Nationality}) \land \text{Country} \in \mathcal{U}_{\text{Hague}} \cup \mathcal{U}_{\text{Paris}} \big]
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{U}_{\text{Hague}}$ = set of Hague Union Contracting Parties
- $\mathcal{U}_{\text{Paris}}$ = set of Paris Convention Union Members

**Conversion logic:** Eligibility is binary — either the applicant meets at least one criterion from Article 3(1) OR the application is invalid.

---

### Step 2 — Preparation of the International Application

The applicant compiles:

$$
A = \{ \text{Reproductions}, \text{Description}, \text{Claim of Priority}, \text{Designation List}, \text{Appointed Representative} \}
$$

| Element | Mandatory? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Reproduction of the design | **Yes** | Photographs, drawings, or 3D renderings |
| Article of indication | **Yes** | Form DM/1 (international application form) |
| Designation of Contracting Parties | **Yes** | Minimum 1 |
| Description | Optional | Brief text explaining features |
| Claim of priority | Optional | Under Paris Convention Article 4 |
| Deferment of publication request | Optional | Up to 30 months |

---

### Step 3 — Filing Routes

$$
\text{Application} \longrightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Route 1: Direct E-filing via WIPO eHague portal} \\
\text{Route 2: Filing through a national IP office (Indirect)} \\
\text{Route 3: Filing through a registered IP attorney}
\end{cases}
$$

**Conversion logic:** All three routes converge at the **International Bureau**, which performs only **formal examination** (not novelty).

---

### Step 4 — Formal Examination by WIPO

The International Bureau checks for:
1. Payment of fees (Fees Rule 13),
2. Correct filing language (English/French/Spanish),
3. Completeness of prescribed elements,
4. Naming of at least one designated state.

$$
\text{If Formal OK} \Rightarrow A_{\text{recorded}} \text{ with International Registration Number (IRN)}
$$

$$
\text{If Formal NOT OK} \Rightarrow A_{\text{deficient}} \text{ with 3-month correction window}
$$

---

### Step 5 — International Registration & Publication

The International Registration becomes effective on the **date of receipt** by WIPO. Publication occurs:
- **Immediately**, OR
- **Deferred up to 30 months** (if deferment requested at filing).

$$
\text{Publication Date} = \text{Receiving Date} + d \quad \text{where} \quad 0 \leq d \leq 30 \text{ months}
$$

---

### Step 6 — National Phase Substantive Examination

Each designated state's national office examines the design under its own substantive law:

$$
\text{Examination Outcome}_i =
\begin{cases}
\text{Grant of Protection (Class A)} \\
\text{Refusal} \Rightarrow \text{Notification to IB} \\
\text{Temporary Refusal (Class B)}
\end{cases}
$$

where $i$ indexes the designated state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Substantive Grounds for Refusal** (national law):
> - Lack of novelty
> - Lack of individual character
> - Functional/utilitarian nature (not a design)
> - Conflict with public order/morality
> - Prior conflicting trademark or design

---

### Step 7 — Renewal Mechanism

$$
T_{\text{current}} = T_{\text{previous}} + 5 \text{ years}
$$

Renewals are filed **directly with WIPO**. If unpaid, the international registration **lapses centrally**, terminating in all designated states simultaneously.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of the Four Acts

| Parameter | 1925 Hague | 1934 London | 1958 Lisbon | 1999 Geneva |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Prior national filing required | **Yes** | Yes (relaxed) | No | No |
| Initial protection period | 5 yrs | 5 yrs | 5 yrs | **5 yrs** |
| Maximum protection | 15 yrs | 15 yrs | 15 yrs | **15 yrs** |
| Deferment of publication | None | 6 months | 12 months | **30 months** |
| Number of designs per app | 1 | Limited | Up to 50 | **Up to 100** |
| E-filing | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Membership acceptance | Closed | Closed | Closed | **Open** |
| Current status | Replaced | Replaced | Closed | **Active** |

---

## 3.3 Comparative Matrix — Hague Agreement vs. Paris Convention

| Feature | Hague Agreement | Paris Convention |
|---|---|---|
| Year of Adoption | 1925 | 1883 |
| Administering Body | WIPO (since 1967) | WIPO |
| Subject Matter | Industrial Designs only | Patents, Marks, Designs, etc. |
| Filing Mechanism | Single international application | National + Priority Claim |
| Function | Direct international registration | Right of Priority only |
| Mandatory Examination | No (formal only) | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Paris Convention gives you a **priority right** (filing date). Hague Agreement gives you an **international registration right** (substantive protection).

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Derivation — Cost Reduction Comparison

Suppose a designer wants to protect 1 design in **10 countries**, with an initial **5-year** protection term.

**Without Hague Agreement (Parallel National Filing):**

$$
C_{\text{national}} = \sum_{j=1}^{10} \left[ F_{\text{filing},j} + C_{\text{attorney},j} + C_{\text{translation},j} \right]
$$

**With Hague Agreement (Single International Filing):**

$$
C_{\text{Hague}} = F_{\text{basic}} + \sum_{j=1}^{10} F_{\text{designation},j} + F_{\text{e-filing}}
$$

**Cost Savings Percentage:**

$$
\text{Savings}\,(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{C_{\text{Hague}}}{C_{\text{national}}}\right) \times 100
$$

For typical 10-country filings in WIPO fee schedules, this yields approximately:

$$
\boxed{\text{Savings}\,(\%) \approx 45\% \text{ to } 70\%}
$$

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Filing Flow (Numbered Algorithm for Process Clarity)

1. **Eligibility Verification** — confirm applicant is a Hague/Paris Union resident or national.
2. **Design Preparation** — high-quality reproductions (min 300 DPI).
3. **Select Designated States** — pay individual designation fees.
4. **E-File via WIPO eHague Portal** — language EN/FR/ES.
5. **WIPO Formal Examination** — within ~1–3 months.
6. **International Registration Date** — same as filing date if no deficiencies.
7. **Publication** — immediate OR deferred.
8. **National Substantive Examination** — each designated office acts independently.
9. **Grant or Refusal** — refusal period typically **6–12 months** post-publication.
10. **Renewal every 5 years** — directly through WIPO.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** A common mistake is writing *"Hague registration gives automatic worldwide protection."* This is **WRONG**. Protection is granted only in **designated states**, and only if **not refused** by those states.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of the Hague System (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applicant: Designer or Company] --> B{Eligible under Article 3?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Prepare International Application Form DM/1]
    B -- No --> B1[Application Rejected at Threshold]
    C --> D{Filing Route Selected}
    D -- E-filing --> E1[WIPO eHague Portal]
    D -- Indirect --> E2[National IP Office Forwarding]
    D -- Attorney --> E3[Registered IP Attorney]
    E1 --> F[International Bureau of WIPO Geneva]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Formal Examination under Common Regulations]
    G --> H{Formalities Satisfied?}
    H -- Yes --> I[International Registration Granted: IRN Allocated]
    H -- No --> I1[3-Month Correction Period]
    I1 --> G
    I --> J{Deferment of Publication?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Hold Publication up to 30 Months]
    J -- No --> L[Immediate Publication in International Designs Bulletin]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Notification to Designated Contracting Parties]
    M --> N1[Designated State 1 National Office]
    M --> N2[Designated State 2 National Office]
    M --> N3[Designated State k National Office]
    N1 --> O1{Substantive Examination}
    N2 --> O2{Substantive Examination}
    N3 --> O3{Substantive Examination}
    O1 -- Granted --> P1[Protection in State 1]
    O1 -- Refused --> Q1[Notification of Refusal to IB and Applicant]
    O2 -- Granted --> P2[Protection in State 2]
    O2 -- Refused --> Q2[Notification of Refusal to IB and Applicant]
    O3 -- Granted --> P3[Protection in State k]
    O3 -- Refused --> Q3[Notification of Refusal to IB and Applicant]
    P1 --> R[Renewal Every 5 Years via WIPO Centralized System]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    Q1 --> S[Optional Appeal at National Level]
    Q2 --> S
    Q3 --> S
```

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy of the Four Acts

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FOUNDATION[Paris Convention 1883]
        ROOT[Right of Priority and National Treatment]
    end
    FOUNDATION --> A1[Hague Act 1925]
    A1 --> A2[London Act 1934]
    A2 --> A3[Lisbon Act 1958]
    A3 --> A4[Geneva Act 1999]
    A4 --> Z[Currently Operative Hague System]
    A3 -.Closed.-> X[No New Accessions After 2016]
    A4 --> WIPOFLOW[WIPO International Bureau Manages Registrations]
```

---

## 4.3 Workflow Block Diagram — Renewal Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[International Registration Active] --> B{Year 5 Reached?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Renewal Fee Paid to WIPO]
    C --> D{Fee Received on Time?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Renewal Successful: New 5-Year Term Begins]
    D -- No --> F[6-Month Grace Period]
    F --> G{Paid Within Grace?}
    G -- Yes --> E
    G -- No --> H[Registration Lapses Globally]
    E --> B
```

---

## 4.4 Rights Granted Under the Hague System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H[Hague International Registration] --> R1[Right to Use the Design in Designated States]
    H --> R2[Right to Prevent Unauthorized Copying]
    H --> R3[Right to License or Assign the Design]
    H --> R4[Right to Claim Damages for Infringement]
    H --> R5[Right to Maintain Protection up to 15 Years]
    R5 --> R51[5-Year Initial Term]
    R5 --> R52[5-Year First Renewal]
    R5 --> R53[5-Year Second Renewal]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed (`A1`, `A2`, `R51`, etc.) to comply with compiler safeguards. No reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` are used as standalone node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*"Define the Hague Agreement and state its primary purpose in international design protection."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Hague Agreement Concerning the International Registration of Industrial Designs is a multilateral treaty signed in **The Hague on 6 November 1925** and administered by the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)**. Its primary purpose is to provide a **single, centralized procedure** for the international registration of industrial designs, whereby an applicant can file **one international application** designating multiple Contracting Parties, instead of filing separate national applications in each country. This significantly reduces **cost, time, and procedural complexity**, while promoting global harmonization of design protection.

*(Marks distribution: 1 — definition, 1 — purpose, 1 — administrative body and reduction of complexity)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*"List any three salient features of the Geneva Act 1999 of the Hague Agreement."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Three salient features of the **Geneva Act (1999)** are:
> 1. **Single international application** with effect in multiple Contracting Parties.
> 2. **Deferment of publication** allowed for a maximum period of **30 months**.
> 3. **Up to 100 industrial designs** can be included in a single international application, with electronic filing through the WIPO eHague system.

*(1 mark per correct feature)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay-Level Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the historical evolution of the Hague Agreement, detailing each revision from 1925 to the Geneva Act 1999. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the procedural steps for filing an international design application under the Geneva Act, with reference to the role of WIPO and designated Contracting Parties. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Historical Evolution (7 Marks)**

The Hague Agreement was conceived in the early 20th century as part of the international harmonization of **industrial property rights** following the **Paris Convention of 1883**.

1. **The Hague Act (1925):** The original text was signed on **6 November 1925** in The Hague, Netherlands. It established the framework for international registration of industrial designs. However, it required **prior national filing** in the country of origin and limited the number of reproductions.

2. **The London Act (1934):** Revised the original text to **relax the prior-filing requirement**, introduced a **6-month deferment of publication**, and widened the procedural scope.

3. **The Lisbon Act (1958):** Introduced a unified **15-year term** (renewable in 5-year tranches), allowed applications without prior national filing, and broadened membership. It is now a **closed Act** — no new countries may join.

4. **The Geneva Act (1999):** The current operative Act. It aligned the Hague system with the **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**, allowed **e-filing**, extended publication deferment to **30 months**, and permitted **up to 100 designs** per application.

> The evolution shows a clear trend toward **decentralized prior-filing requirements, electronic procedures, and increased procedural flexibility**, reflecting advances in international trade and technology.

---

**Part (b) — Procedural Steps (7 Marks)**

The procedural steps for filing under the Geneva Act are:

**Step 1 — Eligibility Check:** The applicant must have habitual residence, industrial/commercial establishment, or nationality in a Hague/Paris Union member.

**Step 2 — Preparation:** The applicant prepares Form DM/1 with reproductions of the design, designation list, and priority claim (if any).

**Step 3 — Filing:** The application is filed via (i) the **WIPO eHague portal**, (ii) a national IP office (indirect), or (iii) an IP attorney.

**Step 4 — Formal Examination:** WIPO performs **formal checks only** — language, fees, designations, and completeness.

**Step 5 — International Registration:** An **International Registration Number (IRN)** is allocated and published in the **International Designs Bulletin**.

**Step 6 — National Substantive Examination:** Each designated Contracting Party independently examines the design under its own substantive law, leading to grant or refusal.

**Step 7 — Renewal:** Renewals occur **every 5 years**, directly through WIPO.

> The **International Bureau of WIPO** acts as the central registry, while each **designated state** retains sovereignty over substantive grant. This **"administrative unity, substantive plurality"** model is the cornerstone of the Hague system.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare the provisions of the Hague Agreement 1925 with the Geneva Act 1999 in terms of protection period, deferment, and number of designs. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Examine the advantages and limitations of the Hague system for an Indian startup seeking design protection abroad. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)**

| Feature | Hague Act (1925) | Geneva Act (1999) |
|---|---|---|
| Prior national filing | Mandatory | Not required |
| Initial protection period | 5 years | 5 years |
| Maximum protection | 15 years | 15 years |
| Deferment of publication | Not available | Up to 30 months |
| Number of designs per application | 1 (single design only) | Up to 100 |
| Filing method | Paper only | E-filing available |
| Current status | Replaced | **Operative** |

The Geneva Act represents a **substantial modernization** of the original framework, accommodating the realities of **mass product design**, **electronic commerce**, and **globalized supply chains**.

---

**Part (b) — Hague System for Indian Startups (7 Marks)**

**Advantages:**

1. **Centralized Filing:** An Indian startup can protect its design in 70+ jurisdictions through a single application.
2. **Cost Efficiency:** Savings of approximately **45–70%** compared to parallel national filings.
3. **Renewal Centralization:** All renewals managed through WIPO, simplifying administration.
4. **Language Flexibility:** Applications accepted in English, French, or Spanish.
5. **Strategic Coverage:** Ideal for startups targeting EU, US, UK, Japan, and Switzerland markets.

**Limitations:**

1. **India is not yet a member** of the Hague Agreement. Indian applicants must rely on **Paris Convention priority** to extend protection abroad.
2. **No substantive novelty examination** by WIPO — risk of refusals at national level.
3. **Designation fees vary** by country and can become substantial for many designated states.
4. **Each designated state may impose its own formalities** post-registration.
5. **Hague registration does not bypass national substantive law** — including India's requirement that designs be registered under the **Designs Act, 2000** for domestic protection.

> Despite limitations, the Hague system remains the **most efficient route** for global design protection. Indian startups are advised to file a national design application first (under the Designs Act, 2000) and use Paris Convention priority to claim the same filing date internationally.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing Hague Agreement with Paris Convention.** Paris = *priority right only*. Hague = *substantive international registration*.
> 2. **Claiming "worldwide protection."** Wrong — only **designated states** are covered.
> 3. **Ignoring substantive examination.** The Hague does NOT check novelty; **national offices** do.
> 4. **Wrong administration.** Do not say "WTO administers Hague" — it is **WIPO**.
> 5. **Wrong current Act.** Saying "1925 Act is current" — incorrect; **Geneva Act 1999** is current.
> 6. **Forgetting the requirement of at least 1 Contracting Party** designation.
> 7. **Missing the role of Common Regulations** — they unify procedural rules across member states.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - Hague Agreement was signed on **6 November 1925** in **The Hague, Netherlands**.
> - Administered by **WIPO International Bureau** (Geneva, Switzerland).
> - Concerns **Industrial Designs only** (not patents, trademarks, or copyrights).
> - Four Acts: **Hague 1925**, **London 1934**, **Lisbon 1958** (closed), **Geneva 1999** (current).
> - Geneva Act key benefits: **e-filing**, **30-month deferment**, **up to 100 designs per application**.
> - Initial protection: **5 years**; renewable twice; max **15 years**.
> - Eligibility: habitual residence, business establishment, OR nationality in a Hague/Paris Union member.
> - WIPO does **formal examination only**; **novelty** examined by national offices.
> - **India is NOT a member** — must use Paris Convention priority.
> - The Hague system is **administratively unified but substantively plural** — each designated state applies its own law.
> - Renewal is **centralized through WIPO**, every 5 years.
> - Major refusal grounds at national level: **lack of novelty, lack of individual character, public order, prior conflicting rights**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TRIPS Agreement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** TRIPS Agreement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **TRIPS (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights)** is an international legal agreement administered by the **World Trade Organization (WTO)** that establishes **minimum standards** for the protection and enforcement of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) among member nations. It was negotiated during the **Uruguay Round (1986–1994)** of GATT and came into force on **1st January 1995**.

The TRIPS Agreement is codified under **Annex 1C of the Marrakesh Agreement (1994)**, which established the WTO. It is often described as the **most comprehensive multilateral treaty on intellectual property** ever concluded.

> [!NOTE] > **Key Statistic for KTU Exams:** TRIPS binds **164 WTO member states** (as of 2024), making it the single most influential IP harmonization treaty in history. Remember the figures: **7 categories of IP**, **20-year minimum patent term**, **50-year term for trademarks**, and **Berne Convention plus 10 years** for copyright.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you invented a brilliant new smartphone design. Without international IP rules, a factory in another country could simply copy your design, manufacture it cheaply, and sell it worldwide — leaving you with no legal recourse in that foreign market.

**TRIPS is like a "Global Rulebook for Inventors and Creators."**

Think of it this way:

| Without TRIPS | With TRIPS |
|---|---|
| Each country has its own weak/strong IP laws | All WTO members must follow a **minimum standard** |
| Copying a product abroad was legally easy | Counterfeiters face **enforced penalties** in every member country |
| Inventors feared to share technology globally | A **20-year patent** is guaranteed worldwide |
| Disputes had no global mechanism | **WTO Dispute Settlement Body (DSB)** resolves IP conflicts |

> [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** **"T-R-I-P-S"** — **T**rade, **R**ights, **I**ntellectual, **P**roperty, **S**tandards. Think of it as the "Universal Passport for IP Protection."

## 1.3 Why TRIPS Matters (Big Picture)

- **Bridges the gap** between trade and IP.
- Forces developing nations (including **India**) to upgrade IP laws.
- Introduces **enforcement obligations** (civil, criminal, border measures).
- Links IP disputes to **WTO trade sanctions** — the ultimate enforcement teeth.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Evolution — The Road to TRIPS

```
1947  → GATT Established (General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade)
1967  → WIPO Founded (World Intellectual Property Organization)
1970s → Paris & Berne Conventions exist, but enforcement is weak
1986  → Uruguay Round launched; IP added to trade agenda
1994  → TRIPS Agreement signed at Marrakesh, Morocco
1995  → TRIPS comes into force (1st January) along with WTO
2001  → Doha Declaration on TRIPS and Public Health (flexibility)
2017  → Amendment to Article 31bis (pharmaceutical exports)
```

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Why was TRIPS created?** Developed nations (USA, EU, Japan) lobbied heavily because their **knowledge-based industries** (pharma, software, entertainment) were losing billions to piracy in developing countries. They made IP a **trade issue** so that violations could trigger **trade retaliation**.

## 2.2 The 7 Pillars of TRIPS — Protected IP Categories

TRIPS mandates protection for the following 7 categories:

| S.No | IP Category | Key Provision | Minimum Term |
|------|-------------|---------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Copyright & Related Rights** | Articles 9–14 | Life + 50 years (or Berne + 10) |
| 2 | **Trademarks** | Articles 15–21 | **7 years (renewable indefinitely)** — registration valid min. 7 years |
| 3 | **Geographical Indications** | Articles 22–24 | **10 years** |
| 4 | **Industrial Designs** | Articles 25–26 | **10 years** (minimum protection) |
| 5 | **Patents** | Articles 27–34 | **20 years** from filing date |
| 6 | **Layout Designs of Integrated Circuits** | Articles 35–38 | **10 years** |
| 7 | **Undisclosed Information (Trade Secrets)** | Articles 39–40 | As long as it remains secret |

## 2.3 The Core Principles (Articles 1–8)

These are **examinable directly** under Module 1:

### 2.3.1 National Treatment (Article 3)
A WTO member must treat nationals of other members **no less favorably** than its own nationals with respect to IP protection.

### 2.3.2 Most-Favoured-Nation (MFN) Treatment (Article 4)
Any advantage given to nationals of one country must be extended to **all WTO members**.

### 2.3.3 Minimum Standards of Protection (Article 1.1)
Members **may** provide more extensive protection but **cannot** provide less than TRIPS prescribes.

### 2.3.4 Technology Transfer & Public Interest (Article 7 — "Objectives")
> *"Protection and enforcement of IP rights should contribute to the promotion of technological innovation and to the transfer and dissemination of technology..."*

### 2.3.5 Principle of Non-Discrimination (Article 8 — "Principles")
Members may adopt measures necessary to protect public health, nutrition, and public interest.

## 2.4 Key Articles — The 50-Year Trademark Term & Other Highlights

> [!NOTE] > **Note on Trademark Term:** While TRIPS Article 18 requires registration to be **renewable for at least 7-year periods**, the **Paris Convention** baseline plus the effective practice is **"indefinite renewability"** — making trademarks effectively **perpetual** if maintained.

| Article | Subject | KTU Quick Fact |
|---------|---------|----------------|
| Art. 27.1 | Patents must be available for **both products & processes** | Excludes only diagnostic, therapeutic, surgical methods, plants/animals (other than micro-organisms), and plant varieties |
| Art. 31 | "Other Use" of Patent without Authorization = **Compulsory Licensing** | Strict conditions apply |
| Art. 31bis (2005) | Export of pharmaceuticals under compulsory license | Crucial for generic drug exports |
| Art. 33 | Patent Term | **20 years** from filing |
| Art. 41–61 | Enforcement Obligations | Civil, criminal, border (customs) measures mandatory |
| Art. 64 | Disputes under TRIPS | Subject to **WTO DSB** procedures |

## 2.5 Flexibilities Built Into TRIPS

> [!TIP] > **High-Yield for KTU:** TRIPS is NOT rigid — it contains many **flexibilities** that developing countries like India have used:

1. **Compulsory Licensing (Art. 31)** — Government can license a patent without patent-holder's consent under specific conditions (public health, national emergency, anti-competitive practices).
2. **Parallel Imports (Art. 6)** — Members free to determine exhaustion regime.
3. **Bolar Exception** — Allows generic manufacturers to use patented inventions for regulatory approval before patent expiry.
4. **Transition Periods (Art. 65–66)** — Developing countries got until **2000**, LDCs until **2006**, later extended to **2034** for pharmaceuticals.
5. **Doha Declaration (2001)** — Reaffirmed TRIPS flexibilities; affirmed right to use compulsory licensing for public health.

## 2.6 TRIPS & India — Real-World Utility

India amended its **Patents Act, 1970** thrice to comply with TRIPS:
- **1999 Amendment** — Mailbox provisions (filing applications in product patent areas).
- **2002 Amendment** — 5-year Exclusive Marketing Rights (EMRs).
- **2005 Amendment** — Full TRIPS compliance; product patents granted in **pharma, chemicals, and agro-chemicals**.

The landmark case **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** tested India's **Section 3(d)** (which prevents **evergreening**) — the Supreme Court upheld the strict patentability standard, balancing TRIPS obligations with public health.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** For a B.Tech student, understanding TRIPS is critical because it directly impacts:
> - **Biotech & pharma patents** (Section 3(d) debates)
> - **Software patents** (computer programs as such are not patentable in India per TRIPS Art. 27)
> - **Semiconductor IP** (layout designs — important for VLSI students)
> - **Startups & tech transfer** (Article 7 obligations)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix — TRIPS Compliance Framework

## 3.1 The TRIPS Compliance Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

Since TRIPS is a legal-framework topic, the "derivation" here is the **logical flow a nation must follow to achieve TRIPS compliance**:

### Step 1 — Verify WTO Membership Status
A country must be a **WTO member** to be bound by TRIPS. Original WTO members (1995) had **1 year** to comply; developing countries had **5 years** (until 2000); LDCs had **11 years**.

### Step 2 — Map Existing National IP Laws vs. TRIPS Standards
Conduct a **gap analysis** comparing domestic statutes to the 7 IP categories.

### Step 3 — Amend National Legislation
Bring statutes up to TRIPS minimum standards (e.g., India's 2005 Patents Act).

### Step 4 — Establish Enforcement Mechanisms
Implement civil, administrative, criminal remedies + border measures.

### Step 5 — Notify WTO of Implementation
Notify the **TRIPS Council** of legislative changes.

### Step 6 — Periodic Review
Submit to **TRIPS Council reviews** and respond to bilateral pressure.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — TRIPS Article-Wise Quick Reference

| TRIPS Article | Subject | Mandatory / Directory | India Status |
|---------------|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| Art. 9–14 | Copyright | Mandatory | Copyright Act 1957 (amended 2012) |
| Art. 15–21 | Trademarks | Mandatory | Trade Marks Act 1999 |
| Art. 22–24 | GI | Mandatory | GI of Goods Act 1999 |
| Art. 25–26 | Industrial Designs | Mandatory | Designs Act 2000 |
| Art. 27–34 | Patents | Mandatory | Patents Act 1970 (amended 2005) |
| Art. 35–38 | IC Layout Designs | Mandatory | Semiconductor IC Layout Design Act 2000 |
| Art. 39–40 | Trade Secrets | Mandatory | Protected via contract/common law |
| Art. 41–61 | Enforcement | Mandatory | Through various statutes + courts |
| Art. 64 | WTO DSB Disputes | Mandatory | India bound |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Applying the TRIPS 20-Year Patent Rule

Let us analyze the patent term calculation:

$$\text{Patent Filing Date} = \text{Filed on 1st January 2010}$$

$$\text{Patent Term (TRIPS Art. 33)} = 20 \text{ years from filing date}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Expiry Date} &= \text{Filing Date} + 20 \text{ years} \\
&= 1^{\text{st}} \text{ January 2010} + 20 \text{ years} \\
&= 1^{\text{st}} \text{ January 2030}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Note:** In practice, renewal fees must be paid annually (in India: **Section 53 of Patents Act**), and non-payment leads to lapse. The **20-year term is non-extendable**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Compulsory Licensing Decision Tree (Article 31)

```
Patent exists & is in force?
        │
        ▼
Refusal to license at reasonable terms?
        │
        ▼
National emergency / public non-commercial use?
        │
   ┌────┴────┐
   YES       NO
   │          │
   ▼          ▼
Direct       Negotiation
government   attempt with
use allowed   patent holder
   │          │
   ▼          ▼
Notify       Compensation
patent       (adequate)
holder       royalty
```

> [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** For compulsory licensing questions, remember the **3 mandatory conditions**:
> 1. Prior negotiation attempt (except emergencies).
> 2. Adequate remuneration to patent holder.
> 3. Non-exclusive and non-assignable license.

---

## 3.5 Mathematical Representation of TRIPS Enforcement Effectiveness

Let us model enforcement strength mathematically (for analytical understanding):

$$E = (C \times A) + (B \times R) + (Cr \times P)$$

Where:
- $E$ = Enforcement Effectiveness Index
- $C$ = Civil remedies available
- $A$ = Average penalty amount
- $B$ = Border measures implemented
- $R$ = Seizure rate of counterfeits
- $Cr$ = Criminal penalties present
- $P$ = Probability of prosecution

> **Interpretation:** A TRIPS-compliant country must have **non-zero values** for ALL three components ($C$, $B$, $Cr$) — otherwise it fails the enforcement test under **Articles 41–61**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of the TRIPS Agreement (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WTO Marrakesh Agreement 1994] --> B[Annex 1A: GATT 1994]
    A --> C[Annex 1B: GATS]
    A --> D[Annex 1C: TRIPS Agreement]

    D --> F[Part II: Standards Concerning IP Availability, Scope and Use]
    D --> G[Part III: Enforcement of IP Rights]
    D --> H[Part IV: Acquisition and Maintenance of IP Rights]
    D --> I[Part V: Dispute Prevention and Settlement]
    D --> J[Part VI: Transitional Arrangements]
    D --> K[Part VII: Institutional Arrangements]
    D --> L[Part VIII: Final Provisions]
    F --> M[Copyright]
    F --> N[Trademarks]
    F --> O[GI]
    F --> P[Industrial Designs]
    F --> Q[Patents]
    F --> R[IC Layouts]
    F --> S[Undisclosed Info]

    G --> U[Border Measures]
    G --> V[Criminal Procedures]
```

## 4.2 The 7 IP Categories — Interaction Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[TRIPS Article 9-14] --> A1[Copyright]
    A[TRIPS Article 9-14] --> A2[Related Rights]
    B[TRIPS Article 15-21] --> B1[Trademarks]

    D[TRIPS Article 25-26] --> D1[Industrial Designs]

    E[TRIPS Article 27-34] --> E2[Process Patents]

    H[Berne Convention] -.Reference.-> A
    I[Paris Convention] -.Reference.-> B
    I[Paris Convention] -.Reference.-> D
    J[IPIC Treaty] -.Reference.-> F
```

## 4.3 TRIPS Dispute Settlement Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[IP Dispute Between Members] --> Consult[Consultations 60 days]
    Consult -->|Failed| Panel[WTO Panel Established]
    Panel --> Report[Panel Report]
    Report --> Appeal[Appellate Body Review]
    Appeal --> Final[Final Ruling]
    Final --> Implement[Implementation 15 months]
    Implement -->|Non-compliance| Retaliate[Trade Retaliation Sanctions]
    Retaliate --> Monitor[DSB Monitors Compliance]
    style Start fill:#ffe4b5
    style Retaliate fill:#ff7f7f
```

## 4.4 TRIPS Compliance Roadmap — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Step1[Step 1: WTO Membership] --> Step2[Step 2: Gap Analysis of National IP Laws]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Legislative Amendments]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Enforcement Infrastructure]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Notify TRIPS Council]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Periodic Review & Reporting]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Dispute Resolution via DSB if needed]
    Step1 -.Uses.-> WTO[WTO Membership Accession Protocol]
    Step3 -.Uses.-> Acts[Patents, Copyright, TM, GI, Designs Acts]
    Step4 -.Uses.-> Enforcement[Courts, Customs, Police]
    Step6 -.Uses.-> Council[TRIPS Council Meetings]
```

## 4.5 TRIPS Flexibilities — Decision Matrix

```mermaid

    Flex[TRIPS Flexibilities] --> L1[Compulsory Licensing Art 31]
    Flex --> L2[Parallel Imports Art 6]
    Flex --> L3[Bolar Exception]

    Flex --> L5[Doha Declaration 2001]
    Flex --> L6[Public Health Safeguards]

    L1 --> L1b[National Emergency]

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the TRIPS Agreement. When did it come into force and under which agreement was it negotiated?**

**Model Answer:**
TRIPS stands for **Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights**. It is an international agreement administered by the **World Trade Organization (WTO)** that sets minimum standards for the protection and enforcement of intellectual property rights among member nations.

TRIPS was negotiated during the **Uruguay Round (1986–1994)** of the **General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT)** and came into force on **1st January 1995**. It is codified as **Annex 1C of the Marrakesh Agreement (1994)**.

It currently binds **164 WTO member states** and covers **7 categories of intellectual property**: copyright, trademarks, geographical indications, industrial designs, patents, layout designs of integrated circuits, and undisclosed information (trade secrets). **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any three key principles of the TRIPS Agreement.**

**Model Answer:**
The three key principles of TRIPS are:

1. **National Treatment (Article 3):** A WTO member must treat nationals of other member countries no less favorably than its own nationals with respect to IP protection.

2. **Most-Favoured-Nation (MFN) Treatment (Article 4):** Any advantage or favor granted to nationals of one country must be extended to nationals of all WTO member countries.

3. **Minimum Standards of Protection (Article 1.1):** Members may provide more extensive IP protection but cannot provide less than the standards prescribed in TRIPS.

Additionally, TRIPS incorporates the **Berne Convention** for copyright and the **Paris Convention** for industrial property. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **7 categories of Intellectual Property** protected under the TRIPS Agreement. Mention the minimum term of protection for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

TRIPS, under **Part II (Articles 9–39)**, mandates minimum standards of protection for the following 7 categories of intellectual property:

**1. Copyright and Related Rights (Articles 9–14):**
TRIPS incorporates the **Berne Convention (1971)** for the protection of literary and artistic works. Computer programs are protected as **literary works**, and databases are protected as **compilations**. The minimum term is the life of the author plus **50 years** (or Berne + 10 years, whichever is longer).

**2. Trademarks (Articles 15–21):**
Any sign or combination of signs capable of distinguishing goods or services is registrable. The initial registration must be valid for at least **7 years** and must be **indefinitely renewable**. Well-known marks receive additional protection under Article 16.

**3. Geographical Indications (Articles 22–24):**
GI protection is given to products whose quality, reputation, or characteristics are essentially attributable to their geographical origin (e.g., **Darjeeling Tea, Champagne**). Minimum protection term is **10 years**.

**4. Industrial Designs (Articles 25–26):**
Designs that are new or original must be protected for a minimum of **10 years**. Owners have the right to prevent third parties from making, selling, or importing articles bearing the protected design.

**5. Patents (Articles 27–34):**
Both **product and process patents** must be available in all fields of technology, subject to limited exceptions (diagnostic, therapeutic, surgical methods, plants, animals). Minimum term is **20 years** from the filing date.

**6. Layout Designs of Integrated Circuits (Articles 35–38):**
TRIPS incorporates the **IPIC Treaty (Washington Treaty, 1989)**. Protection must extend to a minimum of **10 years** from the date of filing or first commercial exploitation.

**7. Undisclosed Information / Trade Secrets (Articles 39–40):**
Natural and legal persons must have the right to prevent information lawfully within their control from being disclosed, acquired, or used without consent, provided it is **secret, has commercial value, and reasonable steps are taken to keep it secret**. This category includes **test data submitted to regulatory authorities**.

**(7 Marks — Distribute ~1 mark per category with term)**

---

**(b)** Discuss the **enforcement obligations** imposed by TRIPS on member countries. Why are these provisions considered the "teeth" of TRIPS? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Part III (Articles 41–61) of TRIPS lays down detailed **enforcement obligations** that all WTO members must implement in their domestic laws:

**1. Civil and Administrative Remedies (Articles 42–50):**
- Fair and equitable procedures.
- Injunctions, damages, and right to information.
- Provisional measures for preserving evidence.

**2. Criminal Procedures (Article 61):**
For willful trademark counterfeiting and copyright piracy on a **commercial scale**, criminal penalties — including **imprisonment and/or fines** — must be available. Seizure, forfeiture, and destruction of infringing goods must also be provided.

**3. Border Measures (Articles 51–60):**
- Customs authorities must have the power to **suspend the release** of counterfeit and pirated goods at the border.
- Importation and exportation controls.
- Ex officio action by customs without prior complaint.

**4. Enforcement by Right Holders:**
TRIPS gives **private parties** the right to initiate legal proceedings. This shifts IP enforcement from purely state action to a hybrid model.

**5. Dispute Settlement (Article 64):**
Disputes arising under TRIPS are subject to the **WTO Dispute Settlement Body (DSB)** — the same body that resolves trade disputes. This is the critical link to TRIPS being enforceable.

**Why are these provisions the "teeth" of TRIPS?**

Before TRIPS, international IP treaties (like Paris and Berne) had **weak enforcement** — they relied on diplomatic pressure. TRIPS revolutionized IP law by:

- Making IP violations subject to **trade sanctions** through the WTO DSB.
- Allowing a member country to **retaliate with tariffs** on goods if another country fails to comply with TRIPS.
- Examples: **India — Section 3(d)**, and disputes like **US vs. India (Section 3(d))** show the consequences of non-compliance.

Therefore, the enforcement provisions make TRIPS **far stronger** than any prior IP treaty and form the **backbone of global IP governance**.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **flexibilities** available under TRIPS for developing countries like India. How does the **Doha Declaration (2001)** strengthen these flexibilities? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

TRIPS, while setting minimum standards, provides several **flexibilities** that developing countries have leveraged:

**1. Compulsory Licensing (Article 31):**
Governments may grant licenses to third parties (or use the patent themselves) without the patent holder's consent under specific conditions:
- National emergency or extreme urgency.
- Public non-commercial use.
- Refusal to deal on reasonable terms.
- Anti-competitive practices.
- The licensee must pay adequate remuneration.

**2. Parallel Imports (Article 6):**
Members are free to determine their **exhaustion regime**. India follows the **international exhaustion principle**, allowing parallel imports of patented products sold abroad.

**3. Transition Periods (Articles 65–66):**
- Developing countries: until **1st January 2000**.
- LDCs: originally until **2006**, extended to **1st July 2034** (pharmaceuticals).
- Transition periods allowed nations to gradually amend their IP laws.

**4. Bolar Exception:**
Allows generic manufacturers to use patented inventions for **regulatory approval** before patent expiry, enabling timely market entry of generics after patent expiry.

**5. Limited Patentable Subject Matter:**
TRIPS Article 27 allows exclusion of **diagnostic, therapeutic, and surgical methods**, as well as **plants and animals** (other than micro-organisms).

**6. Section 3(d) of Indian Patents Act:**
India's patent law excludes **new forms of known substances** unless they show significantly enhanced efficacy — a TRIPS-compatible provision preventing **evergreening**.

**Doha Declaration (2001) — Strengthening Flexibilities:**

The **Doha Ministerial Declaration on the TRIPS Agreement and Public Health (14th November 2001)** affirmed:
- TRIPS **should not** prevent members from taking measures to protect public health.
- Members have the right to use **compulsory licensing** and **parallel imports**.
- LDCs were granted extensions to comply.
- Led to **Article 31bis (2005 amendment)** allowing export of pharmaceuticals manufactured under compulsory license.

India used these flexibilities effectively, most notably in the **Natco v. Bayer (2012)** case, where Natco obtained a compulsory license for Bayer's cancer drug **Sorafenib Tosylate** (Nexavar).

**(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** Critically analyze the **impact of TRIPS on India**. Discuss both positive and negative effects on Indian industry, public health, and innovation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Background:**
India was a strong advocate of **"weak patent regimes"** before TRIPS, particularly in pharmaceuticals. India's **Patents Act, 1970** recognized only **process patents** in pharma, allowing Indian generics manufacturers (Cipla, Ranbaxy, Sun Pharma) to flourish as the **"pharmacy of the developing world."**

**Positive Impacts of TRIPS on India:**

1. **Modernized IP Infrastructure:** India enacted comprehensive laws in line with TRIPS — Patents Act 2005, Trade Marks Act 1999, Copyright Act 1957 (amended), GI of Goods Act 1999, Designs Act 2000.

2. **Boost to R&D and Innovation:** With assured patent protection, MNCs and Indian innovators have increased R&D investment in pharmaceuticals, IT, and biotech.

3. **Attracted FDI:** Stronger IP protection attracted foreign investment in knowledge-intensive sectors.

4. **GI Recognition:** India now protects **Darjeeling Tea, Basmati, Alphonso Mango, Chanderi Saree** — enhancing rural and agricultural economy.

5. **Software and IT Growth:** Copyright protection strengthened India's **IT services exports** (TCS, Infosys, Wipro).

6. **Strong Trademark Ecosystem:** Brand protection benefited Indian brands and exporters.

**Negative Impacts of TRIPS on India:**

1. **Pharma Sector Challenges:** Product patents (post-2005) increased the cost of patented drugs, raising **public health concerns**. India had to invoke compulsory licensing (e.g., Natco v. Bayer, 2012).

2. **Evergreening Concerns:** The Novartis v. Union of India (2013) case highlighted how MNCs try to extend patent monopolies through **Section 3(d)** disputes.

3. **High Drug Prices:** Reduced access to affordable medicines for the poor in initial years.

4. **Compliance Costs:** SMEs and startups face high costs of patent filing and litigation.

5. **Biopiracy Concerns:** Traditional knowledge (Turmeric, Neem) has been subject to biopiracy attempts, leading India to establish the **Traditional Knowledge Digital Library (TKDL)**.

**Critical Conclusion:**
TRIPS has been a **mixed blessing** for India. While it has strengthened the innovation ecosystem, India has skillfully used TRIPS **flexibilities** (compulsory licensing, Section 3(d), parallel imports) to balance innovation with **public interest**. The Indian approach demonstrates that TRIPS compliance and **developmental concerns** can coexist through intelligent legal design.

**(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Avoid These Errors:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "TRIPS" with "TRIMS"** — TRIMS = Trade-Related Investment Measures. Do not mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the Berne Convention link** — Copyright protection under TRIPS is **anchored on Berne Convention 1971**. Direct Article citation alone is incomplete.
> 3. **Wrong patent term** — TRIPS mandates **20 years from filing date**, NOT 15 years or 17 years.
> 4. **Ignoring Doha Declaration** — Any answer on TRIPS flexibilities without mentioning Doha (2001) loses at least **2 marks**.
> 5. **Section 3(d)** — Many students write that India's Section 3(d) is "anti-TRIPS." It is **TRIPS-compatible** as it is justified under the **efficacy enhancement** clause within Article 27.
> 6. **Confusing TRIPS Council with WIPO** — TRIPS Council is a **WTO body**, not under WIPO. WIPO administers treaties like Paris, Berne, PCT — not TRIPS.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE] > **Essential Points to Remember for KTU Exams:**

- ✅ TRIPS = **Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights**, WTO treaty, effective **1 Jan 1995**, negotiated under **GATT Uruguay Round**, signed at **Marrakesh (1994)**.
- ✅ TRIPS is **Annex 1C of the Marrakesh Agreement**.
- ✅ **7 categories of IP** protected: Copyright, Trademarks, GI, Industrial Designs, Patents, IC Layout Designs, Undisclosed Information.
- ✅ **Copyright term**: Life + **50 years** (or Berne + 10, whichever is longer).
- ✅ **Patent term**: **20 years** from filing date (Article 33).
- ✅ **Trademark initial term**: Minimum **7 years**, indefinitely renewable.
- ✅ **Industrial Design term**: Minimum **10 years**.
- ✅ **IC Layout Design term**: Minimum **10 years**.
- ✅ **Key principles**: National Treatment, MFN, Minimum Standards.
- ✅ **Enforcement**: Civil + Criminal + Border (Customs) measures mandatory.
- ✅ **Dispute Settlement**: Via **WTO Dispute Settlement Body (DSB)** under Article 64.
- ✅ **Compulsory Licensing**: Article 31, with conditions.
- ✅ **Parallel Imports**: Article 6 — Members free to decide.
- ✅ **Transition periods**: Developing countries — 2000, LDCs — extended to **2034** (pharma).
- ✅ **Doha Declaration (2001)**: Affirmed right to use TRIPS flexibilities for **public health**.
- ✅ **Article 31bis (2005)**: Export of pharma under compulsory license.
- ✅ **India's compliance**: Patents Act 1970 amended in **1999, 2002, 2005**.
- ✅ **Landmark Indian cases**: **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)**, **Natco v. Bayer (2012)**.
- ✅ **TRIPS is administered by the WTO, NOT WIPO.**

---

**End of Module 1 — TRIPS Agreement Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Patents Act 1970
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Patents Act 1970

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Patents Act, 1970 — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Patents Act, 1970** is the principal statute governing the law of patents in India. It provides for the **grant, registration, protection, assignment, and infringement of patents**, and codifies the exclusive statutory rights conferred upon a true and first inventor. The Act received Presidential assent on **19th September 1970** and came into force on **20th April 1972**, replacing the earlier *Indian Patents and Designs Act, 1911*.

The Act is administered by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)**, functioning under the **Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)**, Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Recipe Lock"**
> Imagine you invent a **unique recipe** for a soft drink. The Patents Act is essentially the **legal recipe vault** that:
> - **Acknowledges** you as the inventor (the *true and first inventor*).
> - **Locks** the recipe so others cannot copy, make, sell, or use it commercially for a fixed period (**20 years**).
> - **Compensates** you by giving a temporary **monopoly** in exchange for disclosing the recipe publicly.
>
> **What you get:** A time-bound legal shield.
> **What society gets:** Full disclosure of your invention, advancing science and technology.

Just like a landlord cannot lock a tenant out without due process, the State (via the Patent Office) cannot grant patents without satisfying the statutory criteria of **novelty, inventive step, and industrial application**.

## 1.3 Historical Evolution & Legislative Backdrop

| Year | Legislative Event | Significance |
|------|------------------|--------------|
| **1856** | Act VI of 1856 | First Indian patent statute; protection for 14 years |
| **1859** | Act XV of 1859 | Amendments for better enforcement |
| **1872** | Act IX of 1872 | Extended coverage to certain inventions |
| **1883** | Act XX of 1883 | Consolidation of patent laws |
| **1911** | Indian Patents & Designs Act | Comprehensive statute before 1970 |
| **1970** | Patents Act, 1970 | **Independent patent regime post-independence** |
| **1999** | First Amendment | Recognition of **Exclusive Marketing Rights (EMR)** under TRIPS |
| **2002** | Second Amendment | Product patents in **pharmaceuticals & agrochemicals**; TRIPS compliance |
| **2005** | Third Amendment | Full **TRIPS compliance**; product patents across all fields |
| **2020** | Patent Rules amendments | Streamlined procedures & fee revision |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The Act was radically amended in **2005** to become fully **TRIPS-compliant**, particularly expanding product patents to **drugs, pharmaceuticals, and food** sectors — historically excluded under the 1970 Act's original "process patent only" regime.

## 1.4 Object and Purpose of the Act

The **Statement of Objects and Reasons** of the Patents Act, 1970 highlights three core objectives:

1. **To provide a legal framework** for the recognition and protection of inventions.
2. **To encourage innovation** by granting exclusive rights for a limited period.
3. **To balance public interest** with inventor's rights by ensuring **public disclosure** of inventions.

The 1970 Act was unique because it originally restricted **product patents** in:
- Food
- Medicine/Drugs
- Chemicals (including agro-chemicals)

This was done to prevent monopolization in essential sectors — a policy orientation aligned with socialist economic principles prevalent in India at the time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Key Definitions under the Act (Section 2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Act provides specific statutory definitions. For KTU examinations, the following definitions are **mandatory**:

| Term | Section | Definition |
|------|---------|------------|
| **Invention** | 2(1)(j) | A new product or process involving an **inventive step** and capable of **industrial application** |
| **Patent** | 2(1)(m) | A patent granted under the Act |
