| **Patentee** | 2(1)(o) | Person for the time being entered on the register as the grantee or proprietor of the patent |
| **True & First Inventor** | 2(1)(y) | The first inventor of the invention (does *not* include the first importer) |
| **Convention Application** | 2(1)(d) | An application filed in India claiming priority from a similar application in a convention country |
| **Priority Date** | 2(1)(zb) | The date of filing of the earliest application in respect of the same invention |

## 2.2 Patentability Criteria (Section 2(1)(j) + Section 3)

### The Three Pillars of Patentability

> [!NOTE]
> An invention is patentable ONLY if it satisfies ALL three criteria:

$$\text{Patentability} = \text{Novelty} \cap \text{Inventive Step} \cap \text{Industrial Application}$$

| Criterion | Explanation | KTU Significance |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Novelty** | Must be **new** — not disclosed in any prior art, anywhere in the world, before the date of filing | Absolute worldwide novelty required |
| **Inventive Step** | Must involve a **non-obvious technical advance** over what exists | Cannot be a trivial variation of known art |
| **Industrial Application** | Must be capable of being **made or used in an industry** | Pure theoretical discoveries excluded |

## 2.3 Non-Patentable Inventions (Section 3 & Section 4)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Weightage Area:** Section 3 of the Patents Act, 1970 is the **most frequently tested** provision in KTU examinations.

The following are **NOT inventions** under the Act:

1. **Frivolous inventions** — those that claim things obviously contrary to well-established natural laws.
2. **Inventions contrary to public order or morality** — including those harmful to human, animal, plant life, or environment.
3. **Discoveries of natural laws / scientific principles** — Einstein's E = mc² is a discovery, not an invention.
4. **Mere discovery of a new property or use** of a known substance.
5. **Admixture of known substances** — merely mixing two known products.
6. **New use of a known substance** (post-2005 amendment — allowed for known drugs).
7. **Methods of agriculture/horticulture** — but **agrochemical products** are now patentable.
8. **Surgical/therapeutic/curative/prophylactic methods** for treatment of humans/animals.
9. **Plants and animals in whole or any part** — but **microorganisms** are patentable.
10. **Biological processes** for production or propagation.
11. **Mathematical/business methods** or algorithms.
12. **Literary, dramatic, artistic, or aesthetic works** (covered by Copyright Act).
13. **Mere schemes or rules** of playing games.
14. **Information presentation** — schemes, plans, layouts for buildings.
15. **Integrated circuit topographies** — though protected separately under the Semiconductor IC Layout Design Act, 2000.
16. **Traditional knowledge** — belonging to any community (added by 2005 amendment).
17. **Atomic energy inventions** — covered by separate legislation.
18. **Traditional Knowledge:** inventions based on traditional knowledge are not patentable (added by 2002 amendment).

## 2.4 Term, Renewal, and Restoration of Patents

| Aspect | Provision | Duration/Condition |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Term of Patent** | Section 53 | **20 years** from the date of filing |
| **Renewal fee** | Section 53(2) | Payable annually from the **3rd year** onwards |
| **Restoration** | Section 60 | Possible if patent ceased due to non-payment, within **18 months** of cessation |
| **Surrender** | Section 63 | Patentee may offer the patent for public benefit |

## 2.5 Rights of Patentee (Section 48)

Where a patent is granted, the **patentee shall have the following exclusive rights**:

1. **Exclusive right** to use the invention throughout India.
2. To **make, use, exercise, sell, distribute, or import** the patented article/product or obtain the patented product.
3. Where the patent is for a **process**, the right to use the process and to **deal in the product obtained directly** by that process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These rights are **negative rights** — they entitle the patentee to **exclude others**, not to personally exploit the invention.

## 2.6 Working of Patents & Compulsory Licensing (Chapter XVI)

| Section | Provision | Trigger Condition |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| **S. 83** | General principles | Patents must be worked in India on a commercial scale |
| **S. 84** | Compulsory License | Non-satisfaction of reasonable requirements, refusal to license on reasonable terms |
| **S. 92** | Compulsory License for public non-medical use | National emergency, extreme urgency, public non-commercial use |
| **S. 92A** | Compulsory License for export | To manufacture for export to countries with insufficient capacity |
| **S. 102** | Revocation | On grounds of non-working, lack of novelty, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trivia:** India's first (and so far only) **Compulsory License** was granted on **9 March 2012** by the Controller General of Patents to **NATCO Pharma** for the cancer drug **Sorafenib Tosylate** (generic name of Bayer's Nexavar®), under Section 84.

## 2.7 Infringement of Patents (Section 104–114)

| Concept | Provision | Key Point |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **Infringement** | Section 48 read with 104 | Unauthorized making, using, selling, importing the patented product/process |
| **Suit for Infringement** | Section 104 | Filed in **District Court** having jurisdiction |
| **Reliefs** | Section 108 | Injunction, damages or account of profits, delivery up of infringing goods |
| **Defence** | Section 107 | Grounds include invalidity, prior user, Bolar exemption |

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Conceptual)

For KTU problem-solving, the following "equations" are conceptual checklists:

| Formula | Meaning |
|---------|---------|
| $P = N \cap IS \cap IA$ | Patentability = Novelty ∩ Inventive Step ∩ Industrial Application |
| $T = 20 \text{ years} - t_{filing}$ | Term = 20 years from filing date |
| $R = \sum_{n=3}^{20} F_n$ | Renewal fees from 3rd to 20th year |
| $CL = \frac{NW}{NR}$ | Compulsory License = Non-Working / Not Reasonable |
| $E = 20\% \text{ (royalty rule)}$ | Standard royalty benchmark: 20% of net profit (not statutory, but judicial) |

## 2.9 Engineering Relevance

- **Pharmaceuticals & Biotechnology:** Patents drive R&D investments; compulsory licensing ensures access.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design patents protect novel mechanisms.
- **Software Engineering:** Algorithms and business methods per se are non-patentable.
- **Electrical/Electronics:** Patents on semiconductor innovations (though topographies separately regulated).
- **Chemical Engineering:** Composition patents critical for new formulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Examination of Key Provisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides an exhaustive analytical breakdown of the **patent granting procedure**, **infringement analysis**, and **compulsory licensing jurisprudence** — structured as step-by-step logical derivations.

## 3.1 Patent Granting Procedure — Analytical Walkthrough

### Step-by-Step Derivation of the Patent Granting Process

Let us analytically derive the procedural steps from **Application to Grant**:

**Step 1: Filing of Application**
The applicant (true and first inventor or assignee) files an application at the **Appropriate Patent Office** (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata) under Section 6, 7, 8, or 54.

$$\text{Application Types} = \{\text{Ordinary (S.6)}, \text{Convention (S.7)}, \text{PCT-NP (S.8)}, \text{Divisional (S.16)}\}$$

**Step 2: Examination Request (Section 11B)**

The application is examined only after the applicant files an **examination request** within **48 months** of priority date, paying the prescribed fee.

**Step 3: Prior Art Search & Examination (Section 12-14)**
The examiner conducts a search for prior art and prepares an **Examination Report** identifying:
- Anticipation of novelty (S. 13(1)(a))
- Lack of inventive step (S. 13(1)(b))
- Non-patentability under Section 3 (S. 13(1)(c))

**Step 4: First Examination Report (FER) Issuance**
The FER is communicated to the applicant. The applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable by 3 months).

**Step 5: Opposition Proceedings**
Two stages of opposition are available:

$$\text{Opposition} = \begin{cases} \text{Pre-Grant (S. 25(1))} \\ \text{Post-Grant (S. 25(2))} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6: Hearing & Decision (Section 14-15)**
The controller hears both parties and decides whether to grant the patent. If objections are overcome, the application proceeds to acceptance.

**Step 7: Publication & Grant (Section 43)**
The patent is published in the **Patent Office Journal** and a certificate of grant is issued.

### Summary Algorithm

```
Input: Invention details
  ↓
Step 1: Application Filing (S.6)
  ↓
Step 2: Publication (S.11A) — within 18 months
  ↓
Step 3: Examination Request (S.11B) — within 48 months
  ↓
Step 4: FER Issuance
  ↓
Step 5: Response by Applicant (6 + 3 months)
  ↓
Step 6: Opposition (Pre or Post Grant)
  ↓
Step 7: Hearing (S.14)
  ↓
Step 8: Acceptance & Grant (S.43)
  ↓
Output: Patent Certificate (20 years from filing)
```

## 3.2 Infringement Analysis — Analytical Matrix

Let us derive when an act constitutes **infringement**:

$$\text{Infringement} = \exists \text{Unauthorized act} \cap \text{Patent in force} \cap \text{No license/consent}$$

| Test | Application |
|------|-------------|
| **Literal Infringement** | Direct copying of every element of the claim |
| **Doctrine of Equivalents** | If the accused process/product performs substantially the same function in substantially the same way to achieve substantially the same result |
| **Contributory Infringement** | Supply of means relating to an essential element of the invention |
| **Indirect Infringement** | Inducement of infringement by another |

### Defences Against Infringement

| Defence | Statutory Basis |
|---------|-----------------|
| **Invalidity** | S. 107(1) — Patent not granted in accordance with the Act |
| **Prior User** | S. 64(1)(e) — Working before priority date |
| **Bolar Exemption** | S. 107A — Use for regulatory submission |
| **Government Use** | S. 100-104 |
| **Compulsory License** | S. 84 |

## 3.3 Compulsory Licensing — Juridical Derivation

The Supreme Court and the IPAB have laid down specific conditions:

$$\text{CL Grant} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} C_i$$

Where:
- $C_1$: Reasonable requirements of the public not satisfied (S. 84(1)(a))
- $C_2$: Patented invention not available at reasonably affordable price (S. 84(1)(b))
- $C_3$: Patented invention not worked in India (S. 84(1)(c))
- $C_4$: Patentee refused to grant license on reasonable terms (S. 84(1)(d))
- $C_5$: Patentee failed to work within India (S. 84(1)(f))

### NATCO v. Bayer Case — Judicial Derivation

Applying the conditions:
- $C_1$: ✓ Bayer imported Nexavar @ ₹2.8 lakh/month; not affordable
- $C_2$: ✓ Unaffordable price to public
- $C_3$: ✓ Not worked in India
- $C_4$: ✓ Bayer refused license to NATCO
- $C_5$: ✓ Not worked in India

All five conditions satisfied → **Compulsory License granted on 9 March 2012**.

Royalty fixed: **6%** of net sales (significantly lower than the 20% benchmark).

## 3.4 TRIPS Compliance — Analytical Breakdown

The 2005 amendment was structurally necessary because TRIPS Article 27 mandated:

$$\text{TRIPS Mandate} = \text{Product Patents} \cap \text{National Treatment} \cap \text{MFN Treatment}$$

| TRIPS Provision | Indian Pre-2005 Status | Post-2005 Compliance |
|-----------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Art. 27.1 — Patentable subject matter | Limited to processes in pharma/chem | All fields covered |
| Art. 31 — Compulsory Licensing | Present | Retained |
| Art. 33 — 20-year term | 14 years (earlier) | 20 years from filing |
| Art. 34 — Reversal of burden | Not present | Inserted — S. 104A |

## 3.5 Rights Conferred (Section 48) — Analytical Expansion

```
Patented Product Rights:
  ├── Right to make
  ├── Right to sell
  ├── Right to use
  ├── Right to offer for sale
  ├── Right to distribute
  └── Right to import

Patented Process Rights:
  ├── Right to use the process
  ├── Right to deal in product obtained
  └── Third parties using the process → burden on them
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Patent Granting Procedure — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Applicant / Inventor"] --> B["File Application S.6 / S.7 / S.8"]
    B --> C["Publication in Journal S.11A"]
    C --> D{"Examination Request S.11B"}
    D -- Filed --> E["Prior Art Search by Examiner"]
    D -- Not Filed --> F["Application Deemed Withdrawn"]
    E --> G["First Examination Report FER"]
    G --> H{"Applicant Response"}
    H -- Satisfactory --> I["Pre-Grant Opposition S.25(1)"]
    H -- Unsatisfactory --> J["Hearing S.14"]
    I --> K{"Objections Received"}
    K -- Yes --> J
    K -- No --> L["Acceptance S.15"]
    J --> M["Controller Decision"]
    M --> L
    L --> N["Patent Granted S.43"]
    N --> O["Post-Grant Opposition S.25(2)"]
    O -- Sustained --> P["Revocation S.64"]
    O -- Dismissed --> Q["Patent Enforced 20 Years"]
```

## 4.2 Patentability Test — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["New Invention Claimed"] --> B{"Section 3 Check"}
    B -- "Excluded Subject Matter" --> C["Not Patentable"]
    B -- "Patentable Category" --> D{"Novelty Test"}
    D -- "Disclosed in Prior Art" --> E["Lacks Novelty"]
    D -- "New" --> F{"Inventive Step Test"}
    F -- "Obvious to Person Skilled in Art" --> G["Lacks Inventive Step"]
    F -- "Non-Obvious" --> H{"Industrial Application"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["PATENT GRANTED"]
    H -- "No" --> J["Not Patentable"]
```

## 4.3 Compulsory Licensing — Application Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Applicant for CL"] --> B["Apply to Controller S.84"]
    B --> C{"Public Interest Test"}
    C -- "Satisfied" --> D["Notice to Patentee S.87"]
    C -- "Not Satisfied" --> E["Application Rejected"]
    D --> F["Patentee Hearing"]
    F --> G{"Royalty Terms Agreed"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Voluntary License"]
    G -- "No" --> I["Controller Hearing S.88"]
    I --> J{"All S.84 Conditions Met"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Compulsory License Granted S.92"]
    J -- "No" --> E
    K --> L["Royalty Fixation"]
    L --> M["License to Work in India"]
```

## 4.4 Infringement Litigation — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Patent Holder Discovers Infringement"] --> B["Cease & Desist Notice"]
    B -- Infringer Complies --> C["Dispute Resolved"]
    B -- Infringer Refuses --> D["Suit in District Court S.104"]
    D --> E["Plaintiff Proves Ownership and Validity"]
    E --> F["Plaintiff Proves Infringement"]
    F --> G{"Defences Pleaded S.107"}
    G -- "Invalidity" --> H["Counter-claim for Revocation S.64"]
    G -- "Prior User" --> I["Defence Upheld"]
    G -- "Bolar Exemption" --> I
    H --> J{"Court Decides"}
    I --> J
    J --> K{"Infringement Established"}
    K -- "Yes" --> L["Reliefs S.108 Injunction Damages or Account of Profits"]
    K -- "No" --> M["Suit Dismissed"]
```

## 4.5 Patent Rights Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Exclusive Rights of Patentee S.48"] --> B["Product Patents"]
    A --> C["Process Patents"]
    B --> D["Make Use Sell Offer for Sale Distribute Import"]
    C --> E["Use the Process"]
    C --> F["Deal in Product Obtained from Process"]
    E --> G["Third Party Burden S.104A"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**
**Define the term "invention" under Section 2(1)(j) of the Patents Act, 1970. What are the three essential conditions for an invention to be patentable?**

**Model Answer:**

Under Section 2(1)(j) of the Patents Act, 1970, *"invention"* means a **new product or process** involving an **inventive step** and capable of **industrial application**.

The three essential conditions are:

1. **Novelty** — The invention must be new and not disclosed in any prior art anywhere in the world before the date of filing.

2. **Inventive Step** — The invention must involve a non-obvious technical advance over what is already known to a person skilled in the art.

3. **Industrial Application** — The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** State all three criteria explicitly. Marks are split: 1 for definition + 1 each for the three conditions.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**
**Distinguish between **product patent** and **process patent** with reference to the historical evolution of the Patents Act, 1970.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Product Patent | Process Patent |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | Patent granted over the **product itself** (article/substance) | Patent granted over the **method/process** of manufacturing |
| **Scope of Protection** | Covers the product **regardless of how it is made** | Only covers the specific process; competitors using a different process may escape infringement |
| **Original Position (1970 Act)** | Excluded in food, drugs, chemicals | Permitted in all sectors |
| **Post-2005 Amendment** | Granted in all fields (TRIPS-compliant) | Continues to be available |
| **Example** | Patent on a specific chemical compound | Patent on a method of synthesizing that compound |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** The original 1970 Act restricted product patents in pharmaceuticals, food, and chemicals to prevent monopolization in essential sectors. The 2005 amendment removed this restriction in compliance with the **TRIPS Agreement**.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**.

---

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the salient features of the Patents Act, 1970. Highlight the major amendments introduced by the 2005 amendment and their significance in light of the TRIPS Agreement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **rights of a patentee** under Section 48 of the Patents Act, 1970. How are these rights affected in the case of a **process patent** versus a **product patent**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The Patents Act, 1970 has several salient features:

**1. Statutory Recognition of Patents**
Section 5 of the Act provides that "a patent shall be granted for an invention." The Act provides for both **product patents** and **process patents**, subject to the specific exclusions in Section 3.

**2. Definition of Invention**
As per Section 2(1)(j), an invention must satisfy **novelty, inventive step, and industrial application**.

**3. Exclusive Rights**
Section 48 grants the patentee exclusive rights to **make, use, exercise, sell, distribute, and import** the patented article or product.

**4. Term of Patent**
Section 53 provides a uniform term of **20 years** from the date of filing of the application.

**5. Concept of Working**
Sections 83-84 emphasize the working of patents in India on a commercial scale. Non-working may attract **compulsory licensing**.

**6. Compulsory Licensing**
Sections 84-92 provide for compulsory licensing to safeguard public interest, especially in essential sectors.

**7. Revocation**
Section 64 provides for revocation on grounds such as prior public knowledge, lack of novelty, or non-working.

**Major 2005 Amendments (TRIPS Compliance):**

| Amendment | Pre-2005 Position | Post-2005 Position |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Product Patents** | Restricted to methods/processes in pharma/agro-chemicals | Granted in all fields of technology |
| **Term** | Variable (5+7+14 years option earlier) | Uniform 20 years |
| **Burden of Proof** | On patent holder | Reversed in process patent suits — S. 104A |
| **Examination** | Automatic | On request within 48 months |

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** The 2005 amendments brought India in full compliance with the **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**, particularly Articles 27, 31, and 33.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Rights of Patentee (Section 48):**

Where a patent is granted, the patentee shall have the **exclusive right** to:

1. **Make** the patented product.
2. **Use** the patented product.
3. **Exercise** the patented invention.
4. **Sell** the patented product.
5. **Distribute** the patented product.
6. **Offer for sale** the patented product.
7. **Import** the patented product.

**Case of Product Patent:**
A product patent gives the patentee an **absolute monopoly** over the article itself. No third party can manufacture, use, sell, distribute, offer for sale, or import the patented product — regardless of the process employed.

**Case of Process Patent:**
A process patent confers rights over the **specific method** of manufacturing. Under **Section 104A**, in any suit for infringement of a process patent, the **burden of proof shifts** to the alleged infringer to prove that the product was not manufactured using the patented process.

**Comparative Effect:**

```
Product Patent:  Total monopoly over the article
Process Patent:  Monopoly limited to the process
                 + Reverse burden of proof under S.104A
```

**Key Case:** In *Bishwanath Prasad Radhey Shyam v. Hindustan Metal Industries (1979)*, the Supreme Court held that **doctrine of equivalents** applies when determining infringement.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the provisions of the Patents Act, 1970 relating to **non-patentable inventions** as enumerated in **Section 3**. Illustrate with relevant examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the concept of **compulsory licensing** under the Patents Act, 1970. Critically examine the **NATCO v. Bayer (2012)** case and its implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

Section 3 of the Patents Act, 1970 enumerates categories of **non-patentable inventions**. These include:

**1. Frivolous Inventions**
Inventions that claim things contrary to well-established natural laws.
*Example:* A "perpetual motion machine" — contrary to the laws of thermodynamics.

**2. Inventions Contrary to Public Order or Morality**
Inventions harmful to human, animal, plant life, or environment.
*Example:* A weapon designed to cause indiscriminate harm.

**3. Discoveries of Natural Laws or Scientific Principles**
Merely discovering a natural phenomenon is not patentable.
*Example:* A discovery that "E = mc²" (Einstein's mass-energy equivalence) is a scientific principle, not an invention.

**4. New Property of a Known Substance**
The mere discovery of a new property of an already known substance is not patentable.

**5. Admixture of Known Substances**
Mere mixing of two or more known products without achieving synergistic or unexpected results.

**6. Methods of Agriculture/Horticulture**
Traditional methods of farming are excluded, but biotechnological innovations may qualify.

**7. Surgical/Therapeutic Methods**
Methods for treatment of humans or animals are not patentable — but **products** used in such methods are.

**8. Plants and Animals**
Plants and animals in whole or any part cannot be patented — but **microorganisms** can.

**9. Mathematical/Business Methods/Algorithms**
Pure algorithms and abstract business methods per se are excluded.

**10. Schemes or Rules of Mental Performance**
Including schemes for playing games, schemes for business, and programs per se.

**11. Traditional Knowledge**
Inventions based on traditional knowledge of any community are not patentable (added by 2002 amendment).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Case:** In *Novartis AG v. Union of India (2013)*, the Supreme Court upheld Section 3(d) — which disallows patents on new forms of known substances unless they show significantly enhanced efficacy.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Concept of Compulsory Licensing:**

Compulsory Licensing (CL) is a statutory remedy whereby the **government authorizes** a third party to manufacture, use, or sell a patented invention **without the patentee's consent**, subject to payment of royalty.

**Statutory Provisions:**
- **Section 83:** General principles (patents should be worked in India).
- **Section 84:** Application for CL on grounds of non-working, unreasonable pricing, or refusal to license.
- **Section 87:** Procedure for application.
- **Section 90:** Royalty fixation.

**Conditions under Section 84:**
1. Reasonable requirements of the public not satisfied.
2. Patented invention not available at reasonably affordable price.
3. Patented invention not worked in India.
4. Patentee refused to license on reasonable terms.
5. Patentee failed to work in India.

**NATCO v. Bayer (2012) — Critical Analysis:**

| Aspect | Details |
|--------|---------|
| **Parties** | NATCO Pharma (Applicant) vs. Bayer Corporation (Patent Holder) |
| **Patent Drug** | Sorafenib Tosylate (used for kidney & liver cancer) |
| **Bayer's Price** | ₹2.8 lakh per month |
| **NATCO's Price** | ₹8,800 per month (97% reduction) |
| **Application Date** | 2011 |
| **Decision Date** | 9 March 2012 |
| **Grounds** | Non-working in India + unaffordable pricing + refusal to license |
| **Royalty Fixed** | 6% of net sales (vs. 20% benchmark) |

**Implications:**

1. **Precedent for Affordability:** Established that unaffordable pricing is a valid ground for CL.
2. **Public Health Priority:** Affirmed India's commitment to access to essential medicines.
3. **TRIPS Flexibility:** Demonstrated use of TRIPS Article 31 flexibilities.
4. **Limited Effect:** Bayer chose to license Nexavar to Natco voluntarily for Indian market — limiting broader impact.
5. **No Subsequent CL:** No further CL has been granted in India since this case, showing the difficulty of the process.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do **NOT** state that the patent was revoked in NATCO v. Bayer — it was a **compulsory license**, not revocation.
> - The royalty was **6%**, NOT 20%.
> - Mention all **five conditions** of Section 84 if the question is on CL.
> - Cite the **correct date** — 9 March 2012.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "invention" with "discovery":** Discoveries per se are not patentable. The question will mark you down if you confuse the two.
>
> 2. **Mixing up sections:** Section 2(1)(j) is the **definition**; Section 3 is the **list of non-patentable inventions**; Section 4 is about inventions relating to atomic energy. Do not interchange.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the 20-year term:** The term of patent is **20 years from the date of filing**, not from the date of grant.
>
> 4. **Missing Section 3(d):** The amended Section 3(d) (2005) disallows patents on **new forms of known substances** unless they show **significantly enhanced efficacy**. This is a frequently-tested provision (refer *Novartis v. Union of India*).
>
> 5. **Infringement vs. Revocation:** Infringement is a **suit** under Section 104; Revocation is **separate** under Section 64. Do not confuse.
>
> 6. **Compulsory License vs. Government Use:** Section 84 is CL; Sections 100-104 are Government Use rights. Different triggers and procedures.
>
> 7. **Confusing Working of Patents:** Working means **manufacturing in India**, not just importing. Bayer's "import-only" model was the trigger in NATCO v. Bayer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Notes:**

### A. Core Definitions
- **Invention:** Section 2(1)(j) — New product/process + inventive step + industrial application.
- **Patent:** Section 2(1)(m).
- **Patentee:** Section 2(1)(o).
- **True and First Inventor:** Section 2(1)(y) — *Does not* include first importer.

### B. Patentability Criteria (3 Pillars)
1. **Novelty** — worldwide absolute.
2. **Inventive Step** — non-obvious to a person skilled in the art.
3. **Industrial Application** — capable of being made/used in industry.

### C. Non-Patentable Items (Section 3)
- Frivolous inventions
- Anti-public order/morality
- Mere discoveries / natural laws
- New property of known substance
- Mere admixture
- Methods of agriculture
- Surgical/therapeutic methods
- Plants and animals (microorganisms allowed)
- Business methods / algorithms / mathematical methods
- Traditional knowledge
- Atomic energy inventions

### D. Patent Term & Renewal
- **Term: 20 years** from the date of filing.
- **Renewal fee** from 3rd year onwards.
- **Restoration** within 18 months of cessation (S. 60).

### E. Rights of Patentee (Section 48)
- Make, Use, Exercise, Sell, Distribute, Offer for Sale, Import.

### F. Working of Patents (Section 83)
- Must be worked on a **commercial scale in India**.

### G. Compulsory Licensing (Section 84)
- **Five conditions** must be satisfied.
- **Royalty benchmark: 20%** (judicial), NATCO got 6%.
- **First and only CL granted: 9 March 2012** (NATCO v. Bayer).

### H. Oppositions
- **Pre-Grant Opposition:** Section 25(1).
- **Post-Grant Opposition:** Section 25(2).

### I. Amendments Timeline
- **2002 Amendment:** Product patents in pharma/agro-chemicals; EMR.
- **2005 Amendment:** Full TRIPS compliance; uniform 20-year term; S. 104A (burden of proof).

### J. Key Cases
- **Novartis AG v. Union of India (2013)** — Section 3(d) interpretation.
- **NATCO v. Bayer (2012)** — Compulsory license.
- **Bishwanath Prasad v. Hindustan Metal (1979)** — Doctrine of equivalents.

### K. Procedural Tips for KTU Answers
- Always cite the **section number** alongside the concept.
- Use headings and sub-points liberally for 14-mark questions.
- For "explain" questions, cover **object + provisions + case law**.
- For "discuss" questions, present **both sides** with a **judicial precedent**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Invention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Invention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Invention

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition of Invention**
> An **Invention** is a new, useful, and non-obvious product, process, machine, composition of matter, or improvement thereof, that is created by the human mind through the application of intellectual effort and technical skill, and which is capable of being used or applied in an industrial or commercial setting.

In the legal and IP-rights framework (specifically as referenced in the **Indian Patents Act, 1970** — Section 2(1)(j)), an invention is defined as:

> *"Invention" means a new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application.*

The three statutory pillars that constitute a patentable invention are:

| # | Statutory Pillar | Layman Term | Legal Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Novelty** | "Is it new?" | Not part of the global state-of-the-art (Prior Art) |
| 2 | **Inventive Step** (Non-Obviousness) | "Is it surprising to a skilled person?" | Not obvious to a person skilled in the art (PSITA) |
| 3 | **Industrial Application** (Utility) | "Can it be made or used in industry?" | Must have practical utility |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Cooking Pot of Ideas"**
> Imagine **invention** is like discovering a new recipe. **Discovery** is when you accidentally find a wild herb in the forest (Nature already had it). **Innovation** is when you improve an existing dish by adding a new spice (modifying the known). But **Invention** is when you combine two completely unrelated ingredients — say, chocolate and chili — in a way no chef has ever tried before, and the result is so unique that it solves a real problem (a new flavor profile) and can be cooked in any restaurant kitchen worldwide. That new recipe is your **invention**: novel (no one has done it), non-obvious (a chef would not naturally try it), and industrially applicable (scalable to restaurants).

### Why Invention Matters in Engineering

For B.Tech students, understanding invention is foundational because:
- Every engineering project, research paper, or design is a potential **invention**.
- It determines whether your work is eligible for **patent protection**.
- It distinguishes between **academic exercise** and **commercially valuable IP**.

---

## 1.3 Engineering Context & Real-World Engineering Examples

> [!TIP]
> **Examples of Engineering Inventions:**
> - **Mechanical:** Wright Brothers' aerodynamic wing design for powered flight.
> - **Electrical:** Transistor (Bardeen, Brattain, Shockley, 1947) — replaced vacuum tubes.
> - **Computer Science:** PageRank algorithm (Brin & Page) that became the basis of Google.
> - **Civil:** Reinforced concrete (Joseph Monier, 1867).
> - **Biotechnology:** CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing tool (Doudna & Charpentier).

---

## 1.4 Discovery vs. Innovation vs. Invention — The Critical Triangle

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Alert:** KTU questions frequently ask students to differentiate between Discovery, Invention, and Innovation. Conflating these three is the single most common reason students lose marks in Part A questions on this topic.

| Aspect | Discovery | Invention | Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Finds what already exists in nature | Creates something that did not exist before | Improves / commercializes an existing invention |
| **Origin** | Nature → Human recognition | Human mind → Creation | Market / commercial application |
| **Patentable?** | ❌ No (natural phenomena) | ✅ Yes (if statutory criteria met) | ❌ Usually no (improvement, not novel) |
| **Example** | Gravity (Newton) | Telephone (Bell) | iPhone (Apple) — based on prior patents |
| **Effort Type** | Observational | Creative + Technical | Commercial + Strategic |
| **IP Right** | None (public domain) | Patent | Trade Secret / Branding / Design |

---

## 1.5 Key Highlight Callouts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Module 2**
> According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PESFT757, Module 2 specifically emphasizes:
> 1. Meaning and concept of Invention
> 2. Types of Inventions
> 3. Criteria for Patentability (Novelty, Inventive Step, Industrial Application)
> 4. Difference between Discovery, Invention, and Innovation
> 5. Non-patentable inventions (under Section 3 & 4 of the Indian Patents Act)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Invention — A Three-Layer Analytical Model

Every patentable invention can be deconstructed into three analytical layers. This is the framework KTU examiners use to value Part B answers.

### Layer 1: The Conceptual Core (The "Idea")

This is the abstract thought or principle that solves a technical problem.
- It is **mental**, not physical.
- It is the **why** behind the invention.
- *Example:* The idea of using alternating current (AC) instead of direct current (DC) for long-distance power transmission.

### Layer 2: The Structural Embodiment (The "Blueprint")

This is the concrete technical manifestation of the idea — the design, schematic, or process flow.
- It is **documented** in the patent specification.
- It specifies **how** the idea is realized.
- *Example:* Tesla's AC induction motor design with rotating magnetic field.

### Layer 3: The Industrial Realization (The "Product")

This is the tangible working product or running process.
- It is **manufactured or executed** in industry.
- It demonstrates **industrial application**.
- *Example:* The mass-produced AC generators and motors used in every factory today.

---

## 2.2 The Three-Pillar Patentability Test (Statutory Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> Under the **Indian Patents Act, 1970**, an invention must satisfy all three of the following conditions simultaneously. Failure of even one pillar causes the invention to be **non-patentable**.

### Pillar 1: Novelty (Newness)

- **Test:** Has the invention been disclosed anywhere in the world (in any form — published paper, product, conference) before the **priority date**?
- **Standard:** **Absolute Novelty** — even a single disclosure anywhere kills novelty.
- **Grace Period:** India provides a **12-month** pre-filing grace period for disclosures at official/recognized exhibitions (Section 11A).

### Pillar 2: Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)

- **Test:** Would a **Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA)** find the invention obvious after reading the prior art?
- **Standard:** The invention must involve a **technical advance** OR **economic significance** OR both, compared to existing knowledge.
- **Key Case:** *Graham v. John Deere Co.* (US, 1966) — established the "teaching, suggestion, motivation" test.

### Pillar 3: Industrial Application (Utility)

- **Test:** Can the invention be **made or used** in any kind of industry (including agriculture)?
- **Standard:** The invention must have a **specific, substantial, and credible utility**.
- **Insufficient:** Mere "research use" or abstract scientific principle.

---

## 2.3 Types of Inventions (KTU High-Yield Classification)

### Type A: By Subject Matter

| Type | Description | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Invention** | A new physical article or chemical compound | A new polymer, a smartphone, a drug molecule |
| **Process Invention** | A new method of manufacturing or doing something | A new welding technique, a chemical synthesis route |
| **Machine Invention** | A new apparatus or device | 3D printer, CRISPR-Cas9 system |
| **Composition of Matter** | A new combination of ingredients | An alloy, a pharmaceutical formulation |
| **Improvement Invention** | Modification to an existing patented invention | USB-C improvement on USB-A |

### Type B: By Patentability Status (India)

| Category | Examples | Patentable? |
|---|---|---|
| **Patentable** | New machine, process, drug, software-tied-to-hardware | ✅ Yes |
| **Non-Patentable (Section 3)** | Frivolous inventions, contrary to public order, mere discovery, atomic energy, agricultural methods, mathematical methods, business methods, computer programs *per se* | ❌ No |
| **Non-Patentable (Section 4)** | Inventions related to atomic energy | ❌ No |

---

## 2.4 The "Inventive Step" Deep-Dive (PSITA Test)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA) — The Imaginary Examiner**
> In patent law, the PSITA is a hypothetical person who is an average practitioner of the relevant technology. They:
> - Know all the **published prior art** in their field.
> - Have ordinary **technical skill and knowledge**.
> - Have **no imagination** but can follow obvious technical steps.
> - Would **not** attempt risky trial-and-error experiments without a clear reason.

**Application Rule:** If the PSITA, upon reading the prior art, would have directly arrived at the invention, the inventive step is **absent**. If the invention requires an **unexpected leap**, **technical surprise**, or **non-obvious combination**, the inventive step is **present**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Invention Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | CAD-based design patents, manufacturing process patents |
| **Computer Science** | Algorithm patents (must be tied to hardware), AI model patents |
| **Electrical/Electronics** | Circuit design, semiconductor fabrication patents |
| **Biotechnology** | Gene-editing tools, drug delivery systems, vaccine platforms |
| **Civil** | New construction materials, earthquake-resistant structural designs |
| **Chemical** | Catalysts, polymer formulations, industrial process patents |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Framework Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Note on "Formulas":** Invention in IP law is qualitative, not quantitative. The "equations" below are **decision frameworks** used in KTU answers to score full marks.

| Framework ID | Decision Test | Question to Ask | Pass Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | Novelty Check | Is it part of global prior art as of priority date? | Must be **NO** |
| **F2** | Inventive Step Check | Would PSITA find it obvious? | Must be **NO** |
| **F3** | Industrial Application Check | Can it be made/used in industry? | Must be **YES** |
| **F4** | Section 3 Exclusion Check | Is it on the non-patentable list? | Must be **NO** |
| **F5** | Sufficient Disclosure Check | Is the specification enabling? | Must be **YES** |
| **F6** | Inventorship Check | Is a natural person named as inventor? | Must be **YES** |

**Combined Patentability Equation (Conceptual):**

$$
\boxed{\text{Patentable Invention} \;=\; (\text{Novelty}) \;\cap\; (\text{Inventive Step}) \;\cap\; (\text{Industrial Application}) \;\cap\; \neg(\text{Statutory Exclusion})}
$$

In words: **All four conditions must be TRUE simultaneously.** Even one failure renders the invention non-patentable.

---

## 2.7 The Invention Lifecycle (Conceptual Flow)

An invention typically follows a 5-stage lifecycle:

1. **Ideation** — Identifying a problem and conceiving a solution.
2. **Reduction to Practice** — Building a prototype or demonstrating the process.
3. **Disclosure** — Documenting the invention in a patent specification.
4. **Filing** — Submitting the patent application to the patent office.
5. **Grant & Commercialization** — Patent issued, technology licensed/manufactured.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Invention" is a conceptual IP-law topic, the "derivations" below are **analytical decision trees** that walk a student through the legal reasoning an examiner expects in a KTU answer. Each step is shown in full — no shortcuts, no truncation.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Determining Patentability of a Student Project

> [!TIP]
> **Scenario:** A final-year B.Tech student develops a new type of **solar panel coating** that increases photovoltaic efficiency by 15%. She publishes her B.Tech project report in the university library. Six months later, she files a patent application. The coating uses a novel nano-composite material. A person skilled in photovoltaics would not have obviously combined these specific nanoparticles based on prior research.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

**Step 1: Apply the Novelty Test (F1)**

The student's B.Tech project report was published **before** the patent application. This constitutes a **prior disclosure** that would normally destroy novelty under the **absolute novelty standard** in India.

$$
\text{Disclosure Date} < \text{Priority Date} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Novelty at risk}
$$

However, the **grace period provision** in India (Section 11A) and the 2020 amendment to the **Patents Rules** provide a **6-month pre-filing grace period** for disclosures arising from:
- Display at a government-recognized exhibition.
- Publication in a recognized academic context under certain conditions.

Since the publication was in a **university library** (a recognized academic institution), the student **may** invoke the grace period if it is within 6 months. The student's disclosure was 6 months ago — borderline. **Conclusion:** Novelty is **potentially preserved** if the grace period is successfully invoked.

**Step 2: Apply the Inventive Step Test (F2)**

The combination of the specific nanoparticles into a coating was **not obvious** to a PSITA in photovoltaics. The 15% efficiency gain is a **technical advance** with **economic significance**. Therefore:

$$
\text{Technical Advance} = \text{YES}, \quad \text{Economic Significance} = \text{YES} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Inventive Step} = \text{PRESENT}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Industrial Application Test (F3)**

The coating can be **manufactured industrially** and applied to mass-produced solar panels.

$$
\text{Industrial Application} = \text{PRESENT}
$$

**Step 4: Check Section 3 Exclusions (F4)**

The coating is **not** a frivolous invention, not contrary to public order, not atomic energy, not a business method, and not a computer program per se.

$$
\neg(\text{Statutory Exclusion}) = \text{TRUE}
$$

**Step 5: Combined Patentability Decision**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Patentable} &= (\text{Novelty}) \;\cap\; (\text{Inventive Step}) \;\cap\; (\text{Industrial Application}) \;\cap\; \neg(\text{Exclusion}) \\
&= (\text{YES}) \;\cap\; (\text{YES}) \;\cap\; (\text{YES}) \;\cap\; (\text{YES}) \\
&= \text{YES}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Conclusion:** The invention is **potentially patentable** in India, subject to successful invocation of the grace period for academic disclosure.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Classifying an Invention Type

**Scenario:** A team of chemical engineers develops a **new process** to extract lithium from seawater using a novel ionic-liquid-based membrane. They have filed a patent application.

### Step-by-Step Classification

**Step 1: Identify the Subject Matter**

The invention is a **method** of extracting lithium — it is a sequence of chemical operations.

$$
\text{Subject Matter} = \text{Process}
$$

**Step 2: Classify Under Type A Taxonomy**

| Feature | Observation |
|---|---|
| Produces a tangible article? | Yes (lithium compound) — but the invention claims the **process**, not the product |
| Inventive step present? | Yes (novel membrane chemistry) |
| Industrial application? | Yes (mining industry, battery supply chain) |

**Step 3: Check Non-Patentability**

It is not a mere discovery (the lithium is not new; the **method** to extract it is). It is not a mathematical method or business method.

**Conclusion:** This is a **Process Invention** with **Product-by-Process** claim potential. It is **patentable** under the Indian Patents Act.

---

## 3.3 The Inventive Step Decision Tree (Analytical Matrix)

| If Prior Art Shows... | And PSITA Would... | Then Inventive Step is... |
|---|---|---|
| The exact same solution | Reproduce it identically | **Absent** (no invention) |
| A similar solution with predictable variation | Modify trivially | **Absent** (obvious variation) |
| Disconnected components with no teaching to combine | Not think to combine | **Present** (non-obvious combination) |
| The problem but no solution | Fail to solve it | **Present** (new technical contribution) |
| A teaching away from the invention | Avoid the path | **Present** (contrarian insight) |

---

## 3.4 KTU-Marked Analytical Equation: Novelty Risk Formula

For a given invention, the **Novelty Risk Score (NRS)** can be conceptualized as:

$$
\text{NRS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot D_i
$$

Where:
- $D_i$ = the disclosure event $i$ (paper, product, conference, online post).
- $w_i$ = the weight of disclosure type (e.g., $w_{\text{printed paper}} > w_{\text{private email}}$).
- $n$ = total number of disclosure events before the priority date.

**Decision Rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NRS} &= 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Novelty is absolute (best case)} \\
\text{NRS} &> 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Novelty may be compromised; check grace period} \\
\text{NRS} &\geq \text{Threshold} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Novelty destroyed (worst case)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Note:** The exact numerical threshold varies by jurisdiction. In India, **any single public disclosure before filing (outside grace period) destroys novelty** — so practically, NRS ≥ 1 outside grace = non-patentable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Pillar Patentability Test — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Invention Submitted] --> B{Is it Novel? \n No prior art disclosure}
    B -- No --> B1[FAIL: Lacks Novelty]
    B -- Yes --> C{Does it have an \n Inventive Step? \n Non-obvious to PSITA}
    C -- No --> C1[FAIL: Obvious Variation]
    C -- Yes --> D{Can it be Industrially \n Applied? \n Made or used in industry}
    D -- No --> D1[FAIL: No Industrial Utility]
    D -- Yes --> E{Is it Excluded under \n Section 3 or 4? \n Indian Patents Act 1970}
    E -- Yes --> E1[FAIL: Statutory Exclusion]
    E -- No --> F[SUCCESS: Patentable Invention]
    B1 --> X[End: Non-Patentable]
    C1 --> X
    D1 --> X
    E1 --> X
    F --> Y[End: Eligible for Patent Grant]
```

---

## 4.2 Invention Lifecycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Problem Identification] --> P2[Ideation and Conceptualization]
    P2 --> P3[Prior Art Search]
    P3 --> P4{Novelty Confirmed?}
    P4 -- No --> P3
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Reduction to Practice \n Prototype or Process Demo]
    P5 --> P6[Patent Specification Drafting \n Claims, Description, Abstract]
    P6 --> P7[Filing at Patent Office \n India: Form 1 and Form 2]
    P7 --> P8[Examination Request \n Form 18]
    P8 --> P9{Objections Raised?}
    P9 -- Yes --> P10[Respond to FER \n First Examination Report]
    P10 --> P9
    P9 -- No --> P11[Patent Granted]
    P11 --> P12[Commercialization \n License, Assignment, Manufacture]
```

---

## 4.3 Discovery vs. Invention vs. Innovation — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Nature1[Nature and Universe]
        N1[Pre-existing Phenomena] --> N2[Discovery]
    end
    subgraph Human1[Human Mind]
        H1[Creative Synthesis] --> H2[Invention]
    end
    subgraph Market1[Market and Commerce]
        M1[Commercial Improvement] --> M2[Innovation]
    end
    N2 --> H2
    H2 --> M2
    N2 -.No Patent.-> Z[IP Status: Public Domain]
    H2 --> Y[IP Status: Patent Granted]
    M2 --> X[IP Status: Trade Secret or Design Right]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Layers of an Invention

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Conceptual Core \n The Idea or Principle] --> L2[Layer 2: Structural Embodiment \n Blueprint, Schematic, Specification]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Industrial Realization \n Manufactured Product or Running Process]
    L1 -.- L1D[Example: AC current concept]
    L2 -.- L2D[Example: AC motor design]
    L3 -.- L3D[Example: Factory-produced AC motor]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for KTU Exam Answers:**
> - Use the **three-pillar flowchart** when explaining patentability criteria.
> - Use the **lifecycle diagram** when answering "process of obtaining a patent" type questions.
> - Use the **Discovery/Invention/Innovation map** for the high-yield differentiation question.
> - Use the **layered architecture** when explaining "what constitutes an invention."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Distinguish between Discovery, Invention, and Innovation with one engineering example each."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Target: 50–70 words)

| # | Concept | Meaning | Engineering Example | IP Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Discovery** | Finding something that already exists in nature | Newton's discovery of gravitational law | No IP protection — public domain |
| 2 | **Invention** | Creating a novel, non-obvious, industrially applicable product/process | Bell's invention of the telephone | Patent protection available |
| 3 | **Innovation** | Improving or commercializing an existing invention | Apple's iPhone (based on prior communication patents) | Usually trade secret or design rights |

**Concluding line (1 mark):** While discovery is natural, invention is technical, and innovation is commercial — together they form the knowledge-value chain in engineering.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"State and explain the three statutory criteria for patentability of an invention under the Indian Patents Act, 1970."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The three statutory criteria are:

1. **Novelty** — The invention must be new; it must not form part of the existing global state-of-the-art (prior art) as of the priority date.
2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — The invention must not be obvious to a Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA); it must involve a technical advance or have economic significance.
3. **Industrial Application (Utility)** — The invention must be capable of being made or used in some kind of industry, including agriculture.

**Concluding line (1 mark):** All three criteria must be satisfied cumulatively; failure of any one criterion renders the invention non-patentable in India.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*"With a neat flowchart, explain the three statutory criteria for patentability of an invention. Discuss how each criterion is tested under the Indian Patents Act, 1970."*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*"Differentiate between Product Invention and Process Invention with two engineering examples each. Why is this distinction important in patent filing?"*

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Introduction (1 mark):** Patentability in India is governed by Section 2(1)(j) read with Sections 3 and 4 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970. The three statutory criteria are Novelty, Inventive Step, and Industrial Application.

**Flowchart (3 marks):** Present the three-pillar flowchart from SECTION 4.1 of these notes, clearly showing the sequential decision gates and failure paths.

**Detailed Explanation of Each Criterion (3 marks):**

**1. Novelty (Section 2(1)(l)):**
- **Test:** Absolute worldwide novelty.
- **Test Date:** Priority date (earliest filing date).
- **Disclosure Sources that Destroy Novelty:** Published paper, public use, sale offer, conference presentation, online disclosure, foreign patent.
- **Grace Period:** 12 months for official/recognized exhibitions (Section 11A).

**2. Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja)):**
- **Test:** Non-obviousness to a Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA).
- **Standard:** Must involve a technical advance, economic significance, or both.
- **Judicial Test:** Graham v. John Deere (US Supreme Court, 1966) — TSM (Teaching, Suggestion, Motivation) test.

**3. Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac)):**
- **Test:** The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry.
- **Industry** includes agriculture, manufacturing, services, and any commercial activity.

**Conclusion (extra credit):** All three criteria are **conjunctive** (AND condition) — failure of one defeats patentability.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Introduction (1 mark):** The Indian Patents Act permits two principal categories of invention claims — **Product** and **Process** — each with distinct legal, commercial, and technical implications.

**Product Invention (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A new physical article, device, chemical compound, or composition of matter.
- **Examples:**
  1. **Wright Brothers' aircraft** (1903) — a new flying machine.
  2. **Transistor** (Bardeen, Brattain, Shockley, 1947) — a new electronic semiconductor device.

**Process Invention (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** A new method, technique, or sequence of operations to manufacture a product or achieve a result.
- **Examples:**
  1. **Haber-Bosch process** — a new industrial method to synthesize ammonia.
  2. **CRISPR gene-editing workflow** — a new method to edit DNA sequences.

**Importance of the Distinction (1 mark):**
- **Scope of Protection:** A product patent protects the article itself (regardless of how made); a process patent protects only the method.
- **Infringement Detection:** Easier to detect product infringement than process infringement.
- **Licensing Strategy:** Companies often file both product and process patents for layered protection.
- **TRIPS Compliance:** Article 27 of TRIPS requires that both product and process patents be available in all fields of technology.

**Conclusion (extra credit):** The Product/Process distinction is the backbone of modern patent portfolio strategy in engineering and pharmaceutical industries.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*"Explain the concept of 'Invention' in the context of Intellectual Property Rights. Discuss with examples the various types of inventions recognized in engineering and technology."*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*"What is the 'Person Skilled in the Art' (PSITA) test? How is it applied in determining the inventive step of an invention? Discuss with a suitable example."*

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Definition of Invention (2 marks):**
An invention is a **new, useful, and non-obvious** creation of the human intellect that solves a technical problem and is capable of industrial application. Under the Indian Patents Act, 1970, Section 2(1)(j), an invention is defined as *"a new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application."*

**Types of Inventions in Engineering (4 marks):**

| # | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Product Invention** | A new tangible article | Apple iPhone, Insulin pump |
| 2 | **Process Invention** | A new method of doing something | Solvay process for soda ash manufacturing |
| 3 | **Machine Invention** | A new apparatus with moving parts | 3D printer, CNC milling machine |
| 4 | **Composition of Matter** | A new combination of substances | Stainless steel alloy, pharmaceutical tablet |
| 5 | **Improvement Invention** | Modification of an existing patented invention | USB-C over USB-A, Lithium-ion over Lead-acid battery |

**Importance for B.Tech Students (1 mark):**
- Recognizes their technical work as potentially protectable IP.
- Encourages innovation culture in academic institutions.
- Foundation for technology transfer and startup ventures (e.g., IPR cells in IITs/NITs).

**Conclusion (extra credit):** Engineering inventions span mechanical, electrical, computer, civil, chemical, and biotechnological domains — each with unique patent strategies.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Definition of PSITA (2 marks):**
The **Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA)** is a hypothetical, legally-constructed persona used in patent law to assess inventive step (non-obviousness). The PSITA is an **ordinary practitioner** in the relevant technical field who:
- Possesses **average knowledge and skill** in the art.
- Has read all **published prior art** in the field.
- Has **ordinary creativity** but **no inventive capacity**.
- Approaches the problem in a **routine, methodical** manner.

**Application of the PSITA Test (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify the **relevant field** of the invention (e.g., photovoltaics, pharmaceuticals, software).
**Step 2:** Define the **PSITA's knowledge base** — what publications and prior art the PSITA would know.
**Step 3:** Pose the question: *"Would the PSITA, starting from the prior art, have directly arrived at the invention?"*
**Step 4:** If **YES** → inventive step is **absent** → invention is obvious → **not patentable**.
**Step 5:** If **NO** → inventive step is **present** → invention is non-obvious → **patent-eligible**.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**
- **Invention:** A new solar panel coating using a specific nano-composite that improves efficiency by 15%.
- **PSITA's Knowledge:** A photovoltaic engineer aware of standard anti-reflective coatings and known nanoparticle dispersions.
- **Application:** Would the PSITA have thought to combine these specific nanoparticles in this exact ratio to achieve the 15% gain? **No** — the combination is non-obvious and produces a surprising technical effect.
- **Result:** Inventive step is **present** → **patentable**.

**Conclusion (extra credit):** The PSITA test is a **judicial tool of objectivity** — it prevents subjective "I think it's obvious" reasoning and anchors the analysis to a defined technical standard.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Discovery with Invention** — citing gravity or electricity as inventions (they are discoveries). This is the **#1 mark-deduction point** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Treating the three patentability criteria as alternatives** — they are **conjunctive (AND)**, not disjunctive (OR). A "novel but obvious" invention is still non-patentable.
> 3. **Ignoring Section 3 and 4 exclusions** — a student might discuss patentability of a "new business method" without noting it is excluded under Section 3(k).
> 4. **No flowcharts in Part B answers** — KTU examiners award **1–2 extra marks** for neat, labeled diagrams. Always include at least one flowchart in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 5. **Using "invention" and "innovation" interchangeably** — examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the "industrial application" criterion** — students often only discuss novelty and inventive step, losing 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Invention (Module 2)**
>
> ✅ An **invention** is a new, non-obvious, industrially applicable product or process.
> ✅ The three **statutory pillars of patentability** are: **Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application** (all must be present).
> ✅ **Discovery** = finding what nature already has (e.g., gravity). **Invention** = creating what did not exist (e.g., telephone). **Innovation** = improving/commercializing the invention (e.g., smartphone).
> ✅ **Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA)** is a hypothetical average practitioner used to test inventive step (non-obviousness).
> ✅ Types of inventions: **Product, Process, Machine, Composition of Matter, Improvement**.
> ✅ **Non-patentable inventions in India** (Section 3): frivolous inventions, atomic energy, agricultural methods, mathematical/business methods, computer programs *per se*, contrary to public order/morality.
> ✅ **Grace Period in India:** 12 months for official exhibitions (Section 11A); 6 months for academic disclosures (under Patents Rules, 2020 amendment — verify latest rules).
> ✅ **Priority date** = the earliest filing date — it is the reference point for novelty assessment.
> ✅ A **patent specification** must disclose the invention sufficiently to enable a PSITA to reproduce it (sufficiency of disclosure).
> ✅ For KTU answers: always use **flowcharts**, **tables of comparison**, and **statutory section references** (Section 2(1)(j), 2(1)(ja), 2(1)(ac), 3, 4) to score full marks.
> ✅ The **TRIPS Agreement (Article 27)** mandates product and process patents in all technology fields — this is the international standard KTU expects you to know.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventive Step
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Inventive Step # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Inventive Step** is a fundamental patentability criterion under the Patents Act, 1970 (India) and TRIPS Agreement. It is defined under **Section 2(1)(ja)** of the Indian Patents Act as: > *"a feature of an invention that involves technical advance as compared to the existing knowledge or having economic significance or both and that makes the invention not obvious to a person skilled in the art."* In international patent jurisprudence, this concept is referred to as the **"non-obviousness"** requirement — an invention must not be something that a person skilled in the relevant field would consider an obvious extension of what already exists. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Inventive Step is the **second of the three core patentability tests** under the Indian Patents Act, 1970, evaluated AFTER novelty (Section 2(1)(l)) and BEFORE industrial application (Section 2(1)(ac)). It is frequently tested in **Part A (3-mark)** questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **chefs apprentice** who has just spent 5 years learning Italian cuisine. Your master gives you a standard recipe for tomato pasta and asks: *"Can you create something new?"* - If you simply **reheat the same pasta** → That is **not novel** (already exists). - If you add **boiled rice to the pasta** → Any apprentice could think of that → That lacks an **inventive step** (it is obvious). - If you accidentally create a **slow-cooked tomato reduction technique** that produces a richer flavor profile never seen in any cookbook, AND this would **not be obvious** to any experienced chef → That has an **inventive step**. **Inventive Step = The "I couldn't have just guessed that" factor.** It is the difference between a *trivial tweak* and a *genuine technical leap*. Without an inventive step, patents would block competition on things anyone could figure out — stifling innovation instead of encouraging it. ## 1.3 Core Distinctions > [!NOTE] > **Novelty vs. Inventive Step — DO NOT CONFUSE** > > - **Novelty (Section 2(1)(l))**: Is this invention **disclosed anywhere in the world** before the priority date? (Yes/No binary test) > - **Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja))**: Even if it is new, would a skilled person find it **obvious** to derive from existing knowledge? (Qualitative judgment test) > > An invention can be **novel but obvious** (e.g., swapping tea for coffee in a known recipe). Such an invention **fails** the inventive step test. ## 1.4 Key Statutory Anchors - **Section 2(1)(ja)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 — Statutory definition. - **Section 3** — Exceptions (some inventions lack inventive step by statutory mandate, e.g., admixtures, mere rearrangement). - **Section 25(1)(e)** — Opposition ground on the basis of lack of inventive step. - **TRIPS Article 27** — Non-discrimination; inventive step is a universal patentability requirement. - **European Patent Convention (EPC) Article 56** — Equivalent provision in EU jurisdiction. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Inventive Step The statutory definition embeds **three distinct requirements**, all of which must be satisfied cumulatively: ### Pillar 1: Technical Advance - The invention must demonstrate a **technical contribution** over the **state of the art (prior art)**. - "Technical" is broadly interpreted — it can be a physical, chemical, electrical, or mechanical improvement. - **Merely aesthetic or mental improvements** are excluded (e.g., a new business method, a new chess rule). ### Pillar 2: Economic Significance - The invention should have **practical commercial value** or **industrial utility**. - This is often a derivative of technical advance — a technically better product usually has market value. - **Example**: A drug delivery mechanism that reduces manufacturing cost by 30% has economic significance. ### Pillar 3: Non-Obviousness to a Person Skilled in the Art (PSSA) - This is the **centerpiece** of the inventive step analysis. - The "Person Skilled in the Art" is a **legal hypothetical construct** — not a real person. - The PSSA is presumed to: - Have **average knowledge** of the general prior art in the field. - Have **access to standard textbooks, journals, and manuals**. - Have **ordinary creativity and diligence**, but **no inventive spark**. - Be **unaware of the invention** (cannot use hindsight). - **They are NOT presumed to be an expert** in adjacent fields or a genius. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Trick Question:** *"Who is the PSSA?"* > **Model Answer:** The PSSA is a hypothetical skilled worker in the relevant technical field possessing ordinary knowledge, average skill, and standard resources, but lacking any inventive capability. They are a **team of ordinary practitioners**, not a single genius. ## 2.2 The Three Classical Tests for Inventive Step (High-Yield) ### Test 1: The "Could-Would" Test (British Origin) - **Question posed:** Would a PSSA, faced with the same problem, **have been able (could)** to arrive at the invention, and **would they have been motivated (would)** to do so? - If **BOTH** answers are "Yes" → No inventive step. - If **either** answer is "No" → Inventive step is present. - **Originated in:** *Monsanto Co. v. Merck & Co.* [2000] RPC 709. ### Test 2: The Problem-Solution Approach (EPO Standard) Used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and increasingly adopted in India: - **Step 1:** Determine the **closest prior art**. - **Step 2:** Identify the **technical problem** that the prior art fails to solve. - **Step 3:** Evaluate whether the claimed solution, starting from the closest prior art, would be **obvious to the PSSA**. ### Test 3: The Graham v. John Deere Test (US Standard) A six-factor balancing test from the US Supreme Court: - Scope and content of the prior art. - Differences between the prior art and the claims. - Level of ordinary skill in the pertinent art. - Secondary considerations (commercial success, long-felt need, failure of others). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | **Concept** | **Definition / Test** | **Statutory Basis** | **Examiner Priority** | |---|---|---|---| | Inventive Step | Non-obvious technical advance with economic significance | Section 2(1)(ja) | 🔴 **Critical** | | PSSA | Hypothetical skilled person with average knowledge | Judicial construct | 🔴 **Critical** | | Could-Would Test | Could + Would the PSSA arrive at invention? | *Monsanto v. Merck* | 🟡 High | | Problem-Solution Approach | Closest prior art → Problem → Obvious solution? | EPO Guidelines | 🟡 High | | Hindsight Bias | Using post-invention knowledge to judge obviousness | Examiner's pitfall | 🟢 Moderate | | Secondary Indicia | Commercial success, long-felt need, failure of others | Graham v. John Deere | 🟢 Moderate | | Section 3(d) Bar | New form of known substance lacks inventive step | Indian Patents Act | 🔴 **Critical** | | Teaching-Suggestion-Motivation (TSM) | Prior art must teach, suggest, or motivate the invention | US Federal Circuit | 🟡 High | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statutory Exceptions under Section 3 — Automatic Loss of Inventive Step:** > - **Section 3(d)**: New form/derivative of a known substance (unless significantly higher efficacy is proven) — *crucial for pharmaceutical patents*. > - **Section 3(e)**: Mere admixture resulting in aggregation of known properties. > - **Section 3(f)**: Mere arrangement/rearrangement of known devices. > - **Section 3(i)**: Substances obtained by mere admixture. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** The **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** case crystallized Section 3(d) — a new crystalline form of Imatinib Mesylate was held to lack inventive step because it did not demonstrate significantly enhanced efficacy. - **Software/Tech:** Used to reject patents on obvious algorithms (e.g., simple database compression techniques). - **Mechanical Engineering:** Distinguishes a genuine mechanism improvement from a trivial substitution (e.g., replacing steel with aluminum where the same load-bearing properties are needed). - **Biotechnology:** Assesses whether a genetically modified organism is a routine laboratory extension or a true breakthrough. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework (Derivations) ## 3.1 The Exhaustive 5-Step Process for Determining Inventive Step This is the **valuation-key framework** examiners expect when you are given a fact pattern. Memorize this for 14-mark questions. ### Step 1: Establish the State of the Art (Prior Art) Identify **all relevant prior art** disclosed before the priority/filing date. This includes: - Published patents worldwide - Scientific journals and conference proceedings - Public use or sale - Traditional knowledge (relevant for Section 3(p)) ### Step 2: Identify the Person Skilled in the Art (PSSA) Construct the hypothetical PSSA by determining: - Their **field of expertise** (mechanical, chemical, biotech, etc.) - Their **educational qualifications** (typically a graduate degree) - Their **professional experience** (5–10 years average) - Their **access to literature and tools** ### Step 3: Frame the Technical Problem - What problem does the invention seek to solve? - Was this problem **known** in the prior art? - Is the problem the **same** as in the prior art, or a **newly identified** problem? ### Step 4: Apply the Obviousness Test - **Sub-step 4a:** Would the PSSA, starting from the closest prior art, naturally arrive at the invention? - **Sub-step 4b:** Is there any teaching, suggestion, or motivation (TSM) in the prior art? - **Sub-step 4c:** Check for secondary indicia (commercial success, long-felt need, failure of others). ### Step 5: Conclusion - If the answer to Step 4 is "**Yes, obvious**" → **No inventive step** → Patent refused. - If the answer to Step 4 is "**No, not obvious**" → **Inventive step present** → Patent may proceed. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example > **Fact Pattern:** A pharmaceutical company develops a new sustained-release tablet for Diabetes Type 2. The active ingredient (Metformin) is known. The new feature is a polymer coating that releases the drug over 12 hours instead of the standard 6 hours. ### Application of Framework: **Step 1 — Prior Art:** Metformin (known drug); sustained-release coatings using polymer X (known in literature). **Step 2 — PSSA:** A pharmaceutical formulation chemist with 5 years of experience in drug delivery systems. **Step 3 — Technical Problem:** Reduce dosing frequency to improve patient compliance. **Step 4 — Obviousness Test:** $$ \text{Is the solution obvious?} = \frac{\text{Prior Art Teaching} + \text{PSSA Routine Skill}}{\text{Unpredictable Hurdle}} $$ - Prior art teaches polymer coatings for sustained release. - PSSA would know that adjusting polymer thickness alters release time. - **However:** If the specific combination of polymer + thickness ratio + drug produces an **unexpected bioavailability** (e.g., 40% higher than predicted) → **Inventive step is present**. **Step 5 — Conclusion:** The invention passes the inventive step test **if and only if** an unexpected technical effect is demonstrated. Otherwise, the patent may be rejected under Section 3(d) for being a mere new form of a known substance. ## 3.3 The Hindsight Bias Trap > [!WARNING] > **Hindsight Bias** is the most common examiner pitfall. > > Once you know the answer, every problem looks easy. Patent examiners and judges must consciously **strip away the knowledge of the invention** and ask: *"Knowing ONLY the prior art, would I arrive at this solution?"* > > The Indian Supreme Court in *F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd. v. Cipla Ltd.* (2015) emphasized that **obviousness must be assessed through the eyes of the PSSA, not the judge.** ## 3.4 The "Inventive Step Formula" (Mnemonic for Exam) While there is no literal algebraic formula in patent law, examiners use this conceptual scoring: $$ \text{Inventive Step Score (ISS)} = (\text{Technical Advance}) + (\text{Economic Significance}) + (\text{PSSA Non-Obviousness}) $$ - All three terms must be **positive** for the score to qualify. - If any term = **0** → **Application fails**. - If all terms = **1** → **Strong inventive step case**. | **Component** | **Score = 0 (Fail)** | **Score = 1 (Pass)** | |---|---|---| | Technical Advance | Routine substitution | New mechanism or measurable improvement | | Economic Significance | No commercial value | Lower cost, higher yield, better safety | | PSSA Non-Obviousness | Obvious extension of prior art | Requires inventive leap | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart: The Inventive Step Determination Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patent Application Filed] --> B{Is it Novel?
Section 2-1-l} B -- No --> C[Application Rejected] B -- Yes --> D[Identify Person Skilled in the Art - PSSA] D --> E[Map Closest Prior Art] E --> F[Define Technical Problem] F --> G{Could-Would Test:
Would PSSA arrive at solution?} G -- Yes --> H{Any Secondary Indicia?
Commercial Success, Long-Felt Need} H -- Yes --> I[Borderline Case - Further Review] H -- No --> J[No Inventive Step - Reject] G -- No --> K{Technical Advance + Economic Significance?} K -- Yes --> L[Inventive Step Confirmed] K -- No --> J L --> M[Proceed to Industrial Application Test] I --> N[Examiner Discretion] N --> M ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Components of Inventive Step ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Statutory Definition - Section 2-1-ja] IS[Inventive Step] end subgraph S2[Three Mandatory Pillars] P1[Technical Advance] P2[Economic Significance] P3[Non-Obviousness to PSSA] end subgraph S3[Evaluation Tools] T1[Could-Would Test] T2[Problem-Solution Approach] T3[TSM Test] T4[Secondary Indicia] end subgraph S4[Statutory Exclusions] E1[Section 3-d: New Form of Known Substance] E2[Section 3-e: Mere Admixture] E3[Section 3-f: Mere Rearrangement] end IS --> P1 IS --> P2 IS --> P3 P3 --> T1 P3 --> T2 P3 --> T3 P3 --> T4 P1 --> E1 P1 --> E2 P1 --> E3 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Matrix: Inventive Step vs. Section 3(d) Bar ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[New Form of Known Substance Claimed] --> Q1{Demonstrates Significantly
Higher Efficacy?} Q1 -- Yes --> Pass[Passes Inventive Step Test] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Discovery of New Property
Not Applicable to Known Substance?} Q2 -- No --> Fail[Fails under Section 3-d
No Inventive Step] Q2 -- Yes --> Pass Start --> Q3{Derivative with
No New Efficacy?} Q3 -- Yes --> Fail Q3 -- No --> Pass ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: The PSSA (Person Skilled in the Art) Construct ```mermaid flowchart TD PSSA[Person Skilled in the Art - PSSA] PSSA --> K1[Average Knowledge of Prior Art] PSSA --> K2[Standard Literature Access] PSSA --> K3[Ordinary Creativity - No Inventive Spark] PSSA --> K4[5-10 Years Field Experience] PSSA --> K5[No Hindsight Bias] PSSA --> K6[Team of Practitioners, Not a Genius] K1 --> Outcome[Evaluates Obviousness from this Construct] K2 --> Outcome K3 --> Outcome K4 --> Outcome K5 --> Outcome K6 --> Outcome ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define inventive step as per the Indian Patents Act, 1970. State its statutory provision. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Inventive step is defined under **Section 2(1)(ja)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 as *"a feature of an invention that involves technical advance as compared to the existing knowledge or having economic significance or both and that makes the invention not obvious to a person skilled in the art."* The three essential elements are: **(i) technical advance**, **(ii) economic significance**, and **(iii) non-obviousness** to a person skilled in the art (PSSA). Without satisfying all three, an invention cannot be patented on the ground of inventive step. *(Marks distribution: Definition - 1, Statutory section - 1, Three elements - 1)* ### Question 2: Who is a "Person Skilled in the Art" (PSSA) in patent law? **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The PSSA is a **hypothetical legal construct** representing a person with **average knowledge, ordinary skill, and standard creativity** in the relevant technical field at the time of the invention.
