The PSSA is presumed to: (i) have access to all standard literature and prior art, (ii) possess 5-10 years of field experience, (iii) work in a **team** rather than as an individual, and (iv) **lack any inventive capability**. Crucially, the PSSA is **not a genius** and is **not influenced by hindsight** knowledge of the invention. *(Marks distribution: Definition - 1, Three attributes - 1, Hindsight exclusion - 1)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the concept of "Inventive Step" with reference to the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Discuss the "Person Skilled in the Art" (PSSA) doctrine in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Critically analyze the "Could-Would Test" and the "Problem-Solution Approach" as judicial tools for assessing inventive step. Use the *Novartis v. Union of India* (2013) judgment to illustrate. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) — Concept of Inventive Step and PSSA Doctrine (7 marks):** The concept of inventive step, codified under **Section 2(1)(ja)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970, is the **non-obviousness criterion** that distinguishes a true invention from a trivial modification of existing knowledge. It is the second of the three patentability tests, applied after novelty and before industrial application. The statutory definition contains **three cumulative requirements**: 1. **Technical Advance** — A measurable improvement over the prior art. 2. **Economic Significance** — Practical or commercial value. 3. **Non-Obviousness to PSSA** — The invention must not be obvious to a skilled worker. The **PSSA doctrine** is the cornerstone of the non-obviousness test. The PSSA is: - A **hypothetical person** with average knowledge in the field. - Equipped with **standard literature and tools**. - Possessing **ordinary creativity** (not inventive spark). - Aware of **all published prior art** before the priority date. - **Not** a person of extraordinary intelligence. - **Stripped of hindsight knowledge** of the actual invention. The PSSA doctrine prevents examiners from using the "retrospectoscope" — judging obviousness with full knowledge of the answer. This is critical in Indian jurisprudence, as emphasized in *Bishwanath Prasad Radhey Shyam v. Hindustan Metal Industries (1979)*. **(b) — Could-Would Test and Problem-Solution Approach (7 marks):** The **"Could-Would Test"** originated in British case law (*Monsanto Co. v. Merck & Co.*) and asks two questions: - **"Could"** the PSSA, faced with the same problem, have arrived at the invention? - **"Would"** the PSSA have been motivated to do so? If **both** answers are "yes" → No inventive step. If **either** is "no" → Inventive step exists. This test balances feasibility (could) against motivation (would), preventing rejection of inventions that are technically possible but commercially or logically unmotivated. The **"Problem-Solution Approach"** is the EPO's three-step methodology: 1. Identify the **closest prior art**. 2. Define the **technical problem** the prior art fails to solve. 3. Determine whether the claimed invention, viewed from the prior art, is an **obvious solution** to that problem. **Illustration — Novartis v. Union of India (2013):** The Supreme Court of India applied the inventive step test to Novartis's claim for a **new crystalline form of Imatinib Mesylate** (Gleevec). The Court held: - The new form was **novel** (passed Section 2(1)(l)). - However, the new form did **not demonstrate significantly enhanced therapeutic efficacy** over the known form. - Therefore, the invention was **barred under Section 3(d)** as a mere new form of a known substance, **lacking inventive step**. - The PSSA, starting from the known drug and prior art on polymorphs, would have been motivated to explore new crystalline forms — making the claim **obvious** in the "would" sense. The judgment established that **incremental modifications** in pharmaceuticals must clear a **higher threshold of demonstrated efficacy** to be patentable in India, in line with public health policy. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Distinguish between "Novelty" and "Inventive Step" as patentability criteria. Why is it possible for an invention to be novel but still lack inventive step? Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the statutory exclusions under **Section 3** of the Indian Patents Act that may render an invention devoid of inventive step. Explain with at least three examples. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) — Novelty vs. Inventive Step (7 marks):** | **Parameter** | **Novelty (Sec 2(1)(l))** | **Inventive Step (Sec 2(1)(ja))** | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Newness — not part of prior art | Non-obviousness over prior art | | **Test** | Binary (Yes/No) | Qualitative judgment | | **Time Frame** | Compared to any single prior disclosure | Compared to combined knowledge of PSSA | | **Standard** | Absolute novelty (worldwide) | Subjective obviousness to skilled person | | **Source** | Section 2(1)(l) | Section 2(1)(ja) | | **Failure Consequence** | No patent | No patent (even if novel) | **Why a Novel Invention Can Lack Inventive Step:** Novelty and inventive step are **independent and cumulative** requirements. An invention can be **novel** (never disclosed before) yet still be **obvious** to a PSSA because: - The PSSA, by combining two known techniques from different prior art references, could have arrived at the invention through routine experimentation. - The "flash of genius" is missing; only a routine application of existing knowledge is present. **Illustration:** A novel mobile phone with a **built-in bottle opener** — the combination is novel (no one has put a bottle opener on a phone), but it is **obvious** to a PSSA (a mechanical engineer designing phone accessories). No inventive step is involved. **(b) — Statutory Exclusions under Section 3 (7 marks):** Section 3 of the Indian Patents Act lists **exclusions** that automatically negate inventive step: 1. **Section 3(d) — New Form of Known Substance:** - A new form, salt, ester, polymorph, or derivative of a known substance is **not patentable** unless it demonstrates **significantly higher efficacy**. - *Example:* A new crystalline form of Aspirin is not patentable per se, unless it shows a 40% improvement in bioavailability. 2. **Section 3(e) — Mere Admixtures:** - A combination of two known substances resulting only in the aggregation of their known properties lacks inventive step. - *Example:* A painkiller that combines Paracetamol and Ibuprofen in their standard dosages (a known combination) — no inventive step. 3. **Section 3(f) — Mere Arrangement/Re-arrangement:** - A new arrangement of known devices producing no new functional effect is excluded. - *Example:* Reversing the order of stages in a manufacturing process where the output remains identical. 4. **Section 3(i) — Substances by Admixture:** - Any substance prepared by a mere admixture of known ingredients. These exclusions reflect India's policy of **preventing evergreening** — a strategy where patent holders make trivial changes to extend their monopoly beyond the original patent term. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Inventive Step Questions:** > 1. **Conflating Novelty with Inventive Step** — *Loses 1-2 marks.* Always mention that they are **separate and cumulative** tests. > 2. **Forgetting Section 2(1)(ja)** — *Loses 1 mark.* Always cite the **exact statutory provision** in the opening line. > 3. **Defining PSSA as a "real person"** — *Loses 1 mark.* The PSSA is a **legal hypothetical construct**, not an actual human being. > 4. **Ignoring Hindsight Bias** — *Loses 1 mark.* Examiners expect explicit mention of hindsight in any 7-mark question. > 5. **Omitting Indian case law** — *Loses 2-3 marks.* Always cite *Novartis v. Union of India (2013)* or *Biswanath Prasad v. Hindustan Metal* in 14-mark answers. > 6. **Writing vague "obviousness" answers** — *Loses 2 marks.* Always apply the **Could-Would Test** or **Problem-Solution Approach** with explicit steps. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inventive Step** > > - ✅ **Statutory Anchor:** Section 2(1)(ja) of the Indian Patents Act, 1970. > - ✅ **Three Pillars:** Technical Advance + Economic Significance + PSSA Non-Obviousness. > - ✅ **PSSA Construct:** Average skill, ordinary creativity, no hindsight, team-based, NOT a genius. > - ✅ **Could-Would Test:** Both must be "No" for inventive step to exist. Origin: *Monsanto v. Merck*. > - ✅ **Problem-Solution Approach:** Closest prior art → Problem → Obvious solution? (EPO method). > - ✅ **Graham v. John Deere Test:** US six-factor test including secondary indicia. > - ✅ **Section 3(d) Bar:** New forms of known substances need significantly higher efficacy (Novartis case). > - ✅ **Section 3(e), 3(f), 3(i):** Exclusions for admixtures, rearrangements, mere combinations. > - ✅ **Hindsight Bias:** Always strip away post-invention knowledge when assessing obviousness. > - ✅ **Secondary Indicia:** Commercial success, long-felt need, failure of others — these can rescue borderline cases. > - ✅ **Cumulative Test:** Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application — all three must pass. > - ✅ **International Equivalents:** TRIPS Art. 27, EPC Art. 56, US 35 U.S.C. § 103. > - ✅ **Mandatory Case Laws:** *Novartis v. UoI (2013)*, *Bishwanath Prasad (1979)*, *F. Hoffmann-La Roche v. Cipla (2015)*.

topics.title: New Invention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** New Invention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: NEW INVENTION — CORE FOUNDATION

## 1.1 Formal Statutory Definition (Indian Patents Act, 1970)

Under the **Indian Patents Act, 1970 (as amended by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005)**, the term *"New Invention"* is legally defined under **Section 2(1)(l)**, which states:

> *"new invention" means any invention or technology which has not been anticipated by publication in any document or used in the country or elsewhere in the world before the date of filing of the patent application with complete specification, i.e., the subject matter has not fallen in public domain or that it is novel.*"

The companion definition in **Section 2(1)(j)** defines *"invention"* as:

> *"invention" means a new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application."*

For an invention to qualify as a **"new invention"**, it must satisfy the twin pillars of **Novelty** and **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — the third pillar being **Industrial Application**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> A *New Invention* under Indian Patent Law is one whose subject matter has **not been disclosed to the public anywhere in the world** (absolute worldwide novelty) **prior to the date of filing** the complete specification, either by **written publication, oral disclosure, use, or any other manner**, before the priority date.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a cake 🍰. You mix flour, sugar, eggs, and bake it. That cake is "known" — anyone can find the recipe on the internet.

Now suppose you invent a **self-chilling cake** that cools itself in 30 seconds using a hidden phase-change material layer. This cake has never been baked, never been written about, never been sold anywhere on Earth.

➡️ **That is a "New Invention."**

If, however, someone in Japan already uploaded a YouTube video of the same self-chilling cake **two years before** you file your patent, then your invention is **NOT new** — it has been **anticipated by prior art** and you cannot get a patent.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Newness" Test Has Two Faces:**
> 1. **Geographical Scope:** Worldwide (not just India).
> 2. **Temporal Scope:** Any time **before the date of filing** (or priority date).
>
> If even **one document, one use, or one disclosure** exists anywhere on Earth before your filing date that describes the same invention → **Novelty is destroyed.**

## 1.3 Key Statutory Reference Points

- **Section 2(1)(l)** — Definition of *"new invention"* (Novelty).
- **Section 13(1)** — Novelty examination by the Controller.
- **Section 14** — Ante-dating of application.
- **Section 29–34** — Anticipation (prior claiming, prior publication, prior use, etc.).
- **Section 102 (Indian Patent Rule)** — Statement of Novelty.
- **Section 107A** — Anticipation by earlier application.
- **TRIPS Article 27** — Patentable subject matter (must be **new, involve an inventive step, and be capable of industrial application**).
</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 2: NEW INVENTION — THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Patentability

Under the **Indian Patents Act, 1970**, any invention to be patented **must simultaneously satisfy**:

| # | Pillar | Statutory Basis | Layman Meaning |
|---|--------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Novelty (Newness)** | Section 2(1)(l) | Never been disclosed to the public, anywhere, before filing date |
| 2 | **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** | Section 2(1)(ja) | Would not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PSITA) |
| 3 | **Industrial Application** | Section 2(1)(ac) | Can be made or used in any kind of industry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If even one pillar fails → the invention is NOT patentable.** A "New Invention" specifically addresses **Pillar 1**.

## 2.2 The Concept of "Prior Art"

**Prior Art** = All knowledge that has been made available to the public **anywhere in the world** before the **priority date / date of filing** through any medium whatsoever.

### 2.2.1 Sources of Prior Art (Worldwide Disclosure)

| Source Type | Example |
|-------------|---------|
| **Written Publication** | Journal article, book, thesis, conference paper, online PDF |
| **Oral Disclosure** | Conference talk, lecture, TV interview, podcast |
| **Public Use** | Manufacturing, selling, offering for sale, public demonstration |
| **Prior Patent** | Earlier-filed patent application (even pending) |
| **Internet Disclosure** | Website, blog, YouTube, social media post |
| **Internal Disclosure (Grace Period)** | Academic publication by inventor — Section 10A grace period of 12 months |

## 2.3 Tests for Determining Novelty

### Test 1: The "Anticipation Test" (Single Document / Single Prior Art Rule)

An invention is **anticipated (NOT novel)** if **a single prior art reference**, taken alone, discloses **all the essential features** of the claimed invention.

> **Rule:** If you, as a PSITA (Person Skilled in the Art), can pick up **one** document and from that document alone reproduce the invention → Novelty is **destroyed**.

### Test 2: The "Mosaicking Prohibition"

> [!WARNING]
> In India (and unlike the **PCT**), the **Mosaic Test** is generally **NOT allowed**. Novelty must be assessed **document-by-document**. Combining two or more references (mosaicking) to destroy novelty is **NOT permitted** for novelty assessment. However, mosaicking **IS permitted** for assessing **Inventive Step (obviousness)**.

### Test 3: The "Enablement Test"

A prior art reference anticipates the invention only if it **enables a PSITA to perform the invention** without undue burden — i.e., it discloses the invention in **sufficient detail to be reproduced**.

## 2.4 Geographical & Temporal Scope — KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Jurisdiction | Novelty Standard | Reference Point |
|--------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **India** | **Absolute Worldwide Novelty** | Any disclosure anywhere in the world |
| **USA (pre-AIA)** | First-to-Invent (historical) | US only |
| **USA (post-AIA, 2013+)** | Absolute Worldwide Novelty | Worldwide |
| **Europe (EPO)** | Absolute Worldwide Novelty | Worldwide |
| **Japan** | Absolute Worldwide Novelty | Worldwide |

> [!NOTE]
> **India follows the strictest novelty standard** — absolute worldwide novelty, in line with **TRIPS Article 27** and the **European Patent Convention (EPC) Article 54**.

## 2.5 The Priority Date & Date of Filing

- **Date of Filing** = The date on which a complete specification (or provisional + complete) is filed with the Indian Patent Office.
- **Priority Date** = The earliest date from which the patent's priority is claimed — typically the **filing date of the earliest application** whose disclosure forms the basis of the current application (governed by the **Paris Convention**).

> **Novelty is measured as of the Priority Date / Date of Filing**, whichever is earlier.

## 2.6 Statutory Exceptions to Novelty (Grace Period)

Under **Section 10A** of the Indian Patents Act (inserted by the 2005 Amendment):

> An invention disclosed by the inventor in a **scientific journal, academic institution, or at a recognised academic conference** shall **NOT destroy novelty**, provided the patent application is filed within **12 months** of such disclosure.

### Eligible Grace Period Disclosures:
- ✅ Publication in a **scientific journal**.
- ✅ Disclosure at a **scientific/technical meeting/conference**.
- ✅ Display at an **international exhibition** (with notification to the Controller).
- ❌ **NOT** eligible: Blog posts, YouTube videos, social media, personal websites, unauthorized disclosures.

## 2.7 Key Related Provisions (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Provision | Purpose | Effect on Novelty |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| **Section 2(1)(l)** | Definition of new invention | Sets the novelty benchmark |
| **Section 13** | Examination of application | Novelty examined by Patent Office |
| **Section 29** | Anticipation by previous publication in India | Destroys novelty |
| **Section 30** | Anticipation by previous publication outside India | Destroys novelty |
| **Section 31** | Anticipation by prior use in India | Destroys novelty |
| **Section 32** | Anticipation by prior use elsewhere | Destroys novelty |
| **Section 34** | Anticipation by prior claiming | Destroys novelty |
| **Section 10A** | Grace period for academic disclosure | **Preserves** novelty |
| **Section 107A** | Novelty in respect of earlier application | Preserves novelty |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of "New Invention" is the **gatekeeper** for every technological innovation in industry:

- **Pharmaceutical R&D** → Must conduct **Freedom-to-Operate (FTO)** searches to ensure their molecule is novel before filing.
- **Software / IT Firms** → Algorithms and software architectures must be searched against global prior art databases (Google Patents, USPTO, EPO Espacenet).
- **Mechanical Engineering** → New mechanisms, manufacturing processes must clear novelty searches.
- **Biotechnology** → New gene sequences, GMO constructs must show global novelty.
- **Startup Ecosystem** → Before pitching to investors, a startup must ensure its IP is novel — else the patent is invalid and the startup has **zero competitive moat**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A novelty search (prior art search) is **Step Zero** of any IP strategy. Without it, the entire patent filing may be wasted.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK

## 3.1 The Legal Reasoning Chain for Determining "New Invention"

Determining whether an invention qualifies as a "New Invention" follows a **structured 5-step analytical chain**, which the KTU Board examiners consistently test.

### STEP 1 — Identify the Date of Filing / Priority Date

The **clock** for novelty starts ticking from here.

$$D_{\text{novelty}} = \min(D_{\text{filing}}, D_{\text{priority}})$$

Where:
- $D_{\text{filing}}$ = Date the complete specification is filed at the Indian Patent Office.
- $D_{\text{priority}}$ = Earliest priority date claimed (under Paris Convention).

> Any disclosure **before** $D_{\text{novelty}}$ → destroys novelty.
> Any disclosure **after** $D_{\text{novelty}}$ → does NOT affect novelty.

---

### STEP 2 — Identify the Subject Matter of the Claimed Invention

Reduce the claimed invention to its **essential technical features** (the "gist"):

$$\text{Invention} = \{F_1, F_2, F_3, \dots, F_n\}$$

Where each $F_i$ is an essential technical feature of the independent claim.

**Example (Pharmaceutical):**
- $F_1$ = Active ingredient: Compound X
- $F_2$ = Dosage form: Oral tablet
- $F_3$ = Release mechanism: Sustained-release matrix

---

### STEP 3 — Conduct a Worldwide Prior Art Search

Search all databases for any disclosure before $D_{\text{novelty}}$:

$$\text{Prior Art Set} = \{PA_1, PA_2, PA_3, \dots, PA_m\}$$

Sources to search:
- **Indian Patent Office database** (ipindia.gov.in).
- **USPTO, EPO Espacenet, WIPO PATENTSCOPE, Google Patents**.
- **Scientific databases**: PubMed, IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, ScienceDirect.
- **Non-patent literature**: Books, journals, theses, conference proceedings.

---

### STEP 4 — Apply the Single-Document Anticipation Test

For **each** prior art reference $PA_i \in \text{Prior Art Set}$, check:

$$\text{Anticipation}(PA_i) = \begin{cases} \text{YES} & \text{if } \{F_1, F_2, F_3, \dots, F_n\} \subseteq PA_i \\ \text{NO} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> **If ANY single $PA_i$ contains ALL essential features → Novelty is DESTROYED.**

**Mosaicking Rule (India):**

$$\text{Mosaicking} = \begin{cases} \text{NOT ALLOWED} & \text{for Novelty assessment} \\ \text{ALLOWED} & \text{for Inventive Step (Obviousness) assessment} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In India, you **cannot combine two prior art documents** to argue lack of novelty. You can combine them only for **inventive step** (obviousness).

---

### STEP 5 — Check for Statutory Exceptions (Grace Period)

If a prior disclosure exists, check whether **Section 10A** applies:

$$\text{Grace Period} = \begin{cases} \text{APPLIES} & \text{if disclosure is in: (a) scientific journal, OR (b) academic conference, AND filed within 12 months} \\ \text{NOT APPLIES} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

If the grace period applies → novelty is **PRESERVED** despite the prior disclosure.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (Numerical-Style Analytical Problem)

### **Problem:**
Inventor **Dr. A** files an Indian patent application on **15 March 2023** for a novel solar cell coating. The following events occurred before 15 March 2023:

- **(i)** A Japanese journal published a paper on 10 January 2022 describing the **exact same coating**.
- **(ii)** Dr. A presented the coating at the **International Solar Energy Conference (ISEC)** on 5 August 2022.
- **(iii)** A US patent (US10101010) was filed on 20 June 2021 (still pending) describing the same coating.
- **(iv)** A YouTube video uploaded by an unrelated person on 1 December 2021 demonstrates the coating.

**Determine whether Dr. A's invention qualifies as a "New Invention."**

### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Date of Filing:**
$$D_{\text{novelty}} = 15 \text{ March 2023}$$

**Step 2 — Subject Matter:**
Essential features = {Solar cell coating of compound X with thickness Y}.

**Step 3 & 4 — Apply Anticipation Test:**

| Event | Date | Discloses All Features? | Grace Period? | Verdict |
|-------|------|------------------------|---------------|---------|
| (i) Japanese journal | 10 Jan 2022 | ✅ YES | ❌ NO (not inventor's own) | ❌ **Novelty DESTROYED** |
| (ii) ISEC conference (by Dr. A) | 5 Aug 2022 | ✅ YES | ✅ YES (Section 10A) | ✅ **Novelty PRESERVED** |
| (iii) US patent (pending) | 20 Jun 2021 | ✅ YES | ❌ NO | ❌ **Novelty DESTROYED** |
| (iv) YouTube video | 1 Dec 2021 | ✅ YES | ❌ NO | ❌ **Novelty DESTROYED** |

**Step 5 — Final Conclusion:**

Since events (i), (iii), and (iv) each independently disclose all essential features before $D_{\text{novelty}}$, the invention is **NOT a New Invention** under Section 2(1)(l).

$$\boxed{\text{Result: Dr. A's invention FAILS the novelty test → NOT patentable on grounds of lack of novelty.}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Even if the **inventor's own** conference disclosure is excused under Section 10A, the **third-party disclosures** (journal, YouTube, foreign patent) destroy novelty. Students must check **WHO** made the disclosure and **WHICH medium** it was in.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Novelty vs. Inventive Step

| Parameter | Novelty (Newness) | Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Legal Basis** | Section 2(1)(l) | Section 2(1)(ja) |
| **Question Asked** | *"Has it been disclosed before?"* | *"Would a PSITA find it obvious?"* |
| **Standard** | Single-document anticipation | Mosaicking permitted |
| **Time Frame** | Before priority date | Before priority date |
| **Test Reference** | Layperson can find the disclosure | Hypothetical PSITA assessment |
| **Failure Consequence** | Patent INVALID | Patent INVALID |
| **Section Reference** | Sections 29–34 | Section 25(1)(e) — opposition ground |

---

## 3.4 Comparative Jurisdictional Analysis

| Jurisdiction | Novelty Rule | Mosaicking for Novelty | Grace Period |
|--------------|---------------|------------------------|--------------|
| **India** | Absolute worldwide | ❌ Not allowed | 12 months (academic only) |
| **USA (post-AIA)** | Absolute worldwide | ❌ Not allowed | 12 months (broad) |
| **Europe (EPO)** | Absolute worldwide | ❌ Not allowed | 6 months (exhibition only) |
| **Japan** | Absolute worldwide | ❌ Not allowed | 6 months (broad) |
| **China** | Absolute worldwide | ❌ Not allowed | 6 months (academic/exhibition) |
</br>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Flowchart: The Novelty Determination Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Patent Application Filed"] --> B["Identify Date of Filing / Priority Date"]
    B --> C["Identify Essential Technical Features of Invention"]
    C --> D["Conduct Worldwide Prior Art Search"]
    D --> E{"Does ANY single prior art reference disclose ALL essential features?"}
    E -- "NO" --> F["Novelty SATISFIED → Proceed to Inventive Step Test"]
    E -- "YES" --> G{"Is the disclosure eligible under Section 10A Grace Period?"}
    G -- "YES (inventor's own academic disclosure within 12 months)" --> H["Novelty PRESERVED"]
    G -- "NO" --> I["Novelty DESTROYED → Invention is NOT New"]
    H --> F
    I --> J["END: Patent Application REJECTED"]
    F --> K["Continue Patentability Examination"]
    K --> L["END: Patent may be granted if all 3 pillars pass"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Three Pillars of Patentability with Focus on Novelty

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1["PILLAR 1: NOVELTY (New Invention)"]
        N1["Section 2(1)(l)"] --> N2["Worldwide Disclosure Test"]
        N2 --> N3["Single-Document Anticipation"]
        N3 --> N4["Section 10A Grace Period"]
    end

    subgraph P2["PILLAR 2: INVENTIVE STEP"]
        I1["Section 2(1)(ja)"] --> I2["PSITA Obviousness Test"]
        I2 --> I3["Mosaicking Permitted"]
    end

    subgraph P3["PILLAR 3: INDUSTRIAL APPLICATION"]
        A1["Section 2(1)(ac)"] --> A2["Capability Test"]
    end

    P1 --> MERGE{"All 3 Pillars Satisfied?"}
    P2 --> MERGE
    P3 --> MERGE
    MERGE -- "YES" --> GRANT["PATENT GRANTED"]
    MERGE -- "NO" --> REJECT["APPLICATION REJECTED"]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Sources of Prior Art

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PA["WORLDWIDE PRIOR ART SOURCES"]

    PA --> W["WRITTEN PUBLICATIONS"]
    PA --> O["ORAL DISCLOSURES"]
    PA --> U["PUBLIC USE"]
    PA --> PP["PRIOR PATENT APPLICATIONS"]
    PA --> I["INTERNET / DIGITAL MEDIA"]

    W --> W1["Journals"]
    W --> W2["Books"]
    W --> W3["Theses"]
    W --> W4["Conference Papers"]

    O --> O1["Conference Talks"]
    O --> O2["Lectures"]
    O --> O3["TV / Radio Interviews"]

    U --> U1["Manufacturing"]
    U --> U2["Sale / Offer for Sale"]
    U --> U3["Public Demonstration"]

    PP --> PP1["Earlier Filed Patents"]
    PP --> PP2["PCT Applications"]
    PP --> PP3["Convention Applications"]

    I --> I1["Websites"]
    I --> I2["Blogs"]
    I --> I3["YouTube"]
    I --> I4["Social Media"]
```

---

## 4.4 Timeline Schematic: Novelty Assessment Window

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["t = -∞ ─── PAST (Any prior disclosure)"] --> T2["Priority Date / Filing Date"]
    T2 --> T3["t = +∞ ─── FUTURE (Irrelevant for novelty)"]

    T1 --> ASSESS{"Does ANY disclosure on left side of T2 disclose the invention?"}
    ASSESS -- "YES" --> DESTROY["NOVELTY DESTROYED"]
    ASSESS -- "NO" --> PRESERVE["NOVELTY PRESERVED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visualization Concept:** The priority date is the **single dividing line** between "destroying prior art" (left) and "irrelevant post-filing events" (right). Everything on the **left** matters; everything on the **right** does not (for novelty).
</br>
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define "new invention" under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. State the relevant section.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under **Section 2(1)(l)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970, a *"new invention"* means any invention or technology **which has not been anticipated by publication in any document or used in the country or elsewhere in the world before the date of filing of the patent application with complete specification**, meaning the subject matter has not fallen in the public domain.

The key elements are:
1. **Absolute worldwide novelty** (not restricted to India).
2. **Temporal limit** — assessed as of the date of filing / priority date.
3. **Disclosure** — through publication, use, or any other manner.

[3 marks — full breakdown: 1 mark for section, 2 marks for elements]

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**What is the grace period for novelty under Indian Patent Law? Mention its statutory provision and scope.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **grace period** is provided under **Section 10A** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 (inserted by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005).

- **Duration:** **12 months** from the date of disclosure.
- **Eligible disclosures:**
  - Publication by the inventor in a **scientific journal**.
  - Disclosure by the inventor at a **scientific/academic conference**.
  - Display at an **international exhibition** (with notification).
- **NOT eligible:** Internet disclosures, YouTube, blogs, third-party disclosures.

If the patent application is filed within 12 months of such disclosure, the disclosure **does NOT destroy novelty**.

[3 marks — full breakdown: 1 mark section, 1 mark duration, 1 mark eligible disclosures]

---

## **PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

#### **(a) Discuss in detail the statutory definition and essential elements of "new invention" under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. How is novelty assessed in India? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A "new invention" is the cornerstone requirement of patentability under Indian law, ensuring that only genuinely unprecedented subject matter receives patent protection.

**Statutory Definition (2 marks):**
Under **Section 2(1)(l)** of the Patents Act, 1970, a "new invention" is defined as any invention or technology which has not been anticipated by publication in any document or used in the country or elsewhere in the world before the date of filing of the patent application with complete specification.

**Essential Elements (2 marks):**
1. **Geographical Universality:** Worldwide scope — any disclosure anywhere destroys novelty.
2. **Temporal Reference:** Novelty is assessed as of the **date of filing** (or priority date).
3. **Forms of Disclosure Anticipated:** Written publication, oral disclosure, public use, or any other manner.
4. **Public Domain:** The subject matter must not have fallen into the public domain.

**Novelty Assessment in India (2 marks):**
Novelty is assessed using the **single-document anticipation test** — a single prior art reference that discloses all essential features destroys novelty. Mosaicking (combining multiple references) is **NOT permitted** for novelty assessment in India. Statutory exceptions under Section 10A provide a 12-month grace period for academic disclosures by the inventor.

[Total: 7 marks]

---

#### **(b) Explain with examples the various sources of prior art that can destroy novelty. Differentiate between anticipation by publication and anticipation by use. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sources of Prior Art (3 marks):**

| Source | Example | Destroys Novelty? |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| Scientific journal article | Paper in Nature, IEEE Transactions | ✅ Yes |
| Book / Textbook | Engineering textbook with the same invention | ✅ Yes |
| Thesis / Dissertation | PhD thesis archived in university | ✅ Yes |
| Conference presentation | Talk at an international conference (by anyone) | ✅ Yes |
| Prior patent application | Earlier filed patent (granted or pending) | ✅ Yes |
| Public use / Sale | Manufacturing and selling the product | ✅ Yes |
| Internet | YouTube video, blog post, website | ✅ Yes |
| **Inventor's own academic disclosure within 12 months** | Conference talk by inventor | ❌ **No — Grace Period (Section 10A)** |

**Anticipation by Publication vs. Use (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Anticipation by Publication | Anticipation by Use |
|-----------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **Section** | Sections 29 & 30 | Sections 31 & 32 |
| **Medium** | Document, journal, book, internet | Physical manufacture, sale, demonstration |
| **Geography** | Anywhere in the world | Anywhere in the world |
| **Public Accessibility** | Document must be publicly accessible | Use must be in public (not secret) |
| **Example** | Publication of a new drug formula in a journal in Germany | Selling a new machine in a shop in Japan |

**Key Distinction:** Publication destroys novelty through *information* in the public domain; use destroys novelty through *physical embodiment* of the invention being accessible to the public.

[Total: 7 marks]

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

#### **(a) What is the date of filing and priority date? Explain their significance in determining novelty of an invention. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Date of Filing (3 marks):**
The date of filing is the date on which the patent application (with complete specification, or provisional specification followed by complete specification within 12 months) is received and accepted by the Indian Patent Office.

For novelty assessment, this date is the **reference point**: any disclosure **before** this date may destroy novelty.

**Priority Date (2 marks):**
Under the **Paris Convention** (to which India is a signatory), an applicant who first files in a convention country can subsequently file in India within **12 months** and claim the **priority date** of the earlier foreign filing.

**Formula:**
$$D_{\text{novelty reference}} = \min(D_{\text{filing}}, D_{\text{priority}})$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. **Earlier priority date** → wider scope of prior art must be examined → stricter novelty test.
2. **Allows international applicants** to safeguard their priority globally without losing novelty.
3. **Critical for novelty** because disclosures between the priority date and Indian filing date are **NOT** citable against novelty.

[Total: 7 marks]

---

#### **(b) Explain the concept of grace period under Section 10A. Discuss its scope, limitations, and importance in academic research. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
Section 10A of the Indian Patents Act provides a **12-month grace period** during which certain disclosures by the inventor **shall not be considered prior art** for the purpose of assessing novelty.

**Scope (3 marks):**
Eligible disclosures:
- ✅ Publication in a **scientific journal** by the inventor.
- ✅ Disclosure at a **scientific or academic conference** by the inventor.
- ✅ Display at an **international exhibition** (with prior notification to the Controller).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- ❌ Does NOT cover internet disclosures, blogs, YouTube, social media.
- ❌ Does NOT cover disclosures by third parties (e.g., a co-author publishing independently).
- ❌ Strict 12-month window — disclosure beyond 12 months destroys novelty.

**Importance in Academic Research (1 mark):**
Encourages **researchers and academicians** to publish their findings in journals/conferences without fear of losing patent rights, bridging the gap between academic publication and IP protection.

[Total: 7 marks]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Novelty with Inventive Step:** Students frequently interchange Section 2(1)(l) and Section 2(1)(ja). Novelty is about **anticipation by prior disclosure**; inventive step is about **obviousness to a PSITA**. They are **distinct tests**.
> 2. **Assuming India-Only Novelty:** A common error is believing novelty is assessed only against Indian prior art. **India follows absolute worldwide novelty** — any disclosure anywhere matters.
> 3. **Allowing Mosaicking for Novelty:** This is **wrong**. Combining two documents to destroy novelty is **NOT permitted** in India (it is permitted only for inventive step).
> 4. **Missing the Grace Period Condition:** The 12-month grace period applies **only to the inventor's own academic disclosures** — not to third-party disclosures.
> 5. **Forgetting Section References:** Always cite the **Section number** along with the answer. Examiners award 1 mark purely for correct statutory citation.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Section 2(1)(l)** defines *"new invention"* — memorize verbatim for the Board exam.
- ✅ **India follows absolute worldwide novelty** — the strictest standard.
- ✅ Novelty is assessed as of the **date of filing** or **priority date**, whichever is earlier.
- ✅ **Single-document anticipation test** — one prior art document disclosing all essential features is enough to destroy novelty.
- ✅ **Mosaicking is NOT permitted** for novelty in India; it is permitted for **inventive step**.
- ✅ **Section 10A** provides a **12-month grace period** for the inventor's own **scientific journal publications, academic conference disclosures, and international exhibition displays**.
- ✅ **Internet disclosures (YouTube, blogs, social media)** do **NOT** qualify for the grace period.
- ✅ Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application = **The Three Pillars of Patentability**.
- ✅ **TRIPS Article 27** mandates worldwide novelty — India is fully compliant.
- ✅ The **Paris Convention** allows claiming **priority date** from earlier foreign filings (within 12 months).
- ✅ **Prior art search** is the most critical preliminary step before any patent filing.
- ✅ Always cite **section numbers** in Board exam answers for full credit.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Patent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product Patent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Product Patent** is a statutory right granted by the national patent office to an inventor or assignee, conferring exclusive rights over a **new, tangible article, substance, device, machine, or composition of matter** for a fixed period (typically **20 years** from the date of filing), in exchange for full public disclosure of the invention.

Under the **Indian Patents Act, 1970 (as amended by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005)**, product patents are recognized for all fields of technology, including pharmaceuticals, chemicals, food products, and biotechnology, provided the product satisfies the tripartite test of **Novelty**, **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)**, and **Industrial Application**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Anchor:** Product patents in India are governed by **Section 2(1)(j)** (definition of "invention"), **Section 2(1)(l)** (patentable subject matter), and **Section 5(1)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 (as amended in 2005).

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line KTU Board Definition:** A *Product Patent* protects the **physical article itself** — a drug molecule, a chip, a chemical compound — whereas a *Process Patent* protects only the *method* of producing it.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cake vs Recipe" Story

Imagine you walk into a bakery and taste the most extraordinary cake you have ever eaten.

- If you patent **the cake itself** (its unique composition, structure, taste profile), that is a **Product Patent**. Nobody else may produce, sell, or import *that specific cake* without your permission for 20 years.
- If you patent **the recipe** (the steps, temperatures, mixing order), that is a **Process Patent**. Others can still bake the cake, but they cannot use *your exact procedure*.

| Analogy Component | Real World Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The cake (final article) | The **Product** (drug molecule, device, compound) |
| The recipe (method) | The **Process** (synthesis route, manufacturing steps) |
| The baker | The **Patent Holder** |
| The bakery licence | **Exclusive Rights** under the Act |
| Patenting the cake | **Product Patent** |
| Patenting the recipe | **Process Patent** |

This is exactly what happened in the landmark **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** case — the Supreme Court denied Novartis a **product patent** on the beta-crystalline form of Imatinib Mesylate, while earlier process patents on the same drug had already been granted.

---

## 1.3 Why Product Patents Matter in Engineering & Industry

- **Pharmaceutical Sector:** Product patents are the *economic backbone* of the global pharma industry — innovator companies recover R&D investment through exclusivity over the drug molecule itself (e.g., Pfizer's patent on Sildenafil Citrate / Viagra).
- **Chemical & Biotech:** New molecules, genetically engineered enzymes, microbial strains.
- **Mechanical & Electronics:** New machine configurations, semiconductor devices, novel alloys.
- **IT/Hardware:** Unique hardware architecture, chip designs (in coordination with design patents).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often frame a 3-mark question asking to *"Distinguish between Product Patent and Process Patent"* — answer using the **Cake vs Recipe** analogy and cite **Section 5 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970**.

---

## 1.4 Categories of Patentable Products

A product patent may be obtained for any of the following tangible inventions:

1. **Chemical Substances** — new molecular entities, formulations, polymorphs.
2. **Pharmaceutical Products** — drug compounds, dosage forms, biologics.
3. **Machines & Apparatus** — new engines, turbines, robotic arms.
4. **Manufactured Articles** — tools, fasteners, structural members.
5. **Biotechnological Products** — recombinant proteins, GMOs (subject to Section 3 exclusions).
6. **Alloys & Compositions** — metal compositions, ceramic blends.
7. **Microorganisms** (after TRIPS compliance, 2005 amendment).

> [!WARNING]
> **Section 3 Exclusions (KTU High-Yield):** Even a tangible product is *not* patentable if it is:
> (a) frivolous or contrary to public order/morality,
> (b) a discovery of a scientific principle,
> (c) a new form of a known substance which does not result in enhanced efficacy,
> (d) a mere admixture resulting in aggregation of properties,
> (e) plants/animals in whole or part (other than microorganisms),
> (f) a traditional knowledge aggregation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Legal Foundation — Statutory Framework

### 2.1.1 Historical Evolution of Product Patents in India

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-1970:} & \quad \text{Product patents allowed in colonial era (Indian Patents \& Designs Act, 1911)} \\
\text{1970:} & \quad \text{Indian Patents Act — Only Process Patents for drugs, food, chemicals} \\
\text{1995:} & \quad \text{TRIPS Agreement signed (Article 27) — Mandated product patents} \\
\text{1999:} & \quad \text{First Amendment — mailbox applications} \\
\text{2002:} & \quad \text{Second Amendment — Exclusive Marketing Rights (EMR)} \\
\text{2005:} & \quad \text{Third Amendment — Product patents restored for all fields}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> India adopted the **TRIPS-compatible product patent regime** on **01 January 2005**, fulfilling its WTO obligation under **Article 27 of the TRIPS Agreement**, which prohibits discrimination between product and process patents across fields of technology.

### 2.1.2 Statutory Definition — Section 2(1)(j)

> **"Invention"** means a new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application.

The term **"new product"** is the operative phrase that legally inaugurates the concept of a product patent under Indian law.

---

## 2.2 The Tripartite Patentability Test (Section 2(1)(ja) & 2(1)(ac))

A product must satisfy **three cumulative conditions** to be granted a product patent:

### Condition 1 — Novelty (Section 2(1)(l))

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Novelty Test} &= \text{Anticipation by prior art} \\
\text{If Prior Art EXISTS} &\Rightarrow \text{Novelty LOST} \Rightarrow \text{Patent REFUSED} \\
\text{If Prior Art ABSENT} &\Rightarrow \text{Novelty PRESENT} \Rightarrow \text{Proceed to Step 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Standard:** *Absolute Novelty Worldwide* — Indian law adopts the *first-to-file* system with **worldwide prior art search**.
- **Reference:** Prior art includes any disclosure anywhere in the world (published, used, or patented) **before the priority date**.

### Condition 2 — Inventive Step / Non-Obviousness (Section 2(1)(ja))

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Person of Ordinary Skill (POSITA)} &\xrightarrow{\text{examines}} \text{Prior Art} \\
\text{Question:} &\text{"Would the POSITA find the product obvious?"} \\
\text{If YES} &\Rightarrow \text{No Inventive Step} \Rightarrow \text{Patent REFUSED} \\
\text{If NO} &\Rightarrow \text{Inventive Step PRESENT} \Rightarrow \text{Proceed to Step 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

- The test is *subjective* and *field-specific* — assessed from the perspective of a person reasonably skilled in the relevant art.

### Condition 3 — Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))

The product must be capable of being **made or used in an industry** — a low threshold easily met by most tangible inventions.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Product Patent | Process Patent |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject Matter** | Tangible article / compound / device | Method / procedure of manufacture |
| **Legal Basis (India)** | Section 2(1)(j), 2(1)(l), Section 5 | Section 2(1)(j), 2(1)(l) |
| **Scope of Protection** | The product itself — *all manufacturing methods* | Only the claimed process |
| **Infringement** | Making, using, selling, importing the product, *regardless of process* | Using the specific patented process |
| **Burden of Proof (Infringement)** | Easy — product presence is sufficient | Difficult — plaintiff must prove process was used |
| **Reverse Engineering** | Does **NOT** defeat the patent | Can be used to design around |
| **TRIPS Compliance** | Required under Article 27 | Always permitted |
| **Term** | **20 years** from filing date | **20 years** from filing date |
| **Renewal** | Annual renewal after 3rd year | Annual renewal after 3rd year |
| **Strategic Value** | Higher (broader exclusivity) | Lower (narrower exclusivity) |
| **KTU Board Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 2.4 Rights Conferred by a Product Patent (Section 48)

Upon grant, the patent holder obtains **exclusive rights** to:

1. **Make** the patented product.
2. **Use** the patented product.
3. **Sell** the patented product.
4. **Import** the patented product.
5. **Offer for sale** the patented product.

Any third party performing these acts **without consent = infringement** under **Section 104**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A product patent gives **positive rights against the world** (erga omnes). The patentee may assign, license, or sue for infringement independently of any process used.

---

## 2.5 Engineering & Industry Utility

| Industry Sector | Strategic Use of Product Patent |
|---|---|
| Pharmaceuticals | Block generic entry for 20 years |
| Biotechnology | Protect recombinant proteins, GM seeds |
| Information Technology | Protect semiconductor chip architectures |
| Chemical Engineering | Protect novel alloys, polymers, catalysts |
| Mechanical Engineering | Protect novel machine configurations |
| Aerospace | Protect composite materials, engine components |

---

## 2.6 Limitations on Product Patent Rights

Even with a product patent, the following **compulsory limitations** apply under the Indian Act:

1. **Compulsory Licensing (Section 84):** Government may license to third parties if:
   - Reasonable requirements of public not satisfied,
   - Product not available at affordable price,
   - Not worked in India (Section 83).
2. **Government Use (Section 100):** Central Government may use the invention for public purposes.
3. **Bolar Exception (Section 107A):** Generic manufacturers may use the patented product *before expiry* for regulatory approval.
4. **Parallel Import (Section 107A(b)):** Importation of patented product by a licensee is permitted.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Patentability Evaluation Workflow

The following analytical sequence is used by the Indian Patent Office to assess a product patent application. Every step must be **explicitly documented** in the First Examination Report (FER).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Subject Matter Classification} \\
\text{Is the application directed to a "product" (tangible article)?} \\
\quad \text{YES} \Rightarrow \text{Proceed to Step 2} \\
\quad \text{NO} \Rightarrow \text{Route to Process Examination} \\[1em]

\textbf{Step 2: Section 3 Exclusion Check} \\
\text{Is the product excluded under Section 3?} \\
\quad \text{YES (e.g., frivolous, immoral, known substance w/o enhanced efficacy)} \Rightarrow \text{REFUSED} \\
\quad \text{NO} \Rightarrow \text{Proceed to Step 3} \\[1em]

\textbf{Step 3: Novelty Examination (Section 2(1)(l))} \\
\text{Search Prior Art — Patents, Publications, Public Use, Public Knowledge} \\
\text{Priority Date (PD) compared to Disclosure Date (DD)} \\
\quad \text{If DD < PD} \Rightarrow \text{Novelty PRESENT} \Rightarrow \text{Step 4} \\
\quad \text{If DD} \geq \text{PD} \Rightarrow \text{Novelty LOST} \Rightarrow \text{REFUSED} \\[1em]

\textbf{Step 4: Inventive Step Examination (Section 2(1)(ja))} \\
\text{Hypothetical POSITA exposed to prior art:} \\
\text{Would the product be obvious?} \\
\quad \text{YES} \Rightarrow \text{No Inventive Step} \Rightarrow \text{REFUSED} \\
\quad \text{NO} \Rightarrow \text{Inventive Step PRESENT} \Rightarrow \text{Step 5} \\[1em]

\textbf{Step 5: Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))} \\
\text{Can the product be made or used in industry?} \\
\quad \text{YES} \Rightarrow \text{PATENT GRANTED} \\
\quad \text{NO} \Rightarrow \text{REFUSED}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Novartis AG v. Union of India (2013)

> **The case that every KTU examiner expects you to know.**

**Facts:**
- Novartis sought a **product patent** on the **beta-crystalline form** of Imatinib Mesylate (anti-leukemia drug — Gleevec).
- The alpha-crystalline form was already known in prior art (claimed in Novartis's earlier process patent, IN-39675).
- The beta form was a **new physical form** (polymorph) of a known substance.

**Step-by-step legal reasoning by the Supreme Court:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Subject Matter} \\
\text{Imatinib Mesylate (beta form)} = \text{Tangible chemical substance} = \text{Product} \\[1em]

\textbf{Step 2: Section 3(d) Check} \\
\text{Question: Does the new form show "enhanced efficacy" over the known form?} \\
\text{Novartis evidence: 30\% better bioavailability} \\
\text{Court's Analysis:} \\
\quad \text{Efficacy} \neq \text{Bioavailability} \\
\quad \text{Efficacy} = \text{Therapeutic effect on disease} \\
\text{Court ruled: NO enhanced efficacy demonstrated} \\[1em]

\textbf{Step 3: Result} \\
\text{Section 3(d) bars patents on new forms of known substances} \\
\text{without enhanced efficacy} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Product patent REFUSED}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Outcome:** A landmark precedent establishing that **mere polymorphs, salts, esters, or new physical forms** are not patentable in India without demonstrating **enhanced therapeutic efficacy** — a uniquely Indian safeguard against **evergreening**.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Product Patent Doctrines

| Doctrine | Principle | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Evergreening** | Repeated minor modifications to extend monopoly | Section 3(d) bars this |
| **Bolar Exception** | Early use for regulatory approval permitted | Section 107A |
| **Compulsory License** | Government override in public interest | Section 84 (Natco v. Bayer 2012) |
| **Parallel Import** | Legitimate importation permitted | Section 107A(b) |
| **Exhaustion of Rights** | First sale exhausts patent rights | Implied in Section 48 |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Natco Pharma v. Bayer Corporation (2012)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Bayer held product patent on Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar)} \\
\textbf{Natco sought Compulsory License under Section 84(b)} \\
\quad \text{(3) — Reasonable price not satisfied} \\
\quad \text{(4) — Not worked in India} \\[0.5em]

\text{Calculation of royalty:} \\
\text{Compulsory License Royalty} = \text{Net Sales} \times \text{Royalty Rate} \\
\text{Royalty Rate fixed} = 6\% \text{ (significantly below Bayer's 28\% demand)} \\[0.5em]

\text{Outcome: First-ever Indian compulsory license granted for a} \\
\text{liver/kidney cancer drug — landmark for public health jurisprudence}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Hook:** When discussing product patents in pharma, the examiner expects citations of **Novartis 2013** AND **Natco 2012**. Memorize both.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Product Patent Application & Grant Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Invention Disclosure] --> B[Prior Art Search]
    B --> C{Novelty Present?}
    C -- No --> Z[Application Abandoned]
    C -- Yes --> D[Draft Patent Specification]
    D --> E[File Application at IPO - Form 1 & 2]
    E --> F[Application Number & Date of Filing]
    F --> G[Publication in Patent Journal - 18 months]
    G --> H{Request for Examination - Form 18}
    H --> I[First Examination Report FER]
    I --> J{Tripartite Test}
    J -- Novelty --> K[Novelty Check]
    J -- Inventive Step --> L[Non-Obviousness Check]
    J -- Industrial Application --> M[Industrial Use Check]
    K --> N{All Conditions Met?}
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N -- No --> O[Hearing Opportunity]
    O --> P{Reconsideration Granted?}
    P -- No --> Z
    P -- Yes --> Q[Patent Granted]
    N -- Yes --> Q
    Q --> R[Patent Published in Gazette]
    R --> S[Sealing of Patent - Section 45]
    S --> T[20 Year Monopoly Begins]
    T --> U[Annual Renewal Fees]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tripartite Patentability Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Product Invention] --> B[Tripartite Test Engine]
    B --> C[Novelty Module]
    B --> D[Inventive Step Module]
    B --> E[Industrial Application Module]
    C --> C1[Prior Art Database Search]
    C1 --> C2{Anticipated?}
    C2 -- Yes --> X[REJECTED - Lack of Novelty]
    C2 -- No --> C3[Novelty Confirmed]
    D --> D1[POSITA Simulation]
    D1 --> D2{Obvious to Skilled Person?}
    D2 -- Yes --> X
    D2 -- No --> D3[Inventive Step Confirmed]
    E --> E1[Industrial Utility Test]
    E1 --> E2{Made or Used in Industry?}
    E2 -- No --> X
    E2 -- Yes --> E3[Industrial Application Confirmed]
    C3 --> F[All Three Confirmed]
    D3 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[PATENT GRANTED]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Product vs Process Patent

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROD[Product Patent]
        P1[Tangible Article]
        P2[Drug Molecule]
        P3[Machine Device]
        P4[Compound Substance]
        P5[Exclusive Rights: Make Use Sell Import]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Process Patent]
        Q1[Method Steps]
        Q2[Synthesis Route]
        Q3[Manufacturing Procedure]
        Q4[Algorithm of Operations]
        Q5[Exclusive Rights: Use the Specific Method]
    end

    P1 --> P5
    P2 --> P5
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    Q1 --> Q5
    Q2 --> Q5
    Q3 --> Q5
    Q4 --> Q5

    style PROD fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style PROC fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style P5 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style Q5 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
```

---

## 4.4 Infringement Analysis Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Third Party Activity Detected] --> B{Does activity involve Product?}
    B -- No --> Z[Not Product Infringement]
    B -- Yes --> C{Does third party have License?}
    C -- Yes --> W[Licensed Activity - No Infringement]
    C -- No --> D{Activity involves Make Use Sell Import?}
    D -- No --> Z
    D -- Yes --> E[Product Infringement Established]
    E --> F[Sue for Relief - Section 104]
    F --> G[Damages Injunction Account of Profits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Legend:** Solid arrows indicate procedural flow; shaded boxes (blue / orange / green / red) represent Patent, Process, Granted, and Infringement outcomes respectively.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Define Product Patent. How is it different from a Process Patent?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Product Patent** is a statutory right granted for a new, tangible article, device, or substance, conferring exclusive rights to the patentee to make, use, sell, and import the product for 20 years.

**Differences:**

| Basis | Product Patent | Process Patent |
|---|---|---|
| Subject Matter | Tangible product | Method/procedure |
| Scope | Broader (covers all processes producing it) | Narrower (only the claimed method) |
| Infringement Proof | Easier (product found = infringement) | Harder (plaintiff must prove process use) |
| TRIPS Mandate | Required by Article 27 | Always permitted |

A product patent protects the **article itself**, while a process patent protects the **route to making it**.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **State the three conditions for grant of a Product Patent under the Indian Patents Act, 1970.**

**Model Answer:**

Under Section 2(1)(j) of the Indian Patents Act, 1970, a product must satisfy the following **three cumulative conditions**:

1. **Novelty** [Section 2(1)(l)] — The product must not have been previously disclosed anywhere in the world before the priority date.
2. **Inventive Step** [Section 2(1)(ja)] — The product must not be obvious to a person ordinarily skilled in the art (POSITA).
3. **Industrial Application** [Section 2(1)(ac)] — The product must be capable of being made or used in an industry.

All three conditions must be satisfied cumulatively; failure of any one leads to refusal of the patent application.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]** (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]:

> **Explain in detail the legal framework governing Product Patents in India. Discuss the historical evolution from the 1970 Act to the 2005 Amendment.**

**Model Solution:**

The legal framework for product patents in India is anchored in the **Indian Patents Act, 1970**, significantly amended through the **Patents (Amendment) Acts of 1999, 2002, and 2005** to comply with the **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**.

**Historical Evolution:**

- **Pre-1970 (Indian Patents & Designs Act, 1911):** Recognized both product and process patents, including for pharmaceuticals. This led to monopolistic practices and high drug prices.
- **1970 — Indian Patents Act:** Restricted to **process patents only** for drugs, food, and chemicals (Section 5). This enabled Indian pharma to manufacture low-cost generics, earning India the title *"Pharmacy of the Global South."*
- **1994 — TRIPS Agreement:** Under **Article 27**, WTO members were mandated to provide **product patents in all fields** without discrimination, with a transition deadline of 1 January 2005 for developing nations.
- **1999 Amendment:** Introduced the **"mailbox" system** to receive product patent applications for later processing.
- **2002 Amendment:** Permitted **Exclusive Marketing Rights (EMR)** for mailbox applications.
- **2005 Amendment (effective 01.01.2005):** **Product patents restored** across all fields — a watershed moment. Pharmaceutical innovators could now obtain 20-year monopolies on drug molecules themselves.

**Key Statutory Provisions:**

- Section 2(1)(j) — Definition of Invention
- Section 2(1)(l) — Novelty requirement
- Section 2(1)(ja) — Inventive Step
- Section 2(1)(ac) — Industrial Application
- Section 5 — Product patents for drugs/food/chemicals
- Section 48 — Exclusive Rights (Make, Use, Sell, Import)
- Section 3 — Non-patentable subject matter (with the famous Section 3(d) safeguard)
- Section 84 — Compulsory Licensing

**Conclusion:** India's product patent regime represents a **delicate balance** between incentivizing innovation and protecting public health, embodied in safeguards like Section 3(d) and Compulsory Licensing under Section 84.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]:

> **Discuss the Section 3(d) provision with reference to the Novartis v. Union of India (2013) case.**

**Model Solution:**

**Section 3(d) — Statutory Text:**

> *"The mere discovery of a new form of a known substance which does not result in the enhancement of known efficacy of that substance, or the mere discovery of any new property or new use for a known substance, or of the mere use of a known process, machine or apparatus unless such known process results in a new product or employs at least one new reactant, shall not be patentable."*

**Explanation:**
Section 3(d) is India's **anti-evergreening safeguard**, preventing innovators from obtaining perpetual patents through minor modifications (e.g., new polymorphs, salts, esters) of existing substances.

**Case Background:**
- **Novartis AG** filed for a product patent on **Imatinib Mesylate in its beta-crystalline form** — the anti-cancer drug Gleevec.
- The **alpha-crystalline form** was known in prior art.
- Novartis argued the beta form was a **new form with improved bioavailability (30%)**.

**Supreme Court Ruling (1 April 2013):**

The Court held:
1. "Efficacy" refers to **therapeutic efficacy on the disease**, not physicochemical properties like bioavailability.
2. Novartis failed to prove **enhanced therapeutic efficacy** of the beta form over the alpha form.
3. Hence, the beta form fell within the **Section 3(d) exclusion**.
4. The patent application was **refused**.

**Significance:**

- **Prevents evergreening** — protecting public access to affordable generics.
- **Reinforces TRIPS flexibilities** — affirms India's right to define patentability criteria.
- **Guided future jurisprudence** on incremental innovation.

**Conclusion:** Novartis v. UOI is the **cornerstone case** on product patents in India, establishing that incremental modifications without enhanced therapeutic efficacy are not patentable. It represents India's commitment to public health over private monopoly.

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]:

> **Explain the rights conferred by a Product Patent under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Discuss the remedies available for infringement.**

**Model Solution:**

**Rights Conferred (Section 48):**

On grant of a product patent, the patentee obtains **exclusive rights** to:

1. **Make** the patented product.
2. **Use** the patented product.
3. **Sell** or offer for sale the patented product.
4. **Import** the patented product into India.

These rights are **negative rights** — the patentee can prevent third parties from performing these acts without consent.

**Infringement (Section 104):**

Infringement occurs when any person, without consent:
- Makes the patented product,
- Uses the patented product,
- Sells or offers for sale the patented product,
- Imports the patented product.

**Note:** With a **product patent**, infringement is established **regardless of the process** used to manufacture it. This is a critical advantage of product patents over process patents.

**Remedies for Infringement:**

| Remedy | Statutory Basis | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Interlocutory Injunction** | Order 39 CPC (Civil Suit) | Maintain status quo during trial |
| **Permanent Injunction** | Section 108 | Final restraint against infringement |
| **Damages or Account of Profits** | Section 108(1)(a) | Compensation for loss suffered |
| **Delivery Up / Destruction** | Section 108(1)(b) | Confiscation of infringing products |
| **Costs of Suit** | Section 108(1)(c) | Litigation cost recovery |

**Defences Available to Accused (Section 107):**

- Prior use (Bolar Exception)
- Parallel import
- Experimental use for research
- Government use authorisation
- Patent invalidity

**Forum:** The **District Court** (Section 104) or the **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** for rectification appeals.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]:

> **Discuss Compulsory Licensing with reference to the Bayer v. Natco Pharma case.**

**Model Solution:**

**Statutory Basis — Section 84:**

A compulsory license may be granted by the Controller General of Patents if:
1. The reasonable requirements of the public have not been satisfied,
2. The patented product is not available at reasonably affordable prices,
3. The patented invention is not worked in India.

**Case Background:**

- **Bayer Corporation** held the **product patent** on **Sorafenib Tosylate**, marketed as **Nexavar**, used for liver and kidney cancer.
- Bayer imported Nexavar at approximately **₹2.8 lakh/month** per patient, making it unaffordable for Indian patients.
- **Natco Pharma** applied for a compulsory license in 2011.

**Government Decision (12 March 2012):**

The Controller General of Patents granted India's **first-ever compulsory license** to Natco, allowing production of a generic version.

**Reasoning:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Royalty Rate} &= 6\% \text{ of net sales to Bayer} \\
\text{Natco Price} &= \text{Rs. 8,800/month (vs Bayer's Rs. 2.8 lakh/month)} \\
\text{Price Reduction} &= \text{~97\%} \\[0.5em]
\text{Justification:} & \\
\quad \text{Section 84(b)} &\Rightarrow \text{Affordable price not provided} \\
\quad \text{Section 84(1)(d)} &\Rightarrow \text{Not worked adequately in India}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance:**

- Demonstrated that **India exercises TRIPS flexibilities** in the public interest.
- Strengthened **access to essential medicines**.
- Set a **global precedent** for compulsory licensing in pharmaceuticals.

**Conclusion:** The Bayer v. Natco case established that product patent rights are subject to public interest safeguards. It affirmed that patents are **statutory monopolies, not absolute rights**, and may be overridden when public health is at stake.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps for KTU Students:**

1. **Confusing "Inventive Step" with "Novelty":** Inventive Step = non-obviousness (Section 2(1)(ja)). Novelty = absence of prior art (Section 2(1)(l)). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are interchanged.

2. **Citing wrong Section for Product Patent:** Always cite **Section 2(1)(j) + Section 5** together, not just Section 5 alone.

3. **Forgetting to mention Section 3(d):** Any answer on pharma product patents that omits Section 3(d) is considered incomplete. Examiners explicitly test this.

4. **Wrong case citation year:** Novartis case was decided in **2013** (not 2009). Bayer v. Natco compulsory license was **2012**. Wrong year = 0.5 mark deduction.

5. **Writing "patent gives right to make" as a positive right:** Patents give **negative rights** — the right to *exclude others*. This is a frequent conceptual error.

6. **Mixing up TRIPS Article:** Product patent mandate is under **Article 27**, not Article 31 (which deals with compulsory licensing).

7. **Ignoring the term of the patent:** Always mention **20 years from date of filing**, not from date of grant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Product Patent** = protection of the *tangible article* itself for **20 years**.
- ✅ Governed by **Indian Patents Act, 1970** (as amended 2005), specifically Sections **2(1)(j), 2(1)(l), 2(1)(ja), 2(1)(ac), 5, 48**.
- ✅ Tripartite test: **Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application** — all three are mandatory.
- ✅ **TRIPS Article 27** mandates product patents; India complied in 2005.
- ✅ **Section 3(d)** — anti-evergreening safeguard (Novartis v. UOI 2013).
- ✅ **Section 84** — Compulsory Licensing (Natco v. Bayer 2012).
- ✅ Rights conferred: **Make, Use, Sell, Import** (Section 48) — these are **negative rights**.
- ✅ **Process Patent** is *narrower*; **Product Patent** is *broader* and stronger.
- ✅ Burden of proof for infringement is easier under product patents.
- ✅ Bolar Exception (Section 107A) allows generic manufacturers to use the product for regulatory approval before expiry.
- ✅ Renewal fees must be paid annually from the 3rd year onwards to keep the patent in force.
- ✅ Filing route: **Indian Patent Office (IPO)** → Controller General of Patents → IPAB for appeals.
- ✅ For KTU answers, always end with a **conclusion** linking public interest vs. private rights.

---

> **End of Notes — PESFT757 / Module 2 / Product Patent**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines, Outcome-Based Education framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate).*
