topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Patent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Process Patent** is a statutory grant of exclusive rights conferred by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** under the **Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2005), which protects a *method*, *procedure*, *sequence of steps*, or *technique* used to manufacture a product or achieve a particular result, rather than the product itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Anchor (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> Under the Indian Patents Act, 1970 (post-TRIPS amendment 2005), both **product patents** and **process patents** are recognized. Section 3 of the Act specifies what is **not** patentable, while **Section 2(1)(j)** defines *invention* as any new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application.

The patent grants the patentee the **exclusive right** to prevent third parties from using, offering for sale, selling, or importing the patented process without consent for a period of **20 years** from the date of filing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Recipe vs. Cake Analogy 🍰:**
> - A **Product Patent** = owning the *cake itself* (e.g., a specific chemical compound). Nobody else can make, sell, or import *that exact compound*.
> - A **Process Patent** = owning the *recipe* to bake the cake. Anyone can bake a different cake using a different recipe, but they cannot use *your* specific method, even if the end result looks identical.
>
> In pharmaceuticals, a company may patent the **process** of synthesizing a drug. If a competitor reverse-engineers and uses the same process, it constitutes infringement — even if the competitor's final product is chemically identical.

### 1.3 Why "Process Patent" Matters in Engineering & Pharma

- **Pharmaceutical Industry**: India adopted product patenting only in **2005** (post-TRIPS). Before this, Indian pharma thrived on *process patents* — generic companies reverse-engineered and used alternative processes to manufacture the same molecule. This is why India is the **"Pharmacy of the Global South."**
- **Chemical & Biotech Industries**: Process patents protect manufacturing methods (e.g., fermentation, purification, crystallization).
- **Software & Electronics**: A novel algorithm-implemented process can be patented if it produces a technical effect and is tied to a hardware application.

### 1.4 Key Statutory Metrics

| Parameter | Value (Statutory) |
|---|---|
| **Patent Term** | **20 years** from date of filing |
| **Maintenance Renewal** | Annually from the 3rd year onwards |
| **Statutory Body** | **CGPDTM**, Kolkata (Head Office) |
| **Territorial Effect** | India only (unless PCT national-phase entry) |
| **Governing Act** | Patents Act, **1970** (Amended **2005**) |
| **International Framework** | **TRIPS Agreement, Article 27** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Legal Framework: The Patents Act, 1970 (As Amended in 2005)

The **Indian Patents Act, 1970** was originally a purely *process-patent* regime. The historical evolution is critical for KTU answers:

1. **1970**: Only **process patents** allowed (no product patents for drugs, food, or chemicals) — this enabled the growth of generic pharma.
2. **1999**: First Amendment (to comply with TRIPS).
3. **2002**: Second Amendment.
4. **2005**: Third Amendment — **product patents** introduced for drugs, food, and chemicals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Module 2, the focus is **Process Patents** — the regime that historically defined India's IP landscape and still governs manufacturing methods across industries.

### 2.2 Section 3(d) and Patentability Exclusions

The Patents Act, 1970 explicitly **excludes** certain categories under **Section 3**. A process patent **cannot** be granted for:

- **Section 3(a)**: Frivolous inventions contrary to natural laws.
- **Section 3(b)**: Inventions contrary to public order, morality, or harmful to human, animal, or plant life.
- **Section 3(c)**: Discovery of a scientific principle or formulation of an abstract theory.
- **Section 3(d)**: New forms of known substances (e.g., a new salt, ester, polymorph of a known drug) **unless** they show significantly enhanced efficacy.
- **Section 3(e)**: Mere admixture resulting in aggregation of properties.
- **Section 3(f)**: Arrangement/rearrangement/duplication of known devices.
- **Section 3(i)**: Processes for treatment of humans/animals (surgical, therapeutic, diagnostic).
- **Section 3(j)**: Plants and animals in whole or any part thereof (other than microorganisms).

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 3(d) is the most heavily contested provision in Indian pharma jurisprudence.** Landmark case: **Novartis AG v. Union of India (2013)** — the Supreme Court denied a patent to Novartis's *Imatinib Mesylate* (Gleevec) on Section 3(d) grounds.

### 2.3 Three Pillars of Patentability (Universal TRIPS Standard)

For any process patent to be granted, the invention must satisfy **three cumulative conditions**:

1. **Novelty (Section 2(1)(l))** — The process must not have been published or used in India or anywhere in the world before the date of filing.
2. **Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja))** — The process must not be obvious to a *person skilled in the art* (PHOSITA).
3. **Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))** — The process must be capable of being made or used in an industry.

### 2.4 Process Patent vs. Product Patent — Comparative Matrix

| Dimension | Process Patent | Product Patent |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject Matter** | Method, technique, sequence | End-product, compound, apparatus |
| **Scope of Protection** | Narrower — only the exact process | Broader — any method yielding the product |
| **Reverse Engineering** | Legal if an alternative process is used | Illegal regardless of process used |
| **Ease of Detection of Infringement** | Difficult (process is internal) | Easy (product is external) |
| **Historical Indian Status** | Permitted since **1970** | Permitted since **2005** |
| **Typical Industry** | Pharma (pre-2005), chemicals | Pharma (post-2005), biotech |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Statutory / Formula Reference |
|---|---|
| **Patent Term** | $T_{patent} = 20 \text{ years}$ (from date of filing) |
| **Renewal Window** | Annual, starting from $t = 3^{rd}$ year |
| **Novelty Test** | No prior publication anywhere globally before filing date $t_0$ |
| **Inventive Step (PHOSITA)** | Non-obvious to a person skilled in the art at $t_0$ |
| **Burden of Proof (Infringement)** | *Shifting* — under **Section 104(A)** in product patent suits, if the defendant uses the patented process to obtain the product, onus shifts to defendant |
| **Territorial Scope** | $P(x) = \begin{cases} \text{Protected}, & x \in \text{India} \\ \text{Unprotected}, & x \notin \text{India} \end{cases}$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharma Manufacturing**: Protects the synthetic route of an Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API).
- **Semiconductor Fabrication**: TSMC's process patents protect chip lithography techniques.
- **Chemical Engineering**: Refining, polymerization, or catalytic conversion processes.
- **Food Tech**: Fermentation, preservation, and extraction processes (e.g., cold-press extraction of coconut oil — relevant to Kerala's coconut industry).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Process Patent Application Lifecycle (India)

The procedural steps from invention conception to granted patent are exhaustively enumerated below for KTU answers.

**Step 1: Novelty Search & Patentability Assessment**

The applicant conducts a prior-art search across patent databases (Indian Patent Advanced Search System — **IPAID**, USPTO, EPO, WIPO PatentScope).

$$
\text{Patentability Score} = f(\text{Novelty},\ \text{Inventive Step},\ \text{Industrial Application})
$$

**Step 2: Drafting the Patent Specification**

Two types of specifications may be filed:

- **Provisional Specification**: Filed to establish priority date when the invention is not fully developed.
- **Complete Specification**: Contains *Title*, *Field of Invention*, *Background*, *Summary*, *Detailed Description*, *Claims*, *Abstract*, and *Drawings*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Claims are the legal heart of the patent.** In a *process patent*, claims are drafted as a sequence of steps: *"A process for manufacturing X, comprising the steps of (a)..., (b)..., (c)..."*

**Step 3: Filing the Application (Form 1 + Form 2 + Form 5 + Form 26)**

Documents required:

| Form | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Form 1** | Application for grant of patent |
| **Form 2** | Complete / Provisional specification |
| **Form 5** | Declaration of inventorship |
| **Form 26** | Power of attorney (if filed through agent) |
| **Form 3 / Form 9** | Examination request + PCT national-phase entry (if applicable) |

**Step 4: Publication (Section 11A)**

The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the priority date. The applicant may request **early publication** (Form 9).

**Step 5: Request for Examination (Section 11B / Rule 24B)**

Filed within **48 months** of priority date. After this, the application is examined by a patent examiner for compliance with Sections 3, 4, and 10 of the Act.

**Step 6: First Examination Report (FER)**

The examiner issues an FER citing objections under:
- Lack of novelty / inventive step (Section 2(1)(j))
- Non-patentability (Section 3)
- Insufficiency of disclosure (Section 10)
- Ambiguity in claims (Section 10(5))

**Step 7: Response to FER (Section 21)**

The applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable to **9 months**) addressing each objection with amendments and arguments.

**Step 8: Hearing and Final Decision**

If objections persist, the Controller holds a hearing under **Section 14**. The application may be:

- **Granted** (with claims as amended)
- **Refused** (with written reasons)

**Step 9: Opposition (Pre-Grant or Post-Grant)**

Under **Section 25(1)** — *Pre-Grant Opposition* (by any person, before grant).
Under **Section 25(2)** — *Post-Grant Opposition* (within **12 months** of grant).

**Step 10: Renewal and Maintenance**

Renewal fees are payable annually starting from the **3rd year** until the **20th year**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Process Patent vs. Infringement Test

| Test | Process Patent | Product Patent |
|---|---|---|
| **Infringement Standard** | Use of identical process steps | Manufacture, sale, import of product |
| **Burden of Proof** | Initial burden on plaintiff | **Section 104(A) shift** — if product is novel and defendant produces it, defendant must prove *different* process |
| **Defence** | Show *alternative process* used | No valid defence under process variation |
| **Remedy** | Injunction, damages, account of profits | Injunction, damages, account of profits, delivery-up |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical / Procedural Example

**Question:** A Kerala-based startup files a process patent application on **01 January 2023** for a novel extraction process of curcumin from turmeric. What are the critical statutory deadlines?

$$
\text{Filing Date } (t_0) = \text{01 January 2023}
$$

**Step A — Publication:**
$$
t_{pub} = t_0 + 18 \text{ months} = \text{01 July 2024}
$$

**Step B — Last date to file Examination Request:**
$$
t_{exam} = t_0 + 48 \text{ months} = \text{01 January 2027}
$$

**Step C — Response to FER (if raised):**
$$
t_{response} = t_{FER} + 6 \text{ months (default)}, \quad \text{max } = 9 \text{ months}
$$

**Step D — Renewal Schedule:**
$$
\text{First renewal due: } 01 \text{ January } 2026 \quad (\text{i.e., } t_0 + 3 \text{ years})
$$

**Step E — Patent expiry (if granted):**
$$
t_{expiry} = t_0 + 20 \text{ years} = \text{01 January } 2043}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Process Patent Lifecycle in India

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Invention Conceived"] --> B["Step 1: Novelty and Prior Art Search"]
    B --> C{"Step 2: Patentable? Yes or No"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Stop: Abandon or Keep as Trade Secret"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Step 3: Draft Provisional or Complete Specification"]
    E --> F["Step 4: File Form 1 Form 2 Form 5 Form 26"]
    F --> G["Step 5: Priority Date Established"]
    G --> H["Step 6: Publication in Patent Journal at 18 Months"]
    H --> I{"Step 7: Examination Request Filed within 48 Months?"}
    I -- "No" --> J["Application Deemed Withdrawn"]
    I -- "Yes" --> K["Step 8: First Examination Report FER Issued"]
    K --> L{"Step 9: Objections Raised?"}
    L -- "Yes" --> M["Step 10: Applicant Response within 6 to 9 Months"]
    M --> N{"Step 11: Satisfactory Response?"}
    N -- "No" --> O["Hearing under Section 14"]
    O --> P{"Final Decision"}
    N -- "Yes" --> P
    P -- "Granted" --> Q["Step 12: Patent Granted 20 Year Term"]
    P -- "Refused" --> R["Step 13: Appeal to IPAB or High Court"]
    Q --> S["Step 14: Annual Renewal from Year 3 Onwards"]
    S --> T["END: Patent Expiry at 20 Years"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Patentability Test (Three Pillars)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["Invention Submission"]
        P["Proposed Process"]
    end

    subgraph N["Pillar 1: Novelty Section 2 1 l"]
        N1["Search global prior art at time t0"]
        N2["No prior disclosure anywhere"]
        N3["Decision: Novel or Not Novel"]
    end

    subgraph IS["Pillar 2: Inventive Step Section 2 1 ja"]
        I1["Evaluate against PHOSITA standard"]
        I2["Non obvious to skilled person"]
        I3["Decision: Inventive or Obvious"]
    end

    subgraph IA["Pillar 3: Industrial Application Section 2 1 ac"]
        IA1["Useful in any industry"]
        IA2["Reproducible at scale"]
        IA3["Decision: Industrially Applicable"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Outcome"]
        GRANT["GRANTED Process Patent"]
        REJECT["REJECTED"]
    end

    P --> N
    N --> IS
    IS --> IA
    IA --> GRANT
    N -- "Fail" --> REJECT
    IS -- "Fail" --> REJECT
    IA -- "Fail" --> REJECT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparative Diagram — Process vs. Product Patent

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROCESS["Process Patent"]
        P1["Protects method or sequence"]
        P2["Narrower scope"]
        P3["Reverse engineering legally permitted via alternative route"]
        P4["Historically protected since 1970 in India"]
    end

    subgraph PRODUCT["Product Patent"]
        PR1["Protects end product or compound"]
        PR2["Broader scope"]
        PR3["Reverse engineering does not circumvent"]
        PR4["Permitted in India only post 2005 amendment"]
    end

    PROCESS -. "Same Act Different Scope" .- PRODUCT
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
Define *Process Patent* and state the duration of patent protection under the Indian Patents Act, 1970.

**Model Answer:**
A **Process Patent** is an intellectual property right granted under the Patents Act, 1970, that protects a *method*, *procedure*, or *sequence of steps* used to manufacture a product or achieve a technical result. It does not protect the end-product itself, only the *route* by which it is produced.

Under **Section 53(1)** of the Act, the term of a patent is **20 years** from the date of filing the application, subject to payment of annual renewal fees from the **3rd year** onwards.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**
What is the role of **Section 3(d)** of the Patents Act, 1970, in evaluating process patents in the pharmaceutical sector?

**Model Answer:**
**Section 3(d)** bars the grant of patents on *new forms* of known substances (e.g., new salts, esters, polymorphs, or metabolites) **unless** the applicant demonstrates **significantly enhanced efficacy** over the known substance.

In the pharmaceutical sector, this prevents **evergreening** — where a company attempts to extend monopoly on a molecule nearing patent expiry by patenting minor variants without genuine therapeutic improvement. The landmark judgment in **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** upheld Section 3(d), preserving India's generic-pharma ecosystem.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]*

**(a)** Explain the legal framework of **Process Patents** under the Indian Patents Act, 1970 (as amended). Discuss the historical evolution from a process-patent regime to the introduction of product patents. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. The Patents Act, 1970 — Original Framework:**
The Patents Act, 1970 was enacted to encourage indigenous innovation while preventing monopolistic control of essential goods. Critically, the original Act permitted **only process patents** for:
- Pharmaceuticals
- Food products
- Chemical substances

This meant a competitor could legally **reverse-engineer** a patented drug and manufacture it using a *different process*. This regime enabled the rise of India's generic pharmaceutical industry (e.g., Cipla, Sun Pharma, Dr. Reddy's), which today supplies affordable medicines to over **200 countries**.

**2. TRIPS Pressure (1995):**
India's accession to the **WTO** obligated it to comply with the **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**, whose **Article 27** mandates both product and process patents across all fields of technology without discrimination.

**3. Amendments:**
- **1999 Amendment**: Established mailbox applications for product patent claims.
- **2002 Amendment**: Strengthened examination procedures.
- **2005 Amendment (Third Amendment)**: Introduced **product patents** for drugs, food, and chemicals — but retained safeguards via **Section 3(d)** and **compulsory licensing (Section 84)**.

**4. Current Status:**
Today, India operates a **dual regime** — process patents continue to exist alongside product patents. Process patents remain strategically important in pharma and chemical manufacturing, especially for **alternative synthesis routes**.

---

**(b)** Discuss the three essential conditions for patentability of a process under the Patents Act, 1970. Use examples to illustrate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Patents Act, 1970 lays down **three cumulative conditions** under **Section 2(1)(j)** for an invention (including a process) to be patentable:

**1. Novelty (Section 2(1)(l)):**
The process must be **new**, meaning it has not been published or used anywhere in the world before the date of filing.
*Example*: If a paper describing the same process was published in a journal on 01 January 2022, and the applicant files on 01 March 2022, the process is **not novel**.

**2. Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja)):**
The process must involve an **inventive step** — it must not be obvious to a *person having ordinary skill in the art* (PHOSITA).
*Example*: A chemist working in a pharma lab would consider it obvious to use standard purification, but a novel **continuous-flow crystallization** method showing unexpected yield improvement would qualify.

**3. Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac)):**
The process must be capable of being **made or used in an industry**.
*Example*: A purely theoretical mathematical procedure with no industrial utility would fail this test.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning ⚠️**
> Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by stating only "novelty" or "invention" as the test. KTU examiners specifically look for the **three cumulative pillars** — Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application. Merely listing them without section references (Section 2(1)(j) read with 2(1)(l), 2(1)(ja), 2(1)(ac)) is treated as incomplete.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Process Patents** and **Product Patents**. Critically analyze the implications of India adopting product patents in 2005 on the pharmaceutical industry. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Process Patent | Product Patent |
|---|---|---|
| Subject | Method of manufacture | End product |
| Scope | Narrow | Broad |
| Reverse Engineering | Permitted | Not permitted |
| Indian Status | Pre-1970 to present | Post-2005 only |

**Implications of 2005 Product Patent Amendment:**

*Positive Impacts:*
- Encouraged **Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)** in pharma R&D.
- Strengthened India's compliance with **TRIPS**.
- Boosted **innovation-led R&D** in domestic pharma companies.

*Negative Impacts:*
- Increased dependence on multinational corporations.
- Higher drug prices during the patent period.
- Threats to **generic manufacturing** — a backbone of Indian pharma exports.

*Safeguards Built-In:*
- **Section 3(d)** to prevent evergreening.
- **Section 84** compulsory licensing for affordable access.
- **Bolar exemption** (Section 107(A)) allowing generic companies to develop and test generics during the patent term.

---

**(b)** Explain the procedural steps involved in obtaining a **Process Patent** in India. State the key statutory forms and deadlines. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The procedural lifecycle consists of **ten steps**:

1. **Novelty Search** — Conduct prior-art search via INPASS, USPTO, WIPO.
2. **Drafting Specification** — Provisional (Form 2) or Complete Specification.
3. **Filing Application** — File **Form 1**, Form 2, Form 5 (inventorship), Form 26 (PoA).
4. **Priority Date** — Established on filing.
5. **Publication** — In Patent Office Journal after **18 months** (Form 9 for early).
6. **Examination Request** — File within **48 months**.
7. **First Examination Report (FER)** — Examiner reviews for Section 3 and Section 10 compliance.
8. **Response to FER** — Within **6 months** (extendable to **9 months**).
9. **Hearing & Decision** — Under **Section 14**, application granted or refused.
10. **Renewal** — Annual fees from **3rd year** onwards until **20 years**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — "Exam Request Deadline" ⚠️**
> Students often confuse the **18-month publication** timeline with the **48-month examination** timeline. Examiners deduct marks if the student writes "examination happens automatically after 18 months" — it does **not**. Examination requires a *positive request* via Form 18 within 48 months, failing which the application is **deemed withdrawn**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Process Patent**

- **Definition**: A Process Patent protects a **method or sequence of steps**, not the end product.
- **Governing Law**: **Patents Act, 1970** (amended in **2005**).
- **Patent Term**: **20 years** from filing date.
- **Renewal**: Annual from **3rd year** onwards.
- **Three Pillars of Patentability**:
  - **Novelty** — Section 2(1)(l)
  - **Inventive Step** — Section 2(1)(ja)
  - **Industrial Application** — Section 2(1)(ac)
- **Section 3(d)**: Anti-evergreening provision; bars new forms of known substances unless significantly enhanced efficacy.
- **Procedural Deadlines**:
  - **Publication** at 18 months.
  - **Examination Request** within 48 months.
  - **FER Response** within 6–9 months.
- **Burden of Proof Shift** under **Section 104(A)** in infringement suits involving process patents.
- **India's Historical Stance**: Process-patent regime enabled the rise of generic pharma (the "Pharmacy of the Developing World").
- **Landmark Case**: **Novartis AG v. Union of India (2013)** — Section 3(d) upheld; Gleevec patent denied.
- **Opposition Provisions**: Pre-grant (Section 25(1)) and Post-grant (Section 25(2) within 12 months).
- **Forms to Remember**: Form 1 (application), Form 2 (specification), Form 5 (inventorship), Form 9 (early publication), Form 18 (examination request), Form 26 (power of attorney).
- **Comparison Hook** for KTU answers: Always present Process Patent in contrast with Product Patent to demonstrate analytical depth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Conditions for Conferring Patent Right
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Conditions for Conferring Patent Right

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Under the **Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2005 to comply with TRIPS), the conferring of a **Patent Right** in India is a statutory privilege granted by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** functioning under the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India.

A patent right is a **negative right** — it does not give the patentee the right to make, use, or sell the invention, but rather the exclusive right to **prevent others** from commercially exploiting the patented invention for a limited term of **20 years** from the date of filing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Definition (Section 2(1)(j) of the Patents Act, 1970):**
> *"Invention means a new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application."*

For a patent right to be conferred, an applicant must satisfy the **Three Pillars of Patentability** (often called the **Trilogy of Novelty, Inventive Step, and Industrial Application**), AND the subject matter must not fall within the **statutory exclusions** listed under **Sections 3 and 4** of the Act.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a unique treehouse in your neighbourhood. For the municipality to give you an **exclusive occupancy certificate** (akin to a patent), three things must be true:

1. **Novelty (New)** — Your treehouse design has **never been built anywhere in the world** before. If your neighbour already built the same one, you cannot get the certificate.
2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obvious)** — The design should not be something a "person having ordinary skill in the art" (PHOSITA — read it as a "reasonably skilled carpenter") could trivially figure out. A treehouse is *obvious*; a treehouse with a retractable elevator using counterweights is *inventive*.
3. **Industrial Application (Useful)** — The treehouse must actually serve a functional purpose (shelter, storage, workspace) — not be a purely abstract artistic idea.

**Additional Conditions** are like the municipal by-laws: even if your treehouse is novel and inventive, you cannot get a certificate if it is built on a **forbidden plot** (atomic energy zones), violates **public order/morality** (e.g., a treehouse designed solely to spy on neighbours), or is a **scientific theorem** (e.g., a "law of gravity for treehouses" — abstract ideas are not patentable).

## 1.3 Core Definition Callouts

> [!NOTE]
> **Patentable Subject Matter Trilogy — The Three Cardinal Conditions:**
> 1. **Novelty** (Section 2(1)(l)) — New invention
> 2. **Inventive Step** (Section 2(1)(ja)) — Non-obvious technical advancement
> 3. **Industrial Application** (Section 2(1)(ac)) — Capable of being made or used in an industry

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** A patent is NOT a certificate of originality or authorship. It is a **time-limited commercial monopoly** granted in exchange for **full public disclosure** of the invention in the patent specification.

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven General Conditions for Conferring Patent Right

For a patent to be conferred under Indian law, the following conditions must cumulatively be satisfied:

### Condition 1: Subject Matter Must Be an "Invention" (Section 2(1)(j))

The subject matter must qualify as an "invention" as legally defined. The Supreme Court in *Bishwanath Prasad Radhey Shyam v. Hindustan Metal Industries (1979)* clarified that an invention means a new and useful art, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter.

### Condition 2: Novelty — Newness (Section 2(1)(l))

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 2(1)(l):** *"New invention means any invention or technology which has not been anticipated by publication in any document or used in the country or elsewhere in the world before the date of filing of the patent application with complete specification."*

**Key elements of Novelty:**
- **Universal Novelty Test:** Anticipation anywhere in the world destroys novelty.
- **Prior Art:** Includes any published document, public use, sale, or knowledge anywhere globally.
- **Burden of Proof:** The applicant must establish that the invention is new.

### Condition 3: Inventive Step — Non-Obviousness (Section 2(1)(ja))

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 2(1)(ja):** *"Inventive step means a feature of an invention that involves technical advance as compared to the existing knowledge or having economic significance or both and that makes the invention not obvious to a person skilled in the art."*

**Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA / PHOSITA) Test:**
- A hypothetical person of ordinary skill in the relevant technical field.
- The invention must not be obvious to such a person from the prior art.

### Condition 4: Industrial Application — Utility (Section 2(1)(ac))

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 2(1)(ac):** *"Capable of industrial application in relation to an invention means that the invention is capable of being made or used in an industry."*

The invention must have a **functional, tangible utility** — it must be reproducible on an industrial scale.

### Condition 5: Disclosure Requirement (Section 10)

The complete specification must **sufficiently and fairly describe** the invention, the best method of performing it, and the claims defining the scope. Failure to disclose renders the patent liable to be revoked.

### Condition 6: Not Falling Under Statutory Exclusions (Section 3)

> [!WARNING]
> **Section 3 enumerates 16 categories of "what are not inventions"** — these are absolute bars to patentability. Even if novel and inventive, subject matter falling under Section 3 cannot be patented.

### Condition 7: Not Relating to Atomic Energy (Section 4)

Inventions relating to **atomic energy** are not patentable; the Government of India holds exclusive rights under the Atomic Energy Act, 1962.

## 2.2 Statutory Exclusions Under Section 3

| **S. No.** | **Exclusion Category** | **Legal Basis** |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | Frivolous inventions (contrary to well-established natural laws) | Sec 3(a) |
| 2 | Inventions contrary to public order or morality | Sec 3(b) |
| 3 | Mere discovery of scientific principles / abstract ideas / formulae | Sec 3(c) |
| 4 | Mere discovery of new property of known substance | Sec 3(d) |
| 5 | New use for known substance (except in medicine/chemistry) | Sec 3(d) proviso |
| 6 | New form of known substance (no enhanced efficacy) | Sec 3(d) proviso |
| 7 | Substances obtained by admixture (no synergistic property) | Sec 3(e) |
| 8 | Mere aggregation of properties | Sec 3(e) |
| 9 | Food, medicine, drug, or chemical substances (products per se) | Sec 3(h) — for product patents |
| 10 | Traditional knowledge — plants and animals in whole or part | Sec 3(j) |
| 11 | Essentially biological processes (microbiological excluded) | Sec 3(j) |
| 12 | Diagnostic methods practiced on humans/animals | Sec 3(i) |
| 13 | Computer programs (per se) and algorithms | Sec 3(k) |
| 14 | Literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works (Copyright domain) | Sec 3(l) |
| 15 | Schemes, rules, methods of mental activity, business methods | Sec 3(m) |
| 16 | Presentation of information | Sec 3(n) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 3(k) Landmark Case:** In *Ferid Allani v. Union of India* and *Microsoft Technology Licensing v. Yogesh Papat*, the Delhi High Court clarified that **computer programs "per se"** are not patentable, but hardware-implemented inventions involving technical contribution ARE patentable.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Statutory Reference** | **Legal Test** | **Standard / Metric** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Definition of Invention | Sec 2(1)(j) | Novel + Inventive Step + Industrial Application | Three-fold test |
| Novelty | Sec 2(1)(l) | Global Anticipation Test | Universal (worldwide) |
| Inventive Step | Sec 2(1)(ja) | PHOSITA Test | Technical/Economic significance |
| Industrial Application | Sec 2(1)(ac) | Utility Test | Capable of industrial use |
| Term of Patent | Sec 53(1) | Filing Date → 20 years | **20 years** (uniform globally post-TRIPS) |
| Renewal Fee | Sec 142 | Annual | From 3rd year onwards |
| Opposition (Pre-grant) | Sec 25(1) | Any person | Before grant |
| Opposition (Post-grant) | Sec 25(2) | Interested person | Within 12 months of grant |
| First-to-File Rule | Sec 6, 7 | Priority based on filing | (Not first-to-invent) |
| Priority Date | Sec 11 | Earliest filing claim | Forms basis of prior art search |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The General Conditions serve as the **gatekeeping mechanism** for India's innovation ecosystem:

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Section 3(d) prevents evergreening — a critical safeguard ensuring genuine innovation in drug development.
- **Software & IT Industry:** Section 3(k) forces tech companies to patent **hardware-embedded inventions** rather than mere algorithms, encouraging deep-tech innovation.
- **Biotechnology & Agriculture:** Section 3(j) protects India's biodiversity and traditional knowledge from bio-piracy (Neem, Turmeric cases).
- **Manufacturing & Mechanical Engineering:** The industrial application requirement ensures patents are granted for **scalable, reproducible** technologies — not mere laboratory curiosities.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Legal Derivations

## 3.1 The Novelty Test — Step-by-Step Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** To determine whether an invention is "new" under Section 2(1)(l) of the Patents Act, 1970.

**Step 1: Identify the "Prior Art"** (Art that existed before the priority date of the patent application).

$$\text{Prior Art} = \sum (\text{Published Documents} + \text{Public Use} + \text{Knowledge in the Public Domain})$$

**Step 2: Determine the Priority Date of the Applicant.**

$$\text{Priority Date} = \begin{cases} \text{International Filing Date (PCT)} \\ \text{Convention Application Date (Paris Convention)} \\ \text{Filing Date in India} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Apply the "Anticipation Test."**

An invention is **anticipated** (and therefore lacks novelty) if a single prior art reference discloses **ALL** essential features of the claimed invention.

$$\text{Novel} \iff \text{Essential Features of Invention} \not\subset \text{Any Single Prior Art Reference}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate "Mosaicking Rule."**

Two or more prior art references **cannot** be combined (mosaicked) to destroy novelty — only a single reference can anticipate.

$$\text{Anticipation} \neq \text{Combination of Multiple References}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exception — Section 31 (Anticipation by previous communication):** Disclosure to Government or research institutions 12 months before filing does not destroy novelty — this is the "Grace Period" for academic disclosures.

## 3.2 The Inventive Step Test — PHOSITA Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** To determine whether the invention is non-obvious to a Person Having Ordinary Skill in the Art (PHOSITA).

**Step 1: Construct the Hypothetical "Person Skilled in the Art."**

The PHOSITA is an **ordinary practitioner** in the relevant technical field, aware of common general knowledge but lacking inventive ingenuity.

**Step 2: Identify the Differences Between the Invention and the Closest Prior Art.**

Let the closest prior art be denoted as $P_a$ and the invention as $I_n$. Define the difference set:

$$\Delta = I_n \setminus P_a = \{d_1, d_2, d_3, \ldots, d_n\}$$

**Step 3: Apply the "Could-Would Test" (KSR v. Teleflex, US Supreme Court, 2007).**

Could the PHOSITA have arrived at the invention by combining prior art references, and would they have been motivated to do so?

$$\text{Inventive Step} \iff \text{PHOSITA could not trivially derive } I_n \text{ from prior art}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate Secondary Considerations.**

| **Factor** | **Indicates Inventive Step** |
|:--|:--|
| Long-felt need satisfied | Yes |
| Commercial success | Yes (if causally linked) |
| Failure of others | Yes |
| Copying by competitors | Yes |
| Unexpected technical effect | Yes |

## 3.3 The Industrial Application Test

**Step 1: Verify the invention is reproducible.**

The invention must be capable of being **made or used** in at least one industry. Pure theoretical constructs or abstract theorems fail this test.

**Step 2: Identify the specific industry.**

The applicant must specify the field of application — pharmaceutical, automotive, electronics, agriculture, etc.

**Step 3: Confirm reproducibility.**

The specification must enable a skilled worker to **replicate** the invention without undue experimentation.

## 3.4 Composite Eligibility Matrix

A patent is conferred **if and only if** all four conditions are simultaneously satisfied:

$$
\text{Patent Granted} \iff 
\begin{cases}
\text{Novelty (Sec 2(1)(l))} = \text{True} \\
\text{Inventive Step (Sec 2(1)(ja))} = \text{True} \\
\text{Industrial Application (Sec 2(1)(ac))} = \text{True} \\
\text{Section 3/4 Exclusion} = \text{False} \\
\text{Sufficient Disclosure (Sec 10)} = \text{True}
\end{cases}
$$

If **any one** condition is false, the patent application will be **refused** under Section 15 (pre-grant opposition) or **revoked** under Section 64 (post-grant revocation).

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three Pillars of Patentability — Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patent Application Filed] --> B{Is it an Invention under Sec 2 1 j?}
    B -- No --> Z1[Application Refused]
    B -- Yes --> C{Novelty Test Sec 2 1 l}
    C -- No --> Z1
    C -- Yes --> D{Inventive Step Sec 2 1 ja}
    D -- No --> Z1
    D -- Yes --> E{Industrial Application Sec 2 1 ac}
    E -- No --> Z1
    E -- Yes --> F{Section 3 Exclusion Check}
    F -- Yes --> Z1
    F -- No --> G{Section 4 Atomic Energy Check}
    G -- Yes --> Z1
    G -- No --> H{Sufficient Disclosure Sec 10}
    H -- No --> Z1
    H -- Yes --> Y[Patent Right Confirmed - 20 Years]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style Y fill:#d4edda
    style Z1 fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.2 Section 3 Exclusions — Categorical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S3[Section 3 Exclusions] --> S3A[Moral Exclusions]
    S3 --> S3B[Discovery Exclusions]
    S3 --> S3C[Substance Exclusions]
    S3 --> S3D[Process Exclusions]
    S3 --> S3E[Information Exclusions]
    
    S3A --> S3A1[Public Order Morality - 3 b]
    S3A --> S3A2[Mere Discovery - 3 c]
    
    S3B --> S3B1[New Property of Known Substance - 3 d]
    S3B --> S3B2[New Use of Known Substance - 3 d]
    S3B --> S3B3[New Form - No Enhanced Efficacy - 3 d]
    
    S3C --> S3C1[Admixtures - 3 e]
    S3C --> S3C2[Food Medicine Drugs - 3 h]
    S3C --> S3C3[Plants and Animals - 3 j]
    
    S3D --> S3D1[Diagnostic Methods - 3 i]
    S3D --> S3D2[Computer Programs Per Se - 3 k]
    S3D --> S3D3[Business Methods - 3 m]
    
    S3E --> S3E1[Schemes and Rules - 3 m]
    S3E --> S3E2[Presentation of Information - 3 n]
    S3E --> S3E3[Literary Artistic Works - 3 l]
    
    style S3 fill:#fff3cd
    style S3A fill:#f8d7da
    style S3B fill:#f8d7da
    style S3C fill:#f8d7da
    style S3D fill:#f8d7da
    style S3E fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.3 Patent Prosecution Lifecycle — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inventor Creates Invention] --> B[File Patent Application - Sec 6]
    B --> C[Publication at 18 Months - Sec 11 A]
    C --> D{Request for Examination within 48 months}
    D -- No --> E[Application Abandoned]
    D -- Yes --> F[Examination by Controller - Sec 12]
    F --> G{Novelty and Inventive Step Present?}
    G -- No --> H[First Examination Report Objections]
    H --> I[Applicant Response within 6 months]
    I --> F
    G -- Yes --> J[Pre-Grant Opposition Window - Sec 25 1]
    J -- Opposed --> K[Opposition Heard]
    J -- Not Opposed --> L[Patent Granted - Sec 43]
    K --> M{Opposition Allowed?}
    M -- Yes --> E
    M -- No --> L
    L --> N[20 Year Term - Sec 53]
    N --> O[Annual Renewal Fees - Sec 142]
    
    style A fill:#d4edda
    style L fill:#cce5ff
    style N fill:#fff3cd
    style E fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.4 PHOSITA Test — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Person Having Ordinary Skill in the Art] --> K[Common General Knowledge]
    P --> S[Standard Skill in the Field]
    P --> R[No Inventive Genius]
    P --> M[Reads and Applies Prior Art Rationally]
    
    K --> T[Inventive Step Test]
    S --> T
    R --> T
    M --> T
    
    T --> Q{Would PHOSITA find the Invention Obvious?}
    Q -- Yes --> X[No Inventive Step - Patent Denied]
    Q -- No --> Y[Inventive Step Established - Patent Eligible]
    
    style P fill:#cce5ff
    style T fill:#fff3cd
    style Y fill:#d4edda
    style X fill:#f8d7da
```

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define "Inventive Step" under Section 2(1)(ja) of the Patents Act, 1970. Why is it considered the most stringent of the three patentability criteria?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Section 2(1)(ja) defines an inventive step as *"a feature of an invention that involves technical advance as compared to the existing knowledge or having economic significance or both and that makes the invention not obvious to a person skilled in the art."*

It is the most stringent criterion because:
1. **Dual test:** Requires BOTH technical advancement AND non-obviousness to PHOSITA.
2. **Subjective evaluation:** Depends on constructing a hypothetical "skilled person," making it fact-intensive.
3. **Distinguishes invention from routine work:** Mere workshop improvements fail this test; only genuine technical contributions qualify.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State any THREE categories of inventions excluded from patentability under Section 3 of the Patents Act, 1970.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Section 3(b):** Inventions contrary to public order or morality (e.g., a device designed solely to defraud).
2. **Section 3(d):** Mere discovery of a new property or new use for a known substance, unless it results in enhanced efficacy in known medicine/chemistry.
3. **Section 3(k):** Computer programs per se and algorithms (as clarified in *Ferid Allani v. UOI*).
4. **Section 3(j):** Plants and animals in whole or any part thereof (excluding micro-organisms).
5. **Section 3(h):** Food, medicine, drug, or chemical substances per se (though processes for their manufacture ARE patentable).

*(Any three with one-line explanation = 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern: ESE Module 2]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three general conditions of patentability** under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. State the relevant statutory provisions and the legal tests applied for each condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the **statutory exclusions under Section 3** of the Patents Act, 1970. Why did the Indian legislature incorporate these exclusions? Illustrate with at least four case laws or examples. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The three general conditions of patentability constitute the **statutory trilogy** required under Section 2(1)(j):

**1. Novelty (Section 2(1)(l)) — 2 Marks**

Novelty requires that the invention must not have been **anticipated by prior publication, use, or knowledge anywhere in the world** before the priority date. The test is **absolute and universal**. In *Bishwanath Prasad v. Hindustan Metal Industries*, the Supreme Court held that anticipation by a single prior art reference is sufficient to destroy novelty. The applicant bears the burden of establishing novelty.

**2. Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja)) — 2.5 Marks**

The invention must involve a **technical advance** or **economic significance** such that it is not obvious to a Person Having Ordinary Skill in the Art (PHOSITA). The PHOSITA is a hypothetical construct — an ordinary practitioner aware of common general knowledge but devoid of inventive genius. The test applied is the *"could-would"* test — could the PHOSITA have arrived at the invention, and would they have been motivated to do so? Secondary considerations such as long-felt need and commercial success also support the existence of an inventive step.

**3. Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac)) — 2.5 Marks**

The invention must be **capable of being made or used in an industry**. This requires the invention to be reproducible, functional, and have a tangible utility. Pure scientific theories, mathematical methods, or abstract ideas fail this test. The specification must enable a person skilled in the art to reproduce the invention without undue experimentation.

**Conclusion:** All three conditions are **cumulative and conjunctive** — failure of any one results in refusal of the patent application.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Section 3 enumerates **16 categories of "what are not inventions."** These exclusions reflect the Indian legislature's policy choices:

**1. Morality and Public Order — Section 3(b) — 1.5 Marks**

Inventions whose commercial exploitation would be contrary to public order or morality are excluded. Example: A device designed to breach national security. The Controller must apply a **balancing test** between innovation and societal harm.

**2. Mere Discovery vs. Invention — Section 3(c) and 3(d) — 2 Marks**

The discovery of a scientific principle or the natural occurrence of a mineral is not patentable. Similarly, mere new properties or new uses of known substances are excluded to **prevent evergreening** — the practice of obtaining multiple patents on the same substance by claiming minor modifications. The famous *Novartis v. Union of India* (2013) case crystallized this position, denying a patent for Imatinib Mesylate (Glivec) on grounds of lack of enhanced efficacy under Section 3(d).

**3. Computer Programs and Business Methods — Section 3(k), 3(m) — 1.5 Marks**

Computer programs "per se" and algorithms are excluded. However, the *Microsoft v. Yogesh Papat* (2016) ruling clarified that hardware-implemented inventions with a technical contribution ARE patentable. Pure business methods and mental activity schemes are also excluded.

**4. Plants, Animals, and Biological Processes — Section 3(j) — 1 Mark**

Plants and animals in whole or part, and essentially biological processes for their production, are excluded. Microbiological processes and micro-organisms ARE patentable. This balances the protection of India's biodiversity with incentives for biotech innovation.

**5. Diagnostic Methods — Section 3(i) — 0.5 Marks**

Methods for the treatment of humans/animals and diagnostic methods practiced thereon are excluded, preserving medical practice as a non-monopolized field.

**Policy Rationale:** The exclusions reflect the TRIPS-compliant flexibilities that India adopted to balance innovation incentives with public interest, public health (especially Section 3(h)), traditional knowledge protection (Section 3(j)), and prevention of bio-piracy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the role of the **Person Having Ordinary Skill in the Art (PHOSITA)** in determining inventive step. How has Indian jurisprudence evolved in applying this test? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** "Disclosure is the price paid for the patent monopoly." Critically examine the **disclosure requirements** under Section 10 of the Patents Act, 1970. What are the consequences of inadequate disclosure? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The PHOSITA is a **hypothetical legal construct** representing an ordinary practitioner in the relevant technical field. The concept originated in U.S. case law (*Graham v. John Deere*, 1966) and was incorporated into Indian jurisprudence through Section 2(1)(ja).

**Characteristics of PHOSITA — 2 Marks:**
- Possesses **common general knowledge** of the field.
- Has **ordinary skill** but no inventive genius.
- Reads and applies prior art **rationally** but not creatively.
- Capable of routine experimentation but not inventive leaps.

**Application in Indian Jurisprudence — 3 Marks:**

In *F. Hoffmann-La Roche v. Cipla* (Delhi HC, 2008), the court held that the PHOSITA test must be applied with reference to the state of the art at the priority date. The Delhi High Court in *Monsanto v. Nuziveedu* (2018) emphasized that the PHOSITA is not a legal abstraction but must be anchored to real technical knowledge.

**Evolution:** Indian courts have moved from a rigid PHOSITA standard to a **flexible, fact-intensive** analysis incorporating secondary considerations (long-felt need, commercial success, failure of others).

**Critical Evaluation — 2 Marks:** The PHOSITA test has been criticized for its subjectivity, but it remains the cornerstone of non-obviousness analysis globally.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The patent system operates on a **quid pro quo** — the patentee receives a 20-year commercial monopoly in exchange for **full public disclosure** of the invention. Section 10 of the Patents Act codifies this disclosure requirement.

**Disclosure Requirements Under Section 10 — 3 Marks:**

**1. Full Description (Sec 10(4)):** The complete specification must **sufficiently and fairly** describe the invention, the method of performing it, and the best method known to the applicant.

**2. Claims (Sec 10(5)):** The claims must define the **scope of the invention** — they are the legally enforceable boundaries of the patent monopoly.

**3. Abstract (Sec 10(7)):** A concise summary of the invention, enabling quick prior art searches.

**4. Best Method Disclosure:** The applicant must disclose the **best mode** of carrying out the invention, even if alternative methods are also contemplated.

**Consequences of Inadequate Disclosure — 2 Marks:**

1. **Revocation under Section 64(1)(h):** The patent may be revoked for non-disclosure or insufficient disclosure.
2. **Rejection of Application:** The Controller may refuse the application during examination.
3. **Unenforceability:** A patent with vague or fraudulent disclosure may be rendered unenforceable against infringers.

**Landmark Case:** In *SmithKline v. Hindustan Ciba-Geigy* (Bombsay HC), the court emphasized that the specification is the "**foundation stone**" of the patent and must enable a person skilled in the art to work the invention.

**Conclusion — 2 Marks:** The disclosure requirement embodies the **social contract** of the patent system — the inventor gains a temporary monopoly, and society gains the technical knowledge to advance innovation.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Patent Right Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Novelty with Inventive Step (–2 marks):** Students often merge these two distinct tests. Novelty = NEW. Inventive Step = NON-OBVIOUS to PHOSITA. They are cumulative but separate.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Section 3(d) Evergreening Doctrine (–2 marks):** In pharmaceutical/IPR questions, students must cite *Novartis v. UOI* (2013) and explain the "enhanced efficacy" test.
> 
> 3. **Section 3(k) Misconception (–1.5 marks):** Writing "software is not patentable" is INCORRECT. Software "per se" is excluded, but **software with hardware integration and technical contribution** IS patentable.
> 
> 4. **Missing Statutory References (–1 mark):** Always cite **Section 2(1)(j), 2(1)(l), 2(1)(ja), 2(1)(ac), and Section 3** with subsection letters. Bare statements without statutory backing attract mark deductions.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring 20-Year Term Specifics (–1 mark):** State the term of patent explicitly as **20 years from the date of filing** under Section 53(1) — not from the date of grant.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — General Conditions for Conferring Patent Right**

### **A. Statutory Trilogy (Section 2(1)(j))**
- ✅ **Novelty** — Section 2(1)(l) — Global, universal, absolute test
- ✅ **Inventive Step** — Section 2(1)(ja) — PHOSITA test, technical/economic significance
- ✅ **Industrial Application** — Section 2(1)(ac) — Reproducibility, utility
- ✅ All three are **cumulative and conjunctive** (AND logic, not OR)

### **B. Key Exclusions to Memorize**
- ✅ **Section 3(b)** — Public order/morality
- ✅ **Section 3(c)** — Mere discovery of principles
- ✅ **Section 3(d)** — Evergreening prevention (cite *Novartis v. UOI*)
- ✅ **Section 3(h)** — Food, medicine, drug products per se
- ✅ **Section 3(i)** — Diagnostic methods
- ✅ **Section 3(j)** — Plants/animals (biological processes)
- ✅ **Section 3(k)** — Computer programs per se
- ✅ **Section 3(l)** — Copyright works
- ✅ **Section 3(m)** — Business methods, mental activity
- ✅ **Section 3(n)** — Presentation of information

### **C. Critical Numbers & Dates**
- ✅ **Patent Term:** **20 years** from filing date (Sec 53(1))
- ✅ **Publication:** **18 months** from priority date (Sec 11A)
- ✅ **Examination Request:** Within **48 months** of filing
- ✅ **Renewal:** From **3rd year** onwards (Sec 142)
- ✅ **Pre-grant Opposition:** Any person, before grant (Sec 25(1))
- ✅ **Post-grant Opposition:** Interested person, within 12 months (Sec 25(2))

### **D. Landmark Cases to Remember**
- ✅ **Bishwanath Prasad v. Hindustan Metal (1979)** — Definition of invention
- ✅ **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** — Section 3(d) enhanced efficacy
- ✅ **Ferid Allani v. UOI (2012)** — Computer program per se
- ✅ **F. Hoffmann-La Roche v. Cipla (2008)** — PHOSITA test in India
- ✅ **Microsoft v. Yogesh Papat (2016)** — Hardware-implemented software
- ✅ **Monsanto v. Nuziveedu (2018)** — Biotech patents

### **E. Quick Mnemonics**
- **"NIA"** for the three conditions: **N**ovelty, **I**nventive Step, **I**ndustrial **A**pplication
- **"3-D"** for key exclusions: **D**iscovery (3c), **D**rugs (3h), **D**ata/Business methods (3m/n)
- **"20-18-48"** for key time periods: **20** years term, **18** months publication, **48** months examination request

### **F. Examiner's Golden Rule**
> Always frame your answer with: **Statute → Definition → Test → Exception/Judicial Interpretation**. This structure guarantees full marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventions Not Patentable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inventions Not Patentable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Under the **Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2005), **Section 3** and **Section 4** explicitly enumerate categories of subject matter that are **excluded from patentability**. These provisions define the *negative space* of the patent system — that is, what an inventor **cannot** protect, regardless of how novel, useful, or industrially applicable the subject matter might be.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Foundation (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Section 3 of the Patents Act, 1970** — defines *what are not inventions* (9 exhaustive clauses, (a) through (j)).
> - **Section 4 of the Patents Act, 1970** — bars patentability of **inventions relating to atomic energy**, deferring to the Atomic Energy Act, 1962.

A patent is a **time-limited statutory monopoly** (20 years from the date of filing) granted in exchange for full public disclosure. Because it is a public-privilege instrument, the State withholds grant where grant would be:
- **Against public interest** (ordre public / morality),
- **Redundant** (already in public domain via discovery), or
- **Inappropriate for market exclusivity** (medical treatment methods, traditional knowledge).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Patent Gate"

Imagine a **fortress** that protects an inventor's rights — this fortress is the Patent Office. However, before you enter, you must pass through **three gates**:

1. **Gate of Novelty** — is it new?
2. **Gate of Industrial Application** — is it useful in industry?
3. **Gate of Section 3/4** — is it *legally eligible* at all?

The third gate acts as a **bouncer**. Even if your invention is brilliantly novel and useful, the bouncer turns you away if your invention falls into the blacklisted categories. For example:

| If you bring… | The "Gatekeeper" says… |
|---|---|
| A new law of gravity | "Sorry, this is a *scientific principle*, not an invention." |
| A method to treat cancer patients | "Sorry, *medical treatment methods* cannot be monopolized." |
| A recipe for a traditional Ayurvedic cure | "Sorry, *traditional knowledge* belongs to the community." |
| A new way to enrich uranium-235 | "Sorry, this falls under *atomic energy* — Section 4 bars it." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examiners:**
> - **Discovery** → finds something that already exists in nature → *not patentable*.
> - **Invention** → creates something that did not exist before → *potentially patentable* (subject to Section 3/4 hurdles).

## 1.3 The Two Pillars of Exclusion

1. **Section 3 — Negative Definition of "Invention"**
   The patent statute first **defines what an "invention" is NOT**. By doing so, it narrows the scope of patent-eligible subject matter before novelty/non-obviousness are even tested.

2. **Section 4 — Atomic Energy Carve-Out**
   A complete prohibition — patents for inventions whose use is necessary for atomic energy purposes are flatly refused (no discretion to the Controller).

---

## 1.4 Visual Snapshot of Exclusions (Quick Recall Block)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "FIDMA-AT-MC" for the 9 clauses of Section 3:**
> - **(a)** Frivolous / contrary to natural laws
> - **(b)** Injurious to public order / morality
> - **(c)** Mere discovery / scientific principle / formulation of abstract theory
> - **(d)** New forms of known substances (unless significantly enhanced efficacy)
> - **(e)** Substances obtained by mere admixture / aggregation
> - **(f)** Arrangement/rearrangement/duplication of known devices (unless producing a new product/efficiency)
> - **(g)** Methods of agriculture / horticulture
> - **(h)** Methods of medical / surgical / curative / prophylactic treatment / diagnostic
> - **(i)** Plants/animals in whole or any part thereof (other than micro-organisms); essentially biological processes (except microbiological); seeds, varieties, and species
> - **(j)** Traditional knowledge / mathematical / business method / computer program *per se*
> - **Section 4** → Atomic energy
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Patentability Test" — Operational Logic

Before an examiner reaches the merits, the invention must clear **three sequential filters**:

$$\text{Patent Grant} \;=\; \text{NOVELTY} \;\cap\; \text{NON-OBVIOUSNESS} \;\cap\; \text{INDUSTRIAL APPLICATION} \;\cap\; \overline{S3 \cup S4}$$

Where:
- $S3$ = any subject matter falling under **Section 3** clauses (a)–(j).
- $S4$ = any invention relating to **atomic energy**.
- $\overline{S3 \cup S4}$ denotes the *eligibility complement* — must be free of all exclusion grounds.

> In Boolean terms: **if ANY exclusion applies, the patent MUST be refused, regardless of how meritorious the invention is.**

## 2.2 Clause-by-Clause Theoretical Breakdown

### **Section 3(a) — Frivolous Inventions & Contradictions to Natural Laws**

**Logic:** Prevents patenting of absurdities (e.g., a "perpetual motion machine") or inventions whose claimed effect is scientifically impossible.

**Why excluded?** Granting monopolies on impossibilities would clutter the register and waste examination resources.

### **Section 3(b) — Inventions Contrary to Public Order or Morality**

**Logic:** A flexible, judge-made exclusion rooted in Article 19(2) and Article 21 of the Constitution (public order) and Article 25 (morality).

**Illustrative examples (not exhaustive):**
- Letter-bomb designs,
- Anti-personnel weapons specifically engineered to cause unnecessary suffering,
- Methods for cloning humans for commercial purposes.

> **Important nuance:** The bar applies only to the **publication/exploitation** of the invention, not the working of the patent itself.

### **Section 3(c) — Discoveries, Scientific Principles, Abstract Theories**

**Logic:** Patents reward *technical contribution to the art*, not *mental insight into nature*. Einstein's $E=mc^{2}$ is profound but **not patentable**.

$$E = mc^{2} \quad \text{(a physical law — Section 3(c) bars patentability)}$$

**Distinction — *Discovery vs. Invention* (KTU high-yield):**

| Feature | Discovery | Invention |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Reveals pre-existing fact | Creates new artificial entity |
| Effort | Intellectual / observational | Technical / constructive |
| Patentable? | No (s.3(c)) | Yes, subject to other tests |
| Example | Identification of a new gene | A genetically engineered drug based on that gene |

> **BUT:** *Practical application* of a discovery IS patentable (e.g., a process to isolate the gene → patentable).

### **Section 3(d) — New Forms of Known Substances**

This is one of the **most litigated clauses**, especially in pharmaceuticals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 3(d) — Statutory Text (paraphrased):**
> "The mere discovery of a new form of a known substance which does not result in the enhancement of the known efficacy of that substance is not patentable."

**Key Interpretive Doctrine — *Novartis v. Union of India* (2013, Supreme Court):**
The drug **Imatinib Mesylate (Gleevec)** was held non-patentable as it was a new crystalline form (beta form) of a known substance (Imatinib) without demonstrated *significantly enhanced therapeutic efficacy*. The Court read down the doctrine to prevent **evergreening** of pharmaceutical patents.

$$\text{Efficacy}_{\text{new form}} > \text{Efficacy}_{\text{known form}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Patent-eligible under 3(d)}$$

### **Section 3(e) — Substances by Mere Admixture / Aggregation**

**Logic:** Mixing two known substances in known proportions does not produce an inventive step unless the combination produces a **synergistic** or **unexpected** effect.

> Example: Mixing paracetamol + caffeine in fixed known proportions → likely not patentable, unless the combination yields unexpectedly superior analgesic effect.

### **Section 3(f) — Mere Arrangement / Rearrangement of Known Devices**

**Logic:** A mere "re-shuffling" of known parts, without a *new product* or *enhanced efficiency*, lacks inventive step.

> **Synergy Test:** If the rearrangement yields an effect *greater than the sum of individual parts*, patent-eligibility may revive.

### **Section 3(g) — Methods of Agriculture and Horticulture**

**Logic:** Patents must not monopolize **fundamental food-production processes**, as this threatens food security.

> **However:** *Equipment* used in agriculture (e.g., a novel tractor) IS patentable; *microbiological processes* and *plant varieties* (Plant Varieties Protection Act route) have separate IP regimes.

### **Section 3(h) — Methods of Medical / Surgical Treatment**

**Logic:** Doctors must be free to treat patients using the best-known method without fear of infringement. A patient is not an infringement venue.

$$\text{Product used in treatment} \;=\; \text{PATENTABLE} \quad ; \quad \text{Method of treatment} \;=\; \text{NOT PATENTABLE}$$

> **KTU Hot Question:** *"Why are medicines patentable but methods of treatment are not?"* — Answer: Products reward the innovator; methods would lock up the *practice of medicine itself*.

### **Section 3(i) — Plants, Animals, Biological Processes**

- **Plants and animals** (in whole or part) — not patentable.
- **Micro-organisms** — patentable.
- **Essentially biological processes** for plant/animal production — not patentable.
- **Microbiological processes** — patentable.
- **Seeds, varieties, species** — protected under PPV&FR Act, 2001 (Plant Variety), not Patents Act.

### **Section 3(j) — The Composite Exclusion (Post-2005 Amendment)**

Excludes:
1. Traditional knowledge,
2. Mathematical methods,
3. Business methods,
4. Computer programs *per se*.

> **Critical caveat — "per se":** A computer program *as such* is excluded; but when embedded in a **technical application** producing a *technical effect / improvement*, it MAY be patentable (e.g., a novel data-compression algorithm integrated into a hardware system).

$$\text{Computer Program}_{\text{per se}} \;=\; \text{NOT PATENTABLE}$$

$$\text{Computer Program}_{\text{with technical contribution}} \;=\; \text{POTENTIALLY PATENTABLE}$$

### **Section 4 — Atomic Energy**

A flat, **non-discretionary bar**:
> "No patent shall be granted in respect of inventions relating to atomic energy."

Such inventions fall under the **Atomic Energy Act, 1962** and **Atomic Energy (Radiation Processing of Food and Allied Products) Rules**, with the Department of Atomic Energy (DAE) as the controlling authority.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Section | Exclusion | Why? | Engineering / Industry Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3(a) | Frivolous / contra-natural | Impossible or trivial claims | Perpetual motion machine |
| 3(b) | Public order / morality | Constitutional morality, public safety | Letter bomb |
| 3(c) | Discoveries / abstract theories | Pre-existing, no human contribution | Discovery of a new star |
| 3(d) | New forms of known substances | Anti-evergreening (TRIPS Art. 27 compliant) | Crystalline form of known drug |
| 3(e) | Mere admixture | No synergistic effect | Mixed juice of known fruits |
| 3(f) | Rearrangement of devices | No new product / efficiency | Interchanging seats on a bus |
| 3(g) | Agriculture/horticulture methods | Food security | A new irrigation cycle |
| 3(h) | Medical treatment methods | Free practice of medicine | A new surgical technique |
| 3(i) | Plants/animals (general) | Separate sui generis regime | A new mango variety |
| 3(j) | TK / math / business methods / software per se | Public domain / abstract | Pure algorithm; "a new e-commerce idea" |
| 4 | Atomic energy | Strategic national interest | Uranium enrichment process |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Exclusions in Engineering Fields

- **Pharmaceutical Engineering:** Section 3(d) forces researchers to demonstrate **enhanced efficacy** rather than merely file new polymorphs.
- **Computer Science Engineering:** Section 3(j) requires coders to embed algorithms in **technical systems** to attract patent protection.
- **Biotechnology:** Section 3(i) channels innovation toward the **microbiological** route rather than macro-biology.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Section 3(f) discourages trivial re-designs and incentivizes genuine mechanical synergy.
- **Civil / Agricultural Engineering:** Section 3(g) keeps agricultural innovation within the open framework of extension services.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Sequential Reasoning Framework — How the Controller Applies Section 3/4

When a patent application lands at the Indian Patent Office, the examiner follows this structured reasoning chain:

**Step 1 → Identify the claimed subject matter**
What is being claimed? A product? A process? A method? A substance?

**Step 2 → Map to the relevant Section 3 clause(s)**
Match the subject matter against the 10 exclusion categories.

**Step 3 → Test for the statutory escape clause**
For Section 3(d): Is there **enhanced efficacy**? For 3(f): Is there a **new product or synergistic effect**? For 3(j): Is there a **technical application**?

**Step 4 → Apply Section 4 (if applicable)**
Does the invention pertain to atomic energy? If yes, refuse without further inquiry.

**Step 5 → Final disposition**
- All exclusions cleared → proceed to novelty / inventive step / industrial application.
- Any exclusion triggered → refusal under Section 15.

Mathematically, the examiner's decision can be expressed as:

$$
D \;=\; 
\begin{cases}
\text{GRANT}, & \text{if } \text{Subject} \notin \{S3_a, S3_b, \dots, S3_j\} \cup S4 \\
\text{REFUSE}, & \text{if } \text{Subject} \in \{S3_a, S3_b, \dots, S3_j\} \cup S4
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Case Law Application of Section 3

The following matrix maps landmark judicial interpretations to the relevant statutory clauses:

| Case (Year) | Court | Patent Refused? | Section Invoked | Key Takeaway |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Novartis v. UoI** (2013) | Supreme Court | Yes | 3(d) | New crystalline form without enhanced efficacy = evergreening, denied. |
| **Dimminaco v. Controller** (2002) | Supreme Court | No | 3(c), 3(j) | If a process yields a novel product, the process is patentable even if it uses known principles. |
| **Bilski v. Kappos** (2010, US) | US Supreme Court | (Reference) | Abstract idea | Abstract business method ≠ patentable unless tied to a machine. |
| **In re Fisher** (US, 2012) | Federal Circuit | (Reference) | 3(i)-like | Laws of nature are not patentable; applications thereof are. |
| **Monsanto v. Coramandel** (2019) | Delhi HC | No | 3(g) | Agricultural equipment ≠ agricultural method; allowed. |

## 3.3 Deep Analytical Walkthrough — *Novartis v. Union of India*

This is the **single most cited** KTU-relevant case under Section 3. Let us walk through it analytically:

**Background:**
- Novartis held a patent on Imatinib (1993) for leukemia treatment.
- In 1997, the compound's beta-crystalline form was developed.
- Novartis sought a patent on the **beta form**, citing better thermodynamic stability.
- The Patent Controller refused under Section 3(d).

**The Two Key Tests laid down by the Supreme Court:**

> **Test 1 — "New Form" Inquiry:**
> A new form is a substance that emerges with a distinct identity, character, or use.

> **Test 2 — "Efficacy" Inquiry:**
> "Efficacy" means **therapeutic efficacy** — the ability of the substance to produce a desired therapeutic effect on the disease in question.

**Numerical comparison in the judgment:**

$$
\text{Efficacy}_{\text{beta form}} \approx \text{Efficacy}_{\text{known form}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Section 3(d) bars grant}
$$

The Court explicitly rejected arguments based on **bioavailability** alone, holding that *improved bioavailability ≠ enhanced therapeutic efficacy* unless the latter is established.

**KTU Significance:**
- Reinforces India's compliance with **TRIPS Art. 27.2** (ordre public/morality exception) and the **Doha Declaration** (public health flexibilities).
- Set the precedent for all subsequent evergreening cases.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Table — Discoveries vs. Inventions vs. Innovations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Discovery} &\xrightarrow{\text{reveals}} \text{Pre-existing Phenomenon} \\
\text{Invention} &\xrightarrow{\text{creates}} \text{Novel Technical Solution} \\
\text{Innovation} &\xrightarrow{\text{refines/commercializes}} \text{Market-Ready Improvement}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Attribute | Discovery | Invention | Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temporal existence | Existed before human thought | Did not exist before | Exists, is improved |
| Patent-eligible? | No (s.3(c)) | Yes, if other tests pass | Yes, if non-obvious |
| Effort type | Observational / analytical | Constructive / experimental | Commercial / iterative |
| KTU example | Discovery of a new antibiotic bacterium | A novel purification process | A faster, cheaper version of the process |

## 3.5 Resolution Logic for "Soft Cases" (Engineering Applications)

When an invention appears borderline, apply the **Stepped Filter** below:

**Step 1:** Is there a *physical transformation* or *technical effect*? If NO → likely **3(j)** exclusion (abstract / business / software per se).

**Step 2:** Is the subject a *living organism* in whole? If YES → likely **3(i)** exclusion (unless micro-organism).

**Step 3:** Does it merely *reformulate* a known substance? If YES → likely **3(d)/(e)** unless enhanced efficacy.

**Step 4:** Is it used in *medical treatment* of a human body? If YES → likely **3(h)** exclusion.

**Step 5:** Does it *contradict natural laws* or threaten *public order*? If YES → **3(a)/(b)** refusal.

**Step 6:** Is it a process for *atomic energy*? If YES → **Section 4** refusal.

**Step 7:** Otherwise, the invention proceeds to novelty / inventive step / industrial application examination.

This seven-step triage is what the **Indian Patent Office's Manual of Patent Practice and Procedure** codifies for examiners.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Patentability Filter Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart visualizes the **decision architecture** the Controller of Patents follows when applying Sections 3 and 4:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Patent Application Filed]) --> idA1{Identify Subject Matter?}
    idA1 --> idA2[Product Claim]
    idA1 --> idA3[Process Claim]
    idA1 --> idA4[Method Claim]
    idA1 --> idA5[Substance Claim]

    idA2 --> chkA1{Frivolous / Natural Law Contradiction?}
    chkA1 -- Yes --> rejA1[REFUSE s.3 a]
    chkA1 -- No --> chkA2{Public Order / Morality Issue?}
    chkA2 -- Yes --> rejA2[REFUSE s.3 b]
    chkA2 -- No --> chkA3

    idA3 --> chkA3{Mere Discovery / Scientific Principle?}
    chkA3 -- Yes --> rejA3[REFUSE s.3 c]
    chkA3 -- No --> chkA4{New Form of Known Substance?}
    chkA4 -- Yes --> chkA4b{Enhanced Therapeutic Efficacy?}
    chkA4b -- No --> rejA4[REFUSE s.3 d Novartis Doctrine]
    chkA4b -- Yes --> chkA5
    chkA4 -- No --> chkA5

    chkA5{Agriculture or Horticulture Method?}
    chkA5 -- Yes --> rejA5[REFUSE s.3 g]
    chkA5 -- No --> chkA6{Medical Surgical Diagnostic Method?}
    chkA6 -- Yes --> rejA6[REFUSE s.3 h]
    chkA6 -- No --> chkA7

    chkA7{Plants or Animals in Whole?}
    chkA7 -- Yes --> rejA7[REFUSE s.3 i]
    chkA7 -- No --> chkA8{Traditional Knowledge or Math or Business Method or Software per se?}
    chkA8 -- Yes --> chkA8b{Technical Application Embedded?}
    chkA8b -- No --> rejA8[REFUSE s.3 j]
    chkA8b -- Yes --> chkA9

    chkA9{Atomic Energy Application?}
    chkA9 -- Yes --> rejA9[REFUSE s.4]
    chkA9 -- No --> okA1[PROCEED to Novelty / Inventive Step / Industrial Application]

    rejA1 --> finalA([REFUSED])
    rejA2 --> finalA
    rejA3 --> finalA
    rejA4 --> finalA
    rejA5 --> finalA
    rejA6 --> finalA
    rejA7 --> finalA
    rejA8 --> finalA
    rejA9 --> finalA
    okA1 --> okA2([EXAMINED ON MERITS])
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Section 3 Exclusion Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    parentA[Non Patentable Universe] --> branchA1[Moral and Policy Exclusions]
    parentA --> branchA2[Conceptual Exclusions]
    parentA --> branchA3[Substance Exclusions]
    parentA --> branchA4[Domain Exclusions]
    parentA --> branchA5[Miscellaneous Exclusions]

    branchA1 --> leafA1a[s.3 a Frivolous]
    branchA1 --> leafA1b[s.3 b Public Order Morality]

    branchA2 --> leafA2a[s.3 c Discovery Principle]
    branchA2 --> leafA2b[s.3 j Math Business Software per se]

    branchA3 --> leafA3a[s.3 d New Form of Known]
    branchA3 --> leafA3b[s.3 e Mere Admixture]
    branchA3 --> leafA3c[s.3 f Rearrangement]

    branchA4 --> leafA4a[s.3 g Agriculture]
    branchA4 --> leafA4b[s.3 h Medical Treatment]
    branchA4 --> leafA4c[s.3 i Plants Animals Biological]

    branchA5 --> leafA5a[s.4 Atomic Energy]
```

## 4.3 Timeline Block Diagram — Evolution of Section 3 (1970 → 2005)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    y1970[1970 Original Act] --> y1972[1972 First Amendment]
    y1972 --> y1999[1999 Amendments]
    y1999 --> y2002[2002 Ordinance]
    y2002 --> y2005[2005 Major Amendment]

    y1970 -- "s.3 d introduced: new forms" --> noteA
    y2002 -- "product patents for drugs" --> noteB
    y2005 -- "TK Math Business Software per se added to s.3 j" --> noteC

    noteA[Note A Anti Evergreening Origin]
    noteB[Note B TRIPS Compliance]
    noteC[Note C Public Domain Protection]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define "non-patentable inventions" under the Patents Act, 1970. List any six categories with one example each.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Non-patentable inventions are subject matters that, despite satisfying the basic tests of novelty and utility, are statutorily excluded from patent protection under **Section 3 (clauses a–j)** and **Section 4** of the Patents Act, 1970.

**Six Categories:**

| Clause | Exclusion | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 3(a) | Frivolous / natural-law contradiction | Perpetual motion machine |
| 3(b) | Public order / morality | Anti-personnel weapon design |
| 3(c) | Mere discovery | Discovery of a new bacterium in nature |
| 3(g) | Agricultural method | A new crop rotation cycle |
| 3(h) | Medical treatment method | A new surgical incision technique |
| 3(j) | Software per se | A standalone sorting algorithm |

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between "discovery" and "invention" with reference to Section 3(c).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Discovery | Invention |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Reveals something already existing in nature | Creates something not existing before |
| Patent status | Not patentable under s.3(c) | Patentable if other tests are satisfied |
| Human effort | Observational / mental | Technical / constructive |
| Patent scope | Not applicable | 20-year monopoly from filing date |
| Example | Discovery of penicillin by Fleming | Development of a novel drug formulation |
| Legal principle | "Discovery is the work of God; invention is the work of man" | Quoted in *American Cotton-Tie Co. v. Simmons* |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU Scheme Note:** KTU ESE typically offers Module-based internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each with sub-parts carrying 7 marks each. Answer ANY ONE full question.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Section 3 in depth, Novartis doctrine, and statutory interpretation.*

**(a) Discuss in detail the provisions of Section 3(d) of the Patents Act, 1970. How did the Supreme Court interpret it in the Novartis case? (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Statutory Provision (paraphrased):**
Section 3(d) excludes from patentability:
> "The mere discovery of a new form of a known substance which does not result in the enhancement of the known efficacy of that substance."

**Key Phrases and Their Meaning:**
- **"Mere discovery"** — to rule out trivial variations like polymorphs, salts, esters, ethers, etc.
- **"New form of a known substance"** — includes new physical forms, derivatives, or combinations.
