<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**What is meant by the publication of a patent application under the Indian Patents Act, 1970? When is a patent application published?**

**Model Answer:**

Publication of a patent application is the act of making the contents of a patent application available to the public by including it in the Patent Office Journal. Under **Section 11A(1)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970, every patent application (except applications abandoned under Section 9(1)) shall be published ordinarily after the expiry of **18 months** from the date of filing or the date of priority claimed, whichever is earlier.

The applicant may, however, request **early publication** by filing **Form 9** along with the prescribed fee. Upon such request, the application may be published within **one month** from the date of request.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for 18-month rule, 1 mark for early publication provision.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Distinguish between 'Publication' and 'Grant' of a patent application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Publication | Grant |
|---|---|---|
| **Statutory Provision** | Section 11A | Section 43 |
| **Nature** | Merely informational — makes application public | Creates a statutory monopoly right |
| **Automatic?** | Yes, after 18 months | No, only after examination and acceptance |
| **Right Conferred** | Provisional right to claim damages (if ultimately granted) | Exclusive right to exclude others (20 years) |
| **Effect on Third Parties** | Constructive notice of the pending application | Full legal enforceability of claims |

*(3 marks — for clear distinction across at least 3 parameters.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 2 Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for the **publication of a patent application** under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Discuss the **legal effects** of publication, including the concept of **provisional rights**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **examination procedure** of patent applications with specific reference to **Sections 11B, 12, and 14** of the Indian Patents Act. What is a **First Examination Report (FER)**, and how should an applicant respond to it? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Procedure for Publication & Provisional Rights (7 Marks)

The publication of a patent application is governed by **Section 11A** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970.

**1. Statutory Timeline (2 marks):**
- The Controller shall publish every patent application in the **Patent Office Journal** ordinarily after the expiry of **18 months** from the date of filing or the date of priority, whichever is earlier.
- Applications withdrawn under Section 9(1) prior to publication are excluded.

**2. Early Publication (2 marks):**
- Under **Section 11A(2)**, the applicant may request earlier publication by filing **Form 9** along with the prescribed fee.
- Upon such request, the application is published within **1 month** of the request, subject to the Patent Office's administrative capacity.
- Early publication is commercially advantageous as it establishes the priority date publicly and starts the provisional rights clock.

**3. Provisional Rights under Section 11A(4) (3 marks):**
- Between publication and grant, the applicant acquires a **provisional right** to claim **damages** (not accounts of profits) for any infringement of the invention as claimed in the published application.
- **Crucial Condition:** Such damages can only be awarded if the patent is **ultimately granted** with claims substantially the same as those published.
- The damages are calculated from the date of publication up to the date of grant.
- This provision is meant to **deter** third parties from copying the invention between publication and grant.

---

##### Part (b) — Examination Procedure & FER (7 Marks)

**1. Request for Examination (Section 11B) — 2 marks:**
- Examination is **not automatic**. The applicant must file **Form 18** along with the prescribed fee to request examination.
- The deadline is **48 months** (per 2021 Amendment Rules) from the filing date or priority date, whichever is earlier.
- Any other person or the applicant may also request examination under certain circumstances.
- Failure to file Form 18 results in the application being **deemed withdrawn** under Section 11B(4).

**2. Examination by Examiner (Section 12) — 2 marks:**
- Upon receipt of the RFE, the Controller refers the application to an examiner possessing the requisite technical expertise.
- The examiner conducts a **prior art search** across patent databases (Indian, USPTO, EPO, WIPO, Derwent) and non-patent literature.
- The examiner then assesses the claimed invention against:
  - **Novelty** (Section 2(1)(j))
  - **Inventive Step** (Section 2(1)(ja))
  - **Industrial Application** (Section 2(1)(ac))
  - **Exclusions** under Section 3 (e.g., Section 3(d) on new forms of known substances)
  - **Disclosure sufficiency** under Section 10(4)
- The examiner prepares a **First Examination Report (FER)** stating the objections and citing the relevant prior art documents (D1, D2, etc.).

**3. FER Issuance and Applicant's Response (Section 12 & Section 21) — 2 marks:**
- The FER is communicated to the applicant within the prescribed period.
- The applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable by **3 months** on request).
- The response may include:
  - **Amendments** to the claims, description, or abstract (within the limits of Section 59).
  - **Written arguments** distinguishing the cited prior art.
  - **Feature comparison tables** demonstrating novelty and inventive step.
- If objections persist, a **hearing** under **Section 14** is scheduled before the Controller.

**4. Final Disposal (1 mark):**
- After considering the applicant's response and hearing, the Controller either **accepts** the application (leading to grant under Section 43) or **refuses** it.
- A refused application may be appealed to the **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** or, post its dissolution, the relevant commercial court under the **Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 2 Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2025 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **grounds on which a patent application may be examined** by the Patent Office. How does the examiner determine **novelty** and **inventive step**? Illustrate with reference to relevant provisions of the Indian Patents Act, 1970. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is a **hearing** under Section 14 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970? Explain the **rights of the applicant** during the hearing and the **consequences** if the applicant fails to appear. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Grounds of Examination, Novelty & Inventive Step (7 Marks)

**1. Statutory Grounds of Examination (3 marks):**

The Patent Examiner evaluates a patent application against the following statutory grounds:

- **Novelty** (Section 2(1)(j)): The invention must not be anticipated by any single prior art reference published anywhere in the world before the priority date.
- **Inventive Step** (Section 2(1)(ja)): The invention must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PSITA) when the prior art is viewed as a whole.
- **Industrial Application** (Section 2(1)(ac)): The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry.
- **Non-exclusion under Section 3**: The invention must not fall within the excluded categories (frivolous inventions, atomic energy inventions, methods of agriculture/horticulture, methods of treatment, plants/animals, mathematical/business methods, traditional knowledge, etc.).
- **Non-exclusion under Section 4**: Atomic energy inventions are not patentable.
- **Sufficiency of Disclosure** (Section 10(4)): The specification must describe the invention sufficiently to enable the PSITA to perform it.
- **Clarity and Conciseness of Claims** (Section 10(4)(c)).
- **Proper Identification of Inventor** (Section 6).
- **First-to-File Compliance** (Section 6).

**2. Determining Novelty (2 marks):**

Novelty is assessed through a **single-document comparison test**. The examiner identifies the closest prior art document (D1) and checks whether it discloses **all** the essential features of the claimed invention. If even a single distinguishing feature exists, the invention is novel.

The **Anticipation Test** has two formulations:
- **Inherent Anticipation:** If a prior art document inherently contains the invention, even if not explicitly stated, it anticipates.
- **Implicit Disclosure:** What a skilled person would directly and unambiguously derive from the prior art counts.

**3. Determining Inventive Step (2 marks):**

Inventive step is assessed through a **multi-document mosaic problem-solution approach**:

- **Could-would approach:** Could the PSITA, starting from the closest prior art, have arrived at the invention? Would they have done so?
- The examiner often combines 2–3 documents (D1, D2, D3) and asks whether combining them would yield the claimed invention.
- Factors like **long-felt need**, **commercial success**, **failure of others**, and **unexpected technical effect** are weighed in favour of inventive step.
- The **Federal Circuit / EPO problem-solution approach** is also relevant.

---

##### Part (b) — Hearing under Section 14 (7 Marks)

**1. Right to Hearing (2 marks):**
- Under **Section 14** of the Indian Patents Act, the Controller must give an applicant an opportunity to be heard before exercising adverse powers such as refusing an application or imposing post-grant amendments.
- This embodies the **principle of natural justice** — *audi alteram partem* (hear the other side).

**2. Procedure for Hearing (2 marks):**
- The applicant is given a **notice of hearing** specifying the date, time, and place (at least **10 days** in advance, as per the Patent Rules).
- The applicant may appear in person, through a registered patent agent (Form 26), or through an authorised representative.
- During the hearing, the applicant may:
  - Make **oral submissions** supplementing written submissions.
  - Present **additional evidence** or expert opinions.
  - Request **further time** to file written submissions.

**3. Consequences of Non-Attendance (2 marks):**
- If the applicant does not attend the hearing despite notice, the Controller may proceed **ex parte** and decide based on the file records.
- The applicant may request **restoration** of the hearing within the prescribed period.
- An adverse order passed after hearing may be **appealed** to the appropriate appellate authority.

**4. Disposal After Hearing (1 mark):**
- Post-hearing, the Controller may accept the application, refuse it, or impose conditions/amendments.
- The decision is communicated in writing with reasons.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks-Losing Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "publication" with "grant."** Many students erroneously state that publication confers monopoly rights. It only confers provisional rights to claim damages (Section 11A(4)).
> 2. **Wrong deadline for RFE.** Using **36 months** instead of the current **48 months** (post-2021 Amendment Rules) is a frequent error.
> 3. **Forgetting the 6 + 3 = 9 month response window.** Students sometimes state only 6 months and forget the extension.
> 4. **Missing the role of the patent agent.** Only a registered patent agent (Form 26) can represent an applicant in hearings.
> 5. **Confusing Sections 11A and 11B.** Publication (11A) is automatic; Examination (11B) requires an explicit request.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Publication** is governed by **Section 11A** and occurs automatically after **18 months** from filing or priority date.
- [ ] **Early publication** can be requested via **Form 9**.
- [ ] **Provisional rights** under Section 11A(4) entitle the applicant to claim **damages** (not profits) for infringement between publication and grant, *only if the patent is ultimately granted*.
- [ ] **Examination** is **not automatic** — it requires filing **Form 18** (Request for Examination).
- [ ] The **deadline for Form 18** is **48 months** (post-2021 amendment) from filing or priority date.
- [ ] The **First Examination Report (FER)** is the examiner's reasoned opinion on patentability.
- [ ] **Patentability grounds** are novelty, inventive step, industrial application, non-exclusion under Section 3, and sufficiency of disclosure under Section 10(4).
- [ ] **Section 14** entitles the applicant to a **hearing** before any adverse order.
- [ ] The applicant must respond to the FER within **6 months**, extendable by **3 months**.
- [ ] Pre-grant opposition under **Section 25(1)** is available after publication but before grant.
- [ ] Post-grant opposition under **Section 25(2)** must be filed within **12 months** of grant.
- [ ] The Form 9, Form 18, Form 3 (working statement), and Form 26 (authorization of patent agent) are critical procedural forms.
- [ ] **Publication ≠ Grant** — a key conceptual distinction that examiners reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Search for Anticipation by Previous Publication and Prior Claim
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Search for Anticipation by Previous Publication and Prior Claim

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Anticipation by Previous Publication** refers to the destruction of the **novelty** of an invention on the basis of information made available to the public *through any document or medium* **before the date of filing** (or priority date) of the patent application under examination. It is the primary ground on which a patent application can be **anticipated, opposed, or revoked** under the Patents Act, 1970.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Anchor (India):** Section 25(1)(d) of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 (as amended in 2005) explicitly lists "anticipation by previous publication" as a ground for opposition and revocation of a patent. Similarly, Section 64(1)(a) makes it a ground for revocation.

**Anticipation by Prior Claim (India)** refers to the situation where an invention lacks novelty because it has been **claimed in a valid claim of another patent** (whether granted in India or filed as a complete specification in India) having an **earlier priority date**, even though the invention may not have been physically published or practiced publicly. It is governed by **Section 25(1)(e)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970.

> [!NOTE]
> **Novelty vs. Anticipation:** Novelty is the *requirement* (Section 2(1)(j) – "new invention"). Anticipation is the *failure mode* – something in the prior art destroys that novelty.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a photographer who takes a stunning landscape photo. The picture is breathtaking *only because no one has ever captured that exact view before*. Now imagine that, six months ago, another photographer had already published the *same composition, lighting, and angle* on the internet.

**The second photograph is no longer novel.** Even if the second photographer never knew about the first, the *photograph itself* had already entered the **public domain** through that earlier publication. This is **anticipation by previous publication**.

Now consider a slightly different twist: another photographer had *kept the photo private* but had filed a **claim/copyright registration** for it a year earlier. Even though nobody has *seen* the photo, the **claim** itself establishes a legal priority that prevents the second photographer from claiming novelty for the same view. This is **anticipation by prior claim**.

> [!NOTE]
> **In one line:** *Publication = the world has seen it. Prior claim = someone has legally staked a claim to it, even if unseen.*

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

While this is a legal topic, the following **standard metrics** are universally applied by patent offices and courts:

| Metric | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **Novelty Grace Period (India)** | **12 months** (for disclosure at academic conferences, government exhibitions, or by an inventor before filing) – Section 31 |
| **Convention Priority Period (Paris Convention)** | **12 months** for patents |
| **PCT National Phase Entry** | **30 / 31 months** from priority date |
| **Critical Date / Priority Date** | Filing date, or earlier priority date if priority is claimed |
| **Person Skilled in the Art (PSIA)** | A **hypothetical competent professional** in the relevant field, used to judge whether prior art teaches the invention |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Anticipation:** Every element of the claimed invention must be found, either expressly or implicitly, in a **single prior art reference** to anticipate novelty. This is the famous *"single prior art rule"* (also called *strict novelty* under Article 54 EPC).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for Anticipation by Previous Publication

A prior publication will **anticipate** a claimed invention if **all** of the following conditions are satisfied:

1. **Existence of a Prior Document** – A patent, published application, article, thesis, technical manual, catalogue, website, or any printed/electronic disclosure.
2. **Single Source Rule** – The disclosure of *every feature* of the claimed invention must be derivable from **one single prior art reference** (no "mosaicing" of multiple references for novelty, though mosaicing is allowed for inventive step).
3. **Enabling Disclosure** – The prior art must enable the **Person Skilled in the Art (PSIA)** to perform the invention without undue burden or inventive contribution.
4. **Public Accessibility** – The document must have been *accessible to at least one member of the public* before the priority/filing date. (Confidential disclosure to a single person under NDA does **not** count.)
5. **Date of Publication ≤ Priority Date** – The publication date must be **on or before** the priority date of the application under examination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Enabling Disclosure Test:** A publication that *mentions* the invention but does not *teach* how to perform it is generally **not** anticipatory. The classic case: *Monsanto v. Coramandal* (AIR 1986 SC 712) where the Indian Supreme Court emphasized the *enabling disclosure* doctrine.

## 2.2 Categories of "Publication" Under Indian Patent Law

| # | Type of Publication | Example | Anticipatory? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Printed patent document** (any country) | US patent, EP patent, JP patent | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | **Published patent application** | Pre-grant publication in Indian Patent Office Journal | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | **Scientific journal / research paper** | IEEE, Nature, Elsevier articles | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | **Thesis / Dissertation** | PhD thesis in university library | ✅ Yes |
| 5 | **Technical manual / catalogue** | Product datasheets, user manuals | ✅ Yes (if enabling) |
| 6 | **Web publication** | Online article, blog post, YouTube video | ✅ Yes (if date verifiable) |
| 7 | **Conference proceedings** | IEEE Conference paper | ✅ Yes |
| 8 | **Oral disclosure at conference** | Talk without written record | ⚠️ Limited; may trigger grace period |
| 9 | **Confidential disclosure under NDA** | Disclosure to a single licensee | ❌ No |

## 2.3 Anticipation by Prior Claim (Section 25(1)(e))

**Prior Claim** is a unique ground under Indian patent law. It applies when:

- A **valid claim** exists in another patent or complete specification, AND
- The priority date of that claim is **earlier** than the application under examination, AND
- The claim is for the **same invention** (or makes the invention obvious).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Under prior claim, the prior art patent need not be *granted* — it is sufficient that a **complete specification** was **filed** in India. This makes prior claim a stronger ground for opposition than a simple publication.

**Illustrative Case:** If *Patent A* (filed 2015, granted 2018) contains a claim for "a solar cell using perovskite material with efficiency > 20%," and *Patent B* (filed 2020) claims a similar invention, then *Patent A's* complete specification filed in 2015 can be used as **prior claim** against *Patent B* — even though no one had built or sold the product.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Anticipation Logic)

| # | Logical Test | Formula / Condition | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Novelty Test (Single Ref.)** | $\forall f \in \text{Claim}, \; f \in \text{PriorArt}_{\text{single}}$ | If TRUE → Anticipated |
| 2 | **Priority Date Test** | $D_{\text{publication}} \leq D_{\text{priority}}$ | If TRUE → Anticipated |
| 3 | **Enabling Disclosure Test** | $\text{PSIA can perform invention} = \text{TRUE}$ | If TRUE → Anticipated |
| 4 | **Public Accessibility Test** | $\text{Member of public} \geq 1$ had access | If TRUE → Anticipated |
| 5 | **Same Invention Test (Prior Claim)** | $\text{Claim}_{\text{prior}} \equiv \text{Claim}_{\text{current}}$ | If TRUE → Anticipated |
| 6 | **Prior Claim Date Test** | $D_{\text{claim\_filed}} < D_{\text{current\_priority}}$ | If TRUE → Prior claim established |

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful Mnemonic for KTU exam:** **"SPEDS-C"** → **S**ingle reference, **P**ublic, **E**nabling, **D**ate earlier, **S**ame invention, **C**omplete specification (for prior claim).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of This Doctrine

| Engineering Domain | Application of Anticipation Doctrine |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Searching prior art before R&D investment to avoid infringement |
| **Software / AI** | Establishing novelty of algorithms in patent journals/conferences |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Validating product designs against international patent databases |
| **Biotechnology** | Genomic sequence novelty vs. prior disclosures in research papers |
| **Electronics** | Semiconductor device novelty vs. IEEE/Japio disclosures |
| **Startups / SMEs** | Freedom-to-Operate (FTO) analysis before commercialization |

## 2.6 Search Strategy (Practical Steps for Engineers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-Step Patent Search Methodology (recommended by WIPO & Indian Patent Office):**
> 1. **Define keywords** using IPC/CPC classification codes.
> 2. **Search Indian Patent Database (IP India – ipindiaonline.gov.in)**.
> 3. **Search international databases** (WIPO PATENTSCOPE, USPTO, EPO Espacenet, Google Patents).
> 4. **Search non-patent literature** (IEEE Xplore, ScienceDirect, Google Scholar).
> 5. **Analyze results** for novelty, inventive step, and freedom-to-operate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Anticipation Decision Tree (Analytical Derivation)

Let us mathematically formulate the anticipation test. Define the sets:

- $\mathbf{C} = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ = set of **claim features** of the application under examination.
- $\mathbf{P} = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m\}$ = set of features disclosed in a **single prior art reference**.
- $\mathbf{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k\}$ = set of features publicly accessible (the "anticipation universe").
- $D_c$ = Priority/filing date of current application.
- $D_p$ = Publication date of prior art.

The **anticipation function** is defined as:

$$
\text{ANTICIPATED} =
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } \mathbf{C} \subseteq \mathbf{P} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{P} \subseteq \mathbf{A} \;\wedge\; D_p \leq D_c \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 1: Verify Set Inclusion (Novelty Test)**
We must check whether every claim feature is present in the prior art:

$$
\forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}, \quad c_i \in \mathbf{P}
$$

**Step 2: Verify Public Accessibility**

$$
\mathbf{P} \subseteq \mathbf{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \exists x \in \mathbf{A}: x \notin \{\text{confidential recipients}\}
$$

**Step 3: Verify Date Condition**

$$
D_p \leq D_c \quad \text{(earlier or equal publication date)}
$$

**Step 4: Final Verdict**

$$
\text{Novelty destroyed} \iff \text{ANTICIPATED} = 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on "Implicit Disclosure":** A feature $c_i$ is considered to be in $\mathbf{P}$ even if not explicitly stated, as long as it is *necessarily inherent* or *implicitly derivable* by the Person Skilled in the Art. This is the **inherent disclosure doctrine** (e.g., *Schering v. Zydus*).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Mark-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A patent application is filed on **15-Mar-2022** claiming a "wireless earbud with hybrid active noise cancellation and biometric heart-rate monitoring." The following prior art is found:

| Ref. No. | Document | Publication Date | Discloses ANC? | Discloses HRM? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | US Patent US10123456B2 | 10-Jan-2020 | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| D2 | IEEE Conference Paper | 22-Jul-2021 | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| D3 | Indian Patent Application (Published) | 05-Dec-2020 | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Identify claim features $\mathbf{C} = \{$ANC, HRM$\}$.

**Step 2:** Check D1: $P_{D1} = \{$ANC$\}$. Is $\mathbf{C} \subseteq P_{D1}$? 

$$
\{\text{ANC, HRM}\} \not\subseteq \{\text{ANC}\} \quad \Rightarrow \text{D1 does NOT anticipate}
$$

**Step 3:** Check D2: $P_{D2} = \{$HRM$\}$. Is $\mathbf{C} \subseteq P_{D2}$?

$$
\{\text{ANC, HRM}\} \not\subseteq \{\text{HRM}\} \quad \Rightarrow \text{D2 does NOT anticipate}
$$

**Step 4:** Check D3: $P_{D3} = \{$ANC, HRM$\}$. Is $\mathbf{C} \subseteq P_{D3}$ AND is the date earlier?

$$
\{\text{ANC, HRM}\} \subseteq \{\text{ANC, HRM}\} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
D_{D3} = \text{05-Dec-2020} \leq D_c = \text{15-Mar-2022} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5:** Final Verdict:

$$
\boxed{\text{D3 anticipates the claim. The patent application lacks novelty.}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that **D1 + D2 combined** would have taught the invention, but they **cannot be combined** for the *novelty* test (single-reference rule). They may, however, be combined for the **inventive step (Section 2(1)(ja))** analysis — a different and easier test to clear.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Publication vs. Prior Claim (Comparison)

| Parameter | Anticipation by Publication (Sec 25(1)(d)) | Anticipation by Prior Claim (Sec 25(1)(e)) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of disclosure** | Public document / public use / public knowledge | A *claim* in a patent/patent application |
| **Patent grant required?** | No (any publication suffices) | No (a *complete specification filed in India* suffices) |
| **Geographic scope of publication** | Anywhere in the world (absolute novelty) | Anywhere in the world, but **must be filed as complete specification in India** for the claim |
| **Date tested** | Publication date | Priority date / filing date of the earlier complete specification |
| **Threshold** | Novelty destruction | Novelty destruction |
| **Can be combined with other grounds?** | Yes | Yes |
| **Typical use** | Opposition pre-grant (Sec 25(1)) and revocation (Sec 64) | Same |
| **Document needed in evidence** | Any published material | Certified copy of the earlier complete specification |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Anticipation Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prior Art Document Identified] --> B{Publication Date <= Priority Date of Application?}
    B -- No --> C[NOT Anticipated - Date Fails]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is Document Publicly Accessible?}
    D -- No --> E[NOT Anticipated - Confidential]
    D -- Yes --> F{Single Reference Discloses ALL Claim Features?}
    F -- No --> G[Move to Inventive Step Analysis]
    F -- Yes --> H{Enabling Disclosure to PSIA?}
    H -- No --> I[NOT Anticipated - Not Enabling]
    H -- Yes --> J[ANTICIPATED - Novelty Destroyed]
    J --> K[Apply to Sec 25 1 d or 25 1 e]
```

```mermaid
    style USER fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is meant by anticipation by prior publication under the Indian Patents Act, 1970?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Anticipation by prior publication refers to the situation where the novelty of an invention is destroyed because the invention was made available to the public through a document, publication, or other form of disclosure *before the date of filing* (or priority date) of the patent application. Under **Section 25(1)(d)** of the Patents Act, 1970, this is a valid ground for pre-grant opposition and revocation under **Section 64(1)(a)**. For a publication to anticipate, it must disclose every essential feature of the claimed invention (single-reference rule), be enabling, be publicly accessible, and have a date on or before the priority date. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Distinguish between anticipation by prior publication and anticipation by prior claim."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Prior Publication (Sec 25(1)(d)) | Prior Claim (Sec 25(1)(e)) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Any public disclosure (patent, paper, web) | A *claim* in an earlier patent/patent application |
| **Requirement** | Publicly accessible document | Complete specification filed in India (even if not granted) |
| **Date tested** | Publication date | Filing/priority date of the earlier claim |
| **Example** | An IEEE paper disclosing the invention | A claim in a prior granted Indian patent |

The key distinction is that prior claim operates on the *legal claim* itself, while prior publication operates on the *publicly available information*. *(3 marks)*

---

## 📝 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of anticipation by previous publication in detail. Discuss the conditions that must be satisfied for a publication to anticipate a claimed invention. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of suitable examples, describe the various types of publications that may be considered as anticipatory prior art under Indian patent law. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution – Question A**

#### **Part (a) – 7 Marks**

Anticipation by previous publication is the doctrine under which a patent claim is held to lack novelty because the same invention was disclosed to the public through a published document *before* the applicant's priority date. The statutory basis is **Section 25(1)(d)** of the Patents Act, 1970.

**Conditions for anticipation (7 marks distributed as follows):**

1. **Existence of a prior disclosure** *(1 mark)* – A document such as a patent, journal article, manual, or website must exist.

2. **Date condition** *(1 mark)* – The publication date must be on or before the priority/filing date of the application under examination.

3. **Public accessibility** *(1 mark)* – The document must have been accessible to the public, not confined to a confidential circle under NDA.

4. **Single-reference rule (strict novelty)** *(2 marks)* – Every essential feature of the claimed invention must be disclosed in *one single* prior art reference. Multiple references cannot be combined for novelty (though they may be combined for inventive step).

5. **Enabling disclosure** *(1 mark)* – The publication must enable the Person Skilled in the Art (PSIA) to perform the invention without undue burden (Monsanto v. Coramandal, 1986).

6. **Implicit / inherent disclosure** *(1 mark)* – Features not explicitly stated but necessarily inherent in the disclosure also count.

#### **Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**Types of publications considered as anticipatory prior art:**

| # | Type | Example | Anticipatory? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Indian/foreign granted patents** | US 10123456B2 | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | **Published patent applications** | Indian Application Publication in IPO Journal | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | **Scientific journal articles** | IEEE, Nature, Springer publications | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | **PhD/M.Tech theses** | University library deposited thesis | ✅ Yes |
| 5 | **Conference proceedings** | IEEE Conference paper | ✅ Yes |
| 6 | **Technical manuals / datasheets** | Product catalogues, user guides | ✅ Yes (if enabling) |
| 7 | **Online publications** | Research blogs, YouTube videos, websites | ✅ Yes (if date is verifiable) |
| 8 | **Books / textbooks** | Standard reference books | ✅ Yes |
| 9 | **Newspaper / press releases** | Reports of new technological breakthroughs | ✅ Yes (if technical disclosure is enabling) |

**Examples for explanation (4 marks):**
- *Example 1:* Publication of a research paper describing a new drug formulation in *Nature Medicine* in 2019 anticipates a patent application filed in 2021 for the same formulation.
- *Example 2:* A YouTube tutorial published in 2018 demonstrating a 3D-printing technique can anticipate a patent filed in 2022 if the technique is fully disclosed and enabling.

**Case law (1 mark):** *Bristol-Myers Squibb v. FDC Ltd.* – the Indian court held that even a product monograph distributed freely can constitute prior publication.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the doctrine of anticipation by prior claim under Section 25(1)(e) of the Indian Patents Act, 1970. How is it different from anticipation by prior publication? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the step-by-step methodology to conduct a prior art search for assessing anticipation. Mention the major patent and non-patent databases used by examiners. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution – Question B**

#### **Part (a) – 7 Marks**

**Doctrine of Anticipation by Prior Claim (Section 25(1)(e)):**

Section 25(1)(e) of the Patents Act, 1970 provides that a patent can be opposed or revoked if the invention was **claimed in a valid claim of another patent** or a **complete specification filed in India** having an **earlier priority date**. Unlike publication, prior claim does not require the public to have had actual access to the information — the *legal claim itself* is sufficient.

**Essential Conditions (4 marks):**
1. There must be a valid claim in an earlier patent or complete specification.
2. The complete specification must be filed in India (for prior claim under Indian law).
3. The priority date of the earlier claim must be earlier than the application under examination.
4. The claimed invention must be the *same* (or substantially identical) to the invention under examination.

**Distinction from Prior Publication (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Prior Publication | Prior Claim |
|---|---|---|
| **Disclosure type** | Public information | Legal claim |
| **Patent grant needed?** | No | No (complete specification filing suffices) |
| **Date considered** | Publication date | Filing/priority date |
| **Novelty test** | Discloses all features | Claims the same invention |
| **Effect** | Section 25(1)(d) | Section 25(1)(e) |

**Case law:** In *F. Hoffmann-La Roche v. Cipla* (2015), the Delhi High Court considered the role of prior claim in assessing novelty.

#### **Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**Step-by-Step Prior Art Search Methodology (4 marks):**

1. **Define the invention** – Identify all technical features and the field of the invention.
2. **Extract keywords** – Use synonyms, broader/narrower terms, and IPC/CPC classification codes.
3. **Formulate search strings** – Combine keywords using Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT).
4. **Search patent databases** – Query Indian and international patent databases.
5. **Search non-patent literature (NPL)** – Query scientific journals, conference proceedings, theses, and the web.
6. **Filter results by date** – Restrict to publications before the priority date.
7. **Analyze for novelty** – Apply the single-reference and enabling-disclosure tests.
8. **Document the search** – Prepare a novelty/anticipation report with classified references.

**Major Databases (3 marks):**

| # | Database | URL | Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IP India** | ipindiaonline.gov.in | Indian patents & applications |
| 2 | **WIPO PATENTSCOPE** | patentscope.wipo.int | International (PCT + national) |
| 3 | **USPTO** | uspto.gov | US patents |
| 4 | **EPO Espacenet** | worldwide.espacenet.com | 100+ countries |
| 5 | **Google Patents** | patents.google.com | Multi-jurisdictional, free |
| 6 | **IEEE Xplore** | ieee.org | Engineering NPL |
| 7 | **ScienceDirect** | sciencedirect.com | Scientific NPL |
| 8 | **Google Scholar** | scholar.google.com | Academic NPL |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Single-reference rule violation** – Combining two references (e.g., D1 + D2) to show novelty destruction. This is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing "publication" with "use"** – Public *use* is a separate ground (Sec 25(1)(c)); do not merge with prior publication (Sec 25(1)(d)).
> 3. **Forgetting the "enabling disclosure" test** – A mere mention is not enough; the disclosure must teach the PSIA to perform the invention.
> 4. **Mixing novelty with inventive step** – Anticipation is a *novelty* test, not an *inventive step* test. Inventive step uses the "person of ordinary skill" approach and allows combining references.
> 5. **Wrong statutory section** – Writing "Sec 25(1)(d)" for prior *claim* (it is Sec 25(1)(e)).
> 6. **Not verifying the date** – Always cross-verify the publication date with the priority date before concluding anticipation.
> 7. **Forgetting Indian jurisdiction requirement for prior claim** – Under Indian law, the earlier complete specification must be *filed in India* (this is jurisdiction-specific).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Anticipation** destroys the **novelty** of a patent claim.
- ✅ **Statutory basis (India):** **Section 25(1)(d)** for prior publication, **Section 25(1)(e)** for prior claim, **Section 64(1)(a)** for revocation.
- ✅ **Single-reference rule:** ALL features must be in ONE prior art document for novelty.
- ✅ **Enabling disclosure:** Prior art must teach the *Person Skilled in the Art* (PSIA) to perform the invention.
- ✅ **Date rule:** Publication date ≤ priority date for anticipation.
- ✅ **Public accessibility:** Confidential disclosures (under NDA) do NOT anticipate.
- ✅ **Grace period in India:** 12 months (Sec 31) for academic/政府 exhibition disclosures.
- ✅ **Prior claim** requires only an *earlier complete specification filed in India* — no grant needed.
- ✅ **Mosaicing** is allowed for *inventive step* but NOT for *novelty*.
- ✅ **Implicit/inherent disclosure** can also anticipate (Schering v. Zydus doctrine).
- ✅ **Search databases:** IP India, WIPO PATENTSCOPE, USPTO, EPO Espacenet, Google Patents (patent); IEEE, ScienceDirect, Google Scholar (non-patent).
- ✅ **Novelty vs. Inventive Step:** Novelty = single reference, must teach all features. Inventive Step = person of ordinary skill, can combine references.
- ✅ **Always remember the "SPEDS-C" mnemonic** for anticipation tests.
- ✅ **For KTU 14-mark answers:** Always structure as *Definition → Statutory basis → Conditions → Case law → Distinction with related doctrine → Examples → Conclusion*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controller and His Powers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Controller and His Powers

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) under the Indian Patents Act, 1970, the **Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** is the apex administrative authority appointed by the Central Government to oversee the functioning of the patent regime in India. The **Controller of Patents** is the statutory officer appointed under **Section 3(1)** of the Patents Act, 1970, who acts as the principal adjudicatory and administrative authority at the Patent Office.

The Controller is the *quasi-judicial* and *administrative head* of the Patent Office, vested with the power to grant patents, reject applications, hear oppositions, rectify the Register of Patents, and exercise supervisory jurisdiction over Deputy Controllers and Assistant Controllers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Controller" specifically refers to the **Controller of Patents** under the Patents Act, 1970 (and analogous officers under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, and Designs Act, 2000). For Module 2, the focus is squarely on the **Controller of Patents** as defined under **Section 2(1)(b)** and appointed under **Section 3** of the Patents Act, 1970.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **Patent Office** as a massive, highly specialised *court of justice* for inventions. The Controller is like the **Chief Justice** of this specialised court.

- **Patent Applicants** are like *plaintiffs* bringing their invention for registration.
- **Opponents** are like *defendants* challenging a patent.
- The **Deputy Controllers / Assistant Controllers** are like *subordinate judges* who handle preliminary or delegated matters.
- The **Appellate Board (IPAB → now Commercial Courts/Circuit Benches)** acts as the *appellate court* above the Controller.

Just as a Chief Justice has powers to *admit cases, hear them, pass orders, summon parties, and even rectify mistakes in records*, the Controller of Patents exercises parallel powers over patent applications, oppositions, and the Register of Patents.

## 1.3 Key Statutory Anchors

| Statutory Provision | Subject Matter |
|---|---|
| **Section 2(1)(b)** | Definition of "Controller" |
| **Section 3(1)** | Appointment of Controller |
| **Section 3(2)** | Appointment of Additional, Deputy, Assistant Controllers |
| **Section 7** | Power of Controller to amend applications |
| **Section 14** | Publication of application |
| **Section 15** | Examination of application |
| **Section 16** | Power of Controller to refuse or require amendment |
| **Section 20** | General powers of Controller in proceedings |
| **Section 25** | Opposition to grant of patent |
| **Section 43** | Rights of patentees |
| **Section 71** | Power of Controller to give directions to co-owners |
| **Section 73** | Determination of question of validity and infringement |
| **Section 78** | Power of Controller to rectify the Register of Patents |

## 1.4 The Controller — At a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Headquarters:** Mumbai (Patent Office, Boudhik Sampada Bhavan, S.M. Road, Antop Hill)
> **Subordinate Offices:** Delhi, Chennai, Kolkata
> **Constituted Under:** Patents Act, 1970 (as amended by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005)
> **Appointing Authority:** Central Government (Department of Industrial Policy & Promotion, Ministry of Commerce and Industry)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Appointment of the Controller (Section 3)

The Central Government appoints the **Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks**, who discharges the functions of the **Controller of Patents** under the Act. The Central Government may also appoint:
- One or more **Additional Controllers of Patents**
- One or more **Deputy Controllers of Patents**
- One or more **Assistant Controllers of Patents**

All such officers discharge their functions under the **general superintendence, direction, and control of the Controller General**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Officers (Top to Bottom):**
> 1. **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)**
> 2. **Controller of Patents** (Head of each Patent Office)
> 3. **Additional Controller of Patents**
> 4. **Deputy Controller of Patents**
> 5. **Assistant Controller of Patents**

## 2.2 Statutory Powers of the Controller — Detailed Analysis

The powers of the Controller can be broadly classified into **six functional categories**:

### (A) Administrative Powers

| Power | Statutory Basis | Description |
|---|---|---|
| General superintendence over Patent Office | Section 3(2) | Controller exercises administrative control over all subordinate officers |
| Power to receive and acknowledge applications | Section 5, 6 | All patent applications are filed before the Controller |
| Maintenance of the Register of Patents | Section 67 | Controller maintains the Register of Patents in the prescribed manner |
| Power to publish applications | Section 11A, 14 | Publication of application after 18 months from date of filing/priority |

### (B) Discretionary Powers (Pre-Grant Stage)

| Power | Statutory Basis |
|---|---|
| Power to require amendment of application | Section 15, 16, 17, 18 |
| Power to refuse application | Section 15(2) |
| Power to permit division of application | Section 16(3) |
| Power to allow conversion of application | Section 18(1) |
| Power to extend time limits | Section 53(2) — Condonation of delay (now via proviso to Section 5(2) for priority) |

### (C) Quasi-Judicial Powers (Opposition & Revocation)

The Controller exercises extensive **quasi-judicial powers** while deciding:
- **Oppositions** to the grant of patents — *Pre-grant* (Section 25(1)) and *Post-grant* (Section 25(2))
- **Revocation** of patents (Section 64)
- **Rectification** of the Register of Patents (Section 78)
- **Surrender** of patents (Section 63)

> [!NOTE]
> These proceedings are *adversarial* in nature. The Controller must observe the principles of **natural justice** — *audi alteram partem* (hear the other side) and *nemo judex in causa sua* (no one should be a judge in their own cause).

### (D) Powers of a Civil Court (Section 73)

This is the most powerful judicial power vested in the Controller. Under **Section 73(2)** of the Patents Act, 1970, the Controller has, for the purposes of any proceeding before him, the **same powers as are vested in a Civil Court** under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, while trying a suit, in respect of the following matters:

1. **Summoning and enforcing the attendance of any person** and examining him on oath.
2. **Requiring the discovery and production** of any document.
3. **Receiving evidence** on affidavits.
4. **Issuing commissions** for the examination of witnesses or documents.
5. **Awarding costs** (subject to Rules).
6. **Reviewing its own decisions** and applications for review.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Controller is NOT a Civil Court per se, but exercises Civil Court-like powers. His orders are enforceable as if they were decrees of a Civil Court. Appeals lie to the **High Court** (Section 71, prior to IPAB abolition) or the **Commercial Court/High Court** (post-IPAB dissolution w.e.f. 20.04.2021 via the Tribunal Reforms Ordinance/Act, 2021).

### (E) Power to Rectify the Register (Section 78)

The Controller has the power to:
- **Cancel the registration** of a patent.
- **Correct any clerical error** in the Register.
- **Enter in the Register** any change in the ownership, name, or address of the patentee.
- **Strike out** any entry wrongly made.

This power is exercisable either on a **rectification application** filed by any person interested (Section 78(1)), or **suo motu** in certain circumstances, or on the application of the patentee.

### (F) Power Regarding Co-Owners (Section 71)

Where two or more persons are registered as joint patentees, the Controller may, on the application of any of them, give such directions regarding the division of the patent rights and the manner in which the patent shall be worked as the Controller thinks fit.

## 2.3 Delegation of Powers (Section 20)

The Controller may, by general or special order, delegate to a **Deputy Controller or Assistant Controller** any of his powers under the Act, **except the power of hearing oppositions and rectification proceedings under Sections 25, 64, and 78**, which must be heard by the Controller personally or by an officer not below the rank of an **Additional Controller**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Statutory Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "formula sheet" for a legal topic is the **Statutory Power Matrix** — the precise legal-procedural map linking every power to its enabling provision.

| **Power** | **Section** | **Nature** | **Delegable?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Receive and examine applications | 5, 6, 12, 15 | Administrative | ✅ Yes |
| Require amendments | 15, 16, 17, 18 | Discretionary | ✅ Yes |
| Refuse application | 15(2) | Discretionary | ✅ Yes |
| Hear pre-grant opposition | 25(1) | Quasi-Judicial | ⚠️ Only to Additional Controller |
| Hear post-grant opposition | 25(2) | Quasi-Judicial | ⚠️ Only to Additional Controller |
| Revocation of patent | 64 | Quasi-Judicial | ⚠️ Only to Additional Controller |
| Civil Court powers | 73(2) | Judicial | ⚠️ Only to Additional Controller |
| Rectification of Register | 78 | Quasi-Judicial | ⚠️ Only to Additional Controller |
| General superintendence | 3(2) | Administrative | ❌ No |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

- For an engineer who has developed a novel technology, the **Controller's office is the gateway** to secure patent protection.
- The **Controller's quasi-judicial orders** are directly enforceable, ensuring that inventors do not need to approach Civil Courts for every dispute.
- The **section 73 power** ensures the Controller can compel the production of evidence, making patent litigation efficient and cost-effective.
- The **rectification power** ensures the Register of Patents remains clean and reflective of true ownership, which is critical for licensing, technology transfer, and M&A due diligence in engineering industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Logical Derivation: How the Controller's Powers Are Derived from Statute

Unlike mathematical topics, the "derivation" here traces the **legal-procedural logic chain** from the Constitution of India → Central Government → Statute → Controller → Delegated Authority. This is the **KTU-expected analytical chain** in examination answers.

### Step 1: Constitutional Foundation

> The **Constitution of India** under **Entry 49 of List I (Union List)** of the Seventh Schedule empowers the Central (Union) Government to legislate on *"Patents, inventions and designs; copyright, trade-marks, merchandise marks"*.

**Logic:** Only the Central Government can create a statutory authority to administer patent law.

### Step 2: Statutory Enablement

> The Central Government, in exercise of this constitutional power, enacted the **Patents Act, 1970**.

**Logic:** The Act is the *parent statute* that creates the office of the Controller and vests it with powers.

### Step 3: Creation of the Office

> **Section 3(1)** of the Patents Act, 1970 states: *"The Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint a person to be the Controller of Patents..."*

**Logic:** The Controller's office owes its existence *entirely* to statutory notification.

### Step 4: Statutory Function Allocation

> **Section 3(2)** allows the appointment of Additional, Deputy, and Assistant Controllers who discharge functions under the Controller's **general superintendence and control**.

**Logic:** The Controller is the *administrative head* of the Patent Office hierarchy.

### Step 5: Conferral of Specific Powers

> The Act then confers specific powers through various sections:
>
> $$\text{Power}_{\text{Controller}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$
>
> where each $P_i$ is a power conferred by a specific section (e.g., $P_{15}$ = power under Section 15, $P_{25}$ = power under Section 25, ..., $P_{78}$ = power under Section 78).

### Step 6: Civil Court-Like Empowerment

> **Section 73(2)** upgrades the Controller from a mere administrative officer to a **quasi-judicial authority** by vesting in him powers of a Civil Court under the CPC, 1908.

**Logic:** Without Section 73, the Controller could not enforce attendance, compel documents, or award costs. This section makes patent adjudication meaningful.

### Step 7: Delegation Logic (Section 20)

> The Controller may delegate powers, **subject to a statutory exception**:
>
> $$\text{Power}_{\text{Delegable}} = \text{Power}_{\text{Total}} - \{P_{25(1)}, P_{25(2)}, P_{64}, P_{78}\}$$

**Logic:** Quasi-judicial functions involving contested rights *cannot* be delegated to lower-ranked officers to ensure quality of adjudication.

### Step 8: Appellate Oversight

> Orders of the Controller are appealable to:
> - The **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** under the Patents Act, 1970 (pre-2021).
> - The **High Court / Commercial Court** post the **Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021** which dissolved the IPAB.

**Logic:** This ensures *hierarchical judicial review* and prevents arbitrariness.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Powers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix compares the **Controller's powers** with similar statutory officers to help students in KTU 2024 Scheme exams draw high-quality comparative answers.

| **Parameter** | **Controller of Patents** | **Registrar of Trade Marks** | **Controller of Designs** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statute** | Patents Act, 1970 | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Designs Act, 2000 |
| **Appointed Under** | Section 3(1) | Section 3(1) | Section 3(1) |
| **Headquarters** | Mumbai (with branches in Delhi, Chennai, Kolkata) | Mumbai | Kolkata |
| **Civil Court Powers** | Section 73 | Section 91 | Section 19 |
| **Opposition Power** | Yes — Section 25 | Yes — Sections 9, 21 | Yes — Section 25 of Designs Act |
| **Rectification Power** | Section 78 | Section 47 | Section 27 of Designs Act |
| **Appellate Authority (Post-IPAB)** | Commercial Court/High Court | Commercial Court/High Court | Commercial Court/High Court |

## 3.3 Power-Source Mapping (Detailed Step-by-Step)

Let us derive, step-by-step, the *complete statutory power chain* for one specific function — the **hearing of a post-grant opposition** — as a worked example.

**Function:** Hearing a Post-Grant Opposition to a Patent

$$\text{Trigger} \xrightarrow{\text{Sec. 25(2)}} \text{Representation by "person interested"} \xrightarrow{\text{Sec. 25(3)}} \text{Notice to patentee}$$

$$\text{Patentee's response} \xrightarrow{\text{Sec. 25(3)}} \text{Controller constitutes hearing}$$

$$\text{Hearing by Controller} \xrightarrow{\text{Sec. 73(2)}} \text{Powers of Civil Court invoked}$$

$$\text{Decision} \xrightarrow{\text{Sec. 25(4)}} \text{Order to grant, refuse, or amend claims}$$

$$\text{Appeal} \xrightarrow{\text{Sec. 71(1) read with Sec. 116}} \text{High Court / Commercial Court}$$

**Explanatory Logic of Each Step:**

1. **Trigger:** Any person interested may file a representation in the prescribed form.
2. **Statutory Foundation:** Section 25(2) empowers the filing of post-grant opposition on specific grounds (Section 25(1) grounds incorporated by reference).
3. **Notice:** The Controller issues notice to the patentee to ensure the *principle of natural justice* (audi alteram partem).
4. **Patentee's Response:** The patentee files a counter-statement within the prescribed time.
5. **Constitution of Hearing:** The Controller fixes a date for hearing, summoning both parties.
6. **Civil Court Powers:** At the hearing, the Controller may use Section 73(2) powers — summon witnesses, require documents, etc.
7. **Decision:** The Controller records evidence, hears arguments, and passes a reasoned order.
8. **Appeal:** An aggrieved party may appeal under Section 71 to the Commercial Court/High Court.

> [!NOTE]
> This **eight-step power chain** is the most-tested KTU examination structure for any procedural IP law question.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, PESFT757]**
**"Define the term 'Controller' under the Patents Act, 1970. Who appoints the Controller?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

As per **Section 2(1)(b)** of the Patents Act, 1970, the *"Controller"* means the Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks appointed under **sub-section (1) of Section 3** of the Act. The Controller is appointed by the **Central Government** by notification in the Official Gazette. The Central Government may also appoint Additional, Deputy, and Assistant Controllers under Section 3(2), who discharge their functions under the general superintendence, direction, and control of the Controller General.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition — 1 Mark; Appointing Authority — 1 Mark; Subordinate Officers — 1 Mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, PESFT757]**
**"State the powers of the Controller as a Civil Court under Section 73(2) of the Patents Act, 1970."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Under **Section 73(2)** of the Patents Act, 1970, the Controller has, for the purposes of any proceedings before him, the **same powers as are vested in a Civil Court** under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, while trying a suit, in respect of the following matters:
1. **Summoning and enforcing the attendance** of any person and examining him on oath.
2. **Requiring the discovery and production** of any document.
3. **Receiving evidence** on affidavits.
4. **Issuing commissions** for the examination of witnesses or documents.
5. **Awarding costs** (subject to Rules).

**Mark Distribution:** Section citation — 1 Mark; Any 4 powers — 2 Marks (0.5 each).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Question, PESFT757]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the appointment, hierarchy, and statutory powers of the Controller of Patents under the Patents Act, 1970. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Appointment:** Under **Section 3(1)** of the Patents Act, 1970, the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint a person to be the Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks, who discharges the functions of the Controller of Patents.

**Hierarchy (Section 3(2)):**
1. Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)
2. Controller of Patents
3. Additional Controller of Patents
4. Deputy Controller of Patents
5. Assistant Controller of Patents

All officers discharge functions under the general superintendence and control of the Controller General.

**Statutory Powers — Classified as follows:**

**(i) Administrative Powers:** Receiving applications (Section 5, 6), maintaining the Register of Patents (Section 67), publishing applications (Section 11A, 14).

**(ii) Discretionary Powers:** Requiring amendments (Sections 15, 16, 17, 18), refusing applications (Section 15(2)), permitting division (Section 16(3)), allowing conversion (Section 18(1)).

**(iii) Quasi-Judicial Powers:** Hearing pre-grant oppositions (Section 25(1)), post-grant oppositions (Section 25(2)), revocation (Section 64), surrender (Section 63), rectification (Section 78).

**(iv) Powers of a Civil Court (Section 73(2)):** Summoning witnesses, compelling documents, receiving affidavits, issuing commissions, awarding costs.

**(v) Rectification Power (Section 78):** Correcting errors, cancelling registrations, entering changes in ownership, striking out wrong entries.

**Mark Distribution:** Appointment — 1 Mark; Hierarchy — 1 Mark; Administrative & Discretionary Powers — 2 Marks; Quasi-Judicial & Civil Court Powers — 2 Marks; Rectification & Conclusion — 1 Mark.

---

**(b) Explain the power of delegation of the Controller under Section 20 of the Patents Act, 1970. What are the non-delegable powers? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Delegation under Section 20:** Section 20 of the Patents Act, 1970 empowers the Controller to delegate, by **general or special order in writing**, any of his powers under the Act to a Deputy Controller or Assistant Controller, **subject to statutory exceptions**.

**Delegable Powers:**
- All **administrative powers** — e.g., receiving applications, acknowledging them, requesting amendments, etc.
- All **discretionary powers** relating to examination — e.g., issuing First Examination Report (FER) under Section 14, requiring amendments under Section 15, etc.
- Routine **procedural matters** — e.g., extending time limits where permissible.

**Non-Delegable Powers:** The following powers **cannot** be delegated to a Deputy or Assistant Controller and must be exercised by the Controller or an officer not below the rank of **Additional Controller**:
1. Power to hear **pre-grant oppositions** under **Section 25(1)**.
2. Power to hear **post-grant oppositions** under **Section 25(2)**.
3. Power of **revocation of patents** under **Section 64**.
4. Power of **rectification of the Register** under **Section 78**.

**Rationale for Non-Delegation:** These powers involve the **adjudication of contested rights** between rival parties, where the principles of natural justice (audi alteram partem) must be strictly observed. The legislature, therefore, ensured that such quasi-judicial functions are discharged only by senior officers of sufficient rank and experience to ensure quality adjudication.

**Mark Distribution:** Section 20 — 1 Mark; Delegable Powers — 2 Marks; Non-Delegable Powers — 3 Marks; Rationale — 1 Mark.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Model Question, PESFT757]**

**(a) Critically analyse the quasi-judicial powers of the Controller of Patents with reference to opposition and revocation proceedings. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Quasi-Judicial Nature:** The Controller, while deciding opposition and revocation proceedings, exercises **quasi-judicial powers** — i.e., powers that are judicial in nature but exercised by an administrative authority. This is established by the fact that:
- The Controller must give **notice** to the parties.
- The Controller must give an opportunity to be **heard**.
- The Controller must record **reasons** for the order.
- The Controller's orders are subject to **appellate review**.

**Opposition Proceedings (Section 25):**

**Pre-Grant Opposition (Section 25(1)):** Any person may file a representation in writing opposing the grant of a patent on the grounds specified in **Section 25(1)(a) to (k)**, including lack of novelty, inventive step, industrial applicability, non-disclosure, wrongful obtaining, public knowledge, traditional knowledge, and anti-genetic variation. The Controller considers the representation along with the application and the applicant's response.

**Post-Grant Opposition (Section 25(2)):** Within **one year** from the date of publication of the grant of patent, any person interested may file a notice of opposition on the same grounds as Section 25(1). The Controller constitutes a hearing, gives both parties an opportunity to be heard, and passes a **reasoned order** within the prescribed time.

**Revocation Proceedings (Section 64):**
- Revocation may be sought by any person interested, the Central Government, or the patentee himself (for partial surrender).
- Grounds for revocation are detailed in **Section 64(1)(a) to (n)**.
- The Controller decides the revocation application after a full adversarial hearing.

**Critical Analysis:**
- ✅ The Controller's quasi-judicial powers ensure **specialised adjudication** of patent disputes without burdening the regular Civil Courts.
- ✅ The power under **Section 73(2)** to summon witnesses and compel documents ensures the proceedings are effective.
- ⚠️ **Criticism:** The Controller is *not* a full-fledged court, and appeals to Commercial Courts often involve a *re-examination* of evidence, leading to delays.
- ⚠️ **Criticism:** Post the dissolution of IPAB in 2021, the appeals now lie to Commercial Courts, which may lack the *technical expertise* of the specialised IPAB.

**Mark Distribution:** Quasi-judicial nature — 1 Mark; Section 25 opposition — 2 Marks; Section 64 revocation — 2 Marks; Critical analysis — 2 Marks.

---

**(b) "The Controller is not a Civil Court, but exercises Civil Court-like powers." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to Section 73 of the Patents Act, 1970. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Statement Analysis:** The statement highlights the **dual character** of the Controller — he is administratively a *government officer* but functionally exercises powers akin to a *judicial authority*.

**Statutory Foundation — Section 73:**

**Section 73(1):** Where any dispute arises — (a) between the patentee and a co-patentee, or (b) between the patentee and a person interested in a contract — regarding the construction, scope, validity, or infringement of a patent, the Controller may determine the question raised.

**Section 73(2):** Without prejudice to any other jurisdiction, the Controller has, for the purposes of any proceedings before him, the **same powers as are vested in a Civil Court** under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, in respect of the following:
1. Summoning and enforcing attendance of any person and examining him on oath.
2. Requiring discovery and production of any document.
3. Receiving evidence on affidavits.
4. Issuing commissions for the examination of witnesses or documents.
5. Awarding costs (subject to Rules).

**Critical Evaluation:**

**✅ Why the Statement is Correct:**
- The Controller can compel the attendance of witnesses — a *judicial power*.
- The Controller can require production of documents — a *judicial power*.
- The Controller can receive evidence on affidavits — a *judicial power*.
- The Controller's orders are *enforceable* as if they were decrees of a Civil Court.

**❌ Why the Statement is Also True (in a Limiting Sense):**
- The Controller is **not** a Civil Court — he is a **statutory tribunal**.
- He is **not** bound by the strict rules of the **Indian Evidence Act, 1872** to the same extent as a Civil Court.
- He **cannot** try all civil disputes — only those enumerated in **Section 73(1)**.
- His orders are **appealable** to the Commercial Court / High Court, unlike decrees of a Civil Court which have higher finality.

**Conclusion:** The statement is *partially correct*. The Controller is a *sui generis* authority — neither purely administrative nor fully judicial — and is best described as a **"Tribunal with Civil Court-like powers for limited purposes."**

**Mark Distribution:** Statement analysis — 1 Mark; Section 73(1) and 73(2) — 2 Marks; Civil Court-like powers — 2 Marks; Limitations — 1 Mark; Conclusion — 1 Mark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Controller General" with "Controller of Patents":** Many students use these terms interchangeably. The Controller General is the *overall* head of patents, designs, and trade marks; the Controller of Patents is the *jurisdictional* head of a specific Patent Office. (Penalty: 0.5–1 Mark)
>
> 2. **Omitting Section Numbers:** Quoting "the Controller can rectify the register" without citing **Section 78** is a fatal flaw. KTU examiners expect **statutory precision**. (Penalty: 1 Mark per omission)
>
> 3. **Misstating Delegation Rules:** Writing that the Controller can delegate *all* powers to a Deputy Controller is incorrect. Quasi-judicial powers can be delegated **only to Additional Controllers**, not Deputy or Assistant Controllers. (Penalty: 1–2 Marks)
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Post-IPAB Change:** Many students still write "appeal lies to the Intellectual Property Appellate Board" — this is *outdated* post the **Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021**. The correct appellate authority is now the **Commercial Court / High Court**. (Penalty: 1 Mark)
>
> 5. **Confusing Section 25(1) and 25(2):** Section 25(1) is *pre-grant* opposition (any person); Section 25(2) is *post-grant* opposition (person interested, within 1 year of publication of grant). Mixing these up is a common error. (Penalty: 1 Mark)
>
> 6. **Using "trademark" and "patent" interchangeably:** They are governed by *different statutes* (Trade Marks Act, 1999 vs Patents Act, 1970). Examiners mark strictly. (Penalty: 1 Mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 2**

- ✅ **Controller** is defined under **Section 2(1)(b)** and appointed under **Section 3(1)** of the Patents Act, 1970.
- ✅ The **Central Government** appoints the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM).
- ✅ The hierarchy is: **CGPDTM → Controller of Patents → Additional Controller → Deputy Controller → Assistant Controller**.
- ✅ The Controller is a **statutory authority** with **administrative, discretionary, quasi-judicial, and Civil Court-like powers**.
- ✅ **Section 73(2)** vests the Controller with the powers of a Civil Court — summoning witnesses, compelling documents, receiving affidavits, issuing commissions, awarding costs.
- ✅ **Section 25(1)** = Pre-grant opposition (any person, before grant).
- ✅ **Section 25(2)** = Post-grant opposition (person interested, within **1 year** of grant publication).
- ✅ **Section 64** = Revocation of patent (Central Government, person interested, or patentee himself).
- ✅ **Section 78** = Rectification of the Register of Patents.
- ✅ **Section 20** = Delegation of powers. Administrative powers are fully delegable; quasi-judicial powers (Sections 25, 64, 78) are delegable **only to Additional Controllers**, not to Deputy or Assistant Controllers.
- ✅ **Section 71** = Power of Controller to give directions to co-owners of patents.
- ✅ Appeals from Controller's orders now lie to the **Commercial Court / High Court** (post the **Tribunal Reforms Act, 2021**, which dissolved the IPAB).
- ✅ The Controller must observe **principles of natural justice** — *audi alteram partem* and *nemo judex in causa sua*.
- ✅ The Controller is **not bound by the strict rules of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872**, but exercises powers similar to a Civil Court under the CPC, 1908.
- ✅ The Patent Office is headquartered in **Mumbai** with branch offices in **Delhi, Chennai, and Kolkata**.
- ✅ Always cite **Section numbers** in KTU answers — statutory precision is the **single biggest differentiator** in valuation.

---
