<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Opposition" in Intellectual Property Rights?

In the context of Intellectual Property Law, **Opposition** is a statutory administrative/quasi-judicial proceeding through which a third party (the "Opponent") formally challenges the grant or registration of an Intellectual Property right before it is finally granted by the competent authority. It is a built-in safeguard mechanism embedded within the IP registration system to ensure that only legally valid IP rights are conferred upon applicants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Opposition is a legal proceeding initiated by a third party (an "opponent") against an application for a patent, trademark, or design before its registration, on specific statutory grounds, to prevent the grant of the IP right or to seek its revocation/cancellation.*

Opposition primarily applies to two major forms of IP in Indian jurisprudence:
1. **Patent Opposition** — governed by the **Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2005)
2. **Trademark Opposition** — governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**

Design opposition and Geographical Indication opposition also exist but are typically examined in later modules.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The "Neighbourhood Objection" System**

Imagine you apply for permission to build a 10-storey commercial building in a quiet residential colony. The municipal corporation publishes your application in the local newspaper for **public notice**. Your neighbours now have a window of time to file **objections** if your building violates zoning rules, blocks their sunlight, or compromises safety.

The municipal authority then holds a hearing — listens to both sides — and decides whether to grant the permission, modify it, or reject it.

**IP Opposition works EXACTLY the same way:**
- The **"building application"** = Patent/Trademark application filed at the IP Office.
- The **"public notice"** = Publication in the **Patent Office Journal** or **Trademark Journal**.
- The **"neighbours objecting"** = Any third party filing an **opposition**.
- The **"municipal hearing"** = The **Hearing Officer/Controller** deciding the matter.
- The **"final approval"** = The **Grant of Patent** or **Registration of Trademark**.

This mechanism prevents monopolies from being granted unfairly and protects **public interest**.

---

## 1.3 Two Principal Categories of Opposition in Indian IP Law

| Category | Governing Statute | Where Covered | Filed Before |
|----------|-------------------|---------------|--------------|
| **Patent Opposition** | Patents Act, 1970 (Sections 25(1) & 25(2)) | Pre-grant & Post-grant | Controller of Patents |
| **Trademark Opposition** | Trade Marks Act, 1999 (Sections 21 & 57) | Pre-registration & Rectification | Registrar of Trademarks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** For Module 2, focus is dominantly on **Patent Opposition (Pre-grant and Post-grant)** under the **Patents Act, 1970**, supplemented by Trademark opposition under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**.

---

## 1.4 Why Opposition Matters — Policy Rationale

1. **Checks and Balances:** Prevents arbitrary or erroneous grants.
2. **Public Participation:** Allows any "person interested" to participate in the IP ecosystem.
3. **Quality Assurance:** Enhances the validity and credibility of the IP register.
4. **Avoids Litigation:** Provides a cheaper administrative alternative to courtroom battles.
5. **TRIPS Compliance:** Aligns India with Article 62 of TRIPS, which mandates publication of IP applications before grant.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Patent Opposition Under the Patents Act, 1970

The Indian Patents Act, 1970 (as amended by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005) provides **two distinct windows** for opposing a patent application:

### A. Pre-Grant Opposition — Section 25(1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Grant Opposition** can be filed **ANYTIME AFTER PUBLICATION** of the application in the **Patent Office Journal** and **BEFORE THE GRANT OF PATENT.**

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| **Statutory Provision** | **Section 25(1)** of the Patents Act, 1970 |
| **Who can file?** | **Any person** (no requirement to be "person interested") — this is a wider standing than post-grant |
| **When to file?** | After publication of application; before grant of patent |
| **Where to file?** | Before the **Controller of Patents** |
| **Form** | **Form 7A** |
| **Fee** | ₹1,600 (for natural person) / ₹8,000 (for other than natural person) — *subject to revision by IPO* |
| **Nature** | **Substantive opposition** — based on merit of the patent |
| **Decided by** | **Controller of Patents** (an administrative authority) |

#### Statutory Grounds for Pre-Grant Opposition [Section 25(1)(a)–(k)]

The opponent can challenge the patent on any of the following **eleven grounds**:

1. Wrongfully obtained the invention.
2. Prior publication in India (anticipation).
3. Prior publication outside India (anticipation).
4. Prior knowledge / public use in India.
5. Obviousness — lack of inventive step.
6. Not a patentable invention (Section 3 — non-patentable subject matter, e.g., atomic energy, frivolous inventions, contrary to public order/morality, mere discoveries, methods of agriculture/horticulture, treatment of animals/humans, business methods, computer programs per se, traditional knowledge, aggregation of features).
7. Insufficient disclosure — invention not sufficiently / clearly / completely described.
8. Claims not fairly based on the disclosure.
9. Failure to disclose information (especially foreign applications).
10. Claims not clear / concise.
11. Failure to deposit biological material (where required).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** Pre-grant opposition is **not maintainable if the patent has already been granted.** Hence speed is of essence.

---

### B. Post-Grant Opposition — Section 25(2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-Grant Opposition** can be filed **WITHIN 12 MONTHS FROM THE DATE OF PUBLICATION OF GRANT** in the Patent Office Journal.

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| **Statutory Provision** | **Section 25(2)** of the Patents Act, 1970 |
| **Who can file?** | **"Person interested"** OR **any person interested in the matter** (narrower standing than pre-grant) |
| **When to file?** | Within **12 months** of grant publication |
| **Where to file?** | Before the **Controller of Patents** |
| **Form** | **Form 7B** |
| **Fee** | ₹8,000 (natural person) / ₹40,000 (other than natural person) — *subject to revision* |
| **Nature** | Substantive opposition + procedural safeguards including **review by Opposition Board** |
| **Decided by** | Initially by **Opposition Board**, then **Controller of Patents** |

#### Statutory Grounds for Post-Grant Opposition [Section 25(2)(a)–(g)]

The opponent may challenge the patent on the following grounds:

1. Wrongful obtainment.
2. Prior publication (India or abroad).
3. Prior public use / knowledge in India.
4. Lack of inventive step.
5. Non-patentable subject matter (Section 3).
6. Insufficient disclosure.
7. Claims not fairly based on the disclosure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference:** Post-grant opposition has only **7 grounds**, whereas pre-grant has **11 grounds**.

---

### C. Opposition Board (For Post-Grant Opposition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constituted under Section 25(3):** When a post-grant opposition is filed, the Controller refers the matter to an **Opposition Board** consisting of **3 members** (one of whom is the Chairman, who is the Controller or a senior officer).

**Functions:**
- Reviews the opposition.
- Provides **recommendations** to the Controller.
- The Controller is **not bound** by the recommendations (Section 25(4)).

---

## 2.2 Trademark Opposition Under The Trade Marks Act, 1999

### Pre-Registration Opposition — Section 21

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, opposition occurs **AFTER ACCEPTANCE of the application but BEFORE REGISTRATION.**

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| **Statutory Provision** | **Section 21(1)** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999 |
| **Procedure Trigger** | Application accepted → advertised in the **Trademark Journal** → 4-month window opens |
| **Who can file?** | **Any person** — no standing restriction |
| **When to file?** | **Within 4 months** (extendable by 1 month on request — Rule 47) from the date of advertisement in the TM Journal |
| **Where to file?** | Before the **Registrar of Trademarks** |
| **Form** | **TM-5** |
| **Fee** | ₹2,700 (online) — *subject to revision* |

#### Statutory Grounds for Trademark Opposition [Section 19(1) / 11 absolute grounds + Section 11 relative grounds]

**Absolute Grounds (Section 9):**
- Mark devoid of distinctive character.
- Mark consisting exclusively of descriptive matter.
- Mark customary in trade.
- Mark likely to deceive / cause confusion.
- Mark contrary to law / morality.
- Mark contains scandalous / obscene matter.

**Relative Grounds (Section 11):**
- Identity/similarity to an earlier trademark.
- Identity/similarity of goods/services.
- Likelihood of confusion among public.
- Use of well-known trademark.
- Dilution of distinctive character.
- Unfair advantage of earlier trademark's reputation.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Comparative Cheat Sheet

| Feature | Pre-Grant Patent Opposition | Post-Grant Patent Opposition | Trademark Opposition |
|---------|----------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Section** | **25(1)** | **25(2)** | **21** |
| **Act** | Patents Act, 1970 | Patents Act, 1970 | Trade Marks Act, 1999 |
| **Form** | **Form 7A** | **Form 7B** | **TM-5** |
| **Standing** | **Any person** | **Person interested** | **Any person** |
| **Time Limit** | Publication → Before Grant | **12 months from grant publication** | **4 months from TM Journal publication** |
| **Authority** | Controller of Patents | Opposition Board → Controller | Registrar of Trademarks |
| **No. of Grounds** | **11 grounds** | **7 grounds** | Absolute + Relative grounds |
| **Fee (approx.)** | ₹1,600 / ₹8,000 | ₹8,000 / ₹40,000 | ₹2,700 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Engineers should care about Opposition:**
> - **Freedom-to-Operate (FTO):** A pending or granted patent may block your R&D; opposition (or monitoring) is a strategic tool.
> - **Defensive Strategy:** File oppositions against blocking patents of competitors.
> - **Academic Research:** Patents on biotechnology, software, AI/ML, pharmaceuticals are routinely opposed.
> - **Startups & SMEs:** Cost-effective tool to prevent large entities from monopolising technology.
> - **Strategic IP Management:** Companies like **Novartis (Gleevec case)**, **Natco (Pharma)**, and **Apple vs Samsung** showcase opposition strategies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedural Flow — Pre-Grant Patent Opposition (Section 25(1))

Below is the exhaustive procedural framework as practiced before the **Indian Patent Office (IPO)**:

### **STEP 1 — Filing of Patent Application**

The applicant files a patent application (Form 1 + Form 2 + Form 3, etc.) at the appropriate Patent Office (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata).

$$
\text{Application} \xrightarrow{\text{Form 1, 2, 3, 5, etc.}} \text{Patent Office}
$$

**Logic:** The patent application is the genesis of the entire opposition process. Without an application, no publication; without publication, no opposition.

---

### **STEP 2 — Examination Request**

The applicant (or any interested person) must file a **Request for Examination (Form 18)** within **48 months** of the priority date. The Patent Office then conducts substantive examination.

---

### **STEP 3 — Publication in Patent Office Journal (Section 11A)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trigger Event for Pre-Grant Opposition:** Once the application is published (either 18 months after filing/priority date OR earlier on request via Form 9), it appears in the **Patent Office Journal** under Section 11A.

$$
\text{Publication Date} = \max\{\text{Filing Date} + 18 \text{ months}, \text{Earlier Request via Form 9}\}
$$

From this date onward, **pre-grant opposition is maintainable.**

---

### **STEP 4 — Filing of Pre-Grant Opposition (Form 7A)**

Any person files **Form 7A** with:
- Statement of Opposition.
- Evidence in support (Affidavits, exhibits).
- Copy of patent application being opposed.
- Payment of prescribed fee.

**Time Window:**
$$
\text{Opposition Window} = [\text{Date of Publication}, \text{Date of Grant}]
$$

---

### **STEP 5 — Preliminary Scrutiny by Controller**

The Controller examines whether:
- The opposition is in proper Form 7A.
- Statutory grounds are invoked.
- Fees are paid.

> If defective → **Rectification opportunity** is granted.

---

### **STEP 6 — Notice to the Applicant**

The Controller issues a **notice** to the patent applicant (the "patentee-in-waiting") requiring them to file a **reply (Counter-Statement)** within **3 months** (extendable to 6 months by the Controller).

---

### **STEP 7 — Reply by the Applicant (Counter-Statement)**

The applicant files their defence and may lead **evidence** in support.

---

### **STEP 8 — Reply to Counter-Statement by Opponent**

The opponent may file **reply evidence** to rebut the applicant's counter-statement.

---

### **STEP 9 — Hearing**

The Controller fixes a **date of hearing** after giving both parties at least **10 days' notice** (Section 25(1) proviso read with Rule 55).

$$
\text{Hearing Date} - \text{Notice Date} \geq 10 \text{ days}
$$

---

### **STEP 10 — Decision by Controller**

After hearing, the Controller passes a **speaking order** deciding whether to:
- **Refuse the patent**, OR
- **Allow the application** to proceed to grant.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedural Flow — Post-Grant Patent Opposition (Section 25(2))

### **STEP 1 — Grant of Patent & Publication**

The patent is granted and published in the **Patent Office Journal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trigger Event:** 12-month window opens from this publication date.

$$
\text{Opposition Window} = [\text{Grant Publication Date}, \text{Grant Publication Date} + 12 \text{ months}]
$$

---

### **STEP 2 — Filing of Post-Grant Opposition (Form 7B)**

A **"person interested"** files **Form 7B** within the **12-month window**.

**Definition of "Person Interested"** (Section 2(1)(t)):
> *"Person interested" includes a person engaged in, or in promoting, research in the same field as that to which the invention relates.*

Judicial interpretation (e.g., *F. Hoffmann-La Roche v. Cipla*) has widened this to include:
- Competitors.
- Manufacturers of generic equivalents.
- Public interest groups.
- Licensees.

---

### **STEP 3 — Constitution of Opposition Board (Section 25(3))**

The Controller constitutes an **Opposition Board** with **3 members**:

$$
\text{Opposition Board} = \{\text{Chairman (Controller or equivalent)}, \text{Member 1}, \text{Member 2}\}
$$

---

### **STEP 4 — Board's Recommendation**

The Opposition Board reviews the opposition and submits **recommendations** to the Controller.

> The Controller is **NOT BOUND** by the recommendations (Section 25(4)).

---

### **STEP 5 — Hearings & Decision by Controller**

The Controller conducts hearings and decides the matter. If the opposition succeeds:
- The patent is **revoked**.
- The Controller records a **revocation order**.

**Effect of Revocation:** The patent is treated as if it was **never granted** (subject to limited protection for acts done during patent life — Section 53).

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Procedural Element | Pre-Grant (Sec 25(1)) | Post-Grant (Sec 25(2)) |
|--------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Trigger** | Publication in Patent Journal | Grant publication |
| **Time** | Until grant | 12 months from grant |
| **Standing** | Any person | Person interested |
| **Form** | 7A | 7B |
| **Counter-Statement Period** | 3 months (extendable) | Similar timelines |
| **Hearing Notice** | ≥10 days | ≥10 days |
| **Board** | No Board | Opposition Board |
| **Outcome** | Refusal / Proceed to Grant | Revocation / Maintenance |

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Insight: Why Pre-Grant is Strategically Dominant

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Reality:** Over **95% of patent oppositions in India** are **Pre-Grant oppositions** because:
> 1. **No 12-month limitation** — opposition can be filed anytime until grant.
> 2. **"Any person"** can file — no standing hurdles.
> 3. **Free of cost effectiveness** — opposition typically occurs before the heavy investment of grant.
> 4. **Faster resolution** — no revocation action needed.
> 5. **Crucial in pharmaceuticals** — to prevent evergreening (e.g., the famous **Novartis v. Union of India** and **Natco v. Bayer (Nexavar)** cases).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*"Distinguish between Pre-Grant Opposition and Post-Grant Opposition under the Patents Act, 1970."*

#### **Model Answer:**

| Basis | Pre-Grant (Sec 25(1)) | Post-Grant (Sec 25(2)) |
|-------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Time** | After publication, before grant | Within 12 months of grant publication |
| **Who can file** | Any person | Person interested |
| **Form** | 7A | 7B |
| **Authority** | Controller | Opposition Board → Controller |
| **Grounds** | 11 | 7 |
| **Effect** | Refusal of application | Revocation of patent |

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*"What is the role of the Opposition Board under Section 25 of the Patents Act, 1970?"*

#### **Model Answer:**
The Opposition Board is constituted under Section 25(3) when a post-grant opposition is filed. It consists of **3 members** with the Controller (or equivalent senior officer) as Chairman. The Board reviews the opposition and submits **recommendations** to the Controller. However, the Controller is **not bound** by these recommendations (Section 25(4)).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the statutory grounds and procedure for filing a **Pre-Grant Opposition** under Section 25(1) of the Patents Act, 1970. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **"Person Interested"** and its judicial interpretation. Why is it significant in post-grant opposition? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Pre-Grant Opposition under Section 25(1):**

**Statutory Provision:** Section 25(1) of the Patents Act, 1970 allows **any person** to file an opposition in **Form 7A** at any time **after publication** of the application in the Patent Office Journal and **before grant**.

**Grounds (11):**
1. Wrongful obtainment of invention.
2. Prior publication in India.
3. Prior publication outside India.
4. Prior public use / knowledge in India.
5. Lack of inventive step (obviousness).
6. Invention not patentable under Section 3.
7. Insufficient / unclear disclosure.
8. Claims not fairly based on disclosure.
9. Non-disclosure of foreign applications.
10. Claims not clear or concise.
11. Failure to deposit biological material.

**Procedure:**
1. Identify published application in Patent Journal.
2. Draft statement of opposition citing specific grounds.
3. File Form 7A with evidence and prescribed fee.
4. Controller scrutinizes for compliance.
5. Notice to applicant for counter-statement (3 months).
6. Reply evidence by opponent.
7. Hearing (minimum 10 days notice).
8. Controller decides — refuse or proceed to grant.

**(b) "Person Interested" — Judicial Interpretation:**

**Statutory Definition:** Section 2(1)(t) defines *"person interested"* as a person engaged in, or promoting research in the same field as the invention.

**Judicial Expansion:**
- **F. Hoffmann-La Roche v. Cipla (2008):** Delhi HC held that "person interested" includes competitors, generic drug manufacturers, and public interest litigants.
- **Stand-alone vs Interested:** The scope is wider than property right holders; mere interest in the field suffices.
- **Pharma Sector:** Considered the dominant context — used to challenge evergreening and bad patents.

**Significance:**
- Limits locus standi for post-grant opposition (narrower than pre-grant).
- Prevents vexatious or speculative oppositions.
- Ensures only **substantively affected** parties can challenge post-grant patents.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the **procedure and grounds for Trademark Opposition** under Section 21 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the **strategic importance of opposition** in the pharmaceutical and technology sectors in India. Cite at least two case laws. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Trademark Opposition under Section 21:**

**Procedure:**
1. Application filed and examined.
2. Registrar accepts the application (absolute grounds).
3. Application advertised in the **Trademark Journal**.
4. **Within 4 months** (extendable by 1 month) any person may file **Form TM-5** with a statement of opposition and evidence.
5. Notice to applicant for counter-statement (within 2 months).
6. Reply evidence by opponent.
7. Hearing by Registrar.
8. Decision — registration allowed or refused.

**Grounds:**
- **Absolute (Section 9):** Non-distinctive, descriptive, customary, deceptive, contrary to morality.
- **Relative (Section 11):** Similarity to earlier mark, likelihood of confusion, dilution of well-known mark.

**(b) Strategic Importance of Opposition:**

**Pharmaceutical Sector:**
- **Natco Pharma v. Bayer (2012) — Nexavar Case:** Natco filed pre-grant opposition against Bayer's sorafenib tosylate patent and was subsequently granted a **compulsory license** — landmark precedent for affordability.
- **Novartis v. Union of India (2013):** Opposition against Gleevec (imatinib mesylate) on grounds of Section 3(d) — Supreme Court upheld refusal, curbing evergreening.

**Technology Sector:**
- Used to challenge software/business method patents of large tech firms.
- Defensive tool for SMEs and startups against patent thickets.

**Strategic Insights:**
- Pre-grant opposition is the dominant tool due to wide standing.
- Opposition is cost-effective compared to revocation or infringement suits.
- Aligns India's IP regime with public health and TRIPS flexibilities.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Forms:** Students write "Form 5" instead of "Form 7A" for pre-grant opposition. *Form 5 is the declaration of inventorship.*
> 2. **Mixing Sections:** Writing Section 25(1) for trademark opposition — the correct provision is **Section 21 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**.
> 3. **Wrong Standing:** Writing "any person" for post-grant opposition — it is **"person interested"**.
> 4. **Wrong Time Limits:** Trademark opposition is **4 months**, not 12 months.
> 5. **Opposition Board confusion:** The Board is constituted only for **post-grant** opposition, not pre-grant.
> 6. **Not citing Sections:** Examiners award full marks only when specific sections (e.g., 25(1), 25(2), 21, 9, 11) are cited.
> 7. **Confusing Controller and Registrar:** Patent opposition → Controller; Trademark opposition → Registrar.
> 8. **Omitting Case Laws:** For 14-mark questions, citing at least 2 case laws is essential.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Pre-Grant Opposition (Section 25(1))** — any person, Form 7A, before grant, 11 grounds.
- ✅ **Post-Grant Opposition (Section 25(2))** — person interested, Form 7B, 12 months from grant, 7 grounds.
- ✅ **Opposition Board (Section 25(3))** — 3 members, recommends to Controller; Controller not bound.
- ✅ **Trademark Opposition (Section 21)** — Form TM-5, 4 months from TM Journal.
- ✅ **Trigger Event:** Publication in Patent/Trademark Journal.
- ✅ **"Person Interested"** — defined in Section 2(1)(t); widened by *Roche v. Cipla*.
- ✅ **Outcome of Successful Opposition:** Patent refused (pre-grant) / Revoked (post-grant) / TM refused.
- ✅ **Section 3(d)** — critical ground in pharma oppositions.
- ✅ **Key Cases:** *Novartis v. UoI*, *Natco v. Bayer*, *Roche v. Cipla*.
- ✅ **TRIPS Compliance:** India's opposition system aligns with international best practices.
- ✅ **Counter-statement period:** 3 months; Reply evidence; 10-day hearing notice.
- ✅ **Fees:** Pre-grant ₹1,600/8,000; Post-grant ₹8,000/40,000; TM ₹2,700.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Grant of Patent** is the formal legal act by which the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** officially confers exclusive, time-bound, statutory rights upon an inventor (or assignee) for a claimed invention, after satisfying all substantive and procedural requirements under the **Patents Act, 1970** (as amended by the **Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Anchor:** Sections 43 to 53 of the Patents Act, 1970 govern the procedural framework for the *Grant of Patents* in India. A patent is **not granted automatically** upon filing — it is a reward conferred only after rigorous examination.

A patent grants the patentee the **exclusive right to prevent third parties** from making, using, offering for sale, selling, or importing the patented invention, for a non-renewable term of **20 years** from the date of filing.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you invent a unique, never-before-seen **automatic spice-dispensing kettle** that releases cardamom at precise temperatures. You cannot just shout in a marketplace that you invented it — you need the **Government's official stamp** that says: *"For the next 20 years, only YOU have the legal right to make, sell, or license this kettle."*

That official stamp = **Grant of Patent**.

Without the grant, your invention remains a *mere application* — it has no enforceable legal teeth. The journey from "I have an idea" to "I hold a patent" is called the **Grant Procedure**, and it involves five critical stages:

1. **Filing** (You submit your claim)
2. **Publication** (Government tells the world)
3. **Examination** (Government checks if your claim is genuinely new)
4. **Opposition** (Third parties challenge your claim)
5. **Grant** (Government officially confers rights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A *patent application* is a REQUEST. A *patent grant* is the FULFILLMENT of that request. Only post-grant does Section 53 confer enforceable monopoly rights.

## 1.3 Three Foundational Principles (Universally Applied)

| Principle | Meaning | Statutory Basis |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| **First-to-File** | Rights go to the person who files FIRST, not invents first | Section 11-A |
| **Territoriality** | Indian patent = protection ONLY in India | Section 3, 4 |
| **Absolute Novelty** | Invention must be unpublished anywhere in the world before filing | Section 13(1)(a) |

## 1.4 Term of Patent (Mandatory KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Term:** **20 years** from the **date of filing** of the application (not from grant date).
> **Renewal:** Annuity fees payable from 3rd year onwards. Non-payment → lapse of patent.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Patent Grant Procedure

The procedure under the Patents Act, 1970 follows a sequential, gate-kept architecture. Each pillar is a mandatory milestone:

### **Pillar 1 — Filing of Application (Section 7, 54)**
The applicant files with the **Patent Office** (Kolkata, Mumbai, Chennai, Delhi — based on jurisdiction). Mandatory forms:

- **Form 1** — Application for grant of patent
- **Form 2** — Provisional or Complete Specification
- **Form 3** — Statement & undertaking regarding foreign applications
- **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship

> **Date of Filing** = date the complete specification (Form 2) is received by the office. This date is sacred — it determines priority and the 20-year term counter.

### **Pillar 2 — Publication of Application (Section 11-A)**

- Application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the filing date (or priority date).
- **Early Publication** can be requested via **Form 9** (within 6 months of filing).
- Pre-grant publication gives the applicant **"interim protection"** — a *caveat* — but NO enforceable rights until grant.

### **Pillar 3 — Request for Examination (Section 11-B)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The application is **NOT examined automatically**. The applicant OR any interested person MUST file **Form 18** within **48 months** of the priority date, requesting examination.

If no Form 18 is filed within 48 months → application is treated as **withdrawn**.

### **Pillar 4 — Examination (Sections 12, 13)**

The examiner conducts a substantive check on:
- **Novelty** (Section 2(1)(j))
- **Inventive Step** (Section 2(1)(ja))
- **Industrial Application** (Section 2(1)(ac))
- **Not excluded** under Sections 3 & 4

The examiner issues the **First Examination Report (FER)**. Applicant must comply within **6 months** (extendable to 12 months via Form 4).

### **Pillar 5 — Grant and Sealing (Section 43)**

Once all objections are overcome, the patent is granted, entered in the **Register of Patents**, and published in the Patent Office Journal. The patent certificate is sealed and dated.

## 2.2 Opposition Proceedings (Section 25)

Two distinct opposition windows exist:

| Type | When | Who Can File | Form |
|------|------|--------------|------|
| **Pre-Grant Opposition** | Before grant | Any person | Form 7-A |
| **Post-Grant Opposition** | Within 12 months of grant | Any interested person | Form 7 |

> **Grounds for Opposition (Section 25):**
> - Wrongful obtainment
> - Prior publication / public knowledge
> - Lack of novelty, inventive step, industrial application
> - Insufficient disclosure
> - Non-patentable subject matter (Sections 3, 4)
> - Wrongful mention of inventor

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **highest-weightage recall surface** for KTU ESE questions. Memorize the form numbers and time limits verbatim.

| Stage | Statutory Section | Form | Time Limit | Key Metric |
|-------|-------------------|------|------------|------------|
| Application Filing | Sec 7, 54 | **Form 1, 2, 3, 5** | — | Date of filing = priority anchor |
| Publication | Sec 11-A | Form 9 (early) | **18 months** (default) | Patent Office Journal |
| Request for Examination | Sec 11-B | **Form 18** | **48 months** from priority | Auto-withdrawal if missed |
| Examination Report | Sec 12, 13 | — | Within **6 months** | FER issuance |
| Response to FER | Sec 21 | Form 8 (amendment) | **6 months** (extendable to **12**) | Compliance period |
| Pre-Grant Opposition | Sec 25(1) | **Form 7-A** | Any time before grant | "Any person" |
| Post-Grant Opposition | Sec 25(2) | **Form 7** | **12 months** from grant | "Interested person" |
| Grant | Sec 43 | — | — | Entered in Register |
| Term | Sec 53 | — | **20 years** | From filing date |
| Renewal | Sec 53(2) | **Form 9** (renewal) | Annually from year 3 | Annuity fees |

## 2.4 Rights of Patentee (Post-Grant) — Section 53

Once granted, the patentee enjoys exclusive rights:

- **Right to Exploit** commercially
- **Right to Assign** (Form 30)
- **Right to License** (Form 32)
- **Right to Sue for Infringement** (Section 104)
- **Right to Surrender** (Section 84 — though technically this is compulsory licensing)

The rights are **negative in nature** — the patentee cannot personally do everything, but can prevent others from doing so.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In B.Tech projects and industry R&D:
- Filing a patent before publishing your **IEEE / Springer paper** preserves novelty.
- Publishing first = self-destruction of novelty under Section 29.
- Patents in **semiconductor, biotech, pharma, AI/ML models** are now the cornerstone of Indian Startup IP strategy post-2020.
---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Legal Derivation of Term

## 3.1 Derivation: Term of Patent Calculation (Common KTU Problem Type)

KTU frequently tests the calculation of patent expiry dates. The logic follows statutory prescription:

> **Rule:** Patent term = **20 years** from the **earliest filing date** (priority date or application date, whichever is earlier).

### Worked Example 1: Simple Term Calculation

> **Problem:** Mr. K filed a patent application on **15th March 2015**. He requests early publication. When does the patent expire if granted?

**Step 1:** Identify the anchor date.
$$\text{Anchor Date} = \text{Date of Filing} = 15\text{-Mar-}2015$$

**Step 2:** Apply statutory term.
$$\text{Term} = 20 \text{ years from anchor date}$$

**Step 3:** Add exactly 20 calendar years.
$$\text{Expiry Date} = 15\text{-Mar-}2015 + 20 \text{ years} = 15\text{-Mar-}2035$$

### Worked Example 2: Convention Application with Priority

> **Problem:** Dr. T filed in USA on **10-Jan-2016** and filed in India (Convention Application under Section 135) on **05-Aug-2016**. Calculate expiry.

**Step 1:** Identify priority date (earliest filing).
$$\text{Priority Date} = \min(10\text{-Jan-}2016,\ 05\text{-Aug-}2016) = 10\text{-Jan-}2016$$

**Step 2:** Term calculation.
$$\text{Expiry} = 10\text{-Jan-}2016 + 20 \text{ years} = 10\text{-Jan-}2036$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why earliest?** This protects the inventor from losing term due to procedural delays across jurisdictions. The Convention Priority (Paris Convention) preserves the ORIGINAL priority date for the 20-year counter.

### Worked Example 3: Pre-Grant Opposition Outcome

> **Problem:** A patent application is published on 01-Jun-2019. A pre-grant opposition is filed on 15-Nov-2022. Application granted on 12-Feb-2024. What is the term?

The opposition outcome does **not** alter the filing date — the 20-year counter is unaffected.
$$\text{Term} = \text{Filing Date} + 20 \text{ years (constant)}$$

Opposition only affects **whether** the patent is granted, not **how long** it lasts (if granted).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: Novelty vs. Priority

For KTU analytical questions on "Who gets the patent?":

**Step 1:** Check publication events.
- If X published in a journal on Date D1, and Y filed application on Date D2:
- If D2 $\geq$ D1 → X has destroyed novelty for Y.

**Step 2:** Apply First-to-File Rule (Section 11-A).
- Among competing applications, the **earliest priority date wins**.

$$\text{Granted To} = \arg\min_{i \in \{\text{applicants}\}} (\text{Priority Date}_i)$$

**Step 3:** Verify against Section 13 prior-art search.
- Worldwide publication (printed/published/used) prior to filing = **anticipation** under Section 13(1)(a).

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Q1. Define "Grant of Patent" under the Patents Act, 1970.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The Grant of Patent is the formal statutory act under **Section 43** of the Patents Act, 1970, by which the **Controller of Patents** officially confers exclusive rights on the applicant after satisfying all procedural (filing, publication, examination) and substantive (novelty, inventive step, industrial application) requirements. The grant is evidenced by entry in the **Register of Patents**, sealing of the patent certificate, and publication in the **Patent Office Journal**. The term is **20 years** from the date of filing, during which the patentee has exclusive rights to exploit, assign, license, and sue for infringement.

---

### **Q2. What is the difference between Pre-Grant and Post-Grant Opposition?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Pre-Grant Opposition | Post-Grant Opposition |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Section** | Section 25(1) | Section 25(2) |
| **When** | Before grant (after publication) | Within **12 months** of grant |
| **Who** | Any person | Any interested person |
| **Form** | Form 7-A | Form 7 |
| **Effect** | May delay or prevent grant | May lead to revocation |

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **procedure for grant of patent** under the Patents Act, 1970, with reference to the statutory forms and time limits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **grounds for opposition** of a patent application. When can a granted patent be revoked? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Procedure for Grant of Patent**

The procedure under Sections 7 to 43 of the Patents Act, 1970 involves the following stages:

**1. Filing of Application (Sec 7, 54):**
The applicant files:
- **Form 1** — Application for grant
- **Form 2** — Provisional/Complete Specification
- **Form 3** — Foreign filing particulars
- **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship

The **date of receipt of complete specification** = Date of Filing (Priority Anchor).

**2. Publication (Sec 11-A):**
- **Default Publication:** 18 months after filing/priority date.
- **Early Publication:** Via **Form 9** — within 1 month of request, but not before 6 months from filing.
- Published in the Patent Office Journal.

**3. Request for Examination (Sec 11-B):**
- Filed via **Form 18**.
- Must be filed within **48 months** from priority date.
- If missed → application deemed withdrawn.

**4. Examination (Sec 12, 13):**
- Examiner conducts search for prior art (worldwide).
- Checks novelty, inventive step, industrial application, and exclusion under Sections 3 & 4.
- **First Examination Report (FER)** is issued.
- Applicant responds within **6 months** (extendable to **12 months** via Form 4).

**5. Grant (Sec 43):**
- If objections are overcome, the patent is granted.
- Entered in the Register of Patents.
- Patent certificate is sealed and dated.
- Published in the Patent Office Journal.

**6. Term (Sec 53):** 20 years from the date of filing, subject to annual renewal fees from year 3 onwards.

#### **Part (b) — Grounds for Opposition and Revocation**

**Grounds for Pre-Grant Opposition (Sec 25(1)):**

$$\text{Grounds} = \big\{ \text{wrongful obtainment},\ \text{prior publication},\ \text{lack of novelty},\ \text{lack of inventive step},\ \text{lack of industrial application},\ \text{insufficient disclosure},\ \text{Section 3/4 exclusion},\ \text{wrongful mention of inventor} \big\}$$

**Grounds for Post-Grant Opposition (Sec 25(2))** — same grounds PLUS:
- Non-working of patent in India
- Patentee has contravened provisions of the Act

**Revocation of Patent:**
- By **Controller** on a petition (Section 64) — within 1 year of grant (or any time for non-working / fraudulent obtainment).
- By **Appellate Board or High Court** (Section 66).
- Grounds include: non-patentable subject matter, lack of novelty, insufficient disclosure, non-working in India for unreasonable time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly confuse **Section 25** (opposition by third parties) with **Section 64** (revocation petition). Section 25 is opposition BEFORE/DURING grant proceedings; Section 64 is revocation AFTER grant. Memorize the trigger forms: **Form 7-A** (pre-grant), **Form 7** (post-grant), and **Form 15** (revocation). Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks per question**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What are the **rights of a patentee** under Section 53? Can these rights be assigned or licensed? Explain. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **First-to-File rule**, **priority date**, and **Convention Application** under the Patents Act, 1970. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Rights of a Patentee (Sec 53)**

A patentee enjoys the following exclusive rights post-grant:

**1. Right to Exploit:**
- Make, use, offer for sale, sell, or import the patented invention.

**2. Right to Assign (Sec 68):**
- The patent can be assigned via **Form 30** with the Controller's approval.
- Assignment must be registered to be valid.

**3. Right to License (Sec 68-69):**
- Patentee can grant licenses via **Form 32**.
- Exclusive, non-exclusive, or compulsory licensing.

**4. Right to Sue for Infringement (Sec 104):**
- Only the patentee can file a suit.
- Reliefs include injunction, damages, account of profits, seizure & destruction of infringing goods.

**5. Right to Surrender (Sec 84 — Compulsory Licensing route):**
- A patentee may request revocation of their own patent for any cause.

**Nature of Rights:**
$$\text{Patentee's Right} = \text{Negative Right to Exclude Others} \neq \text{Positive Right to Exploit}$$

The patentee has the **right to prevent**, not necessarily the right to commercialize. In some sectors (e.g., pharmaceuticals, biotech), the patentee must work the patent in India or face compulsory licensing.

#### **Part (b) — First-to-File, Priority Date, Convention Application**

**1. First-to-File Rule (Sec 11-A):**
India follows the **First-to-File system** — patent rights vest in the person who files the application FIRST, irrespective of who invented first.

$$\text{Patent Granted To} = \arg\min_{i} (\text{Filing Date}_i)$$

This replaces the earlier "First-to-Invent" system to bring India in line with international TRIPS compliance.

**2. Priority Date:**
- The date of the **earliest valid filing** that establishes the priority claim.
- For Convention Applications, priority date = filing date in the originating country (max 12 months).
- For PCT applications, priority date = international filing date.

**3. Convention Application (Sec 135):**
- Under the **Paris Convention**, an Indian resident can file in member countries within **12 months** of their home-country filing.
- Priority date = original filing date.
- Sec 135 enables reciprocity: foreign nationals can claim priority based on their home-country filings.

**PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty) Route:**
- Single international application filed via WIPO.
- Designated 153+ member countries.
- Provides 30/31 month national phase entry window.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently state "20 years from GRANT date." This is **wrong**. The term is **20 years from FILING date**. Examiners deduct full marks for this single error. Also, remember: **priority date ≠ filing date** in Convention/PCT cases — the priority date is the EARLIER anchor.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Grant of Patents**

- ⭐ **Grant Authority:** Controller of Patents (Sec 43); Register of Patents entry is the legal proof.
- ⭐ **Term of Patent:** **20 years** from **DATE OF FILING** (not grant date).
- ⭐ **Renewal:** Annuity fees from 3rd year onward. Non-payment → patent lapses.
- ⭐ **Form 18:** Mandatory request for examination. Deadline = **48 months** from priority date.
- ⭐ **Form 9:** Early publication request. Default publication = **18 months**.
- ⭐ **FER Compliance:** **6 months** (extendable to **12 months** via Form 4).
- ⭐ **Pre-Grant Opposition:** Form 7-A, Sec 25(1), **any person**, before grant.
- ⭐ **Post-Grant Opposition:** Form 7, Sec 25(2), **interested person**, within **12 months** of grant.
- ⭐ **First-to-File Rule:** Sec 11-A — earliest filer wins, regardless of invention date.
- ⭐ **Convention Application:** Sec 135; 12-month priority window under Paris Convention.
- ⭐ **Novelty Anchor:** Worldwide unpublished before filing (Sec 13).
- ⭐ **Three Substantive Tests:** Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application (Sec 2).
- ⭐ **Two Exclusion Sections:** Sec 3 (non-patentable) and Sec 4 (atomic energy).
- ⭐ **Rights of Patentee:** Negative right to exclude — assign (Form 30), license (Form 32), sue (Sec 104).
- ⭐ **Working of Patent:** Mandatory for pharma/chemical patents or compulsory licensing may apply.
- ⭐ **Priority Date ≠ Filing Date** in Convention/PCT routes — use the earlier anchor.
- ⭐ **Publication ≠ Grant** — Pre-grant publication gives NO enforceable rights, only caveat effect.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Term of Patent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Term of Patent # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Term of Patent** (Statutory Definition — Section 53, Indian Patents Act, 1970 as amended by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005) > > *"Subject to the provisions of this Act, the term of every patent granted, after the commencement of the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005, and the term of every patent which has not ceased and which has not been granted under the Indian Patents and Designs Act, 1911, shall be **twenty years from the date of filing of the application** for the patent."* In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the term of a patent refers to the **statutory duration of statutory monopoly** granted by the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM) to a patentee over a patented invention, beyond which the invention enters the **public domain**. | Statutory Anchor | Provision | |---|---| | **Section 53(1)** | Term = 20 years from date of filing | | **Section 53(2)** | Annual renewal fees from 3rd year | | **Section 53(3)** | Terminal disclaimer provision | | **Section 11A** | Date of filing = priority date (for pre-grant opposition) | | **Schedule II + Rule 80** | Renewal fee slabs | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠 > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Land Lease" Analogy for Patent Term** > > Imagine the government as the *owner* of all knowledge in the public domain, and the patentee as a *tenant*. When you file a patent, the government grants you a **20-year lease** on a specific piece of "intellectual land" (your invention). Like any lease, you must: > > 1. **Pay maintenance rent** (renewal fees) every year starting from year 3, or the lease **lapses**. > 2. **Use the land productively** (work the patent commercially in India), failing which compulsory licensing can be invoked (like a tenant in violation of use-clause). > 3. After **20 years**, the land reverts back to the public — anyone can farm it (manufacture, sell, use the invention freely). > > This analogy helps explain why the patent term is **not perpetual** — it balances **private incentive** (20 years of exclusivity) against **public interest** (eventual free access to innovation). ## 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Numerical Anchors (Remember for KTU Boards)** > > - **Statutory Term:** **20 years** > - **Date of reckoning:** **Date of filing** (not date of grant) > - **First renewal due:** **End of 2nd year** (i.e., before expiry of 3rd year) > - **Penalty for late renewal:** **6 months grace period with surcharge** (Rule 80(2)) > - **Restoration window:** **18 months** from date of cessation > - **Working requirement check:** **Every 3 years** (Section 146) > - **Pre-grant publication:** **18 months from priority date** (Section 11A) ## 1.4 Why "20 Years from Filing" is a Crucial Shift > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Alert:** > The 2005 Amendment Act shifted India from the **old 16-year from grant term** (under the 1911 Act regime, governed by Section 14 of the old act) to a uniform **20-year from filing term**, aligning with the **TRIPS Agreement, Article 33** and harmonising with global patent systems (US, EU, Japan). > > Students frequently lose marks by saying *"17 years"* (US legacy) or *"16 years from grant"* (pre-2005 India) — **do not confuse** these historical regimes with the current KTU-tested 20-year rule. --- # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Stage Patent Lifecycle (KTU Board Framework) A patent's "term" is not a single block but a structured lifecycle. The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to map renewal fees, working, and termination onto these stages: ### Stage 1 — **Pre-Grant Phase** (Day 0 to Date of Grant) - Application filed with Form 1, Form 2, Form 5, Form 26. - Application published after **18 months** (Section 11A). - Request for examination to be filed within **48 months** (Rule 24B). - **No renewal fees are due** during this phase. - Provisional vs. complete specification distinction matters: the **date of filing of complete specification** is the term anchor if priority is claimed from the provisional. ### Stage 2 — **Initial Term (Year 1 & Year 2)** — Post-Grant - Patent granted by Controller under Section 43. - **No renewal fee due yet** during these two years (first two years of patent term are free of renewal fees). - This is the "honeymoon period" — patentees often use it to commercialise. ### Stage 3 — **Annual Renewal Phase (Year 3 onwards)** - **Section 53(2)** mandates payment of renewal fees yearly. - **Rule 80** + **Schedule II** prescribe the fee slabs (different for natural persons, startups, and others). - Failure → patent **ceases** automatically. ### Stage 4 — **Grace Period & Restoration (Lapsed Patents)** - **6 months** grace period with surcharge (Rule 80(2)). - If still unpaid, patent **ceases to have effect** from the date of non-payment. - Application for **restoration** under **Section 60** within **18 months** of cessation. ### Stage 5 — **Termination & Public Domain** - After 20 years — patent term **expires**; the invention falls into public domain. - Cannot be restored after expiry. - Working requirements (Form 27) become academic. --- ## 2.2 Statutory Calculation Framework > [!IMPORTANT] > **Computing the Patent Term — The KTU-Worthy Formula** > > $$\text{Patent Term Expiry Date} = \text{Date of Filing} + 20 \text{ Years}$$ > > Where *Date of Filing* is defined under **Section 11A** and **Section 2(f)**: > > $$\text{Date of Filing} = \begin{cases} \text{Date of complete specification} & \text{if no priority claimed} \\ \text{Earliest priority date (under Section 154, Paris Convention)} & \text{if priority claimed} \end{cases}$$ **Key consequences of using the filing date:** - **Patent Pending period counts toward the 20 years** — the clock starts ticking *before* grant. This is unlike trade marks (10 years from registration) or copyrights (life + 60 years). - Encourages **early commercial exploitation** since the term is consumed by prosecution delay. - Aligns with **TRIPS Article 33**. --- ## 2.3 Renewal Fee Structure (Rule 80 + Schedule II) — KTU Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Renewal Fee Table — Indicative Slabs (as amended from time to time)** | Year of Patent | Renewal Fee Status | Statutory Provision | |---|---|---| | **Year 1 & 2** | ❌ No renewal fee | Section 53(2) proviso | | **Year 3** | ✅ Fee due before expiry of 3rd year | Rule 80(1)(a) | | **Year 4 – 6** | ✅ Annual | Rule 80(1)(b) | | **Year 7 – 10** | ✅ Annual | Rule 80(1)(c) | | **Year 11 – 15** | ✅ Annual | Rule 80(1)(d) | | **Year 16 – 20** | ✅ Annual | Rule 80(1)(e) | | **Late payment** | ✅ Within 6 months + surcharge (Form 4) | Rule 80(2) | | **Beyond 6 months** | ❌ Patent ceases; restore via Form 15 | Section 60 | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** "Renewal fees are due every year including years 1 and 2" is a **classic wrong answer**. The Act explicitly exempts the first two years. Writing this will cost you at least 2 marks. --- ## 2.4 "Working" of Patents and Its Effect on Term > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition of "Working" (Section 2(v) + Section 83)** > > *"Working" means the manufacture of the patented article or the use of the patented process, in India, on a commercial scale, to the fullest extent that is reasonably practicable, provided that mere importation does not constitute working.* **Key points on working and patent term:** - Working is **not a condition for grant** of a patent. - Working is **not a condition for continuation** of the term per se — the term runs regardless. - **However**, non-working triggers: - **Compulsory License (Section 84)** — if not worked for 3+ years. - **Revocation (Section 64(1)(f))** — for non-working. - The patentee must file **Form 27** (Statement of Working) annually as per **Rule 131**, even though the term itself is unaffected. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in industry:** > - **Pharma R&D:** A blockbuster drug's 20-year clock starts ticking the day the molecule is filed, *not* when it gets regulatory approval (which can take 8-10 years). Effective monopoly post-approval is often only 10-12 years — driving the **"evergreening"** debate. > - **Software & Hardware:** Filing early protects market share; delay risks shortened effective exclusivity. > - **University Tech Transfer Offices (TTOs):** Use the formula to compute *effective remaining patent life* during licensing negotiations. --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Note to Reader:** Because "Term of Patent" is a **statutory-legal** topic, the mathematical derivations are replaced with rigorous **statutory computation walkthroughs** and an **analytical decision matrix** — exactly what KTU boards test for marks. --- ## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Computing Patent Term Expiry > **Problem Statement:** > An Indian patent application was filed on **15 March 2010** with no priority claim. Examination was requested in 2012, and the patent was granted on **20 August 2014**. What is the patent term expiry date? ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1 — Identify the date of filing.** $$\text{Date of filing} = 15 \text{ March } 2010 \quad \text{(no priority claim, complete spec filed on this date)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the 20-year rule under Section 53(1).** $$\text{Expiry Date} = \text{Date of Filing} + 20 \text{ Years}$$ $$= 15 \text{ March } 2010 + 20 \text{ Years}$$ $$= 15 \text{ March } 2030$$ **Step 3 — Note the irrelevance of the grant date.** The grant date (20 August 2014) and request for examination (2012) **do not** affect the term calculation. They are relevant only for: - Calculation of the 6-month publication window (Section 11A). - Calculation of the 48-month examination request deadline (Rule 24B). **Step 4 — Effective exclusivity window.** $$\text{Effective Monopoly} = \text{Expiry Date} - \text{Date of Grant}$$ $$= 15 \text{ March } 2030 - 20 \text{ August } 2014$$ $$= 15 \text{ years, } 6 \text{ months, } 23 \text{ days}$$ > **Conclusion:** The patentee has effective commercial exclusivity of approximately **15.5 years** post-grant; the rest of the 20-year term was consumed by prosecution. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Priority Date Application (Section 154) > **Problem Statement:** > An Indian applicant files a complete specification in India on **10 October 2012**, claiming priority from a US provisional application filed on **12 October 2011**. What is the effective filing date for computing the patent term? ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1 — Establish priority claim eligibility under the Paris Convention (Section 154).** The Paris Convention allows a **12-month priority window** for patents. $$\text{Delay} = 10 \text{ October } 2012 - 12 \text{ October } 2011 = 364 \text{ days}$$ $$364 \text{ days} \leq 365 \text{ days (12 months)} \implies \text{Priority claim valid}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the priority date for term calculation.** Under Section 53 read with Section 154 and the TRIPS-aligned interpretation, the **priority date is treated as the date of filing** for novelty and term purposes. $$\text{Effective Date of Filing} = 12 \text{ October } 2011$$ **Step 3 — Compute the expiry date.** $$\text{Expiry Date} = 12 \text{ October } 2011 + 20 \text{ Years} = 12 \text{ October } 2031$$ **Step 4 — Compare with non-priority scenario.** Without the priority claim, expiry would be 10 October 2032 — the priority claim *shortens* the effective term by 363 days. This is the "cost" of priority — but it preserves **novelty** against intervening prior art. > **Conclusion:** Priority claims trade off a slightly shorter term for stronger novelty protection. The KTU examiner often tests whether students recognise this trade-off. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Lapse vs. Restoration vs. Expiry | Scenario | Statutory Trigger | Patent Status | Remedy / Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | **Non-payment of renewal fee** | Section 53(2) + Rule 80 | Patent ceases from date of non-payment | **Option A:** Pay within 6 months + surcharge (Form 4)
**Option B:** Restore within 18 months (Section 60, Form 15) | | **Revocation by Controller** | Section 64 (grounds include non-working, lack of novelty, false suggestion) | Patent revoked; subject to appellate remedy | Appeal to **IPAB → High Court → Supreme Court** | | **Surrender by patentee** | Section 63 | Patent deemed abandoned | No restoration; cannot be reclaimed | | **Natural expiry** | Section 53(1) | Term ends after 20 years | Invention enters public domain; no remedy | | **Compulsory license** | Section 84 | Patent *remains in force*; licensee granted | Term continues; royalty payable to patentee | | **Patent of addition** | Section 54, 55 | Co-terminus with main patent | If main patent ceases, addition also ceases | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Problem 3 — Restoration Window (Section 60) > **Problem Statement:** > A patent granted on 1 January 2015 ceased on 1 January 2018 due to non-payment of the 7th-year renewal fee (which was due on 31 December 2017). The patentee now seeks to restore the patent. Is restoration possible, and by what date? ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1 — Identify the date of cessation.** $$\text{Date of cessation} = 1 \text{ January } 2018$$ **Step 2 — Apply the 18-month restoration window under Section 60(1).** $$\text{Deadline} = 1 \text{ January } 2018 + 18 \text{ months} = 1 \text{ July } 2019$$ **Step 3 — Verify procedural requirements.** The patentee must: 1. File **Form 15** with prescribed fees. 2. Apply to the **Controller** of Patents. 3. Show that the non-payment was **unintentional** or due to **reasonable cause** (Section 60(7)). 4. Serve notice to prior users (Section 60(8)). 5. Pay the **lapsed renewal fees + surcharge** at the time of restoration hearing. **Step 4 — Consequence of restoration (Section 60(6)).** If restored, the patent is treated as **continuously in force** from the date of cessation, subject to the rights of ** bona fide** prior users between the cessation date and the restoration date. > **Conclusion:** Restoration is possible until **1 July 2019** — beyond this, the patent is permanently lost into the public domain. --- # 🗂️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Patent Term Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture) > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** Since the term-of-patent topic is governed by statutory timelines, the following Mermaid block renders a **temporal-state transition architecture** mapping every milestone, fee, and event over the 20-year horizon. It functions as a navigable "chronological map" of the patent term. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Day 0 Application Filed Form 1 and 2] --> nodeB[Pre Grant Publication 18 months] nodeB --> nodeC{Examination Requested 48 months max} nodeC --> nodeD[Patent Granted Section 43] nodeD --> nodeE[Year 1 and 2 No renewal fee] nodeE --> nodeF{Year 3 Renewal fee due} nodeF --> nodeG[Year 3 to 20 Annual renewal fees Rule 80] nodeG --> nodeH{Payment status} nodeH --> nodeI[Patent in force Active] nodeH --> nodeJ[6 month grace period surcharge Form 4] nodeJ --> nodeK{Paid in 6 months} nodeK --> nodeI nodeK --> nodeL[Patent ceases Section 53 2] nodeL --> nodeM{Restoration sought 18 months} nodeM --> nodeN[Controller hearing Section 60] nodeN --> nodeO{Patentee shows unintentional non payment} nodeO --> nodeI nodeO --> nodeP[Patent lapses into public domain] nodeI --> nodeQ[Year 20 reached] nodeQ --> nodeR[Patent term expires Public domain permanent] ``` > **How to read this diagram:** > - Each rectangle = a **statutory event** in the term. > - Each diamond = a **decision point** (e.g., fee paid or not). > - The "nodeL → nodeM → nodeN → nodeO" branch is the **restoration pathway** after lapse. > - The "nodeI → nodeQ → nodeR" branch is the **natural expiry pathway**. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Renewal Fee Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start2[Patent granted Date G] --> q1{Is the current patent year less than 3} q1 --> start2 q1 -- Year 1 or 2 --> n1[No fee Pay nothing] q1 -- Year 3 or later --> q2{Has the renewal fee been paid} q2 -- Yes --> n2[Patent in force Continue] q2 -- No --> q3{Is it within 6 months of due date} q3 -- Yes --> n3[Pay fee plus surcharge Form 4] q3 -- No --> q4{Is it within 18 months of cessation} q4 -- Yes --> n4[Apply for restoration Form 15 Section 60] q4 -- No --> n5[Patent lost to public domain Permanently] ``` --- ## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Indian Patent Term vs. Global Regimes | Jurisdiction | Statutory Provision | Term Anchor | Duration | |---|---|---|---| | 🇮🇳 **India (current, post-2005)** | Section 53(1), Patents Act 1970 | Date of filing | **20 years** | | 🇮🇳 **India (pre-2005, 1911 Act regime)** | Section 14, Indian Patents & Designs Act 1911 | Date of grant | 16 years (5 + 7 + 4 extendable) | | 🇺🇸 **USA** | 35 U.S.C. § 154 | Date of issuance (current law) | **20 years** (from earliest non-provisional filing for applications filed after 8 June 1995) | | 🇪🇺 **EU / EPC** | EPC Article 63 | Date of filing | **20 years** | | 🇯🇵 **Japan** | Patent Act Article 67 | Date of filing | **20 years** | | **TRIPS Standard** | Article 33 | Date of filing | Minimum **20 years** | > [!NOTE] > **Why "20 years from filing" is a global norm:** > Article 33 of the TRIPS Agreement prescribes a minimum 20-year term measured from the filing date. India's 2005 amendment harmonised domestic law with this international standard, ending the older 16-year-from-grant regime. --- ## 4.4 Text-Based Architecture — Surrender vs. Revocation vs. Lapse ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ THREE MODES OF ENDING A PATENT'S EFFECTIVE TERM │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ ┌───────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ SURRENDER │ │ REVOCATION │ │ LAPSE / │ │ │ │ Section 63 │ │ Section 64 │ │ EXPIRY │ │ │ │ Patentee │ │ Any person │ │ Automatic │ │ │ │ files notice │ │ files petition│ │ or non-pay │ │ │ │ voluntarily │ │ to Controller │ │ of fees │ │ │ └───────┬───────┘ └────────┬───────┘ └──────┬───────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ │ │ Voluntary act; Adverse ruling; Ceases by │ │ no restoration appeal to IPAB/ operation of │ │ possible High Court law / time │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` --- # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]** > *"What is the statutory term of a patent granted under the Indian Patents Act, 1970, and from which date is it computed?"* **(3 Marks)** ### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): Under **Section 53(1)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 (as amended in 2005), the term of every patent is **20 years from the date of filing of the application** for the patent. The "date of filing" is determined as follows: - Where **no priority** is claimed, the date of filing of the **complete specification** is treated as the date of filing. - Where **priority is claimed** under Section 154 (Paris Convention), the **earliest priority date** is treated as the date of filing for term computation. This 20-year-from-filing regime aligns with **Article 33 of the TRIPS Agreement**, replacing the older 16-year-from-grant term under the 1911 Act. **(3 Marks)** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** > *"Briefly explain the consequences of non-payment of patent renewal fees under the Patents Act, 1970."* **(3 Marks)** ### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): 1. **Cease of patent (Section 53(2)):** If the patentee fails to pay the renewal fee, the patent **ceases to have effect** from the date on which the unpaid fee became due. 2. **Grace period (Rule 80(2)):** The patentee may still pay the renewal fee within a **grace period of 6 months** from the due date, along with the **prescribed surcharge**, on Form 4. 3. **Restoration (Section 60):** If the 6-month grace period is also missed, the patentee may apply to the **Controller for restoration** within **18 months** from the date of cessation, on Form 15, by demonstrating that the non-payment was **unintentional or due to reasonable cause**. The Controller, after hearing any person interested, may restore the patent. **(3 Marks)** --- ## 📘 PART B QUESTIONS — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 2 ESE typically has a 14-mark question with internal choice. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as full alternatives. Sub-parts are 7 + 7 marks. --- ### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > *(a)* Explain in detail the statutory provisions relating to the **term of a patent** under the Indian Patents Act, 1970, including the date of reckoning and the rationale for the 20-year-from-filing rule. **(7 Marks)** > > *(b)* Discuss the **renewal fee regime** under Section 53(2) and Rule 80 of the Patents Act, 1970, and analyse the consequences of non-payment including the **restoration mechanism** under Section 60. **(7 Marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution — Question A ### Part (a) — 7 Marks The term of a patent in India is governed by **Section 53** of the Patents Act, 1970, as amended by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005. **Statutory Provision (Section 53(1)):** The term of every patent granted after the commencement of the 2005 Amendment Act is **20 years from the date of filing** of the application for the patent. This is a uniform rule, regardless of the date of grant. **Date of Filing — Section 11A and Section 2(f):** - Where **no priority** is claimed, the **date of filing of the complete specification** is treated as the date of filing. - Where priority is claimed under **Section 154 (Paris Convention)**, the **earliest priority date** is reckoned as the date of filing for novelty, term, and prior-art purposes. - A **provisional specification** does not start the term — only the complete specification does. **Rationale for the 20-year-from-filing rule:** 1. **TRIPS Compliance (Article 33):** The Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights Agreement mandates a minimum 20-year term computed from the date of filing. The 2005 amendment harmonised Indian law with this standard. 2. **Pre-2005 historical context:** Under the **Indian Patents and Designs Act, 1911** (Section 14), the term was 16 years from the date of grant, with extensions. This led to a peculiar situation where protracted prosecution could effectively **extend** the patent term beyond 20 years — distorting innovation incentives. 3. **Patent Pending Penalty:** The current rule imposes a cost on **delayed prosecution** — the 20-year clock runs during the pre-grant phase, encouraging efficient examination. 4. **Alignment with Global Practice:** The US (35 U.S.C. § 154), EU (EPC Article 63), and Japan all use a 20-year-from-filing regime, enabling consistent global IP strategy. 5. **Balancing Innovation and Public Interest:** The 20-year window is considered sufficient for the patentee to **recoup R&D investment** and earn a reasonable return, after which the invention enters the public domain to promote further innovation. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Renewal Fee Regime — Section 53(2) and Rule 80:** Under **Section 53(2)**, an annual renewal fee is payable to keep the patent in force from the **3rd year onwards**. The first two years of the patent term are free of renewal fees. The fee structure is prescribed in **Rule 80** and **Schedule II** of the Patents Rules. **Fee Schedule (Rule 80):** - Renewal fee due for the 3rd year — to be paid before the expiry of the 2nd year (i.e., before the patent enters its 3rd year of term). - Subsequent renewals — annually, before the expiry of each year. - For startups, small entities, and natural persons, the fees are **reduced by 80%** under the 2016 fee revision. **Consequences of Non-Payment — Three-Stage Mechanism:** **Stage 1 — Cease (Section 53(2)):** Failure to pay the renewal fee on time results in the patent **ceasing to have effect** from the date on which the fee was due. No separate notice is required from the Patent Office. **Stage 2 — Grace Period (Rule 80(2)):** The patentee may regularise non-payment by paying the fee plus a **surcharge within 6 months** of the due date, on **Form 4**. This is an automatic right; no Controller's order is required. **Stage 3 — Restoration (Section 60):** If the 6-month grace period is also missed, the patent can be **restored** only by application to the Controller, on **Form 15**, within **18 months** from the date of cessation. The patentee must show that the non-payment was **unintentional or due to reasonable cause**. **Key Features of Restoration:** - The Controller holds a hearing and may **restore unconditionally or subject to conditions**. - Notice of the restoration application must be served on **any person who was using the invention** between cessation and restoration. - The rights of such prior users are **protected** even after restoration. - The restoration **does not revive** any rights lost to bona fide users before the restoration. **Practical Significance:** The 18-month restoration window and the "unintentional non-payment" test make restoration a **safety net**, not a default. KTU exams frequently test whether students can distinguish between **lapse** (automatic), **grace period** (administrative), and **restoration** (judicial discretion). **(7 Marks)** --- ### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > *(a)* Discuss the **working of patents** as defined under Section 2(v) and Section 83 of the Patents Act, 1970. How does non-working impact the term of a patent? **(7 Marks)** > > *(b)* Critically analyse the **surrender (Section 63)** and **revocation (Section 64)** of patents, comparing their effects on patent term. **(7 Marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution — Question B ### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Definition of "Working" — Section 2(v) and Section 83:** "Working" of a patent, as defined in **Section 2(v)** read with **Section 83**, means the **manufacture of the patented article** or the **use of the patented process** in India, on a **commercial scale**, to the **fullest extent that is reasonably practicable**. **Key elements:** 1. **Manufacture in India** — mere importation does NOT constitute working (Section 2(v) proviso). 2. **Commercial scale** — small-scale or experimental use is excluded. 3. **Fullest extent reasonably practicable** — qualitative standard, not just quantitative. **Annual Disclosure Obligation — Form 27 (Rule 131):** The patentee must file **Form 27** (Statement of Working) annually, disclosing whether the patent is being worked in India and the quantum of production. This filing is mandatory even though it does not directly affect the term. **Impact of Non-Working on Patent Term:** 1. **Term itself is NOT shortened** — the 20-year statutory term runs independently of whether the patent is worked. The clock under Section 53(1) is unaffected by non-working. 2. **Compulsory Licensing (Section 84):** Any person may apply for a compulsory license if the patented invention is **not worked** in India for a period of **3 years** from the date of grant. This does not end the term, but it dilutes the patentee's exclusivity. 3. **Revocation (Section 64(1)(f)):** Non-working is a ground for revocation, which **ends the term** of the patent. 4. **Public Policy:** The working requirement reflects India's concern that patents should not be used to block domestic manufacture, particularly in pharmaceuticals and agriculture — a position rooted in the **Indian Patent Act's public-interest safeguards** (Sections 83–92). **Conclusion:** Non-working does not directly shorten the patent term, but it creates significant legal exposure through compulsory licensing and revocation, making it a practical constraint on patentee behaviour. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Surrender of Patent — Section 63:** Under **Section 63**, a patentee may, at any time, give notice to the **Controller of Patents** offering to **surrender** the patent. The Controller advertises the offer, and any person interested may oppose it. If no valid opposition is received, the Controller accepts the surrender, and the patent is deemed **abandoned**. **Key features:** - **Voluntary act** of the patentee — surrender is initiated by the patentee. - **No restoration** possible once surrendered. - Cannot surrender a patent that is the subject of a **licence** without the licensee's consent (Section 63(2)). - May surrender only part of the claims if the patent is for a combination. **Revocation of Patent — Section 64:** Under **Section 64**, a patent may be revoked on a **petition** filed before the **Controller or the IPAB** (Intellectual Property Appellate Board) by any person interested or by the Central Government. **Grounds for revocation (Section 64(1)) include:** - (a) Non-patentable subject matter (Section 3/4 violations). - (b) Lack of novelty or inventive step. - (c) Non-disclosure or fraudulent suggestion. - (d) Insufficiency of disclosure. - (e) Wrongful mention of source. - (f) **Non-working** in India. - (g) Opposition by the Central Government on public interest grounds. **Comparison:** | Dimension | Surrender (Section 63) | Revocation (Section 64) | |---|---|---| | **Initiator** | Patentee voluntarily | Any person interested, Central Government, Controller | | **Nature** | Voluntary abandonment | Adversarial quasi-judicial process | | **Grounds** | Patentee's discretion | Statutory grounds (9 specific grounds) | | **Remedy** | None (final) | Appeal lies to **IPAB → High Court → Supreme Court** | | **Effect on term** | Patent ends immediately | Patent ends upon revocation order | | **Restoration** | Not available | Not available once revocation is final | | **Public notice** | Controller advertises | Hearing with notice to patentee | **Conclusion:** Both surrender and revocation terminate the patent term, but they differ fundamentally in **initiator, procedure, and reversibility**. Surrender is a strategic decision (e.g., when commercial value diminishes), while revocation is a defensive remedy for third parties aggrieved by an invalid or non-working patent. **(7 Marks)** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Term of Patent" Questions** > > 1. **Confusing "16 years from grant" (pre-2005) with "20 years from filing" (post-2005).** Many students write the older rule. KTU tests the current law. *Lose 2-3 marks.* > > 2. **Forgetting the 2-year holiday on renewal fees.** Saying "renewal fees are due every year from year 1" is factually wrong and costs marks. > > 3. **Conflating "term" with "working".** Students often wrongly state that non-working shortens the term. Section 53(1) is independent of working; the term runs regardless. > > 4. **Confusing 6-month grace period with 18-month restoration window.** These are two different stages. The 6-month period is automatic (Rule 80(2) + Form 4), while the 18-month period requires a Controller's order (Section 60 + Form 15). > > 5. **Writing "surrender and revocation are the same."** They are distinct. Surrender is voluntary; revocation is adversarial. Examiners expect this distinction. > > 6. **Forgetting to cite statutory sections.** Always quote **Section 53, 60, 63, 64, 84, 2(v), 11A, 154** — examiners reward statutory precision. > > 7. **Treating the priority date as something other than the date of filing.** Under Section 154, the priority date replaces the date of filing for term purposes — this is a frequently missed nuance. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Term of Patent"** ### A. Statutory Core - [x] **Section 53(1)** → Term = 20 years from date of filing. - [x] **Section 53(2)** → Renewal fees from 3rd year onwards. - [x] **Rule 80 + Schedule II** → Fee slabs. - [x] **Rule 80(2)** → 6-month grace period with surcharge. - [x] **Section 60** → Restoration within 18 months of cessation (Form 15). - [x] **Section 63** → Voluntary surrender. - [x] **Section 64** → Revocation on statutory grounds. - [x] **Section 2(v) + 83** → Definition of "working". - [x] **Section 11A + 2(f)** → Date of filing. - [x] **Section 154** → Priority date for Paris Convention claims. - [x] **Article 33 TRIPS** → 20-year minimum global standard. ### B. Numerical Anchors - [x] **20 years** = statutory term. - [x] **18 months** = pre-grant publication; restoration window. - [x] **48 months** = examination request deadline. - [x] **6 months** = renewal grace period with surcharge. - [x] **3 years** = working requirement (Section 84); also, 3rd-year renewal begins. ### C. Conceptual Anchors - [x] **Term runs from filing, not grant** — patent pending period counts. - [x] **Renewal fees** are independent of term; non-payment causes lapse. - [x] **Working is not a condition for term** — but is a condition for protection against compulsory licensing and revocation. - [x] **Priority claims shorten effective term** — trade-off for stronger novelty. - [x] **Surrender ≠ Revocation** — voluntary vs. adversarial. - [x] **TRIPS Article 33** is the international anchor for the 20-year rule. ### D. Mnemonic for Fast Recall > **"T-R-S-W-E"** → **T**erm 20 years from filing | **R**enewal fees from year 3 | **S**urrender (Sec 63) | **W**orking (Sec 83) | **E**xpiry → Public domain ---

topics.title: Patents of Addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Patents of Addition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Patent of Addition** is a special category of patent granted under **Section 54 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970** for an invention which constitutes an **improvement, modification, or addition** to an already existing patented invention (referred to as the *main patent* or *principal patent*). The improvement invention, taken alone, would qualify for a patent, but the applicant files it as an "addition" because it is commercially or technically linked to the parent invention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Anchor (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Section 54**: Application for patent of addition.
> - **Section 55**: Term of patents of addition.
> - **Section 56**: Conversion of patent of addition into an independent patent.

### Key Terminology
