| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Main Patent** | The original/principal patent to which the addition relates. |
| **Improvement Invention** | A new invention that modifies, enhances, or adds a feature to the main invention. |
| **Co-terminus Patent** | A patent whose expiry coincides with the term of the main patent. |
| **Independent Patent** | A self-contained patent with its own claims, not dependent on another. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

Imagine you have built a **marvelous bicycle** (this is your *main patent*). Years later, you invent a **gear-shifting system** that, when attached to that specific bicycle, makes it perform significantly better.

- You *could* file the gear system as a completely new, independent patent — but doing so would mean paying double renewal fees, fighting two separate infringement suits, and managing two patent portfolios.
- A smarter alternative (provided by patent law) is to file the gear system as a **Patent of Addition**. The Patent Office treats it as an *attachment* to your bicycle patent. It rides piggyback — it inherits the term, the renewal fees are nominal, and infringement of the addition is treated like infringement of the main patent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industry Example:**
> - **Main Patent**: A pharmaceutical company patents a base molecule `Drug-X` for treating hypertension.
> - **Patent of Addition**: The company later discovers a specific salt-form or polymorph of `Drug-X` that improves bioavailability. Rather than filing a fresh 20-year patent, the improved form is filed as an *addition* to the main patent.

## 1.3 Why Does the Patent System Allow This?

The legal rationale rests on three pillars:

1. **Encouragement of Improvement** — Inventors are incentivised to refine existing technology without losing protection.
2. **Administrative Efficiency** — The Patent Office avoids duplicating examination of shared disclosures.
3. **Economic Relief** — The patentee pays reduced fees on the addition, recognising that the novelty lies in the *delta* over the main invention.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Statutory Conditions for Grant (Section 54 Analysis)

For a Patent of Addition to be granted, the following conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Existence of a Main Patent** — The applicant must already hold, or be applying for, a valid patent. The addition cannot stand alone.
2. **Improvement or Modification** — The invention claimed in the addition must be an improvement, modification, or further development of the main invention.
3. **Patentability on its Own Merits** — Although filed as an addition, the improvement must, by itself, satisfy the tests of **novelty, inventive step, and industrial application** (Sections 2(1)(j), 2(1)(ja), 2(1)(ac)).
4. **Same Applicant** — Generally, the applicant of the patent of addition must be the same as (or the assignee of) the main patent applicant.
5. **No Earlier Disclosure** — The improvement must not have been previously published or claimed in the main patent specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substantive Examination:**
> Even though it is filed as an *addition*, the Controller examines the improvement invention on its own merits. The applicant cannot rely solely on the patentability of the main patent.

## 2.2 Rights of the Patentee

- The patentee of an addition enjoys the **same rights, remedies, and reliefs** as the patentee of the main patent (Section 54(1)).
- Infringement of a patent of addition is treated as infringement of the main patent for procedural purposes — they can be sued upon jointly or separately.
- The patentee may **license, assign, or mortgage** the addition along with (or sometimes independently of) the main patent.

## 2.3 Term of Patent of Addition (Section 55)

The patent of addition is **co-terminus** with the main patent. This is the single most distinctive feature.

$$T_{\text{addition}} = T_{\text{main patent at the time of grant}}$$

If the main patent is granted later than the addition, the term of the addition is recalculated to align with the main patent's term from its own date of grant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Point:** A Patent of Addition does **NOT** receive its own independent 20-year term. Its life is bounded by the life of the main patent.

## 2.4 Renewal Fees — Economic Advantage

The patentee of an addition is **exempt from paying separate renewal fees** under Section 142, provided the main patent is in force. Renewal is automatic and aligned with the main patent's renewal cycle.

| Fee Component | Main Patent | Patent of Addition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Application Fee | Standard | Standard |
| Examination Fee | Standard | Standard |
| **Renewal Fee (annually)** | Mandatory | **Exempted** if main patent is in force |
| Renewal after main patent lapses | N/A | Required if converted to independent |

## 2.5 Conversion to Independent Patent (Section 56)

This is the **escape clause**. If the main patent is:
- Revoked under Section 64, OR
- Deemed invalid by a court, OR
- Surrendered or lapses,

then the patent of addition may be **converted into an independent patent** by the Controller upon the patentee's application. Once converted:
- It obtains its own full term (typically **20 years from the date of filing**).
- It becomes a self-sustaining patent.
- The applicant must pay the **shortfall in renewal fees** retrospectively.

$$T_{\text{independent}} = 20 \text{ years from filing date of the addition}$$

## 2.6 Revocation

Under **Section 64**, a patent of addition can be revoked:
- On the same grounds as a main patent, OR
- If the main patent itself is revoked (provided no application for conversion under Section 56 is pending).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference

| Concept | Statutory Provision | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Application for Addition | Section 54 | High — exam favourite |
| Term Co-terminus | Section 55 | Very High |
| Conversion Mechanism | Section 56 | Very High |
| Exemption from Renewal Fees | Section 142(4) read with Rule 80 | High |
| Revocation Grounds | Section 64 | Moderate |
| Working Statement | Section 146 | Moderate |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical Engineering**: Improvement in turbine blade geometry filed as addition to the main turbine design patent.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: New crystalline form, salt, or dosage form of a known drug.
- **Electronics**: Enhanced circuitry or firmware architecture improvements.
- **Chemical Engineering**: Improved catalytic process for an existing patented reaction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Legal Analysis & Procedural Matrix

> Because *Patents of Addition* is a statutory-legal concept (not a numerical/physical derivation), this section adapts the KTU analytical framework into an **exhaustive legal-procedural breakdown** with explicit conditional logic, mirroring the rigour expected in a board answer.

## 3.1 Procedural Logic Flow — Application to Grant

**Step 1: Pre-Filing Verification**
The applicant first verifies that:
- A main patent exists (or a main application is co-pending).
- The improvement has not been disclosed in the main specification.
- The improvement satisfies Sections 2(1)(j), 2(1)(ja), and 2(1)(ac).

**Step 2: Drafting of Specification**
The applicant files a complete specification describing:
- The main invention (brief reference).
- The specific improvement, with claims clearly distinguishing it from the main patent claims.

**Step 3: Filing and Formality Check**
Form 1, Form 2, Form 5 (and Form 26 if applicable) are filed with the appropriate fees. The Patent Office tags the application as a "Patent of Addition."

**Step 4: Publication**
Published in the Patent Journal under a separate classification — but cross-referenced to the main patent number.

**Step 5: Examination**
Request for examination under Section 11B is filed within **48 months** of priority date. The Controller examines:
- Novelty and inventive step of the *improvement alone*.
- Whether the improvement falls within the scope of the main patent's disclosure.

**Step 6: First Examination Report (FER)**
If objections are raised, the applicant responds within 6 months (extendable up to 12 months under Rule 24B).

**Step 7: Grant and Sealing**
On acceptance, the patent of addition is granted with a number suffixed or cross-indexed to the main patent.

## 3.2 Decision Matrix — When to File as Addition vs Independent Patent

| Scenario | Recommended Filing | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Improvement is technically inseparable from main invention | Patent of Addition | Saves fees; co-terminus protection |
| Improvement has independent commercial value | Independent Patent | Allows separate licensing, longer term |
| Inventor wants 20-year fresh term on improvement | Independent Patent | Addition is bounded by main patent's residual term |
| Improvement is by same applicant and within 1-2 years of main patent | Patent of Addition | Administrative simplicity |
| Main patent is close to expiry (less than 5 years remaining) | Independent Patent | Addition would yield negligible term |

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Table — Addition vs Independent Patent

| Parameter | Patent of Addition | Independent Patent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Term | Co-terminus with main patent | 20 years from filing |
| Renewal Fees | Exempt (if main patent alive) | Mandatory annually |
| Examination | Separate examination | Separate examination |
| Novelty | Required for the improvement | Required for full invention |
| Infringement Action | Joint or separate suit | Separate suit |
| Conversion Possible | Yes, under Section 56 | N/A (already independent) |
| Cost Efficiency | High | Lower |

## 3.4 Logical Equivalence Statement

For exam-writing, the following equivalence is helpful:

$$\text{Protection}_{\text{addition}} = \min(\text{Protection}_{\text{main}}, \text{Novelty of Improvement})$$

In plain English: the *effective protection* from a patent of addition is the lesser of (a) the residual life of the main patent and (b) the patentable scope of the improvement.

## 3.5 Judicial Interpretation Highlights

Indian courts have held that:

1. The Patent Office has **discretion** to refuse an addition if the improvement is large enough to warrant an independent patent of its own (Controller's inherent power).
2. The improvement must be a *genuine modification* and not a mere trivial variant.
3. A patent of addition **cannot be granted after the main patent has expired** — there is nothing to attach it to.
4. During the pendency of the main application, an addition can be filed, but it will be granted only upon grant of the main patent.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture Flow — Lifecycle of a Patent of Addition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inventor conceives Improvement over Main Patent] --> B{Is Main Patent in Force?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Draft Complete Specification for Improvement]
    B -- No --> D[Consider Independent Patent Filing]
    C --> E[File Application under Section 54 with Form 1, 2, 5]
    E --> F[Patent Office Tags as Patent of Addition]
    F --> G[Publication in Patent Journal]
    G --> H[Request for Examination under Section 11B]
    H --> I[Controller Examines Novelty of Improvement Alone]
    I --> J{Objections Raised?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Applicant Responds within 6 to 12 months]
    K --> I
    J -- No --> L[Grant of Patent of Addition]
    L --> M[Patent is Co-terminus with Main Patent]
    M --> N{Main Patent Revoked or Lapsed?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Apply under Section 56 for Conversion]
    O --> P[Converted to Independent Patent with 20-year Term]
    P --> Q[Pay Shortfall Renewal Fees]
    N -- No --> M
    L --> R[Possible Revocation under Section 64]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rights and Limitations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAIN[Main Patent Block]
        M1[Claim Set of Original Invention]
        M2[20 Year Term from Filing]
        M3[Annual Renewal Fees Applicable]
    end

    subgraph ADD[Patent of Addition Block]
        A1[Claim Set of Improvement]
        A2[Co-terminus Term with Main]
        A3[No Separate Renewal Fees]
        A4[Linked Infringement Action]
    end

    subgraph CONV[Conversion Block under Section 56]
        C1[Triggered on Main Patent Revocation]
        C2[Gains Independent 20 Year Term]
        C3[Retrospective Renewal Fee Payment]
    end

    M1 --> A1
    M2 --> A2
    M3 -.Exemption.-> A3
    A1 --> A4
    M1 --> A4
    A2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Relationship Map — Patent of Addition Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Main Patent Application] --> P2[Main Patent Granted]
    P2 --> P3[Inventor Develops Improvement]
    P3 --> P4[Patent of Addition Filed under Section 54]
    P4 --> P5[Examination and Grant]
    P5 --> P6[Parallel Protection with Main Patent]
    P6 --> P7{Commercial Strategy}
    P7 -- Bundle --> P8[Single License for Main plus Addition]
    P7 -- Separate --> P9[Independent Licensing Streams]
    P6 --> P10{Main Patent Fate}
    P10 -- Survives --> P6
    P10 -- Revoked --> P11[Conversion under Section 56]
    P11 --> P12[Independent Patent Term Begins]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Patent of Addition" and state the statutory provision under which it is granted.**

**Model Answer:**
A Patent of Addition is a patent granted for an invention which constitutes an improvement, modification, or further development of an already patented invention (the main patent). It is governed by **Section 54 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970**. The improvement must, by itself, satisfy the tests of novelty, inventive step, and industrial application, but its term is co-terminus with the main patent under **Section 55**.

*(3 marks — Definition 1m, Section reference 1m, Co-terminus feature 1m)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State any three differences between a Patent of Addition and an Independent Patent.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Patent of Addition | Independent Patent |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Term is co-terminus with the main patent under Section 55 | Term is 20 years from the date of filing |
| 2 | No separate renewal fees required if main patent is in force | Mandatory annual renewal fees are payable |
| 3 | Can be converted into an independent patent under Section 56 | Cannot be converted (already independent) |
| 4 | Infringement action can be combined with main patent | Separate, standalone infringement action |

*(3 marks — Each valid distinction 1m)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the legal provisions relating to the grant of a Patent of Addition under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Discuss the conditions that must be satisfied. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Patent of Addition is governed primarily by **Sections 54, 55, and 56** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970.

**Section 54 — Application:**
- The applicant must already hold or have applied for a main patent.
- The invention must be an improvement or modification of the main invention.
- The improvement must satisfy the substantive tests of patentability (novelty, inventive step, industrial application).
- The applicant is generally required to be the same as (or assignee of) the main patent applicant.

**Section 55 — Term:**
- The patent of addition is co-terminus with the main patent.
- It does **not** have its own 20-year term.
- If the main patent is granted later, the term of the addition is recalculated to align with the main patent.

**Conditions for Grant:**
1. **Existence of Main Patent:** The principal patent must be valid and subsisting.
2. **Improvement Criterion:** The invention must be a genuine improvement, not a trivial variant.
3. **Independent Patentability:** The improvement, taken alone, must be novel and involve an inventive step.
4. **No Double Patenting:** The improvement must not have been claimed in the main specification.
5. **Common Applicant:** The applicant should be the same as or the legal successor of the main patent applicant.

*(7 marks — Section 54 explanation 2m, Section 55 explanation 2m, Five conditions 3m)*

---

**(b)** Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of filing a Patent of Addition. When should an applicant prefer an independent patent instead? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages of Patent of Addition:**
1. **Cost Efficiency:** Exemption from separate renewal fees under Section 142(4) significantly reduces the financial burden.
2. **Administrative Convenience:** Linked prosecution with the main patent simplifies paperwork.
3. **Infringement Synergy:** Infringement actions can be consolidated — a single suit may cover both patents.
4. **Encourages Innovation:** Incentivises inventors to refine and improve existing technology.
5. **Conversion Safety Net:** Under Section 56, if the main patent is revoked, the addition can be converted into an independent patent.

**Disadvantages of Patent of Addition:**
1. **Bounded Term:** The patent dies with the main patent, even if filed late.
2. **Reduced Commercial Flexibility:** Harder to license or assign independently.
3. **Risk of Main Patent Revocation:** If the main patent fails, the addition's protection may be jeopardised (though Section 56 offers relief).
4. **Limited Scope:** The improvement must relate to the main invention; broader innovations cannot be filed as additions.

**When to Prefer an Independent Patent:**
1. When the improvement has a long commercial life of its own.
2. When the main patent is close to expiry (less than 5 years remaining).
3. When the improvement is technically separable and has independent market value.
4. When the inventor desires a fresh 20-year term from the date of filing.
5. When the inventor wishes to license or assign the improvement independently of the main patent.

*(7 marks — Advantages 2m, Disadvantages 2m, Independent filing scenarios 3m)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of "conversion of a Patent of Addition into an Independent Patent" under Section 56. Under what circumstances is such conversion permitted? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:**
Section 56 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 provides a statutory mechanism whereby a Patent of Addition may be converted into a fully independent patent. This is essentially a *rescue provision* that protects the inventor's investment in the improvement, even when the principal patent fails.

**Circumstances Permitting Conversion:**
1. **Revocation of Main Patent:** When the main patent is revoked under Section 64 by the Controller or the Appellate Board.
2. **Court Declaration of Invalidity:** When a court declares the main patent invalid in a civil suit.
3. **Surrender of Main Patent:** When the patentee surrenders the main patent under Section 63.
4. **Lapse of Main Patent:** When the main patent lapses due to non-payment of renewal fees.

**Legal Effect of Conversion:**
- The converted patent receives its **own full term of 20 years from the date of filing** of the addition.
- The patentee must pay the **shortfall in renewal fees** that would have been payable had the patent been independent from the beginning.
- The converted patent stands on its own — it is no longer dependent on the main patent.
- The conversion is by **application to the Controller**, who has the discretion to allow it.

**Procedure:**
1. Patentee files a request for conversion with the Patent Office.
2. The Controller examines whether the conditions for conversion are satisfied.
3. Upon satisfaction, the Controller orders the conversion and notifies the patentee.
4. The patent is then re-registered as an independent patent.

*(7 marks — Concept 2m, Circumstances 2m, Legal effect 2m, Procedure 1m)*

---

**(b)** Critically analyse the term "co-terminus" in the context of Patents of Addition. How does it affect the commercial value of the improvement? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Meaning of Co-terminus:**
The term of a Patent of Addition is co-terminus with the main patent under **Section 55** of the Act. This means both patents expire on the same date — typically the date on which the main patent's full 20-year term ends.

**Effect on Commercial Value:**

*Negative Implications:*
1. **Reduced Monopoly Period:** If the improvement is filed late (e.g., 12 years into the main patent's life), the addition enjoys only 8 years of protection instead of 20.
2. **Diminished Licensing Appeal:** Licensees are reluctant to pay high royalties for a short residual term.
3. **Investment Risk Recovery:** R&D costs may not be recovered within the truncated term.

*Positive Implications:*
1. **Predictability:** The patentee knows exactly when the monopoly ends.
2. **Conversion Option:** Under Section 56, commercial value can be restored upon main patent revocation.
3. **Synergistic Marketing:** The addition enhances the marketability of the main patent during its co-terminus life.

**Critical Analysis:**
The co-terminus rule is a **double-edged sword**. While it encourages improvements by reducing costs, it can also *devalue* late-stage innovations. A balanced approach would require the patentee to carefully weigh the timing of filing. From a policy perspective, the provision reflects a trade-off between:
- **Innovation incentive** (rewarding improvements), and
- **Public interest** (preventing indefinite monopolies on derivative inventions).

**Conclusion:**
The co-terminus rule is appropriate when the improvement is commercially linked to the main invention. However, when the improvement stands as a commercially independent innovation, filing as an independent patent is the more commercially prudent choice.

*(7 marks — Meaning 1m, Negative effects 2m, Positive effects 2m, Critical analysis 2m)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Section 54 with Section 56.** Section 54 is the *grant* of addition; Section 56 is the *conversion* of addition into independent patent. Examiners often deduct 1-2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Do NOT state that a Patent of Addition has a 20-year term.** This is a critical error. The term is *co-terminus* with the main patent.
> 3. **Do NOT forget that the improvement must itself satisfy novelty and inventive step.** It cannot ride on the patentability of the main patent.
> 4. **Do NOT omit Section 55** when asked about the term. Many students write only Section 54.
> 5. **Mention the renewal fee exemption** — it is a high-yield point that examiners specifically look for in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Statutory Anchor:** Patent of Addition is governed by **Sections 54, 55, and 56** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970.
- **Co-terminus Term:** The addition's life is bounded by the main patent — **NOT 20 years** in its own right.
- **Renewal Fee Exemption:** No separate renewal fees are payable while the main patent is in force.
- **Improvement Criterion:** The invention must be a genuine improvement or modification, not a trivial variant.
- **Independent Patentability:** The improvement, taken alone, must satisfy novelty, inventive step, and industrial application.
- **Conversion under Section 56:** A safety net — if the main patent dies, the addition can be converted into an independent patent.
- **Revocation:** Under Section 64, a patent of addition can be revoked on the same grounds as a main patent.
- **Infringement:** Can be sued upon jointly with the main patent or separately.
- **Strategic Choice:** File as addition only when the improvement is commercially and technically linked to the main invention.
- **Examiner Favourite:** This topic frequently appears in Part B (14-mark) questions, especially the comparison between addition and independent patents.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Register of Patents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Register of Patents # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **REGISTER OF PATENTS — STATUTORY DEFINITION** > Under **Section 67 of the Patents Act, 1970**, the *Register of Patents* is defined as the **official, statutory record** maintained by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** at the Patent Office, containing complete particulars of all patents granted under the Act. It is the **primary, legally recognized source of truth** regarding the existence, ownership, status, and conditions attached to every patent in India. The Register is more than a mere ledger — it is a **public legal document** recognized by Indian courts and the Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB) as the conclusive evidence of patent rights, subject to the conditions laid down in Sections 67, 68, and 69 of the Patents Act, 1970. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Land Records" Parallel Imagine the Register of Patents as the **"Encumbrance Certificate (EC)"** of a property, but for inventions instead of land: | Property Records Analogy | Register of Patents Equivalent | |--------------------------|--------------------------------| | Registrar's Office | Patent Office (under CGPDTM) | | Land ownership document | Patent grant certificate | | Encumbrance Certificate (EC) | Register of Patents entry | | Mutation of name on title deed | Rectification under Section 68 | | Public access to land records | Inspection rights under Section 69 | | Sub-Registrar | Assistant Controller of Patents | Just as no one can legally claim ownership of a flat without the name appearing in the EC, a patentee cannot legally enforce patent rights without their name being entered into the Register of Patents. > [!NOTE] > **Statutory Foundation:** The Register of Patents is governed primarily by **Sections 67, 68, and 69** of the Patents Act, 1970. Section 43 deals with grant, but the Register itself is mandated under Section 67. ## 1.3 Physical & Digital Form of the Register > [!IMPORTANT] > **DUAL MODE MAINTENANCE** > The Register is maintained in **both physical (bound volumes) and electronic (digital database)** form at the Patent Office. Under the **Patents (Amendment) Rules, 2017** and subsequent e-filing mandates, the digital register accessible via the **IP India Online Portal (`ipindiaonline.gov.in`)** is treated as legally equivalent to the physical register for all evidentiary purposes. --- **Legal Presumption Attached to the Register:** $$\text{Entry in Register} \xRightarrow{\text{Sec. 72(1)}} \text{Prima facie evidence of assignment, license, or transmission of patent}$$ This means a court shall presume, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, that any entry relating to assignment, license, or transmission of a patent is true and accurate. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Statutory Breakdown — Section-Wise Architecture The Indian legal framework governing the Register of Patents is structured across **three principal sections** of the Patents Act, 1970: ### **Section 67 — Maintenance of the Register** The CGPDTM shall cause to be kept a register, called the **Register of Patents**, at the Patent Office. The register shall contain the following particulars: 1. **Name, address, and nationality** of the patentee(s) 2. **Title of the invention** and the date of the patent 3. **Patent number** (application number and grant number) 4. **Date of filing** of the application and date of priority (if any) 5. **Date of grant** of the patent 6. **Date of expiration** of the patent (typically **20 years** from the date of filing) 7. **Conditions and limitations**, if any, subject to which the patent was granted 8. **Any subsequent entry** affecting the validity or proprietorship of the patent, including: - Assignments and transfers - Licences granted - Mortgages, charges, or liens - Renewals or lapses - Surrender or revocation orders ### **Section 68 — Rectification of the Register** > [!IMPORTANT] > **RECTIFICATION POWERS** > Section 68 empowers the **Controller of Patents** to correct any **clerical error, obvious mistake, or any entry made in contravention of the provisions of the Act or Rules**. This rectification may be made: > - **Suo motu** (on the Controller's own motion), OR > - **Upon application** made in the prescribed manner by any aggrieved person. **Grounds for Rectification under Section 68:** - Clerical or typographical error - Wrong entry of name, address, or date - Entry made in contravention of the Act/Rules - Omission of required particulars > [!WARNING] > Section 68 is **NOT** to be confused with **Section 84** (revocation of a patent). Rectification under Section 68 only corrects the **register entry**, not the patent grant itself. ### **Section 69 — Public Inspection Rights** > [!NOTE] > **RIGHT TO INSPECT** > Section 69 grants **any member of the public** the right to inspect the Register of Patents at the Patent Office at any reasonable time during office hours, on payment of the prescribed fee. Certified copies of any entry may also be obtained on application. This provision embodies the **transparency principle** of the patent system and ensures that competitors, researchers, and potential licensees can verify the legal status of a patent. ## 2.2 Custodian Hierarchy ``` PATENT ADMINISTRATION STRUCTURE (INDIA) │ ├── DPIIT (Department for Promotion of Industry & Internal Trade) │ │ (Ministry-level oversight) │ ▼ └── CGPDTM (Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks) │ (Statutory Head) ▼ Patent Offices (Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai) │ ▼ Assistant Controllers / Controllers of Patents │ ▼ Examiners (Technical Experts) │ ▼ REGISTER OF PATENTS (Maintained at each Patent Office) ``` ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Section** | **Subject Matter** | **Statutory Effect** | **KTU Exam Weight** | |-------------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------------| | Sec. 67 | Maintenance of Register | Compulsory duty of CGPDTM | High | | Sec. 68 | Rectification of Register | Corrects errors (not validity) | Very High | | Sec. 69 | Public Inspection | Right to inspect + certified copies | High | | Sec. 72(1) | Presumption of Accuracy | Prima facie evidence in court | High | | Rule 91 | Form of Register | Prescribes tabular format | Medium | **Critical Time Limits & Legal Metrics:** | **Parameter** | **Statutory Value** | **Legal Basis** | |----------------|----------------------|------------------| | Term of Patent | **20 years** from date of filing | Section 53 | | Annual Renewal | Mandatory from **3rd year** onwards | Section 53(2) | | Working Statement (Form 27) | Due **within 6 months** of grant + every financial year | Section 146(2) | | Restoration Lapse Period | Within **18 months** from the date of cessation | Section 60 | | Inspection Fee | Prescribed under **Schedule I** (revised periodically) | Rule 91 | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility For an engineer working in **R&D, manufacturing, or technology transfer**, the Register of Patents is indispensable for: 1. **Freedom-to-Operate (FTO) Analysis** — verifying whether a proposed product/process infringes existing patents. 2. **Licensing & Technology Transfer** — confirming ownership before negotiating a licence. 3. **Prior Art Search** — identifying existing patents in a technology domain. 4. **Investment Due Diligence** — investors verify IP assets in a target company. 5. **Litigation Evidence** — certified copies from the Register are admissible as primary evidence. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: From Application to Register Entry The journey of an invention from conceptualization to entry in the Register of Patents follows a structured legal procedure. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step logical derivation: ### **Phase 1: Pre-Grant Stage** $$\text{Step 1: Inventor conceives invention}$$ This represents the cognitive origin point. The invention must satisfy the three statutory tests: novelty, inventive step, and industrial application (Section 2(1)(j)). $$\text{Step 2: Applicant files application under Sec. 7/54/135}$$ The application may be: - **Ordinary Application** (Section 7) — no priority claim - **Convention Application** (Section 135) — claiming priority under Paris Convention - **PCT National Phase Application** (Section 135, Rule 20) — international application entering India $$\text{Step 3: Filing receipt issued with Application Number}$$ The Patent Office assigns a unique application number (e.g., **202411001234**), which becomes the future identifier in the Register. ### **Phase 2: Examination Stage** $$\text{Step 4: Request for Examination under Sec. 11B}$$ Filed within **48 months** from the date of priority or filing, whichever is earlier. $$\text{Step 5: First Examination Report (FER) issued}$$ The examiner checks against Sections 2(1)(j), 2(1)(ja), 3, and 25 criteria. $$\text{Step 6: Response to FER under Sec. 14}$$ The applicant addresses objections, submits amended claims, or argues technical points within **6 months** (extendable up to **3 months** further under Rule 138). ### **Phase 3: Grant Stage** $$\text{Step 7: Decision to Grant (Sec. 43)}$$ If the Controller is satisfied, an order for grant is passed. $$\text{Step 8: Patent Number Assigned}$$ A unique patent number is generated (e.g., **IN 456789**). $$\text{Step 9: Publication in the Patent Journal}$ The grant is published in the **Weekly Patent Journal**, initiating the **opposition window under Section 25(2)**. ### **Phase 4: Entry into the Register** $$\text{Step 10: Controller causes entry under Sec. 67}$$ This is the **moment the patent enters the Register of Patents**. All statutory particulars (patentee details, term, conditions) are recorded. $$\text{Step 11: Patent Certificate issued under Rule 63}$$ The patentee receives the formal certificate, but **legal recognition** of the patent rights flows from the Register entry. ## 3.2 Rectification Procedure — Analytical Breakdown (Section 68) The process of rectifying an error in the Register follows a structured legal pathway: ``` INITIATION OF RECTIFICATION (Sec. 68) │ ├── Route A: SUO MOTU by Controller │ │ │ ▼ │ Notice issued to patentee │ │ │ ▼ │ Opportunity of hearing (Sec. 80) │ │ │ ▼ │ Order passed → Register corrected │ └── Route B: APPLICATION by aggrieved person │ ▼ Form 14 filed (prescribed format) │ ▼ Fee paid as per Schedule I │ ▼ Notice to patentee │ ▼ Hearing granted │ ▼ Controller's Order │ ├── Order for rectification → Register updated │ └── Order refusing rectification → Appeal under Sec. 117A ``` ## 3.3 Comparative Matrix — Section 68 vs. Section 84 | **Parameter** | **Section 68 (Rectification of Register)** | **Section 84 (Revocation of Patent)** | |----------------|--------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------| | **Purpose** | Correct errors in the register | Challenge the validity of the patent itself | | **Forum** | Controller of Patents | Controller of Patents / IPAB / High Court | | **Scope** | Clerical/manifest errors, wrong entries | Lack of novelty, non-patentability, non-disclosure, etc. | | **Effect** | Rectifies the entry only | Cancels the patent ab initio or partly | | **Time Limit** | No statutory bar | Must be filed **before the date of grant** for certain grounds (Sec. 25(1)) or post-grant under Sec. 84 | | **Parties** | Any aggrieved person / suo motu | Any interested person / Central Government | ## 3.4 Mathematical Representation of Patent Term in the Register The expiry date recorded in the Register is computed as: $$ T_{\text{expiry}} = T_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years} $$ Where: - $T_{\text{expiry}}$ = Date of patent expiration as entered in the Register - $T_{\text{filing}}$ = Date of filing of the application (or priority date for PCT/Convention) **Worked Example:** If a patent application was filed on **15 March 2010**, the entry in the Register would record the date of expiration as: $$ T_{\text{expiry}} = 15 \text{ March } 2010 + 20 \text{ years} = 15 \text{ March } 2030 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Note that the **patent may lapse earlier** due to non-payment of renewal fees (Section 53), in which case the Register will reflect the lapsed status and the restoration period (if applicable). **Renewal Fee Trigger Formula:** $$ \text{Renewal Due From Year} = 3^{\text{rd}} \text{ anniversary of filing date} $$ $$ \text{Renewal Amount} = f(\text{Year}) \quad \text{[Progressive slabs per Schedule I]} $$ # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Register of Patents Information Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Inventor / Applicant] -->|Files Application Sec 7/54/135| B[Patent Office Branch - Mumbai/Delhi/Kolkata/Chennai] B -->|Application Number Assigned| C[Examination Section] C -->|FER Issued Sec 11B| D[Response Stage Sec 14] D -->|Acceptance / Amended Claims| E[Controller Decision Sec 43] E -->|Grant Order| F[Publication in Patent Journal] F -->|Opposition Window Sec 25(2)| G{Opposition Filed?} G -->|No| H[Entry Made in Register of Patents Sec 67] G -->|Yes| I[Opposition Hearing] I -->|Opposition Dismissed| H H --> J[Register Entry Recorded] J --> K[Patent Certificate Issued Rule 63] K --> L[Annual Renewal Required from Year 3] L --> M{Renewal Paid?} M -->|Yes| N[Status: In Force] M -->|No within 6 months| O[Status: Lapsed - Restorable within 18 months Sec 60] O -->|Restored| N O -->|Not Restored| P[Status: Ceased Permanently] J --> Q[Rectification Application Sec 68] Q --> R[Suo Motu by Controller] R --> S[Hearing Sec 80] S --> T{Order} T -->|Rectified| U[Register Updated] T -->|Refused| V[Appeal to High Court Sec 117A] J --> W[Public Inspection Sec 69] W --> X[Certified Copy Issued] ``` ## 4.2 Logical Architecture — Contents of the Register of Patents ```mermaid flowchart LR REG[Register of Patents] REG --> A1[Patent Identification Block] REG --> A2[Patentee Particulars Block] REG --> A3[Legal Status Block] REG --> A4[Transactional History Block] A1 --> B1[Application Number] A1 --> B2[Patent Number] A1 --> B3[Filing Date] A1 --> B4[Priority Date] A1 --> B5[Date of Grant] A1 --> B6[Date of Expiration] A2 --> C1[Name of Patentee] A2 --> C2[Nationality] A2 --> C3[Address for Service] A2 --> C4[True and First Inventor Details] A3 --> D1[Conditions and Limitations] A3 --> D2[Status - In Force / Lapsed / Surrendered] A3 --> D3[Licences of Right Endorsement] A4 --> E1[Assignment Entries] A4 --> E2[Licence Entries] A4 --> E3[Mortgage and Charge Entries] A4 --> E4[Rectification Entries] A4 --> E5[Restoration Entries] A4 --> E6[Revocation Entries] ``` ## 4.3 Stakeholder Interaction Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Patentee] -->|Files| PO[Patent Office] PO -->|Maintains| REG[Register of Patents] REG -->|Inspects| PUB[Public / Competitors] REG -->|Inspects| COURT[Courts of Law] REG -->|Inspects| IPAB[IPAB - Pre-2021 / High Court Now] AGG[Aggrieved Person] -->|Rectification Sec 68| PO CG[Central Government] -->|Revocation Sec 84| IPAB CG -->|Working Direction Sec 146| PO REG -->|Prima Facie Evidence| COURT ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow — Rectification vs. Revocation Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Issue with Patent Entry Identified] --> Q1{Is it a clerical error
or wrong entry in Register?} Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Apply Sec 68 - Rectification] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it about the validity
of the patent itself?} Q2 -->|Yes - Lack of novelty, etc.| R2[Apply Sec 84 - Revocation] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is it post-grant opposition
within 1 year?} Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Apply Sec 25(2) - Post-Grant Opposition] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is it pre-grant opposition?} Q4 -->|Yes| R4[Apply Sec 25(1) - Pre-Grant Representation] Q4 -->|No| R5[Consult IP Attorney for Special Case] R1 --> OUT1[Controller corrects Register] R2 --> OUT2[Patent cancelled/partly cancelled] R3 --> OUT3[Patent revoked if opposition succeeds] R4 --> OUT4[Application refused if pre-grant rep succeeds] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **"What is the Register of Patents? Name the authority responsible for its maintenance under the Indian Patents Act, 1970."** **Model Answer:** The **Register of Patents** is the statutory record maintained under **Section 67** of the Patents Act, 1970, containing particulars of all patents granted in India, including patentee details, patent number, dates of filing, grant, and expiration, and subsequent entries relating to assignments, licences, mortgages, rectifications, etc. It is maintained by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** at the Patent Office headquarters and branches at Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, and Chennai. The Register may be inspected by any member of the public under **Section 69**. *(3 marks — Definition 1m, Authority 1m, Section Reference 1m)* --- ### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - December 2023]** **"Differentiate between rectification of the register under Section 68 and revocation of a patent under Section 84."** **Model Answer:** | **Section 68 — Rectification** | **Section 84 — Revocation** | |-------------------------------|------------------------------| | Corrects errors in the register | Cancels the patent itself | | Filed before the Controller | Filed before Controller/IPAB/High Court | | Limited to clerical mistakes/wrong entries | Based on substantial grounds (novelty, non-disclosure) | | Does not affect patent validity | Affects patent validity ab initio | | No specific time bar | Time bar applies under Sec. 25(1) pre-grant | *(3 marks — Two clear distinctions with examples)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Discuss the provisions relating to the maintenance of the Register of Patents under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. What particulars are recorded therein? **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the procedure for rectification of the Register under Section 68, highlighting the powers of the Controller and the grounds for such rectification. **[7 Marks]** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Maintenance of the Register of Patents:** The **Register of Patents** is the official statutory record mandated by **Section 67** of the Patents Act, 1970. It is maintained by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** at the Patent Office. The register is now maintained in both physical and electronic form, accessible through the IP India Online Portal. **Particulars recorded in the register:** 1. **Identification particulars** — Application number, patent number, title of the invention 2. **Date particulars** — Date of filing, priority date, date of grant, date of expiration 3. **Patentee particulars** — Name, address, nationality, details of true and first inventor 4. **Conditions and limitations** — Any subject to which the patent was granted 5. **Transactional entries** — Subsequent entries relating to assignment, transmission, licence, mortgage, charge, etc. 6. **Status entries** — Renewal status, lapsing, restoration, surrender, revocation **Statutory significance:** The register serves as prima facie evidence of all entries under **Section 72(1)**, and any person may inspect it under **Section 69**. *(Part a — 7 marks: 2 for maintenance, 3 for particulars, 2 for significance)* --- **(b) Rectification Procedure under Section 68:** Section 68 is the **correction mechanism** for the Register. It does not challenge the validity of the patent but only rectifies errors in the entry made therein. **Grounds for rectification:** 1. **Clerical or typographical error** in any entry 2. **Wrong entry** made in contravention of the Act or Rules 3. **Omission** of any required particular 4. **Entry reflecting incorrectly** any assignment, licence, or transmission **Procedure:** - **Step 1:** Application filed in **Form 14** with prescribed fee under Schedule I - **Step 2:** Notice issued to the patentee and other affected persons - **Step 3:** Opportunity of hearing afforded under **Section 80** - **Step 4:** Controller passes an order — either **directing rectification** or **refusing the application** - **Step 5:** If rectification is ordered, the Register is updated accordingly - **Step 6:** An appeal lies to the **High Court** under **Section 117A** within the prescribed time **Suo Motu Powers:** The Controller can also initiate rectification on his own motion after giving notice to the patentee. **Distinction from Revocation:** Rectification under Section 68 does not cancel a patent; it only corrects the record. Revocation under Section 84 affects the patent's substantive validity. *(Part b — 7 marks: 2 for grounds, 3 for procedure, 2 for distinction)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Choice [KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the legal presumption attached to entries in the Register of Patents. How does Section 69 facilitate public access? **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss the significance of the Register of Patents for engineering research, licensing, and litigation with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Legal Presumption and Public Access:** **Section 72(1) — Presumption of Accuracy:** The Indian Evidence Act, 1872, supplemented by Section 72(1) of the Patents Act, 1970, provides that a certified copy of any entry in the Register of Patents, certified by the Controller, shall be **admissible in evidence in all courts** without further proof. In any proceedings before the Controller or any court, the Register shall be **prima facie evidence** of the matters required to be entered therein. This means that a party claiming to be a patentee, assignee, or licensee need only produce the certified Register entry; the burden shifts to the opposing party to prove otherwise. **Section 69 — Public Inspection Rights:** Section 69 grants any person the right to inspect the Register of Patents at the Patent Office during working hours, on payment of the prescribed fee. Further, any person may apply for a certified copy of any entry in the register. This provision: - Promotes **transparency** in the patent system - Enables **Freedom-to-Operate (FTO)** analysis by competitors - Facilitates **due diligence** by investors and acquirers - Allows researchers to map **patent landscapes** in their domain *(Part a — 7 marks: 4 for presumption + 3 for inspection)* --- **(b) Significance for Engineering Research, Licensing, and Litigation:** **1. Engineering Research:** Before initiating R&D, engineers conduct **patentability searches** in the Register to assess existing prior art and avoid reinventing protected technologies. The Register also helps identify **white spaces** (unclaimed technology areas) for innovation. *Example:* A mechanical engineer designing a new hydraulic pump checks the Register to ensure the design does not infringe an existing patented pump mechanism. **2. Licensing and Technology Transfer:** Companies seeking to license patented technology verify the **ownership and legal status** of the patent through the Register. Licences granted by a non-recorded patentee may not bind subsequent assignees unless entered in the Register under **Section 69**. *Example:* A pharma firm licensing a drug formulation technology verifies that the licensor is the current registered patentee with all renewal fees paid. **3. Litigation and Evidence:** In infringement suits, certified copies of Register entries serve as **primary evidence** of patent rights. Courts rely on the Register for: - Confirming patent validity - Establishing chain of title - Verifying the scope of claims - Determining damages based on term remaining *Example:* In a patent infringement suit, the plaintiff produces a certified copy of the Register entry showing ownership, which the court treats as prima facie evidence under Section 72(1). **4. Strategic Significance:** The Register enables **portfolio valuation** by companies, supports **IP-backed financing** by banks, and aids **regulatory compliance** for products entering regulated markets. *(Part b — 7 marks: 2 marks each for three areas + 1 for strategic significance)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES IN REGISTER OF PATENTS QUESTIONS** > > 1. **Confusing Sec. 68 with Sec. 84:** Students frequently interchange rectification and revocation. The examiner awards zero marks if the wrong section is cited for the wrong purpose. Remember: **Sec. 68 corrects the Register; Sec. 84 cancels the Patent.** > > 2. **Forgetting Section 72(1):** The presumption of accuracy clause is a high-yield point that students often miss entirely. > > 3. **Missing the Authority:** Always mention **CGPDTM** and the **Patent Office** as the custodian — vague answers like "the government maintains it" lose marks. > > 4. **No mention of electronic Register:** Failing to mention the **IP India Online Portal** in the modern context costs marks under KTU 2024 scheme updates. > > 5. **Ignoring Rule 91:** The form and manner of the register is prescribed under Rule 91 of the Patents Rules, which students often forget. > > 6. **No mention of appeal rights:** Section 117A appeal rights to the High Court are frequently overlooked. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — REGISTER OF PATENTS** > > ✅ The Register is maintained under **Section 67** of the Patents Act, 1970. > > ✅ Custodian: **Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** at the Patent Office. > > ✅ The Register is maintained in **physical + electronic** form at the IP India Online Portal. > > ✅ **Section 68** deals with **Rectification** — corrects errors only, not patent validity. > > ✅ **Section 69** grants **public inspection rights** with prescribed fees. > > ✅ **Section 72(1)** provides **prima facie evidence** value to Register entries in courts. > > ✅ The Register records: **patent number, dates (filing/grant/expiry), patentee details, conditions, assignments, licences, mortgages, and status entries**. > > ✅ Patent term recorded: **20 years from date of filing** (Section 53). > > ✅ Renewal fees mandatory from **3rd year onwards**. > > ✅ Form of Register prescribed under **Rule 91** of Patents Rules, 2003. > > ✅ Rectification procedure uses **Form 14** with fee under Schedule I. > > ✅ Appeal against Controller's order under Section 68 lies to the **High Court under Section 117A**. > > ✅ Distinguished from **Section 84 Revocation**, which affects patent validity. > > ✅ Engineering utility: **FTO analysis, licensing, due diligence, prior art search, litigation evidence**. > > ✅ Key Sections to memorize: **67, 68, 69, 72(1), 117A** — these are **favourite KTU examiner picks**.

topics.title: Lapse and Restoration of Patents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lapse and Restoration of Patents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Lapse of a Patent** is the legal termination of the patent rights conferred upon a patentee, occurring primarily due to the failure to comply with statutory obligations, the most common being the **non-payment of prescribed renewal fees** within the time limit stipulated under the **Patents Act, 1970**.

**Restoration of a Patent** is the statutory mechanism by which a lapsed patent is revived and reinstated into force, upon application by the patentee, subject to the satisfaction of the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** that the failure to pay renewal fees was **unintentional** and that the restoration is otherwise just and proper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Anchor:** The provisions governing lapse and restoration are codified in **Sections 53, 54, 55, 60, 61, 62, and 63** of the Patents Act, 1970 (as amended). These are **high-frequency KTU examination areas**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **subscription to a premium streaming service** (think Netflix or Spotify Premium). 

- **Lapse** is like your subscription getting **auto-cancelled** because you forgot to pay the monthly bill on the due date. You lose access to all the premium content instantly.
- **Grace Period** is the few extra "courtesy days" the platform gives you after the due date to pay before the account is terminated.
- **Restoration** is the process where you call customer support, prove that the non-payment was **genuinely accidental** (e.g., bank failed, you were hospitalized), and ask them to **reactivate** your subscription.
- **Opposition** is when another user (in the case of patents, a competitor) tells the platform that you don't deserve reactivation because, say, they have already started using the content in the interim.

In patent law, the **"platform"** is the **Patent Office**, the **"premium content"** is the **20-year monopoly right**, and the **"customer support"** is the **Controller of Patents**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:** Lapse is **not the same** as **Revocation** (Section 64) or **Surrender** (Section 54). Lapse is an *automatic* consequence of non-compliance; Revocation is a *judicial/quasi-judicial* cancellation after hearing; Surrender is a *voluntary* act by the patentee.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terms

- **Term of Patent in India:** **20 years** from the date of filing.
- **Renewal Fee Cycle:** Due **annually** after the **2nd year** from the date of grant (not filing).
- **Grace Period for Renewal:** **6 months** after the due date (with surcharge).
- **Restoration Application Window:** **18 months** from the date of lapse.
- **Unintentional Lapse:** The statutory test — failure must be **inadvertent, not deliberate**.
- **Controller of Patents:** The quasi-judicial authority empowered to hear restoration petitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — Grounds for Lapse

A patent ceases to have effect (lapses) under the following circumstances:

1. **Non-Payment of Renewal Fees (Section 53(1)):** The most prevalent cause.
   - Renewal fees become due at the expiration of the **2nd year** from the **date of grant**.
   - If unpaid on the due date, a **grace period of 6 months** is allowed with surcharge.
   - Failure to pay even within the grace period → **automatic lapse** on the date the renewal was due.

2. **Failure to Meet Conditional Requirements (in foreign filings):** Under Section 8, lapses can occur if working statements or information are not furnished.

3. **Expiry of Term:** Upon completion of **20 years** from the date of filing (Section 53(2)).

4. **Voluntary Surrender (Section 54):** Patentee files a request for surrender; Controller may accept it if no third-party interests are prejudiced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 53(4) — The "Self-Evident Lapse" Rule:** Once a patent has lapsed, any person may enter a **caveat** in the register to prevent the patentee from obtaining restoration without notice to the caveator.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Restoration

**Why Restoration?**
- The patent system seeks to balance **public disclosure** with **private monopoly**.
- If a lapse was genuinely accidental (e.g., the patent agent missed the deadline, the patentee was incapacitated, the postal service failed), it would be unjust to permanently destroy a 20-year right that the patentee rightfully obtained.
- Restoration promotes **fairness** without harming the public interest (because opposition is permitted).

**How Restoration Works (Section 60 & 61):**
- The patentee (or his legal representative) must file a **restoration application** in **Form 15** within **18 months** from the date of lapse.
- The Controller publishes the application in the **Patent Office Journal** to invite opposition.
- Any "person interested" may file **notice of opposition** within **2 months** of publication.
- The Controller hears both sides and decides.
- **Test Applied:** Was the failure to pay **unintentional**? Is restoration **just and proper**?

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Statutory Basis** | **Time Limit** | **Condition / Test** | **Prescribed Form** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Lapse due to non-payment | Section 53(1) | After 6-month grace period | Renewal fee unpaid | N/A (automatic) |
| Grace period with surcharge | Section 53(3) read with Rule 80 | 6 months from renewal due date | Payment with surcharge | Form 4 / Form 5 |
| Surrender (voluntary lapse) | Section 54 | Anytime during patent term | No third-party prejudice | Form 14 |
| Restoration application | Section 60 | 18 months from date of lapse | Failure was unintentional | Form 15 |
| Notice of Opposition to Restoration | Section 61(1) | 2 months from publication | Person interested | Form 14 |
| Caveat against restoration | Section 53(4) | After lapse, before restoration | Person interested | Form 12 |
| Restoration fee | Rule 80(1A) | With Form 15 | Prescribed fee | As per Schedule I |
| Rights between lapse & restoration | Section 63 | Interregnum period | Marking right as restored | N/A |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

In the **pharmaceutical industry** (highly relevant to Kerala-based API manufacturers and KTU biotechnology graduates), a single lapsed patent can mean loss of a **multi-crore revenue stream**. Restoration petitions are routinely filed when:
- The patentee forgot the renewal due to corporate restructuring.
- The patent attorney missed the deadline due to non-payment of professional fees.
- Postal/courier delays caused the renewal fee cheque to arrive late.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** Engineers and scientists often invent. Understanding the renewal-fee cycle and the 18-month restoration window is critical for **startup founders** and **researchers commercializing inventions** in the Kerala innovation ecosystem (Kerala Start-up Mission, TBIs at CET/Model Engineering College).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Procedural Matrix

### 3.1 The Complete Procedural Derivation — From Lapse to Restoration

The lifecycle of a lapsed patent undergoing restoration can be analytically broken down into the following sequential steps:

**Step 1: Patent Grant and Term Initiation**
- Upon grant, the patent is published in the Patent Office Journal.
- The **monopoly term of 20 years** begins from the date of filing of the application (not grant).
- The first renewal fee becomes due at the expiration of the **2nd year from the date of grant**.

**Step 2: Renewal Cycle and Lapse Trigger**
- Annual renewal fees must be paid on or before each anniversary of the grant.
- If unpaid on the due date $D$, the patentee has a **grace period** up to date $D + 6 \text{ months}$.
- If still unpaid by $D + 6 \text{ months}$, the patent **lapses automatically** on the original due date $D$.

$$ \text{Date of Lapse} = D + 6 \text{ months (if not paid even in grace period)} $$

**Step 3: Caveat Window (Optional)**
- Any "person interested" may lodge a caveat under **Section 53(4)** to ensure they are heard if restoration is sought.

**Step 4: Restoration Application Filing**
- Patentee files **Form 15** with prescribed fee within **18 months** from date of lapse.
- Application must contain a **statement of circumstances** explaining why renewal was not paid.

$$ \text{Restoration Window} = [D + 6 \text{ months},\; D + 6 \text{ months} + 18 \text{ months}] $$

$$ \text{Available Window} = [D + 6m,\; D + 24m] $$

where $D$ = original renewal due date.

**Step 5: Publication in Journal**
- Controller publishes the application in the Patent Office Journal for **public notice**.

**Step 6: Opposition Phase**
- Any interested person files **notice of opposition in Form 14** within **2 months** of publication.
- Grounds typically include: lapse was deliberate, patentee did not act promptly, restoration would prejudice intervening rights.

**Step 7: Hearing and Disposition**
- Controller hears both patentee and opponent.
- Controller applies the **two-prong test**:
  1. Was the failure **unintentional**?
  2. Is restoration **just and proper**?

**Step 8: Order and Effect**
- If allowed: Patent is restored, and patentee must pay **all unpaid renewal fees** + restoration fee + surcharge.
- Patent takes effect as if it had never lapsed (**retroactive effect**), subject to protections for persons who used the invention in the interregnum (Section 63).

### 3.2 Mathematical Determination of the Restoration Window

To make the timeline absolutely explicit, consider a patent where the renewal fee became due on **1st January 2023**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Due Date } (D) &= \text{1st January 2023} \\
\text{Grace Period Ends} &= D + 6 \text{ months} = \text{1st July 2023} \\
\text{Lapse Date} &= \text{1st July 2023 (if unpaid)} \\
\text{Restoration Window Opens} &= \text{1st July 2023} \\
\text{Restoration Window Closes} &= \text{1st July 2023} + 18 \text{ months} = \text{1st January 2025}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The total gap between due date and final restoration deadline is 24 months** ($6 + 18$). Beyond **1st January 2025**, no restoration is possible — the patent is **permanently dead** and a fresh application would be required (which is barred for the same invention due to prior publication).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Lapse Mechanisms

| **Parameter** | **Lapse (Sec 53)** | **Surrender (Sec 54)** | **Revocation (Sec 64)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Trigger** | Non-payment of renewal fee | Voluntary request by patentee | Petition by any person interested |
| **Initiated by** | Operation of law | Patentee | Third party or Government |
| **Hearing required** | No (automatic) | Yes (Controller's discretion) | Yes (mandatory) |
| **Grounds** | Statutory default | No prejudice to third parties | Non-patentability, non-disclosure, etc. |
| **Reversible?** | Yes (restoration under Sec 60) | Generally no | No (except appeal to IPAB/High Court) |
| **Effect** | Patent ceases to be in force | Patent deemed never to have had effect | Patent deemed void ab initio |

### 3.4 Critical Pitfall: Section 63 — Intervening Rights

A subtle but **exam-favorite** nuance: between the date of lapse and the date of restoration, the patent is **not in force**. If a competitor starts using the invention during this interregnum, they may have built up certain rights. Section 63 protects such users by providing that:

- The restored patent **does not** extend to acts done by third parties between lapse and restoration, **unless** the use was in bad faith and with knowledge of the original patent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU Part B papers.** Memorize the interplay between Section 53 (lapse), Section 60 (restoration), and Section 63 (protection of intervening rights).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Lapse and Restoration of a Patent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patent Granted] --> B[Renewal Fee Due Annually]
    B --> C{Fee Paid on Due Date?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Patent Remains in Force]
    D --> B
    C -->|No| E[Grace Period: 6 Months]
    E --> F{Paid with Surcharge within 6 months?}
    F -->|Yes| D
    F -->|No| G[PATENT LAPSES on Original Due Date - Sec 53]
    G --> H{Any Person Interested?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Caveat Filed - Sec 53(4)]
    H -->|No| J
    I --> J
    J{Patentee Acts within 18 months?}
    J -->|No| K[Patent Permanently Dead]
    J -->|Yes| L[File Form 15 for Restoration - Sec 60]
    L --> M[Controller Publishes in Journal]
    M --> N{Opposition Filed in 2 months?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Controller Hearing]
    N -->|No| P[Controller Considers Ex Parte]
    O --> Q{Failure Unintentional and Just?}
    P --> Q
    Q -->|Yes| R[Order of Restoration - Sec 61]
    Q -->|No| S[Application Rejected]
    R --> T[Patentee Pays Unpaid Renewal Fees + Surcharge]
    T --> U[Patent Restored with Retroactive Effect]
    U --> V[Sec 63 Protects Intervening Third-Party Rights]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stakeholders and Interfaces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patentee] -->|Form 15 Restoration Application| B[Controller of Patents]
    B -->|Publication in Journal| C[General Public]
    C -->|Opposition in Form 14| B
    B -->|Notice of Hearing| A
    B -->|Restoration Order| A
    A -->|Pays Outstanding Renewal Fees + Surcharge| D[Patent Office Accounts]
    B -->|Status Update| E[Register of Patents - Sec 67]
    E -->|Public Inspection| C
    F[Person Interested] -->|Caveat under Sec 53(4)| B
    F -->|Notice of Opposition| B
    G[Intervening User] -->|Claims Protection under Sec 63| B
```

### 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree — Lapse vs. Surrender vs. Revocation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Patent Status at Risk] --> Q1{Cause of Termination?}
    Q1 -->|Non-payment of fee| L[LAPSE - Sec 53]
    Q1 -->|Patentee's own request| S[SURRENDER - Sec 54]
    Q1 -->|Third-party petition| R[REVOCATION - Sec 64]
    L --> L1[Restorable within 18 months - Sec 60]
    L1 --> L2[Restoration via Form 15]
    S --> S1[Controller Hearing for Public Interest]
    R --> R1[IPAB / High Court Appeal Possible]
    L2 --> End[Resolution]
    S1 --> End
    R1 --> End
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the term "Lapse of Patent" and state the statutory provision governing it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Lapse of a patent refers to the cessation of the statutory monopoly rights conferred by a patent, occurring automatically upon the **non-payment of the prescribed renewal fee** within the stipulated time, including the grace period. It is governed by **Section 53 of the Patents Act, 1970**. Under this provision, once the renewal fee (which becomes due annually after the 2nd year from the date of grant) remains unpaid beyond the **6-month grace period**, the patent is deemed to have lapsed on the original due date. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the time limit within which a patentee must apply for restoration of a lapsed patent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Under **Section 60 of the Patents Act, 1970**, an application for the restoration of a lapsed patent must be made by the patentee or his legal representative within a period of **18 months from the date on which the patent lapsed**. The application must be filed in **Form 15** along with the prescribed fee, and the patentee must satisfy the Controller that the failure to pay the renewal fee was **unintentional**. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various **grounds and circumstances** under which a patent may lapse under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Discuss the role of the grace period in determining the date of lapse.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Critically analyze the **procedure for restoration of a lapsed patent** under Sections 60 and 61 of the Patents Act, 1970. What is the test applied by the Controller, and what is the effect of restoration on the rights of the patentee and third parties?

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Model Solution:**

A patent may lapse under the following circumstances under the Patents Act, 1970:

**1. Non-Payment of Renewal Fees (Section 53(1)):** This is the most common ground. The patentee is required to pay renewal fees annually after the 2nd year from the date of grant. (2 marks)

**2. Grace Period (Section 53(3)):** If the renewal fee is not paid on the due date, the patentee is granted a **6-month grace period** during which the fee may be paid along with a prescribed surcharge. (2 marks)

**3. Determination of Date of Lapse:** If the fee is not paid even within the grace period, the patent is deemed to have lapsed on the **original due date** (not on the date the grace period expired). This retroactivity is crucial because it determines the window for restoration applications. (2 marks)

**4. Caveat by Interested Parties (Section 53(4)):** Once the patent lapses, any person interested may file a caveat to ensure they receive notice if the patentee seeks restoration. (1 mark)

**(b) Model Solution:**

The procedure for restoration is statutorily prescribed under Sections 60 and 61:

**1. Eligibility and Time Limit:** Only the patentee or his legal representative can apply, and the application must be filed within **18 months from the date of lapse** (Section 60). (2 marks)

**2. Form and Fee:** The application is made in **Form 15** with the prescribed fee, accompanied by a **statement of circumstances** explaining the unintentional failure. (1 mark)

**3. Publication and Opposition:** The Controller publishes the application in the Patent Office Journal, inviting opposition. Any interested person may oppose within **2 months**. (1 mark)

**4. The Unintentionality Test:** The Controller applies a subjective test — was the failure to pay **inadvertent or accidental**? Judicial precedents (e.g., *Nitin Prasad v. Controller*, 2003) establish that mere negligence may still qualify as unintentional. (2 marks)

**5. Effect of Restoration:** Upon restoration, the patent takes effect as if it had never lapsed. However, **Section 63** protects persons who used the invention in good faith during the interregnum — their acts do not constitute infringement. (1 mark)

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Lapse, Surrender, and Revocation** of a patent with reference to the relevant statutory provisions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **rights of intervening third parties** under Section 63 of the Patents Act, 1970, when a lapsed patent is subsequently restored.

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Model Solution:**

| **Basis** | **Lapse (Sec 53)** | **Surrender (Sec 54)** | **Revocation (Sec 64)** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Nature | Automatic, by operation of law | Voluntary act of patentee | Quasi-judicial, on petition |
| Initiator | Statute | Patentee | Any person interested / Central Government |
| Hearing | Not required | Discretionary of Controller | Mandatory |
| Reversible? | Yes (Sec 60) | Generally no | No (except appeal) |
| Effect | Patent ceases from lapse date | Patent deemed void from date of surrender | Patent void ab initio (from filing) |

(7 marks — 1 mark for each point of comparison × 5 rows, with 2 marks for additional statutory analysis)

**(b) Model Solution:**

Section 63 addresses the rights of persons who used the invention during the period between lapse and restoration.

**1. General Rule:** Where a patent is restored, the patent is deemed to have been continuously in force from the date of grant. (2 marks)

**2. Protection of Intervening Users:** However, this does not prejudice the rights of any person who, in good faith and without knowledge of the lapse, used the invention during the interregnum. Such use does **not** amount to infringement after restoration. (3 marks)

**3. Bad Faith Exception:** If the third party knew of the original patent AND used it in bad faith, the restored patent can be enforced against them. (2 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Lapse & Restoration Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "date of grant" with "date of filing"** — Renewal fees are calculated from the **date of grant**, but the patent term is calculated from the **date of filing**. Students often mix these up and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the retroactive nature of lapse** — The patent lapses on the *original due date*, not on the date the grace period expires. This is critical for restoration window calculation.
> 3. **Omitting Section 63 protection** — In restoration questions, students often describe only the patentee's rights and forget the protection afforded to intervening third-party users. This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in 14-mark answers.
> 4. **Writing "Form 5" instead of "Form 15"** — For restoration, the correct form is **Form 15**. For opposition to restoration, the correct form is **Form 14**. Mixing these up is a classic error.
> 5. **Confusing Restoration (Sec 60) with Revocation (Sec 64)** — These are conceptually opposite actions. Restoration *revives* a dead patent; Revocation *kills* a live patent.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lapse is automatic** upon non-payment of renewal fees after the **6-month grace period** (Section 53).
- **Renewal fees** are due **annually from the 2nd year of grant** (not filing).
- **Restoration application** must be filed within **18 months** of lapse in **Form 15** (Section 60).
- The Controller applies the **"unintentional failure" test** — a subjective, fact-specific inquiry.
- **Opposition to restoration** must be filed within **2 months** of publication of the application.
- **Section 63** protects intervening third-party users in good faith — this is the *safety valve* that makes restoration equitable.
- A **caveat** can be filed by any interested person after lapse to ensure notice of any restoration application (Section 53(4)).
- **Surrender (Sec 54)** and **Revocation (Sec 64)** are distinct from Lapse — do not conflate.
- The total **lapse-to-deadline** period is **24 months** (6 + 18).
- After the restoration window closes, the patent is **permanently dead**, and the invention enters the public domain.
- Restoration has **retroactive effect** — the patent is treated as if it had never lapsed.
- The **Controller of Patents** is the quasi-judicial authority for all restoration proceedings.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compulsory Licence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compulsory Licence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Compulsory Licence** is a statutory authorization granted by a competent governmental authority (such as the Controller General of Patents in India) that permits a third party to manufacture, use, sell, or import a patented invention **without the express consent of the patent holder**, subject to the payment of adequate royalty/remuneration as determined by the authority. It is a non-voluntary, state-imposed exception to the exclusive rights normally vested in a patentee under the patent law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** A compulsory licence is a legally binding mechanism that breaks the monopoly of a patent owner, allowing another party to produce the patented product or use the patented process under specified conditions laid down in the statute, primarily to safeguard public interest, prevent abuse of patent rights, and promote access to essential goods (especially pharmaceuticals).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small village where only one well exists, and the well's owner suddenly stops giving water to anyone (maybe he goes on holiday or hikes the price to ₹1 lakh per bucket). The village panchayat (the government) cannot let people die of thirst. So, the panchayat passes an order: *"Anyone can draw water from the well, but they must pay the owner a fair, fixed price per bucket decided by the panchayat."*

**Mapping to Compulsory Licence:**
- **The Well** = The Patented Invention (e.g., a life-saving drug).
- **The Well Owner** = The Patent Holder (e.g., a pharmaceutical company like Bayer).
- **The Panchayat's Order** = The Compulsory Licence granted by the Patent Controller.
- **Fair Price per Bucket** = Statutory Royalty paid to the patentee.

The owner still gets paid (his IP is respected), but he **cannot** refuse access. This balances **private innovation incentives** with **public welfare**.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics (Bold Highlights)

- **Non-Voluntary** – Granted without the patentee's consent.
- **Statutory** – Rooted in legal provisions (Sections 82–94 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970).
- **Remuneration-Bearing** – The licensee must pay royalty (often 4%–6% of net sales, subject to case facts).
- **Purpose-Limited** – Predominantly aims to address **public health crises**, **non-working of patents**, and **anti-competitive practices**.
- **TRIPS-Compliant** – Recognized under **Article 31** of the TRIPS Agreement, 1994.
- **Territorial** – Valid only within the jurisdiction of the granting country.
- **Revocable/Condition-Based** – Can be cancelled if the grounds cease to exist.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Spotlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, students must understand the **grounds** (Section 84), the **procedure** (Section 87), the **TRIPS flexibilities** (Doha Declaration, 2001), and the **landmark Indian case – Natco Pharma v. Bayer Corporation (2012)** which is a permanent high-yield question in university exams.

### 1.4 Source of Legal Authority

| Statute / International Instrument | Relevant Provision |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Indian Patents Act, 1970** | Sections 82, 83, 84, 85, 86, 87, 88, 89, 90, 91, 92, 92A, 94 |
| **TRIPS Agreement, 1994 (WTO)** | Article 31 (Other Use Without Authorization) |
| **Doha Declaration, 2001** | Paragraphs 4, 5, 6, 7 (TRIPS & Public Health) |
| **Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005** | Inserted Sections 92, 92A (pharma-specific) |
| **TRIPS Waiver Decision, 2003** | Para 6 system for exporting generic drugs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Philosophical Pillars of Compulsory Licensing

The entire doctrine rests on a delicate trinity of policy objectives codified in **Section 83** of the Indian Patents Act (the "Statement of Objects"):

1. **Pillar I – Public Interest Protection:** Patents are granted to *encourage* invention, but they must not become instruments to *deny* essential goods to the public.
2. **Pillar II – Reward for Invention:** Inventors deserve a fair return, but the reward must be balanced against the socio-economic welfare of the nation.
