Strengthening Patent Agent infrastructure is essential for India to become an IP-driven economy.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating Patent Agent with Patent Attorney** — they are NOT the same. Patent Attorney = court practice; Patent Agent = office practice.
> 2. **Forgetting Section numbers** — always cite Section 126, 127, 128, 129, 130 of the Patents Act, 1970.
> 3. **Missing the procedural safeguards** in removal proceedings (Section 129) — natural justice must be mentioned.
> 4. **Writing "anyone" can file a patent** — this is wrong. Only the applicant himself or a **registered** Patent Agent can file on behalf of another person.
> 5. **Omitting the role of CGPDTM** — the Controller General is the apex authority over Patent Agents.
> 6. **Confusing 21 years with 18 years** — 21 years is the age threshold (Section 127).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Patent Agent** is registered under the **Patents Act, 1970**, Sections 126–131.
- ✅ Must be an **Indian citizen**, **21+ years**, with a **science/engineering/technology degree**, and must pass the **Patent Agent Examination**.
- ✅ Registration is made in **Form 22** under Rule 110, with the **Controller General of Patents** as the registering authority.
- ✅ **Section 128** confers rights: right of audience, right to file, right to receive notices, right to appeal.
- ✅ **Section 130** + Rule 117 prescribe **duties and code of conduct**.
- ✅ **Section 129** lists **six grounds** for removal from the register (moral turpitude, misconduct, insolvency, fraud, foreign citizenship, bringing disrepute).
- ✅ Patent Agent ≠ Patent Attorney — Patent Agents **cannot** represent clients in court cases.
- ✅ The **Patent Agent Examination** is held **twice a year** by the CGPDTM.
- ✅ Record retention obligation: **minimum 6 years**.
- ✅ Patent Agents are essential for **drafting, prosecution, maintenance**, and **commercialization** of patents.
- ✅ Always cite the **statutory section** along with your answer to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Copyright Act 1957
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Copyright Act 1957

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: COPYRIGHT ACT, 1957

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Copyright Act, 1957** is the primary legislation of the Republic of India that governs the protection, regulation, and enforcement of copyright in literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works, cinematograph films, and sound recordings. It came into force on **21 January 1958** and has undergone several amendments, the most significant being the **Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012** (which introduced provisions for digital rights management, internet service provider liability, and safe harbour rules) and the **Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2014**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Definition (Section 2 of the Act):**
> *"Copyright means the exclusive right, by virtue of, and subject to the provisions of, this Act, to do or authorise the doing of any of the following acts in respect of a work or any substantial part thereof, namely:— (a) to reproduce the work; (b) to issue copies of the work to the public...; (c) to perform the work in public...; (d) to make any cinematograph film or sound recording in respect of the work; (e) to make any translation or adaptation of the work; (f) to do, in relation to a translation or adaptation of the work, any of the acts specified in relation to the work in clauses (a) to (e)."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Creator's Invisible Fence"

Imagine you are a novelist who has just completed a manuscript after three years of effort. The moment the ink dries on the final page, the **Copyright Act, 1957** acts like an **invisible electric fence** surrounding your book. This fence:

1. **Automatically activates** the moment the work is created (you don't have to register to get copyright).
2. **Prevents strangers** from copying, selling, or translating your book without permission.
3. **Lasts for a fixed period** — typically **60 years** after the author's death for most works.
4. **Allows you to license** usage (e.g., to a publisher) for money, like renting out a portion of your property.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Copyright is a **negative right** — it does not give the author the positive right to use the work, but rather the right to **stop others** from using it in the ways specified by the Act.

### 1.3 Salient Features of the Act

- **Automatic Protection:** Copyright arises on creation; registration under **Section 44** is voluntary but serves as prima facie evidence in court.
- **Sui Generis Protection:** The Act is self-contained and provides a unique system of rights not derived from any other statute.
- **Dual Protection:** Protects both **economic rights** (reproduction, distribution, public performance) and **moral rights** (right to paternity and integrity).
- **Statutory Bodies:** Establishes the **Copyright Office** (Section 9) and the **Copyright Board** (Section 11) for administration and dispute resolution.
- **International Conformity:** Aligned with the **Berne Convention (1886)**, **Universal Copyright Convention (1952)**, and **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**.
- **Anti-Piracy Provisions:** Section 65 prescribes **criminal penalties** for knowingly using pirated copies for trade.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Always remember the magic number — **60 years**. For literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works, copyright subsists for the **life of the author + 60 years** post-mortem. For cinematograph films, sound recordings, photographs, and posthumous publications, the term is **60 years from the date of publication**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Subject Matter of Copyright (Section 13)

Section 13 of the Act enumerates the **eight categories** of works in which copyright subsists:

1. **Original literary works** (includes computer programs, tables, compilations — per Amendment 1999)
2. **Original dramatic works**
3. **Original musical works**
4. **Original artistic works**
5. **Cinematograph films**
6. **Sound recordings**

> [!NOTE]
> The 2012 Amendment added the words *"or any substantial part thereof"* in Section 14, meaning even **substantial copying** (not the entire work) is infringement. This aligns Indian law with international standards.

### 2.2 The "Bundle of Rights" Doctrine

Copyright is not a single right but a **bundle of exclusive rights** vested in the copyright owner. These are codified under **Section 14(1)** and vary by work category:

| **Category of Work** | **Exclusive Rights Granted (Section 14)** |
|---|---|
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical Works | (a) Reproduce; (b) Issue copies to public; (c) Perform in public; (d) Communicate to public; (e) Make cinematograph film or sound recording; (f) Make translation/adaptation; (g) Do any of the above in respect of translation/adaptation |
| Artistic Works | (a) Reproduce; (b) Communicate to public; (c) Make cinematograph film/photograph; (d) Make adaptation |
| Cinematograph Films | (a) Make copies; (b) Issue copies to public; (c) Perform in public; (d) Communicate to public; (e) Make adaptation |
| Sound Recordings | (a) Make records; (b) Communicate to public; (c) Sell or hire; (d) Make adaptation |

### 2.3 Term of Copyright — The "60-Year Rule"

| **Type of Work** | **Term of Protection (Section 22–25)** |
|---|---|
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic (published) | Life of the author + 60 years |
| Anonymous or Pseudonymous works | 60 years from year of publication (or 60 years from year of making known to public, whichever is shorter) |
| Posthumous works | 60 years from year of first publication |
| Cinematograph Films | 60 years from year of first publication |
| Sound Recordings | 60 years from year of first publication |
| Government works | 60 years from year of first publication |
| Works of International Organisations | 60 years from year of first publication |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note (2012 Amendment):** For sound recordings, the term was changed from "60 years from the date of recording" to **"60 years from the date of first publication"** to remove a pending WTO dispute with the European Union.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Copyright Term Calculation

$$
T_{\text{copyright}} = T_{\text{author's life}} + 60 \text{ years post-mortem}
$$

For published works where the author's death is not the trigger:
$$
T_{\text{copyright}} = T_{\text{first publication}} + 60 \text{ years}
$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- For **anonymous/pseudonymous** works: Term = $\min(60 \text{ years from publication}, 60 \text{ years from disclosure})$.
- For **joint authorship**: Term is calculated from the death of the **last surviving author**.

### 2.5 Ownership of Copyright (Section 17)

The general rule is that the **author is the first owner** of copyright. However, several exceptions exist:

- **Employee's work** (made in the course of employment under a contract of service) → Employer owns copyright.
- **Work made for hire** (paid by another person for a valuable consideration) → The payer owns copyright in the absence of an agreement to the contrary.
- **Photograph** (commissioned for a private or domestic purpose) → The person commissioning owns copyright.
- **Artistic work / engraving / photograph** (made in the course of employment in a newspaper/magazine) → The proprietor (employer) owns copyright.
- **Work of a Government Undertaking** → The Government owns copyright.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Utility in Engineering:** Software developers (computer programmers) employed by IT companies are a classic case of **Section 17(c)** — the company owns the copyright in the code unless a contract specifies otherwise. This is a frequent KTU viva question.

### 2.6 Assignment and Licensing (Sections 18–19, 30)

- **Assignment** must be in writing, signed by the assignor, and must specify the **rights assigned, duration, territory, and consideration**. A work of future copyright may also be assigned (Section 18(1)).
- **Licences** can be **voluntary** (granted by the owner) or **compulsory** (granted by the Copyright Board for published works withheld from public, or for translation under Section 32).
- **Compulsory Licence for Translation** (Section 32): If the owner refuses to grant a translation licence or the work is not translated into any language in use in India within specified time, any person may apply to the Copyright Board after **7 years** of first publication.

### 2.7 Moral Rights (Section 57)

These are **non-economic, personal rights** of the author that **cannot be assigned** but can be waived:

- **Right of Paternity (Section 57(a)):** The right to claim authorship of the work and to prevent others from attributing it to someone else.
- **Right of Integrity (Section 57(b)):** The right to prevent distortion, mutilation, modification, or other derogatory action in relation to the work that would be prejudicial to the author's honour or reputation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Law (KTU Favourite):** In *Mannu Bhandari v. Kala Vikas Pictures* (AIR 1987 Del 13), the Delhi High Court held that distortion of a literary work in a film adaptation violated the author's moral right of integrity.

### 2.8 Copyright Infringement (Section 51)

A copyright is infringed when a person, **without a licence from the owner or the Copyright Board**:

1. Does any act that the owner has the exclusive right to do (Section 51(a)(i)).
2. Permits for profit any place to be used for the performance of a work in public without consent (Section 51(a)(ii)).
3. Imports infringing copies into India (Section 51(a)(iii)).
4. Makes or sells infringing articles (Section 51(a)(iv)).
5. Permits the use of a place for recording in public without consent (Section 51(b)).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept of "Substantial Part":** Under the 2012 Amendment, infringement includes taking a *"substantial part"* of the work. The test is **qualitative, not quantitative** — copying the *"heart"* of the work is sufficient. The landmark case *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak* (2008) established that the **threshold of similarity test** applies.

### 2.9 Remedies for Infringement

| **Remedy Type** | **Legal Provision** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| **Civil Remedies** | Sections 55–62 | Injunctions, damages, account of profits, delivery of infringing copies |
| **Criminal Remedies** | Sections 63–70 | Imprisonment (6 months to 3 years), fine (₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000), seizure of copies |
| **Administrative Remedies** | Sections 53–54 | Detention of infringing goods by Customs at the port |
| **Anton Piller Order** | UK-derived civil law | Court-appointed search and seizure of infringing materials |

> [!TIP]
> **Punishment for First Offence (Section 63):** Minimum **6 months imprisonment** (may extend to 3 years) and fine ranging from **₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000**. For second/subsequent offences, the term is enhanced to a minimum of 1 year.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Infringement Determination Framework

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Determining Infringement

Since copyright law is not mathematical, the "derivation" here is the **step-by-step legal test** a court applies to determine infringement. This is the analytical framework expected in KTU long-answer questions.

$$
\boxed{\text{Infringement Test} = \text{Access} + \text{Substantial Similarity} - \text{Defence(s)}}
$$

**Step-by-step application:**

1. **Establish ownership of copyright** in the plaintiff's work (proof of authorship, date of creation, or registration certificate under Section 48).
2. **Demonstrate the work was copied** (burden shifts to the defendant if the plaintiff shows copying from a pre-existing work).
3. **Show "substantial part" copying** — both **quantitative** and **qualitative** aspects evaluated. The *Ladbroke (William Hill) Ltd v. William Hill (Football) Ltd* (1964) test states: *"What is worth copying is prima facie worth protecting."*
4. **Rule out defences** — fair dealing (Section 52), independent creation, de minimis use, statutory licence.
5. **Quantify damages** based on actual loss + account of profits, or apply statutory damages.

### 3.2 Fair Dealing Doctrine (Section 52)

Section 52 enumerates a **closed list of permitted acts** that do NOT constitute infringement. The KTU-favoured categories are:

| **Permitted Act** | **Purpose/Scope** |
|---|---|
| Private use (52(a)) | Personal study, research, criticism, review, news reporting, judicial proceedings, etc. |
| Criticism or Review (52(1)(a)) | Must mention the author and source; the part used must be relevant to the criticism |
| News Reporting (52(1)(b)) | The amount used must be reasonable and necessary |
| Judicial Proceedings (52(1)(c)) | Use in court or before a tribunal |
| Quotation (52(1)(h)) | From a published work for legitimate purpose, with acknowledgement |
| Libraries and Educational Institutions (52(1)(a)/(n)) | Reproduction for non-commercial research |
| Sound recording for utility articles (52(1)(y)) | Spoken words, sermons, lectures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Fair dealing is like the **"fair use exception"** in US law (Section 107 of US Copyright Act). In India, however, it is a **strict statutory list**, not a flexible four-factor test. Any use outside the enumerated list is infringement.

### 3.3 Registration of Copyright (Section 44–50A)

While copyright exists independently of registration, the procedure is:

1. **Application to the Registrar of Copyrights** in the prescribed form (Form XIV under the Copyright Rules, 2013).
2. **Submission of work**: Three copies of the work, along with prescribed fees (currently ₹500 per work, plus ₹10 per page beyond 50 pages).
3. **Advertisement in newspapers** for 30 days inviting objections.
4. **Examination by the Registrar** and entry in the Register of Copyrights.
5. **Certificate of Registration** issued under Section 48 — this serves as **prima facie evidence** in court proceedings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often say that registration creates copyright. **This is WRONG.** Copyright exists on creation; registration merely creates a presumption of ownership.

### 3.4 International Copyright Protection (Sections 39–42A)

The Act provides for **automatic reciprocal protection** in India for works originating from:

- **Berne Convention countries** (164+ member countries)
- **WTO/TRIPS member countries** (164+ member countries)
- **Universal Copyright Convention countries**

Conditions:
1. The work must qualify for protection in the country of origin.
2. The work must not have exceeded its term in the country of origin.
3. The author must be a national of a convention country, OR the work must be first published in a convention country, OR the work must be simultaneously published in India and a convention country.
4. The notice *"©, Year, Author's Name"* is no longer mandatory since 2014 Amendment (was previously required under the UCC).

### 3.5 The Copyright Board (Section 11–35)

Composition: A **Chairperson** and **two members** appointed by the Central Government.

Jurisdiction (Section 22 of Copyright Rules, 2013):
1. Disputes regarding assignment of copyright (Section 19A).
2. Compulsory licensing matters (Sections 31, 31A, 31B, 32, 32A, 33).
3. Rectification of the Register of Copyrights (Section 50).
4. Approving licensing schemes for broadcasting organisations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The Copyright Rules, 2013 replaced the Copyright Rules, 1958, consolidating procedural aspects. The Board functions as a quasi-judicial authority.

### 3.6 Remedies — Civil Suit Procedure (Order XXXIX of CPC, 1908)

For civil infringement suits, the plaintiff can seek:

$$
\text{Relief} = \text{Injunction (Temporary} + \text{Permanent)} + \text{Damages} + \text{Accounts} + \text{Delivery Up}
$$

- **Anton Piller Order:** A court order allowing the plaintiff (with witnesses) to enter the defendant's premises and seize infringing material.
- **Mareva Injunction:** Freezing of the defendant's assets to prevent them from dissipating the same.
- **John Doe Order (Ashok Kumar Order):** Injunction against *unknown infringers*, commonly used in anti-piracy cases against anonymous online infringers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Questions on **anti-piracy provisions** and the role of **Information Technology Act, 2000 (Sections 65, 67)** in conjunction with the Copyright Act are increasingly common.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Rights Under Copyright Act, 1957

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Copyright Act 1957]
    rootA --> nodeB[Works Protected Section 13]
    rootA --> nodeC[Rights Granted Section 14]
    rootA --> nodeD[Term Section 22 to 25]
    rootA --> nodeE[Infringement Section 51]
    rootA --> nodeF[Remedies Sections 55 to 70]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Literary]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Dramatic]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Musical]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Artistic]
    nodeB --> nodeB5[Cinematograph]
    nodeB --> nodeB6[Sound Recording]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Economic Rights]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Moral Rights Section 57]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a[Reproduction]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b[Distribution]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1c[Public Performance]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1d[Communication to Public]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1e[Translation and Adaptation]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Life plus 60 years]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[60 years from Publication]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[60 years from Making]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Direct Infringement]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Indirect Infringement]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Import of Infringing Copies]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Civil]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Criminal]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[Customs]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1a[Injunction]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1b[Damages]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1c[Account of Profits]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF1d[Delivery Up]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF2a[Imprisonment 6 months to 3 years]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF2b[Fine 50000 to 200000]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF2c[Seizure of Copies]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Copyright Infringement Analysis Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Suspected Infringement Case] --> q1{Is there a copyrightable work?}
    q1 -->|No| result1[No Copyright Exists]
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Did defendant have access to work?}
    q2 -->|No| result2[Possibly Independent Creation]
    q2 -->|Yes| q3{Is there substantial similarity?}
    q3 -->|No| result3[No Infringement]
    q3 -->|Yes| q4{Is there a valid defence under Section 52?}
    q4 -->|Yes Fair Dealing| result4[Defence Established]
    q4 -->|No| q5[Infringement Established]
    q5 --> remedy[Apply Remedies]
    remedy --> rc1[Civil Action under Section 55]
    remedy --> rc2[Criminal Prosecution under Section 63]
    remedy --> rc3[Customs Detention under Section 53]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Copyright Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    phase1[Creation of Work] --> phase2[Automatic Copyright]
    phase2 --> phase3[Optional Registration]
    phase3 --> phase4[Commercial Exploitation]
    phase4 --> phase5[Licensing or Assignment]
    phase5 --> phase6[Infringement Monitoring]
    phase6 --> phase7[Remedies and Enforcement]
    phase7 --> phase8[End of Term 60 Years]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For diagram questions, the examiner often asks to draw the **"Rights of a Copyright Owner"** or the **"Hierarchy of Remedies"**. Memorise the bifurcation into **Economic + Moral Rights** and **Civil + Criminal + Administrative** remedies.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define 'Copyright' under the Copyright Act, 1957. Mention any two works in which copyright subsists. **[3 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Copyright is defined under **Section 14** of the Copyright Act, 1957 as the **exclusive right** to do or authorise the doing of any of the following acts in respect of a work or any substantial part thereof:

(a) to reproduce the work;
(b) to issue copies of the work to the public not being copies already in circulation;
(c) to perform the work in public, or communicate it to the public;
(d) to make any cinematograph film or sound recording in respect of the work;
(e) to make any translation or adaptation of the work.

**Two works in which copyright subsists (Section 13):**

1. **Original literary works** — including computer programs, tables, compilations, and databases.
2. **Cinematograph films** — including any work of visual recording on any medium produced through a process from which a moving image may be produced.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently list "book" as a literary work. The correct term is **"literary work"** — the scope is much broader, encompassing computer programs, tables, and even written directions. Lose **0.5 mark** for imprecise terminology.

---

#### Question 2: What is the term of copyright for (a) literary works, and (b) sound recordings under the Copyright Act, 1957? **[3 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Literary Works (Section 22):** Copyright subsists for the **lifetime of the author plus 60 years** computed from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year in which the author dies.

**(b) Sound Recordings (Section 25):** Copyright subsists for **60 years from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year in which the sound recording is first published**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "60 years from the date of recording" for sound recordings. This is **outdated** — the Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012 changed it to "60 years from first publication." Lose **1 mark** for using the older version.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

##### (a) Explain the various rights conferred on the copyright owner under the Copyright Act, 1957. Distinguish between Economic Rights and Moral Rights. **[7 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The Copyright Act, 1957 grants the copyright owner a **bundle of rights** under Section 14. These are classified into **Economic Rights** and **Moral Rights**.

**1. Economic Rights (Section 14(1)):**

These are commercial, assignable rights that allow the owner to derive economic benefit:

| **Right** | **Scope** |
|---|---|
| Right to Reproduce | Make copies of the work in any material form |
| Right to Issue Copies | Distribute copies to the public (incl. by way of rental or lending, after 2012 Amendment) |
| Right to Perform in Public | For literary, dramatic, musical works |
| Right to Communicate to Public | Includes broadcasting, satellite transmission, internet (post-2012 Amendment) |
| Right to Make Cinematograph Film/Sound Recording | Adapt the work into another medium |
| Right to Make Translations and Adaptations | Includes condensing, abridging, transforming the work |

**2. Moral Rights (Section 57):**

These are **personal, non-economic, non-assignable** rights of the author:

- **Right of Paternity (57(a)):** The right to claim authorship of the work and to prevent false attribution.
- **Right of Integrity (57(b)):** The right to restrain or claim damages in respect of any distortion, mutilation, or modification of the work that is prejudicial to the author's honour or reputation.

**Distinction Between Economic Rights and Moral Rights:**

| **Parameter** | **Economic Rights** | **Moral Rights** |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Property rights, commercial in nature | Personal rights, non-commercial |
| Assignability | Can be assigned or licensed | Cannot be assigned, only waived |
| Duration | Limited to copyright term (60 years) | Co-terminus with copyright |
| Transfer on Death | Passes to heirs/assignees | Not transmissible (except to legal heirs for enforcement) |
| Inheritor | Heirs/assignees can enforce | Only author or his legal heirs can enforce |

**Case Law:** In *Amar Nath Sehgal v. Union of India* (2005), the Delhi High Court held that even after assignment of economic rights, the artist's moral rights (right of integrity) remain intact, and the State cannot mutilate or destroy the artistic work.

---

##### (b) Discuss the civil and criminal remedies available for copyright infringement under the Act. **[7 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Civil Remedies (Sections 55–61):**

A copyright owner can file a civil suit before the District Court where the plaintiff resides or carries on business, or where the infringement occurred.

**Available reliefs:**

(a) **Injunction:**
- **Temporary (Interlocutory) Injunction** under Order XXXIX Rules 1 and 2 of CPC.
- **Permanent Injunction** awarded at the conclusion of the suit, restraining the defendant from continuing the infringing acts.

(b) **Damages or Account of Profits:**
- The plaintiff may elect to claim **damages** (compensation for loss suffered) OR an **account of profits** (the defendant's illegal gains). Section 61 provides additional damages up to a maximum of **₹50,000 per infringement** in certain cases.

(c) **Delivery Up of Infringing Copies:**
- The court may order delivery of all infringing copies and plates used for making such copies to the plaintiff, who may then destroy or dispose of them.

(d) **Anton Piller Order:**
- A court-appointed search warrant allowing the plaintiff to enter the defendant's premises and seize infringing material, provided the defendant is not given prior notice. Granted only when there is a real risk of destruction of evidence.

(e) **Mareva Injunction:**
- Freezing of the defendant's assets to prevent dissipation of the same.

**2. Criminal Remedies (Sections 63–70):**

The State is the prosecuting party, and the offence is cognizable, bailable, and triable by a Magistrate of the first class.

**Punishment (Section 63):**

- **Imprisonment:** Minimum **6 months**, extending up to **3 years** for first offence.
- **Fine:** Minimum **₹50,000**, extending up to **₹2,00,000**.

**Enhanced Punishment for Second/Subsequent Offences:**
- Imprisonment: Minimum **1 year**, extending up to **3 years**, and fine as above.

**Specific Offences:**

- **Section 63:** Knowingly infringing or abetting infringement.
- **Section 65:** Knowingly using pirated copies for trade or business.
- **Section 68:** Possession of plates for making infringing copies.
- **Section 70:** Disposing of infringing copies used for purposes of trade/business.

**Procedure:**
- The police officer may seize all copies of the work and the plates used, without warrant (Section 64).
- The court may order destruction of the infringing copies (Section 66).

**Case Law:** In *Microsoft Corporation v. Yogesh Papat* (2005), the Delhi High Court awarded a **permanent injunction and ₹15 lakhs in damages** for software piracy under Sections 55 and 62.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

##### (a) Elaborate on the concept of 'Fair Dealing' under Section 52 of the Copyright Act, 1957. State any four acts that constitute fair dealing. **[7 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Fair Dealing:**

Fair dealing is a **statutory defence** to copyright infringement, codified in **Section 52** of the Copyright Act, 1957. It enumerates specific acts that do **not** constitute infringement of copyright, even when done without the owner's permission. The doctrine balances the **exclusive rights of the author** with the **public interest in dissemination of knowledge, education, and criticism**.

**Key Principle:** Fair dealing in India is a **closed list of exceptions** (unlike the US "fair use" doctrine, which is open-ended). The burden of proof lies on the **defendant** to establish that the act falls within one of the statutory exceptions.

**Four Acts Constituting Fair Dealing:**

**1. Fair Dealing for Private Use, Research, Criticism, Review, or News Reporting (Section 52(1)(a)):**

- A fair dealing with any work (other than a computer programme) for the purpose of **private use, research, criticism, review, or news reporting** is not an infringement.
- **Conditions:**
  - The dealing must be "fair" (i.e., not excessive in relation to the purpose).
  - In cases of criticism or review, the **author and source must be acknowledged**.
  - The portion used must be **necessary and relevant** to the criticism.

**2. Quotation from Published Works (Section 52(1)(h)):**

- A quotation of short passages from a published work, **with due acknowledgement**, is permitted, provided that the quotation is used for a **legitimate purpose** (such as illustration or instruction).
- **Limitation:** It must not exceed the extent justified by the purpose.

**3. Use in Judicial Proceedings (Section 52(1)(c)):**

- Any fair dealing with a literary, dramatic, musical, or artistic work in the course of **judicial proceedings or before a tribunal** is not an infringement.
- **Scope:** Includes court pleadings, expert reports, and evidence.

**4. Reproduction for the Purpose of Examination (Section 52(1)(i)):**

- The reproduction of any work for the purpose of **examinations** (e.g., question papers, model answers) by educational institutions is permitted, provided the work is not commercially available for such purpose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Additional Important Acts:**
> - **Section 52(1)(d):** Reading or reciting in public of a reasonable portion from a published literary or dramatic work.
> - **Section 52(1)(y):** Making a sound recording of a literary, dramatic, or musical work for use in a utility article (e.g., spoken words in a commercial).

---

##### (b) Explain the provisions relating to the term of copyright for various works under the Copyright Act, 1957. Why was the 2012 Amendment significant in this regard? **[7 Marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Term of Copyright — Section 22 to 25:**

The term of copyright varies based on the **category of work** and the **identity of the author**. The underlying principle is that protection extends for a **reasonable period** to incentivize creation while eventually placing the work in the public domain.

**1. Literary, Dramatic, Musical, and Artistic Works (Section 22):**

- **Term:** Copyright subsists **throughout the lifetime of the author plus 60 years**, computed from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of the author's death.
- **Rationale:** The "life plus 60" formula balances the author's economic interest with that of the author's heirs and assigns.

**2. Anonymous and Pseudonymous Works (Section 23):**

- **Term:** **60 years from the year of first publication**, or 60 years from the year when the work was first made known to the public (whichever is **shorter**).
- **Rationale:** Since the author's identity is unknown, the publication date becomes the trigger.
- **Exception:** If the author's identity becomes known before the term expires, the standard "life plus 60" formula applies.

**3. Cinematograph Films (Section 25(1)(b)):**

- **Term:** **60 years from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of first publication** of the film.

**4. Sound Recordings (Section 25(1)(a)):**

- **Term:** **60 years from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of first publication** of the recording.

**5. Posthumous Works (Section 24):**

- **Term:** **60 years from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of first publication.**

**6. Government Works and Works of International Organisations (Section 28):**

- **Term:** **60 years from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of first publication.**

**Significance of the 2012 Amendment:**

The Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012 was a watershed moment for several reasons:

1. **WTO Dispute Resolution:** India faced a complaint from the **European Union** at the WTO regarding the term of protection for sound recordings. The pre-2012 provision calculated the term from the **date of recording**, which was non-compliant with TRIPS. The 2012 Amendment changed the trigger to **"date of first publication,"** aligning Indian law with global standards.

2. **Extension of Term:** The 2012 Amendment increased the copyright term for photographs from **25 years to 60 years**, granting them parity with other artistic works.

3. **Statutory Licence for Broadcasting:** The 2012 Amendment introduced statutory licensing provisions (Section 31D) for the **broadcasting of literary and musical works and sound recordings**, providing clarity to the broadcasting industry.

4. **Technological Updates:** The Amendment added definitions for **"communication to the public"** and **"broadcast"** to encompass digital and internet-based transmissions.

5. **Compulsory Licences for Accessible Format Copies (Section 31B):** Permits the creation of works in formats accessible to **persons with disabilities**, facilitating compliance with the **Marrakesh Treaty (2013)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **death of the author** date with the **publication** date. Remember: For published literary works, the term is from the year next following the year of **death**, NOT publication. Lose **1 mark** for this swap.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Copyright Act, 1957**

- **Commencement Date:** 21 January 1958.
- **Latest Amendment:** Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2014 (effective 9 June 2014).
- **8 Categories of Works (Section 13):** Literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematograph films, sound recordings (originally 7; computer programmes added as literary works in 1999).
- **Term:** **60 years** for all categories — either **life + 60** (literary/dramatic/musical/artistic) or **60 years from first publication** (films, sound recordings, anonymous works).
- **First Owner:** Author (Section 17), with exceptions for employment and commissioned works.
- **Moral Rights (Section 57):** Right of paternity + right of integrity; cannot be assigned, only waived.
- **Infringement (Section 51):** Includes substantial part copying (post-2012), import of infringing copies, and permitting use of premises for infringement.
- **Fair Dealing (Section 52):** Closed list; no US-style flexible "fair use." Includes private use, criticism, review, news reporting, judicial proceedings, and educational use.
- **Remedies:** Civil (injunction, damages, accounts, delivery up, Anton Piller order) + Criminal (6 months to 3 years imprisonment; ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 fine).
- **Statutory Bodies:** Copyright Office (Section 9) + Copyright Board (Section 11).
- **Registration:** Voluntary under Section 44; serves as **prima facie evidence** under Section 48.
- **International Protection:** Automatic under Berne Convention, TRIPS, and UCC.
- **Computer Programs:** Copyright as **literary works** (Section 2(o) post-1999 Amendment).
- **Government Works:** Copyright vests in the Government, but not in respect of enactments of legislature.
- **Anti-Piracy:** Section 65 criminalises use of infringing copies for trade; minimum 6 months imprisonment.
- **Digital Rights:** 2012 Amendment added provisions on **technological protection measures** and **rights management information** (Section 65A, 65B).
- **Important Case Laws:** *Mannu Bhandari v. Kala Vikas Pictures* (moral rights), *Microsoft v. Yogesh Papat* (software piracy), *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak* (substantial similarity test).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Meaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Meaning of Intellectual Property (Foundational Context for Module 3)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Intellectual Property (IP) refers to the legally recognized exclusive rights赋予 to creators, inventors, and owners over creations of the mind — encompassing inventions, literary and artistic works, designs, and symbols, names, and images used in commerce. The World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) defines IP as *creations of the mind*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 Anchor):**
> Module 3 of PESFT757 focuses on the **meaning, nature, and scope of Copyrights, Related Rights, Trademarks, and Designs**. The "Meaning" topic establishes the foundational statutory and conceptual definitions that govern the rest of the module.

### 1.2 Meaning of Copyright (Primary Module 3 Focus)

**Statutory Definition (Indian Copyright Act, 1957 — Section 13 & 14):**
Copyright is a bundle of exclusive legal rights granted to the **author/creator** of original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works, sound recordings, and cinematograph films, to reproduce, communicate, publish, perform, and adapt the work for a specified duration.

$$ \text{Copyright} = f(\text{Originality}, \text{Work of Authorship}, \text{Fixation}, \text{Statutory Recognition}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 13 of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957** enumerates the classes of works eligible for copyright protection:
> 1. Literary works
> 2. Dramatic works
> 3. Musical works
> 4. Artistic works
> 5. Cinematograph films
> 6. Sound recordings

### 1.3 Meaning of Trademark

A **trademark** (TM) is any distinctive sign, logo, word, phrase, symbol, design, or combination thereof that identifies and distinguishes the source of goods or services of one enterprise from those of another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade Marks Act, 1999 (India)** governs trademark registration, protection, and infringement. A trademark must satisfy the test of **distinctiveness** to be registrable under Section 9 of the Act.

### 1.4 Meaning of Industrial Design

An **industrial design** refers to the ornamental or aesthetic aspects of an article — its shape, configuration, pattern, or ornamentation — that appeal to the eye and are capable of being applied industrially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Designs Act, 2000 (India)** protects new and original designs applied to articles manufactured industrially, for a period of **10 years** (extendable by 5 years) from the date of registration.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "House on Rented Land" Model:**
> Imagine you build a beautiful house (your creative work). You own the house (the expression), but the land beneath it is owned by someone else (the idea/underlying concept). Copyright protects your *house* — it does not prevent others from building their own house on the same land. This is the classic idea-expression dichotomy.
>
> A **trademark** is like a unique nameplate on your house that no neighbor can copy. A **patent** is like a structural blueprint that prevents others from copying your construction method. A **design** is like the architectural façade that no one can replicate identically.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Bundle of Rights" Doctrine (Copyright)

The meaning of copyright under KTU Module 3 is best understood as a **bundle of exclusive rights**, not a single monolithic right. Under **Section 14** of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957, the copyright holder enjoys the following rights:

| Right | Description | Practical Application |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Right to Reproduce** | Make copies of the work in any material form | Photocopying, digital copying, reprinting |
| **Right to Communicate** | Broadcast or transmit the work to the public | TV, radio, internet streaming |
| **Right to Publish** | Make the work available to the public | First publication, distribution |
| **Right to Perform** | Recite, play, or perform the work publicly | Stage shows, concerts, public recitation |
| **Right to Adapt** | Translate, transform, or modify the work | Movie adaptations, translations, remixes |
| **Right to Translate** | Convert the work into other languages | Foreign-language editions |

### 2.2 Subject Matter of Copyright — The Eight Categories

KTU Module 3 mandates understanding the meaning of copyright across all eight categories of protected subject matter:

1. **Literary Works** — Books, articles, software code, tables, compilations
2. **Dramatic Works** — Plays, screenplays, choreographic works
3. **Musical Works** — Compositions (with or without words)
4. **Artistic Works** — Paintings, sculptures, drawings, photographs, architectural works
5. **Cinematograph Films** — Visual recordings with synchronized sound
6. **Sound Recordings** — Audio fixations independent of the film
7. **Broadcasts** — Television and radio signals (as neighboring rights)
8. **Performers' Rights** — Rights of performers in their performances

### 2.3 Essential Conditions for Copyright Protection

For a work to qualify for copyright under the meaning of Module 3, it must satisfy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Copyright Protection:**
> - **Originality** — Must originate from the author (not copied)
> - **Work of Authorship** — Must fall within the categories under Section 13
> - **Fixation** — Must be fixed in a tangible medium of expression
> - **Minimal Creativity** — Must possess a modicum of creativity threshold

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Statutory Reference | Duration (India) | Key Test |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|----------|
| Literary/Dramatic/Musical/Artistic Works | Sec 13(1)(a-d), Sec 22 | Life of author + **60 years** | Originality |
| Cinematograph Films | Sec 13(1)(b) | **60 years** from publication | Originality + Fixation |
| Sound Recordings | Sec 13(1)(a) | **60 years** from publication | Originality + Fixation |
| Photographs | Sec 13(1)(a) | **60 years** from publication | Originality + Skill |
| Works of Government/Public Undertaking | Sec 17, Sec 28 | **60 years** from publication | Crown Copyright |
| Anonymous/Pseudonymous Works | Sec 23 | **60 years** from publication | First publication |
| Trademarks | TM Act, 1999, Sec 9 | **10 years** (renewable) | Distinctiveness |
| Industrial Designs | Designs Act, 2000, Sec 11 | **10 years** + **5 years** extension | Novelty + Originality |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Duration Calculation:**
> For literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works, the period runs for the **life of the author plus 60 years** from the beginning of the calendar year following the year of death. Students frequently confuse this with a flat 60-year term.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the engineering domain, the meaning of copyright directly impacts:
- **Software development** — Source code is protected as a literary work
- **CAD drawings** — Protected as artistic works (engineering drawings)
- **Technical manuals** — Protected as literary works
- **Database structures** — Compilations may attract copyright
- **Open-source licensing** — Operates within copyright's exclusive rights framework

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Determining the Term of Copyright Protection

**Problem Framework:** A software engineer authors a complex program in **2008** and dies in **2020**. Determine the duration of copyright protection under Indian law.

**Step 1: Identify the Applicable Statutory Provision**

For literary works (which include computer programs under Indian law), the governing provision is **Section 22** of the Copyright Act, 1957.

$$ \text{Term}_{\text{literary}} = \text{Life of Author} + 60 \text{ years} $$

**Step 2: Determine the Author's Year of Death**

The author died in the calendar year **2020**.

$$ Y_{\text{death}} = 2020 $$

**Step 3: Apply the "Calendar Year Following" Rule**

Under Section 22, the 60-year term begins from the **beginning of the calendar year next following** the year of the author's death.

$$ Y_{\text{start}} = Y_{\text{death}} + 1 = 2020 + 1 = 2021 $$

**Step 4: Calculate the End of Protection**

$$ Y_{\text{end}} = Y_{\text{start}} + 60 = 2021 + 60 = 2081 $$

**Step 5: Express the Final Duration**

$$ \boxed{\text{Copyright Term} = \text{From 1 January 2021 to 31 December 2081 (inclusive)} } $$

**Conversion Logic Summary:**

| Step | Input | Operation | Output |
|------|-------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | Author's death year | Identify | 2020 |
| 2 | Death year | Add 1 (calendar rule) | 2021 |
| 3 | Start year | Add 60 years | 2081 |
| 4 | Final term | Express inclusive range | Jan 2021 – Dec 2081 |

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: The Idea-Expression Dichotomy Test

**Problem Framework:** An engineering student creates a manual for a robotic arm. A competitor publishes a different manual with similar concepts but different wording and diagrams. Does copyright infringement occur?

**Step 1: Apply the Idea-Expression Dichotomy**

$$ \text{Copyright Protection Scope} = \text{Expression} \subset \text{Work} $$

$$ \text{Ideas, Methods, Systems} \notin \text{Copyright Protection} $$

**Step 2: Evaluate the Substantial Similarity Test**

For infringement, two conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

$$ \text{Infringement} = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } (\text{Access to Original Work}) \land (\text{Substantial Similarity in Expression}) \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Step 3: Apply to the Scenario**

- **Ideas/Concepts:** Not protected (robotic arm concepts are free to use)
- **Expression:** Different wording, different diagrams → **No substantial similarity in expression**
- **Verdict:** **No infringement** — the competitor's manual is independently derived

**Step 4: Illustrative Logic Flow**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Work}_{\text{original}} &= \text{Idea} \cup \text{Expression}_{\text{A}} \\
\text{Work}_{\text{competitor}} &= \text{Idea} \cup \text{Expression}_{\text{B}} \\
\text{Shared Ideas} &= \text{Idea (uncopyrightable)} \\
\text{Shared Expression} &= \emptyset \\
\text{Result} &= \text{No Copyright Infringement}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Distinct IP Meanings

| Dimension | Copyright | Trademark | Industrial Design | Patent |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Subject Matter** | Creative expressions | Source identifiers | Aesthetic appearance | Functional inventions |
| **Statute (India)** | Copyright Act, 1957 | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Designs Act, 2000 | Patents Act, 1970 |
| **Requirement** | Originality | Distinctiveness | Novelty + Originality | Novelty + Inventive Step + Utility |
| **Term** | Life + 60 years (most) | 10 years (renewable) | 10 + 5 years | 20 years |
| **Registration** | Automatic (no formality) | Required for full protection | Required | Required |
| **Symbol** | © | ™ or ® | "Design Regd" | "Patented" |
| **Engineering Example** | Source code, manuals | Brand name, logo | CAD aesthetic shape | Mechanical invention |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Meaning of IP Rights Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Intellectual Property] --> B[Industrial Property]
    A --> C[Copyright and Related Rights]
    
    B --> B1[Patents]
    B --> B2[Trademarks]

    B --> B4[Geographical Indications]
    
    C --> C1[Literary Works]
    C --> C2[Artistic Works]
    C --> C3[Musical Works]
    C --> C4[Cinematograph Films]
    C --> C5[Sound Recordings]
    C --> C6[Broadcasts and Performers Rights]
    
    B1 --> B1a[Functional Inventions]
    B2 --> B2a[Source Identifiers]
    B3 --> B3a[Aesthetic Appearance]
    B4 --> B4a[Origin Indicators]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Bundle of Copyright Rights (Section 14, Indian Copyright Act 1957)

```mermaid

    CR[Copyright Holder] --> R1[Right to Reproduce]

    R1 --> R1a[Photocopying and Digital Copying]

    CR --> DR[Duration Control]
    DR --> D1[Literary: Life plus 60 Years]
    DR --> D2[Films and Recordings: 60 Years from Publication]
    DR --> D3[Anonymous Works: 60 Years from Publication]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Idea-Expression Dichotomy (Foundation of Copyright Meaning)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W[Work] --> I[Idea]
    W --> E[Expression]
    
    I --> I1[Concepts and Methods]
    I --> I2[Systems and Processes]
    I --> I3[Unprotected Free Domain]
    
    E --> E1[Words and Text]
    E --> E2[Images and Diagrams]

    I3 -.-> NO[Not Infringing]
    E4 -.-> YES[Protected Expression]
    
    style NO fill:#90EE90
    style YES fill:#FFB6C1
```

### 4.4 Architecture Flow: KTU Module 3 — Meaning Topic Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M3[Module 3 - Meaning of IP Rights] --> M3A[Meaning of Copyright]
    M3 --> M3B[Meaning of Trademark]
    M3 --> M3C[Meaning of Industrial Design]
    M3 --> M3D[Meaning of Related Rights]
    
    M3A --> M3A1[Section 13 - Subject Matter]
    M3A --> M3A2[Section 14 - Bundle of Rights]
    M3A --> M3A3[Section 22 - Duration]
    
    M3B --> M3B1[Section 9 - Distinctiveness]
    M3B --> M3B2[Section 18 - Registration]
    
    M3C --> M3C1[Designs Act 2000 - Section 2]
    M3C --> M3C2[Novelty and Originality Test]
    
    M3D --> M3D1[Performers Rights Section 38]
    M3D --> M3D2[Broadcast Reproduction Rights Section 39]
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree: Determining Copyright Eligibility

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is it an original work?}
    Q1 -->|No| NO1[No Copyright Protection]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Does it fall under Section 13 categories?}
    Q2 -->|No| NO2[No Copyright Protection]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is it fixed in a tangible medium?}
    Q3 -->|No| NO3[No Copyright in India Common Law Countries]
    Q3 -->|Yes| YES1[Copyright Protection Granted]
    
    YES1 --> D1{Determine Duration}
    D1 --> D2[Life plus 60 Years for Literary Works]
    D1 --> D3[60 Years from Publication for Films]
    
    style YES1 fill:#FFD700
    style NO1 fill:#FF6347
    style NO2 fill:#FF6347
    style NO3 fill:#FF6347
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]:** Define copyright as per the Indian Copyright Act, 1957.

**Model Answer:**

Copyright is a statutory right granted under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957, which confers upon the creator of an original literary, dramatic, musical, or artistic work, cinematograph film, or sound recording, a bundle of exclusive rights to reproduce, communicate, publish, perform, and adapt the work. The right arises automatically upon creation of the work in a tangible form, without the requirement of formal registration. The term of protection varies by category, with literary and artistic works protected for the **life of the author plus 60 years**.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]:** What is meant by a "trademark" under the Trade Marks Act, 1999?

**Model Answer:**

A trademark under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, is a distinctive mark capable of graphical representation, used in relation to goods or services to indicate a connection in the course of trade between the goods/services and the proprietor. It includes words, logos, symbols, shapes, colors, or combinations thereof. The mark must satisfy the test of **distinctiveness** and not fall within prohibited categories under Section 9. Registration confers exclusive right to use the mark for **10 years**, renewable indefinitely.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** Discuss the meaning and scope of copyright under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957. Explain the categories of works protected and the bundle of rights conferred.

**Part (a) — Meaning and Categories of Protected Works (7 Marks)**

Copyright, as defined under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957, is the exclusive legal right of the author/creator to control the use, reproduction, and distribution of original works of authorship. **Section 13** enumerates the categories of works entitled to copyright protection:

1. **Literary works** — including books, computer programs, tables, and compilations
2. **Dramatic works** — including plays, screenplays, and choreographic notations
3. **Musical works** — with or without accompanying words
4. **Artistic works** — paintings, sculptures, photographs, architectural works, engravings
5. **Cinematograph films** — visual works with synchronized sound
6. **Sound recordings** — audio fixations independent of any film

For copyright to subsist, the work must be **original**, must fall within one of the above categories, and must be **fixed** in a tangible medium of expression. Registration is not mandatory; copyright arises automatically upon creation.

**Part (b) — Bundle of Rights and Duration (7 Marks)**

Under **Section 14** of the Act, copyright is not a single right but a **bundle of exclusive rights**:

- Right to reproduce the work
- Right to issue copies to the public
- Right to perform the work in public
- Right to communicate the work to the public
- Right to make translations, adaptations, and other transformations

**Duration:** Under Section 22, for literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works, copyright subsists for the **life of the author plus 60 years**, calculated from the beginning of the calendar year following the year of death. For cinematograph films and sound recordings, the term is **60 years from the date of publication**. Government works and works of public undertakings enjoy protection for **60 years from the date of publication** under Section 28.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** Explain the meaning of trademark and industrial design under Indian law. Compare their scope, protection requirements, and duration.

**Part (a) — Meaning and Requirements of Trademarks (7 Marks)**

A trademark under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** is a distinctive sign, symbol, word, phrase, or combination thereof, used to identify and distinguish the goods or services of one undertaking from those of another. **Section 2(1)(m)** defines it as a mark capable of being represented graphically and capable of distinguishing goods or services of one person from those of others.

**Essential Requirements:**
- The mark must be **distinctive** (Section 9)
- It must be capable of **graphical representation**
- It must not be **descriptive** of the goods/services (unless it has acquired secondary meaning)
- It must not fall within prohibited categories (Section 9(2))

**Duration:** Registration is for **10 years**, renewable indefinitely for successive periods of 10 years.

**Part (b) — Meaning and Requirements of Industrial Designs (7 Marks)**

An industrial design under the **Designs Act, 2000** refers to the features of shape, configuration, pattern, or ornamentation of an article applied to it by any industrial process, which appeal to and are judged solely by the eye. **Section 2(d)** defines a design accordingly.

**Essential Requirements:**
- The design must be **new** (novel)
- It must be **original**
- It must relate to features appealing to the **eye**
- It must be **capable of being applied industrially**
- It must not be dictated solely by functional considerations

**Duration:** Registration protects the design for an initial period of **10 years**, extendable by a further **5 years**, totaling a maximum of **15 years**.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Dimension | Trademark | Industrial Design |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| Statute | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Designs Act, 2000 |
| Subject | Source identifier | Aesthetic appearance |
| Test | Distinctiveness | Novelty + Originality |
| Term | 10 years (renewable) | 10 + 5 years |
| Function | Distinguishes source | Attracts eye appeal |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing copyright registration with creation** — Copyright arises automatically; registration is optional for civil remedies.
> 2. **Wrong duration** — Writing "60 years" without specifying "life of author plus 60 years" for literary works loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Idea-Expression confusion** — Students often claim copyright protection over ideas, which is legally incorrect.
> 4. **Mixing trademark and design** — Trademarks protect source identifiers; designs protect aesthetic features. Examiners penalize conflation.
> 5. **Section misquotation** — Always cite the correct statutory section (Section 13, 14, 22 for Copyright; Section 9 for Trademark; Section 2(d) for Designs).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Meaning of IP Rights (Module 3):**
> - Copyright is a **bundle of rights**, not a single right.
> - Copyright protects **expression**, not ideas (Idea-Expression Dichotomy).
> - **Section 13** = Subject matter; **Section 14** = Rights; **Section 22** = Duration.
> - Duration for literary works = **Life of Author + 60 years**.
> - Duration for films/recordings = **60 years from publication**.
> - Copyright arises **automatically** — no registration required.
> - Trademark requires **distinctiveness** and graphical representation.
> - Trademark duration = **10 years** (indefinitely renewable).
> - Industrial Design requires **novelty + originality + eye appeal**.
> - Design duration = **10 years + 5 years extension** (max 15 years).
> - Software code is protected as a **literary work** under copyright.
> - Symbols: **©** (Copyright), **™** (Unregistered Trademark), **®** (Registered Trademark).
> - Infringement requires **access** + **substantial similarity** in expression.
> - **Fair dealing** under Sections 52 provides limited exceptions.
> - KTU focus: Always quote statutory sections accurately for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** General Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Patent? — The Formal Definition

A **Patent** is a statutory right granted by the government (sovereign authority) to an inventor, conferring upon him/her the **exclusive right** to make, use, sell, offer for sale, or import the patented invention for a limited period of **20 years** from the date of filing, in exchange for the public disclosure of the invention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Ready Definition (Indian Patents Act, 1970 — Section 2(1)(j)):**
> "A patent means a patent for any invention granted under this Act, including a patent granted in pursuance of an application made under the Indian Patent Co-operation Treaty (PCT) or under any international arrangement."

A **Patent Right** is, in essence, a **negative right** — it does not give the patentee the right to *use* the invention (which may be restricted by other laws), but the right to *exclude* others from making, using, or commercializing it without authorization.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Land Deed" Model

Imagine a piece of vacant land in a city. The government does not give you ownership for free — you must:
1. **Apply** for the land (file a patent application).
2. **Prove** that the land is genuinely yours (satisfy novelty, inventive step, industrial application).
3. **Disclose** the boundaries, soil quality, and location of your land (write a patent specification).
4. **Pay renewal fees** annually (renewal fees) — if you don't, the land reverts to the public.

In return, you get a **deed** (patent) valid for 20 years, after which the land (knowledge) becomes public property for anyone to use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A patent is essentially a **contract between the inventor and the state** — the inventor gives knowledge to the public in return for a temporary monopoly.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Patentability (TRIPS-Aligned)

Every patent-eligible invention MUST satisfy three substantive criteria under the **Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS) Agreement, 1994 — Article 27**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Triad of Patentability:**
> 1. **Novelty** — The invention must be new (not in prior art).
> 2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — The invention must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA).
> 3. **Industrial Application (Utility)** — The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry.

These three criteria form the **bedrock of patent examination** worldwide, and the KTU examiner expects students to internalize them as a unit.

---

## 1.4 Who Can Be Granted a Patent? — The "True and First Inventor" Doctrine

Under **Section 6 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970**, a patent may be granted to:
- A person claiming to be the **true and first inventor** of the invention.
- An **assignee** of such person (in case the rights have been assigned).
- The **legal representative** of a deceased true and first inventor.
- A **person who is the applicant under the PCT** designating India.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "first to invent" with "first to file." India operates under a **first-to-file** system (since 2002 amendment), meaning the person who files first gets priority — even if they were not the first to actually invent it.

---

## 1.5 What Can and Cannot Be Patented?

### 1.5.1 Patentable Subject Matter (Section 3, *exclusions* are listed)

The following, if they meet the three pillars, are generally patentable:
- **Products** (e.g., a chemical compound, a machine, a pharmaceutical drug).
- **Processes** (e.g., a manufacturing method, a software-implemented process).
- **Machines, articles, substances produced by manufacturing**, or any improvement thereof.

### 1.5.2 Non-Patentable Inventions (Section 3 and Section 4)

> [!WARNING]
> **High-Yield KTU Topic:** Section 3 of the Indian Patents Act lists **17 categories** of non-patentable inventions. Memorize these — they appear almost every KTU examination cycle.

| **Category** | **Example** |
|---|---|
| Frivolous invention | A perpetual motion machine |
| Inventions contrary to public order/morality | A weapon designed solely for mass destruction |
| Discovery of a scientific principle | Einstein's E=mc² (theory itself) |
| Discovery of a living substance occurring in nature | A naturally occurring bacterium |
| Mere admixture or rearrangement | Repackaging old drugs |
| Method of agriculture/horticulture | A new way of sowing seeds |
| Method of treatment of animals/humans | A new surgical technique |
| Plants and animals in whole/parts (other than microorganisms) | A new variety of rose |
| Mathematical/business method | A pure algorithm, a scheme for gambling |
| Computer program *per se* | Source code as such |
| Aesthetic creation | A painting, sculpture (protected by copyright) |
| Topography of IC layouts | Mask works |
| Traditional knowledge | Ancient medicinal formulations |
| Atomic energy inventions | Nuclear weapons (Section 4) |
| Inventions prejudicial to public health | Hazardous chemical compositions |

---

## 1.6 Rights Conferred by a Patent (Section 53)

Upon grant, a patentee has the **exclusive right** to:
1. **Make** the patented product.
2. **Use** the patented product or process.
3. **Sell** the patented product or the product obtained by the patented process.
4. **Offer for sale** the patented product.
5. **Import** the patented product.

These rights are subject to the **Bolar exception** (research use) and **compulsory licensing** provisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The right is **territorial** — a US patent is enforceable only in the US, and an Indian patent is enforceable only in India. Therefore, inventors seek **multiple patents** in different jurisdictions for the same invention.

---

## 1.7 Term of a Patent

| **Patent Type** | **Term (from date of filing)** |
|---|---|
| Standard Patent (Product/Process) | **20 years** |
| International Application under PCT | **20 years** (from international filing date) |

After expiry, the invention falls into the **public domain** — anyone can use, make, or sell it freely.

---

## 1.8 Patent Specification — The Heart of Disclosure

A **specification** is a written description of the invention, disclosing the invention in a manner sufficient for a person skilled in the art to perform it.

There are two types:
1. **Provisional Specification** — A placeholder filed to establish **priority date**; does not contain full disclosure.
2. **Complete Specification** — The full, final document containing description, claims, and abstract.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Limit:** A complete specification must be filed within **12 months** of the provisional specification. Failure to do so results in abandonment of the application.

---

## 1.9 Conceptual Summary of General Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Cornerstones of Patent Law:**
> 1. **Patent** = Negative right to exclude others.
> 2. **Patentability** = Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application.
> 3. **First-to-file** system (not first-to-invent).
> 4. **Disclosure** is the price paid for the monopoly.
> 5. **Territoriality** — patents are jurisdiction-specific.
> 6. **Time-limited** — 20 years from filing date.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Principle Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of the Three Pillars of Patentability

### 2.1.1 Pillar I: Novelty (Section 2(1)(l))

**Definition:** An invention is novel if it has **not been anticipated** by the prior art, whether in India or abroad, before the date of filing or priority date.

**Prior Art** includes:
- Any public use, publication, or knowledge anywhere in the world.
- Any patent or non-patent literature.
- Any oral disclosure (lectures, conferences).
- Any commercial exploitation.

**Test for Novelty:** *"Was the invention disclosed to the public, anywhere in the world, before the date of filing?"*
- If **YES** → Not novel → **Patent refused**.
- If **NO** → Novelty satisfied.

**Grace Period (Section 31):** Disclosure in an official exhibition or a recognized academic conference within **12 months** before filing does not destroy novelty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Novelty vs. Anticipation:** Anticipation requires that the prior art disclose **all the essential features** of the claim — a single missing element means no anticipation. This is the *"anticipation by single reference"* rule.

---

### 2.1.2 Pillar II: Inventive Step / Non-Obviousness (Section 2(1)(ja))

**Definition:** An invention involves an inventive step if it is **not obvious to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA)**, having regard to the prior art as a whole.

**The PHOSITA Test:**
- *Person Skilled in the Art (PHOSITA)* is a hypothetical person of **ordinary skill** in the relevant technical field.
- Would PHOSITA, armed with the prior art, have arrived at the invention in an **obvious manner**?
- If **YES** → Not patentable (no inventive step).
- If **NO** → Inventive step satisfied.

**Factors to determine obviousness (judicial guidelines):**
1. Scope and content of prior art.
2. Differences between prior art and the claimed invention.
3. Level of ordinary skill in the art.
4. Secondary considerations (commercial success, long-felt need, failure of others).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** "Novelty" and "Inventive Step" are **NOT** the same. A product can be novel (new) but still lack an inventive step (obvious to PHOSITA). Example: A new combination of two known drugs, where the combination is obvious to a pharmacist.

---

### 2.1.3 Pillar III: Industrial Application / Utility (Section 2(1)(ac))

**Definition:** An invention is capable of industrial application if it can be **made or used in an industry** (including agriculture).

**Test:** *"Can the invention be made or used in any kind of industry?"*
- If **YES** → Industrial application satisfied.
- If **NO** (e.g., a purely abstract idea) → Not patentable.

**Practical Consequence:** A mere discovery (like a new property of a known substance) without a concrete industrial use is not patentable — the invention must have a **specific, substantial, and credible utility**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Principle Cheat Sheet

| **Principle / Concept** | **Statutory Basis** | **Key Test / Trigger** | **Time Limit / Term** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Patent Definition | Section 2(1)(j) | Statutory right to exclude | — |
| Novelty | Section 2(1)(l) | Not anticipated by prior art | Worldwide search |
| Inventive Step | Section 2(1)(ja) | Not obvious to PHOSITA | At the priority date |
| Industrial Application | Section 2(1)(ac) | Capable of industrial use | At the time of filing |
| True and First Inventor | Section 6 | First-to-file priority | 20 years from filing |
| Non-Patentable Subject Matter | Section 3(1) to 3(17) | 17 statutory exclusions | — |
| Atomic Energy Exclusions | Section 4 | National security carve-out | — |
| Provisional Specification | Section 9 | Establishes priority date | 12 months to complete |
| Complete Specification | Section 10 | Full disclosure + claims | Filed within 12 months |
| Patent Rights | Section 53 | Make, use, sell, offer, import | During term |
| Patent Term | Section 53(1) | 20 years from filing | 20 years |
| Grace Period (Exhibition) | Section 31 | Disclosure in recognized exhibition | 12 months pre-filing |
| Compulsory License | Section 84 | Reasonable requirements unmet | 3 years after grant |
| Bolar Exception | Section 107A(a) | Research/regulatory use | Pre-expiry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners Love This Table:** Most 7-mark and 14-mark questions in KTU ask you to *"Explain the general principles of patent law"* or *"Discuss the criteria for patentability."* Memorize the statutory sections and tests above.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Principles

The general principles of patent law are not abstract legal concepts — they have **direct, measurable impacts** in engineering and technology:

1. **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Patentability criteria decide whether a new drug formulation (e.g., a novel dosage form of metformin) gets protection, which directly impacts **R&D investment decisions worth billions of dollars**.

2. **Software and IT Industry:** The exclusion of "computer program *per se*" under Section 3(k) shapes the entire landscape of **software patenting in India**. Companies like TCS and Infosys navigate this carve-out carefully.

3. **Biotechnology:** The "discovery of living substance in nature" exclusion (Section 3(c)) governs how biotech firms patent **genetically modified organisms, enzymes, and microorganisms**.

4. **Automobile and Mechanical Engineering:** Combination patents and "improvement" patents rely heavily on the **inventive step test** — a small improvement to an engine, if obvious to a mechanical engineer, will be refused.

5. **Defence and National Security:** The atomic energy exclusion (Section 4) reserves critical defence technologies for state control.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Perspective:** Even though IPR is a non-technical paper, KTU frequently links IP principles to engineering applications. A question on "industrial application" could ask you to apply the test to a specific engineering invention.

---

## 2.4 First-to-File vs. First-to-Invent

| **Aspect** | **First-to-File (India, EPC, PCT)** | **First-to-Invent (USA — pre-AIA 2013)** |
|---|---|---|
| Priority basis | Date of filing of application | Date of invention (conception + reduction to practice) |
| Litigation burden | Low — public records determine priority | High — contested "interference" proceedings |
| Current status | Universal (USA moved to first-to-file post-AIA) | Largely obsolete |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Post-America Invents Act (AIA, 2013):** The USA has effectively become a first-to-file jurisdiction. The global norm is now first-to-file.

---

## 2.5 Doctrine of Territoriality

A patent granted in **Country A** has **no legal force** in **Country B**. To enforce a patent in multiple jurisdictions, the inventor must file separate patent applications in each country (or use the **PCT route** for international protection).

**Example:** An Indian patent for a novel solar panel design is **not enforceable** in Germany. To protect the invention in Germany, the inventor must file a separate German patent (or a European patent designating Germany).

---

## 2.6 Doctrine of Independent Invention

Under **Section 6(2)** of the Indian Patents Act, if two or more persons independently invent the **same invention** on the same day, the right to the patent is given to the **person whose claim is first received** in the Patent Office.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Section 6(2) creates a **tie-breaker rule** for simultaneous invention — it is NOT a "first-to-invent" system, only a tie-breaker. Priority remains the filing date.

---

## 2.7 The "Person Skilled in the Art" (PHOSITA) — A Critical Concept

**PHOSITA** is the cornerstone of the inventive step test. It is a hypothetical construct representing:
- A person with **ordinary skill** in the relevant technical field.
- A person who has **read all the prior art** in that field.
- A person who has **no inventive capacity** of their own (they cannot solve problems creatively).

**Why does PHOSITA matter?**
- It provides an **objective standard** for assessing non-obviousness.
- It avoids the subjectivity of "would I, the judge, have found this obvious?"
- It aligns the legal test with the **real-world technical community**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When KTU asks *"Explain the test for inventive step"*, you MUST mention PHOSITA, otherwise you will lose 2-3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Framework — The Patentability Test Sequence

Although this is a legal topic (not mathematical), the patentability test follows a **logical decision tree** that can be modelled algorithmically. KTU students often benefit from this structured approach for answer-writing.

### Step 1: Statutory Standing (Section 6)
$$\text{Is the applicant the true and first inventor (or assignee, legal representative, PCT applicant)?}$$

If **NO** → Patent refused (lack of standing).
If **YES** → Proceed to Step 2.

### Step 2: Subject Matter Eligibility (Section 3 and 4)
$$\text{Is the invention excluded under any of the 17 categories in Section 3 or Section 4?}$$

If **YES** → Patent refused (non-patentable subject matter).
If **NO** → Proceed to Step 3.

### Step 3: Novelty Test (Section 2(1)(l))
$$\text{Is the invention anticipated by any single prior art reference (worldwide)?}$$

If **YES** → Patent refused (lack of novelty).
If **NO** → Proceed to Step 4.

### Step 4: Inventive Step Test (Section 2(1)(ja))
$$\text{Would a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA), armed with the prior art, find the invention obvious?}$$

If **YES** → Patent refused (lack of inventive step).
If **NO** → Proceed to Step 5.

### Step 5: Industrial Application Test (Section 2(1)(ac))
$$\text{Can the invention be made or used in any kind of industry?}$$

If **NO** → Patent refused (no industrial application).
If **YES** → **Patent granted** (subject to procedural requirements and disclosure).

### Step 6: Procedural Requirements
- Complete specification filed (Section 10).
- Claims drafted clearly and concisely.
- Fees paid.
- Examination requested within **48 months** of priority date.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The decision tree above is the **gold standard** for answering any "patentability" question. Examiners allocate 2-3 marks purely for the logical sequence — not just for the conclusion.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Pillars

The three pillars of patentability are often confused by students. This comparative matrix will cement the differences.

| **Aspect** | **Novelty** | **Inventive Step** | **Industrial Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statutory Section** | Section 2(1)(l) | Section 2(1)(ja) | Section 2(1)(ac) |
| **Legal Question** | Is it new? | Is it non-obvious? | Is it usable in industry? |
| **Reference Standard** | Single prior art reference | Combination of prior art references as a whole | Practical industrial use |
| **Time of Assessment** | Before priority date | Before priority date (with PHOSITA hindsight) | At time of filing |
| **Geographic Scope** | Worldwide | Worldwide | Anywhere in industry |
| **Test Threshold** | "Disclosed to the public?" | "Obvious to PHOSITA?" | "Capable of industrial use?" |
| **Failure Consequence** | Anticipation defeats patent | Obviousness defeats patent | Abstractness defeats patent |
| **Grace Period?** | Yes (Section 31, 12 months) | No separate grace period | No separate grace period |
