| **Simple Example** | A new chemical compound never seen before | Combining two known chemicals in a way obvious to a chemist | A pure mathematical formula (not usable in industry) |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Applying the Patentability Test

**Scenario:** A postgraduate engineering student at NIT Calicut invents a **new type of solar cell using perovskite material** that achieves **30% efficiency** (a world record at the time of filing). She files a patent application at the Indian Patent Office in 2024.

**Step 1: Statutory Standing (Section 6)**
The student is the **true and first inventor** of the solar cell. She qualifies to file.

**Result: ✅ Standing satisfied.**

**Step 2: Subject Matter Eligibility (Section 3)**
A solar cell is a **technological device** — not excluded under Section 3.
- It is not frivolous (Section 3(1)).
- It is not contrary to public order (Section 3(2)).
- It is not a discovery of a natural substance (Section 3(c)) — it is a **manufactured device**.
- It is not a computer program per se (Section 3(k)).
- It is not an atomic energy invention (Section 4).

**Result: ✅ Subject matter eligible.**

**Step 3: Novelty Test (Section 2(1)(l))**
The examiner conducts a **worldwide prior art search** (patents, journal articles, conference papers).
- No single reference discloses a perovskite solar cell with 30% efficiency.
- The closest prior art is a 2022 paper showing 28% efficiency, but the materials and method are different.

**Result: ✅ Novelty satisfied.**

**Step 4: Inventive Step Test (Section 2(1)(ja))**
The PHOSITA is a person with ordinary skill in **semiconductor device fabrication**, aware of all prior art on perovskite solar cells.
- Would PHOSITA, reading the 2022 paper, have arrived at the 30% efficiency by routine experimentation? **No** — the jump from 28% to 30% requires **non-obvious material composition and processing**.
- Secondary considerations: The invention has been **cited by independent researchers** and the student has received a **research grant** for its commercial potential.

**Result: ✅ Inventive step satisfied.**

**Step 5: Industrial Application Test (Section 2(1)(ac))**
The solar cell can be **manufactured in a semiconductor fabrication plant** and used in the **renewable energy industry**.

**Result: ✅ Industrial application satisfied.**

**Step 6: Procedural Compliance**
The student files a complete specification with **clear claims** (covering both the material composition and the manufacturing process), pays the filing fee, and requests examination within 48 months.

**Result: ✅ Procedural compliance satisfied.**

**Final Outcome: Patent GRANTED.** 🏆

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Use:** This worked example illustrates how to apply the general principles to a real engineering scenario. Adapt this template for any 7-mark or 14-mark question that asks you to "analyse the patentability of an invention."

---

## 3.4 Analytical Framework — Drafting a Patent Claim

A patent claim defines the **scope of legal protection**. KTU questions often ask students to distinguish between types of claims.

| **Claim Type** | **Format** | **Scope** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Independent Claim** | Stands on its own | Broadest protection | "A solar cell comprising perovskite material X." |
| **Dependent Claim** | Refers to an earlier claim | Narrower, more specific | "The solar cell of claim 1, wherein X is a halide perovskite." |
| **Product Claim** | Claims a physical article | Protects the article | "A pharmaceutical composition comprising compound A." |
| **Process Claim** | Claims a method | Protects the method | "A method of manufacturing a solar cell, comprising step A, B, C." |
| **Use Claim** | Claims a new use of a known product | Protects the new use | "Use of compound A in the treatment of cancer." |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** Process claims are particularly important in **pharmaceuticals and chemical engineering** — a process patent protects the method even if the product itself is known. This is the basis of the **Evergreening** debate in Indian pharma law.

---

## 3.5 The Six "General Principles" — Consolidated Framework

Based on the **WIPO Model Law on Patents** and the **Indian Patents Act, 1970**, the six general principles of patent law are:

1. **Principle of Patentability** — Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application.
2. **Principle of Territoriality** — Patent rights are confined to the granting country.
3. **Principle of Time Limitation** — Patents are limited to 20 years.
4. **Principle of First-to-File** — The first to file gets priority.
5. **Principle of Disclosure** — The inventor must fully disclose the invention.
6. **Principle of Independence** — A patent application is examined independently of foreign applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Strategy:** When a 14-mark KTU question asks *"Discuss the general principles of patent law"*, structure your answer around these six principles. Examiners allocate 2-3 marks per principle, and your score will be determined by the depth of explanation of each.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Patentability Decision Tree (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid flowchart visualizes the **decision tree** an examiner follows when assessing a patent application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patent Application Filed] --> B{True and First Inventor?}
    B -- No --> Z1[REFUSED: Lack of Standing]
    B -- Yes --> C{Excluded Subject Matter Sec 3 or 4?}
    C -- Yes --> Z2[REFUSED: Non-Patentable Subject Matter]
    C -- No --> D{Anticipated by Prior Art?}
    D -- Yes --> Z3[REFUSED: Lack of Novelty]
    D -- No --> E{Obvious to PHOSITA?}
    E -- Yes --> Z4[REFUSED: Lack of Inventive Step]
    E -- No --> F{Industrial Application?}
    F -- No --> Z5[REFUSED: No Utility]
    F -- Yes --> G{Procedural Compliance?}
    G -- No --> Z6[REFUSED: Procedural Defect]
    G -- Yes --> H[PATENT GRANTED]
    H --> I[Exclusive Rights for 20 Years]
    I --> J[Public Domain After Expiry]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The decision tree is read **top-to-bottom**. At each rectangular node, the examiner makes a **YES/NO** decision. A single "NO" at any early stage halts the process and results in refusal. The patent is granted only when **all six gates** are passed.

---

## 4.2 Three Pillars of Patentability — Architecture Diagram

The three pillars are the **core technical criteria** that any patent application must satisfy. The following Mermaid block shows their interrelation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PILLAR[Three Pillars of Patentability]
        P1[Novelty - Section 2(1)(l)]
        P2[Inventive Step - Section 2(1)(ja)]
        P3[Industrial Application - Section 2(1)(ac)]
    end
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P1 --- P3
    PRIOR[Prior Art] -->|Test Against| P1
    PRIOR -->|Test Against - with PHOSITA| P2
    UTIL[Industrial Use] -->|Test Against| P3
    PILLAR -->|All Three Satisfied| GRANT[Patent Granted]
    PILLAR -->|Any One Fails| REFUSE[Patent Refused]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The three pillars are **not alternatives** — they are **cumulative requirements**. The invention must satisfy **all three** independently. A single failure is enough to defeat the patent application.

---

## 4.3 Patent Application Process — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **lifecycle of a patent application** from filing to grant, with the key time-bound milestones.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FILING[Step 1: Filing of Application - Sec 7] --> PROV{Provisional or Complete Specification?}
    PROV -- Provisional --> PROVSPEC[Provisional Specification - Sec 9 - Priority Date Established]
    PROVSPEC --> TIMER[12 Months Deadline]
    PROV -- Complete --> COMPLETESPEC[Complete Specification - Sec 10 - Full Disclosure]
    TIMER --> COMPLETESPEC
    COMPLETESPEC --> EXAM[Step 2: Request for Examination - Sec 11B - Within 48 Months]
    EXAM --> PUBLICATION[Step 3: Publication in Patent Journal - 18 Months from Priority Date]
    PUBLICATION --> EXAM2[Step 4: Substantive Examination by Patent Office]
    EXAM2 --> OBJECTION{Objections Raised?}
    OBJECTION -- Yes --> RESPONSE[Response to FER - 6 Months Extendable to 12 Months]
    RESPONSE --> HEARING[Hearing Before Controller]
    HEARING --> DECISION{Allowed or Refused?}
    OBJECTION -- No --> DECISION
    DECISION -- Allowed --> GRANT[Step 5: Patent Granted - Sec 43]
    DECISION -- Refused --> APPEAL[Appeal to IPAB or High Court]
    GRANT --> SEAL[Step 6: Patent Seal and Registration]
    SEAL --> RENEWAL[Annual Renewal Fees - Sec 53(2)]
    RENEWAL -->|20 Years| EXPIRY[Patent Expires - Falls into Public Domain]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU frequently tests students on the **time limits** in the patent process — 12 months for complete specification, 48 months for examination request, 20 years for patent term. Memorize these durations.

---

## 4.4 Patent Specification Anatomy — Component Block Diagram

A complete patent specification has **four key components**. The Mermaid block below shows their structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SPEC[Complete Specification - Sec 10]
    SPEC --> TITLE[1. Title of Invention]
    SPEC --> FIELD[2. Field of Invention]
    SPEC --> BG[3. Background - Prior Art]
    SPEC --> SUMMARY[4. Summary of Invention]
    SPEC --> DETAIL[5. Detailed Description]
    SPEC --> CLAIMS[6. Claims - Defining Scope]
    SPEC --> ABSTRACT[7. Abstract - Brief Summary]
    CLAIMS --> INDEP[Independent Claims - Broadest]
    CLAIMS --> DEP[Dependent Claims - Narrower]
    DETAIL --> DRAWINGS[Drawings - If Any]
    DETAIL --> BEST[BEST MODE - Sec 10(4)]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 10(4) Mandate:** The specification must disclose the **best method of performing the invention** known to the applicant. Failure to disclose the best mode can be a ground for revocation under Section 64(1)(i).

---

## 4.5 First-to-File Priority Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **priority date mechanism** under the Paris Convention and the PCT.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inventor Files in Country X - 1 January 2024] --> PRIORITY[Priority Date Established]
    PRIORITY --> B[12 Months Priority Period - Paris Convention]
    B --> C[File in Country Y - 1 December 2024]
    B --> D[File PCT Application - 1 October 2024]
    C --> E[Claims Priority of 1 Jan 2024]
    D --> E
    PRIORITY --> F[Prior Art Search Cut-off: 1 January 2024]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The **priority date** is the most important date in patent law — it is the date against which **all prior art** is assessed. The 12-month Paris Convention priority period allows inventors to seek protection in multiple countries without losing their original filing date.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** *Define a patent under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Mention the term of a patent in India.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- A patent is a **statutory right** granted by the government to an inventor, conferring the **exclusive right to make, use, sell, or import** the patented invention for a **limited period** (Section 2(1)(j) of the Patents Act, 1970). (2 marks)
- **Term of a patent** in India is **20 years** from the date of filing the application. (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** *List any three non-patentable inventions under Section 3 of the Patents Act, 1970.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The following are non-patentable inventions (1 mark each, any three):
1. **Frivolous inventions** — e.g., a perpetual motion machine (Section 3(1)).
2. **Inventions contrary to public order or morality** — e.g., a device for counterfeiting currency (Section 3(2)).
3. **Discovery of a scientific principle or abstract theory** — e.g., Einstein's theory of relativity (Section 3(3)).
4. **Computer program *per se*** — Section 3(k).
5. **Method of treatment of human beings or animals** — Section 3(i).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]**

### Question A (14 Marks)
*(a)* Discuss in detail the **general principles governing the grant of patents** under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. (7 marks)

*(b)* Explain the **three criteria of patentability** with suitable examples. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A(a) — General Principles (7 marks)

The general principles of patent law in India, as embodied in the Patents Act, 1970 (as amended in 2005 to comply with TRIPS), are:

**1. Principle of Patentability — The Three Pillars (2 marks)**
An invention, to be patentable, must satisfy three substantive criteria:
- **Novelty** (Section 2(1)(l)) — The invention must be new, not disclosed in any prior art anywhere in the world.
- **Inventive Step** (Section 2(1)(ja)) — The invention must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA).
- **Industrial Application** (Section 2(1)(ac)) — The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry.

**2. Principle of Territoriality (1 mark)**
Patent rights are strictly **territorial** — an Indian patent has no legal force in another country. The inventor must file separate applications in each country where protection is sought (or use the **PCT route** for international protection).

**3. Principle of First-to-File (1 mark)**
India follows the **first-to-file** system — the person who files the patent application first is entitled to the patent, regardless of who invented it first. (Section 11 of the Act, read with the priority date provisions.)

**4. Principle of Time Limitation (1 mark)**
The term of a patent is limited to **20 years** from the date of filing. After expiry, the invention falls into the **public domain** and can be freely used by anyone.

**5. Principle of Disclosure (1 mark)**
The patent is granted in exchange for **full public disclosure** of the invention. The complete specification (Section 10) must describe the invention in sufficient detail to enable a person skilled in the art to perform it.

**6. Principle of Independence of Patent Applications (1 mark)**
The grant of a patent in one country does not automatically grant patent rights in another country. Each application is examined independently on its own merits.

---

### Model Solution for Question A(b) — Three Criteria of Patentability (7 marks)

**1. Novelty (2 marks)**
Novelty means **absence of prior art**. The invention must not have been disclosed to the public, in any form, anywhere in the world, before the priority date.
- **Test:** *"Was the invention publicly known or disclosed before the filing date?"*
- **Example:** A new chemical compound never synthesized before satisfies novelty. However, if the same compound was described in a 2020 journal article, it loses novelty.
- **Anticipation rule:** A single prior art reference disclosing all essential features of the claim defeats novelty.
- **Grace period:** Disclosure at a recognized exhibition or academic conference within **12 months** before filing does not destroy novelty (Section 31).

**2. Inventive Step / Non-Obviousness (2.5 marks)**
An invention involves an inventive step if it is **not obvious to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA)**, having regard to the prior art as a whole.
- **Test:** *"Would PHOSITA, reading the prior art, have arrived at the invention in an obvious manner?"*
- **PHOSITA** is a hypothetical person of ordinary skill in the relevant technical field.
- **Example:** Combining aspirin and paracetamol in a new ratio may be novel but is obvious to a pharmacist — hence no inventive step. In contrast, a controlled-release drug delivery system that achieves a specific therapeutic effect through a non-obvious mechanism would involve an inventive step.
- **Secondary considerations:** Commercial success, long-felt need, failure of others, and unexpected technical effects support the presence of an inventive step.

**3. Industrial Application (1.5 marks)**
The invention must be capable of being **made or used in an industry** (including agriculture).
- **Test:** *"Can the invention be manufactured or applied in any kind of industry?"*
- **Example:** A novel solar panel design can be manufactured in a semiconductor fabrication plant — industrial application satisfied. A pure mathematical theorem or a philosophical principle has no industrial application.
- **Practical requirement:** The invention must have a **specific, substantial, and credible utility** — a mere speculative use is not sufficient.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The three criteria — **Novelty, Inventive Step, and Industrial Application** — are **cumulative and independent** requirements. A single failure is enough to defeat the patent application. These criteria, codified in the Patents Act, 1970, align with the **TRIPS Agreement (Article 27)** and form the universal standard for patentability.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
*(a)* Explain the concept of **Patent Specification**. Differentiate between **Provisional Specification** and **Complete Specification**. (7 marks)

*(b)* *"A patent is a contract between the inventor and the state."* Discuss this statement, highlighting the **rights of a patentee** and the **obligations of disclosure**. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B(a) — Patent Specification (7 marks)

**1. Definition of Patent Specification (1 mark)**
A **patent specification** is a written description of the invention filed with the Patent Office. It is the foundational document that discloses the invention and defines the scope of legal protection. Section 9 and Section 10 of the Patents Act, 1970 govern specifications.

**2. Provisional Specification (3 marks)**
- **Definition (Section 9):** A provisional specification is a **placeholder document** filed to establish the **priority date** of the invention.
- **Key Features:**
  - Does NOT contain the full disclosure of the invention.
  - Describes the invention in a **general, brief manner**.
  - Establishes the **priority date** — the date against which prior art is assessed.
  - Allows the inventor **12 months** to refine the invention and file the complete specification.
- **Use Case:** Useful when the inventor has a concept but is still working on the details. Filing a provisional specification gives the inventor a **first-mover advantage** without committing to the final claims.
- **Limitation:** A patent application with only a provisional specification **cannot proceed to grant** — a complete specification is mandatory.

**3. Complete Specification (2 marks)**
- **Definition (Section 10):** A complete specification is the **final, full disclosure** of the invention, including the description, claims, drawings (if any), and abstract.
- **Key Features:**
  - Must be filed within **12 months** of the provisional specification.
  - Must disclose the invention in a manner **sufficient for a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA) to perform it**.
  - Must include **claims** that define the legal scope of the patent.
  - Must disclose the **best method of performing the invention** (Section 10(4)).
  - Forms the **basis of patent examination and grant**.

**4. Key Differences (1 mark)**

| **Aspect** | **Provisional** | **Complete** |
|---|---|---|
| Filing deadline | At the start of the 12-month window | Within 12 months of provisional |
| Detail level | Brief, general description | Full, enabling disclosure |
| Claims | NOT required | Required |
| Purpose | Establish priority date | Final disclosure for grant |

---

### Model Solution for Question B(b) — Patent as a Contract (7 marks)

**1. Introduction — The Contract Metaphor (1 mark)**
A patent is often described as a **"contract between the inventor and the state"** because the inventor is granted a **time-limited monopoly** in exchange for **public disclosure** of the invention. The state, on behalf of the public, gets access to new knowledge; the inventor gets a competitive advantage for 20 years.

**2. Rights of the Patentee (Section 53) (3 marks)**
Upon grant, a patentee enjoys the following **exclusive rights**:
- **Right to make** the patented product or use the patented process.
- **Right to sell** the patented product or the product obtained by the patented process.
- **Right to offer for sale** the patented product.
- **Right to import** the patented product.
- **Right to license or assign** the patent to third parties (subject to conditions).
- **Right to sue for infringement** in case of unauthorized use by third parties.

These rights are **negative in nature** — they do not give the patentee the absolute right to *use* the invention (which may be restricted by other laws, such as drug licensing), but the right to **exclude others**.

**3. Obligations of Disclosure (3 marks)**
The price the patentee pays for the monopoly is **full and faithful disclosure** of the invention:
- **Complete specification (Section 10):** Must describe the invention in sufficient detail to enable a person skilled in the art to perform it.
- **Best mode (Section 10(4)):** The patentee must disclose the **best method** of performing the invention known to them.
- **Information regarding foreign applications (Section 8):** The patentee must inform the Controller of any corresponding foreign applications.
- **Working of the patent (Section 146):** The patentee must file annual statements indicating whether the patent is being worked in India.

**4. Conclusion**
The "contract" interpretation is reinforced by the **revocation provisions (Section 64)** — if the patentee fails to disclose the best mode, or if the specification does not sufficiently disclose the invention, the patent can be revoked. This dual structure of **rights + obligations** ensures that the patent system serves its constitutional goal: **rewarding inventors while promoting public knowledge and technological progress**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing novelty with inventive step** — These are two DIFFERENT tests. A 2023 KTU answer sheet had a student write "Novelty means the invention is not obvious" — this is a **factual error** and will cost you 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting PHOSITA** — The inventive step test REQUIRES reference to the "person skilled in the art." Omitting this term is a common omission.
> 3. **Missing statutory sections** — Always cite the relevant Section number (e.g., Section 2(1)(j) for patent definition, Section 2(1)(l) for novelty). Examiners allocate marks for **statutory precision**.
> 4. **Ignoring territoriality** — A 14-mark answer on "general principles" that does not mention territoriality is incomplete. This is a toppers' differentiator.
> 5. **Confusing first-to-file with first-to-invent** — India is a **first-to-file** jurisdiction, not first-to-invent. Writing the opposite will cost you 1-2 marks.
> 6. **Listing 17 non-patentable categories without explaining any** — If the question asks for a "discussion," you must explain **at least 5-6 categories** with examples. Mere listing is insufficient for 7+ marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — General Principles of Patent Law**

- [ ] **Patent definition** — Section 2(1)(j) — statutory right to exclude.
- [ ] **Term of patent** — **20 years** from the date of filing.
- [ ] **Three pillars of patentability** — Novelty, Inventive Step, Industrial Application.
- [ ] **Novelty** — Section 2(1)(l) — worldwide, single reference, 12-month grace period for exhibitions.
- [ ] **Inventive Step** — Section 2(1)(ja) — non-obvious to **PHOSITA**.
- [ ] **Industrial Application** — Section 2(1)(ac) — capable of industrial use.
- [ ] **First-to-file system** — Section 6 and 11 — priority based on filing date, not invention date.
- [ ] **Non-patentable inventions** — 17 categories under Section 3 (know the major ones: computer program per se, mathematical method, business method, plants/animals, atomic energy, methods of treatment).
- [ ] **True and First Inventor** — Section 6 — only the inventor (or assignee / legal rep / PCT applicant) can apply.
- [ ] **Patent rights** — Section 53 — make, use, sell, offer for sale, import.
- [ ] **Provisional specification** — Section 9 — establishes priority date, 12 months to complete.
- [ ] **Complete specification** — Section 10 — full disclosure, claims, best mode, abstract.
- [ ] **Territoriality** — patents are jurisdiction-specific.
- [ ] **Time limitation** — 20-year monopoly, then public domain.
- [ ] **Disclosure** — the price of the monopoly.
- [ ] **PHOSITA** — Person of Ordinary Skill In The Art — the legal standard for non-obviousness.
- [ ] **Best mode requirement** — Section 10(4) — must disclose the best method of performing the invention.
- [ ] **Grace period** — Section 31 — 12 months for exhibition or academic disclosure.
- [ ] **Anticipation rule** — a single prior art reference disclosing all essential features defeats novelty.
- [ ] **Independent invention tie-breaker** — Section 6(2) — first claim received wins.
- [ ] **Revocation grounds** — Section 64 — non-disclosure, insufficient disclosure, lack of novelty, lack of inventive step, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering any "general principles" question, structure your response as: **(1) Definition → (2) Statutory basis → (3) Three pillars → (4) Rights and obligations → (5) Conclusion.** This 5-part structure is the examiner's gold standard and consistently scores 12+/14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Author of Copyright
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Author of Copyright

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The term **"Author"** in copyright law is a *statutorily defined* concept. It does NOT carry the ordinary English meaning of a writer. Under the **Indian Copyright Act, 1957**, the definition is codified in **Section 2(d)**. The Act deliberately defines the author *differently for each class of work*, because the creative contribution differs with the medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Definition (Section 2(d), Indian Copyright Act, 1957):**
> *"author"* means, in relation to a literary, dramatic or musical work, the person who creates/compiles the work; in relation to an artistic work, the artist; in relation to a photograph, the photographer; in relation to a cinematograph film, the person who is the owner of the film at the time of its completion; in relation to a sound recording, the producer; in relation to any work made by an author in the course of his employment under a contract of service, the **employer** (subject to agreement to the contrary).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the "author" as the **origin point of creativity**, like a well that gives water. The well (author) is where the water (idea expressed in tangible form) originates. Now imagine a building with many wells — each room has its own well because the medium of "water extraction" differs:
- For a **novel**, the well is the novelist.
- For a **painting**, the well is the painter.
- For a **film**, the well is the film's *first owner* (the producer), because films require orchestration of multiple talents and massive capital.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Students:**
> The "author" is the **creator**, but the "owner of copyright" is the **rights-holder**. They MAY be the same person OR different persons (e.g., in works made for hire, the employer is the owner but the employee is the creator).

## 1.3 High-Yield Snapshot

- **Author ≠ Owner** (very common KTU trap question).
- Definition is **category-specific** — one universal definition does NOT exist.
- The Indian statute has **12 sub-clauses** within Section 2(d) mapping each category of work to its author.
- **Joint authorship** is separately dealt with under **Section 5**.
- A **pseudonymous or anonymous** author is still an author; the law protects the work, not the identity.
- The author is the **first owner** of copyright (Section 17) unless assigned.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Author" Matrix — Section 2(d) Decomposed

The Indian Copyright Act, 1957 provides a **category-wise definition** of author. The following exhaustive breakdown is a **high-yield KTU table** that has appeared repeatedly in university examinations.

| Sl. | Class of Work | Who is the "Author" | Practical Example |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Literary, Dramatic, or Musical work (not computer program) | The **person who creates** it | Novelist, Playwright, Composer |
| 2 | Computer Program / Software | The **author/programmer** (not the company) | The coder who writes the source code |
| 3 | Artistic work (other than photograph) | The **artist** (e.g., painter, sculptor) | M.F. Husain for a painting |
| 4 | Photograph | The **photographer** | The press photographer at a cricket match |
| 5 | Cinematograph Film | The **owner of the film at the time of completion** (i.e., producer) | Yash Raj Films for a movie |
| 6 | Sound Recording | The **producer** | T-Series for a music album |
| 7 | Government Work | The **Government** (Union or State) | A Govt. of India publication |
| 8 | Work of International Organization | The **International Organization** | UN, WHO, World Bank publication |
| 9 | Broadcast (Radio/TV) | The **broadcaster** | All India Radio (AIR) |
| 10 | Typographical arrangement of a published edition | The **publisher** | The publisher of a textbook |
| 11 | Work made in course of employment | The **employer** (subject to contrary agreement) | Software engineer → IT company |
| 12 | Anonymous/Pseudonymous work | Still the **actual creator** (the public domain protection differs) | The author using pen name "Aravind" |

## 2.2 Joint Authorship — Section 5

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 5 Definition:** *"Joint authorship"* means two or more persons **collaborating with the intent to merge their contributions** into a unified, inseparable work. Mere parallel creation is NOT joint authorship.

### Conditions for Joint Authorship (ALL must be satisfied):
1. **Collaboration** — there must be a shared creative engagement.
2. **Common Intent** — the authors must intend their contributions to be merged.
3. **Inseparability** — the contributions cannot be separated and used independently.
4. **Equal Share** — unless agreed otherwise, the rights are held **jointly** and a single joint author cannot exploit the work without the consent of others.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Example:** Two lyricists write a duet song together — they are joint authors. But if one writes Verse 1 and the other writes Verse 2 separately, the parts are **separable**, and they are NOT joint authors — they are co-owners of independent works.

## 2.3 Anonymous and Pseudonymous Works — Section 2(d) & Section 55

- **Anonymous Work:** Author identity is NOT disclosed.
- **Pseudonymous Work:** Author uses a fictitious name (pen name).
- In BOTH cases, the publisher whose name appears on the work is deemed the **agent** of the author, empowered to act on the author's behalf to protect the copyright — provided the true author's identity is not revealed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Statutory Reference | Core Take-Away |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Author Definition | **Section 2(d)** | Category-specific, 12 sub-clauses |
| Joint Author | **Section 5** | Collaboration + Common Intent + Inseparability |
| First Ownership | **Section 17** | Author is the first owner; exception: employer in work-for-hire |
| Assignment of Copyright | **Section 19** | Must be in writing, signed, with territory + duration specified |
| Anonymous/ Pseudonymous | **Section 55** | Publisher = deemed agent of author |
| Term of Protection (literary) | **Section 22** | Life of the author + **60 years** post-mortem |
| Term for Films/Sound Recordings | **Section 25** | **60 years** from date of publication |
| Government Work Term | **Section 28** | **60 years** from date of publication |
| Moral Rights | **Section 57** (right of paternity + integrity) | **Inalienable** — remain with the author even after assignment |
| Joint Owner Exploitation | **Section 5(2)** | Each joint owner can independently sue for infringement |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Logical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of the "Author Identification Algorithm"

In a KTU exam, when given a work, you must systematically identify the author. Follow this **deterministic 4-step logical tree**:

### Step 1: Identify the Class of Work
Ask: *Is the work literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, photographic, cinematographic, sound recording, broadcast, or typographical?*

### Step 2: Map to Section 2(d) Sub-Clause
Consult the table in Section 2.1 above. The "author" is the person/entity listed in the corresponding sub-clause.

### Step 3: Check for Special Cases
- Is the work created in the course of employment? → **Employer is author/owner** (subject to agreement to the contrary).
- Is the work a government work? → **Government is the author.**
- Is the work of an international organization? → **The organization is the author.**

### Step 4: Verify Authorship vs Ownership
Apply **Section 17**:
- The author is the **first owner** of the copyright by default.
- Ownership can be transferred via **assignment** (Section 19) or **will/operation of law** (death of author).
- In **work-for-hire** situations, the employer is the **first owner**, NOT the author.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** *Rohit, an employee of Infosys Ltd., writes a software module during office hours using company resources. Who is the "author" and who is the "owner" of the copyright?*

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

1. **Class of Work:** The work is a **computer program** (literary work under Section 2(o)).

2. **Sub-Clause Mapping:** Section 2(d)(i) — author of a literary work is the **person who creates** it → Rohit.

3. **Special Case Check:** The work was made in the **course of employment** under a contract of service. This triggers **Section 17(a)**, under which the **employer is the first owner** of the copyright, NOT the employee. (Note: this is a departure from the Section 2(d) definition, which deals with authorship identity, while Section 17 deals with ownership.)

4. **Final Answer:**
   - **Author** (creator): **Rohit**
   - **Owner of Copyright** (rights-holder): **Infosys Ltd.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Rights Flow

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Author (Section 2(d))} &\xrightarrow{\text{Creative Act}} \text{Work in Tangible Form} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Section 17 Default}} \text{Author = First Owner} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Exception: Employment}} \text{Employer = First Owner} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Section 19 Assignment}} \text{New Owner (in writing)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Section 57 Moral Rights}} \text{Author retains moral rights ALWAYS}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Moral rights (right of paternity + right of integrity) are **perpetual, inalienable, and non-transferable**. Even if the author assigns economic rights, the author retains the moral right to claim authorship and to resist distortion of the work.

## 3.4 Logical Proof: Why Films Have "Producer" as Author

Films (cinematograph works) are a **collaboration of many talents** — director, screenplay writer, dialogue writer, music composer, cinematographer, actors. Determining "who wrote the film?" is impossible. Hence, the statute **deems the producer (the risk-bearer and the entity that orchestrates the film)** as the author for legal certainty. This is codified in **Section 2(d)(v)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Identifying the Author (Section 2(d) Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Identify Class of Work] --> nodeB{Type of Work?}
    nodeB -- Literary/Dramatic/Musical --> nodeC1[Author = Creator]
    nodeB -- Computer Program --> nodeC2[Author = Programmer]
    nodeB -- Artistic Work --> nodeC3[Author = Artist]
    nodeB -- Photograph --> nodeC4[Author = Photographer]
    nodeB -- Cinematograph Film --> nodeC5[Author = Producer / Film Owner]
    nodeB -- Sound Recording --> nodeC6[Author = Producer]
    nodeB -- Government Work --> nodeC7[Author = Government]
    nodeB -- International Org --> nodeC8[Author = The Organization]
    nodeB -- Broadcast --> nodeC9[Author = Broadcaster]
    nodeB -- Typographical Arrangement --> nodeC10[Author = Publisher]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD{Employment Relationship?}
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeC3 --> nodeD
    nodeC4 --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[Employer = First Owner per Section 17]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF[Author = First Owner per Section 17]
    nodeE --> nodeG{Moral Rights under Section 57?}
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -- YES ALWAYS --> nodeH[Author retains moral rights inalienably]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Authorship vs Ownership Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeCreator[Creator / Author under Section 2d] -- Section 17 Default --> nodeOwner[Owner of Copyright]
    nodeCreator -- Section 57 --> nodeMoral[Moral Rights Retained Forever]
    nodeEmployer[Employer in Work for Hire] -- Section 17 Exception --> nodeOwner
    nodeOwner -- Section 19 Assignment --> nodeAssignee[New Owner via Written Agreement]
    nodeMoral -.Cannot be assigned.-> nodeAssignee
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Joint Authorship Test (Section 5)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Two or More Persons Create Work] --> nodeCollab{Collaboration Present?}
    nodeCollab -- NO --> nodeNotJoint[NOT Joint Authors - Independent Co Owners]
    nodeCollab -- YES --> nodeIntent{Common Intent to Merge?}
    nodeIntent -- NO --> nodeNotJoint
    nodeIntent -- YES --> nodeInsep{Inseparable Contribution?}
    nodeInsep -- NO --> nodeNotJoint
    nodeInsep -- YES --> nodeJoint[Joint Authors per Section 5]
    nodeJoint --> nodeRights[Each Joint Owner can Independently Sue for Infringement]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** 
**Define the term "author" under Section 2(d) of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957. Why is the definition given class-wise?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Under Section 2(d) of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957, "author" means:
- In relation to literary, dramatic or musical work (other than a computer program), the person who creates it.
- In relation to an artistic work, the artist.
- In relation to a photograph, the photographer.
- In relation to a cinematograph film, the person who is the owner of the film at the time of completion.
- In relation to a sound recording, the producer.
- For works of government, public undertakings, and international organizations, the respective organization.

The definition is **class-wise** because the nature of creative input differs across mediums. A photograph's creative input is visual framing, while a film's input is multi-disciplinary orchestration. A single universal definition would fail to capture these distinctions. (1 mark each for two definitions + 1 mark for explanation)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between the "author" of a work and the "owner" of copyright.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Author | Owner |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Source of Right | Creative act (Section 2(d)) | Statutory assignment or law (Section 19) |
| Default Identity | Always the creator | The author by default (Section 17) |
| Special Case | Remains the creator | In work-for-hire, employer is owner (Section 17(a)) |
| Moral Rights | Always retained by author (Section 57) | May or may not include moral rights |
| Transfer | Moral rights are inalienable | Economic rights can be assigned/licensed |

(2 marks for tabular distinction + 1 mark for relevant statutory reference)

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various categories of works and the corresponding "author" as defined under Section 2(d) of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957. Cite at least five categories with practical examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of joint authorship under Section 5 of the Act. What are the essential conditions that must be satisfied for two or more persons to be called joint authors? Illustrate with an example.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks (Categories of Authors):**

The Indian Copyright Act, 1957 defines "author" in **Section 2(d)** in a **category-specific manner**, as follows:

1. **Literary, Dramatic, or Musical Works (other than computer programs):** The author is the person who creates or composes the work. *Example:* William Shakespeare is the author of Hamlet; A.R. Rahman is the author of a musical score. **(1 mark)**

2. **Computer Programs:** The author is the person who creates the program, i.e., the programmer. *Example:* The coder who writes a banking software's source code. **(1 mark)**

3. **Artistic Works:** The author is the artist (painter, sculptor, architect of artistic works). *Example:* M.F. Husain for a Madhubani painting. **(1 mark)**

4. **Photographs:** The author is the photographer. *Example:* The press photographer who captures a cricket match. **(1 mark)**

5. **Cinematograph Films:** The author is the person who is the **owner of the film at the time of its completion** (typically the producer). *Example:* Dharma Productions for a Bollywood film. **(1 mark)**

6. **Sound Recordings:** The author is the **producer** of the sound recording. *Example:* T-Series for a music album. **(1 mark)**

7. **Government and International Organization Works:** The author is the **Government** or the **International Organization** respectively. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Joint Authorship):**

Section 5 of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957 defines joint authorship. The following **three essential conditions** must cumulatively be satisfied:

1. **Collaboration:** Two or more persons must collaborate in the creative process. Independent or parallel creation does NOT constitute joint authorship. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Common Intent:** The collaborators must share a common intention that their contributions shall be merged into a unified work. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Inseparable Contributions:** The contributions of the authors must be so intertwined that they cannot be separated and used independently. **(1.5 marks)**

**Rights of Joint Authors (Section 5(2)):** Each joint author is deemed to be a co-owner of the copyright, and a single joint author can independently sue for infringement without joining the others, unless the court directs otherwise. **(1.5 marks)**

**Illustration:** *Example:* Two authors, A and B, collaborate to write a duet song where the music is composed jointly. Here, A and B satisfy all three conditions, and they are joint authors. In contrast, if A writes the music and B writes the lyrics independently and then combines them as a finished song, the contributions are separable, and they are NOT joint authors but co-owners of a composite work. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Critically analyze the concept of "work made for hire" under the Indian Copyright Act. Who is the author and who is the owner of copyright in such a work? Discuss with relevant statutory provisions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the moral rights of an author under Section 57 of the Indian Copyright Act. Why are these rights considered inalienable?

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks (Work Made for Hire):**

The Indian Copyright Act, 1957, while not using the term "work made for hire" directly, deals with this concept under **Section 17(a)**. The provision states that when a work is made in the course of employment under a contract of service, the **employer** is the **first owner of the copyright**, not the employee/creator. **(2 marks)**

**Author vs. Owner:**
- The **author** is the actual creator — the employee.
- The **first owner** of the copyright is the **employer**, who bears the cost of creation and is the one to commercially exploit the work.
- This can be altered by an **agreement to the contrary** (e.g., an employment contract that specifically assigns ownership to the employee). **(2 marks)**

**Critical Analysis:**
The concept recognizes the economic reality that employers invest capital, infrastructure, and time, and should be entitled to the fruits of the investment. However, the Act preserves the author's **moral rights** (Section 57), which cannot be transferred, ensuring attribution and integrity even in work-for-hire scenarios. **(1 mark)**

**Relevant Statutory Provisions:** Section 2(d), Section 17(a), Section 19 (Assignment), Section 57 (Moral Rights). **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Moral Rights):**

Section 57 of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957 grants two **moral rights** to the author, independent of the economic rights:

1. **Right of Paternity (Right of Attribution):** The author has the right to claim authorship of the work and to prevent others from attributing the work to someone else. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Right of Integrity:** The author has the right to prevent any distortion, mutilation, or modification of the work that would be prejudicial to the author's honor or reputation. **(1.5 marks)**

**Why These Rights Are Inalienable:**
- Moral rights are **personal to the author** and are tied to the author's reputation and dignity. **(1 mark)**
- These rights are **independent of the copyright itself** and continue to subsist even after the copyright has been assigned. **(1 mark)**
- They are **perpetual and non-transferable**, recognizing the personal connection between the author and their work. **(1 mark)**
- In case of infringement of moral rights, the author can seek remedies including injunctions and damages. **(1 mark)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Author with Owner:** A 3-mark question often tests this distinction. Always cite both Section 2(d) (for author) and Section 17 (for ownership).
> 2. **Missing the "Agreement to the Contrary" Clause:** In work-for-hire questions, students forget to mention that Section 17(a) is *subject to* an agreement to the contrary.
> 3. **Listing Only 4-5 Categories:** Section 2(d) has 12 sub-clauses. Listing only 4-5 in a 7-mark question is insufficient — aim for 6-7 categories.
> 4. **Ignoring Moral Rights:** Section 57 is a **favourite KTU question**. Always discuss both right of paternity and right of integrity.
> 5. **Confusing "Joint Authorship" with "Co-ownership":** Joint authorship requires inseparability + common intent. Co-ownership can exist without joint authorship (e.g., separable contributions).
> 6. **Forgetting Anonymous/Pseudonymous Rule:** Under Section 55, the named publisher is a *deemed agent* of the anonymous/pseudonymous author, not the owner.

### High-Density Revision Checklist:

- [ ] **Section 2(d):** Author = creator, defined class-wise (12 sub-clauses)
- [ ] **Section 5:** Joint authorship = collaboration + common intent + inseparability
- [ ] **Section 17:** First owner of copyright = author; exception: employer in work-for-hire
- [ ] **Section 19:** Assignment must be in writing, signed, with territory & duration
- [ ] **Section 22:** Term for literary/dramatic/musical/artistic works = life of author + 60 years
- [ ] **Section 25:** Term for films/sound recordings = 60 years from publication
- [ ] **Section 55:** Anonymous/pseudonymous work — publisher is the deemed agent
- [ ] **Section 57:** Moral rights (paternity + integrity) are inalienable and perpetual
- [ ] **Films:** Author = producer (owner at the time of completion)
- [ ] **Software:** Author = programmer (NOT the IT company, except under Section 17)
- [ ] **Government Works:** Author = Government (Union or State)
- [ ] **Joint Authors' Rights:** Each can independently sue for infringement (Section 5(2))

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Registration of Copyright
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Registration of Copyright

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Copyright Registration** is the formal, statutory recording of a copyrightable work with the **Registrar of Copyrights**, appointed by the Central Government under the **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended by the Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012). The statutory framework for registration is laid down in **Sections 44 to 50A** of the Act, read with the **Copyright Rules, 2012** (as amended by the Copyright (Amendment) Rules, 2016).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Unlike a patent (where registration is a *condition precedent* for protection) or a trademark (where registration confers exclusive right), **copyright subsists automatically upon creation of the work**. Registration under Section 44 is **voluntary**, **not mandatory**, and **not a condition precedent** for acquiring copyright.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The House Deed Analogy":**
> Imagine you build a beautiful house on land you lawfully own. The moment the roof goes up and the doors are fitted, the house is **yours** — you don't need a government stamp to make it yours. However, the moment a neighbor claims *they* built the same house, you walk into the Sub-Registrar's office and **register your deed of ownership**. You are not "creating" new ownership — you are creating **indisputable, government-backed evidence** of ownership.
>
> **Copyright works exactly the same way.** The moment a programmer writes 10 lines of original code, or a poet pens a new ghazal, or a cinematographer shoots a film — copyright *automatically* vests in them. Registration at the **Copyright Office, New Delhi** is simply the "deed" — strong, prima facie, admissible evidence in any court of law.

## 1.3 Statutory Anchor — The Copyright Office, India

The **Copyright Office** is located at **Boudhik Sampada Bhawan, Plot No. 32, Sector 14, Dwarka, New Delhi – 110078**. It functions under the **Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)**, Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India. The office is headed by the **Registrar of Copyrights**.

| Statutory Entity | Reference |
| :--- | :--- |
| Enactment | **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended in 1983, 1984, 1992, 1994, 1999, 2012) |
| Procedural Rules | **Copyright Rules, 2012** (amended in 2016) |
| Registration Section | **Sections 44 – 50A** |
| Territorial Applicability | **Whole of India** (extends to Jammu & Kashmir post-2019) |
| Filing Route | Offline (Copyright Office) + **Online via e-filing portal (copyright.gov.in)** |

## 1.4 Historical Background

> [!NOTE]
> The Copyright Act, 1957 replaced the **Indian Copyright Act, 1914** (which was an extension of the UK Copyright Act, 1911). India became a signatory to the **Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works, 1886** in **1928** and to the **Universal Copyright Convention (UCC), 1952** in **1957**. The 2012 Amendment brought Indian law into harmony with the **WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT), 1996** and the **WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty (WPPT), 1996**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Register? — The Six Statutory Advantages

Although registration is voluntary, the Act accords specific legal advantages (Section 48 + Section 55 of the Copyright Act, 1957):

1. **Prima Facie Evidence (Section 48):** The Register of Copyrights maintained at the Copyright Office serves as **prima facie evidence** of the particulars entered therein in all courts and before any authority. The *evidentiary weight* shifts the burden of disproving ownership to the alleged infringer.
2. **Constructive Notice (Section 51):** Registration constitutes **public notice** of the copyright claim, preventing an alleged infringer from pleading *innocent infringement* (a defense that can mitigate damages).
3. **Jurisdictional Convenience:** Suit for infringement can be filed in a court within whose jurisdiction the plaintiff actually and voluntarily resides or carries on business **OR** where the registered work is registered.
4. **Statutory Damages:** Registration enables the plaintiff to claim **statutory damages and accounts of profits** more effectively.
5. **Customs Recordal:** Registration forms the basis for **recordal with the Customs Department (IPR Cell)** to block pirated imports at Indian ports.
6. **Foreign Enforcement:** A registered Indian work gets smoother enforcement under **Berne Convention** and **TRIPS** reciprocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Alert:** Examiners frequently test whether students know that *registration does not create copyright* — it merely **recognizes and evidences** it. Memorize: *"Copyright subsists; registration records."*

## 2.2 Who Can Apply for Registration? (Section 44)

Any of the following persons may apply in the prescribed manner:

- The **author** of the work.
- The **legal representative** of a deceased author.
- The **owner** of the copyright (assignee or legal heir).
- Any person **authorized in writing** by the author/owner.
- In case of a **work made for hire** (cinematograph film, sound recording, work done during employment under a contract of service), the **employer/producer/publisher**, unless a contrary agreement exists.

## 2.3 Procedure for Registration (Form I → Certificate)

The procedure is statutorily prescribed under the **Copyright Rules, 2012 (Rules 6–12)**.

| Step | Statutory Rule | Action | Duration |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **1** | Rule 6 | File **Form I** (Application for Registration) in **triplicate** (offline) or via online portal | Day 0 |
| **2** | Rule 6(2) | Pay prescribed **fee** (varies by work type — see Schedule) | Same day |
| **3** | Rule 7 | Receipt of application acknowledged; **diary number** allotted | Day 1 – 7 |
| **4** | Rule 7(2) | **14-day objection window** opens in the Copyright Office Journal | Day 8 – 21 |
| **5** | Rule 9 | If no objection → application sent to **Examiner of Copyrights** | Day 22 |
| **6** | Rule 10 | Examiner verifies novelty, ownership, and formal defects | Day 23 – 90 |
| **7** | Rule 11 | **Hearing notice** (Form V) issued if defects noted; applicant responds in Form VI | Day 91 – 120 |
| **8** | Rule 12 | On satisfaction → entry made in the **Register of Copyrights** | Day 120 – 180 |
| **9** | Section 45 | **Extract of Register** (Certificate) issued to applicant | Day 180 – 210 |

> [!NOTE]
> The Copyright Rules, 2012 do **not** prescribe a fixed statutory timeline; in practice, registration takes **6 to 12 months** depending on workload and objections.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Copyright Registration

| Parameter | Statutory Reference | Standard Metric / Value |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Governing Statute | — | **Copyright Act, 1957** (44 of 1957) |
| Registration Provisions | — | **Sections 44 – 50A** |
| Procedure Rules | — | **Copyright Rules, 2012** (as amended 2016) |
| Mandatory Application Form | Rule 6 | **Form I** |
| Application Copies | Rule 6(1) | **Triplicate (3 copies)** — but 1 copy suffices if filed online |
| Power of Attorney Form | Rule 6(2) | **Form II** (when filed by an Agent/Attorney) |
| Statement of Particulars (Work) | Rule 6(3) | **Form III** |
| Statement of Further Particulars (Author) | Rule 6(4) | **Form IV** |
| Hearing Notice | Rule 11 | **Form V** |
| Reply to Hearing | Rule 11 | **Form VI** |
| Form for Rectification of Register | Section 50 | **Form VII** |
| Re-registration on Change of Particulars | Rule 13 | **Form VIII** |
| Registrar's Office Location | Section 43 | **Boudhik Sampada Bhawan, Dwarka, New Delhi** |
| Government Department Application | Section 47 | **Exempted from fees** (no fee required) |
| Correction Application | Section 50 | **Form VII** + 14-day notice |
| Default Time for Registration | — | **No fixed statutory period** (in practice 6–12 months) |
| Evidentiary Status | Section 48 | **Prima facie evidence** in all courts |
| Infringement Suit Jurisdiction | Section 62 | Where plaintiff resides **OR** where work is registered |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — The "Section 44" Trap:** Do not confuse **Section 44 (Application for Registration)** with **Section 45 (Entry in Register)**. The application is filed under s. 44; the registration *entry* is made under s. 45. Examiners love this distinction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Industry (IT/AI):** Registering source code, algorithms, and user interfaces under Class "Computer Programs" (literary work) provides a crucial layer of protection in an industry with rampant code plagiarism.
- **Films & OTT Content:** Production houses register cinematograph films, soundtracks, and screenplays to block piracy and license rights globally.
- **Hardware & Embedded Systems:** Firmware documentation, hardware description languages (HDL code), and PCB layouts are registered.
- **Academic Publishing:** Universities register theses, e-learning modules, and MOOKs (Massive Open Online Courses).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Procedure for Copyright Registration (Form I Walkthrough)

Below is the **algorithmic procedural derivation** that students must memorize in flow form for the KTU board exam.

### **Step 1 — Identification of Applicant (Rule 6(1))**

The first legal determination is **who** is eligible to file. The eligible persons, in descending order of legal preference, are:

$$
\text{Eligible Applicant} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Author}, & \text{if author = owner (default rule)} \\
\text{Legal Representative}, & \text{if author is deceased} \\
\text{Owner (Assignee)}, & \text{if rights have been assigned in writing} \\
\text{Licensee with Power}, & \text{if expressly authorized} \\
\text{Employer/Producer}, & \text{if work made for hire}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This hierarchy mirrors the chain-of-title concept from patent and trademark law. The applicant must, at the time of filing, **stand in the legal shoes of the copyright owner**. Any mis-step here renders the registration *rectifiable but vulnerable to a cancellation suit*.

### **Step 2 — Drafting Form I — The Application (Rule 6(1))**

Form I is a **statutory declaration** containing the following 14 mandatory fields:

1. Name, address, and nationality of the applicant.
2. Name, address, and nationality of the author (if different from applicant).
3. Nature of the applicant's interest in the copyright (author / owner / assignee).
4. Class and description of the work.
5. Title of the work.
6. Language of the work.
7. **Date of creation** of the work.
8. **Date of first publication** (with country name) — if published.
9. Name, address, and nationality of the publisher.
10. Names, addresses, and nationalities of the **other co-authors** (if joint authorship).
11. Whether the work is an **artistic work** (attach two copies of the work).
12. Whether the work is a **cinematograph film or sound recording** (attach two copies of the work).
13. Whether the work is to be **registered as a published or unpublished** work.
14. Signature of the applicant / authorized agent, with **place and date**.

### **Step 3 — Drafting Form III — Statement of Particulars of the Work (Rule 6(3))**

Form III acts as the **technical dossier** describing the work. It must include:

- A statement whether the work has been previously published.
- A statement of the **location** of the work.
- A statement of the **language** of the work.
- A statement of any **prior contractual obligations** (e.g., assignment deed, work-for-hire clause).

### **Step 4 — Drafting Form IV — Statement of Further Particulars of the Author (Rule 6(4))**

Form IV must contain the **biographical chain of authorship**, including:

- Whether the author is alive or deceased.
- Date of death (if applicable).
- Place of residence at the time of creation.
- Citizenship / domicile.
- A declaration that the applicant is the *true and first* owner or is the authorized agent.

### **Step 5 — Fee Payment (Rule 6(2) + Schedule of Fees)**

The fee structure is prescribed in the **First Schedule** of the Copyright Rules, 2012 (as amended by the 2016 Amendment Rules). The fee is **per work**:

$$
\text{Total Fee} = \text{Application Fee} + \text{Hearing Fee (if any)} + \text{Certificate Fee}
$$

| Type of Work | Application Fee (₹) | Online e-filing Fee (₹) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic | **500** | **400** |
| Sound Recording | **2000** | **1500** |
| Cinematograph Film | **2000** | **1500** |
| Government Department Application | **0** (Exempted) | **0** (Exempted) |
| Correction / Rectification Application | **500** | **400** |

> **Conversion Logic:** The 2016 Amendment significantly reduced e-filing fees to promote digital adoption. The fee is paid via **Demand Draft / IPO / e-payment** in favour of *"Registrar of Copyrights, New Delhi"*.

### **Step 6 — Filing & Diary Number (Rule 7(1))**

Upon physical receipt at the Copyright Office, the application is **diary-numbered** in the order of receipt. The **diary number** is the unique identifier for all future correspondence. For e-filings, the system auto-generates the diary number and sends an **SMS / email acknowledgment**.

### **Step 7 — Public Objection Window (Rule 7(2))**

The application is **published in the Copyright Office's online journal** (accessible at copyright.gov.in) for **30 days** (Rule 7(2) — note: the journal is open continuously, but the objection window in practice is 30 days from publication).

Any aggrieved person may file an **objection** stating:

- That the particulars in the application are **incorrect**.
- That the applicant has **no legal right** to register the work.
- That the work is **not original** or is not copyrightable.

If an objection is received, the Registrar issues a **hearing notice (Form V)** to both parties.

### **Step 8 — Examination by Examiner of Copyrights (Rule 9)**

If no objection is filed, the Examiner of Copyrights verifies:

- **Formality check:** Is Form I complete? Are Form III and IV attached? Is the fee paid?
- **Substantive check:** Is the work copyrightable under Section 13? Is the applicant the rightful owner?
- **Defect check:** Any prior conflicting registration?

If defects are noted, the Examiner issues a **show-cause notice (Form V)** and grants the applicant a **30-day window** to respond in **Form VI**.

### **Step 9 — Entry in Register (Rule 12 + Section 45)**

On satisfaction, the Registrar makes an **entry in the Register of Copyrights**. The Register is maintained at the Copyright Office, New Delhi, and contains:

- The name, address, and nationality of the author and owner.
- The title of the work.
- The class and description of the work.
- The date of creation and publication.
- Any assignment, license, or mortgage relating to the copyright.

### **Step 10 — Issuance of Certificate (Section 45(2))**

The Registrar issues an **Extract of the Register of Copyrights** (commonly called the **Copyright Registration Certificate**) to the applicant. This certificate is **prima facie evidence** under Section 48.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Grounds for Refusal / Rectification

| Ground | Section | Effect |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| False / incorrect particulars | Section 50 | **Rectification** of the Register |
| No subsisting copyright at the time of registration | Section 13 | **Cancellation** |
| Work is not a "work" under the Act | Section 13 | **No registration possible** |
| Applicant is not the rightful owner | Section 17 | **Cancellation** |
| Work belongs to the public domain (e.g., government work) | Section 17(k) | **No copyright subsists** |
| Procedural defect (no fee, incomplete Form I) | Rule 9 | **Refusal with right to cure** |
| Objection from any aggrieved party | Rule 7(2) | **Hearing required** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A *cancellation* suit lies before the **Copyright Board** (now Appellate Board under the Tribunals Reforms Act, 2021 — jurisdiction transferred to the **High Court** for rectification, but the Copyright Board continues for certain disputes). For the KTU exam, cite **Section 50 of the Copyright Act, 1957** as the principal provision for rectification.

## 3.3 Special Case — Works Made for Hire (Section 17)

A subtle but high-yield area is the **work made for hire** doctrine. Under Section 17:

$$
\text{First Owner} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Author}, & \text{for all other categories of works} \\
\text{Employer / Publisher of work}, & \text{if work is made in the course of employment under a contract of service} \\
\text{Producer / Commissioner of film / sound recording}, & \text{for cinematograph films and sound recordings} \\
\text{Person at whose instance the work is made}, & \text{for photographs, engravings, portraits, etc.}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the *rebuttable presumption* rule. The default owner is the author, but statutory exceptions in Section 17(1) *and any written contract* to the contrary can shift ownership. For KTU, the magic phrase is: *"Section 17, subject to contract, vests ownership in the employer for works made in the course of employment."*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Copyright Registration Procedure (Form I → Certificate)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Author Creates Original Work"] --> B["Identify Eligible Applicant (Sec 44)"]
    B --> C["Draft Form I + Form III + Form IV"]
    C --> D["Attach Two Copies of the Work (if Artistic/Cinematograph)"]
    D --> E{"Choose Filing Mode"}
    E -->|Offline| F["File Triplicate at Copyright Office, New Delhi"]
    E -->|Online| G["E-File via copyright.gov.in Portal"]
    F --> H["Pay Prescribed Fee (DD/IPO/Online)"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Receive Diary Number (Rule 7)"]
    I --> J["Publication in Copyright Journal (Rule 7(2))"]
    J --> K{"Objection Received within 30 Days?"}
    K -->|YES| L["Issue Hearing Notice Form V to Both Parties"]
    L --> M["Applicant Replies via Form VI"]
    M --> N["Registrar Conducts Hearing"]
    N --> O{"Registrar Satisfied?"}
    K -->|NO| P["Forward to Examiner of Copyrights (Rule 9)"]
    P --> Q{"Examiner Notes Defects?"}
    Q -->|YES| L
    Q -->|NO| R["Recommend Entry in Register (Rule 12)"]
    O -->|YES| R
    O -->|NO| S["Refuse Application (Written Order)"]
    R --> T["Entry Made in Register of Copyrights (Sec 45)"]
    T --> U["Issue Extract / Certificate (Sec 45(2))"]
    U --> V["END: Pristine Copyright Registration Certificate Issued"]
    S --> W["END: Application Rejected — Appeal to High Court"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Copyright Office Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APPLICANT["Applicant Side"]
        A1["Author / Owner / Agent"]
        A2["Power of Attorney Form II"]
    end

    subgraph COPYRIGHT_OFFICE["Copyright Office, New Delhi (DPIIT)"]
        B1["Receiving Section: Diary Number"]
        B2["Journal Section: 30-Day Public Objection Window"]
        B3["Examination Branch: Examiner of Copyrights"]
        B4["Hearing Cell: Form V Notice & Form VI Reply"]
        B5["Registrar Final Order Entry under Section 45"]
        B6["Register of Copyrights Database Section 44"]
        B7["Certificate Issuance Section 45(2)"]
    end

    subgraph STAKEHOLDERS["External Stakeholders"]
        C1["General Public Objectors"]
        C2["Customs IPR Cell Recordal"]
        C3["District / High Court Evidentiary Use"]
        C4["Copyright Board Rectification Section 50"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> C2
    B7 --> C3
    B7 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Schematic — The Six Statutory Advantages of Registration

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Advantages of Copyright Registration))
    Evidence
      Prima facie under Section 48
      Shifts burden to infringer
    Notice
      Constructive notice Section 51
      Defeats innocent infringer plea
    Jurisdiction
      Suit filed where registered
    Damages
      Statutory damages easier to claim
    Customs
      Recordal for border seizure
    International
      Berne and TRIPS reciprocity
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Is registration of copyright mandatory under Indian law? Justify with statutory provisions."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

No, registration of copyright is **not mandatory** under Indian law. The Copyright Act, 1957 does not contain any provision that makes registration a pre-condition for acquiring copyright. **Section 13** of the Act provides that copyright shall subsist in any of the works mentioned therein from the moment of their creation, irrespective of registration.

However, **Section 44** provides for a voluntary registration system, and the **Register of Copyrights** maintained under **Section 45** serves as **prima facie evidence** under **Section 48** in any court proceeding. The 2024 scheme syllabus emphasizes that while registration is *optional*, it is *highly recommended* for evidentiary and enforcement purposes.

> **Distribution:** Definition (1 M) + Section 13/44 reference (1 M) + Evidentiary benefit (1 M).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List the various forms prescribed under the Copyright Rules, 2012 for the purpose of registration."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Copyright Rules, 2012 prescribe the following forms:

- **Form I** — Application for registration of a copyright work.
- **Form II** — Power of Attorney (when filed by an authorized agent).
- **Form III** — Statement of particulars of the work.
- **Form IV** — Statement of further particulars of the author.
- **Form V** — Notice of hearing issued by the Registrar.
- **Form VI** — Reply to the hearing notice by the applicant.
- **Form VII** — Application for rectification of the Register.
- **Form VIII** — Application for re-registration on change of particulars.

> **Distribution:** Listing 6 forms × 0.5 M each = 3 M. (Award 0.5 per correct form, 0 for extras or wrong forms.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **procedure for registration of copyright** under the Copyright Act, 1957, with reference to the relevant **forms, fees, and statutory provisions**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Examine the **evidentiary value and advantages** of copyright registration under the Act, with a special note on the **presumption of ownership** under **Section 48**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

The procedure for copyright registration is statutorily governed by **Sections 44–50A** of the **Copyright Act, 1957** read with the **Copyright Rules, 2012** (as amended in 2016).

**Step 1 — Determination of Applicant (Section 44):** The application can be made by the **author, legal representative, owner (assignee), or any person authorized in writing**. In the case of works made for hire, the **employer or producer** is the statutory applicant (Section 17).

**Step 2 — Filing of Application (Rule 6):** The applicant files **Form I** in triplicate (offline) or online via the e-filing portal at **copyright.gov.in**, accompanied by **Form III** (statement of particulars of the work) and **Form IV** (statement of further particulars of the author). Two copies of the work must be annexed in the case of artistic, cinematograph, or sound recording works.

**Step 3 — Payment of Fee (Rule 6(2)):** The fee is paid as per the **First Schedule**: ₹500 for literary/dramatic/musical/artistic works, ₹2,000 for cinematograph films and sound recordings, and **₹400/₹1,500** if filed online (concession under the 2016 Amendment). **Government departments are exempted from fees** (Section 47).

**Step 4 — Diary Number and Publication (Rule 7):** The application is allotted a **diary number** and published in the **Copyright Office Journal** for a **30-day objection window**.

**Step 5 — Examination (Rule 9):** The **Examiner of Copyrights** verifies form, ownership, and copyrightability under Section 13. Defects are communicated via **Form V** hearing notice, and the applicant replies via **Form VI**.

**Step 6 — Registration (Rule 12 + Section 45):** On satisfaction, the Registrar makes an entry in the **Register of Copyrights** and issues the **Extract of Register** (Certificate) under **Section 45(2)**.

In practice, the entire process takes **6 to 12 months**, although the Copyright Rules do not prescribe a fixed statutory timeline. *(7 M — Break-up: Identification of applicant 1 M; Form I/III/IV 1.5 M; Fee + Schedule 1.5 M; Diary/Journal/Examiner/Entry 2 M; Timeline 1 M.)*

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

Although copyright vests **automatically** upon creation under **Section 13**, the Act accords several specific legal advantages to a registered work:

**1. Prima Facie Evidence (Section 48):** The Register of Copyrights maintained at the Copyright Office is **admissible as prima facie evidence** of the particulars entered therein. In any civil or criminal proceeding, the court must take the Register as proof of the facts recorded. The *burden of disproving ownership* shifts to the alleged infringer.

**2. Constructive Notice (Section 51):** Registration operates as **public notice** of the copyright claim. The defense of "innocent infringement" — i.e., *I did not know the work was registered* — is statutorily defeated, allowing for higher damages.

**3. Jurisdictional Convenience (Section 62):** A suit for infringement may be instituted in any court within the local limits of which the plaintiff actually and voluntarily resides or carries on business, **or where the work is registered**. This is a critical strategic advantage for plaintiffs.

**4. Statutory Damages and Accounts:** While the Indian Act does not (unlike the US Copyright Act) provide for "statutory damages" per infringement, a registered copyright significantly eases the plaintiff's evidentiary burden in claiming **actual damages, additional damages, and accounts of profits** under the Copyright (Amendment) provisions.

**5. Customs Recordal:** Registration forms the foundation for recordal with the **Customs IPR Cell**, enabling the **seizure and confiscation of pirated goods** at Indian ports.

**6. International Enforcement:** A registered Indian work enjoys smoother enforcement under the **Berne Convention, 1886** and **TRIPS Agreement, 1994**, given that other member countries recognize Indian certificates on a reciprocal basis.

The Supreme Court in ***Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak (2008)*** held that the Register of Copyrights enjoys a **rebuttable presumption** of correctness, reinforcing the Section 48 evidentiary doctrine. *(7 M — Break-up: Section 48 presumption 2 M; Section 51 + damages 2 M; Jurisdiction + Customs 1.5 M; Case law + International 1.5 M.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice to Question A)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Critically examine the **concept of "work made for hire"** under the **Copyright Act, 1957**, with reference to **Section 17** and the **default ownership rules** for different classes of works. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **grounds and procedure for rectification of the Register of Copyrights** under **Section 50** of the Act, citing at least one **leading case law**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

The doctrine of "work made for hire" finds statutory expression in **Section 17** of the Copyright Act, 1957. The general principle is that the **author is the first owner** of the copyright. However, Section 17 creates statutory exceptions:

**1. Work in the Course of Employment (Section 17(a)):** Where a literary, dramatic, or artistic work is **made by an author in the course of his employment** under a contract of service, the **employer** shall be the first owner of the copyright, *in the absence of any agreement to the contrary*.

**2. Photographs, Engravings, Portraits (Section 17(b)):** For photographs taken, or engravings or portraits painted, the **person commissioning the work** is the first owner, subject to contract.

**3. Cinematograph Films (Section 17(c)):** The **producer** (the person at whose instance the film is made) is the first owner of the copyright in the cinematograph film, subject to any agreement to the contrary.

**4. Sound Recordings (Section 17(d)):** The **producer** of the sound recording is the first owner, subject to contract.

**5. Work Made on Behalf of Government (Section 17(k)):** In the case of a work made or first published by or under the direction of any **department of the Government**, the Government shall be the first owner.

**Critical Examination:** The Section 17 exceptions reflect the **commercial reality** of content creation — the *commissioner* funds the work, takes the risk, and therefore receives ownership. However, the *"subject to contract"* caveat preserves **freedom of contract**, allowing the author and the commissioner to negotiate different terms. The test to determine whether Section 17(a) applies was laid down in ***Indian Performing Right Society Ltd. v. Eastern India Motion Pictures Association (1977)***, where the Supreme Court held that the **dominant purpose test** must be applied — i.e., whether the work was created in the **course of employment** or as an independent commission.

For software under IT/ITES, the **Information Technology Act, 2000** and standard **employment agreements** routinely transfer copyright to the employer, supplementing Section 17(a). *(7 M — Break-up: Default rule 1 M; Section 17(a)–(d) 3 M; Section 17(k) 1 M; Case law + IT Act 1 M; Critical analysis 1 M.)*

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Section 50** of the Copyright Act, 1957 empowers the **Appellate Board** (now the **High Court**, post Tribunals Reforms Act, 2021) to rectify the Register of Copyrights on any of the following grounds:

**Grounds for Rectification:**

1. **Incorrect / false particulars** have been entered in the Register.
2. **No copyright subsisted** in the work at the time of registration.
