3. The **registration was obtained by fraud** or misrepresentation.
4. The work is **not a copyrightable work** under Section 13 (e.g., ideas, methods, discoveries, mathematical concepts).
5. The applicant was **not the rightful owner** of the copyright.

**Procedure for Rectification (Rule 14 + Section 50):**

- A **rectification application** is filed in **Form VII** before the **Appellate Board / High Court** within whose jurisdiction the Copyright Office is located (i.e., the High Court of Delhi).
- The application is supported by an **affidavit** and documentary evidence.
- Notice is issued to all **interested parties**, including the registered owner and any licensee.
- The **Registrar of Copyrights** is made a respondent.
- A **full evidentiary hearing** is conducted, and the Board passes a **speaking order** directing either the rectification or the cancellation of the entry in the Register.

**Leading Case Law:**

In ***Syndicate of the Press of the University of Cambridge v. B.D. Bhandari (Delhi HC, 2011)***, the Delhi High Court held that *correctness of entries* in the Register of Copyrights is **rebuttable**, and rectification may be ordered if the **evidentiary burden** is discharged by the petitioner. The court emphasized that the Register is not a *conclusive* document but enjoys a *prima facie* presumption.

In ***Blackwood & Sons Ltd. v. A.N. Parasuraman (AIR 1959 Bom 410)***, the Bombay High Court allowed rectification on the ground that the registered owner had **no subsisting copyright** at the time of registration, as the work was in the public domain.

The cumulative effect of these decisions is that the Register of Copyrights is a **living document** — open to scrutiny, challenge, and correction, but nonetheless a powerful *prima facie* shield for the registered owner. *(7 M — Break-up: Grounds 2.5 M; Procedure 1.5 M; Case law 2 M; Concluding analysis 1 M.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **The Mandatory vs. Voluntary Trap:** Many students write that *"registration is mandatory under the Copyright Act"*. This is **factually wrong** and will cost you 1–2 marks instantly. **Always begin with: "Registration is voluntary; copyright subsists upon creation."**
> 2. **The Section 44 vs. Section 45 Confusion:** Application is filed under **Section 44**; entry in the Register is made under **Section 45**. Examiners deduct half a mark for each mis-citation.
> 3. **The Section 17(a) vs. (c) Distinction:** Section 17(a) covers **literary/dramatic/artistic works** in the course of employment; Section 17(c) is for **cinematograph films**. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Form Numbers:** Remember **Form I = Application; Form III = Work Particulars; Form IV = Author Particulars; Form V = Hearing Notice; Form VI = Reply; Form VII = Rectification.** Examiners frequently ask for the "form used to apply" or the "form for rectification" — mis-recalling Form I as Form II (Power of Attorney) is a common error.
> 5. **Case Law Citation:** Always cite the **full case name and court** (e.g., *"Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak, (2008) 12 SCC 481"*), not merely the case name. Half-mark is reserved for correct citation.
> 6. **2024 Scheme Specific:** The syllabus emphasizes **online e-filing** and the **2016 Amendment Rules**. Mentioning e-filing at **copyright.gov.in** is a smart way to score additional marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- ☐ **Copyright Act, 1957** (44 of 1957) — India's primary copyright statute; amended in **2012** to align with WIPO Internet Treaties.
- ☐ Copyright **subsists upon creation** under **Section 13** — *registration is NOT mandatory*.
- ☐ Registration is **voluntary**, governed by **Sections 44 to 50A** + **Copyright Rules, 2012** (amended 2016).
- ☐ The **Copyright Office** is at **Boudhik Sampada Bhawan, Dwarka, New Delhi**, under **DPIIT, Ministry of Commerce and Industry**.
- ☐ The **Registrar of Copyrights** maintains the Register under **Section 45**.
- ☐ **Form I** = Application; **Form III** = Work Particulars; **Form IV** = Author Particulars; **Form V** = Hearing Notice; **Form VI** = Reply; **Form VII** = Rectification; **Form VIII** = Re-registration.
- ☐ The procedure is governed by **Rules 6–12** of the Copyright Rules, 2012.
- ☐ **Fee:** ₹500 (L/D/M/A), ₹2,000 (Film/Sound); online e-filing concession; Government Departments are **exempt**.
- ☐ The **30-day objection window** is critical — the application is published in the Copyright Office Journal.
- ☐ **Section 48** = Prima facie evidence; **Section 51** = Constructive notice; **Section 62** = Jurisdiction.
- ☐ **Section 17** = First owner rules; default owner is the **author**; exceptions for employment, commissioned works, films, and Government works.
- ☐ **Section 50** = Rectification of the Register; lies before the **Appellate Board / High Court**.
- ☐ Key cases: ***Eastern Book Co. v. D.B. Modak (2008)***, ***IPRS v. EIMPA (1977)***, ***Syndicate of Cambridge v. B.D. Bhandari (2011)***.
- ☐ India is a signatory to the **Berne Convention (1928)**, **UCC (1957)**, **TRIPS (1995)**, **WCT & WPPT (post-2012)**.
- ☐ Infringement suit lies in the court where plaintiff resides or where the work is registered (**Section 62**).
- ☐ **E-filing** at **copyright.gov.in** is the modern, recommended route (introduced via 2016 Amendment).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Term of Copyright
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Term of Copyright

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Statutory Definition

The **Term of Copyright** refers to the statutory duration during which the exclusive legal rights granted to the copyright owner (under the *Copyright Act, 1957*, as amended in 2012) subsist in a protected work. Once this term expires, the work enters the **public domain**, meaning any member of the public may freely use, reproduce, or adapt the work without seeking the author's permission or paying royalties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Anchor (KTU High-Yield):** The term of copyright is governed by **Sections 22 to 29** of the *Indian Copyright Act, 1957*. The unified term (after the 2012 Amendment) is now **60 years** post the relevant triggering event (death of author or first publication), replacing the earlier bifurcated regime of 50 + next 50 years.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you plant a tree in your garden. For a fixed number of years, only **you** are allowed to pick its fruits (use, sell, license). Once the protected years pass, the tree's fruits become the **commons**—anyone passing by may freely pluck them.

In the same way:
- The **tree** = your creative work (a novel, a song, a film)
- The **protected harvest years** = the term of copyright
- The **commons** = the public domain

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 60 years and not lifetime only?**
> Copyright extends beyond the author's death (typically **Life + 60 Years**) so that the author's **heirs, dependents, and legal representatives** can economically benefit from the work for at least one full generation. This balances the *author's right to economic reward* with the *society's right to access knowledge* after a reasonable period.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Triggers (Bold Constants)

- **Life of Author =** the natural lifetime of the human creator.
- **60 Years =** the unified post-event protection window (post 2012 Amendment).
- **First Publication =** the date the work is first made available to the public.
- **Date of Death =** the date recorded in the author's legal documents, governing the start of the 60-year post-mortem period.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Works and Their Respective Terms

The Copyright Act classifies works into distinct categories, each carrying its own statutory term calculation. The trigger event (death vs. publication) determines when the **60-year clock** begins to run.

### A. Works of Authorship (Personal Creative Works)
Includes **literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works** (excluding photographs).

$$\text{Term} = \text{Life of Author} + \mathbf{60 \text{ Years}} \text{ from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of death.}$$

- **Examples:** Novels, poems, paintings, software source code (treated as literary work), stage plays, symphonies.
- **Key Insight:** The 60-year count starts from **January 1st of the year following the author's death**, not from the exact death date.

### B. Anonymous & Pseudonymous Works
When the author's identity is not disclosed on the work.

$$\text{Term} = \text{60 Years from the year the work was FIRST PUBLISHED.}$$

If the author reveals their identity before this term expires, the work **transitions** to the standard **Life + 60 Years** rule.

### C. Posthumous Works
Works published after the author's death.

$$\text{Term} = \text{60 Years from the date of FIRST PUBLICATION.}$$

### D. Cinematograph Films & Sound Recordings
Treated as independent works, not tied to the author's life.

$$\text{Term} = \text{60 Years from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of FIRST PUBLICATION.}$$

### E. Photographs
Earlier treated separately; now aligned under the artistic works umbrella.

$$\text{Term} = \text{60 Years from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of FIRST PUBLICATION.}$$

### F. Works of Government, Public Undertakings & International Organizations

$$\text{Term} = \mathbf{60 \text{ Years}} \text{ from the year of FIRST PUBLICATION.}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Notice that works tied to the **author's life** use **Life + 60 Years**, while **corporate / institutional / anonymous / posthumous** works use **First Publication + 60 Years**. Examiners love this dichotomy!

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Category of Work** | **Trigger Event** | **Term of Copyright** |
|---|---|---|
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic Works | Death of the Author | Life $\vert$ Life of Author + 60 Years from Jan 1 of year following death |
| Cinematograph Films | First Publication | 60 Years from Jan 1 of year following publication |
| Sound Recordings | First Publication | 60 Years from Jan 1 of year following publication |
| Photographs | First Publication | 60 Years from Jan 1 of year following publication |
| Anonymous / Pseudonymous | First Publication (if identity undisclosed) | 60 Years from Jan 1 of year following publication |
| Posthumous Works | First Publication | 60 Years from Jan 1 of year following publication |
| Government Works | First Publication | 60 Years from Jan 1 of year following publication |
| Works of Public Undertakings / IGO | First Publication | 60 Years from Jan 1 of year following publication |
| Computer Programs (as literary works) | Death of the Author | Life + 60 Years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** For software engineers and tech entrepreneurs, understanding that **source code** is treated as a *literary work* with a **Life + 60 Years** term means your codebase remains protected for potentially **80–100+ years**, making long-term licensing, escrow, and IP due-diligence critical during M&A activities.

## 2.3 Why the Term is NOT Perpetual

Copyright is intentionally **time-limited** to:
1. Encourage **new creations** by allowing derivative works eventually.
2. Balance **private economic incentive** with the **public interest**.
3. Allow cultural works to enter the **public domain** for education, scholarship, and innovation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Term Calculation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Problem Framework

The calculation of copyright term always follows the **canonical four-step process**. We illustrate this with **three exhaustive case studies** (this satisfies the KTU 14-mark pattern).

### Case Study 1: Literary Work (Life + 60 Years)

**Problem:** Author **X** writes a novel published in **1995**. Author X dies on **15 August 2010**. Compute the term of copyright.

**Step 1: Identify the Category**
The work is a **novel → literary work**.

**Step 2: Identify the Trigger Event**
Literary works use **Death of Author** as the trigger.

**Step 3: Locate the Year Following the Death**
The author died in **2010**.
The "calendar year next following the year of death" is **2011**.

**Step 4: Compute Expiry**
The copyright subsists until the **end of the 60th year**, counted from Jan 1, 2011.

$$\text{Expiry Date} = \text{Jan 1, 2011} + 60 \text{ Years}$$

$$\text{Expiry Date} = \text{December 31, 2070 (end of the year 2070)}$$

**Result:** The novel enters the public domain on **1 January 2071**.

---

### Case Study 2: Cinematograph Film (First Publication + 60 Years)

**Problem:** A movie is first published (certified for public exhibition) on **20 March 2005**. Compute the term of copyright.

**Step 1: Identify the Category**
This is a **cinematograph film**.

**Step 2: Identify the Trigger Event**
Films use **First Publication** as the trigger.

**Step 3: Locate the Year Following First Publication**
First publication year = **2005**.
The next calendar year = **2006**.

**Step 4: Compute Expiry**

$$\text{Expiry Date} = \text{Jan 1, 2006} + 60 \text{ Years}$$

$$\text{Expiry Date} = \text{December 31, 2065 (end of year 2065)}$$

**Result:** The film enters the public domain on **1 January 2066**.

---

### Case Study 3: Sound Recording (First Publication + 60 Years)

**Problem:** An audio album is first published on **5 July 2018**. Compute the term.

**Step 1:** Category = Sound Recording.
**Step 2:** Trigger = First Publication (2018).
**Step 3:** Next calendar year = **2019**.
**Step 4:**

$$\text{Expiry Date} = \text{Jan 1, 2019} + 60 \text{ Years} = \text{December 31, 2078}$$

**Result:** Public domain entry on **1 January 2079**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Trigger Events

| **Dimension** | **Life + 60 Years** | **Publication + 60 Years** |
|---|---|---|
| Applicable To | Natural author works | Corporate, anonymous, posthumous, films, recordings |
| Predictability of Expiry | Variable (depends on death date) | Highly predictable (fixed at publication) |
| Earliest Possible Expiry | 60 years after author's birth (if death at birth) | 60 years after publication |
| Common in Practice | Books, paintings, source code | Movies, music albums, government reports |
| KTU Marking Weight | 8 marks (typical 14-mark Q) | 6 marks (typical 14-mark Q) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Students often forget to use the **"calendar year next following"** rule and instead count from the exact death/publication date. This **costs 2 marks** in valuation. Always start the count from **January 1 of the NEXT year**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Copyright Term Computation

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **decision logic** an examiner / student follows when classifying and computing the term of copyright for any given work.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Identify the Work]) --> cat1{What is the Category?}
    cat1 -- Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic --> path1[Use Trigger: Death of Author]
    cat1 -- Cinematograph Film --> path2[Use Trigger: First Publication]
    cat1 -- Sound Recording --> path3[Use Trigger: First Publication]
    cat1 -- Photograph --> path4[Use Trigger: First Publication]
    cat1 -- Anonymous / Pseudonymous --> path5{Identity Disclosed?}
    cat5 --> path6[Use Trigger: First Publication]
    cat5 --> path7[Switch to Death of Author trigger]
    cat1 -- Posthumous --> path8[Use Trigger: First Publication]
    cat1 -- Government or PSU --> path9[Use Trigger: First Publication]
    path1 --> calc1[Expiry = Jan 1 of Year After Death + 60 Years]
    path2 --> calc2[Expiry = Jan 1 of Year After First Publication + 60 Years]
    path3 --> calc2
    path4 --> calc2
    path6 --> calc2
    path8 --> calc2
    path9 --> calc2
    path7 --> calc1
    calc1 --> domainA([Public Domain Entry])
    calc2 --> domainA
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Lifecycle of a Copyrighted Work

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Stage 1: Creation of Work] --> s2[Stage 2: First Publication or Disclosure]
    s2 --> s3[Stage 3: Active Copyright Protection Period]
    s3 -- Trigger Event Occurs --> s4[Stage 4: 60 Year Clock Begins on Jan 1 of Next Year]
    s4 --> s5[Stage 5: End of 60th Calendar Year]
    s5 --> s6([Stage 6: Entry into Public Domain])
    s6 --> s7[Stage 7: Free Use by All - No Royalties]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Mapping: Trigger-to-Term Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Copyright Term Determination Engine] --> branch1[Trigger A: Death of Author]
    rootA --> branch2[Trigger B: First Publication]
    branch1 --> out1[Term = Life + 60 Years from next Jan 1]
    branch2 --> out2[Term = 60 Years from next Jan 1 of publication year]
    out1 --> category1[Applies to Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic, Software Code]
    out2 --> category2[Applies to Films, Recordings, Photos, Govt, PSU, Anonymous, Posthumous]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Rationale:** Since copyright term is a statutory concept (not a physical object), the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** representation best captures the classification logic, decision branching, and lifecycle flow—making it easier for students to reproduce in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is meant by the "Term of Copyright"? Why is it time-limited?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **term of copyright** is the statutory duration during which the exclusive rights granted under the Copyright Act, 1957 subsist in a work. Once the term expires, the work enters the **public domain**.
It is time-limited because:
1. Copyright aims to balance **private economic incentive** with **public access to knowledge**.
2. It encourages **new creative works** by eventually enabling derivative creations.
3. It allows cultural, scientific, and educational works to enter the **commons** after a reasonable period.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between the term of copyright for a literary work and a cinematograph film.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Basis** | **Literary Work** | **Cinematograph Film** |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger Event | Death of the Author | First Publication |
| Term | Life + 60 Years | 60 Years from Publication |
| Example | Novel, Textbook, Source Code | Movie, Documentary |

---

## 📝 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with examples the term of copyright for the following categories of works under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957:
   - (i) Literary works
   - (ii) Sound recordings
   - (iii) Anonymous works  
   **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An author dies on 12th April 2015. Calculate the exact date on which the copyright in his literary works will expire. What would change if the work was a cinematograph film first published in 2015?  
   **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**(i) Literary Works (3 Marks):**
The term of copyright in literary works subsists for the **lifetime of the author + 60 years** counted from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of death.
*Example:* Tagore's *Gitanjali* (published 1912, Tagore died 1941) — copyright would have lasted until **end of 2001**.

**(ii) Sound Recordings (2 Marks):**
The term is **60 years from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of first publication**.
*Example:* An album released in 2020 enters public domain at the end of **2080**.

**(iii) Anonymous Works (2 Marks):**
Term is **60 years from the year of first publication**. If the author later reveals their identity within this period, the work reverts to the **Life + 60 Years** rule.

---

#### Part (b) — Solution

**For Literary Works (4 Marks):**

Given: Author died on **12 April 2015**.
- Year of death = **2015**
- Calendar year next following = **2016**
- The 60-year clock begins on **1 January 2016**.

$$\text{Expiry} = \text{1 January 2016} + 60 \text{ Years} = \text{31 December 2075}$$

**Result:** Copyright expires on **1 January 2076** (work enters public domain).

**For Cinematograph Film First Published in 2015 (3 Marks):**

- Trigger event = **First Publication (2015)**
- Calendar year next following = **2016**
- Expiry = **31 December 2075** — **coincidentally the same date** in this case.

**Key Difference:**
- Literary work term depends on **author's death date**.
- Film term depends on **first publication date**, making it **independent of any human lifespan**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the term of copyright for works of:
   - (i) Government and Public Sector Undertakings
   - (ii) International Organizations
   - (iii) Posthumous publications  
   **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A software company releases a product on 1st January 2010. The lead developer dies in 2020. Determine the copyright term for:
   - The source code (literary work)
   - The product's user manual
   Also state when each enters the public domain.  
   **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Solution

**(i) Government & PSU Works (3 Marks):**
For works published by the **Government or any public undertaking**, copyright subsists for **60 years from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of first publication**.
*Example:* Government of India reports, RBI publications, ISRO white papers.

**(ii) International Organization Works (2 Marks):**
Works of **international organizations** (UN, WHO, WTO) enjoy copyright for **60 years from the year of first publication**, provided India has reciprocal arrangements under Section 28.

**(iii) Posthumous Publications (2 Marks):**
For works first published **after the author's death**, copyright subsists for **60 years from the year of first publication**.

---

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Source Code as Literary Work (4 Marks):**
- Category = Literary Work (software source code is treated as such).
- Trigger = Death of Author (2020).
- Next calendar year = **2021**.

$$\text{Expiry} = \text{1 January 2021} + 60 \text{ Years} = \text{31 December 2080}$$

**Public domain entry: 1 January 2081.**

**User Manual (3 Marks):**
- If authored by a natural person → also **Life + 60 Years** rule applies.
- If authored by the company (corporate work) → **First Publication + 60 Years**.
- First published on 1 January 2010 → next year = 2011.

$$\text{Expiry} = \text{1 January 2011} + 60 \text{ Years} = \text{31 December 2070}$$

**Public domain entry: 1 January 2071.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Next Calendar Year" Rule:** Always begin counting from **1 January of the year AFTER** the trigger event, NOT from the trigger date itself. This is the **#1 reason students lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Conflating Trigger Events:** Do **NOT** apply the "Death of Author" trigger to films, sound recordings, or photographs. Always use **First Publication**.
> 3. **Anonymous Work Trap:** If the author's identity is later disclosed, the work **shifts** from Publication + 60 to Life + 60 Years — many students miss this transition rule.
> 4. **Government Works Duration:** Always **60 years from publication**, NOT life-based, since Government has no "natural life".

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Term of Copyright** is the statutory duration of protection (Sections 22–29, Copyright Act, 1957).
- ✅ Unified **60-year post-event term** (post 2012 Amendment).
- ✅ **Two trigger events**: *Death of Author* OR *First Publication*.
- ✅ **Life + 60 Years** rule applies to: Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic, Software source code.
- ✅ **First Publication + 60 Years** rule applies to: Films, Sound Recordings, Photographs, Anonymous/Pseudonymous, Posthumous, Government, PSU, International Organizations.
- ✅ Counting **always** begins on **1 January of the calendar year NEXT FOLLOWING** the trigger event year.
- ✅ Work enters **public domain** on **1 January immediately after the expiry of the 60th year**.
- ✅ **Software code** = Literary Work → Life + 60 Years.
- ✅ Copyright term is **time-limited** to balance private incentive and public interest.
- ✅ **Heirs and legal representatives** enjoy the post-mortem rights, not the original author.
- ✅ The **60-year clock is uniform** post-2012 — older works may still be governed by the **50 + next 50** bifurcation if their original term had not yet expired before 2012.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performer's Right
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performer's Right

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Statutory Definition (Copyright Act, 1957 — India)

Under the **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended in **2012**), a **"Performer"** is defined in **Section 2(q)** as including an actor, singer, musician, dancer, acrobat, juggler, conjurer, a person delivering a lecture, or any other person who makes a performance. The corresponding economic and moral rights of such performers are codified as a **"Performer's Right"** under **Sections 38, 38A, and 39** of the Act.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Performer's Right is a **"neighbouring right"** (also called a *related right*). It is **NOT** a subset of the main Copyright, although it sits within the Copyright Act. It is an independent statutory right that coexists with the copyright of the author/composer of the underlying work.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Restaurant Singer" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a singer named *Riya* performing a song originally written and composed by *Amit*. The musical work (lyrics + composition) is protected by *Amit's* copyright. But the *specific act of singing* by Riya — her unique vocal timbre, expression, and the resulting sound recording of her live performance — is **Riya's Performer's Right**. If a TV channel broadcasts Riya's live concert recording without paying her, they have infringed *Riya's* performer's right, even though the original song still belongs to *Amit*. Riya must consent, and she is entitled to royalties.

This distinction is vital because **two layers of intellectual property overlap** on the same performance:

1. **Layer 1 (Copyright in the Work):** Belongs to the author/composer of the underlying literary or musical work.
2. **Layer 2 (Performer's Right):** Belongs to the actor/singer/musician who interprets/executes the work.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (Bolded for Board Exam Recall)

| Term | Meaning (Recall Cue) |
|---|---|
| **Performer** | Actor, singer, musician, dancer, or any person who performs a literary/artistic work (**Section 2(q)**). |
| **Performer's Right** | Statutory right granted to performers over their live/recorded performances (**Sections 38–39**). |
| **Sound Recording** | A recording of sounds from which sounds can be produced (**Section 2(xx)**). |
| **Visual Recording** | A recording of moving images from which moving images can be produced (**Section 2(rr)**). |
| **Neighbouring Right** | A right allied to, but legally distinct from, traditional copyright. |
| **Fixation** | The act of embodying a performance in a sound or visual recording. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Legal Framework — Sections at a Glance

The Performer's Right regime in India rests on **four pillars**:

- **Section 38** — Recognition of Performer's Right as a special/copyright-like right.
- **Section 38A** — Exclusive rights of performers regarding sound and visual recordings.
- **Section 39** — Moral rights of the performer (right to claim authorship + right to integrity).
- **Section 39A** — Provisions regarding assignment and licensing.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How Performer's Right Operates

- **Step 1 — Trigger Event:** A performer delivers a performance of a literary, dramatic, musical, or artistic work. This performance may be **live** (stage, concert) or **recorded**.
- **Step 2 — Legal Recognition:** The instant the performance is fixed in a sound recording or visual recording, the performer acquires statutory rights under Sections 38 and 38A.
- **Step 3 — Exclusive Economic Rights (Section 38A):** The performer has the exclusive right to:
  * Record the performance in a sound recording or visual recording.
  * Reproduce such recordings.
  * Broadcast or communicate the performance to the public.
  * Distribute copies of the recording to the public.
  * Sell or give on commercial rental copies of the recording.
- **Step 4 — Moral Rights (Section 39):** Independent of economic rights and **non-transferable** (but inheritable). These include:
  * The right to **claim authorship** of the performance (i.e., to be identified as the performer).
  * The right to **integrity** — to restrain any distortion, mutilation, or modification of the performance that would be prejudicial to the performer's honour or reputation.
- **Step 5 — Licensing & Assignment:** Performer's rights can be assigned/licensed, **but assignment is subject to specific conditions** in Section 19 (applied via Section 39A), and the performer retains a right to **royalties** on assignments.
- **Step 6 — Term (Duration):** Performer's rights subsist for a period of **25 years** from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year in which the performance is fixed in a sound recording or visual recording.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Board Valuation)

| Feature | Statutory Provision | Key Detail | Board-Relevant Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition of Performer | **Section 2(q)** | Actor, singer, musician, dancer, lecturer, etc. | **Inclusive** (not exhaustive) |
| Economic Rights Granted | **Section 38A** | Recording, reproduction, broadcast, distribution, rental | **Exclusive** rights |
| Moral Rights | **Section 39** | Right to paternity + right to integrity | **Inalienable** but inheritable |
| Term (Duration) | **Section 38(2)** | **25 years** from fixation | Calculated from the **next calendar year** |
| Assignment | **Section 19 read with 39A** | Must be in writing; royalty right preserved | **Mandatory written assignment** |
| Infringement | **Sections 51–55** (with modifications) | Same remedies as copyright infringement | Civil + Criminal + Border seizure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why duration starts "from the beginning of the next calendar year":** This is a **copyright-style rule**. Even if the performance was fixed on 30th December 2020, the 25-year clock only starts from 1st January 2021. So the protection practically runs to **31st December 2045**. KTU examiners love testing this.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

For a B.Tech student, the relevance extends beyond pure law into:

- **Digital Media & OTT Platforms (e.g., Netflix, Spotify):** Must take a **triple licence** — (i) underlying musical/literary copyright, (ii) performer's right from the singer/actor, and (iii) sound recording copyright from the producer.
- **AI & Generative Music:** Modern generative AI models trained on singers' performances raise novel performer's right infringement questions — a frontier of IP litigation.
- **Software/Game Engineering:** Even in-game voice acting by performers triggers performer's rights; voice cloning without consent is actionable.
- **Broadcast Engineering:** FM radio stations and live-streaming platforms must obtain performer's consent before retransmission of live events.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Distinguishing Performer's Right from Copyright

| Parameter | Copyright (Author) | Performer's Right (Performer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Owner** | Author/Composer of underlying work | Actor/Singer/Musician/Dancer |
| **Statutory Basis** | Sections 13–14 of Copyright Act | Sections 38, 38A, 39 |
| **Nature** | Economic + moral (full) | Economic (Section 38A) + moral (Section 39) |
| **Duration (Standard Literary Work)** | Lifetime of author + **60 years** | **25 years** from fixation |
| **Trigger** | Creation of the work | Performance and fixation |
| **Transferable?** | Yes (assigned/licensed) | Yes (but royalty right retained) |
| **Independent Existence?** | Exists independently | Exists alongside copyright in the underlying work |

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem (KTU-Style Reasoning)

**Question:** *A film producer, XYZ Films Ltd., uses a song sung by a playback singer in a movie released in 2018. The song's lyrics were written by Author P and composed by Composer Q. In 2022, a TV channel broadcasts the film's song clip without obtaining any licence. Identify which intellectual property rights are infringed and against whom, listing the relevant sections.*

### Step-by-Step Legal Analysis (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Identify the underlying work.**
The musical work (lyrics + composition) is the intellectual creation of **Author P** (lyricist) and **Composer Q** (composer). Their copyright subsists under **Section 13(1)(a)** of the Copyright Act.

**Step 2 — Identify the performer's contribution.**
The playback singer's rendition constitutes a **performance** under **Section 2(q)**. Once fixed in the film's soundtrack, the singer acquires a **Performer's Right** under **Section 38(1)**.

**Step 3 — Identify the sound recording.**
The film's soundtrack is a **sound recording** under **Section 2(xx)**, owned by **XYZ Films Ltd.** (the producer). The sound recording copyright subsists for **60 years** from the date of release.

**Step 4 — Analyse the broadcast event (2022).**
When the TV channel broadcasts the song clip in 2022, it performs an act of **communication to the public** under **Section 38A(a)(iv)**. For this act, **three consents are mandatory**:

1. Consent of **Author P** (lyricist) — copyright in literary work.
2. Consent of **Composer Q** (composer) — copyright in musical work.
3. Consent of the **playback singer** — performer's right under Section 38A.

**Step 5 — Conclude the infringement scenario.**
Without all three consents, the broadcast amounts to:
- **Infringement of copyright** in the underlying musical/literary work (against Author P and Composer Q) — actionable under **Section 51**.
- **Infringement of performer's right** of the singer — actionable under **Section 38 read with Section 51**.

**Step 6 — Identify available remedies.**
The aggrieved parties (P, Q, and the singer) may seek:

- **Civil remedies (Section 55):** Injunction, damages, account of profits, delivery-up of infringing copies.
- **Criminal remedies (Section 63):** Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to ₹2 lakhs (first offence); enhanced to 6 years + ₹5 lakhs for repeat offences.
- **Border/Seizure remedies (Section 53):** Ground for confiscation of infringing goods at customs.

## 3.3 Durational Calculation — Worked Example

> **Scenario:** A musician records a live concert on **15th August 2020**. Calculate the duration until which the performer's right subsists.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

Given:
- Date of fixation of performance = 15th August 2020.
- Statutory term under **Section 38(2)** = **25 years** from the **beginning of the calendar year next following** the year of fixation.

Step 1: Identify the year of fixation.
$$\text{Year of Fixation} = 2020$$

Step 2: Identify the next calendar year (the year from which the 25-year clock starts).
$$\text{Starting Year of Clock} = 2021$$

Step 3: Add 25 years to the starting year.
$$\text{Expiry Year} = 2021 + 25 = 2046$$

Step 4: Determine the precise date — the right expires at the **end** of the 25th calendar year, i.e., on **31st December 2046**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Performer's Right subsists from 15-Aug-2020 (date of fixation) until 31-Dec-2046}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** If a question mentions a date in December, students often wrongly compute 25 years from that exact date. The correct rule is **"from the beginning of the next calendar year"** — this is a **2-mark** trap. Memorize the phrase verbatim.

## 3.4 Infringement vs. Permitted Acts — Analytical Matrix

| Act | Infringement? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Broadcasting a live concert recording without performer's consent | **Yes** | Violates exclusive right under Section 38A. |
| Re-recording a singer's performance in a remix without consent | **Yes** | Violates Section 38A (right to record). |
| Playing the recording in a residential home for private use | **No** | Falls under **Section 52(1)(a)** — private use exception. |
| Critiquing/reviewing the performance in a news programme with brief clips | **No** | Falls under **Section 52(1)(b)** — fair dealing for criticism or review. |
| Using the recording for judicial proceedings or educational instruction | **No** | Falls under **Section 52(1)** — statutory exceptions. |
| Sound recording made **before 15th June 1994** (date of insertion of Section 38) | **No (pre-statutory)** | Performer's Right was only inserted by the 1994 amendment; earlier performances are not covered. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Conceptual Architecture of Performer's Right

The diagram below illustrates the layered architecture of intellectual property rights that surround a single musical performance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Underlying Work: Lyrics and Composition] -->|Owned by| B[Author and Composer]
    B -->|Copyright| C[Section 13 to 14: Copyright Act]
    D[Live Performance by Singer] -->|Performed by| E[Performer: Singer]
    E -->|Performer's Right| F[Section 38 and 38A]
    G[Sound Recording of Performance] -->|Owned by| H[Producer or Record Label]
    H -->|Sound Recording Copyright| I[Section 13 read with Section 2 xx]
    J[Distribution to Public] -->|Requires| K[Triple Licence Stack]
    K --> L[Licence 1: From Author]
    K --> M[Licence 2: From Composer]
    K --> N[Licence 3: From Performer]
    K --> O[Licence 4: From Sound Recording Owner]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Rights Flow Within Performer's Right

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Performer's Right Bundle] --> P1[Economic Rights]
    P --> P2[Moral Rights]
    P1 --> P1a[Right to Record]
    P1 --> P1b[Right to Reproduce]
    P1 --> P1c[Right to Broadcast]
    P1 --> P1d[Right to Distribute]
    P1 --> P1e[Right to Commercial Rental]
    P2 --> P2a[Right to Claim Authorship: Paternity]
    P2 --> P2b[Right to Integrity: No Mutilation]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Duration Timeline (Visual Schematic)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Performer's Right Duration Timeline
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    axisFormat %Y
    section Fixation Phase
    Performance Fixed      :milestone, m1, 2020-08-15, 0d
    section Protection Phase
    Protection Active      :active, 2021-01-01, 1826d
    section Expiry
    Right Expires          :milestone, m2, 2046-01-01, 0d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Gantt chart visually encodes the rule from Section 38(2). The performance is fixed on 15-Aug-2020 (milestone `m1`), but the **25-year clock starts on 01-Jan-2021** and terminates at the end of 2045 (effective expiration on 31-Dec-2046, indicated by milestone `m2`).

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Infringement & Remedies Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Unauthorized Use of Performance] --> X1{Type of Violation}
    X1 -->|Recording without consent| Y1[Infringement of Sec 38A]
    X1 -->|Distortion or mutilation| Y2[Infringement of Sec 39: Moral Right]
    X1 -->|Commercial rental| Y3[Infringement of Sec 38A e]
    Y1 --> Z[Remedies under Sec 55]
    Y2 --> Z
    Y3 --> Z
    Z --> Z1[Civil: Injunction, Damages]
    Z --> Z2[Criminal: Sec 63, Imprisonment and Fine]
    Z --> Z3[Border Seizure: Sec 53]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q:** Define a **"performer"** under the Copyright Act, 1957. Is a playback singer covered under this definition?

**Model Answer:**
A "performer" is defined under **Section 2(q)** of the Copyright Act, 1957, as an actor, singer, musician, dancer, acrobat, juggler, conjurer, a person delivering a lecture, or any other person who makes a performance. The definition is **inclusive and broad**. **Yes**, a playback singer is expressly covered because the definition expressly includes a "singer". This was clarified by the **2012 amendment** which further strengthened the rights of performers.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q:** State the **duration** of a Performer's Right in India.

**Model Answer:**
Under **Section 38(2)** of the Copyright Act, 1957, a Performer's Right subsists for a period of **25 years** computed from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year in which the performance is fixed in a sound recording or a visual recording. After this period, the performance falls into the public domain and may be freely used.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** As per the 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question in a module carries **internal choice**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternatives. A student may answer either one in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`

**Q:** Discuss in detail the **exclusive rights** granted to a performer under the Copyright Act, 1957. What is the duration of these rights? Explain with relevant statutory provisions.

#### (a) Exclusive Rights of a Performer — Sections 38 & 38A (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The Indian Copyright Act, 1957 (as amended) grants performers a bouquet of exclusive rights under **Section 38A**, which are analogous to (but legally distinct from) the copyright in the underlying work.

The following exclusive rights are vested in the performer:

- **(i) Right to Record:** The exclusive right to record the performance in a sound recording or a visual recording.
- **(ii) Right to Reproduce:** The exclusive right to reproduce the sound recording or visual recording in any material form.
- **(iii) Right to Distribute:** The exclusive right to distribute copies of the sound recording or visual recording to the public.
- **(iv) Right to Communicate/Broadcast:** The exclusive right to broadcast or communicate the performance to the public, including making it available on-demand (e.g., streaming).
- **(v) Right to Commercial Rental:** The exclusive right to sell or give on commercial rental copies of the recording.

These rights are **exclusive**, meaning no third party may perform any of these acts without the **written consent** of the performer (or the performer's legal heirs/assignees).

#### (b) Duration of Performer's Right & Exceptions (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Under **Section 38(2)**, the Performer's Right subsists for a period of **25 years** from the beginning of the calendar year next following the year of fixation.

For example, if a singer's performance was fixed on 15th August 2020, the right subsists till 31st December 2045 (since the clock begins on 1st January 2021).

**Exceptions (Section 52, applicable via Section 38):** The following acts do NOT constitute infringement:

- Use for **private and personal purposes**.
- Use for **criticism or review** (fair dealing).
- Use for **judicial proceedings** or reporting thereof.
- Use for **educational instruction** in non-profit institutions.
- Use in **news reporting** with brief excerpts.

**Term of Moral Rights:** Moral rights under Section 39 subsist co-extensively with economic rights (25 years) and are **non-transferable but inheritable**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**Q:** Distinguish between **Copyright** in a musical work and **Performer's Right** in a sound recording. What is the legal consequence if a streaming platform streams a song without obtaining consent from the singer?

#### (a) Distinction Between Copyright and Performer's Right (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Basis of Distinction | Copyright in Musical Work | Performer's Right |
|---|---|---|
| **Statutory Section** | Section 13 (literary/musical works) | Section 38 read with Section 38A |
| **Owner** | Lyricist (author) & Composer | Singer, musician, or actor who performed |
| **Term** | Lifetime of author + 60 years | 25 years from fixation |
| **Trigger** | Creation of work | Fixation of performance |
| **Nature** | Pure economic + moral | Independent neighbouring right |

The two rights are **independent but coexistent**. In *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak* (2008) and analogous music-industry jurisprudence, Indian courts have upheld that the performer's consent is a **sine qua non** for lawful exploitation, regardless of whether the underlying copyright has been licensed.

#### (b) Legal Consequence of Unlicensed Streaming (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
If a streaming platform (e.g., Spotify, JioSaavn) streams a song without taking the singer's consent:

**Step 1 — Identify the wrong:** The platform commits an act of **communication to the public** (Section 38A(iv)) and **reproduction** (Section 38A(ii)) without the performer's consent, which constitutes **infringement of the Performer's Right** under Section 38.

**Step 2 — Identify the infringer:** The streaming platform, its content-uploaders, and any intermediate hosts who fail to exercise due diligence may all be jointly liable.

**Step 3 — Available remedies:**

- **Civil remedies (Section 55):** Injunction, damages or account of profits, delivery-up of infringing recordings, and corrective public apology.
- **Criminal remedies (Section 63):** Imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹2 lakhs (first offence).
- **Aggravated offence (repeat):** Imprisonment up to 6 years + fine up to ₹5 lakhs.
- **Border remedies (Section 53):** Seizure at customs of infringing recordings.

**Step 4 — Landmark precedent:** The **Super Cassettes Industries v. Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd. (2009)** case established that online intermediaries must ensure proper licensing of performers' rights before hosting content; failure exposes them to contributory liability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on Performer's Right Questions:**
> - **Pitfall 1 (1–2 marks lost):** Forgetting to mention that the 25-year term is calculated from the **"beginning of the next calendar year"** after fixation. Examiners allocate separate marks for "term" and "method of calculation".
> - **Pitfall 2 (2 marks lost):** Confusing **moral rights (Section 39)** with **economic rights (Section 38A)**. Moral rights are **non-transferable but inheritable** — students often wrongly say they are "non-transferable AND non-inheritable".
> - **Pitfall 3 (1 mark lost):** Using the term **"copyright"** when describing Performer's Right. Performer's Right is a **neighbouring right**, distinct from copyright. Examiners penalise this conflation.
> - **Pitfall 4 (1 mark lost):** Omitting that the **2012 amendment** strengthened performers' rights, including royalty entitlements upon assignment.
> - **Pitfall 5 (2 marks lost):** Failing to mention **Section 38A (inserted in 2012)** when discussing exclusive rights. Section 38A, not Section 38, contains the exclusive rights catalogue.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Board Prep):**

- **Definition:** Performer = actor, singer, musician, dancer, acrobat, lecturer (**Section 2(q)**).
- **Statutory Foundation:** **Sections 38, 38A, 39, 39A** of the Copyright Act, 1957.
- **Nature:** A **neighbouring right**, independent from but coexistent with copyright.
- **Triggering Event:** Performance must be **fixed** in a sound or visual recording.
- **Exclusive Rights (Section 38A):** Record, Reproduce, Distribute, Communicate/Broadcast, Commercial Rental.
- **Moral Rights (Section 39):** Right to paternity (claim authorship) + Right to integrity (no mutilation).
- **Duration:** **25 years** from the beginning of the next calendar year after fixation.
- **Assignment Rule:** Must be in writing; performer **retains royalty right**.
- **Moral Rights Nature:** **Non-transferable** but **inheritable**.
- **Infringement:** Remedies under Sections 51–55 (civil), Section 63 (criminal), Section 53 (border).
- **Statutory Exceptions:** Section 52 (private use, criticism, education, news reporting).
- **Historical Note:** Section 38 was inserted by the **Copyright (Amendment) Act, 1994**; Section 38A was added by the **Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012**.
- **Practical Industry Implication:** Any commercial exploitation of a performance requires **consent of the performer** in addition to the underlying copyright holder's licence — this is the **"triple licence"** rule in the music industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assignment and Licence
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assignment and Licence

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Assignment of Intellectual Property Rights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assignment** is the **complete and absolute transfer** of ownership of an Intellectual Property (IP) right from the **assignor** (original owner) to the **assignee** (new owner). After assignment, the assignee becomes the **new proprietor** of the IP, and the assignor retains **no residual rights** unless specifically carved out by contract.

### Legal Definition (Per KTU 2024 IPR Syllabus Terminology)

- Under **Section 2(1)(b) of the Patents Act, 1970**, an "assignment" of a patent means the transfer by the **present or future** benefit of the patent to a person.
- Under **Section 19 of the Copyright Act, 1957**, assignment of copyright must be in writing, signed by the assignor, and must specify the **rights assigned, duration, territorial extent, and consideration**.
- Under **Section 37 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**, a registered trademark can be assigned/transferred with or without the goodwill of the business.
- Under **Section 30 of the Designs Act, 2000**, assignment of design registration is permitted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Assignment = Outright **sale** of the IP. Think of it as selling your house — once sold, you have **no further claim** over it.

---

## 1.2 Licence of Intellectual Property Rights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Licence** is a **permission** granted by the **licensor** (IP owner) to the **licensee** (user) to use the IP for specified purposes, **without transferring ownership**. The licensor retains the underlying property right, while the licensee gets a contractual right to *use* the IP.

### Legal Definition

- **Section 2(1)(r) of the Patents Act, 1970** defines "licensee" as a person to whom a licence is granted.
- **Section 30 of the Copyright Act, 1957** governs licensing of copyright.
- **Section 48 of the Patents Act, 1970** deals with reliefs in suits for infringement and references to licences of right.
- **Section 49 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999** governs registered users and licensing arrangements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Licence = Granting **permission** to use your property. Think of it as renting your house — the house remains yours, but the tenant has a legal right to occupy and use it.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Ownership Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Assignment** | Selling a car outright to a buyer | Buyer becomes new owner |
| **Licence** | Renting the same car for a year | Owner retains ownership |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick for KTU Exams:**
> - **A**ssignment → **A**bsolute transfer (everything moves)
> - **L**icence → **L**imited permission (only use, not own)

---

## 1.4 Categorical Overview Table

| Parameter | Assignment | Licence |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of Transfer | Outright / Permanent | Temporary / Limited |
| Ownership Change | **Yes** | **No** |
| Contract Type | Deeds of Assignment | Licence Agreement |
| Registration Requirement | **Mandatory** under most IP statutes | Not always mandatory |
| Consideration | Lump sum or as agreed | Royalties / Fees / Periodic |
| Revocability | Generally **irrevocable** | Usually **revocable** per terms |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for a Valid Assignment

Under all major IP statutes, a legally valid assignment must satisfy the following conditions:

1. **Written Document**: Must be in writing and signed by the assignor.
2. **Identification of IP**: The patent/copyright/trademark/design being assigned must be clearly identified.
3. **Consideration**: There must be valid consideration (monetary or otherwise).
4. **Registration**: Under Section 69 of the Patents Act, Section 19(2) of the Copyright Act, and Section 45 of the Trade Marks Act, the assignment must be **recorded** in the respective IP Register for it to be valid against third parties.
5. **Territorial Limit**: Must specify the geographical extent (e.g., India, world, specific country).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under **Section 68 of the Patents Act, 1970**, assignments of patents are **void** if made to foreign nationals or entities without the prior written permission of the Central Government, when the patent is for an invention relevant to national security.

---

## 2.2 Types of Assignments

1. **Absolute / Outright Assignment**: Complete transfer without any reversion clause.
2. **Conditional Assignment**: Transfer subject to specified conditions (e.g., royalty reverts upon failure of a milestone).
3. **Partial Assignment**: Transfer of only certain rights (e.g., assigning manufacturing rights but not marketing rights).
4. **Assignment by Operation of Law**: Through inheritance, bankruptcy, or court order.
5. **Future Rights Assignment**: Pre-assignment of IP that may arise in the future (valid under Section 2(1)(b) Patents Act).

---

## 2.3 Types of Licences

| Type | Description | KTU High-Yield Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Exclusive Licence** | Only the licensee may use the IP; even the owner is barred | Highest value; commonly tested |
| **Sole Licence** | Both owner and licensee can use, but no third party | Hybrid arrangement |
| **Non-Exclusive Licence** | Owner may grant multiple licences simultaneously | Most common in tech transfer |
| **Compulsory Licence** | Government-mandated licence under special conditions (Sec. 84 Patents Act) | Critical for pharma KTU questions |
| **Licence of Right** | Granted by patentee under Section 87 of Patents Act | Triggered for food/medicine patents |
| **Cross-Licence** | Two parties mutually licence their IP to each other | Common in tech pooling |
| **Sub-Licence** | Licensee further grants rights to another | Requires licensor's consent |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This cheat sheet serves as your **last-minute revision goldmine** before KTU ESE.

| Concept | Statutory Basis | Mandatory Form | Key Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Patent Assignment | Sec. 2(1)(b), 68, 69 Patents Act | Written & Registered | Central Govt. permission for foreign assignment |
| Copyright Assignment | Sec. 19 Copyright Act 1957 | Written & Signed | Must specify rights, duration, territory |
| Trademark Assignment | Sec. 37–45 TM Act 1999 | Written & Registered | With or without goodwill |
| Design Assignment | Sec. 30 Designs Act 2000 | Written & Registered | Similar to patents |
| Patent Licence | Sec. 2(1)(r), 84, 87 Patents Act | Written (no mandatory registration) | Compulsory licence provisions |
| Copyright Licence | Sec. 30, 30A Copyright Act | Written & Signed | Licensing body provisions |
| Trademark Licence | Sec. 48–53 TM Act | Written, registered user permitted | Quality control by owner |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Pharma Industry**: Assignment and licensing are the **lifeblood** of drug commercialization — patented molecules are licensed to generic manufacturers via compulsory licences (see Natco v. Bayer Nexavar case).
- **Tech Industry**: Software is predominantly distributed through **non-exclusive licensing** (EULAs — End User Licence Agreements).
- **Manufacturing**: Patent pools and cross-licences enable collaborative innovation (e.g., 3G/4G/5G standards).
- **Academia**: Universities licence patented research to industries under **exclusive licensing agreements** for commercialization.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU long-answer questions, **always cite a real case** like **Natco Pharma Ltd. v. Bayer Corporation (2012)** for compulsory licensing — examiners award bonus marks for case law.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Procedural Breakdown

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure for Assignment of a Patent

### Stage 1 — Drafting the Deed of Assignment

1. **Identify parties**: Assignor (current patentee) and Assignee (new owner).
2. **Identify patent**: Patent number, title, date of grant.
3. **Define scope**: Whether assignment is for the full patent term or limited period.
4. **State consideration**: Lump sum, royalty, or any valuable consideration.
5. **Specify territorial extent**: India / Worldwide / Specific jurisdictions.
6. **Include warranties**: That assignor is the true owner and has full right to assign.

### Stage 2 — Execution of the Deed

7. **Signature**: Both parties sign the deed.
8. **Witnessing / Notarization**: As per Indian Contract Act, 1872, attested by two witnesses.
9. **Stamp Duty**: Paid as per Indian Stamp Act or state stamp schedule.

### Stage 3 — Registration with the Patent Office

10. **Application to Controller General of Patents (CGPDTM)**: File in **Form PAT-29** within **6 months** of execution.
11. **Pay requisite fee**: As per First Schedule of Patents Rules, 2003.
12. **Register entry**: The Controller enters the assignment in the Register of Patents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Consequence of Non-Registration:** Per **Section 69(1) of the Patents Act, 1970**, an unregistered assignment is **void against any third person** claiming a conflicting interest. It is, however, valid between the parties *inter se*.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure for Granting a Patent Licence

1. **Negotiation of Terms**: Parties negotiate the licence scope, royalty, duration, and territory.
2. **Drafting the Licence Agreement**: Includes clauses on:
   - Grant clause (exclusive/sole/non-exclusive)
   - Royalty computation
   - Field-of-use restrictions
   - Quality control (especially for trademarks)
   - Sublicensing rights
   - Termination triggers
3. **Execution**: Signing of the licence agreement.
4. **Registration (Optional but advisable)**: With the Controller under Form PAT-30 for recordal.
5. **Payment of Royalties**: As per agreed schedule.
6. **Quality Monitoring**: Licensor monitors to prevent IP misuse.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Assignment vs Licence

The following structured matrix is the **most-tested topic** in KTU Module 3:

\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Parameter} &= \text{Derived Legal Inference} \\
\text{1. Ownership Transfer} &= \text{Assignment = YES, Licence = NO} \\
\text{2. Consideration} &= \text{Assignment = Lump Sum, Licence = Royalties} \\
\text{3. Duration} &= \text{Assignment = Permanent, Licence = Limited} \\
\text{4. Reversion} &= \text{Assignment = No Reversion, Licence = Reversion possible} \\
\text{5. Registration} &= \text{Assignment = Mandatory, Licence = Optional} \\
\text{6. Stamp Duty} &= \text{Assignment = Higher, Licence = Lower (often treated as contract)}
\end{aligned}

---

## 3.4 Key Statutory Conditions — Mathematical Logic Representation

Let $R$ represent an IP right, $O$ the owner, $A$ the assignee, $L$ the licensee, and $T$ the time horizon.

\begin{aligned}
\text{Assignment Equation:} \quad & R(O) \xrightarrow{\text{absolute}} R(A) \\
& \text{After assignment, } R(O) = \varnothing \text{ and } R(A) = R \\
\text{Licence Equation:} \quad & R(O) \xrightarrow{\text{permission}} U(L) \\
& \text{After licence, } R(O) = R \text{ and } U(L) \subset R \text{ (subset of rights)} \\
\text{Where } & U(L) \text{ is the User-right granted to licensee}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> The above algebraic representation is a **KTU pedagogical simplification**. In legal practice, "rights" are not literally additive, but this notation helps visualize that licensing creates a **subset** of usage rights, while assignment creates a **full transfer**.

---

## 3.5 Sub-Analytical Framework: Compulsory Licence under Section 84, Patents Act, 1970

The Controller of Patents may grant a compulsory licence if **any of the following conditions** are met:

\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition 1:} \quad & \text{Reasonable requirements of the public not satisfied} \\
\text{Condition 2:} \quad & \text{Patented invention not available at reasonable price} \\
\text{Condition 3:} \quad & \text{Patented invention not worked in India}
\end{aligned}

### Time Thresholds for Filing:

\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{filing}} &= \max(t_{\text{grant}} + 3 \text{ years}, 4 \text{ years from filing date}) \\
& \text{where } t_{\text{grant}} \text{ = date of patent grant}
\end{aligned}

> [!TIP]
> **Natco v. Bayer (2012)** — KTU frequently tests this case. Natco was granted a compulsory licence for Bayer's Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar) at ₹8,800 per month vs Bayer's ₹2.8 lakh per month — a **97% price reduction**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IP Rights Transfer — Master Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IP Owner - Creator/Inventor] --> B{Decision: Transfer Type}
    B -->|Complete Sale| C[Assignment Deed Drafting]
    B -->|Permission Only| D[Licence Agreement Drafting]
    
    C --> E[Sign + Stamp Duty]
    E --> F[Registration at IP Registry]
    F --> G[New Owner = Assignee]
    
    D --> H{Negotiate Terms}
    H --> I[Exclusive Licence]
    H --> J[Sole Licence]
    H --> K[Non-Exclusive Licence]
    
    I --> L[Royalty Payments + Quality Control]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    
    F --> M[Valid Against Third Parties]
    L --> N[Owner Retains Title + Receives Royalties]
    
    G --> O[End: Assignor Has No Right]
    N --> P[End: Licensor Retains All Residual Rights]
```

## 4.2 Licence Type — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Licence Agreement] --> Q{How Many Licensees?}
    Q -->|One| R{Is Owner Also Permitted?}
    R -->|No| S[Exclusive Licence]
    R -->|Yes| T[Sole Licence]
    Q -->|Multiple| U[Non-Exclusive Licence]
    
    L --> V{Special Conditions?}
    V -->|Government Mandate| W[Compulsory Licence Sec 84]
    V -->|Patentee Declaration| X[Licence of Right Sec 87]
    V -->|Mutual Exchange| Y[Cross Licence]
    V -->|Sub Grant| Z[Sub Licence]
```

## 4.3 Procedural Flow — Assignment Registration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Draft Deed of Assignment] --> P2[Pay Stamp Duty]
    P2 --> P3[Sign + Witness]
    P3 --> P4[File Form PAT-29 at Patent Office]
    P4 --> P5[Controller Examines Application]
    P5 --> P6[Entry Made in Register of Patents]
    P6 --> P7[Assignment Now Valid Against Third Parties]
    
    P5 -.->|Deficiency| P8[Rectification Required]
    P8 --> P4
```

## 4.4 Concept Map — Assignment vs Licence Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Core Concept - IP Rights] --> A1[Assignment]
    C --> B1[Licence]
    
    A1 --> A2[Full Transfer]
    A1 --> A3[Ownership Changes]
    A1 --> A4[Registration Mandatory]
    A1 --> A5[No Royalties - Lump Sum]
    
    B1 --> B2[Permission Granted]
    B1 --> B3[Ownership Retained]
    B1 --> B4[Registration Optional]
    B1 --> B5[Royalties Paid]
    
    B1 --> B6[Exclusive]
    B1 --> B7[Sole]
    B1 --> B8[Non-Exclusive]
    B1 --> B9[Compulsory]
    B1 --> B10[Cross]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Distinguish between Assignment and Licence of Intellectual Property Rights.**

### **Model Answer:**

**Assignment** is the **complete and absolute transfer** of ownership of an IP right from the assignor to the assignee. After assignment, the assignee becomes the **new proprietor**, and the assignor has **no further rights** over the IP. It is governed by Section 2(1)(b) of the Patents Act, 1970 and Section 19 of the Copyright Act, 1957.

**Licence** is a **permission** granted by the licensor (IP owner) to the licensee to use the IP for specific purposes, **without transferring ownership**. The licensor retains all underlying property rights. It is governed by Section 2(1)(r) of the Patents Act, 1970 and Section 30 of the Copyright Act, 1957.

**Key Distinction:** Assignment transfers **title**; Licence transfers only the **right to use**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**What is a compulsory licence under the Patents Act, 1970?**

### **Model Answer:**

A **compulsory licence** is a licence granted by the **Controller General of Patents** under **Section 84 of the Patents Act, 1970** to a third party (other than the patentee) to manufacture, sell, or use the patented invention **without the patentee's consent**, under specific conditions such as:

1. The reasonable requirements of the public are not satisfied.
2. The patented invention is not available at a reasonable price.
3. The patented invention is not worked in the territory of India.

A famous example is **Natco Pharma Ltd. v. Bayer Corporation (2012)**, where the Controller granted a compulsory licence for Bayer's cancer drug Nexavar, citing public health and affordability.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

#### **(a)** Explain the various types of licences under Intellectual Property Law. **(7 Marks)**

#### **(b)** Discuss the legal procedure for assignment of patents in India with relevant statutory provisions. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

The various types of licences under Intellectual Property Law are:

**1. Exclusive Licence**: Grants the licensee the sole right to use the IP, even **excluding the licensor**. Under Section 2(1)(r) Patents Act, the patentee cannot even work the patent themselves. Example: A pharmaceutical company exclusively licences a drug formula to a single manufacturer.

**2. Sole Licence**: Both the licensor and the licensee have the right to use the IP, but **third parties are excluded**. This is a middle-ground arrangement.

**3. Non-Exclusive Licence**: The licensor can grant the same rights to **multiple licensees simultaneously**. Most common in software EULAs (End User Licence Agreements).

**4. Compulsory Licence**: Granted by the Controller under **Section 84, Patents Act, 1970** when the patentee fails to work the patent in India or the public demand is unmet. No consent of patentee required.

**5. Licence of Right**: Under **Section 87, Patents Act**, when the Central Government directs that food, medicine, or chemical processes be available at reasonable prices, any interested party may apply for a licence.

**6. Cross-Licence**: Two IP owners mutually licence their rights to each other. Common in technology pooling for industry standards like 4G/5G.

**7. Sub-Licence**: The licensee further grants rights to another party, **subject to the original licence terms**.

---

### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

The legal procedure for assignment of patents in India is governed by **Sections 68 and 69 of the Patents Act, 1970**:

**Step 1 — Drafting the Deed of Assignment**: The deed must identify the patent (number, date), parties, consideration, territorial extent, and any warranties.

**Step 2 — Compliance with Section 68**: If the patent relates to **national security or atomic energy**, prior written permission of the Central Government is mandatory before any foreign assignment. Failure renders the assignment **void**.

**Step 3 — Execution**: The deed must be signed by both assignor and assignee, witnessed, and stamped as per the Indian Stamp Act.

**Step 4 — Registration**: An application must be made to the **Controller General of Patents** in **Form PAT-29** under **Section 69 of the Patents Act, 1970**, within **6 months** of execution (extendable by 2 months with fees).

**Step 5 — Entry in Register**: Upon satisfaction, the Controller enters the assignment in the **Register of Patents** maintained under Section 67.

**Legal Effect**: Once registered, the assignment is **valid against third parties**. Per Section 69(1), an unregistered assignment is void against any conflicting interest.

**Relevant Case**: In **Ravi Kamal Bali v. Kala Tech (2006)**, the Court held that registration under Section 69 is essential for the assignment to bind third parties.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a)** Critically analyse the doctrine of compulsory licensing in Indian patent law with reference to the Natco v. Bayer case. **(7 Marks)**

#### **(b)** Compare and contrast the provisions for assignment and licensing under the Copyright Act, 1957. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

The **doctrine of compulsory licensing** is enshrined in **Chapter XVI of the Patents Act, 1970** (Sections 82–92). It balances **patentee rights** with **public welfare**, allowing the State to override monopoly in exceptional circumstances.

**Statutory Basis**: Section 84 allows any interested person to apply to the Controller after **3 years from grant** for a compulsory licence if:
- Reasonable requirements of the public are unmet.
- Patented invention is unavailable at affordable price.
- Patented invention is not worked in India.

**Case Study — Natco Pharma Ltd. v. Bayer Corporation (2012)**:

- **Patent**: Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar) — a kidney/liver cancer drug by Bayer.
- **Application**: Natco Pharma filed under Section 84 seeking compulsory licence.
- **Bayer's Price**: ₹2,80,428 per month.
- **Natco's Proposed Price**: ₹8,800 per month (a 97% reduction).
- **Controller's Findings**: Bayer did not work the patent sufficiently in India; the drug was unaffordable; public health demands warranted licence.
- **Decision**: Controller granted compulsory licence to Natco on payment of **6% royalty** to Bayer.
- **Outcome**: Bayer challenged, but both the **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** and the **Supreme Court** upheld the decision.

**Critical Analysis**: While compulsory licensing promotes public welfare, critics argue it **discourages R&D investment** in India. The TRIPS Agreement, however, recognizes compulsory licensing as a valid flex (Doha Declaration, 2001).

---

### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

Under the **Copyright Act, 1957**, assignment and licensing have distinct statutory regimes:

**Assignment (Section 19)**:

\begin{aligned}
\text{Form} &= \text{Written, signed by assignor} \\
\text{Mandatory Content} &= \text{Rights assigned, Duration, Territorial extent, Consideration} \\
\text{Registration} &= \text{Not mandatory but advisable under Section 33} \\
\text{Effect} &= \text{Complete transfer of economic rights} \\
\text{Reversion} &= \text{After 5 years, author can terminate assignment if no work published}
\end{aligned}

**Licensing (Section 30)**:

\begin{aligned}
\text{Form} &= \text{Written agreement} \\
\text{Content} &= \text{Specific uses permitted, Royalty terms, Duration} \\
\text{Registration} &= \text{Not mandatory, but copyright societies may register} \\
\text{Effect} &= \text{Permission to use, no ownership change} \\
\text{Reversion} &= \text{As per agreement terms}
\end{aligned}

**Comparison**:
- **Assignment** is **more formal** and **statutorily regulated** (Section 19 is exhaustive).
- **Licensing** is **more flexible** and based on contractual freedom (Section 30A introduces compulsory licensing for works withheld from public).
- **Assignment** allows the author to **reclaim rights** after 5 years under Section 19A — a unique feature absent in patent assignment.
- **Licensing** does not require such reversion, but is bound by **royalties fixed by the Copyright Board**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating "Assignment" with "Licensing"** — Many students use these terms interchangeably. This is the **#1 cause of mark deduction** in KTU ESE. Always emphasize: Assignment = Transfer of Title; Licence = Transfer of Use.
> 2. **Forgetting statutory sections** — A 14-mark answer without citing specific sections (e.g., Section 84, Section 19) typically **loses 3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting case law** — For compulsory licence questions, not mentioning **Natco v. Bayer (2012)** results in **loss of 2 marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring Section 19A Copyright Act** — Many students miss the **5-year reversion right** of authors in copyright assignment.
> 5. **Stating that licence needs registration** — This is **incorrect** for patents and copyrights; registration is only advisable for evidentiary purposes. Mandatory only for trademarks (registered users).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Assignment = Absolute Transfer of Ownership** (governed by Sec. 2(1)(b), 68, 69 Patents Act; Sec. 19 Copyright Act; Sec. 37–45 TM Act).
- ✅ **Licence = Permission to Use** (Sec. 2(1)(r) Patents Act; Sec. 30 Copyright Act; Sec. 48 TM Act).
- ✅ **Registration of assignment is mandatory** to be valid against third parties (Section 69 Patents Act).
- ✅ **Foreign assignment** of patents relating to national security requires **Central Government permission** under Section 68 — non-compliance makes the assignment **void**.
- ✅ **Types of licences**: Exclusive, Sole, Non-Exclusive, Compulsory, Licence of Right, Cross, Sub.
- ✅ **Compulsory Licence** under Section 84 can be sought **3 years after grant** of patent.
- ✅ **Landmark Case**: **Natco v. Bayer (2012)** — compulsory licence for Nexavar cancer drug.
- ✅ **Copyright reversion right**: Under Section 19A, author can reclaim copyright after **5 years** if assigned work is not published/exploited.
- ✅ **Trademark licence** may be recorded under **Section 49 TM Act** as a "registered user" arrangement.
- ✅ **Royalties** are characteristic of licences; **lump sum consideration** is typical of assignment.
- ✅ **Sublicensing** requires the express consent of the licensor unless otherwise agreed.
- ✅ **Compulsory licence** decision must consider whether the patentee has offered the invention on **reasonable terms**.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Assignment and Licence**

*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme PESFT757 — Intellectual Property Rights.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compulsory Licence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compulsory Licence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compulsory Licence (Statutory Licence):** A **non-voluntary authorisation** granted by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** under the **Indian Patents Act, 1970 (as amended in 2005)**, allowing a third party to manufacture, use, sell, or import a **patented invention** without the consent of the patent holder, subject to the fulfilment of statutory conditions, payment of **adequate royalty**, and demonstration of public interest or non-working of the patent in the territory of India.

The legal basis is primarily enshrined in **Sections 84, 92, 92A, and 94** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970, read harmoniously with **Article 31** of the **TRIPS Agreement (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights, 1994)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Government's Power of Eminent Domain for Medicine"**

Imagine you invent a **life-saving drug** and patent it. You sell it at an extremely high price, making it unaffordable for the common man in India. The government steps in and says: *"This is essential for public health. You will either license it voluntarily to a generic manufacturer at a reasonable price, OR we will legally authorise another company to produce it — but you will receive fair royalty for your invention."*

This is exactly what a **Compulsory Licence** does. It is a **safety valve** built into the patent system to prevent the abuse of monopoly rights by the patent owner. It balances two competing interests:
- **Private Right** → The inventor deserves reward for innovation.
- **Public Interest** → Society must not be denied access to essential goods (medicines, technology).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This topic is the **most frequently asked** question in the IP Law module. Examiners expect students to know **Section numbers, grounds, who can apply, and the famous *Natco v. Bayer* case (Nexavar, 2012)**.

### 1.3 Key Statutory Metrics & Standard Values

| Parameter | Standard Value (Indian Patents Act, 1970) |
|---|---|
| Application Forum | Controller of Patents |
| Minimum waiting period before application | **3 years** from the date of **grant** of patent |
| Royalty Rate | As determined by Controller (subject to appeal) |
| Appeal Authority | Intellectual Property Appellate Board (**IPAB**) / High Court |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Concept

The doctrine of Compulsory Licensing is founded on three pillars of reasoning:

**Pillar 1: Prevention of Abuse of Monopoly**
- A patent is a **time-bound statutory monopoly (20 years)**.
- If the patentee **fails to work the patent in India** (i.e., does not manufacture locally) and instead **imports** the product, it restricts supply and inflates prices.
- Compulsory licence breaks this deadlock.

**Pillar 2: Public Health Safeguard (Doha Declaration, 2001)**
