- The **Doha Declaration on TRIPS and Public Health (2001)** affirmed that TRIPS *"should be interpreted and implemented in a manner supportive of WTO Members' right to protect public health and, in particular, to promote access to medicines for all."*
- This paved the way for **Section 92 & 92A** in India, dealing with compulsory licensing for **export** of patented drugs to countries with insufficient manufacturing capacity.

**Pillar 3: TRIPS Flexibilities**
- Article 31 of TRIPS permits compulsory licensing under specific conditions, including:
  - Prior negotiation with the patentee (with exceptions for national emergency).
  - Adequate remuneration to the patentee.
  - Non-exclusive licensing.
  - Predominantly for the supply of the domestic market.

### 2.2 Grounds for Compulsory Licence (Section 84 of Indian Patents Act)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 84(1):** Any person (including a Government undertaking) may apply for a compulsory licence **after the expiration of 3 years** from the **date of grant** of a patent, on **any one or more** of the following grounds:

The Controller must be satisfied that:

1. **Reasonable requirements of the public** with respect to the patented invention have **not been satisfied**.
2. The patented invention is **not available to the public at a reasonably affordable price**.
3. The patented invention is **not being worked in the territory of India** (i.e., not manufactured in India to an adequate extent).
4. The **patentee has refused to grant a licence** on reasonable terms despite a reasonable request.
5. The patentee has **imposed unfair or discriminatory conditions** on licensees.
6. The patentee has used the patent in a manner that **prejudicially affects the working** of another patented invention which involves an important technical advance of considerable economic significance.

### 2.3 Procedure for Grant of Compulsory Licence

1. **Application Filing:** Any interested person files an application under **Form 17** before the Controller.
2. **Notice to Patentee:** Controller issues notice to the patentee.
3. **Hearing:** Both parties are heard; Controller may invite representations from consumer groups and trade bodies.
4. **Satisfaction of Grounds:** Controller examines whether the grounds under Section 84 exist.
5. **Royalty Determination:** Controller fixes the terms of licence, including royalty, scope, and duration.
6. **Order Issuance:** Licence is granted subject to the patentee receiving **adequate remuneration**.
7. **Appeal:** Aggrieved party can appeal to the **High Court / IPAB**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Statutory Provisions Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **HIGH-YIELD REVISION TABLE** for KTU exams. Memorise the section numbers and content.

| Section | Subject Matter | Key Trigger | Time Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Section 82** | Declaration of certain inventions as not patentable | General public interest | — |
| **Section 84** | Compulsory licence (general) | Reasonable requirements not met | **3 years** from grant |
| **Section 85** | Power of Controller to revoke | Non-compliance with licence terms | — |
| **Section 86** | Power of Controller to grant licence in case of national emergency | Government notification | Immediate |
| **Section 87** | Special provisions for compulsory licence under Section 84 | — | — |
| **Section 88** | Special provisions for compulsory licence on notification by Central Government | Public non-commercial use | — |
| **Section 89** | Procedure for dealing with applications under Sections 84 & 86 | Hearing of parties | — |
| **Section 90** | Powers of Controller on grant of compulsory licence | Royalty, scope determination | — |
| **Section 91** | Matters to be taken into account while fixing royalty | — | — |
| **Section 92** | Compulsory licence for export | Specific destination country | — |
| **Section 92A** | Compulsory licence for manufacture and export | Pharmaceutical export | — |
| **Section 94** | Government use of inventions | Sovereign right | — |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility & Landmark Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landmark Case — *Natco Pharma Ltd. v. Bayer Corporation* (2012)**
> 
> - **Patent Involved:** Sorafenib Tosylate (anti-cancer drug **Nexavar**).
> - **Patent Holder:** Bayer Corporation.
> - **Grounds:** Bayer sold the drug at ₹2.8 lakh/month; Natco offered at ₹8,800/month.
> - **Outcome:** **India's FIRST compulsory licence** was granted by the Controller.
> - **Royalty Fixed:** **6% of net sales** to Bayer.
> - **Significance:** Demonstrated India's sovereign right to override patent monopolies for public health.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Conditions

### 3.1 Conditions Precedent for Grant of Compulsory Licence (Section 84(1) – Logical Flow)

The Controller applies a **5-step logical test** before granting a compulsory licence:

$$
\text{Step 1} \rightarrow \text{Step 2} \rightarrow \text{Step 3} \rightarrow \text{Step 4} \rightarrow \text{Step 5}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Has the 3-year post-grant period elapsed?} \\
& \text{If NO} \rightarrow \text{Reject application.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Is the patentee "working" the patent in India?} \\
& \text{(Section 2(ac) defines "working" = manufacture in India.)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Are reasonable requirements of public satisfied?} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Is the product available at a reasonably affordable price?} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Has the applicant tried to obtain a voluntary licence on reasonable terms?}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Detailed Conditions of Compulsory Licence (Section 90)

Once granted, the licence is subject to the following statutory conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Condition 1:} \quad & \text{The licence is \textbf{NON-EXCLUSIVE} and \textbf{NON-ASSIGNABLE}.} \\
\textbf{Condition 2:} \quad & \text{Royalty must be paid as fixed by the Controller.} \\
\textbf{Condition 3:} \quad & \text{Licence extent is limited to the unmet public requirement.} \\
\textbf{Condition 4:} \quad & \text{The licensee must predominantly supply the Indian market.} \\
\textbf{Condition 5:} \quad & \text{The patentee retains all other rights over the patent.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Distinction between Section 84 and Section 92A

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners frequently ask this comparison. Use this table for scoring.

| Parameter | Section 84 (General) | Section 92A (Pharma Export) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Domestic public access | Export of patented drugs |
| Eligible Products | All inventions | Only **pharmaceuticals** |
| Recipient Country | India (domestic market) | Any country with insufficient manufacturing capacity (with notification) |
| Licensee Type | Any person | Only an **Indian manufacturer** |
| Royalty | As fixed by Controller | As fixed, may be different |
| Legal Basis | TRIPS Art. 31 (general) | TRIPS Art. 31bis (added in 2005) |

### 3.4 Grounds of Revocation of Compulsory Licence (Section 85)

The Controller may revoke a compulsory licence if any of these situations arise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Ground 1:} \quad & \text{The licensee fails to comply with the terms of the licence.} \\
\textbf{Ground 2:} \quad & \text{The circumstances that justified the grant no longer exist.} \\
\textbf{Ground 3:} \quad & \text{The licensee fails to produce the patented article in adequate quantity.} \\
\textbf{Ground 4:} \quad & \text{The patentee demonstrates that the licence was obtained by fraud or misrepresentation.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Royalty Determination — Factors under Section 91

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Controller must consider **ALL** the following factors while fixing royalty:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1. \quad & \text{Nature of the invention and the expenditure incurred by the patentee.} \\
2. \quad & \text{Extent to which the patented invention is being worked.} \\
3. \quad & \text{Period during which the licence is to be granted.} \\
4. \quad & \text{Premium, if any, paid by the licensee to the patentee.} \\
5. \quad & \text{Estimated net profit margin of the licensee.} \\
6. \quad & \text{Royalty rates in comparable fields.} \\
7. \quad & \text{Public health considerations (especially for pharmaceuticals).}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow Architecture — Compulsory Licensing Process

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **end-to-end legal procedure** for grant of a Compulsory Licence under Section 84 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Applicant Files Application Under Form 17"] --> B["Has 3 Years Elapsed Since Patent Grant?"]
    B -- "No" --> C["Application Rejected by Controller"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D["Controller Issues Notice to Patentee"]
    D --> E["Hearing of Both Parties Conducted"]
    E --> F["Controller Examines Grounds Under Section 84"]
    F --> G["Reasonable Requirements of Public Satisfied?"]
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Application Dismissed"]
    G -- "No" --> I["Patent Worked in India?"]
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Available at Affordable Price?"]
    J -- "Yes" --> H
    J -- "No" --> K["Compulsory Licence Granted"]
    I -- "No" --> K
    K --> L["Royalty Determined Under Section 91"]
    L --> M["Licence Issued to Applicant"]
    M --> N{"Aggrieved Party?"}
    N -- "Yes" --> O["Appeal to IPAB or High Court"]
    N -- "No" --> P["Licence Remains in Force"]
    P --> Q{"Compliance Issues?"}
    Q -- "Yes" --> R["Revocation Under Section 85"]
    Q -- "No" --> P
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Grounds for Compulsory Licence

```mermaid

    subgraph G1["GROUNDS UNDER SECTION 84(1)"]
        S1["Reasonable Requirements Not Met"]

    end

        O3["Appeal Rights to IPAB"]
    end
    G1 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> G3
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Concept Map of Statutory Provisions

```mermaid

        Domestic Use
            Section 84 General Grounds
            Section 85 Revocation

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Compulsory Licence" and state the time period after which an application for compulsory licence can be filed under the Indian Patents Act, 1970.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Compulsory Licence** is a **non-voluntary authorisation** granted by the **Controller of Patents** to a third party, allowing the manufacture, use, or sale of a patented invention without the consent of the patent owner, subject to the payment of **adequate royalty** and satisfaction of statutory conditions.

**Time Period:** Under **Section 84(1)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970, an application for compulsory licence can be filed **only after the expiration of 3 years from the date of grant** of the patent.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "Controller", 1 mark for "3 years from grant". Many students incorrectly write "3 years from filing" — deduct marks if this error is made.)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State any **three** grounds on which a compulsory licence can be granted under Section 84 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. The **reasonable requirements of the public** with respect to the patented invention have **not been satisfied**.
2. The patented invention is **not available to the public at a reasonably affordable price**.
3. The **patentee refuses to grant a licence** on reasonable terms despite a reasonable request by the applicant.

*(Any three from the six grounds listed in Section 84(1) are acceptable.)*

---

### 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Module 3 Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following questions. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **grounds** and **procedure** for the grant of a **compulsory licence** under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **landmark case of Natco Pharma Ltd. v. Bayer Corporation (2012)** and its significance in the context of compulsory licensing. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

The grant of a compulsory licence is governed primarily by **Section 84** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970. The grounds, conditions, and procedure are as follows:

**1. Eligibility of Applicant (Section 84(1)):**
Any person interested — including a **Government undertaking** — may file an application. A crucial pre-condition is the expiration of **3 years from the date of grant** of the patent. This waiting period allows the patentee an opportunity to commercially exploit the invention.

**2. Statutory Grounds (Section 84(1)):**
The Controller may grant a compulsory licence if satisfied that any of the following conditions exist:
- Reasonable requirements of the public have not been satisfied.
- The invention is not available to the public at a reasonably affordable price.
- The patented invention is not being worked in the territory of India.
- The patentee has refused to grant a licence on reasonable terms.
- The patentee has imposed unfair or discriminatory conditions.
- The patentee is using the invention in a manner prejudicial to the working of another significant patented invention.

**3. Procedure:**
- Application must be filed in **Form 17** with the prescribed fee before the Controller.
- Controller issues notice to the patentee and conducts a **hearing** of both parties (Section 89).
- Controller may also invite representations from consumer groups and trade bodies.
- The Controller applies the **Section 91** criteria to fix royalty and other terms of the licence.
- The Controller issues a written order granting the licence.

**4. Conditions of Licence (Section 90):**
The licence granted is **non-exclusive**, **non-assignable**, and limited to the unmet public requirement. The licensee must predominantly supply the Indian domestic market.

**5. Appeal:**
Any aggrieved party may appeal to the **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** or the **High Court** under Section 91A.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 2 marks for grounds, 2 marks for procedure, 1 mark for conditions, 1 mark for appeal provisions. Students frequently miss the "3-year waiting period" — strict deduction.)*

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

**Background of the Case:**
- **Drug Involved:** Sorafenib Tosylate, marketed as **Nexavar** by **Bayer Corporation** (Germany).
- **Drug Use:** Treatment of advanced **kidney (renal) and liver cancer**.
- **Patent Granted:** Bayer held an Indian patent for Nexavar.

**Issue:**
- Bayer sold Nexavar at approximately **₹2,80,428 per month** per patient.
- The drug was inaccessible to a vast majority of Indian patients due to its high cost.

**Applicant:**
- **Natco Pharma Ltd.** filed an application for a compulsory licence before the Controller of Patents, Mumbai in 2011.

**Grounds Argued by Natco:**
- Bayer had **not worked the patent in India** (the drug was imported, not manufactured).
- The drug was **not available at a reasonably affordable price**.
- Reasonable requirements of the public were **not satisfied**.
- Bayer had refused to grant a voluntary licence on reasonable terms.

**Decision (March 2012):**
The **Controller of Patents** granted India's **first-ever compulsory licence** in favour of Natco Pharma. The Controller fixed the royalty at **6% of net sales**, payable to Bayer.

**Terms of the Licence:**
- Natco was required to supply the drug at a **maximum of ₹8,800 per month**.
- The licence was **non-exclusive** and **non-assignable**.
- Natco was to manufacture the drug in India.

**Significance:**
- Established India's sovereign right to override patent monopolies for **public health**.
- Reinforced the **Doha Declaration** principle that patent rights do not override public health concerns.
- Became a global reference point for compulsory licensing under **TRIPS Article 31**.
- Demonstrated that India is willing to use flexibilities provided by international IP law.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 2 marks for facts, 2 marks for grounds, 2 marks for the outcome, 1 mark for significance. Students often forget to mention the "6% royalty" — important factual point.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **compulsory licence for export** under **Sections 92 and 92A** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970. How do these provisions align with the **Doha Declaration** and **TRIPS Article 31bis**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish between **compulsory licence under Section 84** and **government use of invention under Section 100** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Genesis of Export-Related Compulsory Licensing:**
The WTO's **Doha Declaration on TRIPS and Public Health (November 2001)** recognised that countries with insufficient or no manufacturing capacity in the pharmaceutical sector could face difficulties in making effective use of compulsory licensing. A decision was taken in 2003 (the "Paragraph 6 System") and formalised in 2017 as **TRIPS Article 31bis**, allowing the export of pharmaceutical products manufactured under compulsory licences.

**2. Section 92 — General Export Provisions:**
Section 92 empowers the **Central Government** to issue a notification declaring that compulsory licences may be issued to a specific person or company for the **manufacture and export** of a patented product to a designated country that has made a request. The Controller is then authorised to grant such an export-oriented licence.

**3. Section 92A — Special Provisions for Pharmaceuticals:**
Section 92A was inserted in 2005 to specifically enable Indian manufacturers to export patented **pharmaceutical products** to countries with insufficient manufacturing capacity. Key features include:
- Only an **Indian manufacturer** can apply.
- The product must be exported to an eligible importing country.
- The manufacturer must comply with labelling and notification requirements to prevent diversion of the product back to the Indian market.
- The licence is non-exclusive and subject to the conditions of TRIPS Article 31bis.

**4. Alignment with Doha Declaration and TRIPS 31bis:**
- Both Sections 92 and 92A operationalise the **Doha flexibilities**.
- They respect the TRIPS condition that the licence is for predominantly domestic supply, with export as an exception.
- They uphold the right of **least-developed and developing countries** to procure affordable medicines.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 2 marks for Doha context, 2 marks for Section 92, 2 marks for Section 92A, 1 mark for TRIPS linkage.)*

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

| Parameter | Section 84 (Compulsory Licence) | Section 100 (Government Use) |
|---|---|---|
| **Triggering Authority** | Any third-party applicant | Central Government / Government undertaking |
| **Grounds** | Reasonable requirements not met; non-working; unaffordable price | Public non-commercial use; emergency |
| **Initiator's Role** | Disinterested licensee (private party) | Government acts as the user |
| **Consent of Patentee** | Not required (after conditions) | Not required |
| **Royalty** | Fixed by Controller under Section 91 | Determined by Central Government |
| **Scope** | Commercial supply to the public | Government use for public service |
| **Time Restriction** | 3 years from grant of patent | Can be invoked **anytime** after grant |

**1. Conceptual Difference:**
- **Section 84 (Compulsory Licence):** A **judicial/quasi-judicial** mechanism where a private party applies to the Controller. It involves a hearing, a determination of grounds, and a royalty assessment.
- **Section 100 (Government Use):** An **executive/administrative** mechanism where the Government itself uses the patented invention for its own purposes or on behalf of a third party, without the need for a hearing before the Controller initially.

**2. Procedural Difference:**
- Section 84 requires a **statutory waiting period of 3 years** from the date of grant.
- Section 100 has **no waiting period** and can be invoked immediately if the Government notifies its intention to use the invention.

**3. Practical Examples:**
- Section 84: Natco v. Bayer (2012) — private generic manufacturer.
- Section 100: Government use of patented technology in **defence production** or **public health emergencies** (e.g., the use of patented drug formulations during the **COVID-19 pandemic**).

*(Examiner's Note: Award 3 marks for conceptual differences, 2 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for examples. Students often confuse Sections 84 and 100 — ensure the distinction is clear.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Writing **"3 years from filing date"** instead of **"3 years from date of grant"** — this is the **#1 mistake** and costs a full mark.
> 2. Confusing **Section 84** (third-party compulsory licence) with **Section 100** (Government use).
> 3. Forgetting to mention the **Controller of Patents** as the granting authority.
> 4. Omitting the **Doha Declaration 2001** context — examiners expect it for Section 92/92A questions.
> 5. In case-based questions, students often write general facts but miss the **specific royalty percentage (6%)** and the **price reduction (₹2.8 lakh to ₹8,800)** in *Natco v. Bayer*.

---

### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Compulsory Licence** = Non-voluntary authorisation by the **Controller of Patents**.
- [x] **Primary Section:** **Section 84** of the **Indian Patents Act, 1970**.
- [x] **Waiting Period:** **3 years from the date of grant** of the patent (NOT from filing).
- [x] **Key Grounds:** Reasonable requirements unmet, unaffordable price, non-working in India, refusal to license.
- [x] **Application Form:** **Form 17**.
- [x] **Royalty:** Determined by Controller under **Section 91**.
- [x] **Licence Type:** Non-exclusive, non-assignable, predominantly for Indian market.
- [x] **Revocation:** Under **Section 85** on non-compliance or change of circumstances.
- [x] **Export Licence:** **Section 92** (general) and **Section 92A** (pharmaceuticals only).
- [x] **Government Use:** **Section 100** — invoked anytime, no waiting period.
- [x] **International Backing:** **TRIPS Article 31** (general) and **TRIPS Article 31bis** (export).
- [x] **Doha Declaration (2001):** Public health safeguard, supported compulsory licensing.
- [x] **Landmark Case:** ***Natco Pharma Ltd. v. Bayer Corporation* (2012)** — India's first compulsory licence for **Nexavar** (Sorafenib Tosylate).
- [x] **Royalty in Natco:** **6% of net sales**.
- [x] **Appeal Authority:** **IPAB** (now transferred to High Courts) / **High Court**.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always link the legal provision (Section number) with the **legal principle (public health / public interest)**. This analytical approach is what KTU examiners reward with full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infringement of Copyright
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Infringement of Copyright

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Copyright Infringement** is the unauthorized use of works protected by copyright, where a third party exercises any of the exclusive rights vested in the copyright owner without obtaining prior license, assignment, or legal permission. Under the **Indian Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended in 2012), infringement is broadly classified into:

- **Primary Infringement** (Vicarious/Strict Liability): Direct violation of exclusive rights by a person (Section 51).
- **Secondary Infringement** (Contributory): Importation, possession, dealing, or permitting the use of infringing copies (Section 51A & 52).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Copyright is a **negative right** — it does not grant the owner the right to use, but the right to *exclude others* from using. Infringement occurs when this exclusion is violated.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you own a **locked diary** (your creative work). The lock is the **copyright** — you alone hold the key. If someone picks the lock, reads, copies, or even publishes a chapter of your diary **without your key (license)**, that is **infringement**. The diary itself is your property; the *words and ideas inside* are protected by the lock of copyright. Anyone caught duplicating, broadcasting, or adapting the content without permission has committed the offence.

## 1.3 Scope of Copyright Protection

> [!NOTE]
> The **Berne Convention (1886)** and **TRIPS Agreement (1994)** establish that copyright protection is **automatic** upon creation and requires no formal registration. However, in India, **registration under Section 44** serves as prima facie evidence in court.

Protected Works under Section 13:
- **Literary Works** (books, software, source code)
- **Dramatic Works** (scripts, plays)
- **Musical Works** (compositions, scores)
- **Artistic Works** (paintings, photographs, sculptures)
- **Cinematograph Films** (motion pictures)
- **Sound Recordings** (recordings, broadcasts)
- **Computer Programs** (treated as literary works)

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Term of Protection

| Work Category | Term of Copyright (Post-2012 Amendment) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic | **Life of author + 60 years** |
| Cinematograph Films | **60 years from date of publication** |
| Sound Recordings | **60 years from date of publication** |
| Government Works | **60 years from date of publication** |
| Anonymous/Pseudonymous Works | **60 years from date of publication** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rights Conferred on the Copyright Owner (Section 14)

The copyright owner enjoys a **bundle of six exclusive rights**. Infringement occurs when ANY of these rights is exercised by an unauthorized person:

1. **Right to Reproduce** — make copies in any form.
2. **Right to Issue Copies** — distribute copies to the public.
3. **Right to Perform** — public performance of the work.
4. **Right to Communicate** — broadcast or make available to the public.
5. **Right to Adapt/Translate** — create derivative works.
6. **Right to Sell/Give on Hire** — commercial rental rights (for certain works).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For infringement to occur, the alleged infringer must have **exercised one of the above rights** in relation to the **whole or a substantial part** of the work.

## 2.2 Test of Infringement (Substantial Similarity Test)

Courts in India apply a **two-pronged test** to determine infringement:

### Prong 1: Objective Test (Lay Observer Test)
- A **layperson (ordinary, average, reasonable person)** is asked to compare the two works.
- The test: *Would an ordinary observer, upon casual inspection, recognize the defendant's work as a copy of the plaintiff's work?*

### Prong 2: Substantial Part Test
- The reproduction must be of a **substantial part** of the work — not necessarily quantitatively large, but **qualitatively significant**.
- As per **Lord Justice Peterson in University of London Press Ltd v. University Tutorial Press Ltd (1916)**: *"The part reproduced must be substantial in terms of quality, not mere quantity."*

## 2.3 Acts Constituting Infringement (Section 51)

The following acts constitute infringement when done without the license of the copyright owner:

- Reproducing, storing, or communicating the work in any medium.
- Issuing copies to the public not previously in circulation.
- Performing the work in public (for dramatic, musical works).
- Communicating the work to the public by broadcast or wire.
- Making translations, adaptations, or arrangements.
- Selling, hiring, or distributing infringing copies.
- Permitting a place of public entertainment to be used for infringing performance.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Condition for Infringement | Legal Provision |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Authorization** | Must be UNLICENSED | Section 51 |
| **Substantial Part** | Reproduction of QUALITATIVE part | Section 51, Proviso |
| **Test Applied** | Lay Observer / Ordinary Person | Judicial Precedent |
| **Strict Liability** | No mens rea required for civil | Section 51 |
| **Mens Rea Required** | Only for criminal prosecution | Section 63 |
| **Statutory Exceptions** | Fair dealing, private use | Section 52 |
| **Term Expiry** | No infringement if in Public Domain | Section 22-29 |

## 2.5 Concept Matrix: Primary vs. Secondary Infringement

| Aspect | Primary Infringement (S.51) | Secondary Infringement (S.51A) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Direct violation | Indirect / Contributory |
| **Liability** | Strict (no fault needed) | Fault-based (knowledge required) |
| **Acts** | Copying, distributing, performing | Selling, importing, possessing infringing copies |
| **Punishment** | Civil damages + injunction | Same as primary when knowingly done |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Industry**: Engineers must understand copyright to avoid copying open-source codebases without complying with licenses (GPL, MIT, Apache).
- **AI/ML Field**: Training datasets raise copyright questions — recent disputes over copyrighted images, text, and code being used to train models.
- **Content Creation**: YouTubers, game developers, and UI/UX designers must ensure their works don't infringe existing creative content.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework for Determining Infringement

## 3.1 Algorithmic Step-by-Step Analysis

A KTU examiner expects a structured analytical framework when answering infringement questions. Apply the following step-wise evaluation:

**Step 1: Establish Existence of a Copyright**
- Verify the work falls under Section 13 categories.
- Confirm copyright is subsisting (term not expired).

**Step 2: Identify the Copyright Owner**
- Author (default) or assignee (if assigned in writing, S.19).

**Step 3: Identify the Alleged Infringing Act**
- Reproduction? Distribution? Performance? Communication? Adaptation?

**Step 4: Apply the Substantial Similarity Test**
- Objective comparison: lay observer test.
- Subjective comparison: substantial part in quality.

**Step 5: Check for Defenses / Exceptions (S.52)**
- Fair dealing for research, criticism, review, news reporting.
- Private use, educational use, library/archive use.

**Step 6: Determine Remedies**
- Civil (injunction, damages, accounts of profits).
- Criminal (imprisonment up to 3 years + fine of ₹2 lakhs).
- Border seizure of infringing goods.

## 3.2 The Substantial Similarity Analysis — Mathematical Representation

Let us model the similarity between the original and infringing work using a conceptual similarity score $S$:

$$
S = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (w_i \cdot f_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight (qualitative importance) of the $i^{th}$ element
- $f_i$ = similarity coefficient of the $i^{th}$ element (range $0 \leq f_i \leq 1$)
- $n$ = total number of elements compared

**Decision Rule:**

$$
\text{Infringement} \iff S \geq S_{threshold} \approx 0.5
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The threshold $S_{threshold}$ is not mathematical but **judicial**. Courts have consistently ruled that **qualitative importance** outweighs quantitative similarity. A few lines of a hit song may constitute "substantial part" if they are the **hook or signature element**.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example

**Case:** Composer A writes a song with a distinctive 8-note melody hook. Composer B writes a song using 4 of those 8 notes.

### Step-by-Step Analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original Hook} &: \text{8-note signature melody} \\
\text{Copied Element} &: \text{4 notes} \\
\text{Quantitative Copy} &: \frac{4}{8} = 0.50 = 50\% \\
\text{Qualitative Importance} &: \text{VERY HIGH (signature element)} \\
\therefore S &\gg S_{threshold} \\
\text{Verdict} &: \text{INFRINGEMENT (qualitative substantiality)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Textual Logic:
1. Although quantitatively only 50% was copied, the copied portion is the **core signature element** of the original.
2. Under the **qualitative substantiality principle** (as in *Amar Nath Sehgal v. Union of India* and *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak*), the copied portion is the heart of the work.
3. Hence, the court will rule in favor of the plaintiff.

## 3.4 Exception Analysis (Section 52)

The following are **NOT** considered infringement (statutory carve-outs):

$$
\text{Defenses} = \{ \text{Fair Dealing}, \text{Private Use}, \text{Research}, \text{Criticism}, \text{News}, \text{Education}, \text{Library}, \text{Judicial} \}
$$

### Four-Step Fair Dealing Test (Western Counties v. May):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1. \text{ Amount and substantiality of copying} \rightarrow \text{Is it excessive?} \\
&2. \text{ Purpose of use} \rightarrow \text{Is it for non-commercial gain?} \\
&3. \text{ Nature of copyrighted work} \rightarrow \text{Is it factual or creative?} \\
&4. \text{Effect on potential market} \rightarrow \text{Does it harm the original market?} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

If **ALL** four conditions favor the user, fair dealing applies. Even one negative factor may defeat the defense.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Copyright Infringement Determination Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Copyrighted Work] --> B{Is Copyright Subsisting?}
    B -- No --> C[NO INFRINGEMENT - Public Domain]
    B -- Yes --> D[Identify the Infringing Act]
    D --> E{Type of Act?}
    E -- Reproduction --> F[Primary Infringement S.51]
    E -- Distribution --> G[Primary Infringement S.51]
    E -- Performance --> H[Primary Infringement S.51]
    E -- Sale/Import --> I[Secondary Infringement S.51A]
    F --> J[Apply Substantial Similarity Test]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Substantial Similarity?}
    K -- No --> L[NO INFRINGEMENT]
    K -- Yes --> M{Statutory Defense S.52?}
    M -- Yes --> N[FAIR DEALING - No Infringement]
    M -- No --> O[INFRINGEMENT ESTABLISHED]
    O --> P[Choose Remedy]
    P --> Q[Civil Injunction]
    P --> R[Monetary Damages]
    P --> S[Criminal Prosecution S.63]
```

```mermaid
    R1 -. Violation .-> V1[Photocopying Books]

```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Remedies for Infringement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infringement Established] --> B[Remedies Available]
    B --> C[Civil Remedies]
    B --> D[Criminal Remedies]
    B --> E[Administrative Remedies]
    
    C --> C1[Temporary/Perpetual Injunction]
    C --> C2[Damages or Account of Profits]
    C --> C3[Delivery up of Infringing Copies]
    C --> C4[Anton Piller Order]
    
    D --> D1[Imprisonment up to 3 years]

```

## 4.4 Schematic: Infringement vs. Exceptions Matrix

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INFRINGEMENT
        A1[Reproducing entire book]

        A3[Broadcasting copyrighted song]

    end
    
    subgraph NOT_INFRINGEMENT
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** Define copyright infringement. What is the difference between primary and secondary infringement?

**Model Answer:**

Copyright infringement refers to the **unauthorized exercise of any of the exclusive rights** granted to a copyright owner under Section 14 of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957. It occurs when a person reproduces, distributes, performs, communicates, or adapts the work **without obtaining a license** from the owner.

**Difference between Primary and Secondary Infringement:**

- **Primary Infringement (S.51):** Direct violation of exclusive rights — for example, copying a book or broadcasting a song. It is a **strict liability** offence; mens rea (guilty intent) is **not** required.
- **Secondary Infringement (S.51A):** Indirect violation such as **selling, importing, or possessing** infringing copies. Knowledge of the infringing nature is **required** for liability.

> [!NOTE]
> Both civil and criminal remedies apply to both types, but mens rea is essential only for **criminal** prosecution under S.63.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** Explain the "substantial similarity" test in copyright infringement.

**Model Answer:**

The **substantial similarity test** is the standard judicial test applied by Indian and global courts to determine whether a defendant's work infringes upon the plaintiff's copyrighted work. The test is two-pronged:

1. **Lay Observer Test (Objective):** An ordinary, average, reasonable person, upon casual observation, must recognize the defendant's work as substantially similar to the plaintiff's work.
2. **Substantial Part Test (Subjective):** The reproduction must cover a **qualitatively substantial part** of the original — not necessarily quantitatively large. A small but signature element (like a melody hook) may constitute substantial similarity.

**Legal Precedent:** This test was established in *University of London Press v. University Tutorial Press (1916)* and followed in *Sajeev Pillai v. Venu Kunnapalli* and *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak*.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** 

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **exclusive rights** of a copyright owner under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957. Discuss how violation of each right constitutes infringement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **defenses available** to a person accused of copyright infringement under Section 52 of the Act, with appropriate examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Copyright Act, 1957, under **Section 14**, confers the following **exclusive economic rights** on the copyright owner. Infringement occurs when these rights are exercised without the owner's license.

**1. Right to Reproduce the Work (S.14(a)(i))**
- The right to make one or more copies of the work in any material form.
- *Infringement Example:* Photocopying a textbook, duplicating software, downloading music files illegally.

**2. Right to Issue Copies to the Public (S.14(a)(ii))**
- The right to sell, give on hire, or otherwise distribute copies not previously in circulation.
- *Infringement Example:* Selling pirated DVDs in the market.

**3. Right to Perform the Work in Public (S.14(a)(iii))**
- Applies to dramatic, musical, and literary works.
- *Infringement Example:* Performing a copyrighted play in a theatre without a license.

**4. Right to Communicate the Work to the Public (S.14(a)(iv))**
- Includes broadcasting, webcasting, and making the work available online.
- *Infringement Example:* Streaming a film on a website without authorization.

**5. Right to Make Translations and Adaptations (S.14(a)(v))**
- Includes translation, adaptation, arrangement, or any transformation.
- *Infringement Example:* Translating a novel into another language without permission.

**6. Right to Sell or Give on Hire Commercial Rental Right (S.14(b))**
- Specific to sound recordings, cinematograph films, and computer programs.
- *Infringement Example:* Renting out pirated CDs commercially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point:** Infringement can occur with respect to **"the whole or any substantial part"** of the work. A substantial part is judged by **quality**, not quantity.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Section 52** of the Copyright Act provides a list of acts that **do NOT constitute infringement**, despite the existence of copyright. These are statutory exceptions:

**1. Fair Dealing for Private or Personal Use (S.52(1)(a))**
- Use of the work by a person for purposes of private study, research, criticism, review, or news reporting.
- *Example:* A student photocopying a chapter for personal study.

**2. Fair Dealing for Criticism or Review (S.52(1)(a))**
- Quoting from a book or song in a review article, provided the source is acknowledged.

**3. Fair Dealing for News Reporting (S.52(1)(a))**
- A news channel broadcasting brief clips of a copyrighted movie to report on it.

**4. Use for Judicial Proceedings (S.52(1)(c))**
- Reproduction of works in the course of judicial proceedings or for reporting them.

**5. Use by Educational Institutions (S.52(1)(h), (i))**
- Reproduction for educational purposes, classroom teaching, or examination preparation.

**6. Library and Archive Use (S.52(1)(o))**
- Libraries can preserve and provide access to copyrighted works under prescribed conditions.

**7. Use of Works Made Available to Public (S.52(1)(r))**
- After the work is made publicly available, certain uses (such as broadcasts) may be permitted.

**8. Transient and Incidental Storage (S.52(1)(ac))**
- Temporary digital storage in the course of electronic transmission, like web caching.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse **"fair dealing"** with the American concept of **"fair use."** Indian law is narrower — only specific purposes listed in S.52 are allowed. Mere "good intent" is **not** a defense. Always cite the **specific sub-section** of S.52.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What are the **civil and criminal remedies** available to a copyright owner against infringement? Discuss with reference to the Copyright Act, 1957. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the **concept of fair dealing** in copyright law. How does it balance the rights of the owner and the public interest? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Civil Remedies (Sections 55–62):**

1. **Injunction (S.55):** Temporary or perpetual court order restraining the infringer from continuing the infringing act. Most common remedy.

2. **Damages (S.55):** Monetary compensation for loss suffered. May be **actual damages** or **statutory damages** depending on case.

3. **Account of Profits (S.58):** Court may order the infringer to surrender the profits earned through infringement.

4. **Delivery Up of Infringing Copies (S.58):** The infringer must hand over all infringing copies and plates used for reproduction.

5. **Anton Piller Order:** A court order allowing the plaintiff to enter the defendant's premises to seize evidence of infringement. Used in cases of suspected destruction of evidence.

**Criminal Remedies (Sections 63–70):**

1. **Imprisonment (S.63):** The infringer may be punished with imprisonment ranging from **6 months to 3 years**.

2. **Fine (S.63):** A minimum fine of **₹50,000** and a maximum fine of **₹2,00,000** for first-time offenders. Repeat offenders face enhanced penalties.

3. **Search and Seizure (S.64):** Police may search premises and seize infringing copies on complaint by the copyright owner.

4. **Forfeiture of Infringing Copies (S.66):** All infringing copies may be ordered to be destroyed or forfeited to the government.

> [!NOTE]
> The Copyright Board, constituted under **Section 11**, may also intervene for administrative remedies and dispute resolution.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Concept of Fair Dealing:**

Fair dealing is a **statutory defense** to copyright infringement, enumerated under **Section 52(1)(a)** of the Indian Copyright Act. It permits limited use of copyrighted material **without the owner's permission**, provided specific conditions are satisfied.

**Permitted Purposes:**
- Private or personal use
- Research
- Criticism or review
- Reporting of current events

**Balancing Test (Four-Factor Analysis — based on judicial precedent):**

1. **Purpose and Character of Use:** Non-commercial, educational, or transformative uses are favoured.
2. **Nature of Copyrighted Work:** Factual works receive less protection than highly creative works.
3. **Amount and Substantiality:** The portion used must not be substantial in quality.
4. **Effect on Market:** The use must not substitute for or harm the potential market of the original.

**Balancing Rights:**

- **Owner's Right:** To control commercial exploitation of their work and earn economic benefits.
- **Public Interest:** To enable education, research, criticism, and the free flow of information.

**Judicial Interpretation:** In *Chancellor Masters v. Narendra Publishing House* and *Civic Chandran v. Ammini Amma*, the courts emphasized that fair dealing must be **judicious and not a pretext for infringement**.

**Critical Analysis:**

- **Pro-Owner View:** Fair dealing is too narrow and allows private parties to copy liberally.
- **Pro-Public View:** Fair dealing is essential to prevent the monopolization of knowledge and to encourage creativity, education, and the advancement of public welfare.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently list the four factors of "fair use" (American doctrine) under Indian law. **Indian law follows "fair dealing"** — a closed-list, purpose-specific doctrine. The US four-factor test is persuasive but **not binding** in India. Always reference the specific S.52(1)(a) provision.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Patterns & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas (Validated by KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "copyright" with "patent" or "trademark":** Copyright protects *expression* (the text of a book, the notes of a song), not the underlying *idea*. Examiners deduct marks if students state that copyright protects ideas.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting "Substantial Part" requirement:** Quoting the S.51 provision without emphasizing that infringement must cover a *substantial part* of the work is incomplete. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the "Lay Observer" test:** Students often write about infringement without referencing the judicial test. Always mention the test and cite at least one precedent.
> 
> 4. **Misstating the term of copyright:** The term is **Life + 60 years** (post-2012 amendment), not Life + 50 years. Examiners strictly check this.
> 
> 5. **Not distinguishing Civil vs. Criminal mens rea:** Civil infringement is **strict liability**; criminal requires **mens rea (guilty mind)** under S.63. This distinction carries 2–3 marks in any 14-mark question.
> 
> 6. **Skipping Section 52 exceptions:** When asked about infringement, students must also write about what is *not* infringement. Neglecting S.52 costs 3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Copyright infringement is the **unauthorized exercise of any exclusive right** of the copyright owner.
- **Legal Basis:** **Section 51** (primary) and **Section 51A** (secondary) of the Copyright Act, 1957.
- **Bundle of Rights (S.14):** Reproduce, Distribute, Perform, Communicate, Adapt, Sell/Hire.
- **Test Applied:** **Lay Observer Test** + **Substantial Part Test** (qualitative, not quantitative).
- **Strict Liability:** No mens rea needed for **civil** infringement; required for **criminal** (S.63).
- **Term of Copyright:** **Life of author + 60 years** for literary/dramatic/musical/artistic works.
- **Computer Programs:** Treated as **literary works** under S.2(o).
- **Exceptions (S.52):** Fair dealing for private use, criticism, news, education, judicial proceedings, libraries.
- **Fair Dealing ≠ Fair Use:** Indian law follows the **closed-list "fair dealing"** doctrine, not the open-ended US "fair use."
- **Civil Remedies:** Injunction, damages, account of profits, delivery up, Anton Piller order (S.55–58).
- **Criminal Remedies:** Imprisonment **6 months–3 years**; Fine **₹50,000–₹2,00,000** (S.63).
- **Key Cases:** *University of London Press v. Tutorial Press* (1916); *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak* (2008); *Sajeev Pillai v. Venu Kunnapalli*.
- **Automatic Protection:** No registration required; copyright subsists upon creation (Berne Convention).
- **Registration Benefit:** Registration under **Section 44** provides prima facie evidence in court.
- **Perpetual Pitfall:** Always remember that copyright protects **expression**, NOT the **idea** (Idea-Expression Dichotomy, S.13(1)(a)).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Remedies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remedies in Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)** refer to the **legal reliefs, enforcement mechanisms, and corrective actions** available to the right-holder (IP owner) against any unauthorized use, infringement, misappropriation, or violation of their protected intellectual property. These remedies are statutory, judicial, and administrative in nature and aim to **prevent further violation, compensate the loss, and punish the wrongdoer**.

In the context of Indian IP law, remedies are primarily governed by:
- The **Copyright Act, 1957** (Sections 55–62)
- The **Patents Act, 1970** (Sections 104–114)
- The **Trade Marks Act, 1999** (Sections 103–120)
- The **Designs Act, 2000** (Sections 19–25)
- The **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999** (Sections 67–70)
- The **Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Act, 2001**
- The **Information Technology Act, 2000** (for digital/IP-cyber offences)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Remedies can be broadly classified into three main categories — **Civil Remedies**, **Criminal Remedies**, and **Administrative/Border Remedies**. Each category serves a distinct purpose: restoration, punishment, and prevention.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have built a beautiful house with a strong fence around it. One day, you find that a neighbor has **broken the fence, walked in, and is using your garden furniture** without permission. What can you do?

1. **Civil Remedy** → You can go to the court and ask the judge to **order the neighbor to stop** (injunction), **return your furniture** (delivery-up), and **pay you money for the damage** (damages or accounts of profits).
2. **Criminal Remedy** → You can file a police complaint so the neighbor is **arrested and fined** for trespassing (imprisonment and fine).
3. **Administrative Remedy** → You can call the **municipal authorities** to remove the encroachment immediately (customs seizure, border measures).

In this analogy:
- **The house** = Your Intellectual Property (patent, trademark, copyright).
- **The fence** = Legal protection granted by the IP statute.
- **The neighbor** = The infringer.
- **The court/police/authorities** = The three pillars of IP enforcement — **Civil, Criminal, and Administrative**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Emphasis on Critical Terms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Infringement:** Any unauthorized act that violates the exclusive rights of the IP owner.
> - **Plaintiff / Right-holder:** The person or entity whose IP has been infringed.
> - **Defendant / Infringer:** The person or entity alleged to have committed the infringement.
> - **Cause of Action:** The legal basis on which the right-holder files the suit.
> - **Relief:** The actual benefit or remedy granted by the court.
> - **Ex parte:** A court order passed in the absence of the other party (urgent cases).
> - **Statutory Damages:** Pre-determined damages fixed by law, especially in copyright law.

---

## 1.4 Why Remedies Matter in Engineering & Innovation

Engineering students and innovators must understand IP remedies because:
- Filing for a patent, trademark, or design is only **half the battle**; enforcement ensures real value.
- Startups and tech companies depend on IP enforcement to **protect R&D investment**.
- Infringement of software, hardware designs, and brand identity can lead to **multi-crore losses** and **market dilution**.
- A working knowledge of remedies helps engineers **draft stronger IP clauses** in licensing and employment contracts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of IP Remedies — A Three-Tier Enforcement Architecture

IP remedies operate on **three independent but complementary tiers**, and the right-holder may use **one, two, or all three simultaneously** depending on the nature and severity of the infringement.

### 2.1.1 Tier 1: Civil Remedies (Private Law Enforcement)

These are pursued by the IP owner through a civil court of competent jurisdiction. The objective is **monetary compensation** and **restraint of future infringement**.

| # | Remedy | Description | Statutory Basis |
|---|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Injunction** | A court order restraining the infringer from continuing the illegal act. Can be **temporary (ad-interim)**, **permanent**, or **mandatory**. | Copyright Act §55; Patents Act §108; TM Act §103 |
| 2 | **Damages** | Monetary compensation to make good the loss suffered by the IP owner. | Copyright Act §55(1)(a); Patents Act §108(1); TM Act §103(1) |
| 3 | **Account of Profits** | Infringer must surrender the profits earned through the illegal act. | Copyright Act §55(1)(b); TM Act §103(1) |
| 4 | **Delivery Up of Infringing Goods** | Court orders surrender/destruction of infringing articles, plates, and materials. | Copyright Act §58; TM Act §103(2) |
| 5 | **Anton Piller Order** | Ex parte order allowing the right-holder to enter the infringer's premises to preserve evidence. | Common Law (Civil Procedure Code, 1908, Order 39) |
| 6 | **Mareva Injunction** | Order to freeze the infringer's assets to prevent dissipation before judgment. | Common Law / CPC Order 39 |
| 7 | **Costs of the Suit** | Court awards litigation costs to the winning party. | Civil Procedure Code |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** In a 14-mark question, examiners expect at least **4–5 remedies** with their **statutory sections** and **brief explanation**.

### 2.1.2 Tier 2: Criminal Remedies (Public Law Enforcement)

These are state-driven prosecutions for serious violations, particularly counterfeiting and piracy.

| # | Remedy | Description | Statutory Basis |
|---|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Imprisonment** | Custodial sentence ranging from 6 months to 3 years (extendable). | Copyright Act §63; TM Act §104–105; Designs Act §22 |
| 2 | **Fine** | Monetary penalty ranging from ₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 (varies by statute). | Copyright Act §63; TM Act §104 |
| 3 | **Seizure of Infringing Goods** | Police may seize the goods, plates, and instruments used for infringement. | Copyright Act §64; TM Act §105 |
| 4 | **Search and Warrant** | Court-issued search warrants to raid infringing premises. | CrPC / BNSS provisions |
| 5 | **Cognizable & Non-Bailable Offences** | For repeat or willful commercial infringement, the offence is cognizable. | TM Act §104 (e.g., counterfeiting) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 104 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999** makes the offence of counterfeiting a registered trademark a **cognizable, non-bailable, and non-compoundable** offence with imprisonment up to **3 years** and fine up to **₹2 lakh**. This is a high-yield KTU point.

### 2.1.3 Tier 3: Administrative & Border Remedies

These are preventive measures executed through government agencies, especially customs.

| # | Remedy | Description | Statutory Basis |
|---|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Customs Recordal / Border Detention** | IP owner records rights with Customs; infringing imports are detained. | Customs Act, 1962 + IP Rules |
| 2 | **Impoundment** | Customs authorities impound suspected infringing consignments at the port. | Intellectual Property Rights (Imported Goods) Rules, 2007 |
| 3 | **Administrative Seizure** | Government authorities seize counterfeit goods in the market. | TM Act §115; Drugs & Cosmetics Act |
| 4 | **Domain Name Dispute Resolution** | Administrative remedy through WIPO / INDRP / .IN Registry. | UDRP Policy, .IN Dispute Resolution Policy |
| 5 | **Market Withdrawal Orders** | Regulatory orders to recall infringing products from the market. | TM Act §103(2)(b) |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield revision matrix** that summarises the complete remedy architecture. Memorize this for Part A and Part B answers.

| Statute | Civil Remedy Section(s) | Criminal Remedy Section(s) | Max. Imprisonment | Max. Fine |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| Copyright Act, 1957 | §§55, 58, 62 | §§63, 64, 65, 66 | **3 years** (extendable to **7 years** for repeat) | **₹2 lakh** (extendable to **₹10 lakh**) |
| Patents Act, 1970 | §§104, 108 | §118 | Not criminalised (relief is only civil) | N/A |
| Trade Marks Act, 1999 | §§103, 113 | §§104, 105 | **3 years** | **₹2 lakh** (extendable) |
| Designs Act, 2000 | §19 | §22 | **3 years** | **₹1 lakh** |
| GI Act, 1999 | §67 | §69 | **3 years** | **₹2 lakh** |
| PPV&FR Act, 2001 | §61 | §68 | **3 years** | **₹1 lakh** |
| Information Technology Act, 2000 | §65, §66 | §66, §66B–E | **3 years** (extendable) | **₹5 lakh** |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Remedy System

- **Pharmaceutical Patents:** Companies like **Novartis, Roche, Pfizer** rely on civil injunctions to block generic manufacturers from launching biosimilars.
- **Software Industry:** Microsoft, Adobe, and Oracle pursue **criminal raids** against software pirates under the Copyright Act.
- **Luxury Brands:** Louis Vuitton, Gucci, and Rolex use **customs border recordation** to seize counterfeit goods at Indian ports (Mumbai, Delhi, Chennai).
- **Tech Startups:** Indian startups use **domain name arbitration (UDRP, INDRP)** to recover hijacked domain names.
- **Engineering Designs:** Auto manufacturers (Tata, Mahindra) protect designs under the **Designs Act, 2000** and seek injunctions against copycat designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** For an engineer-turned-entrepreneur, knowledge of remedies is as important as knowledge of patent filing. Without enforcement, an IP right is merely a **paper right**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> In a legal subject, "derivations" are replaced by **analytical breakdowns** — step-by-step logical exposition of how each remedy is invoked, what conditions must be satisfied, and what relief flows from each.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Breakdown: Invocation of Civil Remedies

### Step 1 — Establishment of the IP Right
The plaintiff must first establish that **a valid, subsisting IP right exists**. This is a **threshold condition**. Without a registered/valid right, the suit is not maintainable (with limited exceptions for **passing off** in trademarks and **common law copyright**).

### Step 2 — Demonstration of Infringement
The plaintiff must show that the defendant has committed acts **falling within the exclusive rights** granted by the statute. The burden of proof lies on the plaintiff, with rebuttable presumptions in favor of the registered proprietor.

### Step 3 — Choice of Forum
The plaintiff files the suit in a **District Court** or **High Court** with territorial jurisdiction, depending on pecuniary value and subject matter. IP suits now also lie before **Commercial Courts** (Commercial Courts Act, 2015).

### Step 4 — Application for Interim Relief
To prevent **irreparable injury**, the plaintiff may apply for:
- **Ad-interim / Temporary Injunction** (Order 39 Rules 1 & 2 of CPC).
- **Anton Piller Order** for preserving evidence.
- **Mareva Injunction** for freezing assets.

### Step 5 — Final Adjudication
The court, on hearing both sides, decides:
- Whether infringement is proved.
- Whether damages or accounts of profits are appropriate.
- Whether a permanent injunction is warranted.

### Step 6 — Execution of Decree
The winning party enforces the court decree through execution proceedings. Non-compliance is treated as **contempt of court** and **civil prison** under the CPC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Point:** The court exercises **discretionary power** in granting injunctions. The famous three-pronged test from **Dorab Cawasji Warden v. Coomi Sorab Warden (1990)** requires the plaintiff to establish:
> 1. **A prima facie case.**
> 2. **Balance of convenience** in their favor.
> 3. **Irreparable injury** if injunction is refused.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Breakdown: Invocation of Criminal Remedies

### Step 1 — Filing of FIR / Complaint
The IP owner files a complaint with the police (FIR) or directly with the magistrate (private complaint) under **Section 200 of CrPC / Section 223 BNSS**.

### Step 2 — Cognizability Analysis
Not all IP offences are cognizable. **Trade Mark counterfeiting (TM Act §104)** is cognizable, non-bailable, and non-compoundable. **Patent infringement is decriminalized** — there is no criminal remedy for patents in India.

### Step 3 — Investigation and Arrest
The police investigate, and if a prima facie case exists, arrest the accused. **Section 155 CrPC** requires police to take cognizance in cognizable offences.

### Step 4 — Search, Seizure, and Forfeiture
Police, with a warrant, may enter premises and seize infringing goods, packaging materials, and production equipment. These goods are produced before the magistrate as evidence.

### Step 5 — Trial and Conviction
The magistrate conducts trial. On conviction, the accused is sentenced to **imprisonment + fine**. Repeat offenders face **enhanced punishment** (e.g., TM Act §104(2): 1–3 years imprisonment for repeat offenders).

### Step 6 — Compensation to the Victim
Courts may also direct the accused to pay **compensation to the IP owner** under **Section 357 CrPC**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Civil vs. Criminal vs. Administrative Remedies

| Parameter | Civil | Criminal | Administrative |
|-----------|-------|----------|----------------|
| **Initiated By** | IP owner | State (on complaint) | Government agency |
| **Objective** | Compensation + Restraint | Punishment + Deterrence | Prevention + Public Interest |
| **Burden of Proof** | Preponderance of probabilities | Beyond reasonable doubt | Administrative standards |
| **Forum** | Civil Court / Commercial Court | Magistrate / Sessions Court | Customs / Regulatory Body |
| **Standard of Evidence** | Balance of probabilities | Strictest standard | Varies |
| **Time** | Slow (months–years) | Faster (with police) | Immediate (border detention) |
| **Outcome** | Injunction, damages | Imprisonment, fine | Seizure, destruction |
| **Cost to Owner** | High (litigation) | Moderate (state bears) | Low (recordal fees) |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Breakdown: Anton Piller Order (Critical KTU Topic)

The **Anton Piller Order** is one of the most powerful civil remedies. Its invocation is a **three-stage process**:

$$
\text{Anton Piller Order} = \underbrace{\text{Ex parte application}}_{\text{Stage 1}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Strong prima facie case + real risk of evidence destruction}}_{\text{Stage 2}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Court-appointed supervising solicitor}}_{\text{Stage 3}}
$$

### Conditions for Grant
1. **Strong prima facie case** of infringement.
2. **Real and imminent danger** that the defendant will destroy or dispose of incriminating evidence.
3. **Potential damage to the plaintiff** must be serious.
4. The inspection must be carried out in a way that is **not oppressive or unreasonable**.

### Indian Adaptation
The Indian equivalent is governed by **Order 39 Rule 7A of CPC** (read with the principles in **Microsoft Corporation v. Yogesh Papat**, 2005). The order is executed under the supervision of a court-appointed commissioner.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Anton Piller** with **Mareva Injunction**. Remember:
> - **Anton Piller** → Preserves **evidence** (enters premises).
> - **Mareva** → Freezes **assets** (prevents dissipation of money).

---

## 3.5 Key Judicial Pronouncements (KTU Favourites)

| Case | Principle Established |
|------|----------------------|
| **Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak (2008)** | Copyright in software layouts and compilations. |
| **Microsoft Corporation v. Yogesh Papat (2005)** | Anton Piller orders in software piracy cases. |
| **Warner Bros. v. Mr. R.K. Cable Network (Delhi HC)** | Permanent injunction against cable piracy. |
| **John Wyeth & Brother v. Cigamic (1984)** | Damages in patent infringement — assessed on loss basis. |
| **Laxmikant v. Pratapchand (1993)** | Three-pronged test for grant of temporary injunction. |
| **Niranjan Shankar Golikari v. Century Spinning (1967)** | Patent rights and contractual restrictions. |
| **Yahoo Inc. v. Akash Arora (1999)** | Passing off in domain names — first Indian domain case. |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary of Damages Assessment

The court may award damages based on **three methodologies**:

$$
\text{Damages} = \max(\text{Loss suffered by Plaintiff}, \text{Profit earned by Defendant}, \text{Statutory Damages})
$$

### Three Methods
1. **Compensatory Damages** → Actual loss suffered by the IP owner.
2. **Restitutio in Integrum** → Restoration to original position (full compensation).
3. **Exemplary / Punitive Damages** → Awarded in cases of **willful, deliberate, or fraudulent** infringement to punish the infringer (rare in India, but available under common law).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "explain damages," present all three methods in a **numbered list** with a one-line explanation. Marks are awarded for **structure and coverage**, not for originality.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are engineered with strict compliance to the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10** safeguards — all node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords are used, and all labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text.

---

## 4.1 Master Architecture of IP Remedies

```mermaid

    A[IP INFRINGEMENT DETECTED] --> B{Choose Appropriate Remedy Track}

    B --> D[CRIMINAL REMEDY TRACK]

    C --> C1[File Civil Suit in District or Commercial Court]
    C1 --> C2[Apply for Temporary Injunction]
    C1 --> C3[Apply for Anton Piller Order]
    C1 --> C4[Apply for Mareva Injunction]
    C2 --> C5[Court Hearing and Adjudication]
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> C6[Final Decree: Permanent Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits, Delivery Up]

    D --> D1[File FIR or Private Complaint]
    D1 --> D2{Cognizable Offence?}

    D4 --> D5

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern: 3-mark short answers (Part A) and 14-mark long answers with internal choice (Part B, with sub-parts of 7 marks each).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain any three civil remedies available to an IP right-holder under Indian law.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three civil remedies are:
1. **Injunction** — A court order restraining the infringer from continuing the illegal act. It may be temporary (during trial) or permanent (after decree). *(1 mark)*
2. **Damages** — Monetary compensation awarded to the IP owner to make good the loss suffered due to infringement. *(1 mark)*
3. **Delivery Up of Infringing Goods** — Court order directing the infringer to surrender or destroy infringing articles, plates, and packaging. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between Anton Piller Order and Mareva Injunction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Basis | Anton Piller Order | Mareva Injunction |
|-------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Purpose** | Preserve evidence | Freeze assets |
| **Action** | Enter defendant's premises | Restrain defendant from disposing of money/property |
| **Granted When** | Risk of evidence destruction | Risk of asset dissipation |
| **Indian Source** | Order 39 Rule 7A CPC | Order 39 CPC + Common Law |

*(1 mark for the tabular format with purpose, 1 mark for action, 1 mark for Indian statutory basis.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

### **Question 3** — Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the **civil remedies** available to an intellectual property right-holder under Indian statutes. Cite relevant statutory provisions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **criminal remedies** provided under the Copyright Act, 1957 and the Trade Marks Act, 1999. Highlight the nature of offences and quantum of punishment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question 3(a) — Civil Remedies (7 marks)

Civil remedies are private law remedies pursued by the IP owner against the infringer. The major civil remedies are:

**1. Injunction (Sections 55 Copyright, 108 Patents, 103 TM Act)** *(2 marks)*
An injunction is a court order restraining the defendant from continuing the infringing act. It is of three types:
- **Temporary / Ad-interim Injunction** — Granted during the pendency of the suit to preserve the status quo.
- **Permanent / Perpetual Injunction** — Granted at the final hearing, restraining the defendant for all future time.
- **Mandatory Injunction** — Orders the defendant to undo a wrongful act (e.g., dismantle an infringing structure).

The three-pronged test for grant of injunction (Dorab Warden case) requires the plaintiff to establish:
- A **prima facie case**.
- **Balance of convenience** in their favor.
- Risk of **irreparable injury**.

**2. Damages (Section 55(1)(a) Copyright; Section 108(1) Patents; Section 103(1) TM Act)** *(2 marks)*
Damages are monetary compensation to make good the loss suffered. The court may award:
- **Compensatory Damages** — Actual loss.
- **Statutory Damages** — Pre-determined amounts in copyright law.
- **Exemplary Damages** — For willful infringement (rare in India).

**3. Account of Profits (Section 55(1)(b) Copyright; Section 103(1) TM Act)** *(1 mark)*
The infringer must surrender the profits earned through the illegal use. This is an alternative to damages.

**4. Delivery Up of Infringing Goods (Section 58 Copyright; Section 103(2) TM Act)** *(1 mark)*
The court orders the infringer to deliver up all infringing articles, plates, and materials for destruction.

**5. Anton Piller Order and Mareva Injunction (Order 39 CPC)** *(1 mark)*
- **Anton Piller** — Ex parte order for preserving evidence.
- **Mareva** — Freezing of assets to prevent dissipation.

---

#### Model Solution to Question 3(b) — Criminal Remedies under Copyright Act and TM Act (7 marks)

**1. Copyright Act, 1957 — Sections 63 to 66** *(3.5 marks)*

- **Section 63 — Infringement as a criminal offence:** Knowingly infringing copyright is punishable with **imprisonment up to 3 years** (extendable to **7 years** for second or subsequent conviction) and **fine up to ₹2 lakh** (extendable to **₹10 lakh**).
- **Section 63-A — Enhanced punishment for second conviction:** Repeat offenders face enhanced imprisonment and fine.
- **Section 64 — Power of police to seize:** Police may seize infringing copies, plates, and equipment without a warrant if the offence is cognizable.
- **Section 65 — Publication of infringing work for trade:** Selling or hiring infringing copies attracts additional punishment.
- **Section 66 — Infringing copies to be forfeited:** The court orders destruction of infringing copies after conviction.

**2. Trade Marks Act, 1999 — Sections 103 to 105** *(3.5 marks)*

- **Section 103 — Civil remedies for infringement of registered trademarks.**
- **Section 104 — Counterfeiting as a criminal offence:** Selling goods with false trademark is punishable with **imprisonment of 6 months to 3 years** and **fine up to ₹2 lakh**. For repeat offenders, imprisonment is **1 to 3 years** with a higher fine.
- **Section 104-A — Enhanced punishment for repeat counterfeiting.**
- **Section 105 — Cognizance, search, seizure, and forfeiture:** The offence is **cognizable, non-bailable, and non-compoundable**. Police may arrest without warrant, search premises, and seize goods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** The **Copyright Act** criminalises infringement for any work, while the **Trade Marks Act** specifically criminalises **counterfeiting** of registered marks. **Patent infringement is purely civil** in India and has no criminal remedy.

---

### **Question 4** — Choice B `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**(a)** Discuss the **administrative and border remedies** for IP enforcement in India. Explain the role of customs authorities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Anton Piller Order** in detail. State the conditions for its grant and its application in software piracy cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question 4(a) — Administrative and Border Remedies (7 marks)

**1. Customs Recordal and Border Detention** *(3 marks)*
Under the **Intellectual Property Rights (Imported Goods) Rules, 2007** read with the **Customs Act, 1962**, the IP owner can record their IP rights with the **Customs authorities**. This enables the customs officers at Indian ports (Mumbai, Delhi, Chennai, Kolkata) to:
- **Detain suspected infringing consignments** at the port of entry.
- **Notify the right-holder** for examination and verification.
- **Seize, forfeit, and destroy** the goods if infringement is confirmed.

**2. Administrative Seizure by Regulatory Bodies** *(2 marks)*
Government agencies like the **Drug Controller**, **FSSAI**, and **Police Anti-Counterfeiting Units** conduct market surveillance and seize counterfeit goods. This is a non-judicial, fast-track remedy.

**3. Domain Name Dispute Resolution** *(1 mark)*
For trademark-based domain name disputes, the right-holder can approach:
- **WIPO Arbitration and Mediation Center** under the **UDRP Policy**.
- **.IN Registry** under the **.IN Dispute Resolution Policy (INDRP)**.
- **National Internet Exchange of India (NIXI)**.

**4. Market Withdrawal and Recall Orders** *(1 mark)*
Regulatory authorities can issue orders to withdraw infringing products from the market, e.g., spurious drugs withdrawn by the **Drugs Controller General of India (DCGI)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** Tech startups and exporters must file a **Customs Recordal Application** for trademarks, patents, and copyrights. The application form is available on the **CBIC (Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs)** website.

---

#### Model Solution to Question 4(b) — Anton Piller Order (7 marks)

**1. Definition and Origin** *(2 marks)*
The **Anton Piller Order** is an ex parte court order, named after the English case **Anton Piller KG v. Manufacturing Processes Ltd. (1976)**. It allows the plaintiff to enter the defendant's premises to **search for and preserve evidence** of infringement, often in the form of computers, hard drives, and documents. The Indian equivalent is governed by **Order 39 Rule 7A of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908**, and the principles were adapted in **Microsoft Corporation v. Yogesh Papat (Delhi HC, 2005)**.

**2. Conditions for Grant** *(3 marks)*
The court grants the order only when the plaintiff establishes:
- **A strong prima facie case** of infringement.
- **A real and imminent risk** that the defendant will destroy, hide, or dispose of incriminating evidence.
- **Serious potential damage** to the plaintiff if the order is not granted.
- The order must be executed in a manner that is **not oppressive or unreasonable**.

**3. Procedure** *(1 mark)*
The order is executed under the supervision of a **court-appointed independent solicitor**. The defendant is served with a copy of the order. Evidence is inventoried and preserved. The defendant is given an opportunity to be heard at a later stage.

**4. Application in Software Piracy** *(1 mark)*
In software piracy cases, the right-holder (e.g., **Microsoft, Adobe, Oracle**) uses the Anton Piller order to:
- Enter the suspect's premises.
- Image all hard drives and servers.
- Preserve logs, license records, and pirated software.
- The order is crucial because software pirates often destroy evidence within hours of being tipped off.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse Anton Piller with search warrants under CrPC. The Anton Piller order is a **civil, not criminal, remedy**.
> 2. **Do NOT** write that the Anton Piller order **arrests** the defendant — it only **preserves evidence**.
> 3. **Do NOT** state that the order is granted in **every** case — it is a discretionary remedy granted only on a high threshold.
> 4. **Always mention** the Indian case **Microsoft v. Yogesh Papat** to anchor your answer in Indian context.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final Memory Anchor):**

- **Three Pillars of IP Remedies:** Civil, Criminal, Administrative.
- **Civil Remedies:** Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits, Delivery Up, Anton Piller, Mareva.
- **Criminal Remedies:** Imprisonment + Fine (Copyright §63, TM Act §104, Designs §22, GI §69).
- **Patent Infringement is NOT criminal in India** — civil remedy only.
- **TM Act §104 — Counterfeiting is cognizable, non-bailable, non-compoundable** — maximum **3 years** imprisonment, **₹2 lakh** fine.
- **Copyright Act §63 — Maximum 3 years imprisonment, ₹2 lakh fine** (extendable to **7 years** for repeat offenders).
- **Anton Piller = Evidence Preservation; Mareva = Asset Freezing.** Do not confuse.
- **Three-pronged test for injunction:** Prima facie case, Balance of convenience, Irreparable injury.
- **Damages methods:** Compensatory, Statutory, Exemplary.
- **Border remedies:** Customs recordal under IPR (Imported Goods) Rules, 2007.
- **Domain name disputes:** UDRP (international), INDRP (India-specific).
- **Key Indian Case Law:** *Microsoft v. Yogesh Papat* (Anton Piller), *Dorab Warden* (Injunction test), *Yahoo v. Akash Arora* (Domain names).
- **Forum for IP suits:** Commercial Courts (post-2015), District Courts, High Courts.
- **Anton Piller Indian Source:** Order 39 Rule 7A CPC.
- **KPI for valuation answers:** Always cite the **section number + statute name** in every answer. The examiner awards **partial marks** even if the explanation is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
