topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Moral Right

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moral Rights** are the **non-economic, personal rights** of an author or creator that protect their **personal and reputational interests** in a copyrighted work, independent of the economic (copyright) rights transferred to third parties. They recognize the **intellectual and emotional bond** between the creator and the creation.

Under the **Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works (1886, revised 1971)**, specifically **Article 6bis**, moral rights are recognized internationally as:

> *"To claim authorship of the work (**Right of Paternity**) and to object to any distortion, mutilation or other modification of, or other derogatory action in relation to, the work, which would be prejudicial to the author's honor or reputation (**Right of Integrity**)."*

In **India**, the **Copyright Act, 1957 (as amended in 2012)** recognizes moral rights under **Sections 57 and 57A**, granting them:

| Statutory Provision | Right Granted |
|---|---|
| **Section 57(1)(a)** | Right to claim authorship of the work (Right of Paternity) |
| **Section 57(1)(b)** | Right to integrity — restrain or claim damages for distortion/mutilation prejudicial to honor or reputation |
| **Section 57A** | Special right of the author in case of commissioned works for **Cinematograph Films / Sound Recordings** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction**: Unlike **economic rights** (which are transferable, assignable, and licensable), **moral rights are perpetual, inalienable (non-transferable), and in India, irrevocable.** They continue to exist even after assignment of copyright.

---

## 🌟 Conceptual Analogy — The "Painter & Painting" Intuition

Imagine **Ravi**, a passionate painter who creates a masterpiece titled *"The Silent River"*. He sells the painting to a wealthy collector, **Mr. Sharma**, for ₹5,00,000.

Now consider two scenarios:

### Scenario A — Economic Rights (Transferable)
- Mr. Sharma can **display, sell, reproduce (prints), or license** the painting to galleries.
- Ravi has given up control over the **commercial exploitation** of the painting.

### Scenario B — Moral Rights (Non-Transferable)
- Even after sale, Ravi retains the right to **sign the painting** (Right of Paternity).
- If Mr. Sharma paints clown faces on trees in the river or uses it to advertise a liquor brand (prejudicial to Ravi's reputation), Ravi can **legally object** (Right of Integrity).
- Ravi can NEVER sell or assign these moral rights to Mr. Sharma — they stay with Ravi **forever**, even after his death (passing to heirs).

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Intuition**: *Economic rights govern your **wallet**; moral rights protect your **dignity**.* A painter may sell his canvas, but he can never sell his identity as the creator.

---

## 🧭 Categories / Components of Moral Rights (Universal Framework)

Moral rights broadly encompass the following entitlements:

1. **Right of Paternity (Attribution)** — to be identified as the author.
2. **Right of Integrity (Non-Derogation)** — to prevent distortion, mutilation, or modification that harms reputation.
3. **Right of Disclosure / Divulgation** — to decide when and how the work is first made public.
4. **Right of Withdrawal / Retraction** — to withdraw the work post-publication under certain conditions.
5. **Right to Object to False Attribution** — to prevent others from being wrongly credited.
6. **Right of Access** (limited jurisdictions) — to physically access the work for preservation or restoration.
7. **Droit de Suite / Resale Royalty** — to share proceeds from subsequent high-value sales (more prominent in continental Europe).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 🔬 Anatomy of Moral Rights — Structured Logical Breakdown

### **A. The Philosophical Foundation**

The doctrine of moral rights is rooted in the **Continental European "droit moral"** tradition, particularly **French and German copyright philosophy**, which views an author's work as an **extension of their personality** (*personality theory* / *Hegelian theory of intellectual property*). This contrasts with the **Anglo-American "copyright" tradition**, which historically treated copyright as a commercial incentive for public benefit (*utilitarian theory*).

> **KTU High-Yield Insight**: India's **Copyright Act, 1957** is a **hybrid statute** — economically aligned with the **Common Law tradition** (transferable economic rights) but morally aligned with the **Civil Law tradition** (inalienable moral rights under Sections 57–57A).

### **B. Components of Moral Rights — Detailed Engineering**

#### **1. Right of Paternity (Right of Attribution)**
- The author's right to **be identified as the creator** of the work.
- Manifests in two forms:
  - **Positive Form**: Right to claim authorship (e.g., demand your name appears on the painting, book, or film credits).
  - **Negative Form**: Right to **publish anonymously or pseudonymously**, or to **object to false attribution** (someone else taking credit for your work).
- **Statutory Basis (India)**: Section 57(1)(a) of the Copyright Act, 1957.

#### **2. Right of Integrity (Right to Non-Derogation)**
- The author's right to **prevent distortion, mutilation, modification, or any other derogatory action** in relation to the work that would be **prejudicial to the author's honor or reputation**.
- **Scope of "Derogatory Action"**:
  - Physical distortion (cutting, cropping, color alterations of artwork).
  - Contextual distortion (using a devotional song in a satirical ad).
  - Substantial alteration of a literary/dramatic work that misrepresents the author's ideology.
- **Statutory Basis (India)**: Section 57(1)(b) of the Copyright Act, 1957.

#### **3. Right of Disclosure (Divulgation)**
- The author's right to **decide when, where, and how** the work is first communicated to the public.
- Closely tied to the right of an author to withhold unpublished manuscripts or refuse publication.

#### **4. Right of Withdrawal / Retraction**
- The author's right to **withdraw a published work from circulation**, subject to compensating assignees/licensees for any resulting losses.
- Recognized strongly under **German copyright law (§ 42 UrhG)** but only residually in India.

#### **5. Right to Object to False Attribution**
- Distinct from paternity rights — protects against someone *else* being falsely credited.

#### **6. Droit de Suite (Resale Royalty Right)**
- A right to share in the **proceeds of subsequent high-value resale** of the original physical work (e.g., paintings sold at auction).
- Statutorily enforced in the **EU (Directive 2001/84/EC)** but **NOT recognized under Indian Copyright Act, 1957**.

---

### **C. Duration, Transferability & Waiver — Comparative Matrix**

| Feature | Moral Rights (India: §57) | Economic Rights (India: §14) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Personal, inalienable | Property, assignable/licensable |
| **Transferable by Assignment?** | ❌ No (absolute bar) | ✅ Yes |
| **Can be Waived?** | ❌ No (in India, irrevocable) | ✅ Yes (subject to conditions) |
| **Duration** | Perpetual — co-extensive with copyright | Limited term (Life + 60 years) |
| **Inheritance** | Passes to legal heirs | Passes to legal heirs |
| **Enforcement Pre-Assignment** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Independent of Copyright Ownership?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

---

### **D. Statutory Framework — International & National**

| Instrument / Statute | Moral Rights Status |
|---|---|
| **Berne Convention, Article 6bis** | Mandatory recognition of paternity + integrity rights |
| **TRIPS Agreement (1994)** | Does NOT explicitly mandate moral rights (silent) |
| **WIPO Copyright Treaty (1996)** | Affirms Berne obligations |
| **Copyright Act, 1957 (India), §57** | Paternity + Integrity rights |
| **Copyright Act, 1957 (India), §57A** | Special rights for authors of cinematograph films & sound recordings |
| **Visual Artists Rights Act, 1990 (USA)** | Federal moral rights limited to visual art |
| **Code de la propriété intellectuelle (France)** | Strongest moral rights — droit moral perpetual, imprescriptible |

---

### **E. Real-World Utility in Engineering, Media & Creative Industries**

- **Software & AI Engineering**: Engineers writing open-source code retain attribution rights even after forking; integrity rights prevent malicious modification that misrepresents the original coder's intent.
- **Film & Media**: Director's right to integrity prevents producers from substantially altering the final cut in a way that distorts the artistic vision (*e.g., post-production color-grading that changes the mood of a documentary*).
- **Architecture**: Architects can object to post-construction modifications (e.g., painting a heritage façade in neon colors) that harm their professional reputation.
- **Academia & Research**: Researchers can object to unauthorized co-authorship or distortion of published findings.

# SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix, Step-by-Step Logic Flow & Case Law Reasoning

Since **Moral Rights** is a **legal-conceptual topic**, the analytical framework below substitutes algebraic derivations with **logical reasoning flow, statutory cross-mapping, and case-law application matrices** — equivalent in rigor to engineering problem-solving.

---

## **Analytical Step-by-Step: Determining Infringement of Moral Right of Integrity**

### **Step 1: Establish Author Identity**

Determine the **original author** of the work under Section 2(d) of the Copyright Act, 1957.

$$
\text{Author} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Literary/Dramatic/Musical/Artistic: the creator} \\
\text{Cinematograph Film: the producer (§2(d)(v))} \\
\text{Sound Recording: the producer (§2(d)(vi))} \\
\text{Photograph: the photographer (§2(d)(iii))}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Explanation**: This bifurcated definition is critical. In films, the **producer is the statutory author** for copyright purposes, but moral rights under §57A vest in the **principal director, screenplay writer, dialogue writer, and music composer** — preserving personal dignity for creative contributors.

---

### **Step 2: Identify the Action Complained Of**

The plaintiff-author must demonstrate one of:

$$
\text{Action} \in \{\text{Distortion}, \text{Mutilation}, \text{Modification}, \text{Derogatory Treatment}\}
$$

> **Explanation**: The statutory language is **non-exhaustive** — the phrase *"or other derogatory action"* is interpreted broadly by Indian courts to include contextual misuse, distortion of intent, or reputational harm.

---

### **Step 3: Apply the "Prejudicial to Honor or Reputation" Test**

Two-pronged judicial assessment:

$$
\text{Liability} = (\text{Derogatory Action}) \land (\text{Prejudicial Effect on Honor or Reputation})
$$

| Prong | Question | Evidence Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Subjective** | Does the modification harm the *honor* (moral standing) of the author? | Author's testimony, expert opinion |
| **Objective** | Would a reasonable person consider it *prejudicial to reputation*? | Industry standards, public perception |

> **Explanation**: Indian courts apply a **hybrid subjective-objective test**, balancing the author's personal sensitivities with reasonable industry expectations.

---

### **Step 4: Defenses Available to the Infringer**

$$
\text{Defenses} = \{\text{Consent}, \text{Ordinary Use}, \text{Necessary Modification}, \text{Good Faith}\}
$$

| Defense | Legal Basis | Burden |
|---|---|---|
| **Express/Implied Consent** | Contractual acquiescence | Defendant |
| **Necessary for Technical Exploitation** | Industry practice (e.g., format conversion) | Defendant |
| **Editorial/Trivial Alteration** | De minimis non curat lex | Plaintiff rebuts |
| **Public Interest Exception** | Limited (e.g., parody, criticism under §52) | Defendant |

---

## **Case Law Analytical Matrix**

| Case Name | Year | Court | Key Holding | Application to Moral Rights |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sajeev Pillai v. Venu Kunnapalli** | 2016 | Kerala HC | Unauthorized editing of cinematograph film violates §57A integrity rights of the director | Directors have **independent** moral rights against producer-led distortion |
| **Mannu Bhandari v. Kala Vikas Pictures** | 1987 | Delhi HC | Adaptation into a film derogatory to author's original work = moral right infringement | First major Indian case on integrity rights |
| **Anand Patwardhan v. Government of India** | 2014 | Bombay HC | Censorship cuts violating artistic integrity | Documentary filmmakers' integrity rights upheld against arbitrary cuts |
| **Smt. S. P. B. Agrawal v. State** (CIT v. Manoj Dwivedi) | 2012 | Delhi HC | Cinematograph cuts must not distort creative intent | Paternity + Integrity both protected |
| **Pritam Singh v. Lingam** (UK precedent cited in India) | 2014 | UK HC | "Honour and reputation" must be substantiated, not fanciful | Sets the **objective threshold** for integrity claims |

---

## **Theoretical Derivation of Indian Moral Rights Framework**

### **Logic Flow from Constitutional Mandate → Statutory Right → Enforcement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Article 19(1)(a)} \xrightarrow{\text{Freedom of Speech \& Expression}} \textbf{Article 21} \xrightarrow{\text{Right to Dignity}} \\
&\textbf{Copyright Act, 1957 §57} \xrightarrow{\text{Codification of Personality Rights}} \textbf{Sections 55, 62} \xrightarrow{\text{Civil/Criminal Enforcement}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation of Logic Chain**:
> 1. The **Indian Constitution** guarantees every citizen the right to express their personality (Art. 19(1)(a)) and the right to live with dignity (Art. 21).
> 2. A creator's work is an **emanation of personality** — distorting it is an affront to dignity.
> 3. The Copyright Act codifies this philosophy into enforceable statutory rights under §57 and §57A.
> 4. Enforcement mechanisms (civil suit for injunction + damages, criminal prosecution) are derived from the general Copyright enforcement provisions (§55 for civil remedies, §62 for jurisdiction).

---

## **Comparative Jurisdictional Derivation — Moral Rights Strength Index (MRSI)**

$$
\text{MRSI} = w_1 \cdot \text{Recognized Components} + w_2 \cdot \text{Duration Score} + w_3 \cdot \text{Alienability Penalty}
$$

Where:

- $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ (normalized weights: $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$)
- **Recognized Components** $\in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ based on number of moral rights statutorily recognized
- **Duration Score** = $1.0$ if perpetual, $0.5$ if co-extensive with copyright, $0.2$ if limited term
- **Alienability Penalty** = $0.0$ if inalienable, $0.5$ if partially waivable, $1.0$ if fully waivable

### **Sample Computation for India**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Recognized Components} &= 2 \text{ (Paternity + Integrity)} \Rightarrow 2/5 \cdot 5 = 2.0 \\
\text{Duration Score} &= 1.0 \text{ (perpetual under §57)} \\
\text{Alienability Penalty} &= 0.0 \text{ (inalienable)} \\
\text{MRSI}_{\text{India}} &= (0.5 \times 2.0) + (0.3 \times 1.0) + (0.2 \times 0.0) \\
&= 1.0 + 0.3 + 0.0 = \mathbf{1.3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Cross-Jurisdiction MRSI Comparison**

| Jurisdiction | Components | Duration | Alienability Penalty | **MRSI** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **France** | 5 | 1.0 | 0.0 | **2.5** (Strongest) |
| **Germany** | 4 | 1.0 | 0.0 | **2.3** |
| **India** | 2 | 1.0 | 0.0 | **1.3** |
| **United Kingdom** | 3 | 1.0 | 0.5 (waivable) | **1.05** |
| **United States** | 1 (limited to VARA) | 0.2 | 0.5 | **0.25** (Weakest) |

> **Explanation**: France and Germany lead in moral rights protection due to their civil-law heritage, while the US trails significantly due to its utilitarian, market-driven copyright philosophy. India occupies a **middle position** — strong in inalienability and duration, but narrow in component coverage.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 **Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Moral Rights" of an author. Mention any two moral rights recognized under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957.*

### ✅ **Model Answer:**

**Definition**: Moral rights are the **non-economic, personal rights** of an author that protect the **personal and reputational relationship** between the creator and their work. Unlike economic rights, moral rights are **inalienable, perpetual, and non-transferable** under Section 57 of the Copyright Act, 1957.

**Two Moral Rights Recognized in India:**

1. **Right of Paternity [Section 57(1)(a)]**: The right to **claim authorship** of the work and to demand that one's name be associated with the creation, or alternatively, to publish anonymously/pseudonymously.

2. **Right of Integrity [Section 57(1)(b)]**: The right to **object to any distortion, mutilation, modification, or other derogatory action** in relation to the work that would be **prejudicial to the author's honor or reputation**. The author can seek injunction and damages.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Recall**: **"P-I-D"** → **P**aternity, **I**ntegrity, **D**uration = Perpetual.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Differentiate between Economic Rights and Moral Rights of an author.*

### ✅ **Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Economic Rights (§14) | Moral Rights (§57 & §57A) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Property rights | Personal rights |
| **Purpose** | Commercial exploitation | Protection of honor & reputation |
| **Transferability** | Transferable by assignment, license, testament | **Inalienable** — non-transferable |
| **Waiver** | Can be waived | **Irrevocable** — cannot be waived in India |
| **Duration** | Life + 60 years (India) | **Perpetual** (co-extensive with copyright) |
| **Inheritance** | Yes (via assignment/Will) | Yes (vests in legal heirs) |
| **Examples** | Right to reproduce, distribute, adapt | Right to paternity, integrity |

---

## 📝 **Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Predicted Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **concept, components, and statutory recognition** of Moral Rights under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **constitutional basis** of moral rights in India with reference to **Article 19(1)(a) and Article 21**, and critically analyze **Mannu Bhandari v. Kala Vikas Pictures (1987)**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ **Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks**

**Concept of Moral Rights (2 Marks):**
Moral rights are non-economic personal rights vested in the author of a work, recognizing the **creative personality** of the author. They are founded on the **personality theory of intellectual property** (originating from European civil-law traditions), which views a creator's work as an extension of their identity.

**Components Recognized in India (3 Marks):**
Under **Section 57(1)** of the Copyright Act, 1957, two moral rights are statutorily codified:

1. **Right of Paternity** — Right to claim authorship and to prevent false attribution. Section 57(1)(a).
2. **Right of Integrity** — Right to object to derogatory treatment. Section 57(1)(b).
3. **Special Rights for Film/Sound Recording Authors** — Under **Section 57A**, authors of cinematograph films and sound recordings retain integrity rights that cannot be overridden by the producer's economic assignment.

**Statutory Recognition (2 Marks):**
- **Section 57**: Applies to all categories of works — literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematograph, sound recording.
- **Section 57A**: Specific protection for creative contributors (directors, screenplay writers, dialogue writers, music composers) in commissioned films/sound recordings.
- **Berne Convention Article 6bis** + **Universal Declaration of Human Rights (Article 27)** form the international foundation.

---

#### ✅ **Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks**

**Constitutional Basis (3 Marks):**

| Article | Text Fragment | Connection to Moral Rights |
|---|---|---|
| **Article 19(1)(a)** | Freedom of speech and expression | An author's creative expression is protected; distortion violates expressive autonomy |
| **Article 21** | Right to life and personal liberty | Right to live with dignity — distortion of one's creation is an affront to personal dignity |

**The constitutional framework treats creative works as an **"emanation of personality"** — any derogatory alteration strikes at the core of Articles 19(1)(a) and 21, justifying statutory protection under §57.**

**Case Analysis: Mannu Bhandari v. Kala Vikas Pictures (Delhi HC, 1987) (4 Marks):**

| Issue | Holding |
|---|---|
| **Parties** | Mannu Bhandari (author of novel *"Aap ka Bunty"*) v. Kala Vikas Pictures (film producer) |
| **Cause of Action** | Producer adapted novel into film with significant modifications; author alleged distortion of her creative vision |
| **Legal Issue** | Whether film adaptation violated the author's moral rights under §57 |
| **Court's Ruling** | The court upheld that **adaptation must preserve the essential spirit and integrity** of the original work; distortion that prejudices honor/reputation violates §57(1)(b) |
| **Significance** | First Indian case to establish that **moral rights are independent of economic assignment** — even after copyright transfer, the author's integrity rights persist |

**Critical Analysis**: The judgment strengthened the personalist framework in Indian IP jurisprudence, though its narrow holding applies only to clear cases of reputational harm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted Pattern]**

**(a)** Examine the **nature and scope of Right of Integrity** under Section 57(1)(b) of the Copyright Act, 1957. What constitutes **"derogatory action"** in light of Indian judicial precedent? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **moral rights regime in India (Civil Law influence) vs. the United States (Common Law/utilitarian influence)**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ **Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks**

**Nature and Scope of Right of Integrity (3 Marks):**
The Right of Integrity is a **negative right** — it empowers the author to **prevent certain actions**, rather than to perform positive acts. It protects against three classes of conduct:

$$
\text{Derogatory Conduct} = \{\text{Distortion}, \text{Mutilation}, \text{Other Modification or Action}\}
$$

The right is **perpetual, inalienable, and transmissible to legal heirs**. It applies to **all categories** of works under §13 of the Copyright Act, 1957.

**What Constitutes "Derogatory Action"? (4 Marks):**

The statutory language is **non-exhaustive** ("or other derogatory action"). Indian courts have interpreted it to include:

| Type | Example | Case Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical distortion** | Cropping a painting to alter composition | *Mannu Bhandari* (1987) |
| **Contextual distortion** | Using devotional song in liquor ad | Industry practice recognition |
| **Substantial alteration of meaning** | Changing the dialogue/wording of a literary work | *Sajeev Pillai v. Venu Kunnapalli* (2016) |
| **Censorship-induced mutilation** | Arbitrary CBFC cuts destroying artistic intent | *Anand Patwardhan v. UoI* (2014) |

**Judicial Test Applied**:
- The modification must be **substantial** (not trivial or technical).
- It must be **prejudicial to honor OR reputation** (the OR is disjunctive).
- Both **subjective (author's feelings)** and **objective (reasonable person standard)** assessments are weighed.

---

#### ✅ **Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks**

**Comparative Analysis:**

| Dimension | **India** | **United States** |
|---|---|---|
| **Philosophical Foundation** | Hybrid — Civil Law personality doctrine + Common Law | Utilitarian / market-incentive theory |
| **Statutory Recognition** | Sections 57 & 57A (broad coverage) | Visual Artists Rights Act, 1990 (VARA) — narrow scope |
| **Works Covered** | All categories under §13 | Only **visual art** (paintings, sculptures, photographs up to 200+ copies) |
| **Rights Recognized** | Paternity + Integrity (extensive) | Attribution + Integrity (limited to visual art) |
| **Duration** | Perpetual (co-extensive with copyright) | Lifetime of the author (VARA); for visual art only |
| **Transferability** | **Inalienable** (cannot be waived) | **Waivable** (explicit written waiver allowed) |
| **Sculpture in Public Place Exception** | Not applicable | Distortion due to placement in public space allowed if not intentional |
| **Droit de Suite / Resale Royalty** | Not recognized | Not recognized (state-level efforts via California Resale Royalty Act ruled unconstitutional in *Sawdust* case) |
| **International Compliance** | Fully Berne-Convention compliant | USA joined Berne Convention only in 1989; VARA was a **retroactive fix** |

**Critical Insights:**

1. **India's "Strong on Paper" Status**: Indian law is **legally robust** (inalienable, perpetual), but **judicially narrow** due to limited case law and the high threshold of "prejudicial to honor/reputation."

2. **USA's "Philosophical Resistance"**: The US historically viewed moral rights as **antithetical to free alienation of property**. VARA was enacted only after **international pressure** to comply with Berne.

3. **Practical Implication**: A software engineer in India who develops code retains stronger attribution rights than a painter in New York, despite the US being the global IP superpower.

**Conclusion**: India's moral rights framework is **theoretically superior in statutory design**, though **enforcement infrastructure remains under-tested**. The US represents a **utilitarian compromise** — strong property rights, weak personal rights.

---

## ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Moral Rights Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Moral Rights with Economic Rights** — Examiners often dock 1–2 marks if students write "moral rights are assignable" or "moral rights have a duration of life + 60 years." **Moral rights are INALIENABLE and PERPETUAL.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting Section 57A** — When asked about *cinematograph films*, many students cite only Section 57. Examiners specifically test **Section 57A** because it was added by the **2012 Amendment** and is a favorite board-exam topic.
>
> 3. **Misidentifying the Statutory Author** — In films, the **producer is the copyright owner**, but **directors/writers hold moral rights**. Students who state "the producer owns all rights" lose marks.
>
> 4. **Failing to Cite Berne Convention Article 6bis** — Any question on moral rights should reference this article to demonstrate **international law awareness** (1–2 marks reserved).
>
> 5. **Skipping Case Law** — Even in Part A, citing at least one landmark case (*Mannu Bhandari* or *Sajeev Pillai*) elevates a 3-mark answer to a full-score response.

---

## ✅ **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

### 🚀 **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- 📌 **Definition**: Moral rights = non-economic, personal rights protecting an author's **honor, reputation, and creative identity**.
- 📌 **Two Pillars**: **Paternity** (attribution) + **Integrity** (non-derogation).
- 📌 **Statutory Pillars (India)**: **Section 57** (general) + **Section 57A** (film/sound recording).
- 📌 **Three Immutable Properties**: **Inalienable** + **Irrevocable** + **Perpetual**.
- 📌 **International Anchor**: **Berne Convention, Article 6bis** — minimum guarantee for all member states.
- 📌 **Constitutional Pillars**: **Article 19(1)(a)** + **Article 21** — freedom of expression and dignity.
- 📌 **Key Case Laws**:
  - *Mannu Bhandari v. Kala Vikas Pictures* (1987) — integrity in adaptation
  - *Sajeev Pillai v. Venu Kunnapalli* (2016) — director's §57A rights
  - *Anand Patwardhan v. UoI* (2014) — censorship vs. integrity
- 📌 **Transferability Rule**: You can **sell the painting**, but you can **never sell the right to call yourself the painter**.
- 📌 **Droit de Suite**: Recognized in EU, **NOT in India or USA**.
- 📌 **Enforcement**: Civil suit (injunction + damages) + Public apology right + Heirs' right to sue.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip**: In any 14-mark question on moral rights, structure your answer as: **(i) Definition → (ii) Statutory framework → (iii) Two components explained → (iv) Case law → (v) Comparative reference (Berne) → (vi) Critical conclusion.** This is the KTU Board Examiner's preferred structure for maximum marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Copyright Establishments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Copyright Establishments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Copyright Establishments** refer to the statutory administrative and quasi-judicial bodies created under the **Copyright Act, 1957 (as amended)** for the effective administration, registration, adjudication, and enforcement of copyright rights in India. The two principal establishments are:

1. **The Copyright Office** — An administrative body headed by the **Registrar of Copyrights**, established under **Section 9** of the Act.
2. **The Copyright Board** — A quasi-judicial adjudicatory body established under **Section 11** of the Act.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must focus on the *composition, powers, jurisdiction, and procedural aspects* of the Copyright Office and Copyright Board as mandated by the Indian Copyright Act, 1957. Numerical/statutory references (e.g., Section 9, 11, 12, 13, 14, 72) are **high-weightage** for both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Copyright Establishments** as the *“infrastructure backbone”* of the Indian copyright system — much like the way a **banking system** functions in a country:

| Real-World Analogy | Copyright Equivalent |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| Reserve Bank of India (RBI) | Registrar of Copyrights (Head of Copyright Office) |
| Banking Ombudsman / Debt Recovery Tribunal | Copyright Board (Adjudicatory body) |
| Bank Branch where you open an account | Copyright Office (where works are registered) |
| Customer submitting a complaint | Author/Licensor filing a dispute |

**In simple words:**
- If you create a song, novel, painting, or software code and want **official recognition**, you go to the **Copyright Office** (like opening a savings account).
- If someone copies your work illegally and you want to **resolve a dispute**, the **Copyright Board** acts like a tribunal (like approaching a consumer court).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember:**
> - **Copyright Office = Administrative + Registration** (Executive function)
> - **Copyright Board = Adjudicatory + Dispute Resolution** (Quasi-Judicial function)

---

## 1.3 Standard Statutory Metrics

Under the Copyright Act, 1957, the following **statutory benchmarks** are mandatory for KTU examinations:

- **Section 9** — Establishment of Copyright Office.
- **Section 10** — Registrar and Deputy Registrars of Copyrights.
- **Section 11** — Establishment of Copyright Board.
- **Section 12** — Composition of Copyright Board.
- **Section 13** — Terms and conditions of service of Chairman and Members.
- **Section 14** — Jurisdiction of Copyright Board.
- **Section 72** — Power of Registrar/Board to call for records, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Copyright Board** is NOT a permanent sitting court. It is a **constituted body** that sits as and when disputes are referred to it. This is a frequently tested **conceptual distinction** in KTU exams.
]<]minimax[>[</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Statutory Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Copyright Office (Section 9)

### 2.1.1 Establishment
- The **Central Government** shall establish a **Copyright Office** for the purposes of the Act.
- Located at **Boudhik Sampada Bhawan, Plot No. 32, Sector 14, Dwarka, New Delhi – 110078**.
- Headed by the **Registrar of Copyrights**.

### 2.1.2 Functions of the Copyright Office
The Copyright Office performs the following structured administrative functions:

1. **Registration of Copyrights** — Under **Section 44 to 50** of the Act.
2. **Maintenance of the Register of Copyrights** — A public record of all registered works.
3. **Receipt and acknowledgment of applications** for registration.
4. **Issuance of certificates of registration**.
5. **Acting as a repository** of copyright-related documents.
6. **Providing information** to the public regarding copyright matters.

### 2.1.3 Registrar of Copyrights (Section 10)
- Appointed by the **Central Government**.
- The Registrar is the **principal administrative officer** of the Copyright Office.
- The Central Government may also appoint **one or more Deputy Registrars of Copyrights** to assist the Registrar.
- The Registrar has the power to:
  - Receive applications for registration.
  - Enter particulars in the Register.
  - Cause the Register to be inspected.
  - Grant certified copies of entries.

---

## 2.2 The Copyright Board (Section 11)

### 2.2.1 Establishment
- The **Central Government** shall, by notification in the Official Gazette, constitute a **Copyright Board** for the purposes of the Act.

### 2.2.2 Composition (Section 12)
The Copyright Board consists of:
- **A Chairman**, and
- **Not less than two and not more than fourteen other Members** (as per the Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012 and Rules).

The Chairman and Members are appointed by the **Central Government** from persons of ability, integrity, and standing who have:
- **Judicial/Quasi-Judicial experience**, OR
- **Special knowledge of, or professional experience in**, matters related to:
  - Literature
  - Drama
  - Music
  - Fine Arts
  - Cinematography
  - Intellectual Property Law

### 2.2.3 Term of Office (Section 13)
- The Chairman holds office for a term of **5 years** or until attaining the age of **65 years**, whichever is earlier.
- Members hold office for a term of **5 years** or until attaining the age of **62 years**, whichever is earlier.
- Eligible for **reappointment**.

### 2.2.4 Jurisdiction and Powers of the Copyright Board (Section 14 and 35)
The Copyright Board has **exclusive jurisdiction** to adjudicate upon:

1. **Disputes relating to assignment of copyright** (Section 19).
2. **Disputes regarding transmission of copyright** on death of the author.
3. **Disputes on the term of copyright** for posthumous works.
4. **Disputes regarding compulsory licensing** (Section 31, 31A, 31B, 31C).
5. **Disputes regarding licensing of translation works** (Section 32).
6. **Rectification of the Register of Copyrights** (Section 50).
7. **Disputes regarding unpublished Indian works** (Section 30A).
8. **Disputes concerning works of international organizations** (Section 35).
9. **Payment of royalties** by broadcasting organizations (Section 31D).
10. **Statutory licences for cover versions and broadcast of literary/musical works** (Sections 30 and 31).

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Concept:** The Copyright Board's jurisdiction is **subject-matter specific** — it is NOT a general civil court. It adjudicates ONLY the disputes enumerated under Section 14 read with the relevant substantive sections.

### 2.2.5 Procedural Powers (Section 74)
The Copyright Board has the powers of a **Civil Court** under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, while trying a suit, specifically with respect to:
- Summoning and enforcing attendance of witnesses.
- Requiring the discovery and production of documents.
- Receiving evidence on affidavits.
- Issuing commissions for examination of witnesses.
- Requisitioning any public record from any court or office.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Statutory Cheat Sheet

| Section | Subject Matter | Key Provision | KTU Weightage |
|:-------:|:--------------:|:--------------|:-------------:|
| **Section 9** | Copyright Office | Establishment | High |
| **Section 10** | Registrar of Copyrights | Appointment & Powers | High |
| **Section 11** | Copyright Board | Constitution | Very High |
| **Section 12** | Composition | Chairman + 2 to 14 Members | Very High |
| **Section 13** | Term of Office | 5 yrs / Age 65 (Chair) / 62 (Member) | High |
| **Section 14** | Jurisdiction | Exclusive Subject-Matter List | Very High |
| **Section 50** | Rectification of Register | Power of Board | High |
| **Section 72** | Powers to call records | Quasi-Judicial powers | Medium |
| **Section 74** | Powers of Civil Court | Procedural powers | High |
| **Section 19** | Assignment disputes | Board adjudication | Medium |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

For B.Tech students (especially CS, IT, ECE, EEE), understanding Copyright Establishments is critical because:

1. **Software Copyright Disputes** — Indian IT companies routinely appear before the Copyright Board in disputes involving source code piracy, software licensing, and DMCA-equivalent issues.
2. **Open Source Compliance** — Knowledge of compulsory licensing (Section 31) helps engineers understand when copyrighted code can be legally reused.
3. **Research Publications** — Academic authorship disputes in engineering often involve copyright transfer agreements governed by Section 19.
4. **IP Start-ups** — Engineering innovators filing copyright for hardware designs, circuit layouts (semi-conductor), and software need to interact with the Copyright Office.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Field Relevance:** Engineers must recognize that their **software code, technical drawings, integrated circuit layouts, and engineering manuals** all fall under the Copyright Act, 1957, and the establishments (Office + Board) are the operational machinery for protection and dispute resolution.
]<]minimax[>[</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Structural Logic

## 3.1 Analytical Logic Flow: From Creation to Adjudication

To understand the establishment mechanism, follow this **structured 6-step analytical flow** that KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers:

### **Step 1: Trigger Event — Creation of a Work**
An author/engineer creates an original literary, dramatic, musical, or artistic work. Under **Section 13**, copyright subsists automatically upon creation — no formal registration is mandatory for protection.

> **Logic:** *Why is registration optional?*
> Because the Copyright Act follows the **Berne Convention** principle of *automatic protection*. However, registration serves as **prima facie evidence** in court.

### **Step 2: Application for Registration (Section 44)**
The owner files **Form IV** (for literary/dramatic/musical/artistic works) or **Form V** (for cinematograph films/sound recordings) along with prescribed fees at the **Copyright Office**.

### **Step 3: Examination by Registrar (Section 45)**
The **Registrar of Copyrights** examines the application. If defects exist, the Registrar may:
- Notify the applicant in writing.
- Allow a **30-day window** to remove objections.

If no defects: The Registrar enters the work in the **Register of Copyrights**.

### **Step 4: Issuance of Certificate (Section 48)**
A **Certificate of Registration** is issued, which serves as **prima facie evidence** of the particulars entered in the Register.

### **Step 5: Trigger of Dispute → Reference to Copyright Board**
If any of the **Section 14** disputes arise, an aggrieved party files an **application before the Copyright Board** in the prescribed manner under the **Copyright Rules, 2013**.

### **Step 6: Adjudication by Copyright Board**
The Board exercises its **quasi-judicial powers** (Section 74) and passes orders that are executable as a decree of a Civil Court.

---

## 3.2 Mathematical/Logical Representation of the Establishment Structure

Although copyright law is qualitative, the **hierarchy of authority** can be represented as a structured logical equation:

$$
\text{Copyright Administration} = f(\text{Office}, \text{Board}, \text{Central Govt.})
$$

Where:

$$
\text{Office} = \text{Registrar} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Deputy Registrars}
$$

$$
\text{Board} = \text{Chairman} + \sum_{j=2}^{14} \text{Members}
$$

$$
\text{Jurisdiction}_{Board} = \bigcup_{k \in \{14, 19, 30, 31, 32, 35, 50\}} \text{Section}_k
$$

> **Interpretation:** The Board’s jurisdiction is the **logical union (∪)** of all specifically enumerated sections under the Act. Anything outside this set lies outside the Board’s exclusive domain.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Comparison: Copyright Office vs. Copyright Board

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Difference} &= \big\{ (\text{Nature}, \text{Office}: \text{Administrative}, \text{Board}: \text{Quasi-Judicial}), \\
&\quad (\text{Head}, \text{Office}: \text{Registrar}, \text{Board}: \text{Chairman}), \\
&\quad (\text{Function}, \text{Office}: \text{Registration}, \text{Board}: \text{Adjudication}), \\
&\quad (\text{Permanence}, \text{Office}: \text{Permanent}, \text{Board}: \text{Constituted Ad-hoc}), \\
&\quad (\text{Legal Force}, \text{Office}: \text{Certificate = Evidence}, \text{Board}: \text{Order = Decree}) \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each tuple in the set represents a discrete axis of comparison. KTU examiners award **1 mark per valid distinguishing point**, and a minimum of **5 points** is expected for full marks on such a question.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Working: How the Copyright Board Resolves a Dispute

**Scenario:** *Author A* assigns copyright to *Publisher B*. Publisher B fails to publish within 1 year. Author A wants revocation.

**Step-by-Step Adjudication Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Author A files Application to Copyright Board under Section 19(8).} \\
&\text{Step 2: Board issues Notice to Publisher B (15-30 days).} \\
&\text{Step 3: Board hears both parties — quasi-judicial proceedings.} \\
&\text{Step 4: Board applies Section 19(8) test: "Public interest + Failure to publish".} \\
&\text{Step 5: Board passes an Order — either revoking assignment or imposing conditions.} \\
&\text{Step 6: Order is executable as a decree of Civil Court.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Principle:** The Board balances **contractual obligations** (between A and B) against **public interest** (access to the work), making it a **hybrid commercial-cum-welfare tribunal**.
]<]minimax[>[</br>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**"What is the composition of the Copyright Board as per the Copyright Act, 1957?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Under **Section 11 and Section 12** of the Copyright Act, 1957, the Copyright Board is constituted by the Central Government. It consists of:

1. **A Chairman**, and
2. **Not less than two and not more than fourteen other Members.**

The Chairman and Members are appointed from persons of **ability, integrity, and standing** who possess either judicial/quasi-judicial experience OR special knowledge in literature, drama, music, fine arts, cinematography, or intellectual property law. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**"Differentiate between the Copyright Office and the Copyright Board."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Copyright Office | Copyright Board |
|:------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| Nature | Administrative body | Quasi-judicial body |
| Head | Registrar of Copyrights | Chairman |
| Function | Registration of works | Dispute adjudication |
| Establishment | Section 9 | Section 11 |
| Permanence | Permanent | Constituted as needed |

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for any 3 valid distinctions)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the establishment, composition, and powers of the **Copyright Board** under the Copyright Act, 1957. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **jurisdiction of the Copyright Board** with reference to Sections 14, 19, 31, and 50 of the Copyright Act. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Establishment, Composition, and Powers of Copyright Board (7 marks)**

**Establishment (Section 11):** The Central Government, by notification in the Official Gazette, constitutes a **Copyright Board** for the purposes of the Copyright Act, 1957. *(1 mark)*

**Composition (Section 12):** The Board consists of:
- **A Chairman**, and
- **Not less than two and not more than fourteen Members.** *(1 mark)*

**Qualifications (Section 12):** Appointed from persons of ability, integrity, and standing having judicial/quasi-judicial experience OR special knowledge in literature, drama, music, fine arts, cinematography, or IP law. *(1 mark)*

**Term of Office (Section 13):** Chairman — 5 years or age **65** (whichever earlier). Members — 5 years or age **62** (whichever earlier). Eligible for reappointment. *(1 mark)*

**Powers (Sections 14 & 74):**
- **Exclusive jurisdiction** over enumerated subject-matter under Section 14.
- Powers of a **Civil Court** under Section 74 (summoning witnesses, requiring document production, receiving evidence on affidavits, issuing commissions). *(2 marks)*

**Additional Powers:** The Board can call for records (Section 72), and its orders are executable as decrees of a Civil Court. *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — Jurisdiction of the Copyright Board (7 marks)**

The jurisdiction of the Copyright Board is **exclusive and limited** to the matters specified under the Act:

1. **Section 19 Disputes:** Adjudication of disputes relating to assignment of copyright and revocation of assignments where the assignee fails to exercise rights. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Section 31 — Compulsory Licensing:** Disputes regarding grant of compulsory licences in works withheld from public, unpublished works, and for benefits of physically disabled persons (Sections 31, 31A, 31B, 31C). *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Section 14 — Statutory Licences:** Covers cover versions, broadcasting of literary and musical works, and licensing of sound recordings. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Section 50 — Rectification of Register:** Power to order rectification of the Register of Copyrights on application by an aggrieved party. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Other Matters:** Term of copyright in posthumous works, transmission on author's death, disputes under Sections 30A, 32 (translation), 31D (royalty), and 35 (international organizations). *(1 mark)*

> **Conclusion:** The Board's jurisdiction is **not general** but is **enumerated and exclusive**, ensuring specialized adjudication in copyright matters. *(BonUs mark for 14-mark depth)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **establishment, functions, and powers of the Copyright Office** under Section 9 and Section 10 of the Copyright Act, 1957. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Critically analyze the **distinction between the Copyright Office and Copyright Board**, citing at least **six valid points of difference** with statutory backing. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Copyright Office (7 marks)**

**Establishment (Section 9):** The Central Government shall establish a **Copyright Office** in India for carrying out the purposes of the Act. The office is presently located at New Delhi. *(1 mark)*

**Registrar (Section 10):** The Central Government appoints a **Registrar of Copyrights** and may appoint one or more **Deputy Registrars** to assist. The Registrar is the administrative head of the Office. *(1 mark)*

**Functions:**
- Receive and process applications for registration of copyright (Forms IV & V). *(1 mark)*
- Maintain the **Register of Copyrights** and cause it to be inspected. *(1 mark)*
- Issue **Certificates of Registration** under Section 48 (prima facie evidence). *(1 mark)*
- Provide certified copies of entries and public information services. *(1 mark)*

**Powers:** The Registrar may call for records, examine applications, refuse defective applications, and allow time for rectification. Orders of the Registrar are appealable to the **Copyright Board** under Section 50. *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — Critical Analysis of the Distinction (7 marks)**

| S.No | Point of Distinction | Copyright Office | Copyright Board |
|:----:|:---------------------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| 1 | Statutory Basis | Section 9 | Section 11 |
| 2 | Nature | Administrative | Quasi-Judicial |
| 3 | Head | Registrar of Copyrights | Chairman |
| 4 | Primary Function | Registration & Record-Keeping | Dispute Adjudication |
| 5 | Permanence | Permanent body | Constituted as required |
| 6 | Output | Certificate of Registration | Order (Decree-equivalent) |
| 7 | Powers | Limited to administrative powers | Civil Court powers (Section 74) |

*(6 marks — 1 mark each for any 6 valid points)*

**Critical Analysis:** While the Copyright Office ensures **preventive protection** through documentation, the Copyright Board provides **curative remedy** through adjudication. The two together form the **complementary architecture** of copyright administration in India — one cannot function effectively without the other. *(1 mark for critical conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Copyright Establishment Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Sections 9 and 11** — Section 9 = Copyright Office; Section 11 = Copyright Board. Examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for interchanging these.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Age Limits** — Chairman: **65 years**; Members: **62 years**. Generic answers stating only "5-year term" lose **1 mark**.
> 
> 3. **Treating the Board as a Permanent Court** — The Copyright Board is **NOT a permanent court**. It is constituted *as and when needed*. Mentioning "permanent court" results in **1 mark deduction**.
> 
> 4. **Missing Section 74 Reference** — When asked about Board powers, students often forget to cite **Section 74 (Civil Court powers)**. This is a **guaranteed 2-mark loss**.
> 
> 5. **No Statutory References** — Every answer MUST cite **Section numbers** (e.g., "Section 12 for composition"). Answers without statutory backing lose **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 
> 6. **Mixing Up Registrar with Copyright Board** — The Registrar is **not a member** of the Copyright Board. The Board is an independent adjudicatory body. This is a **favourite trick question**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Copyright Establishments**
> 
> ✅ **Copyright Office (Section 9)** is established by the **Central Government** and headed by the **Registrar of Copyrights**.
> 
> ✅ **Registrar (Section 10)** is the principal administrative officer; assisted by Deputy Registrars.
> 
> ✅ **Copyright Board (Section 11)** is constituted by notification; **NOT a permanent court**.
> 
> ✅ **Composition (Section 12):** Chairman + **2 to 14 Members**.
> 
> ✅ **Term:** Chairman — 5 years or **65 years age** (whichever earlier); Members — 5 years or **62 years age**.
> 
> ✅ **Jurisdiction (Section 14)** is **exclusive and enumerated**, not general.
> 
> ✅ **Powers (Section 74):** Powers of a **Civil Court** under CPC, 1908.
> 
> ✅ **Registration is NOT mandatory** for copyright protection (Berne Convention), but provides **prima facie evidence**.
> 
> ✅ **Certificate of Registration (Section 48)** is admissible as evidence in all courts.
> 
> ✅ **Rectification of Register (Section 50)** lies with the **Copyright Board**.
> 
> ✅ **Appeal from Registrar's decision** lies to the **Copyright Board**, not to a civil court directly.
> 
> ✅ The Board sits at **New Delhi** and has **Pan-India jurisdiction** in copyright matters.
> 
> ✅ For B.Tech engineers: **software code, technical manuals, circuit layouts** all qualify as "literary works" and are protected by copyright — and disputes are adjudicated by these very establishments.

---

**End of Module 3 — Copyright Establishments Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme PESFT757 — IPR*
]<]minimax[>[</br>

topics.title: Trade Marks Act 1999
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trade Marks Act 1999

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Trade Mark? (Statutory Definition)

Under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**, a **trade mark** is formally defined in **Section 2(1)(zb)** as:

> *"A mark capable of being represented graphically and which is capable of distinguishing the goods or services of one person from those of others, and may include a brand name, a brand, a label, a packet, a ticket, an emblem, a letter, a signature, a word, a numeral, or any combination thereof, or a combination of colours, or the shape of goods or their packaging, or a three-dimensional sign, or a sound mark, or a smell, or a combination of any of these."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 1999 Act replaced the archaic **Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958** to align with **TRIPS (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights)** obligations under the **WTO** framework. It is administered by the **Office of the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)**, operating under the **Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce & Industry, Government of India**. The Act came into force on **15th September 2003**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Signature of a Business"

Imagine you walk into a crowded marketplace with hundreds of identical-looking sweet shops. How do you instantly recognize **"Amul"** chocolate or **"Parle-G"** biscuits? 

A trade mark is essentially the **visual signature of a business** — the unique fingerprint that allows consumers to instantly connect a product on a shelf to its source. Just like your own signature identifies *you* in legal documents, a trade mark identifies *the origin and quality* of goods/services in the commercial world.

Consider the following real-world mapping:

| Real-World Analogy | Trade Mark Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Your handwritten signature | Word Mark (e.g., "Nike") |
| Your face/photograph | Logo/Device Mark (e.g., Apple 🍎) |
| Your voice on phone | Sound Mark (e.g., MGM lion's roar) |
| Your unique cologne smell | Smell/Olfactory Mark |
| Your unique handshake | 3D Shape Mark (e.g., Coca-Cola bottle) |

## 1.3 The Three Core Functions of a Trade Mark

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Board Exams, always remember the three "F" framework:**

1. **Identification Function** — Identifies the **origin/source** of goods or services.
2. **Guarantee Function** — Guarantees a **consistent quality** standard to consumers.
3. **Advertising Function** — Acts as a **marketing tool** that builds brand recognition and goodwill.

## 1.4 What CAN and CANNOT be Registered

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 9 — Absolute Grounds for Refusal** prohibits registration of marks that are:
> - **Devoid of distinctive character** (e.g., a plain circle for a ball manufacturer)
> - **Exclusively descriptive** of the goods (e.g., "Creamy" for ice cream)
> - **Customary in trade** (e.g., "Tractor" for tractor parts)
> - **Generic names** of the goods/services
> - **Likely to deceive** or cause confusion
> - **Immoral, scandalous, or offensive**
> - **Arms of state, international organizations, or flags** (without consent)
> - Identical/similar to **well-known trade marks**

## 1.5 Symbols of Trade Mark Protection

> [!NOTE]
> - **® (Registered Symbol)** — Can ONLY be used after **actual registration** is granted. Using it before registration is a punishable offence under **Section 107(1)**.
> - **™ (Unregistered Symbol)** — Anyone can use this to claim common-law rights, even **before** or **without** registration. It has no statutory backing but signals intent.
> - **℠ (Service Mark Symbol)** — Used for **services** rather than goods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Salient Features of the Trade Marks Act, 1999 (vs. 1958 Act)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Core Focus:** Questions frequently appear comparing the 1999 Act with the 1958 Act. Memorize this comparison table.

| Feature | Trade & Merchandise Marks Act, **1958** | Trade Marks Act, **1999** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition of mark | Narrow — names, symbols, devices | Broad — includes **sound, smell, 3D shapes, colour combinations** |
| Registration tenure | **7 years** (renewable for 14 years) | **10 years** (renewable indefinitely for 10-year blocks) |
| Service Marks | NOT registrable | **Registrable** distinctly |
| Well-known marks | Not recognized statutorily | Explicitly protected (**Section 2(1)(zg)**) |
| Grounds for refusal | Limited | Expanded (absolute + relative grounds) |
| International framework | Not aligned | Aligned with **TRIPS, Madrid Protocol, Nice Classification** |
| Trans-border reputation | Not covered | **Explicitly covered** (Section 11(6)(b)) |
| Appellate authority | High Court | **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** — now shifted to **Commercial Courts** |

## 2.2 Classification of Goods & Services (Nice Classification)

India follows the **Nice Classification (NCL)** system comprising **45 classes**:

- **Classes 1 to 34** → Goods (chemicals, paints, cosmetics, machinery, etc.)
- **Classes 35 to 45** → Services (advertising, insurance, transport, education, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> A separate fee is payable for each class. Multi-class filing is permitted under a single application, with a **surcharge** for each additional class beyond the first.

## 2.3 Types of Trade Marks

| Type | Description | Real Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Word Mark** | Text only | "Google", "Pepsi" |
| **Device/Logo Mark** | Stylized graphic | Apple Logo, BMW roundel |
| **Combined Mark** | Word + Device | "LUX" with star symbol |
| **Three-Dimensional (3D) Mark** | Shape of product/packaging | Coca-Cola bottle, Toblerone triangle |
| **Sound Mark** | Distinctive audio | Nokia tune, MGM roar, Intel chime |
| **Colour Mark** | Single or combination of colours | Tiffany Blue (registered) |
| **Slogan Mark** | Catchy phrase | "Just Do It" (Nike) |
| **Certification Mark** | Indicates quality/origin standards | ISI mark, Hallmark |
| **Collective Mark** | Used by members of an association | CA Institute logo |
| **Series Mark** | Variation of same mark on goods | "Colgate — Total / Sparkling / Fresh" |
| **Well-Known Mark** | Famous across Indian public | Coca-Cola, Tata, Amul |
| **Defensive Mark** | Registered for goods NOT used (phased out in 1999 Act) | — |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Section Mapping (Mandatory to Remember)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Key Sections of Trade Marks Act, 1999:**

| Section | Subject | One-line Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 2(1)(zb)** | Definition of Trade Mark | "The Definition Section" |
| **Section 2(1)(zg)** | Definition of Well-Known Mark | "Fame Factor" |
| **Section 3** | Persons deemed as "registered users" | "Registration User Rule" |
| **Section 9** | Absolute Grounds for Refusal | "Absolute Bar" |
| **Section 11** | Relative Grounds for Refusal | "Relative Confusion Bar" |
| **Section 18** | Application for Registration | "Filing Door" |
| **Section 20** | Examination by Registrar | "Examination Stage" |
| **Section 21** | Acceptance & Advertisement | "Publication Stage" |
| **Section 22** | Opposition | "Objection Window" |
| **Section 23** | Registration | "The Birth Certificate" |
| **Section 25** | Duration of Registration | "10 Year Rule" |
| **Section 28** | Rights Conferred by Registration | "Owner's Toolkit" |
| **Section 29** | Infringement | "Violation Definition" |
| **Section 30** | Limits on Infringement | "Defence Wall" |
| **Section 34** | Assignment & Transmission | "Ownership Transfer" |
| **Section 39** | Rectification of Register | "Correction Power" |
| **Section 57** | Non-Use Cancellation | "Use it or Lose it" |
| **Section 77 to 81** | Penalties (Civil & Criminal) | "Punishment Zone" |
| **Section 103 to 111** | Offences, Penalties & Procedure | "Criminal Prosecution" |
| **Section 134** | Protection of Well-Known Marks | "Anti-Dilution Shield" |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Engineering** → Drug companies trademark brand names (e.g., "Crocin") distinct from generic composition.
- **Software/IT Industry** → Logos and taglines are trade marks (e.g., "ThinkPad", "Windows").
- **Automobile Industry** → 3D shape of car grilles (BMW kidney grille) is registered.
- **Manufacturing Sector** → "ISI" mark certifies quality standard compliance.
- **Startup Ecosystem** → Trademark registration is one of the **first IP assets** any tech startup secures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Statutory Definition — Detailed Deconstruction

**Step 1: Locate the Definition**
The definition of a trade mark is contained in **Section 2(1)(zb)** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999.

**Step 2: Identify the Two Mandatory Requirements**
For any mark to qualify as a registrable trade mark, it MUST satisfy:

1. **Graphical Representability** — The mark must be capable of being represented **visually on paper** (for sound/smell marks, special notation like musical staves is used).
2. **Distinctiveness** — The mark must be capable of **distinguishing** the goods/services of one trader from those of another.

**Step 3: Identify the Non-Exhaustive List of Examples**
The Act provides examples (note the words *"may include"*, indicating the list is illustrative, not exhaustive):

- Brand name
- Label
- Ticket
- Emblem
- Letter / Signature / Word / Numeral
- Combination of colours
- Shape of goods or their packaging
- Three-dimensional sign
- Sound mark
- Smell mark
- Any combination of the above

**Step 4: Apply the Acquired Distinctiveness Doctrine**
Even if a mark is not inherently distinctive, it may acquire distinctiveness through **long and continuous use** (Section 9(1) proviso). Example: "Whirlpool" became distinctive for washing machines despite being a common English word.

## 3.2 Registration Procedure — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** This procedural chain is a guaranteed **14-mark** question. Memorize every step.

### **STEP 1: Search for Existing Marks (Pre-Filing Step)**
The applicant conducts a search in the **Indian Trade Marks Registry database** to ensure the mark is available. Search is done at:
- `ipindiaonline.gov.in` (Public Search)
- Trademark offices in **Mumbai (head office)**, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, and Ahmedabad.

### **STEP 2: Filing of Application (Section 18)**
The applicant files **Form TM-A** either:
- **Online** (e-filing) — recommended, with reduced government fee
- **Offline** (paper) — at the appropriate Trade Marks Registry office based on the **place of business**

**Mandatory particulars in the application:**
- Name and address of the applicant
- Description of goods/services
- Class(es) of goods/services
- Mark representation
- Date of first use (if already in use)
- User affidavit (if claiming prior use)

### **STEP 3: Vienna Codification (For Device Marks Only)**
If the mark contains a logo/device, the Registrar assigns a **Vienna Code** for the figurative elements as per the **Vienna Agreement (1973)**.

### **STEP 4: Examination by Examiner (Section 20)**
The Examiner reviews the application against:
- **Absolute grounds** (Section 9)
- **Relative grounds** (Section 11) — searching for conflicting marks

The Examiner issues an **Examination Report** with one of three outcomes:

| Outcome | Meaning | Next Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Accepted outright** | No objections | Proceed to publication |
| **Objected (raised objections)** | Has issues | Applicant must respond within **30 days** (extendable) |
| **Examination Report with "Hearing Notice"** | Serious objections | Applicant must appear before the Registrar |

### **STEP 5: Reply to Examination Report**
The applicant submits a **written reply** addressing each objection. May include:
- Affidavits of use
- Evidence of acquired distinctiveness
- Arguments distinguishing the mark from cited conflicting marks

If the Registrar is satisfied → mark proceeds for **acceptance**. If not → **refusal order** (appeal lies to **IPAB / Commercial Court**).

### **STEP 6: Acceptance & Advertisement in Journal (Section 21)**
Once accepted, the mark is **published in the Trade Marks Journal** (published weekly on the official website). This is a **PUBLIC NOTICE** inviting opposition.

### **STEP 7: Opposition Period (Section 21)**
Within **4 months** (or such extended period not exceeding 1 month) from the date of advertisement, any "aggrieved person" can file **Notice of Opposition (Form TM-O)**.

The opponent must:
- File notice within the stipulated time
- Pay the prescribed fee
- Serve a copy on the applicant

The applicant then has **2 months** to file a counter-statement. Both parties submit evidence, and the Registrar holds a **hearing** and issues a **decision**.

### **STEP 8: Registration & Issuance of Certificate (Section 23)**
If no opposition is filed, OR if opposition is decided in favour of the applicant, the mark is **registered**. The Registrar issues the **Registration Certificate** with:
- Trademark registration number
- Date of registration
- Class(es) of registration
- The mark's representation
- Proprietor's name

> [!NOTE]
> The **date of registration is the date of filing**, not the date of certificate issuance. Registration is valid for **10 years** from the date of application (Section 25).

## 3.3 Infringement Analysis (Section 29)

A trade mark is said to be **infringed** when a person, not being the registered proprietor or a person authorized to use it, uses an identical or deceptively similar mark in the course of trade in relation to:

1. **Identical goods or services** (where the registered mark is identical)
2. **Similar goods or services** (where the mark is identical OR deceptively similar)
3. **A well-known mark**, even for unrelated goods/services (anti-dilution protection)

### Tests Used by Courts to Determine "Deceptively Similar":

**Test 1: Side-by-Side Comparison Test**
- Marks are compared **alongside each other** for visual similarity.
- Rarely used alone.

**Test 2: Overall Impression Test**
- Marks are compared **separately**, with a time gap, mimicking actual marketplace conditions.
- **PREFERRED TEST** by Indian courts.

**Test 3: Phonetic / Sound Test**
- Are the marks pronounced similarly?
- E.g., "Lakme" vs "Lacme"

**Test 4: Conceptual Test**
- Do the marks convey the same meaning or idea?
- E.g., "Puma" (animal) vs "Jaguar" (animal)

**Test 5: Customer Confusion Test (Layman Test)**
- Would an **ordinary person of average intelligence and imperfect recollection** be confused?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landmark Precedents (No specific case law to memorize, just the principles):**
> - *Amritdhara Pharmacy v. Satya Deo Gupta* (1963) — laid down the **deceptively similar test**
> - *Cadila Health Care v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals* (2001) — applied the **overall impression test**
> - *Laxmikant V. Patel v. Chetanbhai Shah* (2002) — reaffirmed customer confusion test

## 3.4 Assignment, Transmission & Licensing

### Assignment (Section 34–39)
- **Assignment with Goodwill** — Entire business (including goodwill) is transferred. Assignee can use the mark for the same business.
- **Assignment without Goodwill (Gross Assignment)** — Only the mark is transferred, business is not. **PROHIBITED** under the 1999 Act if it causes deception.
- **Partial Assignment** — Mark is assigned for some goods/services or a particular area. Permitted with Registrar's approval.

### Licensing (Section 48–53)
- **Registered User / Licensee** — Person authorized to use the mark under a **written agreement** registered with the Registrar.
- **Franchise arrangements** are typically structured as registered user agreements.

## 3.5 Rights of a Registered Proprietor (Section 28)

> [!NOTE]
> The registered proprietor has the **exclusive right** to use the mark in relation to the goods/services for which it is registered, and to obtain relief in respect of infringement. Importantly, registration confers rights **across all of India**, regardless of where the proprietor is based.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Trade Mark Registration Process in India

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Applicant Initiates Process"] --> B["Step 1: Conduct TM Search in IP India Database"]
    B --> C{"Step 2: Is Mark Available?"}
    C -->|"No - Conflict Found"| D["Modify or Re-design the Mark"]
    D --> B
    C -->|"Yes - Clear"| E["Step 3: File Form TM-A at TM Registry"]
    E --> F["Step 4: Allotment of Application Number and Vienna Codification if Device Mark"]
    F --> G["Step 5: Examination by Examiner Section 20"]
    G --> H{"Step 6: Examination Report Outcome"}
    H -->|"Accepted"| K["Step 8: Advertisement in TM Journal Section 21"]
    H -->|"Objected"| I["Step 7: Applicant Files Reply within 30 days"]
    I --> J{"Registrar Satisfied?"}
    J -->|"Yes"| K
    J -->|"No Refusal Order"| X["Appeal to Commercial Court or IPAB"]
    X --> Y["Court Decision"]
    K --> L{"Step 9: Opposition Period 4 Months Section 21"}
    L -->|"Opposition Filed"| M["Counter Statement and Hearing"]
    M --> N{"Registrar Decision"}
    N -->|"In Favour of Applicant"| P["Step 11: Registration Granted Section 23"]
    N -->|"Against Applicant"| X
    L -->|"No Opposition Filed"| P
    P --> Q["Step 12: Registration Certificate Issued Valid 10 Years"]
    Q --> R["Step 13: Renewal Every 10 Years Indefinitely"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Grounds for Refusal of Registration


```mermaid

    R --> R4["Right to Sue for Passing Off"]

    R5 --> R5A["Prevent Import of Counterfeit Goods at Ports"]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define a "Trade Mark" as per Section 2(1)(zb) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. List any four examples.**
*(Tagged: [KTU University Exam – May 2024])*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As per **Section 2(1)(zb)** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, a trade mark is defined as:

> *"A mark capable of being represented graphically and which is capable of distinguishing the goods or services of one person from those of others."*

The Act provides an illustrative (non-exhaustive) list of examples, which **may include**:

1. A **brand name** (e.g., "Nike")
2. A **label** (e.g., "Brooke Bond" tea label)
3. A **letter, word, or signature**
4. A **shape of goods or their packaging** (e.g., Coca-Cola bottle)

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four examples with illustrations.)*

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between the symbols "™" and "®" used in connection with trade marks.**
*(Tagged: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023])*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **™ (Unregistered Symbol)** | **® (Registered Symbol)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal Status** | No statutory recognition; indicates *intent* to claim rights | Statutory recognition under Trade Marks Act, 1999 |
| **When Used** | Can be used **before** or **without** registration | Can ONLY be used **after** actual registration is granted |
| **Rights Conferred** | Common-law rights only (passing off action) | Exclusive statutory rights under Section 28 |
| **Misuse Penalty** | No penalty for misuse | Misuse is a **punishable offence under Section 107(1)** — fine up to ₹5,000 |

*(Award 1 mark for each correct row — 3 rows × 1 mark = 3 marks.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Model)

---

### **🟢 QUESTION A (14 Marks):**
**"Discuss the salient features of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. How is it different from the Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958? Why was the new Act enacted?"**

**[Part (a) — 7 Marks]** Salient features and reasons for enactment of the 1999 Act.
**[Part (b) — 7 Marks]** Detailed comparison with the 1958 Act.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The **Trade Marks Act, 1999** was enacted to modernize India's trade mark regime and align it with **TRIPS obligations** under the WTO framework. The 1958 Act was archaic and could not address contemporary commercial realities like e-commerce, service industries, and international trade.

**Key Reasons for Enactment:**

1. **TRIPS Compliance** — India's WTO membership (from 1995) required harmonization of IP laws with TRIPS standards.
2. **Service Sector Recognition** — The 1958 Act did not recognize service marks; the 1999 Act explicitly protects services.
3. **Modernization** — Inclusion of non-conventional marks (sound, smell, 3D shapes, colour combinations).
4. **Well-Known Mark Protection** — Statutory recognition of well-known marks to prevent dilution.
5. **Trans-Border Reputation** — Explicit recognition that reputation can extend beyond the place of business.

**Salient Features:**

- **Broad Definition (Section 2(1)(zb))** — Encompasses all visual and non-visual identifiers.
- **10-Year Registration Period** — Renewable indefinitely.
- **Single Registration for Goods & Services** — Nice Classification adopted.
- **Civil and Criminal Remedies** — Both injunctions and imprisonment provisions.
- **International Framework** — Aligned with Madrid Protocol and Paris Convention.

*(Award 2 marks for reasons, 5 marks for features — distribute marks for each point explained.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Comparison Table — 1958 Act vs. 1999 Act:**

| Parameter | 1958 Act | 1999 Act |
|---|---|---|
| **Name** | Trade and Merchandise Marks Act | Trade Marks Act |
| **Definition Scope** | Limited to goods only | Goods **and** services |
| **Service Marks** | Not registrable | **Registrable** |
| **Mark Types** | Only conventional marks | Conventional + sound, smell, 3D, colour |
| **Registration Period** | 7 years, renewable for 14 years | **10 years**, renewable indefinitely |
| **Well-Known Marks** | Not recognized | **Statutorily protected** |
| **Trans-Border Reputation** | Not covered | **Covered** under Section 11(6)(b) |
| **Madrid Protocol** | Not adopted | **India joined in 2013** |
| **Defensive Marks** | Available | **Removed** |
| **Appellate Forum** | High Court | **IPAB → Commercial Courts** |

*(Award 0.5 mark per row for 10 rows = 5 marks + 2 marks for explanatory comments.)*

---

### **🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**"Explain the procedure for registration of a trade mark in India under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. State the grounds for refusal of registration."**

**[Part (a) — 7 Marks]** Step-by-step procedure for registration.
**[Part (b) — 7 Marks]** Grounds for refusal (absolute and relative).

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The registration procedure under the Trade Marks Act, 1999 follows a **multi-stage statutory process:**

**Step 1: Search** — Applicant searches the IP India database for conflicting marks.

**Step 2: Filing Application (Section 18)** — Submission of **Form TM-A** (online or offline) with details of the applicant, mark, and goods/services class.

**Step 3: Vienna Codification** — Assignment of figurative codes if the mark contains a device/logo.

**Step 4: Examination (Section 20)** — The Examiner issues an Examination Report citing objections.

**Step 5: Reply to Objections** — Applicant submits a written response within **30 days** addressing each objection.

**Step 6: Acceptance & Advertisement (Section 21)** — Accepted marks are published in the **Trade Marks Journal** for public notice.

**Step 7: Opposition Period** — Any aggrieved party may file opposition within **4 months** from advertisement (Form TM-O).

**Step 8: Counter-Statement & Hearing** — Applicant files a counter-statement; both parties submit evidence; Registrar decides.

**Step 9: Registration (Section 23)** — If no opposition or opposition fails, the mark is **registered** and certificate issued. The **registration date is the filing date**, valid for **10 years** (Section 25).

**Step 10: Renewal** — Application for renewal may be filed within **6 months before expiry**, with a 6-month grace period thereafter.

*(Award 0.7 mark per step = 7 marks. Include section references for top marks.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Grounds for Refusal of Registration:**

### **A. Absolute Grounds (Section 9) — 3.5 marks:**

A mark shall not be registered if it:

1. Is **devoid of distinctive character** — e.g., a plain shape for a ball.
2. Is **exclusively descriptive** of the goods — e.g., "Sweet" for sugar.
3. Consists **exclusively of customary** or generic terms.
4. Is **likely to deceive** or cause confusion (e.g., "Bisleri-2" for water).
5. Is **immoral, scandalous, or offensive**.
6. Contains or consists of **state emblems, flags, or international organizations** without consent.
7. Is identical/similar to a **well-known mark** (Section 9(2)(b)).

> *Proviso:* A mark that has acquired distinctiveness through **long and continuous use** is registrable.

### **B. Relative Grounds (Section 11) — 3.5 marks:**

A mark shall not be registered if it is:

1. **Identical/similar to an earlier registered mark** for identical/similar goods.
2. Identical/similar to a mark of an **earlier user in India**.
3. Identical/similar to a **well-known mark in India** (Section 11(6)).
4. Identical/similar to an earlier registered **collective or certification mark**.
5. Filed by an **agent/representative** of the proprietor without authorization.

*(Award 0.5 mark per ground × 7 grounds = 3.5 marks for Section 9; 0.5 mark × 7 grounds = 3.5 marks for Section 11. Plus 0.5 mark each for examples.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse the **registration date** (date of filing of application) with the **date of certificate issuance**. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this error.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Many students write **"TM-A Form"** without mentioning that it is filed under **Section 18** — losing **0.5 mark** for missing the statutory reference.
> - **Pitfall 3:** In infringement questions, students forget the **"course of trade"** requirement. Infringement under Section 29 is ONLY when the offending mark is used in commercial transactions.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Confusing **absolute grounds (Section 9)** with **relative grounds (Section 11)**. Remember: *Absolute = inherent defect of the mark; Relative = conflict with prior rights of others.*
> - **Pitfall 5:** Not mentioning the **opposition period is 4 months** (not 30 days). This is a heavily tested numerical.
