> - **Pitfall 6:** Writing "Madrid Protocol is part of the Trade Marks Act" — it is an **international treaty**, not a section of the Act. India acceded to it in **2013**.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Golden Points for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

✅ **Trade Marks Act, 1999** came into force on **15th September 2003** and replaced the **Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958**.

✅ **Statutory Definition** is in **Section 2(1)(zb)** — requires **graphical representation** AND **distinctiveness**.

✅ **Two key requirements** for registration: (1) Graphical Representability, (2) Distinctiveness (inherent or acquired).

✅ **Registration Period:** **10 years** from the **date of filing** (NOT date of certificate). Renewable **indefinitely** for 10-year blocks.

✅ **® symbol** = Registered (after registration only). **™ symbol** = Common law claim (no registration needed). **℠ symbol** = Service mark.

✅ **Nice Classification**: **45 classes** (1–34 = Goods, 35–45 = Services). Multi-class filing allowed with surcharge.

✅ **Examination** under **Section 20** → Reply within **30 days** → Publication in **TM Journal** under **Section 21**.

✅ **Opposition Period** = **4 months** from journal publication (Form TM-O).

✅ **Registration Certificate** issued under **Section 23**. Date of registration = Date of filing of application.

✅ **Absolute Grounds (Section 9)** = Inherent defects. **Relative Grounds (Section 11)** = Conflict with prior rights.

✅ **Infringement (Section 29)** = Use of identical/deceptively similar mark in **course of trade** for same/similar goods/services.

✅ **Well-Known Marks (Section 2(1)(zg))** get protection **even across unrelated goods/services** to prevent dilution.

✅ **Service Marks** are registrable for the first time under the 1999 Act.

✅ **Sound, Smell, 3D Shape, Colour** marks are registrable — non-conventional marks.

✅ **Assignment with Goodwill** is allowed. **Assignment without Goodwill (Gross Assignment)** is **prohibited** if it causes deception.

✅ **Non-Use Cancellation (Section 57)** — A registered mark can be removed from the register if it is not used for a continuous period of **5 years** (the famous "use it or lose it" rule).

✅ **India joined the Madrid Protocol in 2013**, allowing international trade mark filing through a single application.

✅ **Penalties (Section 103-111)**: Falsification of register = imprisonment up to **3 years** or fine up to ₹**2,00,000** or both. Falsely representing a mark as registered under Section 107 = fine up to **₹5,000**.

✅ **Appellate Authority** = **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** [now Commercial Courts under the 2020 tribunals reorganization].

✅ **Trans-border reputation** is recognized under **Section 11(6)(b)** — a mark can be protected in India even if used abroad.

✅ **Passing Off** is a **common law remedy** available even to unregistered trade marks. **Infringement** is a **statutory remedy** for registered marks.

✅ **Slogan Marks** like *"Just Do It"* (Nike) are registrable as trade marks.

✅ **Collective Marks** are owned by an association; **Certification Marks** certify quality (e.g., ISI, AGMARK).

✅ **Five Judicial Tests for Deceptively Similar**: Side-by-Side, Overall Impression, Phonetic, Conceptual, Customer Confusion.

✅ **Three Functions of TM**: Identification, Guarantee of Quality, Advertising.

✅ **Office of the Controller General** (CGPDTM) under **DPIIT, Ministry of Commerce & Industry** administers the Act.

✅ **Five TM Registry Offices in India**: Mumbai (Head), Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, Ahmedabad.

---

🎯 **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Trade Marks Act 1999**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Meaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** This section establishes the foundational definitions. Examiners frequently allot **3-mark direct questions** asking for precise statutory and academic definitions. Memorize the verbatim phrasing.

## 1.1 What is "Meaning" in the Context of Module 4?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PESFT757 – Intellectual Property Rights**, Module 4 typically addresses **Trade Secrets and Confidential Information / Protection of undisclosed information**. The topic *"Meaning"* requires the student to articulate the precise legal, statutory, and academic definitions of the core subject matter covered in this module.

The **"Meaning"** of a legal concept in IPR is not merely a dictionary definition. It is a multi-layered construct comprising:

1. **Lay / General Meaning** — The everyday understanding of the term.
2. **Statutory Meaning** — The definition provided under the relevant Indian statute.
3. **International / Treaty Meaning** — The definition under multilateral treaties (TRIPS, Paris Convention, etc.).
4. **Judicial Meaning** — The interpretation evolved through case law and precedent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A 3-mark question on "Meaning" expects a **layered answer**: Begin with the general meaning, follow with the statutory meaning (cite the Act & Section), and conclude with judicial interpretation. A single-line definition loses marks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe in Your Grandma's Kitchen"

Imagine your grandmother has a **secret recipe** for a specific pickle that has been in your family for three generations. The recipe is not written down in a public cookbook, it is not registered with any government, and no patent protects it. Yet, the family jealously guards it. If a competitor steals the recipe by bribing a worker, the family can still take legal action.

This everyday scenario captures the essence of **Trade Secrets / Undisclosed Information**:

- The recipe = the **trade secret**.
- The secrecy maintained by the family = the **reasonable steps** required under the law.
- The legal recourse against the thief = the **protection against breach of confidence**.

Just as the recipe has economic value because it gives the family a competitive edge, **trade secrets are protected because they confer commercial advantage**, not because they are registered or published.

## 1.3 The Statutory Anchor in India

In India, trade secrets are **not protected by a dedicated statute** like the USA's *Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA)* or the *Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA, 2016)*. Instead, they are safeguarded through:

- **The Indian Contract Act, 1872** (Sections 27, 73, 74 — dealing with agreements in restraint of trade, compensation for breach, and compensation for breach of contract).
- **The Information Technology Act, 2000** (Sections 43A, 72, 72A — dealing with data privacy and unauthorized access).
- **Common Law principles of Breach of Confidence / Breach of Trust.**
- **The Copyright Act, 1957** (for certain compilations and databases).
- **The Patents Act, 1970** (Section 3 — what is *not* patentable, indirectly pushing innovations into trade secret protection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Statutory Benchmark — TRIPS Agreement, Article 39:**
> TRIPS defines **"undisclosed information"** as information that:
> 1. Is **secret** in the sense that it is not, as a body or in the precise configuration and assembly of its components, generally known among or readily accessible to persons within the circles that normally deal with the kind of information in question.
> 2. Has **commercial value** because it is secret.
> 3. Has been **subject to reasonable steps** under the circumstances, by the person lawfully in control of the information, to keep it secret.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Meaning

| Pillar | Keyword | Essence |
|---|---|---|
| **Secrecy** | *Not generally known* | Information must not be in the public domain. |
| **Value** | *Commercial worth* | The secret must confer an economic or competitive edge. |
| **Effort** | *Reasonable steps* | The owner must demonstrate active measures to maintain confidentiality. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam — "S-V-E":** **S**ecrecy, **V**alue, **E**ffort. If any of these three pillars is missing, the information loses its trade secret status.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the "Meaning" of Trade Secrets / Undisclosed Information

The conceptual meaning can be broken down into **five operative layers**, each of which is independently testable in the KTU examination:

### Layer 1: Subjective Meaning (What does it *feel* like?)
- Information that the holder *wants* to keep private.
- Examples: Coca-Cola's formula, Google's search algorithm, a chef's signature dish recipe.

### Layer 2: Objective Meaning (What *is* it legally?)
- Information meeting the **three-pronged TRIPS test** (secrecy + value + reasonable steps).
- Capable of being communicated, stored, or transmitted in tangible or intangible form.

### Layer 3: Functional Meaning (What *does* it do?)
- It provides a **competitive advantage** in the marketplace.
- It enables the holder to derive economic benefit without public disclosure.

### Layer 4: Protective Meaning (How is it *guarded*?)
- Through **contractual obligations** (NDAs, employment contracts, licensing terms).
- Through **physical and digital security** (locked cabinets, encryption, access controls).
- Through **legal action** under contract law, tort law, and IT law for breach.

### Layer 5: Negative Meaning (What is it *not*?)
- It is **not a patent** — it is not registered, published, or time-limited.
- It is **not public knowledge** — once revealed, protection is lost forever.
- It is **not a trademark** — it does not identify the source of goods.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Three Pillars

| Pillar | Why It Exists | How It Operates |
|---|---|---|
| **Secrecy** | Public information is a *public good* — once disclosed, it cannot be "un-disclosed." | The holder must affirmatively restrict access. Mere labeling as "secret" is insufficient if information is freely available. |
| **Commercial Value** | The law protects economic interests, not mere curiosities. | The information must enable the holder to gain a competitive edge, save R&D costs, or maintain market position. |
| **Reasonable Steps** | Prevents frivolous claims of secrecy over trivial or well-known facts. | Courts examine whether the holder took *objective, demonstrable* measures (NDAs, locked files, limited circulation). |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Content Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Below is the consolidated reference table for the topic "Meaning" — memorize it for ESE and internal assessments.**

| Term | Definition (Statutory / Academic) | Source | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trade Secret** | Information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. | TRIPS Art. 39; Common Law | **Most tested term** — used in 3-mark and 14-mark questions. |
| **Undisclosed Information** | Information that is secret, has commercial value, and is subject to reasonable steps to maintain secrecy. | TRIPS Art. 39(2) | Distinguished from "trade secret" — TRIPS uses the broader term. |
| **Confidential Information** | Non-public information disclosed in a relationship of confidence, where the recipient knows or ought to know it is confidential. | Common Law; Indian Contract Act | Often used interchangeably with trade secret in Indian jurisprudence. |
| **Reasonable Steps** | Objective measures taken by the holder to maintain secrecy — includes NDAs, restricted access, encryption, marking documents. | TRIPS Art. 39(2)(c) | Tested via case laws (*John Richard Brady v. Chemical Process Equip. P. Ltd.*). |
| **Breach of Confidence** | Unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information by a party who knows or ought to know the information is confidential. | Common Law; Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Foundation for civil action in India. |
| **NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement)** | A contractual undertaking by which a party agrees not to disclose specified information. | Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Practical tool — frequently cited in answers. |
| **Know-How** | Practical technical knowledge, experience, or skill not in the public domain, often transferred via licensing. | TRIPS; WIPO | Often paired with trade secrets. |

## 2.4 Engineering & Industry Utility

The "Meaning" of trade secrets is not abstract — it has direct, tangible relevance in:

- **Pharmaceutical R&D:** Clinical trial data, formulation processes.
- **Software Engineering:** Source code, proprietary algorithms, AI model weights.
- **Manufacturing:** Process parameters, supplier lists, quality control data.
- **Food & Beverage Industry:** Recipes, processing methods (Coca-Cola, KFC).
- **Aerospace & Defense:** Design specifications, material compositions.
- **Automotive Engineering:** EV battery chemistries, proprietary CAD designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** India's pharmaceutical industry, which exports generics to over 200 countries, relies heavily on **process know-how** as a trade secret. The Supreme Court in *F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd. v. Cipla Ltd.* (2015) examined the boundary between patent disclosure and trade secret protection extensively.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Analytical Framework: The Test for "Meaning"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Analytical Note:** The "meaning" of a trade secret is not declared — it is *determined* through a three-pronged judicial test. Below is the step-by-step analytical framework examiners expect in 14-mark questions.

## 3.1 The Three-Pronged Test — Step-by-Step Application

A piece of information is legally a **trade secret** only if it satisfies all three prongs. The following derivation-style framework illustrates how courts and examiners evaluate each prong.

### Step 1: Establish Secrecy (The "Not Generally Known" Test)

$$
S = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } I \notin D_{public} \\ 0 & \text{if } I \in D_{public} \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Secrecy status (1 = secret, 0 = public)
- $I$ = The information in question
- $D_{public}$ = The set of publicly available or generally known information

**Interpretation:** If $I$ can be found in public literature, industry handbooks, trade publications, or is otherwise readily accessible to persons within the relevant circles, then $S = 0$ and the information **fails the secrecy test**.

### Step 2: Establish Commercial Value (The "Economic Edge" Test)

$$
V = \Delta P \cdot Q_{market}
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Commercial value of the secret
- $\Delta P$ = Price premium or cost advantage gained by using the secret
- $Q_{market}$ = Quantity over which the advantage is realized

**Interpretation:** The secret must confer a measurable economic benefit. Pure scientific curiosity, without commercial application, fails this test.

### Step 3: Establish Reasonable Steps (The "Objective Measures" Test)

$$
R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot M_i
$$

Where:
- $R$ = Reasonable-steps score
- $M_i$ = Individual measure employed (NDA, encryption, locked access, employee training, etc.)
- $w_i$ = Weight assigned to that measure by the court
- $n$ = Total number of measures

**Interpretation:** If $R \geq R_{threshold}$ (the judicial threshold for "reasonable"), the test is satisfied. Courts examine whether the totality of measures, judged objectively, demonstrates genuine intent to maintain secrecy.

### Final Verdict

$$
\text{Trade Secret Status} = S \cdot V \cdot R
$$

**Logical interpretation:** The AND condition — secrecy AND value AND reasonable steps. If *any* factor is zero, the status is zero. The information is only a trade secret when **all three prongs are simultaneously satisfied**.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style Analytical Problem)

**Problem:** *A startup in Kochi develops a novel water purification algorithm. The algorithm is documented in a shared Google Drive accessible to all 50 employees. The startup has not signed NDAs. The algorithm gives the startup a 30% cost advantage. Is this a trade secret?*

**Solution (applying the three-pronged test):**

| Prong | Test | Result | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Secrecy (S)** | Is it generally known? | **FAIL** | Shared Google Drive with 50 employees, no access controls — effectively public within the company. |
| **Value (V)** | Does it confer economic edge? | **PASS** | 30% cost advantage is substantial commercial value. |
| **Reasonable Steps (R)** | Were objective measures taken? | **FAIL** | No NDAs, no access restrictions, no encryption. |

**Verdict:** $\text{Trade Secret Status} = 1 \cdot 0 \cdot 0 = 0$. The algorithm is **not** a trade secret under the TRIPS framework, despite having commercial value, because secrecy and reasonable steps are absent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Takeaway:** This example illustrates that the "meaning" of a trade secret is not a static label — it is the *outcome* of a multi-factor test. KTU examiners reward answers that apply the test dynamically rather than reciting definitions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagram: The Anatomy of "Meaning"

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Layered Meaning of Trade Secrets / Undisclosed Information

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MEANING OF TRADE SECRET"] --> B["LAY MEANING"]
    A --> C["STATUTORY MEANING"]
    A --> D["INTERNATIONAL MEANING"]
    A --> E["JUDICIAL MEANING"]

    B --> B1["Everyday understanding of secret business info"]
    B --> B2["Example: Recipe, formula, process"]

    C --> C1["Indian Contract Act 1872"]
    C --> C2["IT Act 2000"]
    C --> C3["Common Law of Breach of Confidence"]

    D --> D1["TRIPS Article 39"]
    D --> D2["Paris Convention"]
    D --> D3["WIPO Model Provisions"]

    E --> E1["Case: John Richard Brady vs Chemical Process"]
    E --> E2["Case: F Hoffmann La Roche vs Cipla"]

    C1 --> F["PROTECTION MECHANISMS"]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F

    F --> F1["Contractual NDAs"]
    F --> F2["Civil Action for Breach"]
    F --> F3["Criminal Action under IT Act"]
    F --> F4["Equitable Injunctions"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Three-Pronged Test (Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Evaluate Information I"] --> P1{"Is I generally known?"}

    P1 -- YES --> Fail1["FAIL: Not Secret"]
    P1 -- NO --> P2{"Does I have commercial value?"}

    P2 -- NO --> Fail2["FAIL: No Economic Edge"]
    P2 -- YES --> P3{"Were reasonable steps taken?"}

    P3 -- NO --> Fail3["FAIL: Inadequate Protection"]
    P3 -- YES --> Pass["TRADE SECRET CONFIRMED"]

    Pass --> Out1["Legal Remedies Available"]
    Out1 --> Out2["Injunction"]
    Out1 --> Out3["Damages"]
    Out1 --> Out4["Account of Profits"]
    Out1 --> Out5["Criminal Prosecution IT Act"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Distinction Between Related Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY FAMILY"] --> B["Registered IP"]
    A --> C["Unregistered IP"]

    B --> B1["Patents"]
    B --> B2["Trademarks"]
    B --> B3["Designs"]
    B --> B4["Geographical Indications"]

    C --> C1["Copyright Automatic"]
    C --> C2["Trade Secrets"]
    C --> C3["Know How"]

    C2 --> D1["Secret by Nature"]
    C2 --> D2["Value by Use"]
    C2 --> D3["Protected by Effort"]
    C2 --> D4["Indefinite Duration"]
    C2 --> D5["No Registration Required"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The "Meaning" of trade secrets is best understood by contrast. Unlike patents (registered, time-limited, published), trade secrets are **unregistered, indefinite in duration, and protected by the holder's own efforts**. This structural distinction is a frequent **14-mark** question in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A = 3 marks each. Part B = 14 marks each (with internal choice). Answer all questions with specific reference to statutory provisions and case law for full marks.

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct / Short Answer)

### Question 1
**"Define trade secret and state the three essential conditions for information to qualify as a trade secret under the TRIPS Agreement."** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **trade secret** is information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that (i) is not generally known among, or readily accessible to, persons within circles that normally deal with such information, and (ii) derives independent economic value from not being publicly known.

Under **TRIPS Article 39(2)**, information must satisfy three conditions:

1. **Secrecy** — It must not be generally known or readily accessible to relevant persons.
2. **Commercial Value** — It must have economic value precisely because it is secret.
3. **Reasonable Steps** — The holder must have taken reasonable measures to keep it confidential.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only the general definition and omit the three TRIPS conditions. This results in a **1.5-mark loss**. Always cite **Article 39(2)** and list all three prongs explicitly.

---

### Question 2
**"What is the difference between 'trade secret' and 'undisclosed information' as understood in Indian IP jurisprudence?"** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Trade Secret | Undisclosed Information |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of Term** | Common law, US jurisprudence | TRIPS Agreement, Article 39 |
| **Scope** | Narrower — refers to commercial/industrial secrets | Broader — covers scientific, technical, and business information |
| **Statutory Basis in India** | Not defined in any Indian statute | India obligated under TRIPS to protect |
| **Practical Application** | Marketing strategies, formulas, customer lists | Clinical trial data, test results, R&D data |
| **Remedies** | Breach of confidence, contract law | Breach of confidence, IT Act, contractual remedies |

In essence, **undisclosed information** is the broader international term adopted under TRIPS, while **trade secret** is the more commonly used term in commercial practice and US-derived jurisprudence. In India, both are protected through the same common law and contractual frameworks.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the statutory and judicial framework for the protection of trade secrets in India. Discuss the relevant provisions of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and the Information Technology Act, 2000. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the three-pronged test under **TRIPS Article 39** for determining whether information qualifies as undisclosed information. Illustrate your answer with decided case law. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Unlike the USA, India does not have a dedicated trade secret statute. Protection flows from a combination of statutory provisions, common law, and equitable remedies.

**Indian Contract Act, 1872 (3 marks):**
- **Section 27** — Agreements in restraint of trade are void, *except* those involving trade secrets. This carve-out allows reasonable post-employment restrictions to protect confidential information.
- **Section 73** — Compensation for loss or damage caused by breach of contract — applied when an employee violates NDA terms.
- **Section 74** — Compensation for breach of contract where penalty is stipulated — enforces liquidated damages clauses in NDAs.

**Information Technology Act, 2000 (2 marks):**
- **Section 43A** — Compensation for failure to protect data by a body corporate — applicable to digital trade secrets.
- **Section 72** — Penalty for breach of confidentiality by any person — punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to ₹1 lakh, or both.
- **Section 72A** — Punishment for disclosure of information in breach of lawful contract — imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹5 lakhs.

**Common Law and Equity (1 mark):**
- The doctrine of **breach of confidence** — three elements: (i) information of a confidential nature, (ii) communicated in circumstances importing an obligation of confidence, (iii) unauthorized use causing detriment.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**TRIPS Article 39(2) — The Three-Pronged Test (3 marks):**

The test comprises:
1. **Secrecy** — The information is not generally known or readily accessible.
2. **Commercial Value** — The information has economic value precisely because it is secret.
3. **Reasonable Steps** — The holder has taken objective measures to maintain confidentiality.

**Case Law Illustrations (3 marks):**

- ***John Richard Brady v. Chemical Process Equipments Pvt. Ltd.*** (Delhi HC, 1986) — The court recognized trade secret protection in India and held that information shared in a confidential relationship cannot be used by the recipient for unauthorized purposes. **Key takeaway:** Confidentiality can be inferred from circumstances.

- ***F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd. v. Cipla Ltd.*** (Bombay HC, 2015) — The court examined whether clinical trial data submitted to drug regulators could be protected. The court held that data exclusivity is a *form* of trade secret protection under TRIPS Article 39. **Key takeaway:** Even regulatory data can qualify as undisclosed information.

- ***Zee Telefilms Ltd. v. Sundial Communications Pvt. Ltd.*** (Bombay HC, 2003) — The court granted an injunction to prevent the misuse of confidential information related to a television show format. **Key takeaway:** Entertainment formats can be trade secrets.

**Critical Analysis (1 mark):**
The three-pronged test provides a robust framework but suffers from subjectivity in assessing "reasonable steps." Indian courts have applied the test pragmatically, balancing the interests of innovation, employee mobility, and public welfare.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, students frequently lose marks by: (i) failing to cite **specific sections** of the Indian Contract Act and IT Act, (ii) omitting **case law**, and (iii) not providing a **critical analysis** rather than a mere description. A balanced answer must include *introduction, statutory provisions, case law, and conclusion*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Define the term "confidential information" and explain how it differs from a "trade secret." Discuss the role of Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) in protecting such information. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the international framework for the protection of undisclosed information. Compare the approaches of the USA, EU, and India. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition of Confidential Information (2 marks):**
**Confidential information** is non-public information disclosed in a relationship of trust or confidence, where the recipient knows or ought to reasonably know that the information is confidential. It encompasses a broader range of data than trade secrets, including personal data, business strategies, and unpublished research.

**Difference Between Confidential Information and Trade Secret (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Confidential Information | Trade Secret |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Broader — includes personal, business, technical | Narrower — strictly commercial/industrial |
| **Value Requirement** | No strict commercial value test | Must have commercial value |
| **Reasonable Steps** | Inferred from relationship of trust | Must be demonstrably proven |
| **Typical Use** | Employment, consulting, licensing | Manufacturing, R&D, formulation |

**Role of NDAs (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** A Non-Disclosure Agreement is a legally binding contract in which one or more parties agree not to disclose specified information.
- **Key Clauses:** Definition of confidential information, exclusions (publicly known, independently developed, lawfully obtained from third parties), term of confidentiality, remedies (injunction, damages), and governing law.
- **Indian Law:** NDAs are governed by the Indian Contract Act, 1872. They must satisfy the requirements of a valid contract (offer, acceptance, consideration, lawful object).
- **Enforceability:** In *Niranjan Shankar Golikari v. Century Spinning & Manufacturing Co. Ltd.* (1967), the Supreme Court upheld post-employment NDAs as enforceable restraints protecting trade secrets.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**International Framework (2 marks):**
- **TRIPS Article 39** — The first multilateral treaty to mandate protection of undisclosed information. Members must protect information that is secret, valuable, and subject to reasonable steps.
- **Paris Convention** — Earlier treaty, Article 10bis on unfair competition, indirectly protects trade secrets against acts of industrial espionage.
- **WIPO Model Provisions** — Non-binding model provisions on protection against unfair competition, including trade secrets.

**Comparative Analysis (4 marks):**

| Jurisdiction | Legal Basis | Key Features | Strengths & Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|
| **USA** | Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) 2016; Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) | Federal civil action; ex parte seizure; whistleblower immunity | **Strong:** Robust federal framework. **Weak:** High litigation costs. |
| **EU** | Directive (EU) 2016/943 on Trade Secrets | Defines trade secrets; remedies for unlawful acquisition; whistleblower protection | **Strong:** Harmonized across member states. **Weak:** Implementation varies. |
| **India** | Indian Contract Act, 1872; IT Act, 2000; Common Law | No dedicated statute; protection through contracts, torts, IT law | **Strong:** Flexible case-by-case approach. **Weak:** No statutory certainty, patchwork protection. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
While the USA and EU have enacted dedicated trade secret legislation providing certainty and uniformity, India relies on a patchwork of statutes and common law principles. The *Protection of Trade Secrets Bill* has been under discussion in India but is yet to be enacted, highlighting a legislative gap that KTU students should be aware of for current-affairs-based questions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** When comparing jurisdictions, students often list features without analyzing strengths/weaknesses. For 14-mark answers, the examiner expects a **comparative table with critical evaluation**, not a mere description.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this section before your exam.**

- ✅ **Trade Secret** = Secret + Value + Reasonable Steps (TRIPS Art. 39).
- ✅ **No dedicated statute in India** — protection via Contract Act, IT Act, Common Law.
- ✅ **Key Sections:** Indian Contract Act — S. 27, 73, 74 | IT Act — S. 43A, 72, 72A.
- ✅ **TRIPS Article 39** is the international anchor — three-pronged test is universally applicable.
- ✅ **Mnemonic "S-V-E"** = Secrecy, Value, Effort (Reasonable Steps).
- ✅ **Key Case Laws:**
  - *John Richard Brady v. Chemical Process Equipments* — Established trade secret protection in India.
  - *F. Hoffmann-La Roche v. Cipla* — Data exclusivity is a form of trade secret protection.
  - *Zee Telefilms v. Sundial* — Entertainment formats as trade secrets.
  - *Niranjan Shankar Golikari v. Century Spinning* — Enforceability of post-employment NDAs.
- ✅ **Difference between trade secret and patent:** Trade secret = indefinite, unregistered, secret. Patent = 20 years, registered, published.
- ✅ **Difference between trade secret and confidential information:** Confidential information is broader; trade secret is a subset with strict commercial-value and reasonable-steps requirements.
- ✅ **NDAs** are governed by the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and must satisfy the essentials of a valid contract.
- ✅ **Comparative law:** USA (DTSA 2016) and EU (Directive 2016/943) have dedicated statutes; India does not.
- ✅ **The "Meaning" of trade secrets is multi-layered:** Lay → Statutory → International (TRIPS) → Judicial.
- ✅ **Killer 1-liner for any answer:** *"A trade secret is not a right created by law — it is a right protected by law against breach of confidence."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy for the "Meaning" Topic:**
> 1. Always begin with a **layered definition** (general + statutory + international).
> 2. Always cite **at least one Section number** and **one case law**.
> 3. For 14-mark answers, use a **comparative table** to showcase structured thinking.
> 4. Conclude with a **one-line "killer statement"** that synthesizes the meaning.
> 5. Time allocation: 3-mark question = 3–4 minutes | 14-mark question = 12–14 minutes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of IPR Registration

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Registration** in the context of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) is the **statutory, formal, and administrative process** by which an applicant's claim to a particular intellectual property right (Patent, Trademark, Design, Geographical Indication, etc.) is officially recorded on a public register maintained by the competent government authority (e.g., the Indian Patent Office under the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks, or the International Bureau under WIPO), thereby conferring **exclusive, enforceable, and time-bound rights** upon the registered proprietor.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, registration is the **operative act** that converts a mere *inchoate* (incomplete) right into a *perfected* legal right capable of being asserted in a court of law. Without registration (where mandatory), the right cannot be enforced against third-party infringers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Land Registry"**
> Think of registration like registering a plot of land at the Sub-Registrar's office. You may physically possess a plot of land (similar to inventing something or using a brand name), but until you *register* it in your name in the government's record, you cannot:
> 1. Sell it legally,
> 2. Sue a trespasser, or
> 3. Get a bank loan against it.
>
> **IPR Registration works exactly the same way.** Until you formally register your patent, trademark, or design with the government registry, you generally cannot sue infringers or transfer the right cleanly to a buyer. The **Registration Certificate** is the legal "title deed" for your intangible intellectual asset.

## 1.3 Critical KTU Syllabus Highlight — Which IPRs Require Registration?

| Type of IPR | Registration Mandatory? | Term of Protection (if registered) |
|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | **YES** (Under Patents Act, 1970) | **20 years** from date of filing |
| **Trademark** | **YES** (Under Trade Marks Act, 1999) | **10 years**, renewable indefinitely |
| **Industrial Design** | **YES** (Under Designs Act, 2000) | **10 years**, extendable to **15 years** |
| **Geographical Indication (GI)** | **YES** (Under GI of Goods Act, 1999) | **10 years**, renewable |
| **Copyright** | **NO** (Automatic upon creation) | Life + **60 years** (literary); 60 years (films/sound recordings) |
| **Trade Secret** | **NO** (Protected via confidentiality) | Indefinite (as long as secret is maintained) |
| **Plant Variety** | **YES** (Under PPVFR Act, 2001) | **18 years** (trees/vines); **15 years** (others); **15 years** (extant varieties) |

## 1.4 Why Registration is the "Heart" of IPR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Registration transforms three things simultaneously:
> 1. **Legal standing** — enables the owner to sue.
> 2. **Territorial exclusivity** — protection is granted country-by-country.
> 3. **Public Notice (Constructive Notice)** — the world is deemed to have been informed of your claim once it appears on the public register.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Registration — Four Universal Pillars

Regardless of the specific IPR being registered, **every KTU examinable registration system** rests upon the following four pillars:

### Pillar 1: Application (Form & Fees)
- A prescribed application form is filed with the competent Registry.
- Fees are paid (online/offline) as per the **First Schedule** of the relevant Act.
- Applicant must be the rightful claimant (true and first inventor for patents; proprietor for trademarks; producer for GIs).

### Pillar 2: Examination
- The Registry conducts a substantive and/or formal examination.
- For patents: Novelty, Inventive Step, Industrial Application are checked.
- For trademarks: Distinctiveness, non-descriptiveness, non-deceptiveness.
- For designs: Novelty, originality, not contrary to public order/morality.

### Pillar 3: Publication & Opposition
- The application is **published in the official journal / e-gazette**.
- A **third-party opposition window** is opened (typically **4 months** under the Patents Act, 1970; **3 months** under the Trade Marks Act, 1999).

### Pillar 4: Registration & Certificate
- Upon overcoming opposition and satisfying all requirements, the right is **entered on the Register**.
- A **Registration Certificate** is issued — this is the conclusive proof of title.

## 2.2 International Registration Systems (High KTU Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests **three international registration mechanisms** managed by WIPO (World Intellectual Property Organization):

| System | Governed By | Purpose | India's Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PCT** (Patent Cooperation Treaty) | WIPO, 1970 | Single international patent application seeking protection in multiple countries | India is a **Contracting State** since **7th December 1998** |
| **Madrid Protocol** | WIPO, 1989 | International registration of trademarks across member nations | India acceded on **8th July 2013** |
| **Hague System** | WIPO, 1999 (Geneva Act) | International registration of industrial designs | India became a member on **13th December 2017** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbol / Notation | Boundary Condition | Practical Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patent Term** | $T_p = 20 \text{ years}$ | From date of **filing** (not grant) | Calculates exclusivity window |
| **Trademark Term** | $T_{tm} = 10 \text{ years}$ | Renewable; **indefinite** if renewed every 10 yrs | Brand perpetuity |
| **Design Term** | $T_d = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ years}$ | Initial 10 yrs + one extension of 5 yrs | Industrial design life |
| **GI Term** | $T_{gi} = 10 \text{ years}$ | Renewable | Community rights |
| **Copyright (Literary)** | $T_c = \text{Life of author} + 60 \text{ years}$ | Post-mortem calculation | Author's estate planning |
| **Opposition Window (Patent)** | $\Delta t_{opp} = 4 \text{ months}$ | From publication date | Strategic litigation timing |
| **Opposition Window (Trademark)** | $\Delta t_{opp}^{tm} = 3 \text{ months}$ | (or 1 month for TM) | Brand protection horizon |
| **PCT National Phase Entry** | $\Delta t_{PCT} = 30/31 \text{ months}$ | From priority date | Filing deadline |
| **Renewal Cycle (Patent)** | Annual | From **3rd year** onwards | Maintenance fees |
| **Priority Date** | $D_{pri}$ | Date of first filing | Establishes seniority globally |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **"Date of Filing"** with **"Date of Priority"** for patents. The **priority date** (the date of the earliest application whose priority is claimed) is the legally relevant date for assessing novelty against prior art.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

1. **Patent Registration** → Engineers protect novel machinery, software algorithms (technical solutions), or chemical processes. Example: **Bosch** patents fuel injection systems.
2. **Trademark Registration** → Companies register logos, brand names (e.g., **TATA**, **Apple**).
3. **Design Registration** → Aesthetic shape of a smartphone, handle of a tool.
4. **GI Registration** → "**Darjeeling Tea**", "**Kancheepuram Silk**" — protects regional engineering/agricultural products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Patent Registration Procedure (India — Patents Act, 1970)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked procedural question** in KTU examinations. Memorize the chronology.

**Step 1: Filing of Application (Section 7 / 54 of Patents Act, 1970)**
Applicant files Form 1 (Application for Grant of Patent), Form 2 (Provisional or Complete Specification), Form 3 (Statement of Undertaking), Form 5 (Declaration of Inventorship) along with prescribed fees.

**Step 2: Publication of Application (Section 11A)**
The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the date of filing or priority date, whichever is earlier. However, the applicant may request **early publication** using Form 12.

**Step 3: Request for Examination (Section 11B)**
The applicant must file a Request for Examination in **Form 18** within **48 months** from the priority date. If not filed, the application is deemed to be abandoned.

**Step 4: Examination by Controller (Section 12)**
The Controller examines the application for:
- Novelty (Section 2(1)(j))
- Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja))
- Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))
- Non-exclusion under Sections 3 & 4

**Step 5: First Examination Report (FER) Issued**
The examiner issues the **First Examination Report (FER)** citing objections. Applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable to 12 months with fees).

**Step 6: Opposition (Section 25)**
Opposition can be filed:
- **Pre-grant opposition** — by any person after publication until grant (under Section 25(1)).
- **Post-grant opposition** — within **12 months** of grant (under Section 25(2)).

**Step 7: Grant of Patent (Section 43)**
If all objections are overcome, the patent is granted. The **date of grant** is the date on which the patent is entered in the Register.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Date Arithmetic for Patent Term

The standard term of a patent is **20 years** from the date of filing. Let us derive the **Date of Expiry** mathematically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{filing}} &= \text{Date of filing of complete specification} \\
D_{\text{priority}} &= \text{Date of earliest priority claimed} \\
T_{\text{term}} &= 20 \text{ years} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **Date of Expiry** is:

$$
D_{\text{expiry}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A complete specification is filed on **15th March 2020**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{expiry}} &= 15\text{-}03\text{-}2020 + 20 \text{ years} \\
&= 15\text{-}03\text{-}2040
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Logical Conversion:** The 20-year clock starts on the *filing date of the complete specification*, not the priority date and not the publication date. If only a provisional was filed first, the **provisional filing date** is treated as the priority date for novelty assessment, but the **complete specification filing date** is the start of the 20-year term.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Trademark Registration Procedure (India — Trade Marks Act, 1999)

**Step 1: Trademark Search**
A search is conducted in the **TM Public Search** portal to verify availability.

**Step 2: Filing of Application (Section 18)**
Application filed in **Form TM-A** with the Registrar of Trademarks. Applicant may claim priority under the Paris Convention.

**Step 3: Vienna Codification**
The Registrar classifies the figurative elements using the **Vienna Classification** system.

**Step 4: Examination (Section 19)**
The Registrar examines for:
- Distinctiveness (Section 9)
- Non-descriptiveness (Section 9(1))
- Non-deceptiveness (Section 9(2))
- Not barred under Section 9 (absolute grounds) and Section 11 (relative grounds)

**Step 5: Acceptance & Publication (Section 20)**
If accepted, the mark is advertised in the **Trademark Journal**.

**Step 6: Opposition (Section 21)**
Any aggrieved person may file opposition within **3 months** (extendable by 1 month) from the date of publication.

**Step 7: Registration (Section 23)**
If no opposition or opposition fails, the mark is registered. The **date of registration** is the date of filing of the application (this is critical — registration is backdated).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Madrid Protocol International Filing

For international trademark registration under the **Madrid Protocol**, the steps and time-lines are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A} &: \text{File at national Office of Origin (India)} \rightarrow \text{Form MM2} \\
\text{Step B} &: \text{International Bureau (IB, Geneva) registers} \rightarrow \text{Within 12-18 months} \\
\text{Step C} &: \text{Designated national offices examine} \rightarrow \text{12 months to refuse (otherwise binding consent)} \\
\text{Step D} &: \text{International registration valid} \rightarrow \text{10 years, renewable} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Madrid Protocol** provides a critical advantage:
- **18-month examination deadline** by each designated Contracting Party.
- If no refusal is notified within 18 months, the trademark stands protected as if registered nationally.

## 3.5 Geographical Indication (GI) Registration — Special Considerations

A GI is registered under the **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999**. Key features:

1. **Application by Association of Producers/Authorized Users** (Section 11).
2. **Statement of Case** — details the goods, quality, reputation, geographical area, and proof of origin.
3. **Registration confers** — the right to prevent third parties from using the GI on goods not originating from the registered territory.
4. **Term** — Registration is for **10 years**, renewable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Unlike a patent or trademark, a GI is a **community right**, not a private right. The applicant MUST be a group of producers, an organization, or an authority representing the producers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Flowcharts)

## 4.1 Patent Registration Process — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Inventor Files Form 1, 2, 3, 5 with Patent Office] --> B[Step 2: Application Number Allotted]
    B --> C{Step 3: 18 Month Deadline}
    C -->|Default| D[Automatic Publication in Patent Journal]
    C -->|Early Request Form 12| D
    D --> E[Step 4: Applicant Files Form 18 Request for Examination]
    E --> F[Step 5: FER Issued by Examiner]
    F --> G[Step 6: Applicant Files Reply within 6 Months]
    G --> H{Step 7: Objections Overcome?}
    H -->|No| X[Application Abandoned]
    H -->|Yes| I[Step 8: Grant of Patent under Section 43]
    I --> J[Step 9: Opposition Window 12 Months Post Grant]
    J --> K[Step 10: Patent Valid for 20 Years from Filing Date]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style K fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style X fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

## 4.2 Trademark Registration — Opposition & Acceptance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Applicant Files Form TM A with Registrar] --> B[Step 2: Vienna Codification of Figurative Marks]
    B --> C[Step 3: Examination under Section 19]
    C --> D{Section 9 Absolute Grounds}
    D -->|Distinctive Not Found| X1[Refused and Advertised as such]
    D -->|Distinctive and Non Descriptive| E[Step 4: Accepted and Advertised in TM Journal]
    E --> F[Step 5: 3 Month Opposition Window under Section 21]
    F --> G{Opposition Filed?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Step 6: Opposition Heard by Registrar]
    H --> I{Decision}
    I -->|Opposition Succeeds| X2[Mark Refused]
    I -->|Opposition Fails| J[Step 7: Mark Registered under Section 23]
    G -->|No| J
    J --> K[Step 8: Registration Certificate Issued, Valid 10 Years]
    K --> L[Step 9: Renewal Every 10 Years Indefinitely]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style K fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style X1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style X2 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

## 4.3 International Registration — Madrid Protocol Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Indian Applicant File MM2 at Indian IP Office] --> B[Indian Office Forwards to WIPO IB Geneva]
    B --> C[IB Conducts Formal Examination]
    C --> D{Registered in International Register?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Notification to Designated Contracting Parties]
    D -->|No| X[Application Refused]
    E --> F[Each Designated Office 12 to 18 Months to Refuse]
    F --> G{Refusal Issued?}
    G -->|Yes| H[National Refusal Proceedings]
    G -->|No| I[Deemed Registered Nationally]
    I --> J[Protection for 10 Years Renewable]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style J fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style X fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Rights Conferred by Registration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[IPR Registration on Public Register] --> R1[Exclusive Right to Use Exclude Others]
    R --> R2[Right to Assign and License]
    R --> R3[Right to Sue for Infringement]
    R --> R4[Right to Seek Damages and Injunction]
    R --> R5[Constructive Notice to Public]
    R --> R6[Right to Customs Recordal for Border Protection]

    style R fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style R2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style R3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style R4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style R5 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style R6 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**

### Q1. What is meant by registration of a trademark? State the duration of registration. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Registration of a trademark is the formal procedure under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** whereby an applicant's mark is entered in the **Register of Trade Marks** maintained by the Registrar. Upon registration, the proprietor is granted the **exclusive right to use** the mark in relation to the goods or services for which it is registered, and the right to sue for infringement under Section 29.

**Duration:** A registered trademark is protected for a period of **10 years** from the date of filing of the application. It can be **renewed indefinitely** for successive periods of 10 years upon payment of the prescribed renewal fees.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for statutory reference, 1 mark for duration & renewal.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between registration of a patent and registration of a copyright. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Patent | Copyright |
|---|---|---|
| **Mandatory?** | YES — registration is mandatory | NO — copyright arises automatically on creation |
| **Statute** | Patents Act, 1970 | Copyright Act, 1957 |
| **Term** | 20 years from filing date | Life + 60 years (literary works) |
| **What is registered** | Inventions (technical solutions) | Original literary, artistic, musical works |
| **Authority** | Controller General of Patents | Copyright Office (Registrar of Copyrights) |
| **Infringement standing** | Only registered proprietor can sue | Owner can sue even without registration (registration is prima facie evidence) |

*(1 mark for patent column, 1 mark for copyright column, 1 mark for differentiation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> In Part B questions on Registration procedures, students frequently **omit the statutory section numbers** (e.g., Section 25 for opposition, Section 43 for grant) and **miss the time-lines** (e.g., 18 months, 48 months, 3 months). Always cite BOTH the section AND the time-limit. Failure to do so results in a loss of **1.5 to 2 marks per sub-part**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Type]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for registration of a patent** in India under the Patents Act, 1970. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty)** system and explain how an Indian applicant can use it for international patent protection. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The procedure for registration of a patent in India is governed by the **Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2005):

**Step 1 — Filing of Application (Sections 7, 54):**
The applicant files the following with the appropriate Patent Office:
- **Form 1** — Application for grant of patent.
- **Form 2** — Provisional or complete specification.
- **Form 3** — Statement and undertaking regarding foreign applications.
- **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship.
Payment of the prescribed fees as per the **First Schedule**.

**Step 2 — Publication (Section 11A):**
The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the date of filing or priority date. The applicant may request **early publication** using Form 12.

**Step 3 — Request for Examination (Section 11B):**
The applicant (or any third party) must file **Form 18** within **48 months** from the priority date. Failing this, the application is deemed abandoned.

**Step 4 — Examination (Section 12) & First Examination Report (FER):**
The Controller examines for novelty (Sec 2(1)(j)), inventive step (Sec 2(1)(ja)), and industrial application (Sec 2(1)(ac)). The FER is issued, and the applicant must reply within **6 months** (extendable to 12 months).

**Step 5 — Opposition (Section 25):**
- **Pre-grant opposition** — by any person after publication till grant (Section 25(1)).
- **Post-grant opposition** — within **12 months** from the date of grant (Section 25(2)).

**Step 6 — Grant (Section 43):**
If objections are overcome and no successful opposition, the patent is granted and entered in the **Register of Patents**.

**Step 7 — Term:**
The patent is valid for **20 years** from the date of filing of the complete specification, subject to payment of annual renewal fees from the 3rd year onwards.

*(Distribution: 1 mark each for Steps 1–6 with section reference; 1 mark for term & renewal.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) — Overview:**

The **PCT**, administered by **WIPO**, is an international treaty signed in **Washington, D.C., in 1970** that allows inventors to seek patent protection simultaneously in multiple countries through a **single international application**.

**India joined the PCT on 7th December 1998.**

**Procedure for an Indian Applicant:**

**Step 1 — International Application:**
The applicant files an international application (Form PCT/RO/101) with the **Indian Patent Office** (acting as Receiving Office — RO/IN) or directly with WIPO's International Bureau (IB) in Geneva.

**Step 2 — International Search (ISR):**
An **International Searching Authority (ISA)** — typically the Indian Patent Office or European Patent Office — conducts a search for prior art and issues an **International Search Report (ISR)** and a **Written Opinion**.

**Step 3 — International Publication:**
The application is published by WIPO **18 months** after the priority date.

**Step 4 — International Preliminary Examination (Chapter II — Optional):**
The applicant may file a demand for **International Preliminary Examination** to receive a non-binding **International Preliminary Report on Patentability (IPRP)**.

**Step 5 — National Phase Entry (Section 39 of Patents Act, 1970):**
Within **31 months** from the priority date (or as applicable), the applicant must enter the **national phase** in each country where protection is sought, by filing the complete application, paying national fees, and providing translations.

**Advantages of PCT:**
1. **Deferral of costs** — the applicant has up to 30/31 months before national phase fees are due.
2. **Search & preliminary examination** — provides a strong indication of patentability before expensive national filings.
3. **Procedural simplification** — one application, one language, one set of fees for the international phase.

*(Distribution: 2 marks PCT overview & India's accession; 3 marks Step-by-Step procedure; 2 marks Advantages.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Type]**

**(a)** Explain the **procedure for registration of a trademark** under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, highlighting the grounds for refusal and the opposition mechanism. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **Madrid Protocol** system for international registration of trademarks. How has India's accession benefited Indian trademark owners? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Procedure for Trademark Registration under the Trade Marks Act, 1999:**

**Step 1 — Trademark Search:**
Before filing, a search is conducted on the **Indian Trade Marks Registry database** to ensure the mark is not already identical or similar to an existing registered mark.

**Step 2 — Filing of Application (Section 18):**
The applicant files **Form TM-A** with the Registrar of Trade Marks. The application is accompanied by:
- A clear representation of the mark.
- A list of goods/services classified under the **Nice Classification (45 classes)**.
- Prescribed fees.

**Step 3 — Vienna Codification:**
For figurative/device marks, the Registrar allots a **Vienna Code** to classify the visual elements.

**Step 4 — Examination (Section 19):**
The Registrar examines the application on:
- **Absolute grounds for refusal (Section 9)** — lack of distinctiveness, descriptiveness, genericness, deception, or contrary to public order/morality.
- **Relative grounds (Section 11)** — conflict with earlier trademarks, well-known marks, etc.

**Step 5 — Acceptance & Advertisement:**
If accepted, the mark is **advertised in the Trade Marks Journal** to invite opposition.

**Step 6 — Opposition (Section 21):**
Any "aggrieved person" may file **Notice of Opposition (Form TM-O)** within **3 months** (extendable by 1 month) from the date of publication. Grounds include conflict with earlier marks, bad faith, etc.

**Step 7 — Hearing & Decision:**
The Registrar hears both sides and decides whether the mark proceeds to registration.

**Step 8 — Registration (Section 23):**
If no opposition is filed or the opposition fails, the mark is **entered in the Register of Trade Marks**. The **date of registration is deemed to be the date of filing of the application**.

**Step 9 — Term & Renewal:**
The registration is valid for **10 years** and is renewable indefinitely.

**Grounds for Refusal (Section 9 — Absolute):**
1. Marks devoid of distinctive character.
2. Marks consisting exclusively of descriptive terms.
3. Marks which are customary in current language.
4. Marks contrary to public order or morality.
5. Deceptive marks.

*(Distribution: 1 mark each for Steps 1–6; 1 mark for grounds of refusal.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The Madrid Protocol for International Registration of Trademarks:**

The **Madrid Protocol** (adopted in **1989**, entered into force in **1996**) is an international treaty administered by **WIPO** that allows a trademark owner to obtain protection in multiple countries through a **single international application**.

**India acceded to the Madrid Protocol on 8th July 2013.**

**Procedure:**

**Step 1 — File at Office of Origin:**
The Indian applicant files **Form MM2** with the **Indian Trade Marks Registry** (acting as the Office of Origin).

**Step 2 — Forwarding to WIPO:**
The Indian Registry forwards the application to the **International Bureau (IB) of WIPO** in Geneva.

**Step 3 — Formal Examination by IB:**
The IB registers the mark in the **International Register** and notifies all designated Contracting Parties.

**Step 4 — National Examination by Designated Offices:**
Each designated national office has **12 months** (or 18 months if extension is declared) to refuse protection on its national grounds. If no refusal is notified, the mark is **deemed protected** in that country.

**Step 5 — International Registration:**
The international registration is valid for **10 years** and can be renewed indefinitely.

**Benefits to Indian Trademark Owners:**

1. **Single Application, Multiple Countries** — One application can designate 100+ contracting parties.
2. **Cost-Effective** — Eliminates the need for separate national applications, agents, and translations in each country during the international phase.
3. **Centralized Management** — Subsequent changes (assignments, renewals, address changes) can be recorded through a single procedure with WIPO.
4. **Strategic Expansion** — Indian SMEs and startups can now expand brand protection to global markets like the EU, USA, Japan, China with minimal procedural friction.
5. **Madrid Agreement synergy** — The Protocol removes the earlier requirement of a prior national registration (under the Madrid Agreement), enabling "trademark-on-application" basis filings.

*(Distribution: 2 marks Madrid Protocol overview & India's date of accession; 3 marks Step-by-step procedure; 2 marks benefits to Indian owners.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Must Memorize:**

- **Patent** — Mandatory registration. Term: **20 years** from filing date of complete specification. Section 43 governs grant. Annual renewal from 3rd year.
- **Trademark** — Mandatory registration. Term: **10 years**, renewable indefinitely. Date of registration = date of filing. Section 23 governs registration.
- **Design** — Term: **10 years** + one extension of **5 years** = **15 years total**. Governed by Designs Act, 2000.
- **GI** — Community right. Term: **10 years** renewable. Filed by **association of producers**, not individuals.
- **Copyright** — **NO registration required** for subsistence. Arises automatically on creation. Term: Life + 60 years (literary/dramatic/musical/artistic).
- **PCT** — Filed with **Indian Patent Office** as Receiving Office (RO/IN). National phase entry **30/31 months** from priority date.
- **Madrid Protocol** — Filed with **Indian Trade Marks Registry** as Office of Origin. Designated offices have **12-18 months** to refuse.
- **Hague System** — International design registration. India joined **13 Dec 2017**.
- **Opposition Windows** — Patent (pre-grant any time till grant; post-grant 12 months); Trademark (3 months + 1 month extension); Design (3 months).
- **Date Arithmetic** — $D_{\text{expiry}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years}$ (patents); $D_{\text{expiry}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 10 \text{ years}$ (TM, GI).
- **Priority Date** — Crucial for novelty assessment; established by the earliest filed application under the Paris Convention.
- **WIPO** — Geneva-based UN agency administering PCT, Madrid, Hague.
- **Indian Authority** — Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM), under DPIIT, Ministry of Commerce & Industry.
- **Public Register** — Registration on the register provides **constructive notice** to the entire world.
- **First Examination Report (FER)** — Issued for patents under Section 12; reply within 6 months.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden KTU Rule:** Always cite the **section number + time-limit + actor (Controller/Registrar)** together when describing any registration procedure. Examiners award extra marks for **statutory precision**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Duration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR), **Duration** (also called the **Term of Protection**) refers to the legally defined **time period for which the statutory monopoly rights granted to an IP holder remain in force**, commencing from a specific triggering event (such as the date of filing, date of grant, date of creation, or date of first publication) and terminating upon expiry, non-renewal, or explicit relinquishment of the right.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT757 – Module 4):**
> Duration is a **statutory limitation** on the *temporal scope* of IP rights. It balances two competing public interests — (a) rewarding the creator/inventor with a temporary exclusive monopoly, and (b) ensuring the knowledge/creation eventually enters the **public domain** for the greater good of society.

Each form of IP has a **distinct statutory duration** prescribed under its respective Indian legislation:

| IP Right | Governing Statute | Trigger Event | Standard Term |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | Patents Act, 1970 | Date of filing of application | **20 years** |
| **Copyright** | Copyright Act, 1957 | Date of author's death / first publication | **Life + 60 years** (literary) |
| **Trade Mark** | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Date of registration | **10 years** (renewable) |
| **Industrial Design** | Designs Act, 2000 | Date of registration | **10 years** (extendable to 15) |
| **Geographical Indication** | GI of Goods Act, 1999 | Date of registration | **10 years** (renewable) |
| **Plant Variety** | PPV&FR Act, 2001 | Date of registration | **9–18 years** (crop dependent) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Apartment Lease" Model**
>
> Imagine an IP right as an **apartment owned by a developer**:
> - The government (legislator) gives the developer an **exclusive lease** on the apartment (the IP) for a **fixed number of years**.
> - During the lease, **only the developer can collect rent** (royalties / license fees / market exclusivity).
> - When the lease expires, the apartment **becomes public property** — anyone can stay there for free (the **public domain**).
> - If the developer wants to extend the lease (e.g., trademarks), they must **pay a renewal fee** on time.
> - Some apartments (like copyright) are leased for **the lifetime of the developer + a buffer period**, because creativity is treated as an extension of the author's personality.
>
> In short: **Duration = the length of the legal "lease" of the IP.**

## 1.3 Underlying Constitutional & Policy Rationale

The duration of every IP right is anchored in the **Article 14 (equality)** and **Article 19(1)(a)/(g) (freedom of trade & profession)** limitations under the Indian Constitution, read with **Article 39(b)** (distribution of material wealth for common good) and the **TRIPS Agreement (1994)** obligations.

- **Patents** → Limited to **20 years** to align with TRIPS Article 33.
- **Copyright** → **Life + 60 years** as a compromise between the **Berne Convention** (life + 50 minimum) and Indian cultural policy favoring authors.
- **Trademarks** → Indefinite (via renewals every **10 years**) because consumer protection requires continuity, not expiry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition — The "Three-Question Test" for Duration

Whenever you encounter a duration problem in KTU exams, ask these **three structured questions** in order:

1. **Which IP right is involved?** → Determines the parent statute.
2. **What is the trigger event?** → Filing date / Date of death / Date of registration / Date of creation.
3. **Is the right renewable or non-renewable?** → Determines whether the term is *fixed* or *potentially perpetual*.

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Duration — IP by IP

### 2.2.1 Patent — Term = **20 Years**

- **Trigger Event:** Date of **filing of the application** (not the date of grant).
- **Statutory Basis:** Section 53(1) of the Patents Act, 1970 (amended 2005 to comply with TRIPS).
- **Calculation Formula:**

$$
T_{patent} = D_{filing} + 20 \text{ years}
$$

- **Renewability:** ❌ **NOT renewable.** Once expired, the invention falls into the **public domain** and any person may exploit it freely.
- **Maintenance:** An **annual renewal fee** must be paid from the **3rd year onward** (Form-4). Failure to pay leads to lapse.
- **Special Cases:**
  - **Convention application (PCT):** Term is calculated from the **priority date** (date of filing in the originating country).
  - **Restoration:** A lapsed patent can be **restored within 18 months** of the date of cessation (Section 60).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Under the **Old Indian Patents Act (pre-2005)**, the term was only **14 years from the date of grant** for conventional patents and **7 years from the date of sealing** (or 5 years from filing, whichever was longer) for the pre-amendment regime. Always check the *applicable date of filing* to determine which regime governs.

---

### 2.2.2 Copyright — Term = **Life of Author + 60 Years**

- **Trigger Event:** **Death of the author** (for literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works).
- **Statutory Basis:** Section 22 to 28 of the Copyright Act, 1957 (as amended 2012).
- **Calculation Formula:**

$$
T_{copyright} = D_{death} + 60 \text{ years}
$$

The 60 years are counted from the **end of the calendar year in which the author dies**.

- **Work-Specific Rules:**

| Type of Work | Duration |
|---|---|
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic (author known) | **Life + 60 years** |
| Anonymous / Pseudonymous works | **First publication + 60 years** (or 60 years from making, if unpublished within that period) |
| Posthumous / Joint authorship with unknown author | **First publication + 60 years** |
| Cinematograph films | **First publication + 60 years** |
| Sound recordings | **First publication + 60 years** |
| Government / International organization works | **First publication + 60 years** |
| Photographs | **First publication + 60 years** |

- **Renewability:** ❌ **NOT renewable.** Copyright is **automatic** upon creation (no registration required in India) and ends automatically at the end of the calculated term.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-2012 Position (for old exam questions):** The Copyright Act, 1957 (original) provided for **Life + 50 years** for literary/artistic works, and **50 years from first publication** for films/sound recordings. The 2012 amendment extended all terms to **60 years** to align with EU and Japanese standards, but **did NOT revive any copyright that had already lapsed** under the old 50-year rule.

---

### 2.2.3 Trade Mark — Term = **10 Years (Renewable Indefinitely)**

- **Trigger Event:** **Date of registration** of the mark.
- **Statutory Basis:** Section 25 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999.
- **Calculation Formula:**

$$
T_{trademark} = D_{registration} + 10 \text{ years}
$$

- **Renewability:** ✅ **Yes — indefinitely.** The registered proprietor can file a renewal application in **Form TM-R** within **6 months before expiry**, with a **6-month grace period** after expiry (on payment of surcharge). A mark can be **kept in force perpetually** through renewals.
- **Removal:** A trademark may be removed from the Register for **non-use** for a continuous period of **5 years** (Section 47).

---

### 2.2.4 Industrial Design — Term = **10 Years + Extension to 15 Years**

- **Trigger Event:** Date of registration.
- **Statutory Basis:** Section 11 of the Designs Act, 2000.
- **Calculation Formula:**

$$
T_{design} = \min\left(D_{registration} + 10 \text{ years},\ D_{registration} + 15 \text{ years (if extended)}\right)
$$

- **Renewability:** ✅ **One-time extension of 5 years** allowed (on application before expiry), taking total term to **15 years maximum**.

---

### 2.2.5 Geographical Indication (GI) — Term = **10 Years (Renewable)**

- **Statutory Basis:** Section 28 of the GI of Goods (Registration & Protection) Act, 1999.
- **Calculation:**

$$
T_{GI} = D_{registration} + 10 \text{ years (renewable for 10-year blocks)}
$$

- **Renewability:** ✅ **Yes, in blocks of 10 years.** Unlike trademarks, GI rights are **held by registered producers / authorized users** under a *collective* model.

---

### 2.2.6 Plant Variety — Term = **9 to 18 Years (Crop-Dependent)**

- **Statutory Basis:** Section 24 of the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights (PPV&FR) Act, 2001.
- **Crop-specific terms:**

| Crop Category | Duration |
|---|---|
| Trees and vines | **18 years** |
| Other crops (extinction-resistant species) | **15 years** |
| Short-duration crops (cereals etc.) | **9 to 12 years** |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Duration Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table for Last-Minute Revision**

| IP Right | Formula | Numerical Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| Patent | $D_{file} + 20$ yrs | **20 years** |
| Copyright (literary) | $D_{death} + 60$ yrs | **60 years post-mortem** |
| Copyright (film/recording) | $D_{1st\ pub} + 60$ yrs | **60 years from publication** |
| Trade Mark | $D_{reg} + 10$ yrs | **10 yrs, ∞ renewals** |
| Industrial Design | $D_{reg} + 10$ (+5 ext.) | **Max 15 years** |
| GI | $D_{reg} + 10$ yrs | **10-year renewable blocks** |
| Plant Variety | Crop-specific | **9, 12, 15, or 18 yrs** |
| Trade Secret | Indefinite (till secret maintained) | **No statutory term** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **engineering project management**, the duration of IP rights directly impacts:

- **Technology Transfer Decisions** — A 20-year patent term may justify licensing negotiations only in the early years; the value of a patent **depreciates** as expiry approaches.
- **R&D Investment Recovery** — A new drug costs ~$2.6 billion; the **patent term restoration under Hatch-Waxman (USA)** recognizes the regulatory delay and effectively extends market exclusivity.
- **Brand Strategy** — Companies like **Coca-Cola and IBM** maintain trademarks for **100+ years** through systematic 10-year renewals, treating the mark as a perpetual asset.
- **Open-Source & Public Domain Engineering** — Knowing the expiry of a patent allows engineers to design **around expired inventions** without infringement risk.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Problems Modeled on KTU Pattern**

---

## 3.1 Example 1 — Patent Term Calculation

**Problem:** *Ravi filed a patent application in India on 15 March 2010. The patent was granted on 20 July 2013. Calculate the date of expiry of the patent. Will it be affected by the 2005 amendment?*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing statute:**

Since the application was filed on **15 March 2010** (after 01 January 2005), the **Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005** applies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2005 amendment introduced **product patents** in food, drugs, and chemicals, and changed the term to **20 years from the date of filing** (previously 14 years from grant under the pre-2005 regime).

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$
T_{expiry} = D_{filing} + 20 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the value:**

$$
T_{expiry} = 15\text{-}03\text{-}2010 + 20 \text{ years}
$$

$$
T_{expiry} = 15\text{-}03\text{-}2030
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

The patent will **expire on 15 March 2030**, irrespective of the grant date (20 July 2013). The grant date is **irrelevant** for term calculation under the post-2005 regime.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Copyright Term Calculation (Literary Work)

**Problem:** *Author Suresh, a citizen of India, wrote a novel in 2000. He died on 10 June 2015. (i) When will the copyright in the novel expire? (ii) Would the answer change if the novel was a joint work co-authored with an unknown person?*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the work type and applicable law:**

- Work type: **Literary work (novel)**.
- Applicable law: **Copyright Act, 1957 (as amended 2012)**.
- Term: **Life of the author + 60 years** (Section 22).

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$
T_{expiry} = D_{death} + 60 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the death date:**

$$
D_{death} = 10\text{-}06\text{-}2015
$$

**Step 4 — Add 60 calendar years (counted from end of the year of death per Section 22(2)):**

$$
\text{End of year of death} = 31\text{-}12\text{-}2015
$$

$$
T_{expiry} = 31\text{-}12\text{-}2015 + 60 \text{ years}
$$

$$
T_{expiry} = 31\text{-}12\text{-}2075
$$

**Step 5 — Joint authorship with unknown person (Part ii):**

Under **Section 22(1)(b)**, if the work is **anonymous or pseudonymous** (and authorship cannot be ascertained), the term is computed from the **date of first publication**, not the death of the author.

$$
T_{expiry} = D_{1st\ publication} + 60 \text{ years}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often compute the term from the **publication date** for normal literary works — this is **wrong**. Section 22(1)(a) governs known authors and ties the term to the **author's death**, not publication.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Trademark Renewal Lapse

**Problem:** *A trademark was registered on 5 August 2005. The proprietor failed to renew it. (i) When did the registration cease? (ii) What is the maximum period within which restoration is possible?*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Statutory provision:**

- **Section 25(1)**: Term is **10 years** from date of registration.
- **Section 25(3)**: A renewal application may be made **within 6 months before expiry**.
- **Section 25(4)**: **Grace period of 6 months after expiry** is allowed with surcharge.
- **Section 26**: Restoration by the Registrar for **removal of mark from register** in cases of non-payment (or non-use removal under Section 47).

**Step 2 — Compute the original expiry date:**

$$
T_{expiry} = 05\text{-}08\text{-}2005 + 10 \text{ years} = 05\text{-}08\text{-}2015
$$

**Step 3 — Compute grace period end:**

$$
T_{grace\ end} = 05\text{-}08\text{-}2015 + 6 \text{ months} = 05\text{-}02\text{-}2016
$$

**Step 4 — Restoration window:**

The proprietor can apply for **restoration within 1 year** from the date of removal (per the discretionary power of the Registrar).

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The registration stood as valid until **5 August 2015**, with a final grace period until **5 February 2016**. After that, the mark is removed from the Register.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Duration at a Glance

| Parameter | Patent | Copyright | Trademark | Design | GI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Term** | 20 yrs | Life + 60 yrs | 10 yrs (∞ renewal) | 10+5 yrs | 10 yrs (renewable) |
| **Trigger** | Filing date | Death / Publication | Registration | Registration | Registration |
| **Renewable?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Once | ✅ Yes |
| **Maintenance Fee** | Annual (from Yr 3) | None | Per renewal | Per renewal | Per renewal |
| **Public Domain Entry** | Automatic | Automatic | On non-renewal | On non-renewal | On non-renewal |
| **TRIPS Compliance** | Art. 33 | Art. 12 | Art. 18 | Art. 26 | Art. 23 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
