1. **Direct Infringement (Sec. 48 read with Sec. 104)**
   - Manufacturing the patented product.
   - Using the patented process.
   - Selling or offering for sale the patented product.
   - Importing the patented product.

2. **Indirect / Contributory Infringement (Sec. 107)**
   - Supplying (other than staple commercial product) means relating to an essential element of the invention, knowing them to be **suitable for putting the invention into effect**.
   - Example: Selling a specialized tool that has no non-infringing use, when the tool is essential to operate a patented machine.

> [!WARNING] Key KTU Distinction
> **Process Patent vs. Product Patent**: Under Indian law (post-2005 TRIPS compliance), the burden of proof reverses in **process patent** infringement suits. If the defendant produces a product identical to the one made by the patented process, **the burden shifts to the defendant to prove the product was made by a different process** (Sec. 104-A).

### 2.2.2 Trademark Infringement (Section 29, TM Act, 1999)

> [!IMPORTANT] Definition
> A registered trademark is **infringed** when a person, not being the registered proprietor or a permitted user, uses in the course of trade a mark that is **identical with or deceptively similar to** the registered trademark in relation to **identical or similar goods/services**, causing **likelihood of confusion**.

**The KTU Test for TM Infringement — "Triple Identity + Confusion"**
1. **Identity of the mark** or **deceptive similarity** with the registered mark.
2. **Identity or similarity of goods/services**.
3. Use in the **course of trade** (commercial use).
4. **Likelihood of confusion** among the relevant public.

**Categories of TM Infringement (Sec. 29):**
- **29(1)** — Identical mark, identical goods.
- **29(2)** — Identical/similar mark, identical/similar goods, likelihood of confusion.
- **29(3)** — Identical/similar mark, identical/similar goods, likelihood of association.
- **29(4)** — Identical/similar mark, even on dissimilar goods, if the mark is **well-known in India** (anti-dilution).
- **29(5–9)** — Use of packaging, labels, advertising material, etc., that are deceptively similar.

### 2.2.3 Copyright Infringement (Section 51, Copyright Act, 1957)

> [!IMPORTANT] Definition
> Copyright in a work is **infringed** when any person, without a licence from the copyright owner or the Registrar of Copyrights, **does anything the exclusive right of the copyright owner permits** — including reproducing, communicating, adapting, translating, distributing, or renting — without authorization.

**Modes of Copyright Infringement (Section 51):**
- Reproducing the work in any material form.
- Storing it in any medium by electronic means.
- Communicating it to the public.
- Issuing copies to the public (not being copies already in circulation).
- Performing it in public.
- Translating, adapting, or arranging it.
- Selling, hiring, or commercial dealing with infringing copies.

### 2.2.4 Design Infringement (Section 22, Designs Act, 2000)

> [!IMPORTANT] Definition
> A registered design is **infringed** if any person applies it, or any fraudulent or obvious imitation of it, to any article, or imports, sells, or hires any article to which the design or imitation is applied, for the purposes of trade, without the registered proprietor's consent.

---

## 2.3 The Master "KTU Formula Sheet" — Civil Remedies at a Glance

> [!NOTE] Examiner's Favourite
> This table covers **almost every 3-mark and 14-mark question** on remedies in the KTU ESE 2024 paper pattern.

| # | Remedy | Nature | Statutory Basis | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Temporary / Interim Injunction** | Equitable | Order XXXIX, CPC, 1908 | Preserve status quo during trial |
| 2 | **Permanent / Perpetual Injunction** | Equitable | Specific Relief Act, 1963, Sec. 36–42 | Final restraint on future infringement |
| 3 | **Damages** (compensatory) | Monetary | Sec. 108, Patents Act / Sec. 114, TM Act | Compensate actual loss + injury to reputation |
| 4 | **Damages for conversion / seizure** | Monetary | Sec. 108(2), Patents Act | For infringing goods seized |
| 5 | **Account of Profits** | Restitutionary | Sec. 108(1)(b), Patents Act | Disgorgement of infringer's ill-gotten gains |
| 6 | **Delivery up / Destruction** | Equitable | Sec. 53, Copyright Act / Sec. 115, TM Act | Physical destruction of infringing goods |
| 7 | **Anton Piller Order** | Equitable (interim) | English common law (Indian courts via inherent power) | Surprise search of infringer's premises to preserve evidence |
| 8 | **Mareva Injunction** | Equitable (interim) | English common law | Freeze defendant's assets to prevent dissipation |
| 9 | **Costs of the suit** | Procedural | CPC + relevant IP Act | Reimbursement of legal expenses |
| 10 | **Relief in case of groundless threats** | Defensive | Sec. 106, Patents Act / Sec. 142, TM Act | Declaration that threats were groundless + injunction against them |

---

## 2.4 The KTU "Defence Matrix" — When Infringement is NOT Infringement

| # | Defence | Applicable to | Statutory Basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prior use** (made serious commercial use before priority date) | Patent | Sec. 64(1)(e), Patents Act |
| 2 | **Bona fide prior use** | Trademark | Sec. 30(1), TM Act |
| 3 | **Fair use / Fair dealing** | Copyright | Sec. 52, Copyright Act (private use, research, criticism, review, news reporting, judicial use) |
| 4 | **Exhaustion of rights / Parallel imports** | Patent, TM | Implied from statute + Sec. 30(3) read with Sec. 30(4), TM Act |
| 5 | **Innocent infringement** | Patent, TM, Copyright | Sec. 59(1), Patents Act; Sec. 31(1), TM Act |
| 6 | **Experimental use / Research exception** | Patent | Sec. 47(iii), Patents Act |
| 7 | **Compulsory License / Government use** | Patent | Sec. 84, 92, Patents Act |
| 8 | **Revocation of the IP right itself** | Patent, TM, Design | Counter-claim in infringement suit |
| 9 | **Laches / Acquiescence / Delay** | All IP | Common law equity defence |
| 10 | **Estoppel** | All IP | Common law |

> [!TIP] KTU High-Yield Term
> **Section 52 of the Copyright Act** contains **22 exhaustive fair-dealing exceptions**. A KTU 14-mark question on "What constitutes fair dealing?" with 7 marks for enumerating the exceptions and 7 marks for the *fair-dealing test (purpose + nature + quantum + effect on market)* is a classic pattern.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

| Industry / Engineering Field | IP Infringement Risk | Typical Remedy Sought |
|---|---|---|
| **Pharmaceutical Engineering** | Generic drug launch before patent expiry (Section 3(d) disputes) | Injunction + damages + account of profits |
| **Mechanical / Automotive** | Copying of patented components | Anton Piller + injunction |
| **Software Engineering** | Code reproduction, open-source licence violation | Damages + injunction + delivery up |
| **Civil / Construction** | Architectural copyright violation | Damages for infringement of artistic work |
| **Electronics / Semiconductor** | Patent infringement on chip designs | Cross-border injunction (rare in India) |
| **Brand-heavy industries (FMCG)** | Trademark counterfeiting | Customs recordal + criminal prosecution + civil suit |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 The Algorithmic Flow of a Civil IP Infringement Suit in India

> [!NOTE] This is the standard KTU analytical framework for answering *"Explain the procedure for IP infringement litigation in India."*

**Step 1 — Pre-litigation Verification**

Before filing, the rights-holder must:
- Confirm the **IP is valid, subsisting, and registered** (where required).
- Verify the **ownership chain** (assignments, licences).
- Identify the **defendant** and the **infringing act** with documentary evidence.

**Step 2 — Jurisdiction**

- **District Court** where the plaintiff resides or carries on business, OR
- **District Court** where the defendant resides or the cause of action arose.
- For patents, the **IPAB (Intellectual Property Appellate Board)** historically heard appeals; post-2021, jurisdiction lies with **Commercial Courts and the High Court**.

**Step 3 — Maintainability and Limitation**

The suit must be filed within the **prescribed limitation period**:
$$\text{Limitation Period for IP Suits} = 3 \text{ years from the date the right arose}$$

For a continuing infringement, each fresh act gives a fresh cause of action.

**Step 4 — Pleadings**

- **Plaint** (Order VII, CPC) must plead:
  - Ownership of the IP.
  - Particulars of the registered right (number, date, classes).
  - Specific acts of infringement with dates.
  - Reliefs sought (injunction, damages, account of profits, etc.).
- **Written Statement** by the defendant must include:
  - Denial of infringement.
  - Counter-claim for revocation of the IP (if applicable).
  - Pleas in bar (limitation, estoppel, etc.).

**Step 5 — Interim Relief — Anton Piller + Mareva + Temporary Injunction**

The plaintiff typically files an **ex parte** application (without notice to defendant) under **Order XXXIX Rules 1 & 2, CPC** for:
- **Temporary injunction** to restrain further infringement.
- **Anton Piller order** for surprise search of the defendant's premises.
- **Mareva injunction** to freeze bank accounts/assets.

The court must be satisfied of the **triple test**:
$$\text{Triple Test for Interim Injunction} = \begin{cases} \text{(i) Prima facie case of infringement} \\ \text{(ii) Balance of convenience in favour of plaintiff} \\ \text{(iii) Irreparable injury if injunction refused} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Discovery, Inspection, and Trial**

- Discovery and inspection of documents.
- Cross-examination of witnesses.
- Expert evidence (especially for patents — Section 115, Patents Act, mandates expert assistance).

**Step 7 — Judgment and Final Reliefs**

The court may grant:
- **Permanent injunction.**
- **Damages** OR **Account of Profits** (the plaintiff elects, but not both for the same infringement).
- **Delivery up / destruction** of infringing goods.
- **Costs.**

**Step 8 — Appeal**

Appeal lies to the **High Court** under **Section 13 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015** (for commercial disputes of specified value) or the **Letters Patent Appeal** for original-side suits.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Calculation of Damages in IP Suits

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Favourite 14-Mark Question
> *"Discuss the principles governing the assessment of damages in IP infringement suits."*

There are **four doctrinal bases** for damages in IP cases, which the court selects based on facts:

### 3.2.1 Doctrine 1 — Actual / Compensatory Damages

The plaintiff is restored to the position they would have been in had the infringement not occurred.

$$\text{D}_{\text{actual}} = \text{Lost Sales} \times \text{Profit Margin} + \text{Consequential Loss}$$

**Example derivation:**

Suppose:
- Infringer sold 10,000 infringing units.
- Each unit would have carried a royalty/license fee of **₹100** had the plaintiff licensed it.
- Plaintiff's market share loss = 20% of infringing sales.

Then:
$$\text{D}_{\text{actual}} = (10{,}000 \times 20\%) \times \text{Profit Margin} + \text{Consequential Damages}$$

If profit margin per unit is ₹50:
$$\text{D}_{\text{actual}} = 2{,}000 \times ₹50 + \text{Consequential Damages} = ₹1{,}00{,}000 + \text{Consequential Damages}$$

### 3.2.2 Doctrine 2 — Account of Profits (Restitutionary / Disgorgement)

The infringer must surrender the profits earned by the wrongful act:

$$\text{AoP} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Infringing Sales}_i \times \text{Infringer's Profit Margin per Unit}_i)$$

The plaintiff does **not** need to prove their own loss — only the infringer's gain.

### 3.2.3 Doctrine 3 — Statutory / Pre-estimated Damages

Some statutes (e.g., **Section 31(1) of the Copyright Act, 1957** — for sound recordings) and contractual clauses fix damages **per copy infringed**. Not available as a matter of right for all IP.

### 3.2.4 Doctrine 4 — Nominal / Aggravated / Exemplary Damages

- **Nominal** = a token amount (₹1 or ₹10,000) where infringement is proven but no real loss is shown.
- **Aggravated** = additional sum for injury to feelings or reputation.
- **Exemplary / Punitive** = deterrent damages in cases of flagrant, deliberate infringement (rare in India but recognised in *Lacroix Ducharest v. SAIL* and similar rulings).

---

## 3.3 The "Doctrine of Equivalents" vs. "Literal Infringement" — A Key Patent Law Derivation

In patent law, infringement is judged by two tests:

$$\text{Infringement Test} = \begin{cases} \text{(a) Literal Infringement: Every element of the claim is present verbatim} \\ \text{(b) Doctrine of Equivalents: The accused product performs substantially the same function, in substantially the same way, to achieve substantially the same result (FWR test)} \end{cases}$$

If **either** is satisfied → **infringement exists**.

The **FWR (Function-Way-Result) test** originated in *Graver Tank v. Linde Air Products* (US, 1950) and is applied in India via *Raj Prakash v. Mangat Ram Chowdhry* (1978).

**Worked Example — Doctrine of Equivalents**

Suppose the patent claim recites:
> *"A locking mechanism comprising: (a) a bolt of cylindrical shape, (b) a spring exerting force, (c) a latch engaging the bolt."*

The accused device uses:
- (a) a bolt of **hexagonal** shape (not cylindrical),
- (b) a **magnetic** force (not a spring),
- (c) a latch that performs the **same function**.

**Analysis:**
- **Literal infringement?** No — shapes and forces differ.
- **FWR test?**
  - **Function** = locking = **SAME** ✓
  - **Way** = blocking + restraint = **substantially the same** ✓
  - **Result** = secured closure = **SAME** ✓
- **Verdict:** Infringement under the **Doctrine of Equivalents**, even without literal copying.

> [!WARNING] Limitation
> The doctrine is **not** applicable if the accused product avoids a specific element disclosed in the prior art (**"prosecution history estoppel"**). KTU 14-mark questions sometimes ask for a one-sided debate — note the **"file wrapper estoppel"** in answer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Remedies Across IP Types

| Remedy | Patent (Act 1970) | Trademark (Act 1999) | Copyright (Act 1957) | Design (Act 2000) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Civil suit lies in** | District Court | District Court / Commercial Court | District Court | District Court |
| **Injunction** | Sec. 108(1)(a) | Sec. 134(1)(a) | Sec. 55 / 62 | Sec. 22(2) |
| **Damages / Account of profits** | Sec. 108(1)(b) | Sec. 135(1) | Sec. 55 / 62 | Sec. 22(2) |
| **Delivery up** | Sec. 108(2) | Sec. 135(1) | Sec. 63 | Sec. 22(2) |
| **Criminal prosecution** | Limited (Sec. 118–124) | Sec. 103–105 | Sec. 63–70 | Sec. 22(2) read with 47 |
| **Customs border action** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Relief for groundless threats** | Sec. 106 | Sec. 142 | N/A | N/A |

---

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Decision Tree — Choosing Between Damages and Account of Profits

The court does **not** award both. The plaintiff elects, but the court has discretion to apply the one that fits justice.

```
            Is the infringer's profit clearly traceable to the wrongful act?
                            |
                  +---------+---------+
                  |                   |
                 YES                  NO
                  |                   |
        +---------v---------+    +----v----+
        | Difficult to      |    | Easier  |
        | prove plaintiff's |    | to prove|
        | lost sales        |    | lost    |
        +---------+---------+    | sales   |
                  |              +----+----+
                  |                   |
                  v                   v
        +---------+--------+   +-------+-------+
        | Account of       |   | Compensatory  |
        | Profits (AoP)    |   | Damages       |
        +------------------+   +---------------+
```

> [!TIP] KTU 2024 Exam Tip
> If the question does not specify, present **both** formulas and let the examiner choose. This shows analytical depth and protects partial marking.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The IP Enforcement Architecture in India

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**"Distinguish between 'Infringement' and 'Passing Off' in trademark law." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT] Examiner's Key
> Award 1.5 marks each for a clear point of distinction.

| **Aspect** | **Infringement** | **Passing Off** |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of right** | Statutory right under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** | Common law tort (actionable even **without registration**) |
| **Plaintiff's burden** | Need only prove use of identical/deceptively similar mark for identical/similar goods | Must prove **reputation, goodwill, deception, and damage** |
| **Nature** | Statutory tort | Equitable action in tort |
| **Sections** | Sections 27–29, TM Act | No statutory section; based on common law |

*(Word limit expected: ~80–100 words. Each valid distinction carries a mark.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**"What is the doctrine of equivalents in patent law? Mention its purpose." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **doctrine of equivalents** is a judicial doctrine in patent law which holds that an accused product or process infringes a patent even if it does not literally contain every element of the patent claim, provided it performs **substantially the same function, in substantially the same way, to achieve substantially the same result** (the **FWR test**).

**Purpose:**
1. To **prevent design-around** of patents by minor, insubstantial changes to claim elements.
2. To protect the **inventor's true contribution** from being copied in disguised form.
3. To align legal protection with the **spirit of the patent** rather than the literal text.

*(~100 words; award 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for purpose.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** *"Discuss in detail the civil remedies available to a patentee in India for infringement of a patent."* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *"Explain the concept of 'Account of Profits' as a remedy in IP infringement suits. How is it distinguished from damages?"* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**1. Introduction** *(1 mark)*
Under the **Patents Act, 1970**, Sections 104 to 117 deal with reliefs in suits for infringement. The patentee, exclusive licensee, or the Government (in cases of public interest) can file a suit before the **District Court** having jurisdiction.

**2. Civil Remedies — Enumerated** *(5 marks)*

**(i) Injunctions:**
- **Temporary (interim) injunction** under **Order XXXIX, CPC** to restrain the defendant during the pendency of the suit. The court must be satisfied of the **triple test**:
  - *Prima facie case of infringement*,
  - *Balance of convenience* in favour of the patentee,
  - *Irreparable injury*.
- **Permanent injunction** under **Section 108(1)(a), Patents Act** restraining the defendant from further infringement.

**(ii) Damages or Account of Profits** *(Section 108(1)(b))*:
- The patentee may elect to claim either **damages** (compensation for loss) or **account of profits** (surrender of the infringer's gains), but not both.
- Damages may include **actual loss + injury to reputation + exemplary damages** in cases of flagrant infringement.

**(iii) Delivery Up / Destruction of Infringing Goods** *(Section 108(2))*:
- The court may order the infringer to **deliver up** the infringing articles for **destruction or forfeiture**.

**(iv) Costs of the Suit**:
- Reimbursement of legal expenses as per **Section 35, CPC**.

**3. Other Equitable Remedies** *(1 mark)*
- **Anton Piller Order** (surprise search to preserve evidence).
- **Mareva Injunction** (freezing of the defendant's assets).
- Both are granted through the **inherent powers** of the court.

**4. Conclusion** *(remaining marks for structured framing)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**1. Definition** *(1 mark)*
**Account of Profits** is a restitutionary remedy requiring the infringer to **disgorge the profits** earned from the wrongful use of the patentee's intellectual property. It is based on the principle of **unjust enrichment** rather than compensation for loss.

**2. Statutory Basis** *(1 mark)*
**Section 108(1)(b) of the Patents Act, 1970**; equivalent provisions exist in the Trade Marks Act (Sec. 135) and Copyright Act (Sec. 62).

**3. Method of Calculation** *(3 marks)*

$$\text{Account of Profits} = \text{Total Revenue from Infringing Sales} - \text{Proper Deductions}$$

The deductions include:
- Direct costs of producing the infringing goods.
- Variable overheads directly attributable to the infringing activity.
- General overheads are apportioned on a **fair basis** (e.g., turnover ratio).

The plaintiff is **not** required to prove their own loss — they need only establish the infringer's gain.

**4. Distinction from Damages** *(2 marks)*

| Parameter | Damages | Account of Profits |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Compensate plaintiff | Disgorge infringer's gain |
| **Burden on plaintiff** | Prove actual loss | Prove infringer's profit |
| **Quantum basis** | Loss suffered | Gain made |
| **Pleading requirement** | Quantify loss | Quantify infringer's profit |
| **Equitable flavour** | Less | More (discretionary) |

**5. Conclusion** *(remaining marks)*: Account of profits serves as a **deterrent**, ensuring the infringer is not allowed to profit from the wrongful act, even if the plaintiff's actual loss is minimal.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** *"Explain the various defences available to an alleged infringer in a patent infringement suit."* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *"Discuss the role of the Anton Piller order in IP enforcement. What safeguards are imposed to prevent its abuse?"* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**1. Introduction** *(1 mark)*
A defendant in a patent infringement suit may raise several defences, both **statutory** (under the Patents Act) and **common law** (equitable defences). The burden of proving the defence lies on the defendant.

**2. Statutory Defences** *(4 marks)*

**(i) Prior Public Knowledge / Lack of Novelty (Section 64(1)(a))**:
- The invention was publicly known or used in India before the priority date.
- This is a **revocation ground** that operates as a defence.

**(ii) Prior Claim (Section 64(1)(d))**:
- The invention was claimed in a prior Indian application with an earlier priority date.

**(iii) Prior Commercial Use in India (Section 64(1)(e) read with Section 47)**:
- The defendant had made **serious commercial use** of the invention in India before the priority date, on a scale reasonably adequate for the nature of the invention.

**(iv) Obviousness / Lack of Inventive Step (Section 64(1)(j))**:
- The invention would have been obvious to a person skilled in the art.

**(v) Not Patentable Subject Matter (Section 3)**:
- The invention falls within the excluded categories (e.g., business methods, traditional knowledge, atomic energy).

**(vi) Revocation Counter-Claim (Section 64)**:
- The defendant can file a **counter-claim for revocation** of the patent within the infringement suit.

**3. Common Law / Equitable Defences** *(2 marks)*
- **Laches** (delay in filing).
- **Acquiescence** (the patentee stood by while the defendant incurred expense).
- **Estoppel** (the patentee led the defendant to believe no action would be taken).
- **Unclean hands** (the patentee obtained the patent by fraud).

**4. Conclusion** *(remaining marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**1. Concept and Origin** *(1 mark)*
The **Anton Piller order** is an **ex parte** (without notice) injunction ordering the defendant to permit the plaintiff's lawyer to enter the defendant's premises for the purpose of **inspecting, copying, and removing** documents or articles that may constitute evidence of infringement. Originated in *Anton Piller KG v. Manufacturing Processes Ltd.* (1976, England), adopted in India through the **inherent powers of the High Court** (Article 215, Constitution + Section 151, CPC).

**2. Role in IP Enforcement** *(3 marks)*
- **Evidence preservation:** Prevents the defendant from destroying incriminating documents upon learning of the suit.
- **Surprise element:** Ensures the defendant cannot move assets or hide the infringing goods.
- **Crucial in counterfeiting cases:** Especially trademark and copyright piracy, where damages depend on quantifying illicit production.

**3. Safeguards Against Abuse** *(3 marks)*

The Supreme Court of India in **Manohar Lal Sharma v. Union of India** and various High Court rulings have laid down safeguards:

| Safeguard | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Two-locks doctrine** | The plaintiff's representative and a **court-appointed neutral observer (supervising solicitor)** must both be present |
| **Confidentiality** | The plaintiff must give an **undertaking** to keep the materials confidential |
| **Daytime execution** | The order must be executed only in **daylight** |
| **Written undertaking for damages** | The plaintiff must compensate the defendant if the order is later found to be wrongful |
| **Full disclosure** | The plaintiff must make **full and frank disclosure** of all material facts |
| **Independent counsel** | The plaintiff's lawyer should be **independent** (not the same firm acting later in the suit) |

**4. Conclusion** *(remaining marks)*

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall
> - Students often write only the **definition** of remedies and **omit the procedural aspect** (jurisdiction, limitation, interim relief triple test). The 2024 scheme expects both the **substantive** and the **procedural** side.
> - In **Anton Piller** questions, students **forget to mention the safeguards**. Examiner specifically deducts marks when the safeguards are absent.
> - For **Account of Profits** questions, the **formula** must be written explicitly. Verbal description without the equation loses 2 of 7 marks.
> - Confusing **"infringement"** with **"piracy"** is a recurring error. Piracy is a *subset* of infringement, used in specific statutes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP] Final 5-Minute Revision Notes

### ⚡ Statutory Sections — Quick Recall

- **Patents Act, 1970** → Sec. 48 (rights), Sec. 104–117 (remedies), Sec. 64 (revocation), Sec. 106 (groundless threats), Sec. 107 (indirect infringement), Sec. 118–124 (offences).
- **Trade Marks Act, 1999** → Sec. 27 (TM registration proof of validity), Sec. 28 (rights of registered proprietor), Sec. 29 (infringement), Sec. 30 (limits on infringement), Sec. 103–105 (criminal), Sec. 134–142 (civil + threats).
- **Copyright Act, 1957** → Sec. 13–14 (works + rights), Sec. 51 (infringement), Sec. 52 (fair dealing — 22 exceptions), Sec. 55 (civil remedies), Sec. 62 (jurisdiction), Sec. 63 (criminal).
- **Designs Act, 2000** → Sec. 2(d) (definition), Sec. 11 (term), Sec. 22 (infringement), Sec. 47 (criminal).

### ⚡ One-Line Definitions to Memorize

1. **Infringement** = unauthorized violation of a statutorily granted IP right.
2. **Passing Off** = common law tort protecting unregistered marks/goodwill.
3. **Anton Piller** = ex parte search order to preserve evidence.
4. **Mareva Injunction** = order freezing defendant's assets.
5. **Account of Profits** = disgorgement of infringer's wrongful gain.
6. **Doctrine of Equivalents** = infringement by substantially equivalent function-way-result.
7. **Fair Dealing** = Section 52, Copyright Act — non-infringing uses.
8. **Groundless Threats** = declaration + injunction against wrongful IP threats.

### ⚡ Numerics to Memorize

- **20 years** — Patent term.
- **Life + 60 years** — Copyright (literary/dramatic/musical/artistic).
- **60 years from publication** — Anonymous/posthumous/Corporate works.
- **10 years** — TM registration term (renewable).
- **10 + 5 = 15 years** — Design registration.
- **3 years** — Limitation period for IP suits.
- **3-tier test** — For interim injunction (prima facie + balance of convenience + irreparable injury).
- **22 exceptions** — Section 52, Copyright Act (Fair Dealing).

### ⚡ Cross-Linking to Other Modules

- **Module 1** → Definitions and rationale of IPR.
- **Module 2** → Acquisition of patents and TM rights (registration prerequisites for infringement).
- **Module 3** → International treaties (TRIPS, Paris, Berne) that **mandate** these remedies in member states.
- **Module 4 (this)** → **Enforcement** — the operative module for litigation.
- **Module 5 (typical)** → IP management, valuation, and commercialization (licensing, technology transfer) — a non-conflict route that **replaces** litigation.

### ⚡ Memory Mnemonics

- **3 D's of Civil Remedy** = **D**amages + **D**elivery up + **D**isgorgement (Account of Profits) + **Injunction**.
- **Triple Injunction Test** = **PBI** = **P**rima facie + **B**alance of convenience + **I**rreparable injury.
- **FWR (Patent DoE)** = **F**unction + **W**ay + **R**esult.
- **Three Tiers of Enforcement** = **C**ivil + **C**riminal + **C**ustoms (all start with "C").
- **52 Sections to Remember for Copyright** = Sec. **51** (infringement) + Sec. **52** (defence).
- **Injunction Stack** = **T**emporary → **P**ermanent → **Mareva** → **Anton Piller**.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assignment and Transmission of Trademarks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Assignment and Transmission of Trademarks

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Assignment of a Trademark** is a voluntary, bilateral, and contractual transfer of ownership rights over a registered or unregistered trademark from the **proprietor (assignor)** to another person or entity (**assignee**) during the lifetime of the proprietor, for a consideration or gratuitously. It is governed primarily by **Sections 37 to 45 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**.

**Transmission of a Trademark** is the transfer of title to a trademark that occurs by operation of law, without the need for a contract between the parties. It typically arises in three scenarios:
1. **Transmission by inheritance** upon the death of the proprietor.
2. **Transmission by operation of law** (e.g., bankruptcy, insolvency, court decree, merger, or amalgamation).
3. **Transmission under a will** or intestate succession.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Trademark Act, 1999 (Sections 37–45) unifies the law for both registered and unregistered trademarks, replacing the fragmented provisions of the Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958. A trademark is a **species of property** (per Section 39), and like any property, it can be assigned or transmitted, but with statutory restrictions to protect public interest and prevent deception.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **trademark as a "brand-ownership certificate"** — like the title deed of a house:

- **Assignment** is like **selling your house while you are alive**. You sign a sale deed, receive money (consideration), and the buyer takes over ownership. Both parties must consent, and the deal is registered with the Sub-Registrar (in trademarks, with the **Trade Marks Registry**).
- **Transmission** is like **inheriting the house** when the owner dies, or the house being transferred by a **court order** in a divorce or bankruptcy case. There is no sale deed — the law itself moves the title from one person to another.

Just as a house cannot be sold in a way that misleads buyers about its location, boundaries, or quality, a trademark **cannot be assigned in a manner that creates public confusion** about the goods/services it covers. The law draws a line: **property can move, but the consumer's trust cannot be compromised.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 21 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999** recognizes both **registered and unregistered trademarks** as property capable of assignment and transmission. This was a revolutionary shift from the old 1958 Act which only protected registered marks.

---

### Distinction Between Assignment and Transmission

| Parameter | Assignment | Transmission |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Voluntary, contractual act | Involuntary or by operation of law |
| **Consent** | Requires mutual agreement | No consent required between parties |
| **Parties** | Assignor and Assignee | Transferor and Transferee (by law) |
| **Consideration** | Usually for monetary value (price) | Generally no consideration |
| **Triggering Event** | Sale, gift, agreement during lifetime | Death, insolvency, court order, merger |
| **Governing Provisions** | Sections 37, 38, 39, 40, 42, 44 | Sections 38, 42, 43, 45 |
| **KTU Keyword** | "Act of parties" | "Operation of law" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Principle:** While the *legal mechanics* differ, the *consequences* are identical — both result in a change of proprietor on the register, and both require recording with the Registrar of Trade Marks for the protection of the new proprietor (Section 45).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Modes of Assignment (Section 38)

Section 38 enumerates **four permissible modes** in which a trademark and its goodwill may be assigned:

#### Mode 1: Assignment of an Unregistered Trademark Used in the Same Business
- The assignment must be **along with the goodwill of the business** concerned in all the goods or services for which the mark is used.
- A bare assignment of the mark without goodwill is **void** for an unregistered mark.
- *Example:* "Coca-Cola" assigning its unregistered mark to Pepsi would have to transfer the related bottling business as well.

#### Mode 2: Assignment of a Registered Trademark With or Without Goodwill
- A **registered trademark** may be assigned either:
  - **With the goodwill of the business** (the entire or part of it), OR
  - **Without the goodwill of the business.**
- However, if assigned **without goodwill**, the assignment must satisfy the **Section 40 territorial restriction** (described below).
- The assignee cannot use the mark in a manner that deceives the public about the source, quality, or connection with the assignor.

#### Mode 3: Assignment to a Company or Successor of Business
- A trademark may be assigned to a person who becomes the owner of one or more enterprises, in which the mark is used.
- This facilitates **corporate restructuring, mergers, and acquisitions**.

#### Mode 4: Assignment of Trademarks Used in Relation to Particular Goods or Services
- Assignment can be limited to **specific goods or services**, not necessarily all goods covered by the mark.
- This is the principle of **partial assignment**, subject to the rule against confusion.

### The "Associated Trademarks" Doctrine (Section 44)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Where two or more trademarks are registered as **associated marks** (under Section 16), they must be assigned **together as a single unit**. This prevents the fragmentation of a brand family. *Example:* The "TATA" group must transfer all associated marks ("TATA Steel," "TATA Motors," "TATA Salt") together, or none at all.

### Section 40: The Territorial Limitation Rule

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most heavily examined provision** in KTU exams on this topic.

When a **registered trademark is assigned without goodwill**, two cumulative conditions must be satisfied:
1. The assignment must be **limited to goods or services to be sold, exported, or otherwise traded in a particular territory** (within India or abroad).
2. The assignee must **not use the mark in relation to goods or services sold in any other territory** to avoid creating false impression of trade connection.

**Why this rule exists:** To prevent "split markets" where the original owner retains the mark for one geographic market and assigns it to a competitor in another, leading to parallel imports and consumer deception.

### Requirement of Writing and Registration (Section 42)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students assume that oral assignment of a trademark is valid. It is not.

- A **registered trademark can only be assigned by a written instrument** (deed of assignment).
- The **assignee must apply to the Registrar** in the prescribed form (Form TM-23 or TM-24) within **six months** of the assignment (extendable by the Registrar).
- Until the assignment is recorded in the Register, the assignment **does not affect third parties dealing with the assignor**.

### Transmission Provisions (Sections 42, 43, 45)

- **Section 42:** Special provisions relating to associated trademarks and the Registrar's power to refuse registration if assignment causes confusion.
- **Section 43:** Power of Registrar to allow correction of register on transmission.
- **Section 45:** Registration of assignments and transmissions.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Statutory Reference | Key Rule | KTU Memory Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| Assignment defined | **Section 37** | Trademark is a form of property | "Property tag" |
| Permissible modes | **Section 38(1)–(2)** | 4 modes recognized | "4M rule" |
| Assignability with/without goodwill | **Section 38(2)** | Allowed for registered marks only | "Goodwill is optional" |
| Territorial restriction | **Section 40** | No goodwill = territory-bound | "Geo-fence rule" |
| Associated marks | **Section 44** | Must be assigned as a block | "Block transfer" |
| Written instrument required | **Section 42(1)** | Mandatory for assignment | "Writing rule" |
| Application to register | **Section 42(2)** | Form TM-23/TM-24, 6 months | "Register within 6" |
| Transmission recognition | **Section 43** | On death, insolvency, court decree | "Law moves title" |
| Third-party protection | **Section 45(1)** | Until recorded, no effect on third parties | "Notice to public" |
| Rectification of register | **Section 58** | Wrong entry can be cancelled | "Cleanup power" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Public Notice Function:** Registration of assignment/transmission in the Trade Marks Register serves a **constructive notice** function. The Trade Marks Register is a public document, and any search by a third party (e.g., a potential licensee or buyer) is deemed to give them constructive knowledge of all recorded entries.

### Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

In engineering-driven industries (electronics, automotive, software, IoT), the trademark assignment machinery enables:
- **Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A):** When Infosys acquired a smaller tech firm, the trademark transfer formalities fell under Sections 38–45.
- **IP-backed financing:** Banks assess trademark portfolios as collateral; clean assignment chains are essential for valuation.
- **Franchise and licensing models:** The Coca-Cola bottling ecosystem across states relies on precise assignment documentation.
- **Cross-border technology transfers:** Foreign engineering firms assigning their marks to Indian joint ventures must comply with Section 40 territorial norms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### A. Step-by-Step Legal Analysis Framework: "Is the Assignment Valid?"

For a KTU 14-mark answer, a student must structure the validity analysis of a trademark assignment as follows:

**Step 1: Identify the Mark and the Parties**
- Is the mark **registered** or **unregistered**?
- Identify the **assignor** (current proprietor) and the **assignee** (proposed new owner).

**Step 2: Check the Form of Transfer**
- For a **registered mark**: Has a **written instrument** (deed) been executed? (Section 42)
- For an **unregistered mark**: Only valid if transferred **along with the goodwill of the business** (Section 38(1)).

**Step 3: Apply the Mode of Assignment (Section 38)**
- Determine which of the 4 statutory modes applies.
- Confirm the assignment is for a valid consideration or as a gift.

**Step 4: Check the Goodwill Question**
- If goodwill is included → straightforward assignment.
- If goodwill is excluded → invoke **Section 40** territorial restriction.

**Step 5: Verify Territorial Compliance (if no goodwill)**
$$
\text{Validity} = (\text{Goods limited to specified territory}) \;\land\; (\text{No use in other territories})
$$
Both conditions are **conjunctive** — failure of either invalidates the assignment.

**Step 6: Examine Associated Trademarks (Section 44)**
$$
\text{If associated marks exist} \Rightarrow \text{All must be assigned together}
$$
Violation renders the assignment **void ab initio**.

**Step 7: Check Filing with the Registrar (Section 45)**
- Application in **Form TM-23** (assignment) or **TM-24** (transmission) must be filed within **six (6) months** of the event.
- On such application, the Registrar shall, on being satisfied, **record the change in the Register**.

**Step 8: Conclude on Validity and Third-Party Effect**
- Valid assignment + recorded = **fully effective against all parties**.
- Valid assignment + not recorded = effective **inter se** (between parties) but **not against third parties**.

### B. Analytical Matrix: Assignment vs. Transmission vs. Licensing

| Test | Assignment | Transmission | Licensing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Change of ownership** | Yes (permanent) | Yes (permanent) | No (temporary use) |
| **Source of transfer** | Contract | Death / Court / Law | Agreement |
| **Registration needed** | Yes (Form TM-23) | Yes (Form TM-24) | No (but recordable) |
| **Goodwill transfer** | Optional (for registered) / Mandatory (for unregistered) | Inherits with the property | No |
| **Consideration** | Yes (typically) | No | Yes (royalty) |
| **KTU Section** | 37, 38, 42 | 38, 43, 45 | 49 |
| **Effect on Register** | New proprietor entered | New proprietor entered | Licensee noted |

### C. Worked Numerical/Logical Example

**Problem:** "A" owns the registered trademark "FLUXION" for industrial sensors. "A" wishes to assign it to "B" only for the state of Tamil Nadu, without goodwill. "A" also holds the associated mark "FLUXION-PRO" for the same goods. Is the assignment valid?

**Step 1:** Registered mark → written instrument mandatory ✓ (assume executed)
**Step 2:** No goodwill transfer → **Section 40 applies**
**Step 3:** Territorial limitation: Limited to Tamil Nadu only ✓
**Step 4:** Restriction on B: B must not use the mark outside Tamil Nadu — must be covenanted in the deed
**Step 5:** Associated mark "FLUXION-PRO" exists → **Section 44 requires joint assignment**
**Step 6:** Result: The assignment is **invalid** because A retained "FLUXION-PRO." To make it valid, A must either:
  - Assign "FLUXION-PRO" along with "FLUXION" to B, OR
  - Apply to the Registrar for **dissolution of the association** between the two marks.

**Step 7:** Final act — file Form TM-23 with the Registrar within 6 months, else third parties are not bound.

### D. Procedural Flow (Recorded in Plain Algebraic Logic)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Event} &\rightarrow \text{Deed of Assignment / Transmission} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Form TM-23 (Assignment) or TM-24 (Transmission)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Submission to Registrar of Trade Marks} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Registrar's Examination (6-month window)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Entry in the Register of Trade Marks} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Public constructive notice established} \\
&\rightarrow \text{New proprietor protected against third parties}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Until the Registrar records the assignment/transmission in the Register, the new proprietor cannot sue for infringement in his own name for transactions occurring after the assignment (per Section 45 proviso).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Conceptual Map of Trademark Transfer Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trademark Owner - Proprietor] --> B{Mode of Transfer}
    B -->|During Lifetime| C[Assignment]
    B -->|By Operation of Law| D[Transmission]

    C --> E[Voluntary Contract]
    C --> F[Section 38 Modes - 4 Types]
    C --> G[Written Instrument Mandatory]

    D --> H[Inheritance / Will]
    D --> I[Insolvency / Bankruptcy]
    D --> J[Court Decree / Merger]

    E --> K[With Goodwill]
    E --> L[Without Goodwill - Section 40]

    K --> M[Form TM-23 to Registrar]
    L --> M
    F --> M
    G --> M
    H --> N[Form TM-24 to Registrar]
    I --> N
    J --> N

    M --> O[Entry in Trade Marks Register]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Constructive Notice to Public]
    P --> Q[New Proprietor Protected]
```

### Diagram 2: Validity Test Block Architecture for Assignment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Assignment Proposed] --> Q1{Is the mark registered?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Written Instrument Required - Section 42]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Goodwill must be transferred - Section 38(1)]

    Q2 --> Q4{Goodwill transferred?}
    Q3 --> EndNo[Valid Assignment Path]

    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5[Proceed to Associated Mark Check]
    Q4 -->|No| Q6[Apply Section 40 Territorial Limit]

    Q6 --> Q7{Territory restricted?}
    Q7 -->|Yes| Q5
    Q7 -->|No| EndInvalid1[Invalid - Public Confusion Risk]

    Q5 --> Q8{Associated Marks exist?}
    Q8 -->|Yes| Q9[Transfer all together - Section 44]
    Q8 -->|No| EndValid[Valid - File Form TM-23]

    Q9 --> EndValid
    EndNo --> EndValid
    EndInvalid1 --> EndNote[Document is void]
    EndValid --> Step1[File within 6 months]
    Step1 --> Step2[Register records the change]
    Step2 --> Step3[Full effect against third parties]
```

### Diagram 3: Distinction Tree - Assignment vs Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[Trademark Transfer] --> A[Assignment]
    Root --> B[Transmission]

    A --> A1[Contract-based]
    A --> A2[Lifetime of proprietor]
    A --> A3[Section 38 modes]
    A --> A4[Form TM-23]

    B --> B1[Law-based]
    B --> B2[Post death / Court order]
    B --> B3[Sections 38, 43]
    B --> B4[Form TM-24]
```

### Diagram 4: Procedural Timeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Event - Assignment or Transmission] --> T1[Month 0 - Deed Executed]
    T1 --> T2[Within 6 months - Application to Registrar]
    T2 --> T3[Registrar Examination]
    T3 --> T4[Entry in Register]
    T4 --> T5[Public Notice Effective]
    T5 --> T6[Full Proprietary Rights Crystallize]
    T1 -.Misses 6 months.-> T7[Extension Application under Section 42]
    T7 -.Approved.-> T3
    T7 -.Rejected.-> T8[Assignment is inter-se only - no third-party effect]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** Examiners often award 1–2 marks for correctly drawn "flow-based" diagrams in a 14-mark question. Use these as visual reinforcement in your answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define "assignment" and "transmission" of a trademark. How do they differ?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Assignment** is a voluntary transfer of ownership of a trademark from one person (assignor) to another (assignee) by a contract during the lifetime of the proprietor, governed by Sections 37, 38, and 42 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999.
- **Transmission** is a transfer of title by operation of law, e.g., by inheritance, insolvency, or court decree, governed by Sections 38, 43, and 45.
- The key difference: assignment is by **act of parties** (with consent and usually for consideration), while transmission occurs **without consent** and generally **without consideration** (e.g., death, court order).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
*What is the requirement of writing under Section 42 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- Under **Section 42(1)**, a **registered trademark can be assigned only by a written instrument**.
- An oral or implied assignment is **void**.
- The instrument must clearly identify the mark, the assignor, the assignee, the goods/services, and whether goodwill is included.
- Under **Section 42(2)**, the assignee must apply to the Registrar (in Form TM-23) within **six months** of the assignment, which is extendable by the Registrar.
- Until recorded, the assignment does **not affect third parties** (Section 45).

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**
*(a) Discuss in detail the various modes of assignment of trademarks as enumerated under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. (7 Marks)*

*(b) Explain the territorial restriction imposed by Section 40 on assignment of registered trademarks without goodwill. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Section 38 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999 permits assignment of both registered and unregistered trademarks as a form of property, subject to statutory restrictions designed to prevent public deception.

**Mode 1 — Unregistered mark with goodwill (1 mark):**
- An unregistered trademark can be assigned **only along with the goodwill of the business** in which it is used.
- Without goodwill, the assignment is **void**.

**Mode 2 — Registered mark with or without goodwill (1.5 marks):**
- A registered trademark may be assigned with or without goodwill.
- However, if without goodwill, the territorial limitation under **Section 40** applies.
- Goods and services must be clearly demarcated.

**Mode 3 — Assignment to a successor of business (1 mark):**
- Trademark may be assigned to a person who becomes the owner of one or more enterprises in which the mark is used.
- Facilitates corporate restructuring, mergers, and acquisitions.

**Mode 4 — Assignment for specific goods/services (1 mark):**
- Trademark may be assigned in relation to particular goods or services only, not necessarily the entire register.
- Subject to anti-confusion doctrine (Section 41).

**Associated Trademarks Rule (1 mark):**
- Under **Section 44**, where trademarks are associated, they must be assigned **together** as a block.
- Single-point fragmentation is prohibited.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The four modes balance the proprietary right of the owner with the consumer interest in not being misled.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Section 40 is a **restrictive provision** designed to prevent the practice of "market splitting," which could result in parallel goods bearing the same mark being introduced into the same market by different owners, leading to consumer confusion.

**Scope of the Provision (1 mark):**
Applies only when a **registered trademark is assigned without goodwill** of the business.

**Twin Conditions (3 marks):**
1. **Goods/services must be limited to a specific territory** — The assignment deed must expressly state the territory (e.g., a state, a region, or a country) in which the assignee will use the mark.
2. **Covenant against use elsewhere** — The assignee must contractually undertake **not to use the mark** in relation to goods/services sold in any other territory.
- Both conditions are **cumulative** (AND logic). Failure of either invalidates the assignment.

**Illustration (1 mark):**
If A assigns "AQUA" to B for Kerala only, B cannot export "AQUA" branded products to Karnataka, Maharashtra, or abroad. The territorial limit creates a "geo-fence."

**Rationale (1 mark):**
- Protects the public from deception regarding origin.
- Prevents the assignee from free-riding on the assignor's reputation in unrelated markets.
- Maintains the distinct identity of goods in different markets.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Section 40 is a critical anti-confusion mechanism and a high-frequency KTU topic.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**
*(a) Examine the provisions relating to transmission of trademarks under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. (7 Marks)*

*(b) Critically analyze the role of the Registrar in registering assignments and transmissions. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Transmission of a trademark is the transfer of title by operation of law, recognized under **Sections 38, 42, 43, and 45** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999.

**Modes of Transmission (3 marks):**
1. **Transmission by inheritance / will** — Upon death of the proprietor, the trademark devolves upon legal heirs or beneficiaries under a will. The estate of the deceased becomes the new proprietor.
2. **Transmission by operation of law** — Includes court decrees (e.g., divorce settlements), insolvency proceedings, and corporate amalgamations.
3. **Transmission under succession** — Recognized both in the case of registered and unregistered trademarks.

**Procedure for Registration (1.5 marks):**
- The person acquiring title must apply to the Registrar in **Form TM-24**, supported by documentary proof (death certificate, succession certificate, court order, etc.).
- Filing must occur within **six months**, extendable by the Registrar.
- Once recorded, the Register reflects the change of ownership.

**Protection of Transferee (1 mark):**
- Upon entry in the Register, the new proprietor enjoys all rights of the original proprietor, including the right to sue for infringement.
- The Registrar is empowered under **Section 43** to rectify any errors in the register consequent to transmission.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Transmission ensures continuity of brand ownership across generations and corporate restructurings, balancing private property rights with public notice.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Registrar of Trade Marks is the statutory authority responsible for maintaining the **Register of Trade Marks** as a public document. Sections 42 and 45 confer specific powers relating to recording assignments and transmissions.

**Powers of the Registrar (3 marks):**
1. **Accepting applications** in Form TM-23 (assignment) or TM-24 (transmission).
2. **Examining validity** of the instrument — ensuring it complies with Section 38 modes, Section 40 territorial rule, and Section 44 associated marks doctrine.
3. **Recording entries** in the Register upon satisfaction.
4. **Refusing registration** if the assignment causes confusion, or if associated marks are not transferred together.

**Constructive Notice Function (1.5 marks):**
- The Register is a public document; any third party who conducts a search is **deemed to have constructive notice** of all recorded entries.
- This protects the new proprietor against prior unrecorded interests.
- The proviso to **Section 45(1)** protects third parties who acted in good faith before the assignment was recorded.

**Time Limit and Extensions (1 mark):**
- Default time limit: **6 months** from the date of assignment/transmission.
- The Registrar has **discretion to extend** the period on a case-by-case basis.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The Registrar acts as a gatekeeper ensuring that the assignment/transmission complies with the Act, balancing private rights with public interest in a clean, reliable Register.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Marks are **frequently lost** by students who **fail to cite section numbers** (e.g., saying "the Act says..." instead of "Section 40 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999 provides...").
> - Avoid vague answers like "assignment is the transfer of a mark." Examiners expect the **distinction between goodwill and non-goodwill assignments** and the **territorial restriction rule** to be explicitly stated.
> - **Do not confuse assignment with licensing.** Licensing (Section 49) does not transfer ownership — it only grants a right to use. Mixing these is a common 2-mark deduction.
> - Always mention **Form TM-23 (assignment) and Form TM-24 (transmission)** when discussing procedure.
> - In diagrams, use **block labels, not raw sentences**, to avoid Mermaid syntax errors.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Assignment** = voluntary, contractual, lifetime of proprietor (Section 37, 38, 42).
- ✅ **Transmission** = involuntary, by operation of law — death, insolvency, court order (Sections 38, 43, 45).
- ✅ **Four modes of assignment** are enumerated under Section 38.
- ✅ **Unregistered mark** can be assigned **only with goodwill** of the business.
- ✅ **Registered mark** can be assigned **with or without goodwill** (Section 38(2)).
- ✅ **Without goodwill = territorial restriction** (Section 40) — goods must be limited to a defined territory.
- ✅ **Associated trademarks** must be transferred **together as a block** (Section 44).
- ✅ **Written instrument is mandatory** for assignment of a registered mark (Section 42).
- ✅ **Form TM-23** for assignment; **Form TM-24** for transmission.
- ✅ **6-month filing window** with the Registrar; extension is discretionary.
- ✅ **Until recorded, no effect on third parties** (Section 45 proviso).
- ✅ **Registrar's role** = record keeper, validator, gatekeeper against confusion.
- ✅ **Trademarks are property** — recognized as such for both registered and unregistered marks (Section 21).
- ✅ **Public notice function** of the Register is central to the assignment/transmission machinery.
- ✅ **Rectification power** of the Registrar (Section 58) covers wrong entries due to defective assignments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for this Topic:** *"Property can travel, but consumer trust cannot. The assignment and transmission provisions in the Trade Marks Act are the legal engineering that enables brand ownership to move while keeping the marketplace honest."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passing Off
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Passing Off

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — PASSING OFF

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Passing Off** is a *common law tort* that protects the **goodwill** and **reputation** associated with a business, its goods, or services, even when the business has no registered trademark. It prevents one trader from misrepresenting its goods/services as those of another, thereby causing damage to the established goodwill.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, Passing Off falls under **Module 4 — Protection of Unregistered Marks and Trade Secrets / Enforcement of IP Rights**, and is firmly rooted in the **Equity and Common Law tradition** (originally a branch of the tort of *deceit*). The action is founded on the principle that *"a man may not sell his own goods under the pretence that they are the goods of another man."*

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have been running a small tea stall named **"KTU Chai Corner"** for 25 years in Kerala. Customers recognize you by name, by the taste of your *kattan chai*, and by the look of your steel tumbler. You never registered the name. Suddenly, a new shop opens next door — same colour boards, same name, same steel tumbler, same recipe. Customers are confused. The new shop is **"passing off"** its tea as yours.

> **In simple words:** Passing Off is the law's way of saying — *you cannot wear someone else's identity, even if they forgot to write it down in a government register.*

### 1.2 Key Terminology for KTU Board Answers

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Goodwill** | The attractive force that brings in custom; the benefit and advantage of a good name, reputation, and connection. |
| **Common Law Right** | A right enforceable through court decisions, not statutes (in India, supplemented by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**). |
| **Slander of Goods / Title** | A related tort where false statements are made about a competitor's goods. |
| **Misrepresentation** | A false representation made to the public, intentionally or negligently. |
| **Trinity Test** | The threefold test laid down in *Erven Warnink v. Townend* — Reputation, Misrepresentation, Damage. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In India, although Passing Off is a common law remedy, **Section 27(2) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999** expressly preserves the right to sue for Passing Off, regardless of whether the mark is registered. This makes Passing Off a vital statutory-adjacent remedy for **unregistered** trademarks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evolution of Passing Off — Legal Journey

The doctrine has matured over centuries. For KTU answers, write this in chronological order:

1. **Pre-Industrial Era (1700s)** — Action based on *fraud/deceit*. Plaintiff had to prove actual fraudulent intent.
2. **19th Century — Reddaway v. Banham (1896)** — Shift to *likelihood of confusion*. Goodwill emerged as a protectable asset.
3. **20th Century — Erven Warnink v. Townend (1979)** — The House of Lords crystallized the modern **Five-Element Test** (often called the *Advocate General's Test*).
4. **Modern Era — Reckitt & Colman v. Borden (1990)** — Known as the **"Jif Lemon" case**, this is the definitive UK authority.
5. **Indian Context — Cadila Healthcare v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals (2001)** — The Supreme Court adopted the **classic trinity** as the Indian test.

## 2.2 The Essential Elements (The KTU High-Yield "Trinity")

For every KTU answer on Passing Off, the examiner expects you to identify and explain these three classical elements:

### **Element 1 — Reputation / Goodwill**
- The plaintiff must prove that its business, goods, or services enjoy **a name or reputation in the market**.
- The reputation must be **substantial** and identifiable by a class of consumers.
- *Goodwill ≠ Reputation*. Goodwill is the **legal proprietary right** that arises once reputation is established and is attached to the business, not to the owner personally.

### **Element 2 — Misrepresentation**
- The defendant must have made a **false representation** to the public, intentionally or otherwise.
- This misrepresentation must be **likely to deceive** an ordinary consumer, not a gullible one.
- The misrepresentation may arise from use of the same name, get-up, packaging, slogan, or even colour combination.

### **Element 3 — Damage / Likelihood of Damage**
- The plaintiff must show **actual damage** or a **real probability/likelihood of damage** to its goodwill.
- Damage may be in the form of loss of sales, dilution of reputation, or injury to business identity.

## 2.3 The Extended Five-Element Test (Erven Warnink / Reckitt)

For a 14-mark KTU answer, expand the Trinity into this academic five-point structure:

1. **Goodwill or Reputation** attached to the goods/services.
2. **Misrepresentation** by the defendant leading (or likely to lead) the public to believe that the defendant's goods/services are those of the plaintiff.
3. **Damage** (actual or likely) to the plaintiff as a result of the mistaken belief.

*(The two further elements added by the UK Lords are usually treated as sub-categories of the above three.)*

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Passing Off vs. Trademark Infringement

| Parameter | Passing Off | Trademark Infringement |
|---|---|---|
| **Statutory Basis** | Common Law Tort (Sec. 27(2), TM Act 1999) | Statutory (Sec. 29, TM Act 1999) |
| **Registration Required** | **No** — protects unregistered marks | **Yes** — only registered marks |
| **Plaintiff's Burden** | Prove goodwill + misrepresentation + damage | Use of identical/similar mark in respect of identical/similar goods |
| **Goods/Services** | Must be similar or related | Section 29 covers identical goods (no proof of confusion needed) |
| **Remedies** | Injunction, damages, accounts of profits, delivery up | Same, plus statutory damages |
| **Territorial Scope** | Limited to area where goodwill subsists | Pan-India (or designated jurisdiction) |
| **Honest Concurrent Use** | Generally not a defence | Defence under Sec. 12, TM Act |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statutory Reference (KTU Favourite):** *Section 27(2) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999* — *"Nothing in this Act shall be deemed to affect the right of action against any person for passing off or the right to recover damages or seek any other relief in respect of such passing off."*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Startups and SMEs** in Kerala's tech/industrial corridors (Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut) rely heavily on Passing Off since they often launch brands *before* TM registration is granted.
- **Domain Name Disputes** under the .IN Dispute Resolution Policy (INDRP) often invoke Passing Off.
- **Software & Tech Products**: Many Indian tech firms (Infosys, TCS) fought early brand battles on Passing Off grounds before TM registration matured.
- **Real-World Utility**: It protects **trade dress**, **product packaging**, **get-up**, **colour schemes**, and even **trade libels**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Legal Derivation: From Deceit → Goodwill → Modern Trinity

Below is the structured analytical derivation that KTU examiners reward with full marks.

### **Step 1 — Origins in the Tort of Deceit (Action on the Case)**

Originally, Passing Off was treated as a species of deceit. The plaintiff had to demonstrate that:
- The defendant made a false representation.
- The defendant knew it was false (scienter).
- The plaintiff suffered damage as a consequence.

This narrow origin was set out in cases such as **Singleton v. Boulton (1788)**.

### **Step 2 — The Doctrinal Shift (Reddaway v. Banham, 1896)**

In *Reddaway v. Banham*, the defendant sold "Camel Hair Belting" even though it was made of goat hair. The plaintiff had used the same description for years for genuine camel-hair products. The House of Lords held that the defendant had passed off its goods by capitalising on the plaintiff's established trade name.

**Legal shift:** The action moved away from fraud → to **likelihood of confusion / damage**.

### **Step 3 — The Modern Doctrinal Anchor (Erven Warnink v. Townend, 1979)**

The plaintiff, the Dutch distillers of **Advocaat**, sued the English defendants for selling a drink called "Keeling's Old English Advocaat." The House of Lords, per Lord Diplock, identified the following five ingredients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Modern Passing Off (Erven Warnink)} = \{\, & \text{Goodwill of the plaintiff}, \\
& \text{Misrepresentation by defendant}, \\
& \text{Intent or likelihood to deceive}, \\
& \text{Actual or probable damage to plaintiff}, \\
& \text{Defendant's conduct being calculated to injure}\, \}
\end{aligned}
$$

These five ingredients are collapsed into the **three classical elements** (goodwill, misrepresentation, damage) for KTU-level questions.

### **Step 4 — Codification in India (Cadila Healthcare v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals, 2001)**

The Indian Supreme Court endorsed the **classic trinity** as the operative test:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Passing Off (Indian Law)} \equiv \big[\, \text{Goodwill} \;\wedge\; \text{Misrepresentation} \;\wedge\; \text{Damage} \,\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

The Supreme Court further laid down **seven factors** to assess confusion in pharmaceutical cases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confusion Score} = f(\, & \text{Nature of marks}, \\
& \text{Degree of resemblance}, \\
& \text{Nature of goods}, \\
& \text{Class of purchasers}, \\
& \text{Mode of purchase}, \\
& \text{Trade channels}, \\
& \text{Field of application} \,)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The Indian Supreme Court has consistently held that Passing Off is a **trans-actional tort**, i.e., the right is not in the mark *per se* but in the **goodwill attached to the business**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Application of the Trinity

| Element | What to Prove | Standard of Proof | Indian Case Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Goodwill** | Trade name is recognized in market by class of consumers | Reputation + Duration + Sales volume | *Ammritdhara Pharmacy v. Satya Deo Gupta (1963)* |
| **Misrepresentation** | Likelihood of deception among ordinary consumers | Average consumer test | *Cadbury India v. Neeraj Food Products (2007)* |
| **Damage** | Actual loss or likelihood of loss | Real and tangible | *Reckitt & Colman v. Borden* (UK authority) |

## 3.3 Defences Available Against a Passing Off Action

For KTU 14-mark questions, mention these defences:

1. **Honest Concurrent Use** — Both parties have used the mark honestly over a long period.
2. **Descriptive Use** — The mark is a generic or descriptive term (e.g., "Cold Cream").
3. **No Misrepresentation** — Differences are sufficient to avoid confusion.
4. **No Goodwill in Plaintiff's Mark** — Plaintiff has no local reputation.
5. **Delay / Acquiescence** — Plaintiff waited too long to sue, leading defendant to believe it was acceptable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Pillars of Passing Off (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Passing Off Action Filed] --> B{Does the Plaintiff have Goodwill?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Has the Defendant Misrepresented Goods/Services?}
    B -- No --> X[Suit Dismissed: No Reputation]
    C -- Yes --> D{Is there Actual or Likely Damage?}
    C -- No --> Y[Suit Dismissed: No Confusion]
    D -- Yes --> Z[Court grants Relief to Plaintiff]
    D -- No --> W[Suit Dismissed: No Damage]
    Z --> Z1[Injunction]
    Z --> Z2[Damages / Accounts of Profits]
    Z --> Z3[Delivery Up of Infringing Goods]
```

## 4.2 Passing Off vs. Trademark Infringement — Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Trade Dispute Arises] --> Q1{Is the Mark Registered?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Use of Same/Similar Mark on Same/Similar Goods?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Does the Business have Goodwill?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> PathA[Trademark Infringement under Sec. 29]
    Q2 -- No --> PathB[No Action]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Likelihood of Confusion?}
    Q3 -- No --> PathC[No Action]
    Q4 -- Yes --> PathD[Passing Off Action under Sec. 27 2]
    Q4 -- No --> PathE[No Action]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Passing Off Suit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plaintiff: Established Trader] --> R1[Reputation in Market]
    R1 --> G1[Goodwill Attached to Business]
    P1 --> S1[Files Suit in District Court / High Court]

    D1[Defendant: New Entrant] --> M1[Misrepresentation via Similar Mark/Get-up]
    M1 --> C1[Likely Confusion among Consumers]

    G1 --> S1
    C1 --> S1
    S1 --> E1[Court Applies Trinity Test]
    E1 --> O1[Relief Granted]
    O1 --> Inj[Injunction]
    O1 --> Dam[Monetary Damages]
    O1 --> Del[Delivery Up of Goods]
    O1 --> Acc[Accounts of Profits]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Passing Off. [3 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
Passing Off is a common law tort that protects the goodwill and reputation attached to a trader's business, even when the trader has not registered the mark under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. It is actionable under **Section 27(2)** of the Act. To succeed, the plaintiff must establish the classic trinity: **(i) goodwill or reputation** in its business, **(ii) misrepresentation** by the defendant likely to deceive the public, and **(iii) actual or probable damage** to the plaintiff's goodwill.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Passing Off and Trademark Infringement. [3 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer (Table for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Passing Off | Trademark Infringement |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Common Law (Sec. 27(2), TM Act) | Statutory (Sec. 29, TM Act) |
| **Registration** | Not required | Compulsory |
| **Burden of Proof** | Goodwill + Misrepresentation + Damage | Use of identical/similar mark |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Discuss the essential ingredients of Passing Off. State the leading case laws. [14 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Sub-parts (a) and (b)]**

#### **Part (a) — Essential Ingredients [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The action for Passing Off, as established by the House of Lords in **Erven Warnink v. Townend (1979)** and **Reckitt & Colman v. Borden (1990)**, requires the plaintiff to prove three classical ingredients:

**1. Goodwill or Reputation (2 marks):**
The plaintiff must prove that its business enjoys an established reputation in the relevant market. Goodwill is the attractive force which brings in custom; it is the benefit and advantage of a good name. In India, the Supreme Court in **Ammritdhara Pharmacy v. Satya Deo Gupta (1963)** held that the name "Ammritdhara" had acquired distinctiveness through long user, entitling it to protection.

**2. Misrepresentation (2 marks):**
The defendant must have made a false representation, intentionally or otherwise, calculated to deceive the public into believing that its goods/services are those of the plaintiff. The test is whether an **ordinary, unwary consumer** is likely to be confused. In **Cadbury India v. Neeraj Food Products (2007)**, the defendant sold chewing gum as "Glue-Cadbury" which the Court held to be misrepresentation.

**3. Damage or Likelihood of Damage (3 marks):**
The plaintiff must show actual damage, or, more commonly, a real and tangible likelihood of damage. Damage can take the form of loss of sales, dilution of reputation, or injury to business identity. The House of Lords in **Reckitt & Colman v. Borden (1990)** ("Jif Lemon" case) held that damage need not be precisely quantifiable; it suffices if there is a probability of injury.

#### **Part (b) — Leading Case Laws [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Reddaway v. Banham (1896)** — The plaintiff sold "Camel Hair Belting" made of genuine camel hair. The defendant sold goat-hair belting under the same name. Held: Passing Off established; the defendant's conduct would injure the plaintiff's goodwill. This case shifted the action away from requiring proof of fraud to mere likelihood of confusion.

2. **Erven Warnink v. Townend (1979)** — The "Advocaat" case. Established the modern five-element test, later consolidated into the classic trinity.

3. **Reckitt & Colman v. Borden (1990)** — The "Jif Lemon" case. The plaintiff used a lemon-shaped plastic container with a real lemon's dimpled texture. The defendant sold lemon juice in a similarly shaped container. The House of Lords confirmed the trinity and awarded relief.

4. **Cadila Healthcare v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals (2001)** — Indian Supreme Court applied the trinity and laid down seven factors for assessing confusion in pharmaceutical product cases.

5. **Ammritdhara Pharmacy v. Satya Deo Gupta (1963)** — Early Indian authority; similarity of trade names in medicinal products likely to deceive the public.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse Passing Off with the tort of **deceit**. Modern Passing Off does *not* require proof of fraudulent intent — mere misrepresentation + likelihood of damage is enough.
> - **Do NOT** state that Passing Off requires registration. The opposite is true.
> - **Do NOT** forget to cite **Section 27(2)** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. Examiner's pet question.
> - **Do NOT** omit leading cases. At least **3 cases** (with facts + holding) are expected for 7 marks.
> - **Do NOT** claim that goodwill belongs to the mark — it is attached to the *business*, not the *trademark*.

---

### **Question B — Explain the doctrine of Passing Off with reference to Indian and English case laws. How is it different from Trademark Infringement? [14 Marks]**
> **[KTU University Exam — Internal Choice Model]**

#### **Part (a) — Doctrine of Passing Off with Case Laws [8 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The doctrine of Passing Off is rooted in the equitable principle that *"no man ought to sell his own goods as those of another."* It is a common law remedy preserved in India by **Section 27(2)** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999.

**Origin (2 marks):** The action emerged from the writ of *deceit* but evolved into an action on the case. In **Singleton v. Boulton (1788)**, the Court allowed an action where the defendant misled consumers.

**English Development (3 marks):** The doctrine matured through *Reddaway v. Banham (1896)* → *Erven Warnink v. Townend (1979)* → *Reckitt & Colman v. Borden (1990)*, culminating in the **classic trinity**: Goodwill, Misrepresentation, Damage.

**Indian Position (3 marks):** The Indian Supreme Court endorsed the trinity in **Cadila Healthcare v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals (2001)** and **Ammritdhara Pharmacy v. Satya Deo Gupta (1963)**. The Indian Courts apply a multi-factor test considering the **nature of goods, class of buyers, trade channels, and similarity of marks**.

#### **Part (b) — Difference from Trademark Infringement [6 Marks]**

**Model Solution (Detailed Table for 6 marks):**

| Sl. | Parameter | Passing Off | Trademark Infringement |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nature** | Common Law Tort | Statutory Right |
| 2 | **Registration** | Not mandatory | Mandatory |
| 3 | **Statute** | Sec. 27(2), TM Act 1999 | Sec. 29, TM Act 1999 |
| 4 | **Burden** | Goodwill + Misrepresentation + Damage | Use of identical/similar mark |
| 5 | **Goods must be identical?** | Must be similar/related | Not required (under Sec. 29) |
| 6 | **Remedy for Loss** | Damages + Injunction + Delivery up | Damages + Injunction + Delivery up + Statutory Damages |
| 7 | **Territoriality** | Local goodwill determines scope | All-India protection (for registered TM) |
| 8 | **Defences** | Honest Concurrent Use, No Goodwill | Bona fide use, Limitations |

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Passing Off = Common Law Tort**, protects unregistered goodwill.
- ✅ **Section 27(2), TM Act 1999** is the statutory hook — memorize the section number.
- ✅ **Classic Trinity:** Goodwill + Misrepresentation + Damage.
- ✅ **Modern Five-Element Test** = Erven Warnink v. Townend (1979).
- ✅ **Indian Anchor Case** = Cadila Healthcare v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals (2001).
- ✅ **UK Anchor Case** = Reckitt & Colman v. Borden (1990) — "Jif Lemon."
- ✅ **Earliest Indian Case** = Ammritdhara Pharmacy v. Satya Deo Gupta (1963).
- ✅ **Goodwill belongs to the BUSINESS, not the trademark or the owner.**
- ✅ **Misrepresentation test** = ordinary unwary consumer (not gullible or sophisticated).
- ✅ **Damage** need not be actual — *likelihood* is sufficient.
- ✅ **Passing Off does NOT require fraud/intent** — mere representation suffices.
- ✅ **Honest Concurrent Use** is a defence but not always absolute.
- ✅ **Pharmaceutical cases** follow the seven-factor test from Cadila.
- ✅ **Remedies** — Injunction, Damages, Accounts of Profits, Delivery Up.
- ✅ **Always end with at least 3 leading cases** in a 14-mark answer.

> [!NOTE]
