> **Exam Tip:** In 14-mark answers, follow this structure: (i) Definition with Sec. 27(2), (ii) Classical Trinity, (iii) Three to Four Leading Cases, (iv) Comparison Table, (v) Defences, (vi) Conclusion. This framework guarantees full marks under KTU 2024 valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Act 2000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design Act 2000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design (Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000):** A *design* means only the **features of shape, configuration, pattern, ornament or composition of lines or colours** applied to any article, whether in two-dimensional or three-dimensional, or in both forms, by any industrial process or means, whether manual, mechanical or chemical, separate or combined, which in the finished article appeal to and are judged solely by the eye; but does not include any mode or principle of construction or anything which is in substance a mere mechanical device.

The **Designs Act, 2000** is the primary Indian legislation that governs the **registration, protection, and enforcement of industrial designs** in India. It repealed and replaced the colonial-era **Designs Act, 1911**, and is administered by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)** under the **Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective of the Act:** To protect novel and original visual designs applied to articles of manufacture, thereby promoting innovation, industrial design creativity, and fair competition in trade and industry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a design like the "outfit" of a product.**
> Imagine you design a brand-new water bottle. The water bottle itself (the *invention*) might be protected by a patent. But the **distinctive ribbed pattern, the colour combination, the ergonomic curve, and the shape of the cap** — that's the "outfit" or **visual appeal** of the bottle. The Designs Act 2000 protects that "outfit" from being copied by competitors.
>
> **Key insight:** A design protects how a product **looks**, not how it **works**.

## 1.3 Why Was the New Act Needed?

The 1911 Act was outdated and incompatible with India's obligations under the **TRIPS Agreement (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights)** of the **WTO**, which required a minimum **10-year protection term** for industrial designs. The 2000 Act was enacted to:

- **Comply with TRIPS obligations**.
- Modernize registration procedures.
- Strengthen enforcement and remedies.
- Expand the scope of protection to cover both **2D and 3D designs**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology for Board Exam:**
> - **Article** — Any article of manufacture and any substance, artificial or partly artificial.
> - **Proprietor** — The person in whose name the design is registered.
> - **Controller** — The Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks appointed under Section 4.
> - **Register** — The Register of Designs maintained by the Patent Office.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Salient Features of the Designs Act, 2000

| **Section / Provision** | **Key Feature** | **Explanation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 2(d)** | Definition of Design | Shape, configuration, pattern, ornament, or composition of lines/colours |
| **Section 3** | Prohibition of registration | Designs contrary to public order/morality excluded |
| **Section 4** | Controller of Designs | CGPDTM acts as Controller of Designs |
| **Section 5** | Appointment of Officers | Examining and other officers appointed |
| **Section 7** | Registration of Design | Application to Patent Office in prescribed form |
| **Section 11** | Priority Date | Date of application in India (or convention/foreign priority) |
| **Section 12** & **13** | Examination & Registration | Substantive examination for novelty & originality |
| **Section 16** | Copyright on Registration | 10 years from date of registration |
| **Section 17** | Restoration of lapsed designs | Restoration within 12 months of removal |
| **Section 19** | Assignment & Transmission | Designs are transferable like personal property |
| **Section 22** | Infringement of copyright | Civil remedies available |
| **Section 31** | Penalty for piracy | Imprisonment up to 2 years OR fine up to **₹50,000** |
| **Section 44** | Reciprocal arrangements | Protection to foreign nationals from notified countries |

## 2.2 Essential Conditions for Registration of a Design

For a design to be registered under the Act, it must satisfy **all** the following conditions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Novelty / Newness** — The design must be new or original, not previously published or used in India or anywhere else in the world before the date of application.
> 2. **Originality** — It must originate from the applicant's own intellectual creativity and not be a copy of an existing design.
> 3. **Applicability to an Article** — The design must be applicable to a particular article, whether 2D or 3D.
> 4. **Visible on Finished Article** — The features of the design must be visible on the finished article.
> 5. **Judged Solely by the Eye** — The design must be capable of being judged solely by the eye — functional/technical aspects are NOT considered.
> 6. **Not Disclosing Any Functional Feature** — The design must not disclose any function/mode of construction of the article.
> 7. **Not Contrary to Public Order or Morality** — Designs which are scandalous, obscene, or contrary to public order are prohibited.

## 2.3 What is *NOT* a Design (Section 2(d) Proviso)

> [!WARNING]
> The following are **excluded** from registration:
> - A **mode or principle of construction** (functional features).
> - **Mechanical devices** in substance.
> - **Trademarks** (protected under Trade Marks Act, 1999).
> - **Works of sculpture** (protected under Copyright Act, 1957).
> - **Books, pamphlets, periodicals** as such (copyrighted).
> - **Maps, plans, charts** (copyrighted).
> - **Computer programs** (copyrighted).
> - **Any non-physical / intangible design**.

## 2.4 Procedure for Registration (Step-by-Step)

> [!TIP]
> The registration process mirrors the patent application process in structure but is simpler.

1. **Filing of Application (Section 5 & 7)** — Application in Form-1 along with prescribed fee, four copies of representation (drawings/photographs) of the article, and statement of novelty (claim) to the Patent Office at Kolkata.
2. **Preliminary Examination (Section 8)** — Patent Office checks formalities and classification under the **Locarno Classification** (International Classification for Industrial Designs).
3. **Substantive Examination (Section 12)** — Examiner verifies novelty, originality, and compliance.
4. **Acceptance & Advertisement in Journal (Section 13)** — If accepted, the design is published in the **Designs Journal** for public opposition (within prescribed period).
5. **Registration & Grant of Certificate (Section 14)** — On acceptance, the design is entered in the **Register of Designs** and a certificate issued.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Value / Rule** | **Statutory Reference** |
|---|---|---|
| **Term of protection** | **10 years** from date of registration | Section 16 |
| **Maximum renewals** | 1 renewal period of 5 years (extended from 15 to 10+5 = 15 years under amended rules) | Section 16(2) |
| **Imprisonment for piracy** | Up to **2 years** (may extend to 3 years on subsequent conviction) | Section 31(1) |
| **Maximum fine** | Up to **₹50,000** (may extend to ₹1,00,000 on subsequent conviction) | Section 31(1) |
| **Restoration period** | Within **12 months** of removal from register | Section 17 |
| **Application fee (approx.)** | ₹1,000 to ₹4,000 depending on entity | Schedule of Fees |
| **Priority convention period** | **6 months** (Paris Convention priority) | Section 11 |
| **Jurisdiction for piracy suits** | District Court having jurisdiction (not below DC) | Section 22 |
| **International classification** | **Locarno Classification** (current 14th edition) | Schedule III |
| **Reciprocity countries** | UK, Ireland, Australia, New Zealand, Sri Lanka, etc. | Section 44 |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial design protection is critical in:**
> - **Automotive Industry** — Distinctive car body shapes (e.g., Tata Nexon grille design).
> - **Consumer Electronics** — iPhone/smartphone aesthetics.
> - **Furniture Industry** — Unique chair shapes.
> - **Fashion & Apparel** — Pattern designs on textiles.
> - **Jewellery & Handicrafts** — Ornamental patterns.
> - **Packaging** — Bottle, can, and container shapes.
>
> The Act enables Indian manufacturers and designers to legally prevent copycats, thus incentivizing design innovation and brand-building in **"Make in India"** initiatives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Duration of Protection Calculation

The term of design protection is a frequently examined KTU topic. Below is the **step-by-step logical derivation** of the protection period:

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{initial}} + T_{\text{renewal}} = 10 \text{ years} + 5 \text{ years} = 15 \text{ years (max)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic behind the 10+5 formula:**
> The Designs Act, 2000 originally granted **10 years** of protection from the date of registration. Under the **Designs (Amendment) Rules, 2021** and aligned with the original statutory provision, the proprietor may apply for a **renewal** for a further period of **5 years** upon expiry, by paying the prescribed renewal fee.

### Step-by-Step Expansion:

| **Step** | **Action** | **Statutory Reference** | **Time Period** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Date of filing of application | Section 7 | Day 0 |
| 2 | Date of registration on acceptance | Section 14 | Variable |
| 3 | Initial copyright term begins | Section 16(1) | From date of registration |
| 4 | Initial protection period | Section 16(1) | **10 years** |
| 5 | Application for renewal before expiry | Section 16(2) | Prior to completion of 10 years |
| 6 | Extended protection period | Section 16(2) | Additional **5 years** |
| 7 | **Total Maximum Protection** | Combined | **15 years** |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Penalties for Piracy

The penalty structure under **Section 31** of the Designs Act can be represented as:

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{piracy}} = \begin{cases} I \leq 2 \text{ years}, & F \leq \text{₹}50,000 & \text{First offence} \\ I \leq 3 \text{ years}, & F \leq \text{₹}1,00,000 & \text{Subsequent offence} \end{cases}}
$$

Where:
- $I$ = Term of imprisonment
- $F$ = Monetary fine (in ₹)
- Both punishments may be imposed **simultaneously** (cumulative).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Case Law Highlight (KTU Board Favourite):**
> In *Reckitt Benckiser (India) Pvt. Ltd. v. Wyeth Ltd.* and the *Britannia Industries v. ITC Ltd.* cases, the courts emphasized that for design infringement, the **"ordinary observer test"** is applied — if an ordinary observer would be deceived into thinking the article is the registered design, infringement is established.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Design Protection Worldwide

| **Jurisdiction** | **Term of Protection** | **Renewal** | **Examining System** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **India (Designs Act, 2000)** | 10 + 5 years | Yes (5 years) | Substantive examination |
| **European Union (EU Design Regulation)** | 5 + 5 = 10 years max (registered) | Renewable up to 5×5 years | Registration-based |
| **UK** | Up to 25 years (5+5+5+5+5) | Renewable 4 times | Substantive examination |
| **USA (Patent Act)** | **15 years** (design patent) | Non-renewable | Substantive examination |
| **Japan (Design Act)** | 20 years from registration date | Non-renewable | Substantive examination |
| **China (Patent Law)** | 15 years from filing date | Non-renewable | Substantive examination |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Infringement vs. Piracy

> [!NOTE]
> **Common confusion cleared:** *Infringement* and *Piracy* are related but distinct concepts under the Act.

| **Aspect** | **Infringement (Section 22)** | **Piracy (Section 31, Criminal)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Civil wrong | Criminal offence |
| **Forum** | District Court | Magistrate Court |
| **Remedy** | Injunction, damages, account of profits | Imprisonment + fine |
| **Burden of Proof** | Balance of probabilities | Beyond reasonable doubt |
| **Party Involved** | Registered proprietor vs. infringer | State vs. pirate |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Grounds for Cancellation (Section 19)

A registered design can be cancelled by the Controller on any of the following grounds:

1. Design was **not new or original** at the date of registration.
2. Design was **previously registered** in India.
3. Design was **published in India or elsewhere** prior to registration.
4. Design is **not registrable** under Section 3 (public order/morality).
5. Design was **fraudulently obtained**.
6. The design has been **used in a manner contrary to good commercial practice** (added via amendment).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Design Registration Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applicant files Form-1 Application] --> B[Preliminary Formality Check at Patent Office Kolkata]
    B --> C{Formality Compliance?}
    C -->|No| D[Rectification Notice to Applicant]
    D --> A
    C -->|Yes| E[Classification under Locarno System]
    E --> F[Substantive Examination by Examiner]
    F --> G{Novel and Original?}
    G -->|No| H[Refusal Order]
    G -->|Yes| I[Acceptance Order]
    I --> J[Advertisement in Designs Journal]
    J --> K{Opposition Received within Period?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Opposition Hearing before Controller]
    L --> M{Opposition Allowed?}
    M -->|Yes| H
    M -->|No| N[Registration in Register of Designs]
    K -->|No| N
    N --> O[Certificate of Registration Issued]
    O --> P[Copyright Subsists for 10 Years]
    P --> Q{Renewal Applied before Expiry?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Extended Protection for 5 Years]
    Q -->|No| S[Design Falls into Public Domain]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Scope of Design Protection (Section 2(d) Analysis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Visual Appearance of Article] --> B{Components}
    B --> C[Shape]
    B --> D[Configuration]
    B --> F[Pattern]
    B --> G[Ornament]
    B --> H[Composition of Lines or Colours]
    B --> I[2D, 3D, or Both Forms]
    C --> J{Applied to Article?}
    D --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -->|Yes| K{Judged Solely by Eye?}
    K -->|Yes| L{Excluded Categories?}
    K -->|No| M[NOT PROTECTABLE as Design]
    L -->|Mode/Principle of Construction| M
    L -->|Mechanical Device| M
    L -->|Trademark| M
    L -->|Copyrighted Work| M
    L -->|None| N[PROTECTABLE Industrial Design]
    L -->|Public Order/Morality Violation| M
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Rights & Remedies Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R[RIGHTS OF PROPRIETOR]
        R1[Exclusive right to use the design]
        R2[Right to assign or license]
        R3[Right to sue for infringement]
        R4[Right to seek damages]
        R5[Right to apply for renewal]
    end
    subgraph REM[REMEDIES FOR INFRINGEMENT]
        REM1[Civil: Injunction]
        REM2[Civil: Damages or Account of Profits]
        REM3[Civil: Order for Seizure of Infringing Articles]
        REM4[Criminal: Imprisonment up to 2 years]
        REM5[Criminal: Fine up to Rs.50,000]
    end
    subgraph DEF[DEFENCES AVAILABLE]
        DEF1[Prior publication in India or abroad]
        DEF2[Independent creation by defendant]
        DEF3[Design is not novel or original]
        DEF4[Registration was fraudulently obtained]
    end
    R --> REM
    REM --> DEF
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — International Design Protection Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IND[INDIA]
        IND1[Designs Act 2000]
        IND2[CGPDTM Administration]
        IND3[10 plus 5 year term]
    end
    subgraph INTL[INTERNATIONAL INSTRUMENTS]
        INTL1[Paris Convention 1883]
        INTL2[TRIPS Agreement 1994]
        INTL3[Hague Agreement 1999]
    end
    subgraph NAT[NATIONAL JURISDICTIONS]
        NAT1[EU Registered Community Design]
        NAT2[US Design Patent]
        NAT3[Japan Design Act]
        NAT4[China Design Patent]
    end
    IND --> INTL
    INTL --> NAT
    INTL1 --> IND2
    INTL2 --> IND3
```

## 4.5 Schematic — Conceptual Distinction: Patent vs. Design vs. Trademark

| **Property Right** | **Protects** | **Subject Matter** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | Function / Invention | How the product **works** | A new chemical formula for a drug |
| **Design** | Appearance / Aesthetics | How the product **looks** | The shape of a Coca-Cola bottle |
| **Trademark** | Source Identification | Brand identity | The "Coca-Cola" word mark or logo |
| **Copyright** | Creative Expression | Literary, artistic, musical works | The jingle of a TV advertisement |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU University Exam — Part A (3-Mark Questions)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

### **Question 1:** Define "Design" as per Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000. List any **four essential conditions** for a design to be registrable.

**Model Answer:**

As per **Section 2(d)** of the Designs Act, 2000, a *design* means only the features of **shape, configuration, pattern, ornament or composition of lines or colours** applied to any article, whether in two-dimensional or three-dimensional form, by any industrial process, which in the finished article **appeal to and are judged solely by the eye**, but does not include any mode or principle of construction.

The four essential conditions for registration are:

1. The design must be **new or novel**.
2. The design must be **original** (originating from the applicant's own creative work).
3. The design must be **applicable to an article**.
4. The design must be **judged solely by the eye** and not be purely functional.

---

### **Question 2:** What is the **term of protection** for a registered design? Can it be renewed?

**Model Answer:**

Under **Section 16(1)** of the Designs Act, 2000, the **copyright in a registered design** subsists for a period of **ten years** from the date of registration.

**Yes**, the protection can be **renewed** under **Section 16(2)** for a further period of **five years**, on application made in the prescribed form and upon payment of the prescribed renewal fee, prior to the expiry of the initial ten-year period.

Thus, the **maximum total term of protection** is **fifteen years (10 + 5)**, after which the design falls into the public domain.

---

## 5.2 KTU University Exam — Part B (14-Mark Questions)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Internal Choice Pattern]**

### **Choice A: Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **salient features and objectives** of the Designs Act, 2000. How does it differ from the earlier **Designs Act, 1911**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **procedure for registration of a design** under the Designs Act, 2000. Mention the role of the **Locarno Classification** in design registration. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer (a) — Salient Features and Objectives:**

The **Designs Act, 2000** was enacted to provide comprehensive protection for industrial designs in India, replacing the **Designs Act, 1911**. Its salient features are:

1. **Modern Definition of Design** — Section 2(d) provides a clear and comprehensive definition covering shape, configuration, pattern, ornament, and composition of lines/colors applied to 2D/3D articles.

2. **TRIPS Compliance** — The Act was enacted to bring Indian law in line with the **TRIPS Agreement**, which mandates a minimum **10-year protection term** for industrial designs (Article 25 of TRIPS).

3. **Term of Protection** — Provides **10 years** of protection from registration, renewable for an additional **5 years** (Section 16), totalling 15 years maximum.

4. **Reciprocal Arrangements** — **Section 44** provides for reciprocal protection to nationals of notified convention countries (e.g., UK, Australia, New Zealand, Ireland, Sri Lanka).

5. **Single Application for Multiple Designs** — **Rule 13** permits filing of a single application for multiple designs pertaining to the same class under the **Locarno Classification**, subject to payment of additional fees.

6. **Criminal and Civil Remedies** — Provides both **civil remedies (injunction, damages)** under **Section 22** and **criminal penalties (imprisonment up to 2 years + fine up to ₹50,000)** under **Section 31** for piracy.

7. **Restoration of Designs** — **Section 17** allows restoration of designs within **12 months** of removal from the register.

8. **Establishment of Design Authority** — Section 4 establishes the **Controller of Designs** (the Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks) as the administrative head.

#### **Differences from the Designs Act, 1911:**

| **Aspect** | **Designs Act, 1911** | **Designs Act, 2000** |
|---|---|---|
| Term of protection | 5 + 5 = 10 years max | 10 + 5 = 15 years max |
| TRIPS Compliance | Not TRIPS-compliant | Fully TRIPS-compliant |
| Definition scope | Narrow | Broad (2D and 3D) |
| Reciprocity | Limited | Extended via Section 44 |
| Computer programs | Not specified | Explicitly excluded |
| Scope of criminal penalty | Limited | Imprisonment up to 2 years + fine |

---

#### **Model Answer (b) — Procedure for Registration:**

The procedure for registration of a design is governed by **Sections 5, 7, 8, 12, 13, and 14** of the Act and the **Designs Rules, 2001**.

**Step 1 — Filing of Application (Section 5 & 7):**

- Application is filed in **Form-1** in duplicate to the **Patent Office at Kolkata** (the sole office for designs in India).
- Accompanied by:
  - **Four copies** of representations (drawings, photographs, or tracings) of the article.
  - **Statement of novelty/claim** describing the design's distinctive features.
  - **Prescribed fee** (as per the Schedule).
  - **Power of attorney** if filed through an agent.

**Step 2 — Preliminary Examination (Section 8):**

- The Patent Office checks the **formalities** of the application (correct form, fee, representations).
- If deficiencies are found, a notice is issued to the applicant to rectify within prescribed time.

**Step 3 — Classification (Locarno Classification):**

- The design is classified under the **Locarno Classification** — an **international classification system for industrial designs** established under the **Locarno Agreement, 1968**.
- The classification has **32 classes** and **219 sub-classes** covering all categories of articles (e.g., Class 06: Furniture, Class 10: Clocks and Watches, Class 23: Fluid Distribution Equipment).

**Step 4 — Substantive Examination (Section 12):**

- An **Examiner of Designs** examines the application for:
  - **Novelty** (new or original).
  - **Originality**.
  - **Compliance with exclusions** (Section 3 and 2(d) proviso).
  - **Non-similarity** to prior published designs.

**Step 5 — Acceptance and Publication (Section 13):**

- If accepted, the design is **advertised in the Designs Journal** published by the Patent Office.
- A period is given for **public opposition** (third parties can file notice of opposition).

**Step 6 — Registration (Section 14):**

- If no valid opposition is filed (or opposition fails), the design is **registered in the Register of Designs**.
- A **Certificate of Registration** is issued to the proprietor.

> [!TIP]
> **Locarno Classification Significance:** The Locarno system allows uniform classification across jurisdictions, enabling examiners worldwide to compare and assess designs, and helps applicants in conducting prior-art searches across classes.

---

### **Choice B: Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model - Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **rights of a registered proprietor of a design** under the Designs Act, 2000. What acts constitute **infringement** of these rights? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **penalties for piracy of a design** and the **defences** available to a person accused of design infringement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer (a) — Rights of the Proprietor and Infringement:**

Under **Section 22** of the Designs Act, 2000, during the existence of copyright in a design, the **registered proprietor** has the following exclusive rights:

1. **Right to Use the Design Exclusively** — The proprietor has the exclusive right to apply the design to the article in respect of which it is registered, and to make, sell, or distribute articles bearing the registered design.

2. **Right to Assign or License** — The proprietor can **assign, transmit, or license** the design to any third party, either wholly or partially (Section 19). The assignment must be in writing and registered with the Controller.

3. **Right to Sue for Infringement** — The proprietor can file a **civil suit** in the **District Court** (not below District Judge) for infringement of copyright in the design.

4. **Right to Seek Reliefs** — The Court may grant:
   - **Injunction** (temporary or permanent) restraining further infringement.
   - **Damages or account of profits**.
   - **Order for destruction** of infringing articles.
   - **Delivery up** of infringing goods.

5. **Right to Apply for Renewal** — The proprietor may apply for a **renewal** of the design registration for a further 5-year period before expiry.

**Acts Constituting Infringement (Section 22):**

The following acts amount to infringement of the copyright in a registered design:

- **Application of the registered design** to any article without the licence or written consent of the proprietor.
- **Selling, hiring, or exposing for sale** any article bearing a registered design, knowing that the design was applied without authorisation.
- **Importing** articles bearing the registered design.
- **Manufacturing articles in a manner that resembles** the registered design to a degree that would deceive an ordinary observer.

The test for infringement is the **"ordinary observer test"** — if an ordinary observer would be **deceived** into believing that the alleged infringing article is the registered design, infringement is established.

---

#### **Model Answer (b) — Penalties and Defences:**

**Penalties for Piracy (Section 31):**

The Act provides for **criminal prosecution** for piracy, in addition to civil remedies. The penalty structure is:

| **Nature of Offence** | **Imprisonment** | **Fine** |
|---|---|---|
| First offence | Up to **2 years** | Up to **₹50,000** |
| Subsequent conviction | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹1,00,000** |

- Both **imprisonment and fine** may be imposed **simultaneously** — the punishment is **cumulative, not alternative**.
- The offence is **cognizable, bailable, and non-compoundable** (a significant change from the 1911 Act).
- Magistrate of the **First Class** (or higher) has the jurisdiction to try the offence.

**Defences Available to the Accused (Section 22(2)):**

The following defences may be raised against an infringement or piracy action:

1. **Prior Publication Defence** — That the design was **not new or original**, having been previously published in India or elsewhere before the date of registration.

2. **Independent Creation** — That the defendant **independently developed** the design without knowledge of the registered design.

3. **Use in Private/Non-Commercial Manner** — The alleged infringing use was purely for personal, academic, or experimental purposes.

4. **Registration Invalidity** — The registration was **fraudulently obtained** or procured through misrepresentation.

5. **Foreign Vessel/Aircraft Defence** — The design was used on a **foreign vessel or aircraft** temporarily in Indian waters/airspace (Section 22(2) proviso).

6. **Statutory Right of Government** — Use of the design by the **Central or State Government** for its own purposes (Section 23).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Common Mistake Callout:**
> - Students often confuse the **TRIPS-compliant term (10 years)** with the **maximum extended term (15 years)** — clearly distinguish in your answer.
> - Don't write that a design is for **only 10 years** without mentioning the **5-year renewal** under Section 16(2).
> - In infringement questions, students forget to mention the **"ordinary observer test"** — this is a critical judicial test and is often a marking point.
> - Do NOT confuse **"Design"** under the Designs Act with **"Industrial Design"** loosely — be precise with the **Section 2(d)** definition.
> - Remember: **Section 31 deals with criminal piracy**, while **Section 22 deals with civil infringement** — they are different remedies.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — Designs Act, 2000**

- **Statute:** Designs Act, 2000 (replaced Designs Act, 1911); received Presidential assent on **25th May 2000**; came into force on **11th May 2001**.
- **Definition [Section 2(d)]:** Shape, configuration, pattern, ornament, composition of lines/colours — applied to any article — judged **solely by the eye** — excludes **mode/principle of construction** and **mechanical devices**.
- **Administration:** **Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks (CGPDTM)**, operating under the **Ministry of Commerce and Industry**.
- **Filing Office:** **Patent Office, Kolkata** (the sole office for designs in India).
- **Application Form:** **Form-1** along with 4 copies of representations.
- **Classification System:** **Locarno Classification (32 classes, 219 sub-classes)**.
- **Term of Copyright:** **10 years from date of registration** (Section 16).
- **Renewal:** **Additional 5 years** on application (Section 16(2)) — **Maximum 15 years**.
- **Novelty Test:** Design must be **new or original** globally (not just in India).
- **Infringement:** Civil remedy under **Section 22** in **District Court**.
- **Piracy (Criminal):** **Section 31** — imprisonment up to **2 years** (first offence) or **3 years** (subsequent) + fine up to **₹50,000** / **₹1,00,000**.
- **Test for Infringement:** **Ordinary Observer Test**.
- **Reciprocity:** **Section 44** — protects foreign nationals from notified countries (UK, Australia, etc.).
- **Restoration:** **Section 17** — within **12 months** of removal from register.
- **Restoration Period:** Removed designs may be restored within **12 months** from the date of removal (Section 17).
- **Priority Date:** Date of application in India or convention priority date (Section 11) — **6 months** for Paris Convention.
- **Key Exclusions:** Trademarks, copyrights, mode of construction, mechanical devices, computer programs.
- **Public Order/Morality Bar:** Section 3 — scandalous, obscene, or immoral designs cannot be registered.
- **International Linkages:** **Paris Convention 1883**, **TRIPS Agreement 1994**, **Hague Agreement 1999** (not yet ratified by India, but influential).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Board Exam:**
> **"S-S-P-C-M-P-J-O-E"** for what constitutes a Design:
> **S**hape | **S**urface configuration | **P**attern | **C**olour composition | **M**eans (industrial process) | **P**rinciple excluded | **J**udged by eye | **O**rnament | **E**xcludes mechanical devices
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Meaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** This section establishes the foundational definitions. Examiners frequently allot **3-mark direct questions** asking for precise statutory and academic definitions. Memorize the verbatim phrasing.

## 1.1 What is "Meaning" in the Context of Module 4?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PESFT757 – Intellectual Property Rights**, Module 4 typically addresses **Trade Secrets and Confidential Information / Protection of undisclosed information**. The topic *"Meaning"* requires the student to articulate the precise legal, statutory, and academic definitions of the core subject matter covered in this module.

The **"Meaning"** of a legal concept in IPR is not merely a dictionary definition. It is a multi-layered construct comprising:

1. **Lay / General Meaning** — The everyday understanding of the term.
2. **Statutory Meaning** — The definition provided under the relevant Indian statute.
3. **International / Treaty Meaning** — The definition under multilateral treaties (TRIPS, Paris Convention, etc.).
4. **Judicial Meaning** — The interpretation evolved through case law and precedent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A 3-mark question on "Meaning" expects a **layered answer**: Begin with the general meaning, follow with the statutory meaning (cite the Act & Section), and conclude with judicial interpretation. A single-line definition loses marks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe in Your Grandma's Kitchen"

Imagine your grandmother has a **secret recipe** for a specific pickle that has been in your family for three generations. The recipe is not written down in a public cookbook, it is not registered with any government, and no patent protects it. Yet, the family jealously guards it. If a competitor steals the recipe by bribing a worker, the family can still take legal action.

This everyday scenario captures the essence of **Trade Secrets / Undisclosed Information**:

- The recipe = the **trade secret**.
- The secrecy maintained by the family = the **reasonable steps** required under the law.
- The legal recourse against the thief = the **protection against breach of confidence**.

Just as the recipe has economic value because it gives the family a competitive edge, **trade secrets are protected because they confer commercial advantage**, not because they are registered or published.

## 1.3 The Statutory Anchor in India

In India, trade secrets are **not protected by a dedicated statute** like the USA's *Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA)* or the *Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA, 2016)*. Instead, they are safeguarded through:

- **The Indian Contract Act, 1872** (Sections 27, 73, 74 — dealing with agreements in restraint of trade, compensation for breach, and compensation for breach of contract).
- **The Information Technology Act, 2000** (Sections 43A, 72, 72A — dealing with data privacy and unauthorized access).
- **Common Law principles of Breach of Confidence / Breach of Trust.**
- **The Copyright Act, 1957** (for certain compilations and databases).
- **The Patents Act, 1970** (Section 3 — what is *not* patentable, indirectly pushing innovations into trade secret protection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Statutory Benchmark — TRIPS Agreement, Article 39:**
> TRIPS defines **"undisclosed information"** as information that:
> 1. Is **secret** in the sense that it is not, as a body or in the precise configuration and assembly of its components, generally known among or readily accessible to persons within the circles that normally deal with the kind of information in question.
> 2. Has **commercial value** because it is secret.
> 3. Has been **subject to reasonable steps** under the circumstances, by the person lawfully in control of the information, to keep it secret.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Meaning

| Pillar | Keyword | Essence |
|---|---|---|
| **Secrecy** | *Not generally known* | Information must not be in the public domain. |
| **Value** | *Commercial worth* | The secret must confer an economic or competitive edge. |
| **Effort** | *Reasonable steps* | The owner must demonstrate active measures to maintain confidentiality. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam — "S-V-E":** **S**ecrecy, **V**alue, **E**ffort. If any of these three pillars is missing, the information loses its trade secret status.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the "Meaning" of Trade Secrets / Undisclosed Information

The conceptual meaning can be broken down into **five operative layers**, each of which is independently testable in the KTU examination:

### Layer 1: Subjective Meaning (What does it *feel* like?)
- Information that the holder *wants* to keep private.
- Examples: Coca-Cola's formula, Google's search algorithm, a chef's signature dish recipe.

### Layer 2: Objective Meaning (What *is* it legally?)
- Information meeting the **three-pronged TRIPS test** (secrecy + value + reasonable steps).
- Capable of being communicated, stored, or transmitted in tangible or intangible form.

### Layer 3: Functional Meaning (What *does* it do?)
- It provides a **competitive advantage** in the marketplace.
- It enables the holder to derive economic benefit without public disclosure.

### Layer 4: Protective Meaning (How is it *guarded*?)
- Through **contractual obligations** (NDAs, employment contracts, licensing terms).
- Through **physical and digital security** (locked cabinets, encryption, access controls).
- Through **legal action** under contract law, tort law, and IT law for breach.

### Layer 5: Negative Meaning (What is it *not*?)
- It is **not a patent** — it is not registered, published, or time-limited.
- It is **not public knowledge** — once revealed, protection is lost forever.
- It is **not a trademark** — it does not identify the source of goods.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Three Pillars

| Pillar | Why It Exists | How It Operates |
|---|---|---|
| **Secrecy** | Public information is a *public good* — once disclosed, it cannot be "un-disclosed." | The holder must affirmatively restrict access. Mere labeling as "secret" is insufficient if information is freely available. |
| **Commercial Value** | The law protects economic interests, not mere curiosities. | The information must enable the holder to gain a competitive edge, save R&D costs, or maintain market position. |
| **Reasonable Steps** | Prevents frivolous claims of secrecy over trivial or well-known facts. | Courts examine whether the holder took *objective, demonstrable* measures (NDAs, locked files, limited circulation). |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Content Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Below is the consolidated reference table for the topic "Meaning" — memorize it for ESE and internal assessments.**

| Term | Definition (Statutory / Academic) | Source | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trade Secret** | Information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. | TRIPS Art. 39; Common Law | **Most tested term** — used in 3-mark and 14-mark questions. |
| **Undisclosed Information** | Information that is secret, has commercial value, and is subject to reasonable steps to maintain secrecy. | TRIPS Art. 39(2) | Distinguished from "trade secret" — TRIPS uses the broader term. |
| **Confidential Information** | Non-public information disclosed in a relationship of confidence, where the recipient knows or ought to know it is confidential. | Common Law; Indian Contract Act | Often used interchangeably with trade secret in Indian jurisprudence. |
| **Reasonable Steps** | Objective measures taken by the holder to maintain secrecy — includes NDAs, restricted access, encryption, marking documents. | TRIPS Art. 39(2)(c) | Tested via case laws (*John Richard Brady v. Chemical Process Equip. P. Ltd.*). |
| **Breach of Confidence** | Unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information by a party who knows or ought to know the information is confidential. | Common Law; Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Foundation for civil action in India. |
| **NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement)** | A contractual undertaking by which a party agrees not to disclose specified information. | Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Practical tool — frequently cited in answers. |
| **Know-How** | Practical technical knowledge, experience, or skill not in the public domain, often transferred via licensing. | TRIPS; WIPO | Often paired with trade secrets. |

## 2.4 Engineering & Industry Utility

The "Meaning" of trade secrets is not abstract — it has direct, tangible relevance in:

- **Pharmaceutical R&D:** Clinical trial data, formulation processes.
- **Software Engineering:** Source code, proprietary algorithms, AI model weights.
- **Manufacturing:** Process parameters, supplier lists, quality control data.
- **Food & Beverage Industry:** Recipes, processing methods (Coca-Cola, KFC).
- **Aerospace & Defense:** Design specifications, material compositions.
- **Automotive Engineering:** EV battery chemistries, proprietary CAD designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** India's pharmaceutical industry, which exports generics to over 200 countries, relies heavily on **process know-how** as a trade secret. The Supreme Court in *F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd. v. Cipla Ltd.* (2015) examined the boundary between patent disclosure and trade secret protection extensively.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Analytical Framework: The Test for "Meaning"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Analytical Note:** The "meaning" of a trade secret is not declared — it is *determined* through a three-pronged judicial test. Below is the step-by-step analytical framework examiners expect in 14-mark questions.

## 3.1 The Three-Pronged Test — Step-by-Step Application

A piece of information is legally a **trade secret** only if it satisfies all three prongs. The following derivation-style framework illustrates how courts and examiners evaluate each prong.

### Step 1: Establish Secrecy (The "Not Generally Known" Test)

$$
S = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } I \notin D_{public} \\ 0 & \text{if } I \in D_{public} \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Secrecy status (1 = secret, 0 = public)
- $I$ = The information in question
- $D_{public}$ = The set of publicly available or generally known information

**Interpretation:** If $I$ can be found in public literature, industry handbooks, trade publications, or is otherwise readily accessible to persons within the relevant circles, then $S = 0$ and the information **fails the secrecy test**.

### Step 2: Establish Commercial Value (The "Economic Edge" Test)

$$
V = \Delta P \cdot Q_{market}
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Commercial value of the secret
- $\Delta P$ = Price premium or cost advantage gained by using the secret
- $Q_{market}$ = Quantity over which the advantage is realized

**Interpretation:** The secret must confer a measurable economic benefit. Pure scientific curiosity, without commercial application, fails this test.

### Step 3: Establish Reasonable Steps (The "Objective Measures" Test)

$$
R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot M_i
$$

Where:
- $R$ = Reasonable-steps score
- $M_i$ = Individual measure employed (NDA, encryption, locked access, employee training, etc.)
- $w_i$ = Weight assigned to that measure by the court
- $n$ = Total number of measures

**Interpretation:** If $R \geq R_{threshold}$ (the judicial threshold for "reasonable"), the test is satisfied. Courts examine whether the totality of measures, judged objectively, demonstrates genuine intent to maintain secrecy.

### Final Verdict

$$
\text{Trade Secret Status} = S \cdot V \cdot R
$$

**Logical interpretation:** The AND condition — secrecy AND value AND reasonable steps. If *any* factor is zero, the status is zero. The information is only a trade secret when **all three prongs are simultaneously satisfied**.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style Analytical Problem)

**Problem:** *A startup in Kochi develops a novel water purification algorithm. The algorithm is documented in a shared Google Drive accessible to all 50 employees. The startup has not signed NDAs. The algorithm gives the startup a 30% cost advantage. Is this a trade secret?*

**Solution (applying the three-pronged test):**

| Prong | Test | Result | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Secrecy (S)** | Is it generally known? | **FAIL** | Shared Google Drive with 50 employees, no access controls — effectively public within the company. |
| **Value (V)** | Does it confer economic edge? | **PASS** | 30% cost advantage is substantial commercial value. |
| **Reasonable Steps (R)** | Were objective measures taken? | **FAIL** | No NDAs, no access restrictions, no encryption. |

**Verdict:** $\text{Trade Secret Status} = 1 \cdot 0 \cdot 0 = 0$. The algorithm is **not** a trade secret under the TRIPS framework, despite having commercial value, because secrecy and reasonable steps are absent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Takeaway:** This example illustrates that the "meaning" of a trade secret is not a static label — it is the *outcome* of a multi-factor test. KTU examiners reward answers that apply the test dynamically rather than reciting definitions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagram: The Anatomy of "Meaning"

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Layered Meaning of Trade Secrets / Undisclosed Information

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MEANING OF TRADE SECRET"] --> B["LAY MEANING"]
    A --> C["STATUTORY MEANING"]
    A --> D["INTERNATIONAL MEANING"]
    A --> E["JUDICIAL MEANING"]

    B --> B1["Everyday understanding of secret business info"]
    B --> B2["Example: Recipe, formula, process"]

    C --> C1["Indian Contract Act 1872"]
    C --> C2["IT Act 2000"]
    C --> C3["Common Law of Breach of Confidence"]

    D --> D1["TRIPS Article 39"]
    D --> D2["Paris Convention"]
    D --> D3["WIPO Model Provisions"]

    E --> E1["Case: John Richard Brady vs Chemical Process"]
    E --> E2["Case: F Hoffmann La Roche vs Cipla"]

    C1 --> F["PROTECTION MECHANISMS"]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F

    F --> F1["Contractual NDAs"]
    F --> F2["Civil Action for Breach"]
    F --> F3["Criminal Action under IT Act"]
    F --> F4["Equitable Injunctions"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Three-Pronged Test (Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Evaluate Information I"] --> P1{"Is I generally known?"}

    P1 -- YES --> Fail1["FAIL: Not Secret"]
    P1 -- NO --> P2{"Does I have commercial value?"}

    P2 -- NO --> Fail2["FAIL: No Economic Edge"]
    P2 -- YES --> P3{"Were reasonable steps taken?"}

    P3 -- NO --> Fail3["FAIL: Inadequate Protection"]
    P3 -- YES --> Pass["TRADE SECRET CONFIRMED"]

    Pass --> Out1["Legal Remedies Available"]
    Out1 --> Out2["Injunction"]
    Out1 --> Out3["Damages"]
    Out1 --> Out4["Account of Profits"]
    Out1 --> Out5["Criminal Prosecution IT Act"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Distinction Between Related Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY FAMILY"] --> B["Registered IP"]
    A --> C["Unregistered IP"]

    B --> B1["Patents"]
    B --> B2["Trademarks"]
    B --> B3["Designs"]
    B --> B4["Geographical Indications"]

    C --> C1["Copyright Automatic"]
    C --> C2["Trade Secrets"]
    C --> C3["Know How"]

    C2 --> D1["Secret by Nature"]
    C2 --> D2["Value by Use"]
    C2 --> D3["Protected by Effort"]
    C2 --> D4["Indefinite Duration"]
    C2 --> D5["No Registration Required"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The "Meaning" of trade secrets is best understood by contrast. Unlike patents (registered, time-limited, published), trade secrets are **unregistered, indefinite in duration, and protected by the holder's own efforts**. This structural distinction is a frequent **14-mark** question in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A = 3 marks each. Part B = 14 marks each (with internal choice). Answer all questions with specific reference to statutory provisions and case law for full marks.

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct / Short Answer)

### Question 1
**"Define trade secret and state the three essential conditions for information to qualify as a trade secret under the TRIPS Agreement."** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **trade secret** is information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that (i) is not generally known among, or readily accessible to, persons within circles that normally deal with such information, and (ii) derives independent economic value from not being publicly known.

Under **TRIPS Article 39(2)**, information must satisfy three conditions:

1. **Secrecy** — It must not be generally known or readily accessible to relevant persons.
2. **Commercial Value** — It must have economic value precisely because it is secret.
3. **Reasonable Steps** — The holder must have taken reasonable measures to keep it confidential.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only the general definition and omit the three TRIPS conditions. This results in a **1.5-mark loss**. Always cite **Article 39(2)** and list all three prongs explicitly.

---

### Question 2
**"What is the difference between 'trade secret' and 'undisclosed information' as understood in Indian IP jurisprudence?"** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Trade Secret | Undisclosed Information |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of Term** | Common law, US jurisprudence | TRIPS Agreement, Article 39 |
| **Scope** | Narrower — refers to commercial/industrial secrets | Broader — covers scientific, technical, and business information |
| **Statutory Basis in India** | Not defined in any Indian statute | India obligated under TRIPS to protect |
| **Practical Application** | Marketing strategies, formulas, customer lists | Clinical trial data, test results, R&D data |
| **Remedies** | Breach of confidence, contract law | Breach of confidence, IT Act, contractual remedies |

In essence, **undisclosed information** is the broader international term adopted under TRIPS, while **trade secret** is the more commonly used term in commercial practice and US-derived jurisprudence. In India, both are protected through the same common law and contractual frameworks.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the statutory and judicial framework for the protection of trade secrets in India. Discuss the relevant provisions of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and the Information Technology Act, 2000. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the three-pronged test under **TRIPS Article 39** for determining whether information qualifies as undisclosed information. Illustrate your answer with decided case law. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Unlike the USA, India does not have a dedicated trade secret statute. Protection flows from a combination of statutory provisions, common law, and equitable remedies.

**Indian Contract Act, 1872 (3 marks):**
- **Section 27** — Agreements in restraint of trade are void, *except* those involving trade secrets. This carve-out allows reasonable post-employment restrictions to protect confidential information.
- **Section 73** — Compensation for loss or damage caused by breach of contract — applied when an employee violates NDA terms.
- **Section 74** — Compensation for breach of contract where penalty is stipulated — enforces liquidated damages clauses in NDAs.

**Information Technology Act, 2000 (2 marks):**
- **Section 43A** — Compensation for failure to protect data by a body corporate — applicable to digital trade secrets.
- **Section 72** — Penalty for breach of confidentiality by any person — punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to ₹1 lakh, or both.
- **Section 72A** — Punishment for disclosure of information in breach of lawful contract — imprisonment up to 3 years or fine up to ₹5 lakhs.

**Common Law and Equity (1 mark):**
- The doctrine of **breach of confidence** — three elements: (i) information of a confidential nature, (ii) communicated in circumstances importing an obligation of confidence, (iii) unauthorized use causing detriment.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**TRIPS Article 39(2) — The Three-Pronged Test (3 marks):**

The test comprises:
1. **Secrecy** — The information is not generally known or readily accessible.
2. **Commercial Value** — The information has economic value precisely because it is secret.
3. **Reasonable Steps** — The holder has taken objective measures to maintain confidentiality.

**Case Law Illustrations (3 marks):**

- ***John Richard Brady v. Chemical Process Equipments Pvt. Ltd.*** (Delhi HC, 1986) — The court recognized trade secret protection in India and held that information shared in a confidential relationship cannot be used by the recipient for unauthorized purposes. **Key takeaway:** Confidentiality can be inferred from circumstances.

- ***F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd. v. Cipla Ltd.*** (Bombay HC, 2015) — The court examined whether clinical trial data submitted to drug regulators could be protected. The court held that data exclusivity is a *form* of trade secret protection under TRIPS Article 39. **Key takeaway:** Even regulatory data can qualify as undisclosed information.

- ***Zee Telefilms Ltd. v. Sundial Communications Pvt. Ltd.*** (Bombay HC, 2003) — The court granted an injunction to prevent the misuse of confidential information related to a television show format. **Key takeaway:** Entertainment formats can be trade secrets.

**Critical Analysis (1 mark):**
The three-pronged test provides a robust framework but suffers from subjectivity in assessing "reasonable steps." Indian courts have applied the test pragmatically, balancing the interests of innovation, employee mobility, and public welfare.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, students frequently lose marks by: (i) failing to cite **specific sections** of the Indian Contract Act and IT Act, (ii) omitting **case law**, and (iii) not providing a **critical analysis** rather than a mere description. A balanced answer must include *introduction, statutory provisions, case law, and conclusion*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Define the term "confidential information" and explain how it differs from a "trade secret." Discuss the role of Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) in protecting such information. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the international framework for the protection of undisclosed information. Compare the approaches of the USA, EU, and India. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition of Confidential Information (2 marks):**
**Confidential information** is non-public information disclosed in a relationship of trust or confidence, where the recipient knows or ought to reasonably know that the information is confidential. It encompasses a broader range of data than trade secrets, including personal data, business strategies, and unpublished research.

**Difference Between Confidential Information and Trade Secret (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Confidential Information | Trade Secret |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Broader — includes personal, business, technical | Narrower — strictly commercial/industrial |
| **Value Requirement** | No strict commercial value test | Must have commercial value |
| **Reasonable Steps** | Inferred from relationship of trust | Must be demonstrably proven |
| **Typical Use** | Employment, consulting, licensing | Manufacturing, R&D, formulation |

**Role of NDAs (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** A Non-Disclosure Agreement is a legally binding contract in which one or more parties agree not to disclose specified information.
- **Key Clauses:** Definition of confidential information, exclusions (publicly known, independently developed, lawfully obtained from third parties), term of confidentiality, remedies (injunction, damages), and governing law.
- **Indian Law:** NDAs are governed by the Indian Contract Act, 1872. They must satisfy the requirements of a valid contract (offer, acceptance, consideration, lawful object).
- **Enforceability:** In *Niranjan Shankar Golikari v. Century Spinning & Manufacturing Co. Ltd.* (1967), the Supreme Court upheld post-employment NDAs as enforceable restraints protecting trade secrets.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**International Framework (2 marks):**
- **TRIPS Article 39** — The first multilateral treaty to mandate protection of undisclosed information. Members must protect information that is secret, valuable, and subject to reasonable steps.
- **Paris Convention** — Earlier treaty, Article 10bis on unfair competition, indirectly protects trade secrets against acts of industrial espionage.
- **WIPO Model Provisions** — Non-binding model provisions on protection against unfair competition, including trade secrets.

**Comparative Analysis (4 marks):**

| Jurisdiction | Legal Basis | Key Features | Strengths & Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|
| **USA** | Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) 2016; Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) | Federal civil action; ex parte seizure; whistleblower immunity | **Strong:** Robust federal framework. **Weak:** High litigation costs. |
| **EU** | Directive (EU) 2016/943 on Trade Secrets | Defines trade secrets; remedies for unlawful acquisition; whistleblower protection | **Strong:** Harmonized across member states. **Weak:** Implementation varies. |
| **India** | Indian Contract Act, 1872; IT Act, 2000; Common Law | No dedicated statute; protection through contracts, torts, IT law | **Strong:** Flexible case-by-case approach. **Weak:** No statutory certainty, patchwork protection. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
While the USA and EU have enacted dedicated trade secret legislation providing certainty and uniformity, India relies on a patchwork of statutes and common law principles. The *Protection of Trade Secrets Bill* has been under discussion in India but is yet to be enacted, highlighting a legislative gap that KTU students should be aware of for current-affairs-based questions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** When comparing jurisdictions, students often list features without analyzing strengths/weaknesses. For 14-mark answers, the examiner expects a **comparative table with critical evaluation**, not a mere description.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this section before your exam.**

- ✅ **Trade Secret** = Secret + Value + Reasonable Steps (TRIPS Art. 39).
- ✅ **No dedicated statute in India** — protection via Contract Act, IT Act, Common Law.
- ✅ **Key Sections:** Indian Contract Act — S. 27, 73, 74 | IT Act — S. 43A, 72, 72A.
- ✅ **TRIPS Article 39** is the international anchor — three-pronged test is universally applicable.
- ✅ **Mnemonic "S-V-E"** = Secrecy, Value, Effort (Reasonable Steps).
- ✅ **Key Case Laws:**
  - *John Richard Brady v. Chemical Process Equipments* — Established trade secret protection in India.
  - *F. Hoffmann-La Roche v. Cipla* — Data exclusivity is a form of trade secret protection.
  - *Zee Telefilms v. Sundial* — Entertainment formats as trade secrets.
  - *Niranjan Shankar Golikari v. Century Spinning* — Enforceability of post-employment NDAs.
- ✅ **Difference between trade secret and patent:** Trade secret = indefinite, unregistered, secret. Patent = 20 years, registered, published.
- ✅ **Difference between trade secret and confidential information:** Confidential information is broader; trade secret is a subset with strict commercial-value and reasonable-steps requirements.
- ✅ **NDAs** are governed by the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and must satisfy the essentials of a valid contract.
- ✅ **Comparative law:** USA (DTSA 2016) and EU (Directive 2016/943) have dedicated statutes; India does not.
- ✅ **The "Meaning" of trade secrets is multi-layered:** Lay → Statutory → International (TRIPS) → Judicial.
- ✅ **Killer 1-liner for any answer:** *"A trade secret is not a right created by law — it is a right protected by law against breach of confidence."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy for the "Meaning" Topic:**
> 1. Always begin with a **layered definition** (general + statutory + international).
> 2. Always cite **at least one Section number** and **one case law**.
> 3. For 14-mark answers, use a **comparative table** to showcase structured thinking.
> 4. Conclude with a **one-line "killer statement"** that synthesizes the meaning.
> 5. Time allocation: 3-mark question = 3–4 minutes | 14-mark question = 12–14 minutes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Author and Proprietor of Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Author and Proprietor of Design # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Author and Proprietor of Design The concepts of **"Author"** and **"Proprietor"** of a design are foundational pillars of design law under the **Designs Act, 2000** (India). These two distinct legal identities govern who *creates* a design and who *owns* the design, respectively. Understanding the precise legal distinction between these two terms is critical for KTU examinations because the question paper frequently tests whether students can correctly identify scenarios of authorship versus proprietorship. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** The Designs Act, 2000 defines both the *author* and the *proprietor* of a design under **Section 2(d)** and **Section 2(1)(s)** respectively. These definitions determine the rightful owner of design rights and the eligibility to apply for registration. --- ## 1.2 Author of Design — Formal Legal Definition Under **Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000**, the **"author"** of a design means: > *"the person who creates the design."* In other words, the author is the **natural person** who invents, originates, or applies the design to an article. The author is essentially the **intellectual creator** — the human mind behind the aesthetic conception of the design. ### Key Characteristics of an "Author": - Must be a **natural person** (an individual human being), not a company or organization. - The person who **applies the design** to an article through intellectual effort. - The author is identified at the **moment of creation**, regardless of who subsequently owns the design. - The author may or may not be the owner of the design rights. > [!NOTE] > **Design ≠ Author of a Literary Work:** Unlike copyright law where the author can be a legal entity under certain conditions, the author of a design under the Designs Act is strictly a natural person who applies creative effort. --- ## 1.3 Proprietor of Design — Formal Legal Definition Under **Section 2(1)(s) of the Designs Act, 2000**, the **"proprietor"** of a design means: > *"the person for the time being entitled to the benefit of a design."* The proprietor is the **legal owner** of the design right — the person who holds the bundle of exclusive rights conferred by registration. Unlike the author (who is always an individual creator), the proprietor can be an **individual, a partnership firm, a company, or any legal entity**. ### Key Characteristics of a "Proprietor": - Can be a **natural person OR a legal entity** (company, firm, etc.). - The proprietor is the person **entitled to the commercial benefit** of the design. - The proprietary right may arise through **creation, commission, employment contract, or assignment**. --- ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Architect and the Building Company** Imagine **Riya**, a talented product designer, who sketches a beautiful new shape for a water bottle. Riya is the **author** of the design — she is the creative mind. Now, **ABC Plastics Ltd.** commissions Riya to design the bottle for them and pays her for it. Who owns the design rights? Under the Designs Act, ABC Plastics Ltd. is the **proprietor**, because they commissioned the design and are entitled to its benefit. Riya is still the author, but she holds the design *in trust* for the company. > **Key Takeaway:** *Author = Creator (Mind behind the work)* | *Proprietor = Owner (Wallet that benefits from the work)* --- ## 1.5 Why This Distinction Matters in KTU Examinations | Aspect | Author | Proprietor | |---|---|---| | Legal Source | Section 2(d) | Section 2(1)(s) | | Identity | Always a natural person | Can be natural or legal person | | Role | Creator of the design | Owner of design rights | | Determined By | Intellectual creation | Entitlement to benefit | | Can file application? | Yes (if also the proprietor) | Yes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Distinction for KTU:** Examiner questions in PESFT757 frequently ask: *"Differentiate between the author and proprietor of a design."* This is a **3-mark short answer** that appears almost every semester in either Part A or as a sub-part of a 14-mark question. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Section 2(d) — Author of Design (Exhaustive Analysis) The **Designs Act, 2000** defines the author narrowly as the **person who creates the design**. This seemingly simple definition carries several layers of legal interpretation: ### 2.1.1 Components of "Creation" The act of creation involves: 1. **Conception** — forming the idea or mental image of the design. 2. **Application** — applying that idea to a specific article (e.g., sketching the pattern on a fabric, moulding a unique bottle shape). 3. **Materialization** — bringing the design into a tangible, recognizable form. ### 2.1.2 Legal Incidents of Authorship - The author automatically holds **moral recognition** (credit for creation). - The author holds an **initial proprietary interest** unless there is a contract or commission that transfers ownership. - The author must be a **human being**; AI-generated designs have no recognized author under the current Act. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The author of a design is *always* a natural person. A company cannot be an "author" even if the design is created within the company. The company's designers (employees) are the authors, while the company becomes the **proprietor** through the contract of service. --- ## 2.2 Section 2(1)(s) — Proprietor of Design (Exhaustive Analysis) The proprietor's definition is broader and more complex. Section 2(1)(s) lists **four scenarios** under which a person becomes the proprietor of a design: ### 2.2.1 The Four Modes of Acquiring Proprietorship | S.No. | Mode of Proprietorship | Statutory Basis | Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Author is the Proprietor** (where the design is not commissioned and not under employment) | Sec 2(1)(s)(i) | A freelance designer creates a design on their own initiative and owns it. | | 2 | **Author holds in trust for the Commissioner** (where the design is executed for a person who commissions it) | Sec 2(1)(s)(ii) | Riya designs a bottle for ABC Plastics Ltd. → ABC is the proprietor, Riya holds it in trust. | | 3 | **Employer is the Proprietor** (where the design is created in the course of employment under a contract of service) | Sec 2(1)(s)(iii) | An employee of a car company designs a new dashboard layout during office hours. | | 4 | **Person to whom design is transferred or assigned** | Sec 2(1)(s)(iv) | A designer sells or assigns their design to another company via a written deed. | ### 2.2.2 Scenario 1 — Author as Proprietor (Default Rule) When the author creates a design **independently** (not under commission, not in the course of employment), the author himself/herself is the proprietor. The author is both the **creator** and the **owner**. > **Example:** A student creates a unique design for a pen stand in their hostel room. The student is both the **author** and the **proprietor**. ### 2.2.3 Scenario 2 — Commissioned Designs (Trust Doctrine) This is a critical and frequently tested area. When a person **commissions** another person to create a design for them, the **commissioner becomes the proprietor**, and the author holds the design **in trust for the commissioner**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Legal Doctrine:** *"Holds in trust"* means the author has legal custody of the design on behalf of the commissioner. The author cannot use the design for their own benefit and must transfer all rights to the commissioner. **Conditions for the Trust Doctrine to Apply:** - There must be a **commission agreement** (express or implied). - The design must be **executed for the commissioner** (not for the author's own use). - The commissioner must have **paid for or agreed to pay** for the design. ### 2.2.4 Scenario 3 — Employee Designs (Contract of Service) When an employee creates a design **in the course of employment** under a **contract of service**, the **employer is the proprietor**. This applies to the famous distinction between: - **Contract of Service** (employer-employee relationship) → Employer is proprietor. - **Contract for Services** (independent contractor relationship) → Independent contractor is the proprietor (unless commissioned). | Contract of Service | Contract for Services | |---|---| | Employee-Employer relationship | Independent contractor relationship | | Employer = Proprietor | Contractor = Proprietor (unless commissioned) | | Salary/ wages paid | Fees for specific task | | Design created during working hours | Design created at contractor's discretion | ### 2.2.5 Scenario 4 — Assignment or Transfer A proprietor (or author-as-proprietor) can **assign or transfer** the design rights to another person through a **deed of assignment**. The assignee becomes the new proprietor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statutory Requirement:** Under **Section 30 of the Designs Act, 2000**, assignments of design rights must be in **writing** and must be **registered with the Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks** to be valid against third parties. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Statutory Provision | Key Rule | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Definition of Author | Sec 2(d), Designs Act, 2000 | The person who creates the design | | 2 | Definition of Proprietor | Sec 2(1)(s), Designs Act, 2000 | Person entitled to the benefit of a design | | 3 | Author is always a natural person | Judicial interpretation | Company cannot be an author | | 4 | Proprietor can be legal entity | Sec 2(1)(s) | Company, firm, or individual | | 5 | Default proprietorship | Sec 2(1)(s)(i) | Author = Proprietor (default) | | 6 | Commissioned design | Sec 2(1)(s)(ii) | Commissioner = Proprietor, Author holds in trust | | 7 | Employee design | Sec 2(1)(s)(iii) | Employer = Proprietor | | 8 | Assignment | Sec 2(1)(s)(iv) + Sec 30 | Assignee = Proprietor (after written assignment) | | 9 | Who can file application for registration? | Sec 5, Designs Act, 2000 | The proprietor or anyone claiming through the proprietor | | 10 | Joint proprietorship | Sec 2(1)(s) proviso | Two or more persons can be joint proprietors | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In industrial design and product engineering, the **author-proprietor distinction** is critical for: 1. **Automotive Industry:** When a car company's design team creates a new car model, the **company** is the proprietor of the design (not the individual designer), and the company files for design registration. 2. **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphones, watches, and gadgets have unique industrial designs. The company commissioning the design (e.g., Apple, Samsung) is the proprietor. 3. **Furniture & Textile Industry:** Freelance designers often create designs for furniture companies. Without a written contract, disputes arise over authorship vs. proprietorship. 4. **Software & UI Design:** UI/UX designs, although protected under copyright, can also be protected as designs when applied to physical products (e.g., icons, app interface designs on a physical device). > [!NOTE] > **Practical Tip for Engineering Students:** If you create a design as part of your college project, ensure you have a written agreement with any sponsoring company. Otherwise, the company may claim proprietorship, and you may lose your rights. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Framework — Determining "Who is the Proprietor?" Below is a **step-by-step logical decision tree** that KTU examiners expect students to apply when answering proprietorship questions: ### Step-by-Step Algorithm > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1: Identify the Creator** > → The person who actually created the design is the **author** (always a natural person). > **Step 2: Check for Commission** > → Was the design *executed for a person who commissioned it*? > - **YES** → The commissioner is the **proprietor**; the author holds the design in trust. > - **NO** → Go to Step 3. > **Step 3: Check for Employment** > → Was the design created *in the course of employment* under a contract of service? > - **YES** → The **employer** is the proprietor. > - **NO** → Go to Step 4. > **Step 4: Default Rule** > → The **author himself/herself** is the proprietor. > **Step 5: Check for Assignment** > → Has the proprietor transferred the design to another person through a written, registered assignment? > - **YES** → The **assignee** is the new proprietor. > - **NO** → The proprietor identified in Steps 1–4 remains the proprietor. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Application to Common Scenarios The following table applies the above framework to typical KTU-style problem questions: | Scenario | Author | Proprietor | Reasoning | |---|---|---|---| | A freelance designer creates a logo for her own portfolio | The designer | The designer | Default rule — author is proprietor. | | An advertising agency designs a bottle shape for a beverage company | The agency's designer | The beverage company | Commissioned design — commissioner is proprietor. | | An in-house engineer designs a new drone body at his company | The engineer | The engineering company | Employee design under contract of service. | | A design student creates a chair design as a hobby | The student | The student | No commission, no employment — author is proprietor. | | Designer X sells the design to Company Y via a registered deed | Designer X | Company Y | Assignment — assignee is proprietor. | | A consultant designs a smartphone cover for a manufacturer (paid per project) | The consultant | The manufacturer | Commissioned design (contract for services leads to commission). | | Two designers jointly create a design in a studio | Both designers | Both designers (joint proprietors) | Joint authorship and joint proprietorship. | --- ## 3.3 Mathematical Representation of Ownership Transfer While this is a legal topic, KTU examiners often test the *flow of ownership* in graphical or structural form. The ownership transfer can be represented as: $$ \text{Author} \xrightarrow{\text{Creation}} \text{Initial Ownership} $$ $$ \text{Initial Ownership} \xrightarrow{\text{Commission / Employment / Assignment}} \text{Proprietor} $$ The **proprietorship transfer equation** can be symbolically represented as: $$ P_{final} = f(\text{Author},\ \text{Commission},\ \text{Employment},\ \text{Assignment}) $$ Where: - $P_{final}$ = Final Proprietor - $f$ = Function determining proprietorship based on circumstances - **Author** = The natural creator - **Commission** = 1 if commissioned, 0 if not - **Employment** = 1 if under contract of service, 0 if not - **Assignment** = 1 if assigned, 0 if not The **decision function** $f$ can be expressed as: $$ P_{final} = \begin{cases} \text{Commissioner} & \text{if } \text{Commission} = 1 \\ \text{Employer} & \text{if } \text{Commission} = 0 \text{ and } \text{Employment} = 1 \\ \text{Author} & \text{if } \text{Commission} = 0 \text{ and } \text{Employment} = 0 \text{ and } \text{Assignment} = 0 \\ \text{Assignee} & \text{if } \text{Assignment} = 1 \end{cases} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** This is analogous to a **multiplexer (MUX)** in digital electronics. The selection of the final proprietor depends on the **control signals** (commission, employment, assignment). The *author* is the *input*, and the *proprietor* is the *output* selected by the control logic. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Step-by-Step Solution **Problem (KTU-style 14-mark question):** > *"Mr. Arvind is employed as a senior designer at NovaTech Industries Ltd. During his employment, Arvind independently creates a new design for a smartwatch strap in his personal time, using his personal laptop, but the design is closely related to NovaTech's ongoing project. Discuss the rights of Mr. Arvind and NovaTech Industries Ltd. in the design."* ### Model Solution — Step-by-Step Reasoning: **Step 1: Identify the Author** The author of the design is **Mr. Arvind**, the natural person who created it. (Author is always a natural person.) **Step 2: Check for Commission** There is no evidence that any third party commissioned the design. NovaTech did not formally commission Arvind to create this specific design. → **Commission = NO**. **Step 3: Check for Employment** Arvind is employed by NovaTech under a contract of service as a senior designer. The design is related to NovaTech's ongoing project. → **Employment = YES** (debatable, but related to company's business). **Step 4: Apply the Rule** Under **Section 2(1)(s)(iii)**, when a design is created in the course of employment under a contract of service, the **employer (NovaTech)** is the proprietor. Even if Arvind created the design in his personal time, because: - He is employed as a **designer** (his job role is design creation). - The design is **related to NovaTech's business**. → **NovaTech Industries Ltd. is the proprietor**. **Step 5: Conclusion** Mr. Arvind is the **author**, but NovaTech is the **proprietor** entitled to the design rights. Arvind may have a claim for **additional compensation** if his employment contract does not adequately cover such designs, but the design right belongs to NovaTech. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly conclude that Arvind is the proprietor because he used his "personal time and personal laptop." This is wrong. The **nature of the employment contract** and the **relevance to the employer's business** are the deciding factors, not the time or tools used. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Flow of Proprietorship Rights The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how design rights flow from the author to the proprietor based on the statutory provisions: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Creator Applies Design] --> B{Is the creator a
natural person?} B -->|Yes| C[Author = Natural Person
under Section 2 d] B -->|No| D[No valid author
design cannot be claimed] C --> E{Was the design
commissioned?} E -->|Yes| F[Commissioner becomes
