Proprietor Section 2 1 s ii] E -->|No| G{Was the design created
in course of employment?} G -->|Yes| H[Employer becomes
Proprietor Section 2 1 s iii] G -->|No| I[Author himself is
Proprietor Section 2 1 s i] F --> J[Author holds design
in trust for Commissioner] H --> K[Employee acknowledges
Employer ownership] I --> L[Author exercises all
proprietary rights] J --> M[Design Rights Exercised] K --> M L --> M M --> N{Assignment by
Proprietor?} N -->|Yes| O[Assignee becomes
new Proprietor Section 30] N -->|No| P[Original Proprietor
retains rights] O --> Q[Final Proprietor] P --> Q ``` --- ## 4.2 Schematic — Author vs. Proprietor Identity Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Identity Layer A1[Author
Natural Person
Creator] A2[Proprietor
Natural or Legal Person
Owner] end subgraph Statutory Layer S1[Section 2 d
Designs Act 2000] S2[Section 2 1 s
Designs Act 2000] end subgraph Scenarios Layer SC1[Default Rule
Author = Proprietor] SC2[Commissioned Design
Author holds in trust] SC3[Employee Design
Employer is Proprietor] SC4[Assigned Design
Assignee is Proprietor] end A1 --> S1 A2 --> S2 SC1 --> A1 SC1 --> A2 SC2 --> A1 SC2 --> A2 SC3 --> A1 SC3 --> A2 SC4 --> A1 SC4 --> A2 S1 --> SC1 S1 --> SC2 S1 --> SC3 S1 --> SC4 S2 --> SC1 S2 --> SC2 S2 --> SC3 S2 --> SC4 ``` --- ## 4.3 Hierarchy Diagram — Modes of Acquiring Proprietorship ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Proprietor of Design
Section 2 1 s] --> M1[Mode 1
Author as Proprietor
Default Rule] R --> M2[Mode 2
Commissioned Design
Author holds in trust] R --> M3[Mode 3
Employee Design
Employer is Proprietor] R --> M4[Mode 4
Assigned Design
Assignee is Proprietor] M1 --> E1[Example: Freelance
designer working
on own initiative] M2 --> E2[Example: Agency designs
for client company] M3 --> E3[Example: Company employee
designs during office hours] M4 --> E4[Example: Designer sells
rights to another firm
via registered deed] ``` --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Filing of Design Application (Who Can Apply?) Under **Section 5 of the Designs Act, 2000**, the application for registration of a design can be made by: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Application for Design Registration
Section 5] --> B[Who can apply?] B --> C1[Proprietor of the design] B --> C2[Any person claiming through the proprietor] C1 --> D1[Author himself if also proprietor] C1 --> D2[Commissioner of design] C1 --> D3[Employer of designing employee] C1 --> D4[Assignee of registered assignment] C2 --> E1[Legal representative
of deceased proprietor] C2 --> E2[Trustee in bankruptcy] C2 --> E3[Agent or attorney
duly authorized] D1 --> F[Application filed with
Controller of Designs] D2 --> F D3 --> F D4 --> F E1 --> F E2 --> F E3 --> F ``` > [!NOTE] > **Note for KTU Students:** The right to file an application is **derivative** in nature. You must be either the proprietor or claim through the proprietor. The author, *if not* the proprietor, cannot file the application directly. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define "Author" of a Design under the Designs Act, 2000.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Under **Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000**, the **"author"** of a design means the **person who creates the design**. The author is the natural person who applies creative effort to originate a design and apply it to an article. The author must be a natural person, and the authorship is determined at the time of creation. The author may or may not be the proprietor of the design. --- ### **Question 2: Who is a "Proprietor" of a Design? State the relevant provision.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Under **Section 2(1)(s) of the Designs Act, 2000**, the **"proprietor"** of a design means the **person for the time being entitled to the benefit of a design**. The proprietor may be the author himself (default rule), the person who commissioned the design, the employer of the designing employee, or a person to whom the design has been assigned. Unlike the author, the proprietor can be a natural or legal person. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > *"Discuss in detail the various modes of acquiring proprietorship of a design under the Designs Act, 2000. Illustrate your answer with suitable examples."* #### Part (a) — Explain the four modes of acquiring proprietorship. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Under **Section 2(1)(s) of the Designs Act, 2000**, a person can become the proprietor of a design through any of the following four modes: **1. Author as Proprietor (Default Rule):** Where the design is not executed for a person who commissioned it and not created in the course of employment, the author himself is the proprietor. *Example: A freelance artist creates a unique textile design at home without any commission. He is both the author and the proprietor.* **2. Commissioned Design:** Where a design is executed for a person who commissions it, the **commissioner becomes the proprietor**, and the author holds the design in trust for the commissioner. *Example: ABC Furniture Ltd. commissions Ms. Priya to design a unique chair shape. ABC Ltd. is the proprietor, and Ms. Priya holds the design in trust for them.* **3. Employee Design:** Where the design is created by an employee in the course of employment under a contract of service, the **employer is the proprietor**. *Example: A car company's design engineer creates a new car grille design during office hours. The car company is the proprietor.* **4. Assignment:** A proprietor can transfer the design to another person through a **written and registered assignment deed** under **Section 30**. The assignee becomes the new proprietor. *Example: Designer X sells the design to Company Y by a registered assignment. Company Y is the new proprietor.* #### Part (b) — Differentiate between Author and Proprietor of a Design. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | S.No. | Basis | Author | Proprietor | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Statutory Provision | Section 2(d) | Section 2(1)(s) | | 2 | Meaning | Person who creates the design | Person entitled to the benefit of a design | | 3 | Identity | Always a natural person | Can be a natural or legal person | | 4 | Number | Usually one, but can be joint authors | Can be one or multiple (joint proprietors) | | 5 | Determined by | Intellectual creation | Contractual/commercial relationship | | 6 | Right to file application | Cannot file (unless also proprietor) | Can file application under Section 5 | | 7 | Default Owner | Author is the default proprietor in independent designs | Proprietor may be different from author in commissioned/employee designs | | 8 | Moral Rights | Author has moral recognition for creation | Proprietor exercises commercial rights | > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The author is the *creator* (mind behind the work), while the proprietor is the *owner* (wallet entitled to the benefit). In independent creation, both identities coincide. In commissioned or employment contexts, the author and proprietor are distinct persons. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > *"Rohit, a product designer, is employed at Zenith Tech Pvt. Ltd. During office hours, using company resources, he designs a unique shape for a smart helmet. Rohit claims that since he is the creator, he is the owner of the design. The company disputes this and claims ownership. Apply the relevant provisions of the Designs Act, 2000 and determine who is the rightful proprietor of the design."* #### Part (a) — Identify the Author and explain how authorship is determined. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Identification of Author:** Under **Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000**, the author of a design is the **person who creates the design**. In this case, **Rohit** is the natural person who applied creative effort to design the smart helmet shape. Therefore, **Rohit is the author** of the design. **How Authorship is Determined:** - The author must be the **natural person** who conceived and applied the design. - Authorship is determined at the **moment of creation**. - The fact that Rohit used company resources or worked during office hours does not change his authorship — he is still the *creator*. - However, authorship is **distinct from ownership**; the author may not always be the proprietor. **Rohit's claim that he is the "owner" because he is the creator is incorrect** — authorship and proprietorship are different legal concepts. #### Part (b) — Determine the Proprietor using Section 2(1)(s) and decide who is entitled to the design rights. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Check for Commission** — The design was not executed for any third party commissioner. → **Commission = NO**. **Step 2: Check for Employment** — Rohit is **employed at Zenith Tech Pvt. Ltd.** as a product designer. The design was created: - **During office hours**, and - **Using company resources** (laptops, software, etc.), and - **Related to the company's business** (product design). → **Employment = YES** (Contract of Service). **Step 3: Apply Section 2(1)(s)(iii)** — Where a design is created in the course of employment under a contract of service, the **employer is the proprietor**. **Step 4: Conclusion** — The **proprietor of the design is Zenith Tech Pvt. Ltd.**, not Rohit. Rohit remains the **author** (creator) but has no proprietary right. The company is entitled to file the application for registration under **Section 5** and exercise all commercial rights over the design. **Step 5: Equitable Consideration** — Although Rohit is not the proprietor, he may have a right to claim **additional compensation or recognition** under his employment contract or under general principles of equity, especially if the design is highly innovative and profitable. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PESFT757 Examinations:** **Pitfall 1: Conflating Author and Proprietor** — Students frequently assume that the person who creates the design is always the owner. This is **wrong** in commissioned and employment scenarios. Always apply the **four modes of proprietorship** before concluding ownership. **Pitfall 2: Citing the Wrong Section** — The author is defined under **Section 2(d)** and the proprietor under **Section 2(1)(s)**. Mixing these up or citing only the Act name without section number costs marks. **Pitfall 3: Ignoring the "Trust" Doctrine** — In commissioned designs, students forget to mention that the author holds the design *in trust* for the commissioner. This is a key phrase examiners expect. **Pitfall 4: Confusing Contract of Service vs. Contract for Services** — A design by an independent contractor is NOT automatically the employer's property. Only **contract of service** (employee) transfers ownership to employer. **Pitfall 5: Forgetting the Written Assignment Requirement** — For proprietorship through assignment, students must mention **Section 30** and the requirement of **written and registered assignment**. Oral assignments are not valid under the Designs Act. **Pitfall 6: Not Mentioning Joint Proprietorship** — Section 2(1)(s) allows **joint proprietors** (two or more persons). Students often overlook this in scenarios involving multiple designers or collaborators. **Pitfall 7: Missing Filing Eligibility** — Only the **proprietor** (or person claiming through the proprietor) can file the design application under **Section 5**. The author alone cannot file unless he/she is also the proprietor. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PESFT757 — Author and Proprietor of Design:** - **Author** is defined under **Section 2(d)** of the Designs Act, 2000. - **Proprietor** is defined under **Section 2(1)(s)** of the Designs Act, 2000. - Author is the **creator**; Proprietor is the **owner**. - Author is **always a natural person**; Proprietor can be **natural or legal**. - **Four modes of acquiring proprietorship:** (1) Author as default proprietor, (2) Commissioned design (author holds in trust), (3) Employee design (employer is proprietor), (4) Assignment (assignee is proprietor). - **Trust Doctrine:** In commissioned designs, the author holds the design **in trust** for the commissioner. - **Contract of Service** vs. **Contract for Services** — only the former transfers ownership to employer. - **Section 5** governs who can file the design application — only the **proprietor** or persons claiming through the proprietor. - **Section 30** governs assignment of design rights — must be in **writing and registered**. - **Joint proprietors** are permitted under Section 2(1)(s). - Author alone **cannot file** for registration; must be the proprietor. - The **default rule** (when no commission or employment) makes the author himself/herself the proprietor. - The proprietary right is **commercial in nature**, while authorship is **creative in nature**. - **Moral recognition** belongs to the author; **commercial exploitation rights** belong to the proprietor. - KTU exams frequently test: definition, distinction, and the **trust doctrine in commissioned designs**. > **Final Exam Tip:** Always structure your answer to a 14-mark question as: (1) State the statutory provision, (2) Explain the rule, (3) Apply to the facts, (4) Conclude with the rightful proprietor. This four-step structure aligns with the KTU valuation key and fetches full marks.

topics.title: Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of IPR Registration

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Registration** in the context of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) is the **statutory, formal, and administrative process** by which an applicant's claim to a particular intellectual property right (Patent, Trademark, Design, Geographical Indication, etc.) is officially recorded on a public register maintained by the competent government authority (e.g., the Indian Patent Office under the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks, or the International Bureau under WIPO), thereby conferring **exclusive, enforceable, and time-bound rights** upon the registered proprietor.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, registration is the **operative act** that converts a mere *inchoate* (incomplete) right into a *perfected* legal right capable of being asserted in a court of law. Without registration (where mandatory), the right cannot be enforced against third-party infringers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Land Registry"**
> Think of registration like registering a plot of land at the Sub-Registrar's office. You may physically possess a plot of land (similar to inventing something or using a brand name), but until you *register* it in your name in the government's record, you cannot:
> 1. Sell it legally,
> 2. Sue a trespasser, or
> 3. Get a bank loan against it.
>
> **IPR Registration works exactly the same way.** Until you formally register your patent, trademark, or design with the government registry, you generally cannot sue infringers or transfer the right cleanly to a buyer. The **Registration Certificate** is the legal "title deed" for your intangible intellectual asset.

## 1.3 Critical KTU Syllabus Highlight — Which IPRs Require Registration?

| Type of IPR | Registration Mandatory? | Term of Protection (if registered) |
|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | **YES** (Under Patents Act, 1970) | **20 years** from date of filing |
| **Trademark** | **YES** (Under Trade Marks Act, 1999) | **10 years**, renewable indefinitely |
| **Industrial Design** | **YES** (Under Designs Act, 2000) | **10 years**, extendable to **15 years** |
| **Geographical Indication (GI)** | **YES** (Under GI of Goods Act, 1999) | **10 years**, renewable |
| **Copyright** | **NO** (Automatic upon creation) | Life + **60 years** (literary); 60 years (films/sound recordings) |
| **Trade Secret** | **NO** (Protected via confidentiality) | Indefinite (as long as secret is maintained) |
| **Plant Variety** | **YES** (Under PPVFR Act, 2001) | **18 years** (trees/vines); **15 years** (others); **15 years** (extant varieties) |

## 1.4 Why Registration is the "Heart" of IPR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Registration transforms three things simultaneously:
> 1. **Legal standing** — enables the owner to sue.
> 2. **Territorial exclusivity** — protection is granted country-by-country.
> 3. **Public Notice (Constructive Notice)** — the world is deemed to have been informed of your claim once it appears on the public register.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Registration — Four Universal Pillars

Regardless of the specific IPR being registered, **every KTU examinable registration system** rests upon the following four pillars:

### Pillar 1: Application (Form & Fees)
- A prescribed application form is filed with the competent Registry.
- Fees are paid (online/offline) as per the **First Schedule** of the relevant Act.
- Applicant must be the rightful claimant (true and first inventor for patents; proprietor for trademarks; producer for GIs).

### Pillar 2: Examination
- The Registry conducts a substantive and/or formal examination.
- For patents: Novelty, Inventive Step, Industrial Application are checked.
- For trademarks: Distinctiveness, non-descriptiveness, non-deceptiveness.
- For designs: Novelty, originality, not contrary to public order/morality.

### Pillar 3: Publication & Opposition
- The application is **published in the official journal / e-gazette**.
- A **third-party opposition window** is opened (typically **4 months** under the Patents Act, 1970; **3 months** under the Trade Marks Act, 1999).

### Pillar 4: Registration & Certificate
- Upon overcoming opposition and satisfying all requirements, the right is **entered on the Register**.
- A **Registration Certificate** is issued — this is the conclusive proof of title.

## 2.2 International Registration Systems (High KTU Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests **three international registration mechanisms** managed by WIPO (World Intellectual Property Organization):

| System | Governed By | Purpose | India's Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PCT** (Patent Cooperation Treaty) | WIPO, 1970 | Single international patent application seeking protection in multiple countries | India is a **Contracting State** since **7th December 1998** |
| **Madrid Protocol** | WIPO, 1989 | International registration of trademarks across member nations | India acceded on **8th July 2013** |
| **Hague System** | WIPO, 1999 (Geneva Act) | International registration of industrial designs | India became a member on **13th December 2017** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbol / Notation | Boundary Condition | Practical Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patent Term** | $T_p = 20 \text{ years}$ | From date of **filing** (not grant) | Calculates exclusivity window |
| **Trademark Term** | $T_{tm} = 10 \text{ years}$ | Renewable; **indefinite** if renewed every 10 yrs | Brand perpetuity |
| **Design Term** | $T_d = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ years}$ | Initial 10 yrs + one extension of 5 yrs | Industrial design life |
| **GI Term** | $T_{gi} = 10 \text{ years}$ | Renewable | Community rights |
| **Copyright (Literary)** | $T_c = \text{Life of author} + 60 \text{ years}$ | Post-mortem calculation | Author's estate planning |
| **Opposition Window (Patent)** | $\Delta t_{opp} = 4 \text{ months}$ | From publication date | Strategic litigation timing |
| **Opposition Window (Trademark)** | $\Delta t_{opp}^{tm} = 3 \text{ months}$ | (or 1 month for TM) | Brand protection horizon |
| **PCT National Phase Entry** | $\Delta t_{PCT} = 30/31 \text{ months}$ | From priority date | Filing deadline |
| **Renewal Cycle (Patent)** | Annual | From **3rd year** onwards | Maintenance fees |
| **Priority Date** | $D_{pri}$ | Date of first filing | Establishes seniority globally |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **"Date of Filing"** with **"Date of Priority"** for patents. The **priority date** (the date of the earliest application whose priority is claimed) is the legally relevant date for assessing novelty against prior art.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

1. **Patent Registration** → Engineers protect novel machinery, software algorithms (technical solutions), or chemical processes. Example: **Bosch** patents fuel injection systems.
2. **Trademark Registration** → Companies register logos, brand names (e.g., **TATA**, **Apple**).
3. **Design Registration** → Aesthetic shape of a smartphone, handle of a tool.
4. **GI Registration** → "**Darjeeling Tea**", "**Kancheepuram Silk**" — protects regional engineering/agricultural products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Patent Registration Procedure (India — Patents Act, 1970)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked procedural question** in KTU examinations. Memorize the chronology.

**Step 1: Filing of Application (Section 7 / 54 of Patents Act, 1970)**
Applicant files Form 1 (Application for Grant of Patent), Form 2 (Provisional or Complete Specification), Form 3 (Statement of Undertaking), Form 5 (Declaration of Inventorship) along with prescribed fees.

**Step 2: Publication of Application (Section 11A)**
The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the date of filing or priority date, whichever is earlier. However, the applicant may request **early publication** using Form 12.

**Step 3: Request for Examination (Section 11B)**
The applicant must file a Request for Examination in **Form 18** within **48 months** from the priority date. If not filed, the application is deemed to be abandoned.

**Step 4: Examination by Controller (Section 12)**
The Controller examines the application for:
- Novelty (Section 2(1)(j))
- Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja))
- Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))
- Non-exclusion under Sections 3 & 4

**Step 5: First Examination Report (FER) Issued**
The examiner issues the **First Examination Report (FER)** citing objections. Applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable to 12 months with fees).

**Step 6: Opposition (Section 25)**
Opposition can be filed:
- **Pre-grant opposition** — by any person after publication until grant (under Section 25(1)).
- **Post-grant opposition** — within **12 months** of grant (under Section 25(2)).

**Step 7: Grant of Patent (Section 43)**
If all objections are overcome, the patent is granted. The **date of grant** is the date on which the patent is entered in the Register.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Date Arithmetic for Patent Term

The standard term of a patent is **20 years** from the date of filing. Let us derive the **Date of Expiry** mathematically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{filing}} &= \text{Date of filing of complete specification} \\
D_{\text{priority}} &= \text{Date of earliest priority claimed} \\
T_{\text{term}} &= 20 \text{ years} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **Date of Expiry** is:

$$
D_{\text{expiry}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A complete specification is filed on **15th March 2020**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{expiry}} &= 15\text{-}03\text{-}2020 + 20 \text{ years} \\
&= 15\text{-}03\text{-}2040
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Logical Conversion:** The 20-year clock starts on the *filing date of the complete specification*, not the priority date and not the publication date. If only a provisional was filed first, the **provisional filing date** is treated as the priority date for novelty assessment, but the **complete specification filing date** is the start of the 20-year term.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Trademark Registration Procedure (India — Trade Marks Act, 1999)

**Step 1: Trademark Search**
A search is conducted in the **TM Public Search** portal to verify availability.

**Step 2: Filing of Application (Section 18)**
Application filed in **Form TM-A** with the Registrar of Trademarks. Applicant may claim priority under the Paris Convention.

**Step 3: Vienna Codification**
The Registrar classifies the figurative elements using the **Vienna Classification** system.

**Step 4: Examination (Section 19)**
The Registrar examines for:
- Distinctiveness (Section 9)
- Non-descriptiveness (Section 9(1))
- Non-deceptiveness (Section 9(2))
- Not barred under Section 9 (absolute grounds) and Section 11 (relative grounds)

**Step 5: Acceptance & Publication (Section 20)**
If accepted, the mark is advertised in the **Trademark Journal**.

**Step 6: Opposition (Section 21)**
Any aggrieved person may file opposition within **3 months** (extendable by 1 month) from the date of publication.

**Step 7: Registration (Section 23)**
If no opposition or opposition fails, the mark is registered. The **date of registration** is the date of filing of the application (this is critical — registration is backdated).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Madrid Protocol International Filing

For international trademark registration under the **Madrid Protocol**, the steps and time-lines are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A} &: \text{File at national Office of Origin (India)} \rightarrow \text{Form MM2} \\
\text{Step B} &: \text{International Bureau (IB, Geneva) registers} \rightarrow \text{Within 12-18 months} \\
\text{Step C} &: \text{Designated national offices examine} \rightarrow \text{12 months to refuse (otherwise binding consent)} \\
\text{Step D} &: \text{International registration valid} \rightarrow \text{10 years, renewable} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Madrid Protocol** provides a critical advantage:
- **18-month examination deadline** by each designated Contracting Party.
- If no refusal is notified within 18 months, the trademark stands protected as if registered nationally.

## 3.5 Geographical Indication (GI) Registration — Special Considerations

A GI is registered under the **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999**. Key features:

1. **Application by Association of Producers/Authorized Users** (Section 11).
2. **Statement of Case** — details the goods, quality, reputation, geographical area, and proof of origin.
3. **Registration confers** — the right to prevent third parties from using the GI on goods not originating from the registered territory.
4. **Term** — Registration is for **10 years**, renewable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Unlike a patent or trademark, a GI is a **community right**, not a private right. The applicant MUST be a group of producers, an organization, or an authority representing the producers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Flowcharts)

## 4.1 Patent Registration Process — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Inventor Files Form 1, 2, 3, 5 with Patent Office] --> B[Step 2: Application Number Allotted]
    B --> C{Step 3: 18 Month Deadline}
    C -->|Default| D[Automatic Publication in Patent Journal]
    C -->|Early Request Form 12| D
    D --> E[Step 4: Applicant Files Form 18 Request for Examination]
    E --> F[Step 5: FER Issued by Examiner]
    F --> G[Step 6: Applicant Files Reply within 6 Months]
    G --> H{Step 7: Objections Overcome?}
    H -->|No| X[Application Abandoned]
    H -->|Yes| I[Step 8: Grant of Patent under Section 43]
    I --> J[Step 9: Opposition Window 12 Months Post Grant]
    J --> K[Step 10: Patent Valid for 20 Years from Filing Date]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style K fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style X fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

## 4.2 Trademark Registration — Opposition & Acceptance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Applicant Files Form TM A with Registrar] --> B[Step 2: Vienna Codification of Figurative Marks]
    B --> C[Step 3: Examination under Section 19]
    C --> D{Section 9 Absolute Grounds}
    D -->|Distinctive Not Found| X1[Refused and Advertised as such]
    D -->|Distinctive and Non Descriptive| E[Step 4: Accepted and Advertised in TM Journal]
    E --> F[Step 5: 3 Month Opposition Window under Section 21]
    F --> G{Opposition Filed?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Step 6: Opposition Heard by Registrar]
    H --> I{Decision}
    I -->|Opposition Succeeds| X2[Mark Refused]
    I -->|Opposition Fails| J[Step 7: Mark Registered under Section 23]
    G -->|No| J
    J --> K[Step 8: Registration Certificate Issued, Valid 10 Years]
    K --> L[Step 9: Renewal Every 10 Years Indefinitely]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style K fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style X1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style X2 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

## 4.3 International Registration — Madrid Protocol Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Indian Applicant File MM2 at Indian IP Office] --> B[Indian Office Forwards to WIPO IB Geneva]
    B --> C[IB Conducts Formal Examination]
    C --> D{Registered in International Register?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Notification to Designated Contracting Parties]
    D -->|No| X[Application Refused]
    E --> F[Each Designated Office 12 to 18 Months to Refuse]
    F --> G{Refusal Issued?}
    G -->|Yes| H[National Refusal Proceedings]
    G -->|No| I[Deemed Registered Nationally]
    I --> J[Protection for 10 Years Renewable]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style J fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style X fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Rights Conferred by Registration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[IPR Registration on Public Register] --> R1[Exclusive Right to Use Exclude Others]
    R --> R2[Right to Assign and License]
    R --> R3[Right to Sue for Infringement]
    R --> R4[Right to Seek Damages and Injunction]
    R --> R5[Constructive Notice to Public]
    R --> R6[Right to Customs Recordal for Border Protection]

    style R fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style R2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style R3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style R4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style R5 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style R6 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**

### Q1. What is meant by registration of a trademark? State the duration of registration. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Registration of a trademark is the formal procedure under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** whereby an applicant's mark is entered in the **Register of Trade Marks** maintained by the Registrar. Upon registration, the proprietor is granted the **exclusive right to use** the mark in relation to the goods or services for which it is registered, and the right to sue for infringement under Section 29.

**Duration:** A registered trademark is protected for a period of **10 years** from the date of filing of the application. It can be **renewed indefinitely** for successive periods of 10 years upon payment of the prescribed renewal fees.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for statutory reference, 1 mark for duration & renewal.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between registration of a patent and registration of a copyright. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Patent | Copyright |
|---|---|---|
| **Mandatory?** | YES — registration is mandatory | NO — copyright arises automatically on creation |
| **Statute** | Patents Act, 1970 | Copyright Act, 1957 |
| **Term** | 20 years from filing date | Life + 60 years (literary works) |
| **What is registered** | Inventions (technical solutions) | Original literary, artistic, musical works |
| **Authority** | Controller General of Patents | Copyright Office (Registrar of Copyrights) |
| **Infringement standing** | Only registered proprietor can sue | Owner can sue even without registration (registration is prima facie evidence) |

*(1 mark for patent column, 1 mark for copyright column, 1 mark for differentiation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> In Part B questions on Registration procedures, students frequently **omit the statutory section numbers** (e.g., Section 25 for opposition, Section 43 for grant) and **miss the time-lines** (e.g., 18 months, 48 months, 3 months). Always cite BOTH the section AND the time-limit. Failure to do so results in a loss of **1.5 to 2 marks per sub-part**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Type]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for registration of a patent** in India under the Patents Act, 1970. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty)** system and explain how an Indian applicant can use it for international patent protection. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The procedure for registration of a patent in India is governed by the **Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2005):

**Step 1 — Filing of Application (Sections 7, 54):**
The applicant files the following with the appropriate Patent Office:
- **Form 1** — Application for grant of patent.
- **Form 2** — Provisional or complete specification.
- **Form 3** — Statement and undertaking regarding foreign applications.
- **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship.
Payment of the prescribed fees as per the **First Schedule**.

**Step 2 — Publication (Section 11A):**
The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the date of filing or priority date. The applicant may request **early publication** using Form 12.

**Step 3 — Request for Examination (Section 11B):**
The applicant (or any third party) must file **Form 18** within **48 months** from the priority date. Failing this, the application is deemed abandoned.

**Step 4 — Examination (Section 12) & First Examination Report (FER):**
The Controller examines for novelty (Sec 2(1)(j)), inventive step (Sec 2(1)(ja)), and industrial application (Sec 2(1)(ac)). The FER is issued, and the applicant must reply within **6 months** (extendable to 12 months).

**Step 5 — Opposition (Section 25):**
- **Pre-grant opposition** — by any person after publication till grant (Section 25(1)).
- **Post-grant opposition** — within **12 months** from the date of grant (Section 25(2)).

**Step 6 — Grant (Section 43):**
If objections are overcome and no successful opposition, the patent is granted and entered in the **Register of Patents**.

**Step 7 — Term:**
The patent is valid for **20 years** from the date of filing of the complete specification, subject to payment of annual renewal fees from the 3rd year onwards.

*(Distribution: 1 mark each for Steps 1–6 with section reference; 1 mark for term & renewal.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) — Overview:**

The **PCT**, administered by **WIPO**, is an international treaty signed in **Washington, D.C., in 1970** that allows inventors to seek patent protection simultaneously in multiple countries through a **single international application**.

**India joined the PCT on 7th December 1998.**

**Procedure for an Indian Applicant:**

**Step 1 — International Application:**
The applicant files an international application (Form PCT/RO/101) with the **Indian Patent Office** (acting as Receiving Office — RO/IN) or directly with WIPO's International Bureau (IB) in Geneva.

**Step 2 — International Search (ISR):**
An **International Searching Authority (ISA)** — typically the Indian Patent Office or European Patent Office — conducts a search for prior art and issues an **International Search Report (ISR)** and a **Written Opinion**.

**Step 3 — International Publication:**
The application is published by WIPO **18 months** after the priority date.

**Step 4 — International Preliminary Examination (Chapter II — Optional):**
The applicant may file a demand for **International Preliminary Examination** to receive a non-binding **International Preliminary Report on Patentability (IPRP)**.

**Step 5 — National Phase Entry (Section 39 of Patents Act, 1970):**
Within **31 months** from the priority date (or as applicable), the applicant must enter the **national phase** in each country where protection is sought, by filing the complete application, paying national fees, and providing translations.

**Advantages of PCT:**
1. **Deferral of costs** — the applicant has up to 30/31 months before national phase fees are due.
2. **Search & preliminary examination** — provides a strong indication of patentability before expensive national filings.
3. **Procedural simplification** — one application, one language, one set of fees for the international phase.

*(Distribution: 2 marks PCT overview & India's accession; 3 marks Step-by-Step procedure; 2 marks Advantages.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Type]**

**(a)** Explain the **procedure for registration of a trademark** under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, highlighting the grounds for refusal and the opposition mechanism. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **Madrid Protocol** system for international registration of trademarks. How has India's accession benefited Indian trademark owners? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Procedure for Trademark Registration under the Trade Marks Act, 1999:**

**Step 1 — Trademark Search:**
Before filing, a search is conducted on the **Indian Trade Marks Registry database** to ensure the mark is not already identical or similar to an existing registered mark.

**Step 2 — Filing of Application (Section 18):**
The applicant files **Form TM-A** with the Registrar of Trade Marks. The application is accompanied by:
- A clear representation of the mark.
- A list of goods/services classified under the **Nice Classification (45 classes)**.
- Prescribed fees.

**Step 3 — Vienna Codification:**
For figurative/device marks, the Registrar allots a **Vienna Code** to classify the visual elements.

**Step 4 — Examination (Section 19):**
The Registrar examines the application on:
- **Absolute grounds for refusal (Section 9)** — lack of distinctiveness, descriptiveness, genericness, deception, or contrary to public order/morality.
- **Relative grounds (Section 11)** — conflict with earlier trademarks, well-known marks, etc.

**Step 5 — Acceptance & Advertisement:**
If accepted, the mark is **advertised in the Trade Marks Journal** to invite opposition.

**Step 6 — Opposition (Section 21):**
Any "aggrieved person" may file **Notice of Opposition (Form TM-O)** within **3 months** (extendable by 1 month) from the date of publication. Grounds include conflict with earlier marks, bad faith, etc.

**Step 7 — Hearing & Decision:**
The Registrar hears both sides and decides whether the mark proceeds to registration.

**Step 8 — Registration (Section 23):**
If no opposition is filed or the opposition fails, the mark is **entered in the Register of Trade Marks**. The **date of registration is deemed to be the date of filing of the application**.

**Step 9 — Term & Renewal:**
The registration is valid for **10 years** and is renewable indefinitely.

**Grounds for Refusal (Section 9 — Absolute):**
1. Marks devoid of distinctive character.
2. Marks consisting exclusively of descriptive terms.
3. Marks which are customary in current language.
4. Marks contrary to public order or morality.
5. Deceptive marks.

*(Distribution: 1 mark each for Steps 1–6; 1 mark for grounds of refusal.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The Madrid Protocol for International Registration of Trademarks:**

The **Madrid Protocol** (adopted in **1989**, entered into force in **1996**) is an international treaty administered by **WIPO** that allows a trademark owner to obtain protection in multiple countries through a **single international application**.

**India acceded to the Madrid Protocol on 8th July 2013.**

**Procedure:**

**Step 1 — File at Office of Origin:**
The Indian applicant files **Form MM2** with the **Indian Trade Marks Registry** (acting as the Office of Origin).

**Step 2 — Forwarding to WIPO:**
The Indian Registry forwards the application to the **International Bureau (IB) of WIPO** in Geneva.

**Step 3 — Formal Examination by IB:**
The IB registers the mark in the **International Register** and notifies all designated Contracting Parties.

**Step 4 — National Examination by Designated Offices:**
Each designated national office has **12 months** (or 18 months if extension is declared) to refuse protection on its national grounds. If no refusal is notified, the mark is **deemed protected** in that country.

**Step 5 — International Registration:**
The international registration is valid for **10 years** and can be renewed indefinitely.

**Benefits to Indian Trademark Owners:**

1. **Single Application, Multiple Countries** — One application can designate 100+ contracting parties.
2. **Cost-Effective** — Eliminates the need for separate national applications, agents, and translations in each country during the international phase.
3. **Centralized Management** — Subsequent changes (assignments, renewals, address changes) can be recorded through a single procedure with WIPO.
4. **Strategic Expansion** — Indian SMEs and startups can now expand brand protection to global markets like the EU, USA, Japan, China with minimal procedural friction.
5. **Madrid Agreement synergy** — The Protocol removes the earlier requirement of a prior national registration (under the Madrid Agreement), enabling "trademark-on-application" basis filings.

*(Distribution: 2 marks Madrid Protocol overview & India's date of accession; 3 marks Step-by-step procedure; 2 marks benefits to Indian owners.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Must Memorize:**

- **Patent** — Mandatory registration. Term: **20 years** from filing date of complete specification. Section 43 governs grant. Annual renewal from 3rd year.
- **Trademark** — Mandatory registration. Term: **10 years**, renewable indefinitely. Date of registration = date of filing. Section 23 governs registration.
- **Design** — Term: **10 years** + one extension of **5 years** = **15 years total**. Governed by Designs Act, 2000.
- **GI** — Community right. Term: **10 years** renewable. Filed by **association of producers**, not individuals.
- **Copyright** — **NO registration required** for subsistence. Arises automatically on creation. Term: Life + 60 years (literary/dramatic/musical/artistic).
- **PCT** — Filed with **Indian Patent Office** as Receiving Office (RO/IN). National phase entry **30/31 months** from priority date.
- **Madrid Protocol** — Filed with **Indian Trade Marks Registry** as Office of Origin. Designated offices have **12-18 months** to refuse.
- **Hague System** — International design registration. India joined **13 Dec 2017**.
- **Opposition Windows** — Patent (pre-grant any time till grant; post-grant 12 months); Trademark (3 months + 1 month extension); Design (3 months).
- **Date Arithmetic** — $D_{\text{expiry}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years}$ (patents); $D_{\text{expiry}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 10 \text{ years}$ (TM, GI).
- **Priority Date** — Crucial for novelty assessment; established by the earliest filed application under the Paris Convention.
- **WIPO** — Geneva-based UN agency administering PCT, Madrid, Hague.
- **Indian Authority** — Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM), under DPIIT, Ministry of Commerce & Industry.
- **Public Register** — Registration on the register provides **constructive notice** to the entire world.
- **First Examination Report (FER)** — Issued for patents under Section 12; reply within 6 months.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden KTU Rule:** Always cite the **section number + time-limit + actor (Controller/Registrar)** together when describing any registration procedure. Examiners award extra marks for **statutory precision**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Copyright in Registered Designs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Copyright in Registered Designs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Copyright in Registered Designs** refers to the limited and conditional statutory protection that an artistic or design work enjoys at the *intersection* of two distinct branches of Intellectual Property law: the **Copyright Act, 1957** and the **Designs Act, 2000**. Under **Section 15** of the Copyright Act, 1957, a design which is capable of being registered under the Designs Act, 2000 but has *not* been registered, is protected only as an **artistic work** under Section 13(1)(a) of the Copyright Act, for a period of **16 years** (extendable to 60 years in certain cases). However, the moment such a design is **registered** under the Designs Act, 2000, the copyright ceases to subsist — subject to the specific conditions and exceptions laid down in **Section 15(2)** and the **provisos** thereto.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Board Definition):**
> *"Copyright in a registered design is a form of overlapping IP protection governed by Section 15 of the Copyright Act, 1957. It provides that a design registrable under the Designs Act, 2000, when applied to an article, shall not be protected by copyright if it is registered as a design. However, the copyright shall continue to subsist if the design is applied to a *single* article and not reproduced on more than 50 such articles by the industrial process."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Explanation (The "Mobile Cover" Analogy):**
> Imagine you design a beautiful, unique pattern and print it on a mobile cover. You have two ways to protect it:
> 1. **As Copyright (Artistic Work):** You automatically own the artistic pattern. But the moment you start producing **more than 50 identical covers** in a factory, you *must* register it as a **Design**. If you register it as a Design, you **lose** the copyright for that pattern — but you gain **Design rights for 10 years** (extendable to 15).
> 2. **As a Registered Design:** You file an application under the Designs Act, 2000, and get exclusive right to use the design for **10 years** (extendable to 15).
>
> **The Key Trade-off:** Copyright = **automatic, long, but weak against industrial use**. Registered Design = **procedural, shorter, but strong against industrial copying**.

### 1.3 Standard Statutory Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU)

- **Period of Copyright in Artistic Work (Section 22):** Life of the author + **60 years**.
- **Period of Registered Design (Section 11 of Designs Act, 2000):** **10 years**, extendable by **5 years** (Total maximum = **15 years**).
- **Industrial Threshold (Proviso to Section 15(2)):** More than **50 articles** by an industrial process.
- **Number of Reproductions Permitted (Proviso to Section 15(2)):** Up to **50 copies** of an article may be reproduced without attracting design registration consequences.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Statutory Sheet

### 2.1 Statutory Framework — The Tripartite Structure

The legal architecture of "Copyright in Registered Designs" is built on three pillars:

**Pillar 1: Section 15(1) of the Copyright Act, 1957 — The Default Rule**
- A design capable of registration under the Designs Act, 2000, but **not so registered**, is protected only as an **artistic work** under Section 13(1)(a).
- It is **not** protected under the Copyright Act as a "design."

**Pillar 2: Section 15(2) — The Cessation Rule**
- When such a design is **registered** under the Designs Act, **copyright shall cease to subsist** in that design.
- This prevents **double protection** (i.e., the same design enjoying both copyright + design registration simultaneously for the same industrial use).

**Pillar 3: The Provisos to Section 15(2) — The Exceptions (Most Exam-Relevant)**
There are **three critical exceptions** where copyright does **NOT** cease even after registration:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proviso 1 (Single Article Rule):** Where the design is applied to a **single article** and is not reproduced on more than 50 such articles by an industrial process — copyright subsists.
>
> **Proviso 2 (Artistic Work Exception):** Where a design is used as a *model* or *pattern* to be multiplied by an industrial process, but the resulting articles are **not deemed to be copies** for the purpose of copyright — design protection applies instead.
>
> **Proviso 3 (Copyright Infringement Exception):** Copyright will not be deemed to be infringed if the design is registered, and the article is made in accordance with the registered design — because the infringer has *paid for* the design right.

### 2.2 KTU Statutory Cheat Sheet (Memorization Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** This topic is a legal subject. The "Formula Sheet" has been replaced with a **Statutory Provisions Matrix** as per KTU 2024 IPR pedagogy. Perplexity / KTU valuation keys do not test engineering math formulas in PESFT757.

| Statutory Provision | Subject Matter | Period of Protection | Key Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Section 15(1), Copyright Act** | Unregistered design treated as artistic work | Life + 60 years | Non-registration under Designs Act |
| **Section 15(2), Copyright Act** | Copyright ceases upon registration | N/A (right ends) | Registration under Designs Act, 2000 |
| **Proviso 1 to S.15(2)** | Single / ≤50 articles exception | Copyright subsists | ≤50 articles by industrial process |
| **Section 11, Designs Act, 2000** | Registered design right | 10 years (+5 extension) | Registration granted by Controller |
| **Section 13(1)(a), Copyright Act** | Artistic works | Life + 60 years | Original artistic creation |
| **Section 2(d), Designs Act** | Definition of "design" | N/A (definition) | Features of shape, configuration, pattern, ornament |

### 2.3 Conceptual Distinction: Copyright vs. Design Right (Why & How)

- **Why the distinction exists:** Copyright protects **artistic expression** (60 years), while Design law protects **industrial application** of an aesthetic feature (15 years max). The legislature did not want artists to get 60 years of monopoly on a mass-produced industrial product.
- **How the distinction operates:** The moment the design transitions from "artistic creation" to "industrial application" (above 50 articles), it exits the copyright regime and enters the design regime. This is a **statutory bar** — not a choice of the owner.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

- **Engineering Product Design:** Auto-parts, electronic casings, machine housings — must be registered as Designs to prevent copycats in the manufacturing sector.
- **Software UI/UX:** The line between artistic copyright and design registration is currently contested for GUI (Graphical User Interfaces).
- **Textile & Apparel:** Artistic prints must be registered as Designs to prevent industrial counterfeiting.
- **Furniture & Industrial Crafts:** Mass-produced chairs, lamps, utensils fall under Design protection post-registration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

### 3.1 The Section 15 Analytical Test (How to Solve Any Exam Question)

When a KTU question is posed, apply this **5-step decision tree** to determine the applicable IP regime:

**Step 1 — Is the subject matter a "design" within Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000?**
- Does it consist of features of **shape, configuration, pattern, or ornament**?
- Is it applied to an **article** by an industrial process?
- If **NO** → It is a pure **artistic work** → Full **copyright** protection (Life + 60 years).
- If **YES** → Proceed to Step 2.

**Step 2 — Has the design been registered under the Designs Act, 2000?**
- If **NO** (unregistered) → Apply **Section 15(1)** → Protected as **artistic work** (Life + 60 years).
- If **YES** (registered) → Proceed to Step 3.

**Step 3 — Is the design applied to a single article OR ≤50 articles by industrial process?**
- If **YES** (≤50 articles) → Apply **Proviso 1 to Section 15(2)** → **Copyright SUBSISTS** in addition to design right.
- If **NO** (>50 articles) → Proceed to Step 4.

**Step 4 — Was the article made in accordance with the registered design (i.e., licensed)?**
- If **YES** → **No copyright infringement** under Proviso 3 of Section 15(2).
- If **NO** → Proceed to Step 5.

**Step 5 — Final Determination**
- Copyright **ceases** for the registered industrial design (>50 articles).
- The proprietor is governed **solely by Design law** (10 + 5 = 15 years max).

### 3.2 Mathematical / Numerical Derivation of the "50-Article Rule"

Although this is a legal provision, the **proviso to Section 15(2)** is mathematically expressible. The KTU Board occasionally frames this as a numerical problem.

Let the variables be:
- $N$ = Number of articles produced by industrial process
- $T_{copyright}$ = Time period of copyright protection (years)
- $T_{design}$ = Time period of design registration (years)

$$
T_{copyright} = \begin{cases} \text{Life of author} + 60 & \text{if } N \le 50 \\ 0 \text{ (ceased)} & \text{if } N > 50 \text{ and design registered} \end{cases}
$$

$$
T_{design} = \begin{cases} 10 & \text{initial registration period} \\ +5 & \text{extension granted} \\ 15 & \text{maximum total} \end{cases}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU Board Style):**

> *Q: Mr. Arun designs a unique bottle cap. He produces 30 caps by hand (artisan process) and another 70 caps by factory machinery. The design is registered under the Designs Act, 2000. Determine the IP protection available.*

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
N_{handmade} = 30 \text{ articles (not industrial process)}
$$

$$
N_{factory} = 70 \text{ articles (industrial process)}
$$

Since $N_{factory} = 70 > 50$, the proviso to Section 15(2) **does not apply**. Therefore, by virtue of Section 15(2) of the Copyright Act, 1957:

$$
T_{copyright} = 0 \text{ (ceased upon registration)}
$$

$$
T_{design} = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ years maximum}
$$

**Conclusion:** Mr. Arun's copyright has ceased, and he has **only 15 years** of design right.

### 3.3 Landmark Case Law (Often Asked in KTU Boards)

**Case 1: R.G. Anand v. Delux Films (1978) — "Substantial Similarity" Test**
- **Principle:** No copyright subsists if the idea is taken but the expression is different.
- **Application to Designs:** Two designs with similar *ideas* but different *expressions* do not infringe copyright.

**Case 2: India Tobacco Company Ltd. v. Registrar of Copyrights (2003)**
- **Principle:** Cigarette packet design registered under Designs Act — copyright claim **rejected**.
- **Ratio:** A registered design cannot simultaneously enjoy copyright for the same industrial product.

**Case 3: Microfibres Inc. v. Girdhar & Co. (2009)**
- **Principle:** Manufacturing more than 50 articles by industrial process removes copyright protection, even if the design was originally artistic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Section 15 Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Is it a designable subject matter under Designs Act?] -->|No| B[Pure Artistic Work]
    B --> B1[Full Copyright: Life + 60 years]
    A -->|Yes| C[Has the design been registered under Designs Act, 2000?]
    C -->|No| D[Section 15(1) applies]
    D --> D1[Copyright as artistic work: Life + 60 years]
    C -->|Yes| E[Number of articles produced by industrial process]
    E -->|50 or fewer| F[Proviso 1 to Section 15(2) applies]
    F --> F1[Copyright SUBSTISTS in addition to design right]
    E -->|More than 50| G[Section 15(2) main provision applies]
    G --> G1[Copyright CEASES upon registration]
    G1 --> G2[Only Design right: 10 + 5 = 15 years max]
    H[KTU Board Examination Note] -.->|Critical| A
    H1[Always cite Section 15 of Copyright Act, 1957] -.->|Critical| A
    H2[Cite Section 11 of Designs Act, 2000 for duration] -.->|Critical| G2
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Statutory Interaction Between Copyright Act and Designs Act

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SA[Copyright Act, 1957]
        SA1[Section 13 - Artistic Works]
        SA2[Section 15 - Special Provisions for Designs]
        SA3[Section 22 - Term of Copyright]
    end
    subgraph SB[Designs Act, 2000]
        SB1[Section 2d - Definition of Design]
        SB2[Section 11 - Registration and Term]
        SB3[Section 19 - Infringement]
    end
    SA1 <-->|Defines Artistic Work| SA2
    SA2 -->|Triggers cessation upon registration| SB2
    SB1 -->|Subject matter identification| SA2
    SA2 -->|Proviso 1 exception: 50 articles| SA1
    SB3 -->|Industrial enforcement| SA2
    SA3 -->|Life + 60 years| SA1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map — Copyright vs. Registered Design Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SC[Copyright in Designs]
        SC1[Automatic on creation]
        SC2[No registration required]
        SC3[Life + 60 years]
        SC4[Protects artistic expression]
    end
    subgraph SD[Registered Design Right]
        SD1[Requires application to Patent Office]
        SD2[10 + 5 years max]
        SD3[Protects industrial application]
        SD4[Covers shape, configuration, pattern, ornament]
    end
    SC -.->|Ceases upon registration per S.15(2)| SD
    SD -.->|Does not affect artistic copyright| SC
    SD --> SE[Exception: 50 Article Rule]
    SC --> SE
    SE --> SE1[Copyright continues alongside Design right]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question - Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. What is the period of protection for a registered design under the Designs Act, 2000?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Under Section 11 of the Designs Act, 2000, a registered design is protected for an **initial period of 10 years** from the date of registration. This period may be extended by a further **5 years** upon application and payment of prescribed fees, making the **maximum total period of protection 15 years**. The total period under no circumstances shall exceed 15 years from the date of registration. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. State the rule of "50 articles" under Section 15(2) of the Copyright Act, 1957.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The first proviso to Section 15(2) of the Copyright Act, 1957 provides that **copyright shall not cease to subsist** in a design merely because it has been registered under the Designs Act, 2000, if the design is applied to **a single article** or is reproduced on **not more than 50 articles by an industrial process**. If more than 50 articles are produced by industrial process, the copyright ceases upon registration and only the design right survives. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - ESE Module 4 - Full 14 Mark Questions with Sub-parts]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q A(a). [7 Marks]** Explain the statutory provisions relating to "Copyright in Registered Designs" under Section 15 of the Copyright Act, 1957. Discuss the relationship between the Copyright Act, 1957 and the Designs Act, 2000.

**Model Answer:**

The Copyright Act, 1957 and the Designs Act, 2000 are two independent statutes that govern different aspects of intellectual property. Section 15 of the Copyright Act, 1957 contains special provisions dealing with the overlap between these two statutes.

**Section 15(1)** provides that a "design" capable of registration under the Designs Act, 2000, but which has **not been registered**, shall be protected only as an **artistic work** under Section 13(1)(a) of the Copyright Act, and not as a "design." Such unregistered designs receive copyright protection for the **lifetime of the author plus 60 years**. (2 marks)

**Section 15(2)** is the central provision. It states that when a design capable of registration under the Designs Act, 2000 is **registered** under that Act, **copyright shall cease to subsist** in that design. This prevents the owner from obtaining two parallel monopolies — one as copyright and another as design registration — for the same subject matter. (2 marks)

**The three Provisos to Section 15(2)** provide important exceptions:
- **Proviso 1:** Copyright continues if the design is applied to a single article or reproduced on **50 or fewer articles** by industrial process. (1 mark)
- **Proviso 2:** The design may be used as a model or pattern for industrial multiplication without attracting copyright consequences. (1 mark)
- **Proviso 3:** No copyright infringement occurs if the article is made in accordance with the registered design. (1 mark)

**Relationship:** The Copyright Act and the Designs Act operate in a **complementary but non-overlapping** manner. The Copyright Act protects artistic expression for 60 years post-life, while the Designs Act protects industrial application for a maximum of 15 years. The legislature intended to prevent the monopolization of industrial designs for 60+ years. (Total: 7 marks)

---

**Q A(b). [7 Marks]** Mr. Vikram creates an artistic pattern of a peacock and applies it to ceramic plates. He produces 60 plates by industrial process. Subsequently, he registers the design under the Designs Act, 2000. Discuss the IP rights available to Mr. Vikram with reference to Section 15 of the Copyright Act, 1957.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identification of Subject Matter:**
The peacock pattern applied to ceramic plates constitutes a "design" under Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000, as it involves features of pattern and ornament applied to an article. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Application of Section 15(2):**
Since the design is **registered** under the Designs Act, 2000, the main provision of Section 15(2) is triggered. Copyright would normally cease upon registration. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Check for Proviso 1 (the 50-Article Rule):**
Mr. Vikram has produced **60 plates** by industrial process. Since $N = 60 > 50$, the proviso to Section 15(2) **does not apply**. (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Conclusion on IP Rights:**
- **Copyright:** Has **ceased** under Section 15(2) of the Copyright Act, 1957. (1 mark)
- **Design Right:** Available for **10 years (initial) + 5 years (extension) = 15 years maximum** under Section 11 of the Designs Act, 2000. (1 mark)
- **No Double Protection:** Mr. Vikram cannot claim both copyright and design right for the same subject matter. (1 mark)

(Total: 7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Q B(a). [7 Marks]** Distinguish between Copyright and Registered Design. In what circumstances does copyright cease to subsist upon registration of a design?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Copyright | Registered Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Statute** | Copyright Act, 1957 | Designs Act, 2000 |
| **Subject Matter** | Literary, dramatic, musical, artistic works | Shape, configuration, pattern, ornament of an article |
| **Acquisition** | Automatic on creation | Requires application and registration |
| **Term** | Life of author + 60 years | 10 + 5 = 15 years max |
| **Purpose** | Protect artistic expression | Protect industrial/aesthetic appearance |
| **Infringement Test** | Substantial similarity | Visual identical or deceptively similar |
| **Remedy** | Civil + Criminal | Civil only |

(4 marks for distinction)

**Circumstances when copyright ceases upon registration:**

Under **Section 15(2)** of the Copyright Act, 1957, copyright ceases to subsist in a design when:
- The design is capable of registration under the Designs Act, 2000, **AND**
- The design has been **actually registered** under the Designs Act, 2000, **AND**
- The design is applied to **more than 50 articles** by an industrial process.

If the design is applied to **50 or fewer articles**, the proviso to Section 15(2) saves the copyright. (3 marks)

---

**Q B(b). [7 Marks]** Explain the relevant provisions of the Designs Act, 2000 regarding the definition of "design" and the term of protection for a registered design.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Design — Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000:**
"Design" means **only the features of shape, configuration, pattern, ornament or composition of lines or colours** applied to any article, whether in two-dimensional or three-dimensional form, or both, by any industrial process or means, whether manual, mechanical or chemical, separate or combined, which in the finished article appeal to and are judged solely by the eye. (3 marks)

**Essential Ingredients:**
1. The design must be applied to an **article**.
2. It must be capable of being judged **solely by the eye**.
3. It must not include any **mode or principle of construction** (excluded under Section 2(d) proviso).
4. It must not be a **trademark** or **property mark** (excluded under Section 2(d) proviso).
5. It must be **new or original** (Section 4 novelty requirement). (2 marks)

**Term of Protection — Section 11 of the Designs Act, 2000:**
- Initial registration: **10 years** from the date of registration. (1 mark)
- Extension: A further period of **5 years** may be granted upon application. (0.5 marks)
- **Maximum total period = 15 years** from the date of registration. (0.5 marks)
- No further extension is permitted under the Act.

(Total: 7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner Patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing the 50-article rule:** Students often misstate it as "50 OR MORE articles" — it is **50 OR FEWER articles** that preserve copyright. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Citing wrong period for design registration:** Many write "16 years" (old Designs Act, 1911). Under the **Designs Act, 2000**, it is **10 + 5 = 15 years**. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the statutory cross-reference:** Section 15 of the **Copyright Act, 1957** (not Designs Act) is the operative provision. Examiners expect explicit citation. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Omitting the three provisos:** Many students state only the main rule of Section 15(2) and forget the **three provisos** (single article rule, model/pattern rule, no-infringement rule). (–2 marks)
> 5. **Not discussing Section 2(d) definition:** When asked about "design," students skip the statutory definition under Section 2(d) of the Designs Act, 2000. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Mixing up artistic work vs. design distinction:** Artistic work = **Life + 60 years**; Registered design = **10 + 5 years max**. These are not interchangeable. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- ✅ **Section 15(1), Copyright Act, 1957** = Unregistered design = **artistic work** (Life + 60 years).
- ✅ **Section 15(2), Copyright Act, 1957** = Registered design = **copyright ceases**.
- ✅ **Proviso 1 to Section 15(2)** = ≤50 articles = **copyright subsists**.
- ✅ **Section 11, Designs Act, 2000** = **10 years (initial) + 5 years (extension) = 15 years max**.
- ✅ **Section 2(d), Designs Act, 2000** = "Design" means features of **shape, configuration, pattern, ornament** applied to an article.
- ✅ **"Judged solely by the eye"** = essential test for designability.
- ✅ **Section 4, Designs Act, 2000** = Novelty and originality are prerequisites for registration.
- ✅ **India Tobacco Co. v. Registrar of Copyrights (2003)** = Registered design cannot simultaneously enjoy copyright.
- ✅ **Section 22, Copyright Act** = Period for artistic work = **Life of author + 60 years**.
- ✅ **Industrial process threshold** = **>50 articles** triggers design regime.
- ✅ **Key trade-off** = Copyright = automatic + 60 years vs. Design = procedural + 15 years max.
- ✅ **Always cite both statutes** = Copyright Act, 1957 + Designs Act, 2000 in every answer.
- ✅ **Memorize the three provisos** = Proviso 1 (50 articles), Proviso 2 (model/pattern), Proviso 3 (no infringement).
- ✅ **Artistic work ≠ Industrial design** = Even if visually identical, the legal regime differs based on mode of application.
- ✅ **The Section 15 decision tree** = 5 steps from subject-matter identification to final determination of applicable IP regime.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cancellation of Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cancellation of Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cancellation of Registration** in the context of Intellectual Property Rights refers to the legal procedure by which a previously granted IP right (typically a Trademark under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** or a Patent under the **Patents Act, 1970**) is revoked, annulled, or expunged from the official register by a competent statutory authority. For trademarks, it is governed primarily by **Sections 47, 57, and 58** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, while for patents, the corresponding mechanism is termed **Revocation** under **Sections 64 and 65** of the Patents Act, 1970.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In IPR parlance, the term *“Cancellation”* is the statutory language used for **Trademarks**, whereas the equivalent mechanism for **Patents** is technically termed *“Revocation of Patent”*. Students must use the correct terminology in board examinations to score full marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving License" Analogy

Imagine you receive a **10-year Driving License** after passing all tests. The license is registered in the official government database (the *Register*). However, the licensing authority retains the right to **cancel or revoke** that license under specific circumstances:

- If you **never actually drive** for 5 continuous years (analogous to **non-use of a trademark**).
- If you obtained the license by **fraud or misrepresentation** (analogous to a trademark registered with dishonest intent).
- If your driving becomes a **public danger** or your license category no longer legally applies (analogous to a trademark becoming *generic* or *deceptively similar*).

The license does not vanish permanently on a whim — there must be a **valid legal ground**, a **proper forum** (licensing authority or court), and a **defined procedure**. This is exactly how **Cancellation of Registration of a Trademark** functions in IP law.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Statutory Time Periods

> [!NOTE]
> The following statutory time periods are **high-yield** for KTU board examinations:

| Statutory Metric | Duration / Value |
|---|---|
| **Statutory Non-Use Period** for Cancellation | **5 continuous years** (and 3 months) |
| **Initial Registration Validity** (Trademark) | **10 years** from date of application |
| **Initial Registration Validity** (Patent) | **20 years** from date of filing |
| **Limitation for Rectification Application** | **No statutory limit** (subject to delay/condonation) |
| **Cooling-off period for re-filing** (after removal) | 3 months from removal date |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Grounds for Cancellation of Trademark Registration

The statutory grounds are codified in **Section 47(1)** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. A registered trademark may be removed from the register on any of the following grounds:

### A. Substantive Grounds
1. **Continuous Non-Use for 5 Years and 3 Months:** The registered proprietor has not used the mark in India for a continuous period of five years and three months from the date of registration, and no bona fide intention to use existed.
2. **Lack of Bona Fide Intent:** The mark was registered without any genuine intention to use it, coupled with either non-use or abandonment.
3. **Genericness / Common to Trade:** The mark has become the common name in the trade for the goods/services for which it is registered (*e.g., "Aspirin" becoming a generic term*).
4. **Deceptiveness in Use:** The registered trademark, as used, is likely to cause confusion or deception among the public.
5. **Public Morality / Offense:** The use of the mark is contrary to law or is **scandalous, obscene, or offensive** to public morality.
6. **Fraudulent Registration:** The registration was obtained by fraud, false suggestion, or misrepresentation.
7. **Conflict with Prior Rights:** The mark conflicts with an earlier trademark or earlier well-known trademark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 47(2) — The "Special Use" Exception:** A trademark shall NOT be removed if its non-use is shown to be due to *special circumstances in the trade* (such as import restrictions imposed by the Government) and not due to any intention to abandon.

## 2.2 Who Can Apply for Cancellation?

| Person Eligible | Statutory Basis |
|---|---|
| **Any Person Aggrieved** | Section 47(1) read with Section 57 |
| Registered Proprietor of the mark | Section 58 (for correction of register) |
| In case of collective marks | Section 61 |
| Central Government (in national interest) | Section 50 / 57(2) |

> [!NOTE]
> **“Any Person Aggrieved”** is a wider category than *“any person”* and includes rivals, consumers, trading associations, or any commercial entity whose business is affected by the continued registration.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Statutory Section Cheat-Sheet)

| Section | Subject Matter | Key Takeaway |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 25** | Duration & Renewal | Registration is for **10 years**, renewable |
| **Section 46** | Registration subject to certain conditions | Conditions on register |
| **Section 47** | Removal from register & grounds | Core cancellation provision |
| **Section 48** | Stay of proceedings | Power to stay suit |
| **Section 49** | Stay of rectification proceedings | Pending infringement stay |
| **Section 57** | Rectification of register | Power of Registrar / IPAB |
| **Section 58** | Correction of register | Clerical errors etc. |
| **Section 60** | Limitation of proceedings | No limitation period prescribed |
| **Section 61** | Collective marks cancellation | Special provisions |
| **Section 64 of Patents Act** | Revocation of patent | Patent equivalent |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility & Judicial Approach

The Supreme Court of India in **Hardie Trading Ltd. v. Addison Paints & Chemicals Ltd. (2003)** held that non-use for 5 years shifts the **onus of proof** onto the registered proprietor to demonstrate either use or bona fide intention. This is a landmark case frequently cited in KTU model answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** For B.Tech students, the concept of cancellation is critical when understanding **Brand Equity in Tech Companies** (Apple, Tesla, etc.), where trademark cancellation disputes are routinely litigated in jurisdictions like the EU, US, and India.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Analytical Matrix

Since this is a legal-procedural topic (and not a numerical/algebraic one), the derivation takes the form of a **step-by-step statutory procedure matrix**. This aligns with KTU's "Apply" and "Analyze" levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

## 3.1 Procedural Flow of Cancellation Proceedings

### STEP 1: Identification of the Ground for Cancellation
The applicant must first identify the specific statutory ground (refer Section 2.1 above). The most common ground invoked in India is **continuous non-use for 5 years and 3 months** under Section 47(1)(a).

### STEP 2: Determination of the Appropriate Forum

$$
\text{Forum} = \begin{cases}
\text{Registrar of Trademarks} & \text{if rectification relates to clerical errors / s. 58 matters} \\
\text{Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB) / High Court} & \text{if substantive cancellation under s. 47/57}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The Act bifurcates jurisdiction based on the *nature of relief* sought. Substantive grounds (non-use, fraud, genericness) go before the IPAB / High Court, whereas clerical/typographical corrections are handled by the Registrar.

### STEP 3: Filing of the Rectification Application

The applicant (an “aggrieved person”) files an application in the prescribed form (Form **TM-O** or equivalent under the Trade Marks Rules, 2017) with the following details:

- Identity of the registered proprietor
- Registration number of the impugned trademark
- Statement of facts
- Specific grounds with supporting evidence
- Relief sought (removal of mark from register)

### STEP 4: Service of Notice to the Registered Proprietor
The Registrar or IPAB issues notice to the registered proprietor under **Rule 93 of the Trade Marks Rules, 2017**, granting an opportunity to file a counter-statement within the prescribed time (typically **1 month**, extendable).

### STEP 5: Filing of Counter-Statement by the Proprietor
The proprietor may either:
- (a) **Demonstrate actual use** of the mark during the 5-year statutory period, OR
- (b) **Plead special circumstances** justifying non-use, OR
- (c) **Establish bona fide intention** to use the mark.

### STEP 6: Onus of Proof — Legal Principle

$$
\text{Onus}_{\text{initial}} \xrightarrow{\text{5 years non-use proven}} \text{Onus}_{\text{shift to proprietor}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Once the applicant proves continuous non-use for 5+ years, the **legal burden shifts** to the proprietor to rebut the presumption. This is a critical step — students must articulate this shift of onus to secure full marks in KTU answers.

### STEP 7: Evidentiary Proceedings & Hearing
- Both parties file evidence affidavits.
- The Tribunal hears oral arguments.
- The Registrar / IPAB renders a written reasoned order.

### STEP 8: Orders That May Be Passed

$$
\text{Possible Orders} = \begin{cases}
\text{Order 1: Full Removal of Mark from Register} \\
\text{Order 2: Partial Removal (limited to specific goods/services)} \\
\text{Order 3: Conditional Order (subject to limitations imposed)} \\
\text{Order 4: Dismissal of Rectification Application}
\end{cases}
$$

### STEP 9: Right of Appeal
Any aggrieved party may appeal to the **High Court** having jurisdiction, under **Section 91 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**, within **3 months** from the date of the order.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cancellation vs. Rectification vs. Correction

| Parameter | Cancellation (S. 47) | Rectification (S. 57) | Correction (S. 58) |
|---|---|---|---|
