| **Nature** | Substantive removal | Substantive alteration | Clerical/incidental |
| **Trigger** | Aggrieved person / Government | Aggrieved person | Proprietor / Registrar |
| **Grounds** | Non-use, fraud, genericness | Any entry wrongly remaining | Clerical error, obvious mistake |
| **Forum** | IPAB / High Court | Registrar / IPAB | Registrar |
| **Effect** | Mark removed | Register corrected | Register corrected |
| **Finality** | Subject to appeal to HC | Subject to appeal | Generally administrative |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Procedural Flowchart — Cancellation of Trademark Registration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AGGRIEVED PERSON IDENTIFIES GROUND"] --> B{"GROUND IS SUBSTANTIVE OR CLERICAL?"}
    B -- "Clerical" --> C["APPROACH REGISTRAR OF TRADEMARKS"]
    B -- "Substantive" --> D["APPROACH IPAB OR HIGH COURT"]
    C --> E["FILE APPLICATION IN PRESCRIBED FORM"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["ISSUE OF NOTICE TO PROPRIETOR"]
    F --> G["PROPRIETOR FILES COUNTER STATEMENT"]
    G --> H{"EVIDENCE OF USE PRODUCED?"}
    H -- "Yes - Use Proven" --> I["APPLICATION DISMISSED"]
    H -- "No - Use Not Proven" --> J["MARK REMOVED FROM REGISTER"]
    I --> K["RIGHT TO APPEAL TO HIGH COURT WITHIN 3 MONTHS"]
    J --> K
    J --> L["ENTRY REMOVED FROM TRADEMARK REGISTER"]
    K --> M["HIGH COURT DECISION FINAL"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Cancellation Grounds

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SECTION 47 - GROUNDS FOR CANCELLATION"] --> B["NON USE GROUNDS"]
    A --> C["PUBLIC INTEREST GROUNDS"]
    A --> D["PRIORITY GROUNDS"]
    A --> E["FRAUD GROUNDS"]
    B --> B1["5 Years 3 Months Continuous Non Use"]
    B --> B2["No Bona Fide Intention to Use"]
    C --> C1["Offensive to Public Morality"]
    C --> C2["Use Contrary to Law"]
    D --> D1["Conflict with Earlier Trademark"]
    D --> D2["Conflict with Well Known Mark"]
    E --> E1["Fraudulent Registration"]
    E --> E2["False Suggestion of Connection"]
```

## 4.3 Jurisdictional Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CANCELLATION OF TRADEMARK"] --> B["WHO FILES?"]
    B --> B1["Any Person Aggrieved"]
    B --> B2["Registered Proprietor Voluntary"]
    B --> B3["Central Government"]
    A --> C["WHERE TO FILE?"]
    C --> C1["Registrar of Trademarks - For Section 58 Matters"]
    C --> C2["IPAB - For Section 47 and 57 Matters"]
    C --> C3["High Court - Appellate Jurisdiction"]
    A --> D["WHAT IS THE RELIEF?"]
    D --> D1["Full Removal from Register"]
    D --> D2["Partial Removal"]
    D --> D3["Conditional Retention"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node identifiers are purely alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels contain only raw uppercase text without any markdown formatting tags.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define "Any Person Aggrieved" under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> The expression **"any person aggrieved"** is not defined in the Trade Marks Act, 1999, but has been interpreted by Indian courts. It includes a person who has used a trademark identical or deceptively similar to the registered mark, a rival trader in the same goods, a consumer, a person dealing in the goods, and a registered user whose interests are affected. The applicant must demonstrate that they are *“substantially interested”* in having the mark removed and not merely a busybody or a person moved by curiosity.

*(Word limit: ~80 words; 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. What is the statutory period of non-use that invites cancellation of a trademark? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> Under **Section 47(1)(a)** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, a registered trademark may be removed from the register on the ground that the registered proprietor has **not used the mark in India for a continuous period of five years and three months** from the date of registration, and there is no bona fide intention to use the mark. The 3-month additional period is provided to enable the proprietor to demonstrate use up to the date of application.

*(Word limit: ~60 words; 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provision (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **ONE** of the following:

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **grounds for cancellation of a trademark** under Section 47 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **procedure for removal of a trademark from the register**, including jurisdiction, filing, and the role of the IPAB. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The grounds for cancellation of a registered trademark are enumerated under **Section 47(1)** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. The following are the principal grounds:

1. **Non-Use for 5 Years and 3 Months (Section 47(1)(a)):** Continuous non-use without any bona fide intention to use constitutes the most common ground. The applicant must first prove the date of registration and the continuous 5-year non-use, after which the **onus shifts** to the proprietor.

2. **Lack of Bona Fide Intention (Section 47(1)(b)):** If at the time of registration the proprietor had no genuine intention to use the mark, the mark becomes liable to be removed.

3. **Genericness (Section 47(1)(c)):** When the trademark becomes the common name of the goods or services in the trade, registration may be cancelled. *Example: "Thermos" became generic for vacuum flasks.*

4. **Deceptiveness (Section 47(1)(d)):** When the mark as used is likely to deceive or cause confusion among the public.

5. **Offense to Public Morality (Section 47(1)(e)):** Marks that are scandalous, obscene, or contrary to public decency.

6. **Use Contrary to Law (Section 47(1)(f)):** Where continued use would be contrary to any prevailing statute.

7. **Fraud or False Suggestion (Section 47(1)(g)):** Where the registration was secured by fraud, false suggestion, or misrepresentation.

8. **Conflict with Prior Rights:** The mark conflicts with an earlier trademark or earlier well-known mark (under Section 11(10)).

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

The procedure for cancellation is as follows:

1. **Identification of Forum:** The aggrieved person files the application before the **Registrar** (for corrections under Section 58) or before the **IPAB / High Court** (for substantive cancellation under Sections 47 and 57).

2. **Filing of Application:** The application is filed in the prescribed form with the statement of grounds and supporting evidence.

3. **Issuance of Notice:** The Registrar/IPAB issues notice to the registered proprietor under **Rule 93 of the Trade Marks Rules, 2017**.

4. **Counter-Statement:** The proprietor files a counter-statement within the prescribed period (typically 1 month, extendable up to 3 months).

5. **Evidence & Hearing:** Both parties file evidence affidavits. The Tribunal hears oral arguments.

6. **Order:** The Tribunal may pass an order of **removal, partial removal, conditional retention, or dismissal**.

7. **Appeal:** An appeal lies to the **High Court** under Section 91 within **3 months**.

8. **Finality:** On removal, the mark ceases to be the property of the proprietor. However, an application for re-registration can be filed after **3 months** from the date of removal.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Adapted]**

**(a)** Distinguish between **Cancellation (S. 47)**, **Rectification (S. 57)**, and **Correction (S. 58)** of a trademark. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze the **judicial approach to the 5-year non-use rule**, citing the leading case of *Hardie Trading v. Addison Paints (2003)*. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Parameter | Section 47 | Section 57 | Section 58 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Term** | Removal from register | Rectification of register | Correction of register |
| **Nature** | Substantive | Substantive | Clerical / Incidental |
| **Trigger** | Any person aggrieved | Any person aggrieved | Proprietor / Registrar |
| **Grounds** | Non-use, fraud, genericness | Any entry wrongly remaining on the register | Clerical error, obvious mistake |
| **Forum** | IPAB / High Court | Registrar / IPAB | Registrar |
| **Effect** | Mark removed from register | Register rectified | Register corrected |

The key distinction is that **Sections 47 and 57** are **substantive** provisions that may result in the **loss of trademark rights**, while **Section 58** is purely **administrative** and deals with corrections that do not affect the substance of the registration.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

The leading decision on the 5-year non-use rule is the Supreme Court ruling in **Hardie Trading Ltd. v. Addison Paints & Chemicals Ltd., AIR 2003 SC 3377**, where the Court laid down the following principles:

1. **Initial Burden:** The applicant must first establish the date of registration and prove the continuous non-use for 5 years and 3 months.

2. **Shift of Onus:** Once the applicant establishes non-use, the **burden shifts** to the registered proprietor to demonstrate:
   - (a) Actual use of the mark during the relevant period, OR
   - (b) Bona fide intention to use, OR
   - (c) Special circumstances preventing use.

3. **Special Circumstances:** The Court clarified that “special circumstances in the trade” must be genuinely beyond the control of the proprietor (such as war, import restrictions, etc.) and not self-created difficulties.

4. **Defensive Use:** Mere defensive registration, without commercial use, is insufficient to defeat a cancellation application.

5. **No Mechanical Approach:** The Court warned that non-use alone does not automatically lead to removal — the Tribunal must look at the totality of facts and the conduct of the proprietor.

This case is a cornerstone of trademark jurisprudence in India and a frequent citation in KTU model answer sheets.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *Cancellation* (Trademarks) with *Revocation* (Patents). Mixing the two results in **2-mark deduction**.
> - **Always cite the Section number** (S. 47 / S. 57 / S. 58) explicitly. Examiners specifically check this.
> - **Use the term "Any Person Aggrieved"** — not "any person." The wider interpretation carries marks.
> - **Mention the 5 Years and 3 Months** rule precisely, not just "5 years."
> - For **Patent revocation**, remember to cite **S. 64 of the Patents Act, 1970**, not the Trade Marks Act.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Examination:**

- **Cancellation of Registration** refers to the removal of a trademark from the official register, distinct from the **Revocation of Patents** under Section 64 of the Patents Act, 1970.
- The **primary statutory provision** is **Section 47 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**, supplemented by **Sections 57, 58, and 61**.
- The **most common ground** is **continuous non-use for 5 years and 3 months** from the date of registration.
- **Onus of Proof** shifts to the proprietor once 5-year non-use is established by the applicant.
- **Special circumstances** in trade can rebut a non-use claim — these must be *genuine and not self-created*.
- The **aggrieved person** filing the application must demonstrate a *substantial commercial interest* in removal.
- **Jurisdiction lies** with the **IPAB / High Court** for substantive matters, and the **Registrar** for clerical corrections.
- **Appeal to the High Court** is available under **Section 91** within **3 months** of the order.
- The **landmark case** is *Hardie Trading Ltd. v. Addison Paints & Chemicals Ltd. (2003)*, which must be cited in long-answer questions.
- A removed mark can be **re-registered** only after a **3-month cooling-off period** from the date of removal.
- **Genericness** is a crucial ground — once a mark becomes a common name, it loses legal protection.
- **Fraudulent registration** is a ground for cancellation, distinct from mere non-use.
- The **Trademarks Rules, 2017** govern the procedural aspects, with **Form TM-O** being the prescribed application form.
- **Engineers, B.Tech students, and tech professionals** should understand cancellation because tech companies regularly face cancellation disputes (e.g., brand squatting, defensive registrations).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Piracy** in Intellectual Property Rights refers to the **unauthorized reproduction, distribution, transmission, public performance, or making available of copyrighted works** (literary, artistic, musical, cinematographic, or software) without obtaining a valid license or permission from the copyright owner, thereby infringing the exclusive rights granted under copyright law.

### 1.1 Etymology and Legal Foundation

The term **"piracy"** is borrowed from maritime terminology where it referred to the illegal seizure of ships and cargo. In the IPR context, it metaphorically describes the **"theft" of intellectual cargo** — the creative expression of an author, composer, or inventor. It is a strict-liability tort in most jurisdictions, meaning the intent of the infringer is secondary to the act of unauthorized use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Anchor (India):** Piracy in the Indian context is governed primarily by the **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended in 2012). It is treated as a **civil wrong (infringement)**, a **criminal offence**, and can also be addressed under **Information Technology Act, 2000** for digital/online piracy.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **🍎 Conceptual Analogy: The Unauthorized Lemonade Stand**
>
> Imagine **Ravi** spends 6 months perfecting a secret lemonade recipe (the right mix of mint, lemon, and rock salt). He registers his recipe under the **Recipe Copyright Office** and gets a **Certificate of Protection**. He sets up a shop at the corner of the street.
>
> Now, **Suresh** walks past, tastes the lemonade, goes home, and copies the *exact same recipe* (proportions, presentation, and even the logo). He sets up a shop 200 meters away, selling the same product for half the price because he saved all the R&D cost.
>
> **What did Suresh do?** He committed **P-IRACY** of the lemonade recipe. He made an unauthorized copy of an IP-protected work and commercially exploited it. The law (Copyright Act) gives Ravi the right to sue, seize the infringing goods, and claim damages.
>
> This is precisely what happens when a person downloads a cracked copy of Adobe Photoshop, distributes a pirated Blu-ray of a new movie, or sells counterfeit software in a market. The act of *copying* and *commercializing* another's creative labor without consent = **Piracy**.

### 1.3 Why "Piracy" Is a Special Term in IP

| Aspect | Piracy vs. General IP Infringement |
|---|---|
| **Scope** | Narrower term — specifically targets **copyrighted works** (films, music, software, books) |
| **Medium** | Usually involves **mass unauthorized reproduction** at commercial scale |
| **Intent** | Often **commercial intent** (profit-making), though private non-profit use can also qualify |
| **Severity** | Treated as a **serious criminal offence** with imprisonment and heavy fines |
| **Distinction** | Plagiarism is a *moral* wrong (academic integrity); Piracy is a *legal* wrong (statutory tort + crime) |

### 1.4 Key Borderline Distinctions (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piracy ≠ Plagiarism ≠ Counterfeiting ≠ Infringement**
>
> - **Piracy:** Unauthorized **copying and distribution** of copyrighted material (focus: mass reproduction).
> - **Plagiarism:** Presenting someone else's work as one's own (focus: attribution/honesty — *not* necessarily a legal wrong).
> - **Counterfeiting:** Copying of **trademarks** on physical goods (focus: consumer deception & brand).
> - **Infringement:** Umbrella term covering violation of **any** IP right (patent, trademark, design, copyright).
>
> A question on KTU may ask: *"Is downloading a movie from a torrent site piracy or infringement?"* — The answer is **both** (piracy is a *form* of copyright infringement carried out at scale).

### 1.5 Standard Metrics in the Field

- **Software Piracy Rate:** Expressed as a **percentage** of total software in use that is unlicensed (global average ≈ **37%** as per BSA reports).
- **TRIPS Compliance Threshold:** WTO member countries must provide **criminal procedures and penalties** for *willful* copyright piracy on a **commercial scale** (TRIPS Article 61).
- **Commercial Scale Threshold (India):** Infringing copies of works with retail value exceeding **₹50,000** qualify as cognizable offences under Section 63 of the Copyright Act, 1957.
- **Statutory Damages Cap:** Indian courts can award damages up to **₹2,00,000 per work infringed** (and the court can even extend this where actual loss is higher).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Framework: The Anatomy of Piracy

Piracy, although appearing as a single act, is a multi-stage violation involving distinct **operational components**. For KTU exam purposes, the examiner expects you to break it down into these logical layers:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4-Stage Piracy Model (Examination Hook):**
> 1. **Access** → The pirate obtains the original work (legally purchased DVD, leaked studio copy, stolen master file).
> 2. **Reproduction** → A duplicate copy is made (burning, ripping, screen-recording, keygen generation).
> 3. **Distribution** → The copy is shared with the public (sale on streets, torrent upload, peer-to-peer networks, USB sharing).
> 4. **Profit / Harm** → The pirated work either generates illegal revenue (commercial piracy) OR causes economic harm by displacing legitimate sales (non-commercial piracy).

> If **any** of the rights under Section 14 of the Copyright Act, 1957 (e.g., the right to reproduce, communicate to public, distribute, or make translations) is exercised without a license, the chain of piracy is established.

### 2.2 Typology of Piracy — KTU High-Yield Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Classification (Recommended Diagram-Free Memory Aid):**
> Piracy is classified along **three orthogonal axes**:
>
> **Axis A — By Medium of Work:**
> - **Software Piracy** (game cracks, license-key generators, OEM counterfeiting)
> - **Film / Cinematographic Piracy** (camcorder recording, Blu-ray rips, screen-cam torrents)
> - **Music / Sound Recording Piracy** (MP3 rips, mixtapes, streaming reuploads)
> - **Book / Publishing Piracy** (photocopied textbooks, illegal PDFs, libgen/mirror sites)
>
> **Axis B — By Distribution Channel:**
> - **Physical / Hard-Disk Piracy** (CDs, DVDs, pen drives sold in markets)
> - **Online / Internet Piracy** (torrents, streaming sites, file-hosting)
> - **Broadcast Piracy** (unauthorized signal decryption — Section 37 of Copyright Act)
>
> **Axis C — By Scale & Intent:**
> - **Commercial Piracy** — for profit; **cognizable criminal offence** with imprisonment.
> - **Non-Commercial / End-User Piracy** — personal use; civil liability & limited remedies.
> - **Organized / Syndicated Piracy** — large operations; treated as organized crime.

### 2.3 Legal Anatomy: Sections of the Indian Copyright Act, 1957

| Section | Subject | Relevance to Piracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 13** | Works in which Copyright Subsists | Defines what can be pirated (literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematographic, sound recordings). |
| **Section 14** | Meaning of Copyright | Lists the **exclusive rights** (reproduce, publish, perform, communicate) that piracy violates. |
| **Section 51** | When Copyright is Infringed | The **pivot section** — any unauthorized exercise of Section 14 rights = infringement = piracy. |
| **Section 62** | Remedies for Infringement | Civil remedies — injunction, damages, account of profits, delivery of infringing copies. |
| **Section 63** | Offence of Infringement (Criminal) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹2,00,000** (first offence); 6 years + ₹5,00,000 (subsequent). |
| **Section 65** | Tampering with Software | Specific offence for software piracy — knows circumvention of technological protection measures. |
| **Section 65A & 65B** | Anti-Circumvention | Criminalises bypassing DRM, digital locks, and access-control mechanisms. |
| **Section 67** | Possession of Plates for Piracy | Criminalises owning master copies (printing plates, master discs) intended for piracy. |
| **Section 70** | Offences by Companies | Vicarious liability — directors and officers of a pirate company are personally liable. |
| **TRIPS Art. 61** | International Obligation | Mandatory criminal penalties for *willful* commercial-scale piracy in all WTO nations. |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The Numerical Framework for Damages in Piracy Cases (Indian Law)**

In a copyright piracy suit, the plaintiff (right-holder) may elect **one** of the following damages models:

| Model | Formula | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Damages** | $D_{actual} = L_{lost} + P_{infringer}$ | When clear loss and infringer's profit are proven |
| **Statutory Damages** | $D_{statutory} = \min(\text{₹200{,}000 per work},\; L_{proven})$ | Default remedy in India (cap on damages) |
| **Account of Profits** | $D_{profits} = R_{infringer} - C_{legitimate}$ | Disgorgement of illegal gains |
| **Aggravated Damages** (e.g., John Doe orders) | $D_{agg} = D_{base} \times k$ | Where infringement is deliberate & willful ($k > 1$) |

Where:
- $L_{lost}$ = License fees the plaintiff would have earned.
- $P_{infringer}$ = Profits gained by the infringer.
- $R_{infringer}$ = Gross revenue from the pirate's sales.
- $C_{legitimate}$ = Legitimate cost of producing the original work.
- $k$ = Aggravation multiplier set by the court (typically $1.5$ to $3$).

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering & Society

> **💡 Real-World Utility (For KTU Application-Type Questions):**
>
> 1. **Software Industry Economics:** The global software industry loses over **$46 billion annually** to piracy (BSA Global Software Survey). Engineers building proprietary products must price competitively while accommodating this leakage.
> 2. **Cybersecurity Linkage:** Pirated software often ships with **malware, trojans, and cryptominers** — making anti-piracy awareness a component of enterprise cybersecurity policy.
> 3. **Compliance for Tech Firms:** IT companies placing engineers abroad (USA, EU) must enforce strict licensing — corporate end-user piracy exposes firms to **BCP (Business Continuity)** and **legal liability** risks.
> 4. **Innovation Incentive:** Piracy depresses creator revenues, which over time **reduces R&D investment** in new technologies — a direct threat to the engineering innovation pipeline.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown — Establishing Piracy

### 3.1 The Legal Test for Establishing Piracy (For 7-Mark Analytical Questions)

When a question asks *"How do you establish that an act amounts to piracy?"*, examiners expect a structured 5-step reasoning chain:

> **Step 1 — Existence of a Copyrighted Work**
> The plaintiff must first prove that a valid copyright **subsists** in the work. This is established by demonstrating:
> - The work falls within a protected category under **Section 13** of the Copyright Act.
> - The statutory requirements of **originality** and **fixation** are met.
> - The copyright has not expired (in India, life of the author + **60 years** post-mortem for most works).

> **Step 2 — Ownership of Copyright**
> The plaintiff must show they are the **owner of the copyright** or an exclusive licensee. Proof is by:
> - Author declaration (for unpublished works).
> - Copyright registration certificate (prima facie evidence under Section 48).
> - Chain of title in case of assigned or inherited rights.

> **Step 3 — Identity of Infringed Material**
> The plaintiff must prove that the **infringing copy** is a **substantial reproduction** (not necessarily a verbatim one) of the copyrighted work. The court applies the *ordinary observer test*: would a lay observer conclude the work was copied from the original?

> **Step 4 — Unauthorized Use**
> The plaintiff must show that the defendant exercised one of the **exclusive rights under Section 14** — e.g., reproduced, distributed, communicated, performed — **without a valid license**.

> **Step 5 — Causal Harm (For Damages)**
> The plaintiff must show that the unauthorized use caused **economic loss**, **moral injury**, or resulted in the infringer's **unjust enrichment**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Illustration — Damages Calculation

> **Q (Worked Example):** A software firm developed a proprietary CAD tool selling at ₹20,000/license. A pirate sold 800 unauthorized copies at ₹2,000 each over 2 years. The court found that the legitimate market would have absorbed **60% of these sales** at full price. Additionally, the court found **aggravation factor $k = 2$** because the defendant had sold the same pirated software earlier (subsequent offence under Section 63). Compute the damages.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute License-Fee Loss } (L_{lost}) &= 800 \times 0.60 \times ₹20{,}000 \\
&= 480 \times ₹20{,}000 \\
&= ₹96{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Compute Pirate's Unjust Profit } (P_{infringer}) &= 800 \times ₹2{,}000 \\
&= ₹16{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Actual Damages (Loss of License + Disgorgement)} &= ₹96{,}00{,}000 + ₹16{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹1{,}12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: Aggravation for Subsequent Offence} &= ₹1{,}12{,}00{,}000 \times 2 \\
&= ₹2{,}24{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5: Check Statutory Cap (Section 62)} \quad &\text{Cap} = 800 \times ₹2{,}00{,}000 = ₹16{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\
&\text{Since } ₹2.24 \text{ crores} \ll \text{cap, full award stands.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** The court may award approximately **₹2.24 crores** in damages, **plus** an injunction against further sale, **plus** destruction of infringing copies, **plus** imprisonment up to **6 years** for the subsequent offence under Section 63.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: How Online Piracy Operates (Process Model)

> **Step 1 — Acquisition:** A release-group (online piracy syndicate) obtains the original work — a studio screener, an internal build, a leaked album, or a cracked software binary.
>
> **Step 2 — Ripping / Cracking:** Tools like *MakeMKV*, *HandBrake*, or *key generators* are used to strip DRM and compress the file.
>
> **Step 3 — Upload:** The file is uploaded to a **hosting platform** (Mega.nz, Google Drive mirrors) or seeded on a **torrent tracker** (ThePirateBay, 1337x, RARBG successors).
>
> **Step 4 — Indexing:** Specialized **piracy search engines** (piracy-related portals, Telegram channels, Discord servers) index and provide one-click download/stream access.
>
> **Step 5 — Monetization:** Revenue is generated through:
> - **Ad revenue** on streaming portals.
> - **Crypto-mining** injected into download pages.
> - **Ransomware** embedded in cracked software.
> - **Subscription tiers** for premium links.
>
> **Step 6 — Legal Response:** Right-holders issue **DMCA / IT Act takedown notices**, file **John Doe suits** for injunction, and work with **INTERPOL** to dismantle syndicate leadership.

### 3.4 Defences Available Against a Piracy Charge

| Defence | Legal Basis | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Fair Use (India: Fair Dealing)** | Section 52, Copyright Act | Limited use for criticism, review, research, education, news reporting. |
| **License from Owner** | Express or implied licence | No infringement if consent was validly given. |
| **First Sale Doctrine** | Section 14(d) read with Section 51 | Owner of a *particular* copy can resell it, but cannot reproduce. |
| **Statute of Limitations** | Limitation Act, 1963 — 3 years | Suit barred if filed beyond limitation period. |
| **Independent Creation** | Defence to copyright | Must be *truly* independent; suspicious similarity rebuts this. |
| **Work in Public Domain** | Copyright expired | No protection post-expiry (60 years p.m.a. in India). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** A frequently-held misconception is that *"sharing with friends is fine."* In law, even **non-commercial** distribution to the public (e.g., uploading a movie to Telegram) is **piracy** under Section 14(a) — and is *cognizable* if commercial scale is established.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Piracy Ecosystem — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of the piracy value chain**, illustrating how an infringing copy flows from the original creator to the end-consumer, and the legal/technological interventions that attempt to interdict it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Creator / Studio] --> B[Original Work with Copyright]
    B --> C{Channel of Acquisition}
    C --> C1[Leaked Master Copy]
    C --> C2[Stolen Screener / Pre-Release]
    C --> C3[Cracking Reverse Engineering of Software]
    C1 --> D[Reproduction and Ripping]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Upload to Host or Torrent]
    E --> F1[Piracy Portal Indexing]
    E --> F2[Telegram or Discord Channel]
    E --> F3[Physical Disc Duplication]
    F1 --> G[End User Download or Stream]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> H[Retail Sale in Black Market]
    G --> I[Monetization via Ads or Malware or Crypto Mining]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Right Holder Revenue Loss]
    J --> K[Legal and Technological Response]
    K --> K1[DMCA Takedown Notice]
    K --> K2[John Doe Injunction in Court]
    K --> K3[INTERPOL and Police Raids]
    K --> K4[Anti Piracy DRM and Watermarking]
    K1 --> L[Reduction in Piracy]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic for Classifying an Act as Piracy

This second Mermaid diagram provides a **decision-tree** that an examiner may use as a model answer scaffold for a 7-mark question on *"How do you determine whether an act is piracy?"*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Identify the Act] --> Q1{Is there a protected work under Section 13}
    Q1 -- No --> X1[No Piracy Possible]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the plaintiff the copyright owner or licensee}
    Q2 -- No --> X2[No Standing to Sue]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Did the defendant exercise a Section 14 right}
    Q3 -- No --> X3[No Infringement]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Was there a valid license or fair dealing}
    Q4 -- Yes --> X4[Defence Applies]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Substantial similarity to original work}
    Q5 -- No --> X5[Independent Creation Defence]
    Q5 -- Yes --> R1[Act Established as Piracy]
    R1 --> R2{Civil or Criminal}
    R2 -- Civil --> R3[Injunction plus Damages under Section 62]
    R2 -- Criminal --> R4[Imprisonment plus Fine under Section 63]
    R2 -- Online --> R5[IT Act 2000 plus Section 65A and 65B]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] — Q1 (3 Marks)**
*Define the term "piracy" in the context of Intellectual Property Rights. How is it distinct from counterfeiting?*

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Style):**
Piracy refers to the **unauthorized reproduction, distribution, or communication to the public of copyrighted works** (films, music, software, books) without permission from the copyright owner, in violation of the exclusive rights under Section 14 of the Copyright Act, 1957.

**Distinction from Counterfeiting:**

| Parameter | Piracy | Counterfeiting |
|---|---|---|
| IP Right | Copyright | Trademark |
| Subject | Creative expression | Branded goods |
| Example | Pirated DVD of a film | Fake Rolex watch |
| Statute | Copyright Act, 1957 | Trademarks Act, 1999 |

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Q2 (3 Marks)**
*State the punishment provided under Section 63 of the Copyright Act, 1957 for the offence of copyright piracy.*

**Model Answer:**
Under **Section 63** of the Copyright Act, 1957, any person who knowingly infringes copyright or abets such infringement is punishable with:
- **Imprisonment:** Minimum **6 months**, extendable to **3 years** for the first offence.
- **Fine:** Minimum **₹50,000**, extendable to **₹2,00,000**.
- For a **subsequent offence**: imprisonment up to **6 years** and fine up to **₹5,00,000**.
- For **psychotropic substance use during offence**: imprisonment up to **10 years** (per 2012 amendment).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Question A (14 Marks)**
*(a)* Explain the various forms of piracy in Intellectual Property Rights. Differentiate between commercial and non-commercial piracy. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* Discuss the role of Sections 65, 65A, and 65B of the Copyright Act, 1957 in combating software piracy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer to (a):**
Piracy manifests in several forms, classified by the medium, the channel, and the scale of activity. The major forms are:

1. **Software Piracy:** Unauthorized copying, distribution, or use of proprietary software. Examples include OEM counterfeiting, soft-lifting (installing on multiple machines), and keygen/crack distribution.
2. **Film/Cinematographic Piracy:** Camcorder recording in theatres, ripping of optical media (Blu-ray/DVD), and online streaming. India loses an estimated ₹18,000+ crores annually to film piracy.
3. **Music/Sound Recording Piracy:** Ripping of audio tracks, unauthorized uploads on streaming platforms, and physical cassette/CD duplication.
4. **Publishing Piracy:** Photocopying of textbooks, illegal e-book distribution, and library-of-pirated-books (libgen) hosting.
5. **Broadcast Signal Piracy:** Unauthorized decryption and redistribution of pay-TV signals under Section 37 of the Copyright Act.

**Commercial vs Non-Commercial Piracy:**

| Feature | Commercial Piracy | Non-Commercial Piracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Profit-making motive | Personal use or sharing |
| **Scale** | Mass reproduction (hundreds+) | Single copy or limited sharing |
| **Liability** | Criminal + civil | Civil only (in India, generally) |
| **Example** | Selling pirated DVDs in market | Downloading a movie for personal viewing |
| **Punishment** | Up to 6 years imprisonment + fine | Injunction + statutory damages |

The TRIPS Agreement (Article 61) mandates criminal liability only for **commercial-scale** piracy, reflecting this distinction.

**Model Answer to (b):**
Sections 65, 65A, and 65B of the Copyright Act, 1957 form a **three-layered legal shield** specifically designed to protect software and digital works from piracy:

**Section 65 — Specific Offence of Software Tampering:**
Any person who knowingly **uses, sells, or distributes** infringing copies of computer programs (software) is liable for imprisonment up to 3 years + fine up to ₹2 lakhs. Importantly, this section was strengthened in the 2012 amendment to treat software piracy as a **standalone offence**, separate from general copyright infringement.

**Section 65A — Protection of Technological Protection Measures (TPMs):**
This section criminalises the act of **circumventing technological protection** such as Digital Rights Management (DRM), encryption, password-protection, and license-key verification — *regardless of whether the underlying use is infringing*. This is critical because the act of *bypassing* the protection itself is the offence.

**Section 65B — Protection of Rights Management Information (RMI):**
This penalises the **tampering or removal of Rights Management Information** — the metadata embedded in digital files that identifies the author, ownership, and licensing terms. Removing a watermark, copyright notice, or author tag (even if the file itself is then shared legally under fair use) is an offence.

**Combined Effect:** Together, these three sections create a **deterrence architecture** that targets the **tools, the techniques, and the identifying information** involved in software piracy — making India compliant with the WIPO Internet Treaties (WCT and WPPT).

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Question B (14 Marks)**
*(a)* Discuss the economic and social impacts of piracy on creative industries. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* Explain the international legal framework for combating piracy, with emphasis on TRIPS Agreement and WIPO treaties. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer to (a):**
The impacts of piracy on creative industries are multi-dimensional, spanning economic, social, and cultural dimensions.

**Economic Impacts:**
- **Direct Revenue Loss:** The global film industry loses approximately **$40–97 billion annually** to piracy (depending on the methodology used by the US Chamber of Commerce and the IPI). In India, the figure is estimated at **₹18,000 crores annually** for the film industry alone.
- **Job Displacement:** Reduced revenues force studios and publishers to cut production budgets, leading to layoffs in the **entire creative value chain** — writers, technicians, musicians, VFX artists, and post-production staff.
- **Reduced R&D:** For software, lower revenues translate directly into **lower R&D investment**, slowing the rate of technological innovation.
- **Tax Revenue Loss:** Governments lose tax income on legitimate sales, affecting public services.

**Social Impacts:**
- **Security Risks:** Pirated software is a primary vector for **malware, ransomware, and cryptominers** — costing global consumers billions in cybersecurity damages.
- **Quality Degradation:** Pirated copies often lack the producer's quality controls, leading to **incomplete experiences** (missing subtitles, broken features, virus-laden installers).
- **Undermining of Rule of Law:** Widespread piracy erodes the **social contract** that respects creators' livelihoods, which in turn discourages younger generations from pursuing creative professions.
- **Cultural Erosion:** When local creators cannot monetize their work, **regional languages and indigenous art forms** suffer disproportionately — a form of cultural homogenization.

**Model Answer to (b):**
The international legal framework for combating piracy rests on three pillars:

**1. The Berne Convention (1886) — Foundation of Copyright Protection:**
- Establishes the principle of **automatic protection** (no registration required).
- Mandates **national treatment** (foreign works protected identically to domestic works).
- Defines the **minimum standards of protection** that all member countries must grant.
- Includes **moral rights** (right of paternity and integrity).

**2. The TRIPS Agreement (1994) — Trade-Linked Enforcement:**
- Article 9 incorporates the Berne Convention obligations into the WTO framework.
- **Article 61** mandates **criminal procedures and penalties** for *willful* trademark counterfeiting and *copyright piracy on a commercial scale*. This is the key enforcement provision.
- Requires **effective civil remedies** (injunctions, damages, seizure of infringing goods).
- Establishes **border enforcement measures** — customs can seize pirated goods at the point of import/export.

**3. WIPO Internet Treaties (1996):**
- **WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT)** — extends copyright protection to the **digital environment**, addressing the unique challenges of online piracy.
- **WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty (WPPT)** — protects performers and producers of sound recordings in the digital age.
- Both treaties mandate **anti-circumvention laws** (the legal basis for Section 65A and 65B in India).

**4. Beijing Treaty on Audiovisual Performances (2012):**
- Strengthens protection for **actors and performers** in audiovisual works — relevant to film piracy.

**5. ACTA and Regional Treaties:**
- The Anti-Counterfeiting Trade Agreement (signed 2011) and EU IPRED (2004) provide additional **enforcement teeth** for international cooperation.

> **India's Compliance:** India, as a WTO and WIPO member, has updated its Copyright Act (especially in 2012) to align with TRIPS Article 61 and the WIPO Internet Treaties — making Sections 65, 65A, 65B, 67, and 70 the operational enforcement tools.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Piracy with Plagiarism:** A *very common* mistake. Plagiarism is an *ethical* violation (attribution failure) — Piracy is a *legal* violation (unauthorized copying for distribution). Examiners specifically mark down when these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Commercial Scale" Threshold:** TRIPS Article 61 and Section 63 require *commercial scale* for **criminal** liability. A private download is civil wrong, not criminal offence. Many students wrongly state that *any* piracy is a criminal act.
> 3. **Mislabelling Counterfeiting as Piracy:** Counterfeiting is a **trademark** issue, piracy is a **copyright** issue. A fake Nike shirt is counterfeiting, not piracy.
> 4. **Forgetting Section 65A/65B Distinction:** Students often merge these. **65A protects the lock (DRM). 65B protects the label (metadata/RMI).** Examiners allocate marks for this distinction.
> 5. **Missing the "Moral Rights" Angle:** For film/music piracy cases, the author's **moral right of paternity and integrity** (Sections 57 of the Copyright Act) is *also* violated — a high-value point examiners reward.
> 6. **Wrong Statutory Figures:** Quoting the old (pre-2012 amendment) figures for Section 63 (which was just ₹200 minimum) loses marks. Use the **current** 2012-amended figures: ₹50,000–₹2,00,000.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **🎯 KTU Last-Minute Recall Sheet — Piracy**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Piracy = unauthorized reproduction + distribution of copyrighted works for **commercial scale** (TRIPS threshold).
>
> ✅ **Indian Statute:** Copyright Act, **1957** (as amended in **2012**), particularly **Sections 14, 51, 62, 63, 65, 65A, 65B, 67, 70**.
>
> ✅ **Forms of Piracy:** Software, Film, Music, Book, Broadcast Signal — distinguished by the **medium** and the **distribution channel** (physical vs online).
>
> ✅ **Commercial vs Non-Commercial:** Commercial = profit motive + mass scale → criminal; Non-Commercial = personal use → civil only.
>
> ✅ **Criminal Penalty (Section 63):** **6 months to 3 years** (first offence); **up to 6 years** (subsequent); fine **₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000 / ₹5,00,000**.
>
> ✅ **Anti-Circumvention Trio:** **Section 65** (software tampering) | **Section 65A** (DRM circumvention) | **Section 65B** (RMI removal).
>
> ✅ **Vicarious Liability (Section 70):** Directors and officers of a pirate company are **personally** liable — cannot hide behind the corporate veil.
>
> ✅ **International Framework:** **Berne Convention (1886) → TRIPS (1994) → WCT & WPPT (1996) → Beijing Treaty (2012)**.
>
> ✅ **TRIPS Article 61:** Mandates criminal liability for *willful* piracy on a *commercial scale* — the global standard India aligns with.
>
> ✅ **Distinctions to Remember:**
> - Piracy ↔ Plagiarism (legal vs ethical)
> - Piracy ↔ Counterfeiting (copyright vs trademark)
> - Piracy ↔ Infringement (subset vs superset)
>
> ✅ **Real-World Hooks:** Software → malware risk; Film → industry loss of ₹18,000 cr/year; Music → Mixtape culture; Books → Library Genesis.
>
> ✅ **Defences:** Fair Dealing (Section 52), License, First Sale Doctrine, Independent Creation, Public Domain (60 years p.m.a. in India).
>
> ✅ **Moral Rights (Section 57):** Often forgotten — but piracy of films/music violates the author's right of **paternity** (attribution) and **integrity** (no distortion).
>
> ✅ **Exam Trigger Phrases:** *"Explain piracy"* → Definition + Forms + Section 63. *"Compare"* → Use the distinction table. *"Discuss economic impact"* → Use the ₹-figures + job-loss + R&D angle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Confidential Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Confidential Information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Confidential Information** refers to any non-public information, technical know-how, commercial data, or proprietary knowledge that is disclosed in circumstances importing an obligation of confidence, and which possesses the qualities of secrecy, novelty, and commercial value. It is protected under the equitable doctrine of **Breach of Confidence** (a branch of the law of obligations) rather than through statutory registration, distinguishing it fundamentally from patents, trademarks, and copyrights under the Indian IPR framework.

Under the **WTO TRIPS Agreement (Article 39)**, undisclosed information is recognized as a category of Intellectual Property. India, as a TRIPS signatory, protects confidential information through:
- **The Indian Contract Act, 1872** (Sections 27, 73, 74)
- **The Information Technology Act, 2000** (Sections 43, 43A, 66, 72, 72A)
- **Common Law principles of equity and breach of confidence**
- **The Competition Act, 2002** (for anti-competitive use)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Confidential Information is the **only form of IPR that does NOT require registration**. Its protection arises from the manner of acquisition, the nature of the information, and the circumstances of disclosure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Secret Recipe" Analogy:** Imagine a small-town baker who has spent **20 years** perfecting a recipe for a spice cake that is famous in the region. The recipe is never written down formally in a patent office. It exists only in her notebook, in her head, and in the trusted hands of her two daughters. If a competitor bribes her employee to leak the recipe, the law does not give the baker a "patent certificate" to defend. Instead, the law says: *"You had a legitimate secret, you protected it reasonably, and the other party misused it in confidence."* This is the essence of Confidential Information — **protection through secrecy, not through registration**.

Think of it like a **diary with a lock**:
- The lock is your **NDA / employment contract**
- The diary is your **trade secret**
- Breaking the lock and reading it = **breach of confidence**
- The law doesn't care who made the diary; it cares that someone **trespassed on your trust**

## 1.3 Core Components of Confidential Information

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Secrecy** | The information is not in the public domain |
| **Commercial Value** | It has actual or potential economic worth |
| **Reasonable Steps** | The owner has taken objective measures to keep it secret |
| **Obligation of Confidence** | Disclosure occurred under a duty of trust (express or implied) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars Test (from Coco v. A.N. Clark (Engineers) Ltd., 1969):** For an action of breach of confidence to succeed, the plaintiff must establish: **(1)** the information has the necessary quality of confidence; **(2)** it was imparted in circumstances importing an obligation of confidence; **(3)** there is unauthorized use causing damage.

## 1.4 Classification under KTU Framework

- **Technical Information** — manufacturing processes, formulae, designs, R&D data
- **Commercial Information** — customer lists, pricing strategies, marketing plans, supplier lists
- **Financial Information** — balance sheets, profit margins, salary structures
- **Personnel Information** — HR records, performance reviews
- **Strategic Information** — M&A plans, expansion blueprints, R&D pipeline

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — How Confidential Information is Protected

The legal protection operates through a **four-stage analytical framework** that examiners frequently test:

### Stage 1: Identification of Confidential Information
- The plaintiff must first **identify with specificity** what information is claimed as confidential.
- **Vague claims fail** — you cannot say "all my business information is confidential."
- The information must be **identifiable and separable** from public knowledge.

### Stage 2: Quality of Confidence Test
The information must possess:
- **Originality of acquisition** (not merely a reproduction of public data)
- **Substantial competitive advantage** if disclosed
- **Restricted accessibility** (not freely available to the industry)

### Stage 3: Circumstances Importing Obligation
- **Express obligations** — signed NDAs, employment contracts, licensing agreements
- **Implied obligations** — fiduciary relationships, employer-employee, professional advisor-client
- **Innocent acquisition** — even if no NDA exists, if the recipient *knew or ought to have known* the information was confidential, obligation arises

### Stage 4: Unauthorized Use or Disclosure
- Actual or **threatened** use constitutes breach
- Use need not be malicious — even **inadvertent** disclosure can attract liability
- **"Springboard doctrine"** — the defendant cannot use confidential info to gain an unfair head-start even after the information enters public domain

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** The **Springboard Doctrine** (per Terrapin Ltd. v. Builders' Supply Co. (Hayes) Ltd., 1967) is a frequently tested concept. It prevents a defendant from claiming innocence because "the secret is now public" — they are barred from exploiting the temporal advantage gained from the breach.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Legal Doctrine | Key Principle | Statutory / Case Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **Breach of Confidence** | Equitable remedy for misuse of secret info | Common Law (Coco v. A.N. Clark, 1969) |
| **Trade Secret Protection** | Information with commercial value kept secret | Article 39, TRIPS Agreement |
| **NDA Enforceability** | Contract-based protection | Indian Contract Act, 1872, Sections 27, 73 |
| **Employee Duty of Fidelity** | Implied duty during employment | Wipro v. Beckman Coulter (2008) |
| **Data Protection / Cyber Breach** | Electronic confidentiality | IT Act 2000, Sec 43A, 72, 72A |
| **Springboard Doctrine** | Bar on unfair temporal advantage | Terrapin v. Builders' Supply (1967) |
| **Damages Calculation** | Loss suffered OR unjust enrichment | Indian Contract Act + Common Law |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

Confidential Information is the **bedrock of industrial strategy** in the following sectors:

- **Pharmaceutical & Biotech:** Drug formulations, clinical trial data (especially prior to patent filing)
- **Software & IT:** Source code, algorithms, customer databases
- **Manufacturing:** Process know-how, vendor lists, cost structures
- **Defence & Aerospace:** Technical specifications, mission-critical data
- **Automotive:** EV battery chemistry, propulsion system designs
- **Food & Beverage:** Coca-Cola's formula, KFC's spice blend

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statute of Limitations Note for KTU:** Civil suits for breach of confidence must be filed within **3 years** from the date the breach became known (Limitation Act, 1963, Article 113). For IT Act offences, the limitation period is **3 years** from commission of the offence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Case Law Derivations

## 3.1 The Three-Element Test — Coco v. A.N. Clark (Engineers) Ltd. (1969)

This is the **foundational analytical matrix** KTU examiners use to grade answers on confidential information. Let us derive the test step-by-step:

$$
\text{Actionable Breach of Confidence} = f(C_q, O_i, U_a)
$$

where $C_q$ = Quality of Confidence, $O_i$ = Obligation imported, $U_a$ = Unauthorized act.

### Step 1: Establish Quality of Confidence ($C_q$)

$$
C_q = S \times V \times A_c
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \text{Secrecy status (binary: 1 if not public, 0 if public)} \\
V &= \text{Commercial value (quantified in monetary or strategic terms)} \\
A_c &= \text{Access control level (objective measures of protection)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The information must score non-zero on ALL three parameters. If $S = 0$, the entire claim collapses because public information cannot be confidential.

### Step 2: Establish Obligation of Confidence ($O_i$)

$$
O_i = E_o \lor I_o \lor C_o
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_o &= \text{Express obligation (NDA, contract clause)} \\
I_o &= \text{Implied obligation (fiduciary relationship)} \\
C_o &= \text{Constructive obligation (recipient knew or should have known)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The "$\lor$" (logical OR) means **any one** of these is sufficient. A relationship of trust can exist WITHOUT a written NDA — this is the equitable foundation of the doctrine.

### Step 3: Establish Unauthorized Use ($U_a$)

$$
U_a = D \lor E \lor (S_b \land T_a)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \text{Direct disclosure to a third party} \\
E &= \text{Exploitation for personal/commercial gain} \\
S_b &= \text{Springboard advantage (using info to leapfrog competition)} \\
T_a &= \text{Temporal head-start period (even after public disclosure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** A plaintiff need not prove direct disclosure. If the defendant **used** the information internally to accelerate product development, that constitutes actionable misuse.

## 3.2 Remedies Available — Analytical Matrix

| Remedy Type | Legal Basis | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Injunction (Interim/Permanent)** | Equity | Stops further disclosure or use |
| **Damages (Compensatory)** | Contract Act + Tort | Reimbursement of actual loss |
| **Account of Profits** | Equity | Defendant surrenders profits earned from breach |
| **Delivery-up / Destruction** | Equity | Physical return of documents/materials |
| **Anton Piller Order** | Civil Procedure Code, 1908, Order 39 | Surprise search of premises to prevent destruction of evidence |
| **Mareva Injunction** | Civil Procedure Code | Freezing of assets to prevent dissipation |
| **Criminal Prosecution** | IT Act 2000, Sec 66/72 | Up to 3 years imprisonment + fine for cyber breach |

## 3.3 The Springboard Doctrine — Mathematical Representation

$$
\text{Disadvantage Period} = T_{current} - T_{public}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{current} &= \text{Time at which defendant would have developed the product independently} \\
T_{public} &= \text{Time at which information entered public domain}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** If $T_{public} > T_{current}$, the defendant gained an **unfair temporal advantage**. The plaintiff can seek an injunction for the duration $(T_{public} - T_{current})$ to neutralize this advantage, even though the information is no longer secret.

## 3.4 Landmark Case Law Derivations for KTU

### Case 1: **John Richard Brady v. Chemical Process Equipments Pvt. Ltd. (Delhi HC, 1987)**

**Facts:** Brady's father, an engineer, left a chemical process manual with his employer. After his death, the employer continued using it.

**Legal Derivation:**
$$
\text{Breach} = (\text{Information has } C_q) \land (\text{No } E_o) \land (\text{Implicit } I_o \text{ from employment context})
$$

**Holding:** The information was confidential despite no written NDA. The employer was restrained from using it. The court established that **implied obligations** can arise from the **context of disclosure**.

### Case 2: **American Express Bank v. Ms. Priya P. Shah (Delhi HC, 2011)**

**Holding:** Customer lists of a bank constitute confidential information. The court held:
$$
\text{Customer List} = \{N_i, C_i, H_i \mid i \in \text{Clients}\}
$$
where $N_i$ = name, $C_i$ = contact, $H_i$ = historical transaction data. The aggregate database, though individual elements may be public, constitutes confidential information due to the **compilation effort**.

### Case 3: **Zee Telefilms v. Sundial Communications (Bombay HC, 2003)**

**Holding:** Business strategies and marketing plans shared during M&A negotiations are protected by implied confidentiality even **without an explicit NDA** because the very nature of the negotiation imports an obligation of confidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The "compilation doctrine" — individually public elements, when assembled through **substantial effort**, create a confidential database. This is critical for IT and e-commerce companies holding customer databases.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — The Life Cycle of Confidential Information

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Information Generation] --> B{Quality Test: Secret + Valuable + Identifiable?}
    B -- No --> Z[Public Domain: No Protection]
    B -- Yes --> C[Classification as Confidential]
    C --> D[Implement Protection Measures]
    D --> D1[NDA / Contract]
    D --> D2[Access Controls]
    D --> D3[Cypher Encryption]
    D --> D4[Need-to-Know Policy]
    D1 --> E[Information in Use]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{Breach Detected?}
    F -- No --> G[Continue Protection]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[Remedy Selection]
    H --> H1[Injunction]
    H --> H2[Damages]
    H --> H3[Account of Profits]
    H --> H4[Criminal Action]
    H1 --> I[Resolution]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
```

## 4.2 Three-Pillar Test Architecture (Coco v. A.N. Clark)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pillar 1: Quality of Confidence] --> P1A[Secrecy Status]
    P1 --> P1B[Commercial Value]
    P1 --> P1C[Reasonable Protection]
    P2[Pillar 2: Obligation of Confidence] --> P2A[Express NDA]
    P2 --> P2B[Implied Trust]
    P2 --> P2C[Constructive Notice]
    P3[Pillar 3: Unauthorized Use] --> P3A[Disclosure]
    P3 --> P3B[Personal Exploitation]
    P3 --> P3C[Springboard Advantage]
    P1 --> R[Actionable Breach]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    R --> RM[Remedies: Injunction / Damages / Account of Profits]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Confidential Information vs. Other IPRs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose IP Form] --> Q1{Requires Registration?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Patent]
    Q1 -- Yes --> A2[Trademark]
    Q1 -- Yes --> A3[Copyright - Auto but Optional]
    Q1 -- No --> B1[Confidential Information / Trade Secret]
    A1 --> P1[Duration: 20 Years]
    A2 --> P2[Duration: 10 Years Renewable]
    A3 --> P3[Duration: Life + 60 Years]
    B1 --> P4[Duration: Indefinite while Secret Maintained]
    P1 --> End[Protection Comparison]
    P2 --> End
    P3 --> End
    P4 --> End
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Confidential Information Management System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph GenerationPhase
        G1[R and D Division] --> G2[Marketing Intelligence]
        G2 --> G3[Customer Data Repository]
    end
    GenerationPhase --> Classification{Classification Engine}
    Classification -- High Sensitivity --> HSDB[High Security Database]
    Classification -- Medium Sensitivity --> MSDB[Standard Encrypted Storage]
    Classification -- Public --> PD[Public Disclosure Channel]
    HSDB --> AccessControl{Access Request}
    MSDB --> AccessControl
    AccessControl -- Approved --> UserAuth[Authenticated User]
    AccessControl -- Denied --> AuditLog[Audit Trail Activated]
    UserAuth --> Monitor[Activity Monitoring]
    Monitor -- Anomaly Detected --> Alert[Breach Alert]
    Alert --> IRT[Incident Response Team]
    IRT --> Legal[Legal Action: Injunction / Damages / Criminal Prosecution]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"Define confidential information. List any three essential elements that distinguish it from public information."**

**Model Answer:**

Confidential information is any non-public data, technical know-how, or commercial knowledge that possesses commercial value and is protected through reasonable measures of secrecy rather than through statutory registration. It is safeguarded under the equitable doctrine of breach of confidence.

**Three Essential Elements:**
1. **Secrecy** — The information must not be part of the public domain.
2. **Commercial Value** — It must have actual or potential economic worth.
3. **Reasonable Protection** — The owner must demonstrate objective steps (NDAs, encryption, restricted access) to maintain confidentiality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Definition = 1 mark, Three elements = 1.5 marks, Brief explanation = 0.5 marks. **Always conclude with one sentence on the legal basis** (Article 39, TRIPS / Indian Contract Act).

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"What is the Springboard Doctrine? Explain with an example."**

**Model Answer:**

The Springboard Doctrine is an equitable principle that prevents a defendant from exploiting confidential information to gain an unfair temporal head-start, even after the information subsequently enters the public domain. The doctrine bars the defendant from using the period of secrecy to leapfrog the competition.

**Example:** An ex-employee leaks a company's product roadmap to a rival. By the time the court hears the case, the product is publicly announced. The rival cannot claim innocence because the leaked roadmap gave them a **2-year development head-start**. The court can grant an injunction for the duration of that unfair advantage.

*Source: Terrapin Ltd. v. Builders' Supply Co. (Hayes) Ltd. (1967)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the three essential ingredients required to establish a successful action for breach of confidence. Cite the leading case.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Distinguish between Confidential Information and Patent Protection. Under what circumstances would a company prefer trade secret protection over patent protection?**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**The Three-Ingredient Test — Coco v. A.N. Clark (Engineers) Ltd. (1969):**

**Ingredient 1: Quality of Confidence**
The information must possess the necessary quality of confidence. This means it must:
- Not be trivial or public information
- Possess a degree of originality and novelty
- Have commercial or industrial value
- Example: A customer database compiled with substantial effort (American Express Bank v. Priya P. Shah, 2011)

**Ingredient 2: Circumstances Importing Obligation of Confidence**
The information must have been communicated in circumstances where the recipient knew or ought to have known that it was confidential. This obligation can be:
- **Express:** Through a written NDA or confidentiality clause
- **Implied:** Through the relationship of the parties (employer-employee, doctor-patient, banker-client)
- **Constructive:** Where the recipient had notice of the confidential nature

**Ingredient 3: Unauthorized Use Causing Damage**
The defendant must have used or disclosed the information without the plaintiff's consent, causing actual or threatened damage. The use need not be malicious — even **innocent misuse** can attract liability.

**Conclusion:** All three ingredients must be satisfied **cumulatively** for a successful action. The burden of proof lies on the plaintiff to establish each element on a **balance of probabilities**.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

| Parameter | Confidential Information | Patent |
|---|---|---|
| **Registration** | Not required | Mandatory |
| **Duration** | Indefinite (as long as secret) | 20 years from filing |
| **Cost** | Low (mainly NDA enforcement) | High (filing, prosecution, renewal) |
| **Disclosure** | Information remains secret | Full public disclosure |
| **Protection Scope** | Confidential relationships only | Statutory monopoly rights |
| **Reverse Engineering** | Permitted (in most jurisdictions) | Prohibited |

**When a Company Prefers Trade Secrets:**
1. **Process Know-How** that is difficult to reverse-engineer (e.g., Coca-Cola formula)
2. **Short Product Lifecycles** where 20-year protection is irrelevant
3. **Cost-Sensitive Industries** where patent filing costs are prohibitive
4. **Continuous Improvement** models where keeping processes secret allows ongoing refinement
5. **Negative Knowledge** — knowing what does NOT work has value but cannot be patented

**Example:** Google's search algorithm is a closely guarded trade secret rather than a patent, as disclosure would allow competitors to clone the technology.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the various remedies available to a plaintiff in an action for breach of confidence. Highlight the relevance of the Anton Piller Order.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the role of NDAs in protecting confidential information. What are the key clauses that must be included in a robust NDA?**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Remedies for Breach of Confidence:**

**1. Injunctions (Equitable Remedy)**
- **Interim Injunction:** Granted during pendency of suit to prevent further disclosure
- **Permanent Injunction:** Granted at the conclusion of trial as a final restraint
- **Springboard Injunction:** Restricts use for the duration of unfair advantage gained

**2. Damages (Common Law Remedy)**
- **Compensatory damages** for actual loss suffered
- **Exemplary damages** in cases of willful, malicious breach
- Calculated based on loss OR unjust enrichment (whichever is higher)

**3. Account of Profits (Equitable Remedy)**
- Defendant must surrender all profits earned through misuse of confidential information
- Particularly useful when actual loss is difficult to quantify

**4. Delivery-Up and Destruction**
- Court orders return or destruction of confidential documents/materials
- Prevents further dissemination

**5. Anton Piller Order (Civil Procedure Code, Order 39)**
- A **pre-emptive search order** allowing the plaintiff to enter the defendant's premises
- Purpose: Prevent destruction of evidence before trial
- Requirements: **Strong prima facie case, real risk of destruction, potential serious damage**
- Executed under court supervision with defendant's solicitor present
- *Anton Piller KG v. Manufacturing Processes Ltd. (1976)*

**6. Mareva Injunction**
- Freezes the defendant's assets to prevent dissipation before judgment

**7. Criminal Prosecution (IT Act, 2000)**
- Section 72: Breach of confidentiality by an intermediary — up to 2 years imprisonment
- Section 72A: Disclosure in breach of contract — up to 3 years imprisonment
- Section 66: Computer-related offences — up to 3 years imprisonment

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Role of NDAs in Confidential Information Protection:**

A **Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)** is a legally binding contract that establishes an express obligation of confidence between parties. It serves as the **first line of defense** in any confidential information regime.

**Key Clauses in a Robust NDA:**

| Clause | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Definition of Confidential Information** | Clearly identifies what is protected (broad enumeration + residual clause) |
| **Obligations of Receiving Party** | Duty to safeguard, restrict access, no copying |
| **Permitted Disclosures** | Exceptions for legal compulsion, employee need-to-know |
| **Term and Duration** | Specifies how long the obligation survives (often 3-5 years post-termination) |
| **Return/Destruction Clause** | Mandates return of materials upon contract termination |
| **Remedies for Breach** | Acknowledgment that breach causes irreparable harm and injunctive relief is appropriate |
| **Jurisdiction and Governing Law** | Specifies court and law applicable (e.g., courts of Kerala, Indian law) |
| **Non-Solicitation Clause** | Prevents poaching of employees/customers (in commercial NDAs) |
| **Liquidated Damages** | Pre-estimated compensation for breach |
| **Survival Clause** | Specifies obligations that survive termination |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "Trade Secret" with "Confidential Information" — Trade Secrets are a *subset* of Confidential Information with commercial value.
> 2. **Always cite Article 39 of TRIPS** when discussing international obligations.
> 3. **Do not write** that confidential information is "registered" — it is NEVER registered.
> 4. **For damages questions**, mention BOTH common law damages AND equitable account of profits.
> 5. **In case law questions**, mention the specific court (e.g., "Delhi High Court in John Richard Brady v. Chemical Process Equipments").
> 6. **For Part B 14-mark questions**, ensure your answer covers BOTH sub-parts with sub-headings — examiners allocate marks per sub-part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Confidential Information**

- **Definition:** Non-public, commercially valuable information protected through secrecy and contractual/equitable obligations
- **Legal Basis:** Common Law (breach of confidence) + Article 39 TRIPS + Indian Contract Act + IT Act 2000
- **Three Pillars (Coco v. A.N. Clark, 1969):** Quality of confidence + Obligation imported + Unauthorized use
- **No Registration Required** — this is the ONLY IPR without statutory registration
- **Duration:** Indefinite, as long as secrecy is maintained
- **Key Doctrines:**
  - **Springboard Doctrine** (Terrapin v. Builders' Supply, 1967)
  - **Compilation Doctrine** (American Express v. Priya P. Shah, 2011)
  - **Implied Confidentiality** (Brady v. Chemical Process Equipments, 1987)
- **Remedies:** Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits, Anton Piller Order, Mareva Injunction, Criminal Prosecution
- **Statutory Protection in India:**
  - Indian Contract Act, 1872 — Sections 27, 73, 74
  - IT Act, 2000 — Sections 43, 43A, 66, 72, 72A
  - Limitation Period: **3 years** (Article 113, Limitation Act 1963)
- **Key Tools:** NDAs, Employment Contracts, Licensing Agreements, Encryption, Access Controls
- **Difference from Patent:** No registration, no public disclosure, no time limit, but narrower scope (only protects against breach of confidence, not independent invention or reverse engineering)
- **Difference from Copyright:** Protects the *expression*, not the underlying confidential idea/information; copyright arises automatically on creation
- **High-Yield Industries:** Pharma, IT/Software, Defence, Food & Beverage, Manufacturing
- **Anton Pillar Order:** Pre-emptive search to prevent evidence destruction (Anton Piller KG v. Manufacturing Processes, 1976)
- **Compensation Framework:** Loss suffered OR unjust enrichment — whichever is higher
- **Common Student Mistake:** Treating "Trade Secret" and "Confidential Information" as synonyms — Trade Secret is a *subset* focused on commercial/industrial secrets

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act 2000
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act 2000

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act, 2000** (hereinafter referred to as the **SICLD Act, 2000**) is a sui generis piece of Indian legislation enacted by the Parliament of India to provide for the **protection of layout-designs (topographies) of integrated circuits**. The Act received the assent of the President of India on **4th September 2000** and came into force on **1st November 2000**.

The Act is administered by the **Geographical Indications Registry** functioning under the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM), Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sui Generis Protection**: SICLD is NOT a patent, NOT a copyright, and NOT a trademark. It is a *separate, stand-alone* IP right (sui generis) that protects the three-dimensional configuration (topography) of the electronic circuitry in an integrated circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Trigger for Enactment**: India, being a signatory to the **Treaty on Intellectual Property in Respect of Integrated Circuits (IPIC Treaty)** adopted in Washington in 1989, was obliged to provide legal protection to semiconductor chip layouts. Failure to enact such a law would have attracted trade sanctions under the Special 301 provisions of the US Trade Act.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have baked a **cake with a unique layered design** — say, alternating layers of chocolate sponge, vanilla cream, and strawberry mousse arranged in a specific geometric pattern. Someone could:

- Eat the cake (the *function* of the cake) — patents protect function.
- Take a photograph of the cake (the *artistic appearance*) — copyright protects artistic expression.
- Call their cake "ChocoTastic" (the *brand name*) — trademark protects brand identity.

But what if a competitor **reverse-engineers the exact three-dimensional layering pattern** of your cake and bakes a structurally identical one, just with different flavors? The cake industry had no specific law for this until someone created one.

The **SICLD Act, 2000** does exactly this for **microchips**. The "layered design" here is the microscopic 3D arrangement of transistors, resistors, and interconnections (the *layout-design* or *topography*) etched onto a semiconductor wafer.

| Domain | Protected Element | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Patent | Invention (function) | The *recipe* of the cake |
| Copyright | Artistic expression | The *photograph* of the cake |
| Trademark | Brand identifier | The *name* "ChocoTastic" |
| **SICLD** | **3D layout/topography** | **The *layered geometric pattern* of the cake** |

---

## 1.3 Key Statutory Definitions (Section 2 of the Act)

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 2(j) — Layout-Design**: Means a layout of transistors and other circuitry elements and the interconnections between such elements, embodied in an integrated circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 2(r) — Semiconductor Integrated Circuit**: Means a product having transistors and other circuitry elements, which are inseparably formed on a semiconductor material or an insulating material, inside or on its surface, so as to form an integral piece of material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 2(c) — Commercial Exploitation**: Includes (i) sale, lease, rental, or otherwise trading of the IC; (ii) offering for the aforesaid purposes; (iii) importing the IC for the aforesaid purposes — but **excluding** any non-commercial, academic, or evaluation use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 2(h) — Integrated Circuit**: Means a product, in its final or intermediate form, in which the elements, at least one of which is an active element, and some or all of the interconnections are integrally formed in or on a piece of material.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Statutory Constants

- **Term of Protection**: **10 years** (from the date of filing the application for registration OR the date of first commercial exploitation anywhere in the world, whichever is earlier).
- **Statutory Fee Range**: As per the Third Schedule of the Act (prescribed by the Rules).
- **International Convention**: Adheres to the **IPIC Treaty, 1989 (Washington Treaty)** and is **TRIPS-Compliant** (Article 35–38 of the TRIPS Agreement).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Salient Features of the SICLD Act, 2000

The Act is structured into **63 Sections** distributed across **9 Chapters**, supplemented by the **Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Rules, 2001**. The core architecture can be summarized as follows:

### A. Chapter-Wise Architecture

| Chapter | Sections | Subject Matter |
|---|---|---|
| I | 1–2 | Preliminary (short title, extent, commencement, definitions) |
| II | 3–6 | Registration of Layout-Designs |
| III | 7–10 | Rights of the Registered Proprietor |
| IV | 11–14 | Assignment & Transmission |
| V | 15–18 | Rectification & Correction of Register |
| VI | 19–20 | **Infringement** of Layout-Design Rights |
| VII | 21–28 | **Civil Proceedings**, Reliefs, and Damages |
| VIII | 29–37 | **Offences, Penalties, & Criminal Proceedings** |
| IX | 38–63 | Miscellaneous (Appellate Board, Registrar's powers, etc.) |

### B. Condition for Registration (Section 7)

A layout-design is registrable if:
1. It is **original** (the result of the creator's own intellectual effort and is not commonplace in the IC industry).
2. It has not been **commercially exploited** anywhere in the world more than **2 years** before the filing date.
3. It is **distinctive** in the sense that on a visual examination of the layout, the overall impression it produces is significantly different from any other registered layout-design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Originality Standard**: Unlike copyright, SICLD does not require a *modicum of creativity*. The standard is that the design resulted from the **intellectual effort of the creator** and is **not commonplace** among creators of layout-designs and manufacturers of semiconductor integrated circuits.

### C. Rights Conferred (Section 7–10)

Upon registration, the registered proprietor (or licensee) has the **exclusive right** to:

- **Section 7**: Use the layout-design commercially.
- **Section 8**: Restrain any third party from reproducing, importing, selling, or otherwise distributing the protected layout-design **without authorization**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Rights are NOT Automatic**: Unlike copyright, the rights under SICLD Act are **not granted automatically upon creation**. Registration with the competent authority is **mandatory** for enforceability.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Statutory Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Statutory Provision | Duration / Quantum | Key Caveat |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Term of Protection | Section 11 | **10 years** | Whichever expires earlier from filing OR first commercial exploitation |
| Filing Deadline post Commercial Exploitation | Section 5(1) | **2 years** | Lapsed if applied after this window |
| Civil Damages (Max) | Section 24 | **₹10,00,000** (Rupees Ten Lakhs) | OR actual damages, whichever is higher |
| Criminal Fine (Imprisonment) | Section 35–37 | Up to **3 years** + Fine up to **₹5,00,000** | Repeat offenders face enhanced penalty |
| Appellate Authority | Section 32 | **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** | Now transferred to **Commercial Courts / High Courts** post abolition of IPAB |
| Non-Commercial Use Exception | Section 10 | Permitted | For teaching, research, private non-commercial evaluation |
| Reverse Engineering Exception | Section 10 | Permitted | Solely for the purpose of **evaluating or analysing** the layout-design |

---

## 2.3 Why Is This Law Engineering-Relevant?

> [!IMPORTANT]
