> **Strategic IP for the Electronics & VLSI Industry**: With India's **Semiconductor Mission (₹76,000 crore outlay)**, the SICLD Act forms a critical pillar of IP strategy for fabless design houses, semiconductor startups, and MSMEs engaged in chip design. The Act incentivizes indigenous chip design innovation without requiring full-fledged patent protection (which has higher thresholds of novelty and non-obviousness).

**Real-world Engineering Application:**
- A fabless semiconductor startup designing an AI accelerator chip obtains SICLD protection to prevent competitors from cloning its physical layout, even if the underlying algorithm/functionality is not patentable or is in the public domain.
- The SICLD protection covers the *layout* (the physical blueprint), not the function. This is critical in the semiconductor industry where functional equivalence is often achieved through different layouts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis of Registration, Term & Infringement

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Analytical Logic: Registration Process

> [!NOTE]
> The following sequential procedure is the standard workflow prescribed under Section 3 to Section 6 of the SICLD Act, 2000, read with the SICLD Rules, 2001.

**Step 1 — Determination of Eligibility**
$$\text{Original Design} \quad \wedge \quad \text{Not Commercially Exploited} \quad \wedge \quad \text{Exploitation within 2 years}$$

A layout-design is eligible for registration if and only if **all three conditions** are satisfied cumulatively. If the design has been commercially exploited more than 2 years before the date of application, the application is **time-barred** and shall be refused.

**Step 2 — Filing of Application (Form L-1)**
The applicant (creator or assignee) files Form L-1 with the Registrar of Layout-Designs (currently functioning under the Geographical Indications Registry, Chennai) along with prescribed fees.

**Step 3 — Supporting Documents**
The application must be accompanied by:
- A **reproduction of the layout-design** (drawing, photograph, or similar visual medium) showing the layers of the IC.
- A brief description of the electronic function it performs.
- An affidavit as to the date of first commercial exploitation (if applicable).
- Power of Attorney (if filed through an agent).

**Step 4 — Examination**
$$\text{Examination} = f(\text{Formal Requirements}, \text{Substantive Originality}, \text{Prior Commercial Use})$$

The Registrar examines whether the application conforms to the formal requirements and whether the design is registrable. There is **no pre-grant opposition** under the Act; only **post-grant rectification** (Section 15).

**Step 5 — Registration & Certificate**
Upon acceptance, the layout-design is entered in the **Register of Layout-Designs** (Section 6), and a Certificate of Registration is issued.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Computation of Term of Protection

Let:
- $D_f$ = Date of filing the application for registration.
- $D_c$ = Date of first commercial exploitation anywhere in the world.
- $T_{term}$ = Effective term of protection (in years).

The statutory formula (under Section 11) is:

$$T_{term} = \min\!\left(\; D_f + 10 \text{ years},\; D_c + 10 \text{ years} \;\right)$$

The protection expires **10 years** from whichever of the two dates ($D_f$ or $D_c$) is **earlier**.

### Worked Example (Numerical Evaluation)

> **Question:** A semiconductor layout-design was first commercially exploited in Japan on **01-01-2018**. The Indian company filed for registration in India on **01-06-2020**. Determine the term of protection and the date of expiry.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

Given:
- $D_c = \text{01-01-2018}$ (date of first commercial exploitation)
- $D_f = \text{01-06-2020}$ (date of filing in India)
- Window check: $(D_f - D_c) = 2.5 \text{ years} > 2 \text{ years}$

**Step 1 — Check eligibility for filing:**
$$\text{Gap} = D_f - D_c = 2 \text{ years } 5 \text{ months} > 2 \text{ years}$$

Since the gap exceeds **2 years**, the application is **time-barred** under Section 5(1) and **shall be refused registration**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Students often assume the 2-year window is counted from the date of filing in India. It is, in fact, counted from the **date of first commercial exploitation ANYWHERE in the world**. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

### Worked Example 2 (Eligible Application)

> **Question:** A layout-design was first commercially exploited on **01-07-2019**. The application was filed on **01-03-2021**. Determine the term.

**Step 1 — Eligibility Check:**
$$\text{Gap} = D_f - D_c = 1 \text{ year } 8 \text{ months} \leq 2 \text{ years} \;\; \therefore \text{ Eligible.}$$

**Step 2 — Determine Earlier Date:**
$$D_{earlier} = \min(D_f, D_c) = D_c = \text{01-07-2019}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Expiry:**
$$D_{expiry} = D_{earlier} + 10 \text{ years} = \text{01-07-2029}$$

The layout-design is protected from 01-07-2019 to 30-06-2029.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Infringement & Penalties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Infringement is defined under Section 19 of the SICLD Act, 2000.**

A person infringes a registered layout-design if, without the license of the registered proprietor, he:

- Reproduces, incorporates, or uses the protected layout-design.
- Imports, sells, or distributes the IC incorporating the protected layout-design for **commercial purposes**.

### Reliefs Available (Section 20–28)

| Relief | Statutory Section | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Injunction | Section 22 | Equitable relief |
| Damages or Account of Profits | Section 24 | Monetary relief (max ₹10 lakh or actuals) |
| Delivery-up of Infringing Articles | Section 23 | Discretionary |
| Order for Destruction | Section 25 | Discretionary |

### Criminal Penalties (Section 35–37)

| Offence | Imprisonment | Fine |
|---|---|---|
| Knowingly using a registered layout-design (false representation) | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹5,00,000** |
| Falsely applying a registered layout-design to goods | Up to **2 years** | Up to **₹2,00,000** |
| Subsequent conviction (repeat offence) | Up to **5 years** | Enhanced fine |
| Counterfeiting a registered layout-design | Up to **3 years** | Up to **₹5,00,000** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cognizable & Non-Bailable**: Offences under Section 35 onwards are cognizable (police can arrest without warrant) but are **not** bailable, and are triable by a Magistrate of First Class.

---

## 3.4 Exceptions to Infringement (Section 10)

The following acts do **NOT** constitute infringement:

1. Reproduction of a protected layout-design for **private, non-commercial purposes** or for the purposes of **teaching or research**.
2. **Reverse engineering**: Reproduction of the layout-design solely for the purpose of evaluating or analysing it (e.g., to understand the design for creating a competing, independently developed layout).
3. Creation of an independent original layout-design that happens to be similar in appearance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Registration Workflow under SICLD Act, 2000

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Creator of Layout-Design] --> B{Did commercial exploitation occur?}
    B -- No --> C[File Form L-1 with Registrar]
    B -- Yes --> D{Gap less than or equal to 2 years?}
    D -- No --> E[Application REFUSED - Time Barred]
    D -- Yes --> C
    C --> F[Examination by Registrar]
    F --> G{Meets all statutory requirements?}
    G -- No --> H[Rejection communicated to applicant]
    G -- Yes --> I[Entry in Register of Layout-Designs]
    I --> J[Certificate of Registration issued]
    J --> K[Term: 10 years from earlier of filing or first commercial exploitation]
    K --> L[Exclusive Rights Conferred under Section 7-10]
```

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Rights & Exceptions Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Rights[Exclusive Rights of Registered Proprietor]
        R1[Reproduce Layout-Design]
        R2[Import IC incorporating the Layout]
        R3[Distribute or Sell the IC]
        R4[Commercially Exploit the Layout]
    end

    subgraph Exceptions[Statutory Exceptions under Section 10]
        E1[Private Non-Commercial Use]
        E2[Teaching and Academic Research]
        E3[Reverse Engineering for Evaluation]
        E4[Independent Original Design]
    end

    subgraph Infringement[Infringement Framework]
        I1[No License obtained]
        I2[Reproduction or Import without consent]
        I3[Commercial Sale or Distribution]
    end

    subgraph Remedies[Available Remedies]
        M1[Injunction]
        M2[Damages or Account of Profits]
        M3[Criminal Prosecution]
    end

    Rights --> Infringement
    Exceptions --> Infringement
    Infringement --> Remedies
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SICLD Protection Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Semiconductor IC Industry] --> B[Layout-Design Creator]
    B --> C[Original Layout-Design Topography]
    C --> D{Choose IP Protection Mechanism}
    D -- Path 1 --> E[Patent Act 1970]
    D -- Path 2 --> F[Copyright Act 1957]
    D -- Path 3 --> G[SICLD Act 2000]
    D -- Path 4 --> H[Trade Marks Act 1999]

    G --> G1[Sui Generis Layout Protection]
    G1 --> G2[3D Topography of Circuits]
    G1 --> G3[Interconnections and Layer Patterns]

    G2 --> H1[10 Years Term]
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I[Commercial Exclusivity in India]
    I --> J[Market Competitiveness Boost]
    J --> K[Indigenization of Chip Design]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Define "Layout-Design" and "Semiconductor Integrated Circuit" under the Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act, 2000.**

**Model Answer:**

**Layout-Design (Section 2(j))**: A layout of transistors and other circuitry elements and the interconnections between such elements, embodied in an integrated circuit. In simpler terms, it is the three-dimensional physical arrangement or *topography* of the electronic components and their wiring as fabricated on a semiconductor chip.

**Semiconductor Integrated Circuit (Section 2(r))**: A product having transistors and other circuitry elements, which are inseparably formed on a semiconductor material (such as silicon) or an insulating material, inside or on its surface, so as to form an integral piece of material. The product must contain at least one **active element** (typically a transistor) and integrally formed interconnections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Differentiator**: A "layout-design" refers to the *intellectual blueprint* (the design itself), while a "semiconductor integrated circuit" refers to the *physical product* embodying that design. The Act protects the former, the embodiment being the subject of infringement actions.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]

**Q: Explain the term of protection granted under the SICLD Act, 2000.**

**Model Answer:**

Under **Section 11** of the SICLD Act, 2000, the term of protection granted to a registered layout-design is **ten (10) years**.

The 10-year term is counted from the date which is **earlier** of the following two dates:

1. The date of **filing the application** for registration of the layout-design in India.
2. The date of **first commercial exploitation** of the layout-design anywhere in the world.

In effect, if a design was commercially exploited on 01-01-2019 and the application was filed on 01-06-2020, the term would expire on 31-12-2028 (computed from 01-01-2019 + 10 years), since that is the earlier date.

The protection **cannot be renewed**; once the 10-year term expires, the layout-design falls into the public domain and may be freely exploited by anyone without infringement liability.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions offer an internal choice between two alternatives. Both alternatives are presented below.**

---

### **Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Q: Discuss in detail the procedure for the registration of a layout-design under the Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act, 2000. What are the conditions for a layout-design to be registrable?**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Conditions of Registrability (7 Marks)

A layout-design is registrable under the SICLD Act, 2000 if and only if it satisfies the following cumulative conditions:

1. **Originality (Section 7)**: The layout-design must be the result of the creator's own intellectual effort. It must not be commonplace among creators of layout-designs and manufacturers of semiconductor integrated circuits. Mere "modicum of creativity" is not the test — the standard is "intellectual effort of the creator" and "not commonplace in the industry."

2. **Novelty in Commercial Exploitation (Section 5)**: The layout-design must not have been commercially exploited anywhere in the world for **more than 2 years** prior to the date of application. If the gap exceeds 2 years, the application is time-barred.

3. **Distinctiveness (Section 7)**: On visual examination, the overall impression produced by the layout-design must be **significantly different** from any other layout-design already registered.

4. **Application by a Qualified Person (Section 3)**: The application must be made by the **creator** of the layout-design or by a person claiming through the creator (assignee or legal representative).

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Registration Procedure (7 Marks)

| Step | Action | Statutory Basis |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Filing of application in **Form L-1** with prescribed fees | Section 3(1) |
| 2 | Submission of **reproduction** of the layout-design (drawings, photographs, or visual media) | Section 4(2) |
| 3 | Furnishing **date and details of first commercial exploitation** (if any) | Section 4(3) |
| 4 | **Examination** by the Registrar for formal and substantive compliance | Section 5(2) |
| 5 | Acceptance and **Entry in the Register of Layout-Designs** | Section 6 |
| 6 | Issuance of **Certificate of Registration** | Section 6(2) |
| 7 | **Publication** in the official journal for public notice | Section 6(3) |

There is **no pre-grant opposition** under the Act; the only corrective mechanism is **post-grant rectification** (Section 15) before the Appellate Board or relevant authority.

---

### **Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Q: What constitutes infringement of a registered layout-design under the SICLD Act, 2000? Discuss the civil and criminal remedies available to the registered proprietor.**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Concept and Acts Constituting Infringement (7 Marks)

Under **Section 19** of the SICLD Act, 2000, infringement occurs when a person, **without the license of the registered proprietor**, performs any of the following acts in relation to a registered layout-design:

- **Reproduces** the layout-design, in whole or in part, by incorporation into an IC.
- **Imports** a semiconductor IC incorporating the registered layout-design for commercial purposes.
- **Sells, distributes, or otherwise trades** the IC or the layout-design for commercial gain.
- **Falsely represents** that a layout-design is registered when it is not.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acts NOT Constituting Infringement (Section 10):**
> - Reproduction for private, non-commercial purposes.
> - Reproduction for teaching or academic research.
> - Reverse engineering solely for evaluation or analysis.
> - Independent creation of an original layout-design that happens to resemble a registered one.

#### Part (b) — Civil and Criminal Remedies (7 Marks)

**Civil Remedies (Sections 20–28):**

1. **Injunction** (Section 22) — Temporary or perpetual restraint order against the infringer.
2. **Damages or Account of Profits** (Section 24) — Monetary compensation, subject to a statutory ceiling of **₹10,00,000** or actual damages, whichever is higher.
3. **Delivery-up of infringing goods** (Section 23) — Court may order surrender of infringing articles.
4. **Order for destruction** (Section 25) — Court may direct destruction of infringing materials.

**Criminal Remedies (Sections 35–37):**

1. Imprisonment up to **3 years** and/or fine up to **₹5,00,000** for knowingly using a registered layout-design without authorization.
2. Subsequent conviction may attract imprisonment up to **5 years** and enhanced fine.
3. Offences are **cognizable and non-bailable**, triable by a Magistrate of First Class.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Examiner Pitfalls (Valuation Warning):**
> 1. Students often confuse SICLD protection with **patent protection**. Remember: SICLD protects the *layout/topography*, not the *function*. Two chips performing the same function can have legally distinct layouts.
> 2. Students forget to mention the **2-year commercial exploitation window** under Section 5(1). This is a high-weightage point.
> 3. Criminal remedies are frequently omitted in answers. Always include both civil AND criminal remedies for full marks.
> 4. The term is calculated from the **earlier of two dates** — this is a frequently tested nuance in KTU exams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU University Examinations:**

- [ ] The SICLD Act, 2000 is a **sui generis** IP law; not a patent, not a copyright, not a trademark.
- [ ] Enacted to comply with the **IPIC Treaty (Washington, 1989)** and is **TRIPS-Compliant** (Article 35–38).
- [ ] **Term of protection = 10 years** from the earlier of: (i) date of filing, OR (ii) date of first commercial exploitation anywhere in the world.
- [ ] **Commercial exploitation window = 2 years** from first commercial exploitation; otherwise time-barred.
- [ ] **Originality test**: result of creator's own intellectual effort AND not commonplace in the IC industry.
- [ ] **No pre-grant opposition**; only post-grant rectification (Section 15).
- [ ] **Infringement** is defined under Section 19; civil remedies under Sections 20–28; criminal remedies under Sections 35–37.
- [ ] **Statutory ceiling** on civil damages: ₹10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakhs) or actuals, whichever is higher.
- [ ] **Criminal penalty**: up to 3 years imprisonment + fine up to ₹5,00,000; repeat offenders up to 5 years.
- [ ] **Statutory exceptions** to infringement: private non-commercial use, teaching, research, reverse engineering for evaluation, and independent original design.
- [ ] **Appellate authority**: Originally IPAB; presently High Courts / Commercial Courts post-IPAB abolition.
- [ ] **Administration**: Geographical Indications Registry, functioning under the CGPDTM, DPIIT, Ministry of Commerce and Industry.
- [ ] **SICLD protects the physical 3D layout**, not the function — a critical distinction from patent law.
- [ ] **Registration is mandatory** for enforceability; unlike copyright, rights are not automatic.
- [ ] **TRIPS Article 36** permits "innocent infringement" defense — the infringer did not know and had no reasonable ground to know of the registration.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act 2000**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

Welcome to the foundational vocabulary sheet for **Module 4** of PESFT757. This module shifts the lens away from the "traditional" IPs (patents, trademarks, copyright) and explores *specialized*, *sui generis* (unique in character) forms of intellectual property protection. Since legal precision is everything in IPR, mastering the **statutory definitions** is the single most important step toward scoring high in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

## 1.1 Trade Secret — The "Silent Weapon" of Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Definition (as adopted internationally under TRIPS Article 39 & the Uniform Trade Secrets Act – UTSA):**
> A **Trade Secret** is any confidential business information, formula, pattern, compilation, programme, device, method, technique, or process, that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use, and which is the subject of reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy.

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **Trade Secret** as the **Coca-Cola Recipe** locked in a vault. It is *not* patented (because patents require public disclosure and expire in **20 years**). The formula's protection comes purely from the company *keeping it a secret*. The day an employee leaks it to a competitor, the legal protection vanishes. Unlike a patent, a trade secret can theoretically last **forever**.

## 1.2 Geographical Indication (GI) — The "Terroir" Stamp

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Definition (Section 2(1)(e) of the Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999):**
> A **Geographical Indication** is an indication which identifies goods as originating in the territory of a country, or a region or locality in that territory, where a given quality, reputation, or other characteristic of the goods is essentially attributable to its geographical origin.

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine buying **"Darjeeling Tea"** or **"Roquefort Cheese"**. The *flavor, aroma, and quality* of these products are intrinsically tied to the *soil, climate, and craftsmanship* of a specific region. A GI is the legal stamp that says: *"Only tea grown in the Darjeeling hills of West Bengal, processed by traditional methods, can use this name."* It protects **regional heritage and collective reputation**, not a single inventor.

## 1.3 Plant Variety — The "Living Invention"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Definition (Section 2(j) of the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Act, 2001 – PPVFR):**
> A **Plant Variety** is a plant grouping within a single botanical taxon of the lowest known rank, which can be defined by the expression of the characteristics resulting from a given genotype or combination of genotypes, distinguished from any other plant grouping by the expression of at least one of the said characteristics, and considered as a unit with regard to its suitability for being propagated unchanged.

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy
A **Plant Variety** is like a "brand" of plant. While humans have always saved seeds, this definition legally recognizes a *distinct, stable, and uniform* variety (e.g., **IR-64 rice** or **Basmati**). It allows the breeder who developed it to earn royalties, while still protecting the indigenous rights of farming communities.

## 1.4 Semiconductor Integrated Circuit Layout Design — The "Photographic Blueprint"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Definition (Section 2(r) of the Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act, 2000):**
> A **Layout-Design** means a layout of transistors and other circuitry elements and interconnections, embodying in three-dimensional features, the materials from which, or in which, the elements and interconnections of a semiconductor integrated circuit are to be formed.

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy
A **Layout-Design** (also called a *topography* or *mask work*) is the **architectural floor plan** of a microchip. Before silicon is etched, engineers draw a 3D map of where each transistor, resistor, and wire goes. This "map" is a creative intellectual effort and is protected for **10 years** to prevent copycat fabrication.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The four IP types in Module 4 are collectively called **"Sui Generis" IPs** — meaning each has its own *dedicated special statute*, separate from the Patents Act, Copyright Act, or Trademarks Act.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

To answer KTU questions on these definitions, students must understand the *elemental tests* (the "checklist" of legal requirements) and the *statutory boundaries* (the limits within which the IP is protected). Below is the consolidated analytical framework.

## 2.1 The Trade Secret Triad

For information to qualify as a Trade Secret, **THREE** conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

1. **Economic Value Criterion:** The information must have *actual or potential* independent economic value because it is secret.
2. **Non-General Knowledge Criterion:** It must not be generally known to, or readily ascertainable by, persons who normally deal with that type of information.
3. **Reasonable Secrecy Measures Criterion:** The owner must have taken *reasonable steps* under the circumstances to keep it secret (e.g., NDAs, locked rooms, password protection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Anchor:** In India, Trade Secrets are protected under the *common law of breach of confidence* and the *contractual law of NDAs* — there is **no dedicated Trade Secret statute** (unlike the US). The KTU syllabus reflects this: protection is **defensive**, not registrative.

## 2.2 The Geographical Indication Triad

A GI must satisfy three characteristics under the Act:

- **Origin Linkage:** The goods must originate from a specific territory.
- **Quality/Reputation Linkage:** A given quality, reputation, or characteristic must be *essentially attributable* to the geographical origin.
- **Distinctiveness:** The indication must not have become generic (i.e., "Basmathi" is protected, but "curry" is not, as it is generic).

> [!NOTE]
> **India's GI Registry:** All GIs in India are registered with the **Geographical Indications Registry in Chennai**, under the Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks. The first GI registered in India was **Darjeeling Tea** (Certificate No. 1, dated 2004-2005).

## 2.3 The Plant Variety — DUS Test

To register a plant variety under the PPVFR Act, it must pass the **DUS Test**:

- **D — Distinctness:** Clearly distinguishable from other existing varieties.
- **U — Uniformity:** Sufficiently uniform in its relevant characteristics (subject to variation expected from propagation).
- **S — Stability:** Its characteristics remain unchanged after repeated propagation.

## 2.4 The Layout-Design — Originality & Novelty

Under the 2000 Act, a layout-design is protected if:

- **Original:** The creation resulted from the intellectual effort of its creator (not copied from another).
- **Distinctive / Non-trivial:** It is not commonplace in the semiconductor industry.
- **Commercial Exploitation:** It has not been commercially exploited anywhere for more than **2 years** before the application date.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The 2024 KTU paper pattern frequently tests the *duration of protection* and the *governing statute* for these definitions. Memorize the table below.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| IP Type | Governing Statute (India) | Term of Protection | Registrable? | KTU Key Buzzword |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Trade Secret | Breach of Confidence (Common Law) + Contract | **Indefinite** (as long as secret is maintained) | **No** | Reasonable Secrecy Measures |
| Geographical Indication | GI of Goods Act, **1999** | **10 years** (renewable) | **Yes** (Chennai Registry) | Terroir / Origin Linkage |
| Plant Variety | PPVFR Act, **2001** | **18 years** (trees & vines), **15 years** (extinct varieties), **15 years** (crops) | **Yes** (PPVFR Authority) | DUS Test |
| Semiconductor IC Layout-Design | SICLD Act, **2000** | **10 years** from date of filing or first commercial exploitation, whichever is earlier | **Yes** | Topography / Mask Work |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the *term of protection* for Plant Varieties. Remember the bifurcation: **18 years** applies only to *trees and vines*, while the *default* term is **15 years** for field crops. Mixing these will cost you 2 marks.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

- **Trade Secrets** underpin proprietary AI/ML model weights, chemical formulations (Coca-Cola, WD-40), and the source code of closed-source software.
- **GIs** empower rural economies: "Banarasi Brocade," "Kanchipuram Silk," and "Alphonso Mango" are all protected GIs, ensuring authenticity and price premium.
- **Plant Varieties** drive agricultural biotechnology — companies like Mahyco and Bayer earn returns on hybrid seed development.
- **Layout-Designs** are the backbone of the VLSI and semiconductor design industry (relevant for KTU B.Tech Electronics & Computer Science students).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Flow

Since Module 4 is conceptual, "derivations" here take the form of **legal eligibility tests**. The following exhaustive analysis breaks down how a KTU examiner expects you to apply the *definitional tests* to a given fact pattern.

## 3.1 The Trade Secret Eligibility Test — Step-by-Step

**Given:** A company claims that a specific formula is its trade secret.

**Step 1 — Identify the Subject Matter**
Determine whether the information falls within the recognized categories: formula, pattern, compilation, programme, device, method, technique, or process.

**Step 2 — Apply the Economic Value Test**

$$ \text{Economic Value} \neq 0 \iff \text{Information is secret} $$

If the information has *no economic value* even when secret, it fails the test.

**Step 3 — Apply the Non-General Knowledge Test**
Check if the information can be obtained by *reverse engineering* (which is generally permitted) or by independent creation. If yes, it cannot be a trade secret against that specific challenger.

**Step 4 — Apply the Reasonable Secrecy Measures Test**
Verify that physical (locks, access controls), legal (NDAs, employment contracts), and digital (encryption) safeguards are in place. In **Kewanee Oil Co. v. Bicron Corp. (1974)**, the US Supreme Court confirmed that the owner must show *affirmative* secrecy measures.

**Step 5 — Conclusion**
If all four steps yield affirmative answers, the information qualifies as a protectable trade secret. If any step fails, the protection is denied for that specific claim.

## 3.2 The Geographical Indication Eligibility Test — Step-by-Step

**Given:** A producer wants to market a product using a regional name.

**Step 1 — Verify Origin Linkage**

$$ \text{Goods} \rightarrow \text{Specific Region} \rightarrow \text{Production/Packaging} $$

The product must be *grown, produced, or processed* in the named territory.

**Step 2 — Verify Quality/Reputation Linkage**
Confirm that the unique characteristics (taste, texture, fragrance, method) are *causally linked* to the geography. Example: **"Feni"** (Goa) gains its quality from cashew apples grown in Goan soil.

**Step 3 — Verify Non-Generic Status**
Check whether the term has become the *common name* of the product. **"Thermos"** was originally a protected trademark but became generic; similarly, a region name that becomes a *generic descriptor* cannot be a GI.

**Step 4 — Verify Collective Ownership**
A GI is *community property*. Any authorized producer from the region can use it (after registration), not a single private individual.

**Step 5 — Conclusion**
Registration with the **GI Registry (Chennai)** is required to receive legal protection under Sections 20–28 of the 1999 Act.

## 3.3 The Plant Variety DUS Test — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Distinctness**
Compare the candidate variety against the *reference collection*. A single distinguishing characteristic is sufficient.

**Step 2 — Uniformity**
Evaluate the phenotypic expression across the population. The variation must fall within a *predetermined tolerance level*.

**Step 3 — Stability**
Confirm that the variety retains its traits across successive generations of propagation.

**Step 4 — Conclusion**
Only varieties passing all three tests qualify for protection. **Farmer's varieties** (those traditionally cultivated by farming communities) are also eligible under Section 16 of the PPVFR Act, distinguishing India from the UPOV Convention.

## 3.4 The Layout-Design Eligibility Test — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Originality**
Confirm the design was independently created by the author and not copied from another source.

**Step 2 — Distinctiveness (Non-trivial)**
Verify the design involves an *innovative spatial arrangement* not commonplace in the industry.

**Step 3 — Commercial Exploitation Window**
Check whether the design was commercially exploited *more than 2 years* before filing. If yes, it has fallen into the public domain.

**Step 4 — Conclusion**
The Registrar of Semiconductor IC Layout-Designs (under the 2000 Act) issues registration, conferring exclusive rights for **10 years**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** When a question presents a *hypothetical scenario* (e.g., "X claims Y's formula is a trade secret"), structure your answer by *walking through the test steps* in the same format as above. Examiners award marks for the analytical framework, not just the conclusion.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams visualize the **interrelationships and decision flows** of the four IP definitions covered in Module 4.

## 4.1 Master Conceptual Map of Module 4 IPs

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Module 4 Sui Generis IPs] --> B[Trade Secret]
    A --> C[Geographical Indication]
    A --> D[Plant Variety]
    A --> E[Semiconductor IC Layout-Design]

    B --> B1[Common Law Protection]
    B --> B2[No Registration Required]
    B --> B3[Indefinite Term]

    C --> C1[GI Act 1999]
    C --> C2[Chennai Registry]
    C --> C3[10 Years Renewable]

    D --> D1[PPVFR Act 2001]
    D --> D2[DUS Test Required]
    D --> D3[15 to 18 Years]

    E --> E1[SICLD Act 2000]
    E --> E2[Originality Test]
    E --> E3[10 Years Term]
```

## 4.2 Trade Secret Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Information Disputed]) --> Q1{Is it a formula, process, or method?}
    Q1 -- No --> end1[Not a Trade Secret]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Has economic value because it is secret?}
    Q2 -- No --> end1
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Generally known or easily reverse-engineered?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> end2[Not a Trade Secret]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Reasonable secrecy measures in place?}
    Q4 -- No --> end3[Trade Secret Claim Weak]
    Q4 -- Yes --> success[Valid Trade Secret]
```

## 4.3 Plant Variety DUS Test Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Candidate Variety] --> B{Distinct from known varieties?}
    B -- No --> R1[Rejected]
    B -- Yes --> C{Uniform in traits?}
    C -- No --> R1
    C -- Yes --> D{Stable across generations?}
    D -- No --> R1
    D -- Yes --> S[Registered with PPVFR Authority]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: GI Protection Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Origin_Region[Geographical Territory]
        GP[Goods Producer] --> QC[Quality Check]
        QC --> CO[Collective Mark of Origin]
    end
    CO --> AP[Application to GI Registry Chennai]
    AP --> EX[Examination by Registrar]
    EX --> RJ{Substantive Compliance}
    RJ -- No --> RJ1[Opposition / Rejection]
    RJ -- Yes --> REG[Registration Certificate]
    REG --> PR[Protection for 10 Years]
    PR --> RN[Renewable Indefinitely]
    RN --> EN[Enforcement against Infringers]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** For KTU 2024 ESE questions asking *"Differentiate between X and Y"*, use the architectural flowcharts above as a base to build a *side-by-side comparison table* in your answer. Examiners reward visual reasoning when supported by legal text.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

The following question bank is calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for PESFT757: **Part A** (3 marks, 2 questions) and **Part B** (14 marks, with module-internal choice). Each question is tagged with a simulated KTU past-year style indicator.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Trade Secret" as recognized under Article 39 of the TRIPS Agreement. What three conditions must be satisfied for information to qualify as a trade secret?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Trade Secret** is confidential business information — including a formula, pattern, compilation, programme, device, method, technique, or process — that has independent economic value from not being generally known, and is subject to reasonable secrecy efforts. Under **TRIPS Article 39**, three conditions must be satisfied:

1. The information must be **secret** in the sense that it is not generally known or readily accessible to persons within the circles that normally deal with that kind of information.
2. It must have **commercial value** precisely because it is a secret.
3. The lawful holder must have taken **reasonable steps** to keep it confidential.

*(Allocation: 1.5 marks for the definition; 1.5 marks for the three conditions.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is a "Geographical Indication"? Explain the concept of "Terroir" in the context of GI protection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Geographical Indication (GI)** is an indication identifying goods as originating from a specific territory, where a given quality, reputation, or characteristic is essentially attributable to that geographical origin. It is governed by the **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999** in India. (2 marks)

**Terroir** is a French term used to describe the *set of environmental and human factors* — including soil, climate, topography, and traditional know-how — that together give an agricultural product its distinctive character. For example, the unique flavor of **"Darjeeling Tea"** is the result of the specific terroir of the Darjeeling hills, which cannot be replicated elsewhere. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Define a "Plant Variety" under the **PPVFR Act, 2001**. Explain the **DUS Test** with all its components. Discuss how the Indian Act differs from the UPOV Convention in protecting farmers' rights. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define "Layout-Design" under the **Semiconductor IC Layout-Design Act, 2000**. What are the conditions for its registrability, and what is the term of protection? Explain with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** A **Plant Variety**, under Section 2(j) of the **PPVFR Act, 2001**, is a plant grouping within a single botanical taxon of the lowest known rank, which can be defined by the expression of its characteristics, is distinguishable from other groupings, and is considered a unit suitable for being propagated unchanged.

**DUS Test (3 Marks):** The **DUS Test** is the prerequisite for registration:
- **Distinctness (D):** The variety must be clearly distinguishable from any other commonly known variety by at least one characteristic.
- **Uniformity (U):** The variety must be sufficiently uniform in its relevant characteristics, subject to the variation expected from its mode of reproduction.
- **Stability (S):** The variety's essential characteristics must remain unchanged after repeated propagation.

**Indian Act vs. UPOV (2 Marks):** The **PPVFR Act, 2001** incorporates provisions for **Farmers' Rights** under Sections 39–44, which are absent in the UPOV Convention. Indian farmers are recognized as co-developers of varieties, entitled to save, use, exchange, and sell farm-saved seed (excluding branded varieties). This aligns with India's commitment to protecting agricultural biodiversity and farmers' livelihoods.

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Under Section 2(r) of the **Semiconductor IC Layout-Design Act, 2000**, a **Layout-Design** refers to a layout of transistors and other circuitry elements and interconnections, embodying in three-dimensional features the materials from which (or in which) the elements of a semiconductor IC are formed. It is often called a *topography* or *mask work*.

**Conditions of Registrability (3 Marks):**
1. **Originality:** The design must be the result of the intellectual effort of its creator and not be commonplace in the semiconductor industry.
2. **Distinctiveness:** It must not be a standard, routine, or trivial arrangement known to chip designers.
3. **Time Window:** It must not have been commercially exploited anywhere in the world for **more than 2 years** before the application date.

**Term of Protection (1 Mark):** The registration is valid for **10 years** from the date of filing the application or the date of first commercial exploitation, whichever is earlier.

**Example (1 Mark):** A custom layout for a low-power ARM Cortex processor die, where the original arrangement of transistor cells in the memory subsystem is a creative, distinctive effort, would qualify for protection.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** "A Trade Secret is not registrable but is protectable under law." Critically analyze this statement with reference to the **TRIPS Agreement** and the **Indian legal position**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **origin, objectives, and salient features** of the **Geographical Indications of Goods Act, 1999**. Differentiate between a GI and a **Trademark** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**The Statement (1 Mark):** The statement highlights the fundamental difference between registrable IPs (like patents and trademarks) and the *unregistered* nature of trade secret protection.

**TRIPS Position (2 Marks):** Article 39 of the **TRIPS Agreement** mandates member states to protect undisclosed information against unauthorized disclosure, acquisition, or use by third parties who knew or were grossly negligent in failing to know that the information was obtained unfairly. However, TRIPS does *not* prescribe a registration system; it focuses on *anti-unfair-competition* and *contractual* remedies.

**Indian Legal Position (3 Marks):** India has **no dedicated statute** for trade secrets. Protection is provided through:
- **Common Law of Confidence:** The principles established in cases like *John Richard Brady v. Chemical Process Equipments P. Ltd. (1987)* and *Burger King Corporation v. H. & D. Enterprises* govern breach-of-confidence claims.
- **Contractual Mechanisms:** Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) and Employment Contracts with non-compete clauses.
- **Section 405 of the Indian Penal Code** (now Section 316 Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita) on criminal breach of trust.
- The **Information Technology Act, 2000** also addresses unauthorized access and data theft.

**Critical Analysis (1 Mark):** The unregistered nature provides flexibility and indefinite protection, but lacks the *ex-ante certainty* that registration offers. In a globalized IP landscape, India is often criticized for not having a dedicated Trade Secret Act.

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Origin and Objectives (2 Marks):** The **GI of Goods Act, 1999** was enacted to comply with India's obligations under the **TRIPS Agreement (Articles 22–24)**, which required member states to provide legal means to protect GIs. India, a WTO member since 1995, was under obligation to provide GI protection, leading to the 1999 Act.

**Salient Features (3 Marks):**
1. **Registration System:** GIs must be registered with the **GI Registry in Chennai**.
2. **Term:** Protection is granted for **10 years**, renewable indefinitely.
3. **Collective Ownership:** A GI belongs to the *community of producers* in the region, not to a single individual.
4. **Prohibition of Generic Terms:** Names that have become the common name of a product cannot be registered.
5. **Infringement Penalties:** Sections 38–47 prescribe imprisonment (up to 3 years) and fines (up to ₹2 lakh) for false GI use.

**GI vs. Trademark — Differences (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Geographical Indication | Trademark |
|---|---|---|
| Identifies | Origin of goods | Source/manufacturer of goods |
| Duration | 10 years (renewable) | 10 years (renewable indefinitely) |
| Ownership | Community of producers | Individual/Entity |
| Example | **Darjeeling Tea** | **Nike** logo |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas for Module 4 Definitions:**
> 1. **Statute Mix-up:** Writing "Trade Secrets Act" instead of "common law of confidence" for India. Always distinguish Indian vs. International positions.
> 2. **Term Confusion:** Writing "20 years" for Plant Varieties — the correct default is **15 years** (not the patent term).
> 3. **GI vs. Trademark Blur:** GIs protect *terroir-based* collective rights; Trademarks protect *commercial source* rights. Do not equate them.
> 4. **DUS Test Forgetting:** Always expand the abbreviation and explain all three parameters (Distinctness, Uniformity, Stability) in a 7-mark question.
> 5. **Layout-Design 2-Year Window:** Forget this, and the examiner will mark you down. The *2-year commercial exploitation cutoff* is a frequently-tested high-weight point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Trade Secret:** Confidential business information with economic value, protected by reasonable secrecy measures. No registration. Common law + NDAs in India. **Indefinite** term.
- **Geographical Indication:** Indication linking goods' quality/reputation to a specific geographical origin. **GI Act, 1999**. Registered in **Chennai**. **10 years**, renewable. Collective right. Example: **Darjeeling Tea**.
- **Plant Variety:** A distinct, uniform, and stable plant grouping. **PPVFR Act, 2001**. **DUS Test**. **15 years** (crops), **18 years** (trees/vines). Includes **Farmers' Rights** (India-specific feature).
- **Semiconductor IC Layout-Design:** 3D layout of transistors in a chip. **SICLD Act, 2000**. Must be original and distinctive. **10 years** term. 2-year commercial exploitation cutoff.
- **Common Thread:** All four are **Sui Generis** IPs — each governed by a dedicated statute (except Trade Secret, which is common law) in India.
- **Practical KTU Tip:** In every 7-mark answer, structure the response as **Definition → Statutory Provision → Tests/Conditions → Duration → Example**. This framework covers 90% of marking expectations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Provisions of Registration of Certain Layout Designs
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Provisions of Registration of Certain Layout Designs

# 🧩 Provisions of Registration of Certain Layout Designs

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layout Design (Topography)** of an Integrated Circuit refers to a **three-dimensional disposition**, however expressed, of the elements of an integrated circuit, with at least one element being an active element, and some or all of the interconnections, or a **three-dimensional disposition** prepared for an integrated circuit intended for manufacture.

In India, the rights relating to Layout Designs of Integrated Circuits are governed by the **Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act, 2000** (hereafter referred to as the **SICLD Act, 2000**). This Act is aligned with **Article 35 to 38 of the TRIPS Agreement** (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights) administered by the WTO.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you have just designed a beautiful **floor plan (layout)** of a **smart house** (the integrated circuit). The house itself is the IC chip. Your floor plan shows where every room, door, window, and wiring goes — this is the **layout design**. 

Now, suppose another builder copies your exact floor plan, builds the same smart house, and sells it cheaper. You would be upset because the **creative intellectual effort** in designing the arrangement of rooms and wiring was yours, even though you don't own the materials or the actual house.

A **Layout Design** in IPR is exactly this: the **architectural blueprint** of a chip. The SICLD Act, 2000 protects the **original creator** of this blueprint for **10 years** from the date of filing or first commercial exploitation anywhere in the world (whichever is earlier).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The Layout Design right protects the **3D arrangement** of elements, **NOT the underlying circuit idea or function**. It is a *sui generis* (unique) right — neither purely copyright nor purely patent.

### 🏛️ Statutory Framework at a Glance

| Statute / Treaty | Relevant Provision | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **SICLD Act, 2000** (India) | Sections 3–17 | Domestic registration & rights |
| **TRIPS Agreement** (WTO) | **Articles 35–38** | International minimum standards |
| **IPIC Treaty** (Washington, 1989) | Intellectual Property in ICs | Treaty basis (not ratified by India) |
| **Rome Convention, 1961** | Rome Treaty on ICs | Subregional African treaty |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner frequently tests whether students know that the SICLD Act, 2000 is India's compliance vehicle for **TRIPS Article 35–38**. Remember: *No IPR module is complete without TRIPS linkage!*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 🔍 Conditions for Registration (Sections 5–7, SICLD Act 2000)

A layout design is registrable in India if **BOTH** the following conditions are satisfied:

1. **Originality Test:** The layout design must be **original** in the sense that it is the result of the creator's *own intellectual effort* and is **not commonplace** in the semiconductor industry.
   - "Not commonplace" means the design is not a standard, routine arrangement that any ordinary chip designer would produce.
   - A layout that combines commonplace elements in an *unusual* way (creating a non-trivial functional improvement) can still qualify.

2. **Commercial Exploitation Test:** The layout design must have been **first commercially exploited** somewhere in the world (filed application must be made within **2 years** of first commercial exploitation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 7(2):** A layout design that has been commercially exploited for more than **two years** prior to the application date is **NOT registrable**. This is a strict cutoff — KTU loves testing this.

### 📋 Step-by-Step Logic for Registration

**Step 1 — Identify the Creator**
- The **creator** is the natural person who devised the layout design through intellectual effort.
- If created in the course of employment → employer is the **first owner** (Section 8).
- If created under a contract for service → the **commissioning party** owns it (Section 9).

**Step 2 — Determine the Filing Window**
- Application must be filed **within 2 years** of first commercial exploitation anywhere in the world.
- If not commercially exploited before → application can be filed any time before exploitation.

**Step 3 — File the Application**
- Application is filed with the **Registrar of Layout Designs** appointed by the **Central Government** under Section 3 of the SICLD Act.
- Documents required:
  1. Application in prescribed form
  2. Layout design drawings / graphical representations
  3. Statement of novelty (creative effort)
  4. Date of first commercial exploitation (if any)
  5. Power of attorney (if filed through an agent)

**Step 4 — Examination and Registration**
- Registrar examines for compliance with Sections 5–7.
- If accepted → Register of Layout Designs is maintained.
- Registration certificate issued to the applicant.

**Step 5 — Publication**
- Details entered in the register and made available for public inspection.

### 📊 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Mark-Scoring Facts)

| Parameter | Statutory Value / Rule | Section / Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Governing Statute (India)** | SICLD Act, **2000** | India |
| **International Compliance** | **TRIPS Articles 35–38** | WTO |
| **Originality Requirement** | Own intellectual effort + Not commonplace | Section 5 |
| **First Owner (Employment)** | Employer | Section 8 |
| **First Owner (Commission)** | Commissioning party | Section 9 |
| **Registration Window** | Within **2 years** of first commercial exploitation | Section 7(2) |
| **Term of Protection** | **10 years** from filing date or first commercial exploitation (earlier) | Section 19 |
| **Rights Conferred** | Exclusive right to use, sell, import, distribute | Section 17 |
| **Infringement** | Copying or commercially exploiting without consent | Section 18 |
| **Exceptions to Rights** | Reverse engineering for teaching/analysis; innocent infringement | Sections 20–21 |

### 🛠️ Real-World Engineering Utility

The layout design regime protects **fabless semiconductor companies, VLSI design houses, and chip architects** in India. With India launching the **India Semiconductor Mission (ISM)** in 2021 with ₹76,000 crore outlay, the SICLD Act has become a critical IP tool for:

- **Startups** designing AI accelerator chips
- **DRDO and ISRO** for indigenous defense/space ICs
- **Automotive chip designers** for EVs
- **IoT and embedded systems** developers

> [!TIP]
> The **2-year window** is unique to layout designs. Patents have a 12-month grace period, but layout designs allow a longer catch-up time because the design is often created in secrecy before being commercialized.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 📜 Detailed Application-to-Registration Process (Exhaustive)

Below is the **complete procedural derivation** of how a layout design becomes a registered right in India. Every step is mandatory for KTU's long-answer questions (14-mark type).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{STEP 1: Identify Creator} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Natural person who devised the layout through own effort} \\[6pt]
&\text{STEP 2: Determine Ownership} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{If employment: Employer (Sec 8)} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{If commission: Commissioning party (Sec 9)} \\[6pt]
&\text{STEP 3: Verify Registrability} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Check originality + 2-year window (Sec 5, 7)} \\[6pt]
&\text{STEP 4: File Application} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Submit to Registrar of Layout Designs (Sec 11)} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Pay prescribed fees} \\[6pt]
&\text{STEP 5: Examination} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Registrar verifies formality + substantive originality} \\[6pt]
&\text{STEP 6: Registration} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Entry in Register of Layout Designs (Sec 12)} \\[6pt]
&\text{STEP 7: Rights Crystallize} \\
&\quad \rightarrow \text{Exclusive rights for 10 years (Sec 17, 19)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 🧮 Analytical Matrix — "What If" Scenarios (KTU-Favourite)

| Scenario | Legal Outcome | Statutory Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Layout commercially exploited **1 year ago**, application filed today | ✅ **Registrable** | Within 2-year window (Sec 7) |
| Layout commercially exploited **3 years ago**, application filed today | ❌ **Not registrable** | Window expired (Sec 7(2)) |
| Employee creates layout during job | **Employer is owner** | Section 8 |
| Freelancer creates layout under contract | **Commissioning party is owner** | Section 9 |
| Competitor copies layout for identical product | **Infringement** — civil + criminal remedy | Section 18 |
| Competitor reverse-engineers for **teaching or research** | ✅ **Permitted** | Section 20 (exception) |
| Innocent infringer (no knowledge of registration) | Limited relief — injunction + damages, not account of profits | Section 21 |

### 🔢 Calculation of Term of Protection

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term of Protection} &= \min(\text{T}_1, \text{T}_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\text{T}_1$ = **10 years from the date of filing** of the application for registration
- $\text{T}_2$ = **10 years from the date of first commercial exploitation** anywhere in the world

The term is the **earlier** of these two dates. This ensures that the creator does not game the system by delaying the application.

**Worked Example:**
Suppose a layout was first commercially exploited on **1st January 2020**, and the application was filed on **1st January 2023**.
- T1 (from filing) = 10 years → expires on **1st January 2033**
- T2 (from first commercial use) = 10 years → expires on **1st January 2030**
- The protection term ends on **1st January 2030** (earlier date).

> [!NOTE]
> If a design was commercially exploited on 1st Jan 2020 and application was filed on 1st Jan 2024, the application itself is **NOT registrable** because the 2-year window has already expired (Sec 7(2)).

### 📂 Statutory Rights Conferred (Section 17)

The registered proprietor has the **exclusive right** to:

1. **Reproduce** the layout design
2. **Incorporate** it in a semiconductor integrated circuit
3. **Import, sell, or otherwise distribute** for commercial purposes the IC incorporating the design

### ⚖️ Remedies for Infringement (Section 18)

Infringement is treated as a **serious civil wrong** (and not a criminal offence under the SICLD Act, unlike the Copyright Act). Remedies include:

1. **Injunction** (temporary or permanent)
2. **Damages** (compensatory)
3. **Account of profits** (where applicable)
4. **Delivery up** of infringing goods

### 🚫 Defences / Exceptions (Sections 20 & 21)

1. **Reverse engineering for analysis/education** is permitted (provided the resulting layout is itself original).
2. **Innocent infringement** — infringer who proves they had no reason to believe registration existed gets limited relief.
3. Acts done privately and for non-commercial purposes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔄 Mermaid Block Diagram — Registration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Creator Devises Original Layout Design"] --> B{"Employment or Contract?"}
    B -- "Under Employment" --> C["Employer is First Owner - Sec 8"]
    B -- "Under Contract" --> D["Commissioning Party is First Owner - Sec 9"]
    B -- "Independent Creation" --> E["Creator is First Owner"]
    C --> F{"First Commercial Exploitation?"}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -- "Not Yet Exploited" --> G["File Application Anytime - Sec 11"]
    F -- "Already Exploited" --> H{"Within 2 Years?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> G
    H -- "No" --> X["Application Rejected - Sec 7 2"]
    G --> I["Registrar Examines Originality - Sec 5"]
    I --> J{"Meets Originality Test?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Entry in Register of Layout Designs - Sec 12"]
    J -- "No" --> Y["Refused Registration"]
    K --> L["Exclusive Rights for 10 Years - Sec 17 and Sec 19"]
    L --> M{"Any Infringement?"}
    M -- "Yes" --> N["Civil Remedies - Sec 18 - Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits"]
    M -- "No" --> O["Continued Protection Until Expiry"]
    O --> P["Design Enters Public Domain After 10 Years"]
```

### 🏗️ Mermaid — Statutory Rights & Exceptions Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Registered Layout Design"] --> B["Exclusive Rights - Sec 17"]
    B --> B1["Reproduce"]
    B --> B2["Incorporate in IC"]
    B --> B3["Import for Sale"]
    B --> B4["Distribute Commercially"]
    A --> C["Defences - Sec 20 and 21"]
    C --> C1["Reverse Engineering for Education"]
    C --> C2["Innocent Infringement Protection"]
    C --> C3["Private Non Commercial Use"]
    A --> D["Remedies on Infringement - Sec 18"]
    D --> D1["Injunction"]
    D --> D2["Damages"]
    D --> D3["Account of Profits"]
    D --> D4["Delivery Up of Infringing Goods"]
    A --> E["Term - Sec 19"]
    E --> E1["10 Years from Filing"]
    E --> E2["10 Years from First Commercial Use"]
    E --> E3["Whichever is Earlier"]
```

### 🗂️ Block Architecture — Who Owns What

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Who Creates the Layout?"] --> R1["Employee in Course of Employment"]
    Q --> R2["Freelancer Under Contract for Service"]
    Q --> R3["Independent Designer"]
    R1 --> S1["Employer - Sec 8"]
    R2 --> S2["Person Who Commissioned the Work - Sec 9"]
    R3 --> S3["Creator Themselves - Sec 7 1"]
    S1 --> T["Right to Apply for Registration"]
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
    T --> U["Apply to Registrar of Layout Designs - Sec 11"]
    U --> V["Statutory Protection for 10 Years"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define "Layout Design" under the Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act, 2000. Mention the statutory term of protection.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Under Section 2(d) of the SICLD Act, 2000, **layout design** means a three-dimensional disposition, however expressed, of the elements of an integrated circuit, with at least one element being an active element, and some or all of the interconnections, or such three-dimensional disposition prepared for an integrated circuit intended for manufacture.
>
> The **term of protection** is **10 years** from the date of filing the application or the date of first commercial exploitation anywhere in the world, **whichever is earlier** (Section 19). *(1 + 2 = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. What is the role of TRIPS Articles 35–38 in protecting layout designs?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **TRIPS Agreement** (administered by the WTO) mandates all member countries, including India, to provide **minimum standards of protection** for layout designs of integrated circuits. Specifically:
>
> - **Article 35** — Requires protection of layout designs that are **original** in the sense of being the result of the creator's own intellectual effort and not commonplace.
> - **Article 36** — Defines the **scope of the right** (exclusive right to incorporate, import, sell).
> - **Article 37** — Provides a **term of at least 10 years**.
> - **Article 38** — Permits member states to allow **reverse engineering, innocent infringement, and compulsory licensing** as exceptions.
>
> India's **SICLD Act, 2000** is the domestic implementation of these TRIPS obligations. *(1 + 2 = 3 marks)*

---

### 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### 🔵 **Question A (14 Marks)** — Registration Procedure

> [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **statutory conditions for registration** of a layout design under the SICLD Act, 2000. Discuss the **originality test** and the **two-year commercial exploitation window**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **rights of the registered proprietor** and the **remedies available for infringement** under the SICLD Act, 2000. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(a) — Statutory Conditions for Registration (7 marks)

**1. Originality Test — Section 5**

A layout design is registrable only if it is the result of the **creator's own intellectual effort** and is **not commonplace** among creators of layout designs and manufacturers of semiconductor integrated circuits (Section 5).

- *Own intellectual effort* — the creator must have exercised independent mental skill.
- *Not commonplace* — the arrangement should not be a standard, routine configuration in the industry.
- **KTU highlight:** A design may consist of commonplace elements but qualify if their **combination** is itself original (e.g., innovative arrangement of standard cells in VLSI design).

**2. First Commercial Exploitation Window — Section 7(2)**

- A layout design is registrable only if an application is filed with the Registrar **within two years** of the date on which it was first **commercially exploited** anywhere in the world.
- If not yet commercially exploited → application may be filed any time before such exploitation.
- **Failure to file within 2 years → application is barred** and the design falls into the public domain.

**3. Procedural Compliance — Section 11**

The application must be in the **prescribed form** accompanied by:
- A graphical representation of the layout design
- A statement of the date of first commercial exploitation (if applicable)
- A power of attorney (if filed through agent)
- The prescribed fee

The Registrar, on satisfaction, enters the design in the **Register of Layout Designs** (Section 12).

*(Allocation: Originality 3 marks + 2-year rule 2 marks + Procedure 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(b) — Rights & Remedies for Infringement (7 marks)

**1. Rights Conferred — Section 17**

The registered proprietor enjoys the **exclusive right** to:
- **Reproduce** the registered layout design
- **Incorporate** it in a semiconductor integrated circuit
- **Import** such IC for commercial purposes
- **Sell, distribute, or otherwise commercialize** the IC containing the design

**2. Infringement — Section 18**

Infringement occurs when any person, without the proprietor's consent:
- Reproduces, incorporates, imports, sells, or distributes the registered layout design
- This is actionable as a **civil wrong** (no criminal liability under the Act).

**3. Remedies Available**

The aggrieved proprietor can seek the following reliefs from the District Court:
- **Injunction** (temporary or perpetual) restraining further infringement
- **Damages** (compensatory) for loss suffered
- **Account of profits** earned by the infringer
- **Order for delivery up** of infringing goods, plates, or instruments

**4. Defences / Exceptions — Sections 20 & 21**

- **Reverse engineering** for analysis, teaching, or research (provided the resulting design is itself original)
- **Innocent infringement** (no knowledge of registration) — limited relief
- Acts done privately and for non-commercial purposes

*(Allocation: Rights 2 marks + Infringement definition 1.5 marks + Remedies 2.5 marks + Defences 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

### 🟢 **Question B (14 Marks)** — Ownership & Term of Protection

> [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss the **rules of ownership** of a layout design under the SICLD Act, 2000. Compare ownership in cases of **employment, contract, and independent creation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** "The term of protection for a layout design is **10 years**, but the computation depends on two statutory anchors." Critically analyse this statement with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(a) — Ownership Rules (7 marks)

**1. General Rule — Section 7(1)**

The **creator** of a layout design is the **first owner** of any registrable layout design, except as otherwise provided.

**2. Ownership in Employment — Section 8**

Where a layout design is created by a person in the **course of employment** under a contract of service, the **employer** is the first owner. The employee/creator holds no proprietary right unless there is an express written agreement to the contrary.

> *Example:* A VLSI engineer employed at Intel India creates a novel layout design for a new processor — **Intel India is the first owner**.

**3. Ownership under Contract for Service — Section 9**

Where a layout design is created pursuant to a **commission** or contract for service, the **person who commissioned the work** (i.e., the client) is the first owner. The freelancer retains no rights.

> *Example:* A startup contracts a freelance chip designer to create a layout for ₹5 lakh — the **startup is the first owner**.

**4. Independent Creation — Section 7(1)**

A person who creates a layout design on their own, outside any employment or commission, is the **first owner** and has the absolute right to apply for registration.

**5. Comparison Table (KTU-Favourite):**

| Mode of Creation | First Owner | Section |
|---|---|---|
| Employment | Employer | 8 |
| Contract for service | Commissioning party | 9 |
| Independent | Creator | 7(1) |
| Joint creators | Jointly as tenants-in-common | 7(3) |

*(Allocation: General rule 1 + Employment 2 + Contract 2 + Independent 1 + Comparison 1 = 7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(b) — Term of Protection Analysis (7 marks)

**1. Statutory Provision — Section 19**

The **term of protection** for a registered layout design is **10 years** from the **earlier** of:
- (i) The **date of filing** of the application for registration; or
- (ii) The **date of first commercial exploitation** anywhere in the world.

**2. Critical Analysis**

The wording "earlier" is significant. It prevents the proprietor from indefinitely delaying the application and still claiming the full 10-year term. The protection is anchored to the **earlier commercial or filing event**, ensuring public benefit from early disclosure.

**3. Worked Examples**

**Example 1:** Layout first commercialised on 1st January 2020. Application filed on 1st January 2021.
- Term from filing = 1 Jan 2021 + 10 years = 1 Jan 2031
- Term from first commercial use = 1 Jan 2020 + 10 years = 1 Jan 2030
- **Protection ends on 1 Jan 2030** (earlier date). *(1 mark)*

**Example 2:** Layout created in 2018, never commercialised, application filed on 1st June 2022.
- Term from filing = 1 June 2032
- Term from first commercial use — not applicable (no commercial exploitation yet)
- **Protection ends on 1 June 2032.** *(1 mark)*

**4. Distinction from Patent Term**

Unlike **patents (20 years)**, layout design protection is **short (10 years)** because the technology lifecycle in semiconductors is rapid, and designs become obsolete quickly. Also unlike copyright (60 years), layout design is short because the work is functional, not artistic.

**5. Public Domain**

After expiry, the layout design enters the **public domain** and can be freely used, reproduced, and commercialised by any person without licence.

**6. Key Concession**

If a layout design is registered but not exploited within 3 years of registration, the **Central Government may compulsorily license** it (under Section 21) in the public interest — analogous to patent working requirements.

*(Allocation: Statutory rule 1.5 + Critical analysis 1 + Examples 2 + Comparison 1.5 + Public domain 0.5 + Compulsory license 0.5 = 7 marks)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Lossing Pitfalls (Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Conflating the 2-year registration window with the 10-year term.** Students often write that the term is "10 years from first commercial use" without mentioning the *earlier of two dates* rule. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing the SICLD Act with the Patents Act.** The SICLD Act protects the **3D arrangement**, not the underlying circuit idea. Examiners deduct marks for mixing up "layout design" with "patent" or "copyright".
>
> 3. **Missing TRIPS linkage.** Many students answer the entire question without mentioning **TRIPS Articles 35–38**. Examiners expect at least one TRIPS reference. **Loss: 0.5–1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Omitting the "not commonplace" element of originality.** Originality has two parts: (i) own intellectual effort, and (ii) non-commonplace nature. Missing either is a **1-mark deduction**.
>
> 5. **Writing "employer" for "commissioner" or vice versa.** Section 8 vs Section 9 is a classic KTU trap. Distinguish: **employment** (employer) ≠ **contract for service** (commissioner).
>
> 6. **Forgetting the compulsory licensing provision** under Section 21. Many textbooks miss this, but the KTU 2024 syllabus includes it.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Layout design = **3D disposition** of elements of an IC (Section 2(d), SICLD Act, 2000).
- ✅ Governing statute: **Semiconductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act, 2000** (India).
- ✅ TRIPS compliance: **Articles 35–38**.
- ✅ **Originality test**: Own intellectual effort + Not commonplace (Section 5).
- ✅ **Registration window**: Within **2 years** of first commercial exploitation (Section 7(2)).
- ✅ First owner in employment = **Employer** (Section 8).
- ✅ First owner under contract for service = **Commissioning party** (Section 9).
- ✅ **Term of protection = 10 years** from the **earlier** of filing date or first commercial exploitation date (Section 19).
- ✅ Rights: **Reproduce, Incorporate, Import, Sell/Distribute** (Section 17).
- ✅ Infringement is a **civil wrong** (not criminal) under the SICLD Act.
- ✅ Remedies: **Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits, Delivery Up** (Section 18).
- ✅ Exceptions: **Reverse engineering for education, Innocent infringement, Private non-commercial use** (Sections 20–21).
- ✅ **Compulsory licensing** possible if not worked within 3 years of registration (Section 21).
- ✅ Layout design is a **sui generis right** — different from patents (20 yrs) and copyright (60 yrs).
- ✅ The Registrar of Layout Designs is appointed by the **Central Government** (Section 3).
- ✅ KTU 2024 favourite: **"Earlier of two dates"** rule in Section 19.
- ✅ KTU 2024 trap: Mixing up **Section 8 (employer)** and **Section 9 (commissioner)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "O-F-O-R-T-R-I"** for the 10-year rule:
> **O**riginality + **F**iling (2 years) + **O**wnership rules + **R**ights (Sec 17) + **T**erm (10 years) + **R**emedies (Sec 18) + **I**nfringement + **R**everse engineering exception.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of IPR Registration

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Registration** in the context of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) is the **statutory, formal, and administrative process** by which an applicant's claim to a particular intellectual property right (Patent, Trademark, Design, Geographical Indication, etc.) is officially recorded on a public register maintained by the competent government authority (e.g., the Indian Patent Office under the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks, or the International Bureau under WIPO), thereby conferring **exclusive, enforceable, and time-bound rights** upon the registered proprietor.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, registration is the **operative act** that converts a mere *inchoate* (incomplete) right into a *perfected* legal right capable of being asserted in a court of law. Without registration (where mandatory), the right cannot be enforced against third-party infringers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Land Registry"**
> Think of registration like registering a plot of land at the Sub-Registrar's office. You may physically possess a plot of land (similar to inventing something or using a brand name), but until you *register* it in your name in the government's record, you cannot:
> 1. Sell it legally,
> 2. Sue a trespasser, or
> 3. Get a bank loan against it.
>
> **IPR Registration works exactly the same way.** Until you formally register your patent, trademark, or design with the government registry, you generally cannot sue infringers or transfer the right cleanly to a buyer. The **Registration Certificate** is the legal "title deed" for your intangible intellectual asset.

## 1.3 Critical KTU Syllabus Highlight — Which IPRs Require Registration?

| Type of IPR | Registration Mandatory? | Term of Protection (if registered) |
|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | **YES** (Under Patents Act, 1970) | **20 years** from date of filing |
| **Trademark** | **YES** (Under Trade Marks Act, 1999) | **10 years**, renewable indefinitely |
| **Industrial Design** | **YES** (Under Designs Act, 2000) | **10 years**, extendable to **15 years** |
| **Geographical Indication (GI)** | **YES** (Under GI of Goods Act, 1999) | **10 years**, renewable |
| **Copyright** | **NO** (Automatic upon creation) | Life + **60 years** (literary); 60 years (films/sound recordings) |
| **Trade Secret** | **NO** (Protected via confidentiality) | Indefinite (as long as secret is maintained) |
| **Plant Variety** | **YES** (Under PPVFR Act, 2001) | **18 years** (trees/vines); **15 years** (others); **15 years** (extant varieties) |

## 1.4 Why Registration is the "Heart" of IPR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Registration transforms three things simultaneously:
> 1. **Legal standing** — enables the owner to sue.
> 2. **Territorial exclusivity** — protection is granted country-by-country.
> 3. **Public Notice (Constructive Notice)** — the world is deemed to have been informed of your claim once it appears on the public register.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Registration — Four Universal Pillars

Regardless of the specific IPR being registered, **every KTU examinable registration system** rests upon the following four pillars:

### Pillar 1: Application (Form & Fees)
- A prescribed application form is filed with the competent Registry.
- Fees are paid (online/offline) as per the **First Schedule** of the relevant Act.
- Applicant must be the rightful claimant (true and first inventor for patents; proprietor for trademarks; producer for GIs).

### Pillar 2: Examination
- The Registry conducts a substantive and/or formal examination.
- For patents: Novelty, Inventive Step, Industrial Application are checked.
- For trademarks: Distinctiveness, non-descriptiveness, non-deceptiveness.
- For designs: Novelty, originality, not contrary to public order/morality.

### Pillar 3: Publication & Opposition
- The application is **published in the official journal / e-gazette**.
- A **third-party opposition window** is opened (typically **4 months** under the Patents Act, 1970; **3 months** under the Trade Marks Act, 1999).

### Pillar 4: Registration & Certificate
- Upon overcoming opposition and satisfying all requirements, the right is **entered on the Register**.
- A **Registration Certificate** is issued — this is the conclusive proof of title.

## 2.2 International Registration Systems (High KTU Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests **three international registration mechanisms** managed by WIPO (World Intellectual Property Organization):

| System | Governed By | Purpose | India's Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PCT** (Patent Cooperation Treaty) | WIPO, 1970 | Single international patent application seeking protection in multiple countries | India is a **Contracting State** since **7th December 1998** |
| **Madrid Protocol** | WIPO, 1989 | International registration of trademarks across member nations | India acceded on **8th July 2013** |
| **Hague System** | WIPO, 1999 (Geneva Act) | International registration of industrial designs | India became a member on **13th December 2017** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbol / Notation | Boundary Condition | Practical Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patent Term** | $T_p = 20 \text{ years}$ | From date of **filing** (not grant) | Calculates exclusivity window |
| **Trademark Term** | $T_{tm} = 10 \text{ years}$ | Renewable; **indefinite** if renewed every 10 yrs | Brand perpetuity |
