| **Design Term** | $T_d = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ years}$ | Initial 10 yrs + one extension of 5 yrs | Industrial design life |
| **GI Term** | $T_{gi} = 10 \text{ years}$ | Renewable | Community rights |
| **Copyright (Literary)** | $T_c = \text{Life of author} + 60 \text{ years}$ | Post-mortem calculation | Author's estate planning |
| **Opposition Window (Patent)** | $\Delta t_{opp} = 4 \text{ months}$ | From publication date | Strategic litigation timing |
| **Opposition Window (Trademark)** | $\Delta t_{opp}^{tm} = 3 \text{ months}$ | (or 1 month for TM) | Brand protection horizon |
| **PCT National Phase Entry** | $\Delta t_{PCT} = 30/31 \text{ months}$ | From priority date | Filing deadline |
| **Renewal Cycle (Patent)** | Annual | From **3rd year** onwards | Maintenance fees |
| **Priority Date** | $D_{pri}$ | Date of first filing | Establishes seniority globally |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **"Date of Filing"** with **"Date of Priority"** for patents. The **priority date** (the date of the earliest application whose priority is claimed) is the legally relevant date for assessing novelty against prior art.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

1. **Patent Registration** → Engineers protect novel machinery, software algorithms (technical solutions), or chemical processes. Example: **Bosch** patents fuel injection systems.
2. **Trademark Registration** → Companies register logos, brand names (e.g., **TATA**, **Apple**).
3. **Design Registration** → Aesthetic shape of a smartphone, handle of a tool.
4. **GI Registration** → "**Darjeeling Tea**", "**Kancheepuram Silk**" — protects regional engineering/agricultural products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Patent Registration Procedure (India — Patents Act, 1970)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked procedural question** in KTU examinations. Memorize the chronology.

**Step 1: Filing of Application (Section 7 / 54 of Patents Act, 1970)**
Applicant files Form 1 (Application for Grant of Patent), Form 2 (Provisional or Complete Specification), Form 3 (Statement of Undertaking), Form 5 (Declaration of Inventorship) along with prescribed fees.

**Step 2: Publication of Application (Section 11A)**
The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the date of filing or priority date, whichever is earlier. However, the applicant may request **early publication** using Form 12.

**Step 3: Request for Examination (Section 11B)**
The applicant must file a Request for Examination in **Form 18** within **48 months** from the priority date. If not filed, the application is deemed to be abandoned.

**Step 4: Examination by Controller (Section 12)**
The Controller examines the application for:
- Novelty (Section 2(1)(j))
- Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja))
- Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))
- Non-exclusion under Sections 3 & 4

**Step 5: First Examination Report (FER) Issued**
The examiner issues the **First Examination Report (FER)** citing objections. Applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable to 12 months with fees).

**Step 6: Opposition (Section 25)**
Opposition can be filed:
- **Pre-grant opposition** — by any person after publication until grant (under Section 25(1)).
- **Post-grant opposition** — within **12 months** of grant (under Section 25(2)).

**Step 7: Grant of Patent (Section 43)**
If all objections are overcome, the patent is granted. The **date of grant** is the date on which the patent is entered in the Register.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Date Arithmetic for Patent Term

The standard term of a patent is **20 years** from the date of filing. Let us derive the **Date of Expiry** mathematically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{filing}} &= \text{Date of filing of complete specification} \\
D_{\text{priority}} &= \text{Date of earliest priority claimed} \\
T_{\text{term}} &= 20 \text{ years} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **Date of Expiry** is:

$$
D_{\text{expiry}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A complete specification is filed on **15th March 2020**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{expiry}} &= 15\text{-}03\text{-}2020 + 20 \text{ years} \\
&= 15\text{-}03\text{-}2040
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Logical Conversion:** The 20-year clock starts on the *filing date of the complete specification*, not the priority date and not the publication date. If only a provisional was filed first, the **provisional filing date** is treated as the priority date for novelty assessment, but the **complete specification filing date** is the start of the 20-year term.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Trademark Registration Procedure (India — Trade Marks Act, 1999)

**Step 1: Trademark Search**
A search is conducted in the **TM Public Search** portal to verify availability.

**Step 2: Filing of Application (Section 18)**
Application filed in **Form TM-A** with the Registrar of Trademarks. Applicant may claim priority under the Paris Convention.

**Step 3: Vienna Codification**
The Registrar classifies the figurative elements using the **Vienna Classification** system.

**Step 4: Examination (Section 19)**
The Registrar examines for:
- Distinctiveness (Section 9)
- Non-descriptiveness (Section 9(1))
- Non-deceptiveness (Section 9(2))
- Not barred under Section 9 (absolute grounds) and Section 11 (relative grounds)

**Step 5: Acceptance & Publication (Section 20)**
If accepted, the mark is advertised in the **Trademark Journal**.

**Step 6: Opposition (Section 21)**
Any aggrieved person may file opposition within **3 months** (extendable by 1 month) from the date of publication.

**Step 7: Registration (Section 23)**
If no opposition or opposition fails, the mark is registered. The **date of registration** is the date of filing of the application (this is critical — registration is backdated).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Madrid Protocol International Filing

For international trademark registration under the **Madrid Protocol**, the steps and time-lines are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A} &: \text{File at national Office of Origin (India)} \rightarrow \text{Form MM2} \\
\text{Step B} &: \text{International Bureau (IB, Geneva) registers} \rightarrow \text{Within 12-18 months} \\
\text{Step C} &: \text{Designated national offices examine} \rightarrow \text{12 months to refuse (otherwise binding consent)} \\
\text{Step D} &: \text{International registration valid} \rightarrow \text{10 years, renewable} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Madrid Protocol** provides a critical advantage:
- **18-month examination deadline** by each designated Contracting Party.
- If no refusal is notified within 18 months, the trademark stands protected as if registered nationally.

## 3.5 Geographical Indication (GI) Registration — Special Considerations

A GI is registered under the **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999**. Key features:

1. **Application by Association of Producers/Authorized Users** (Section 11).
2. **Statement of Case** — details the goods, quality, reputation, geographical area, and proof of origin.
3. **Registration confers** — the right to prevent third parties from using the GI on goods not originating from the registered territory.
4. **Term** — Registration is for **10 years**, renewable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Unlike a patent or trademark, a GI is a **community right**, not a private right. The applicant MUST be a group of producers, an organization, or an authority representing the producers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**

### Q1. What is meant by registration of a trademark? State the duration of registration. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Registration of a trademark is the formal procedure under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** whereby an applicant's mark is entered in the **Register of Trade Marks** maintained by the Registrar. Upon registration, the proprietor is granted the **exclusive right to use** the mark in relation to the goods or services for which it is registered, and the right to sue for infringement under Section 29.

**Duration:** A registered trademark is protected for a period of **10 years** from the date of filing of the application. It can be **renewed indefinitely** for successive periods of 10 years upon payment of the prescribed renewal fees.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for statutory reference, 1 mark for duration & renewal.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between registration of a patent and registration of a copyright. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Patent | Copyright |
|---|---|---|
| **Mandatory?** | YES — registration is mandatory | NO — copyright arises automatically on creation |
| **Statute** | Patents Act, 1970 | Copyright Act, 1957 |
| **Term** | 20 years from filing date | Life + 60 years (literary works) |
| **What is registered** | Inventions (technical solutions) | Original literary, artistic, musical works |
| **Authority** | Controller General of Patents | Copyright Office (Registrar of Copyrights) |
| **Infringement standing** | Only registered proprietor can sue | Owner can sue even without registration (registration is prima facie evidence) |

*(1 mark for patent column, 1 mark for copyright column, 1 mark for differentiation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> In Part B questions on Registration procedures, students frequently **omit the statutory section numbers** (e.g., Section 25 for opposition, Section 43 for grant) and **miss the time-lines** (e.g., 18 months, 48 months, 3 months). Always cite BOTH the section AND the time-limit. Failure to do so results in a loss of **1.5 to 2 marks per sub-part**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Type]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for registration of a patent** in India under the Patents Act, 1970. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty)** system and explain how an Indian applicant can use it for international patent protection. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The procedure for registration of a patent in India is governed by the **Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2005):

**Step 1 — Filing of Application (Sections 7, 54):**
The applicant files the following with the appropriate Patent Office:
- **Form 1** — Application for grant of patent.
- **Form 2** — Provisional or complete specification.
- **Form 3** — Statement and undertaking regarding foreign applications.
- **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship.
Payment of the prescribed fees as per the **First Schedule**.

**Step 2 — Publication (Section 11A):**
The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the date of filing or priority date. The applicant may request **early publication** using Form 12.

**Step 3 — Request for Examination (Section 11B):**
The applicant (or any third party) must file **Form 18** within **48 months** from the priority date. Failing this, the application is deemed abandoned.

**Step 4 — Examination (Section 12) & First Examination Report (FER):**
The Controller examines for novelty (Sec 2(1)(j)), inventive step (Sec 2(1)(ja)), and industrial application (Sec 2(1)(ac)). The FER is issued, and the applicant must reply within **6 months** (extendable to 12 months).

**Step 5 — Opposition (Section 25):**
- **Pre-grant opposition** — by any person after publication till grant (Section 25(1)).
- **Post-grant opposition** — within **12 months** from the date of grant (Section 25(2)).

**Step 6 — Grant (Section 43):**
If objections are overcome and no successful opposition, the patent is granted and entered in the **Register of Patents**.

**Step 7 — Term:**
The patent is valid for **20 years** from the date of filing of the complete specification, subject to payment of annual renewal fees from the 3rd year onwards.

*(Distribution: 1 mark each for Steps 1–6 with section reference; 1 mark for term & renewal.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) — Overview:**

The **PCT**, administered by **WIPO**, is an international treaty signed in **Washington, D.C., in 1970** that allows inventors to seek patent protection simultaneously in multiple countries through a **single international application**.

**India joined the PCT on 7th December 1998.**

**Procedure for an Indian Applicant:**

**Step 1 — International Application:**
The applicant files an international application (Form PCT/RO/101) with the **Indian Patent Office** (acting as Receiving Office — RO/IN) or directly with WIPO's International Bureau (IB) in Geneva.

**Step 2 — International Search (ISR):**
An **International Searching Authority (ISA)** — typically the Indian Patent Office or European Patent Office — conducts a search for prior art and issues an **International Search Report (ISR)** and a **Written Opinion**.

**Step 3 — International Publication:**
The application is published by WIPO **18 months** after the priority date.

**Step 4 — International Preliminary Examination (Chapter II — Optional):**
The applicant may file a demand for **International Preliminary Examination** to receive a non-binding **International Preliminary Report on Patentability (IPRP)**.

**Step 5 — National Phase Entry (Section 39 of Patents Act, 1970):**
Within **31 months** from the priority date (or as applicable), the applicant must enter the **national phase** in each country where protection is sought, by filing the complete application, paying national fees, and providing translations.

**Advantages of PCT:**
1. **Deferral of costs** — the applicant has up to 30/31 months before national phase fees are due.
2. **Search & preliminary examination** — provides a strong indication of patentability before expensive national filings.
3. **Procedural simplification** — one application, one language, one set of fees for the international phase.

*(Distribution: 2 marks PCT overview & India's accession; 3 marks Step-by-Step procedure; 2 marks Advantages.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Type]**

**(a)** Explain the **procedure for registration of a trademark** under the Trade Marks Act, 1999, highlighting the grounds for refusal and the opposition mechanism. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **Madrid Protocol** system for international registration of trademarks. How has India's accession benefited Indian trademark owners? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Procedure for Trademark Registration under the Trade Marks Act, 1999:**

**Step 1 — Trademark Search:**
Before filing, a search is conducted on the **Indian Trade Marks Registry database** to ensure the mark is not already identical or similar to an existing registered mark.

**Step 2 — Filing of Application (Section 18):**
The applicant files **Form TM-A** with the Registrar of Trade Marks. The application is accompanied by:
- A clear representation of the mark.
- A list of goods/services classified under the **Nice Classification (45 classes)**.
- Prescribed fees.

**Step 3 — Vienna Codification:**
For figurative/device marks, the Registrar allots a **Vienna Code** to classify the visual elements.

**Step 4 — Examination (Section 19):**
The Registrar examines the application on:
- **Absolute grounds for refusal (Section 9)** — lack of distinctiveness, descriptiveness, genericness, deception, or contrary to public order/morality.
- **Relative grounds (Section 11)** — conflict with earlier trademarks, well-known marks, etc.

**Step 5 — Acceptance & Advertisement:**
If accepted, the mark is **advertised in the Trade Marks Journal** to invite opposition.

**Step 6 — Opposition (Section 21):**
Any "aggrieved person" may file **Notice of Opposition (Form TM-O)** within **3 months** (extendable by 1 month) from the date of publication. Grounds include conflict with earlier marks, bad faith, etc.

**Step 7 — Hearing & Decision:**
The Registrar hears both sides and decides whether the mark proceeds to registration.

**Step 8 — Registration (Section 23):**
If no opposition is filed or the opposition fails, the mark is **entered in the Register of Trade Marks**. The **date of registration is deemed to be the date of filing of the application**.

**Step 9 — Term & Renewal:**
The registration is valid for **10 years** and is renewable indefinitely.

**Grounds for Refusal (Section 9 — Absolute):**
1. Marks devoid of distinctive character.
2. Marks consisting exclusively of descriptive terms.
3. Marks which are customary in current language.
4. Marks contrary to public order or morality.
5. Deceptive marks.

*(Distribution: 1 mark each for Steps 1–6; 1 mark for grounds of refusal.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The Madrid Protocol for International Registration of Trademarks:**

The **Madrid Protocol** (adopted in **1989**, entered into force in **1996**) is an international treaty administered by **WIPO** that allows a trademark owner to obtain protection in multiple countries through a **single international application**.

**India acceded to the Madrid Protocol on 8th July 2013.**

**Procedure:**

**Step 1 — File at Office of Origin:**
The Indian applicant files **Form MM2** with the **Indian Trade Marks Registry** (acting as the Office of Origin).

**Step 2 — Forwarding to WIPO:**
The Indian Registry forwards the application to the **International Bureau (IB) of WIPO** in Geneva.

**Step 3 — Formal Examination by IB:**
The IB registers the mark in the **International Register** and notifies all designated Contracting Parties.

**Step 4 — National Examination by Designated Offices:**
Each designated national office has **12 months** (or 18 months if extension is declared) to refuse protection on its national grounds. If no refusal is notified, the mark is **deemed protected** in that country.

**Step 5 — International Registration:**
The international registration is valid for **10 years** and can be renewed indefinitely.

**Benefits to Indian Trademark Owners:**

1. **Single Application, Multiple Countries** — One application can designate 100+ contracting parties.
2. **Cost-Effective** — Eliminates the need for separate national applications, agents, and translations in each country during the international phase.
3. **Centralized Management** — Subsequent changes (assignments, renewals, address changes) can be recorded through a single procedure with WIPO.
4. **Strategic Expansion** — Indian SMEs and startups can now expand brand protection to global markets like the EU, USA, Japan, China with minimal procedural friction.
5. **Madrid Agreement synergy** — The Protocol removes the earlier requirement of a prior national registration (under the Madrid Agreement), enabling "trademark-on-application" basis filings.

*(Distribution: 2 marks Madrid Protocol overview & India's date of accession; 3 marks Step-by-step procedure; 2 marks benefits to Indian owners.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Must Memorize:**

- **Patent** — Mandatory registration. Term: **20 years** from filing date of complete specification. Section 43 governs grant. Annual renewal from 3rd year.
- **Trademark** — Mandatory registration. Term: **10 years**, renewable indefinitely. Date of registration = date of filing. Section 23 governs registration.
- **Design** — Term: **10 years** + one extension of **5 years** = **15 years total**. Governed by Designs Act, 2000.
- **GI** — Community right. Term: **10 years** renewable. Filed by **association of producers**, not individuals.
- **Copyright** — **NO registration required** for subsistence. Arises automatically on creation. Term: Life + 60 years (literary/dramatic/musical/artistic).
- **PCT** — Filed with **Indian Patent Office** as Receiving Office (RO/IN). National phase entry **30/31 months** from priority date.
- **Madrid Protocol** — Filed with **Indian Trade Marks Registry** as Office of Origin. Designated offices have **12-18 months** to refuse.
- **Hague System** — International design registration. India joined **13 Dec 2017**.
- **Opposition Windows** — Patent (pre-grant any time till grant; post-grant 12 months); Trademark (3 months + 1 month extension); Design (3 months).
- **Date Arithmetic** — $D_{\text{expiry}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years}$ (patents); $D_{\text{expiry}} = D_{\text{filing}} + 10 \text{ years}$ (TM, GI).
- **Priority Date** — Crucial for novelty assessment; established by the earliest filed application under the Paris Convention.
- **WIPO** — Geneva-based UN agency administering PCT, Madrid, Hague.
- **Indian Authority** — Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM), under DPIIT, Ministry of Commerce & Industry.
- **Public Register** — Registration on the register provides **constructive notice** to the entire world.
- **First Examination Report (FER)** — Issued for patents under Section 12; reply within 6 months.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden KTU Rule:** Always cite the **section number + time-limit + actor (Controller/Registrar)** together when describing any registration procedure. Examiners award extra marks for **statutory precision**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Opposition of Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Opposition of Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Opposition of Registration** is a statutory legal proceeding provided under Intellectual Property law whereby a third party (the "Opponent") formally challenges the grant/registration of an IP right (primarily a trademark, but conceptually applicable to patents and designs) before it is finally entered into the Register. In Indian law, for trademarks, this mechanism is codified under **Sections 21 and 22 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**, and for patents under **Section 25 of the Patents Act, 1970** (both pre-grant and post-grant opposition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Opposition is a *pre-grant safeguard* (trademarks, patent pre-grant) and a *post-grant remedy* (patent post-grant). It allows any "person aggrieved" to invoke the Registrar's jurisdiction before the IP becomes a monopoly right.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Notice Board" Intuition

Imagine you apply for a special parking spot reserved for "XYZ Company" in a large apartment complex. Before the management officially allocates and paints your name on the spot, they publish a notice on the common notice board: *"Anyone objecting to this allocation must write to us within 30 days."* During that window, your neighbour (who already has a similar spot or believes you don't deserve one) files an objection. The management then holds a hearing before finalising the allocation.

**Mapping the Analogy to IPR:**
- **The Notice Board** = The **Trade Marks Journal** (or Patent Journal in patent law), published weekly by the Registrar of Trade Marks / Controller of Patents.
- **The 30-day window** = The statutory opposition period (**4 months for trademarks**, **6 months pre-grant / 12 months post-grant for patents**).
- **The neighbour's objection** = The **Notice of Opposition** filed in Form TM-O (trademarks) / Form 7A (patents).
- **The management hearing** = The **inter partes hearing** before the Registrar.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for engineers:** A product name you wish to launch (e.g., "VoltEdge" for an EV controller) can be blocked *before* it becomes a registered right. This protects both startups from copycats *and* incumbents from confusingly similar marks.

### 1.3 Key Statutory Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Trademark Act, 1999 | Patents Act, 1970 |
|---|---|---|
| Statutory Provision | **Section 21** (opposition), **Section 22** (rectification) | **Section 25(1)** (pre-grant), **Section 25(2)** (post-grant) |
| Standing to oppose | "Any person" / person aggrieved | Pre-grant: "Any person"; Post-grant: "Any person interested" |
| Opposition window | **4 months** from Journal advertisement | Pre-grant: **6 months** from publication; Post-grant: **12 months** from grant |
| Form prescribed | **Form TM-O** | **Form 7A** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** Opposition is **administrative** (before the Registrar), not judicial. Only after the Registrar's decision can the aggrieved party approach the **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** — replaced by **Commercial Courts** post 2021 amendments — or the High Court.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Opposition Proceedings

Opposition is bifurcated into two distinct legal tracks. Understanding the difference is **critical for KTU 14-mark answers**.

**A. Pre-Grant Opposition (Patents)**
- Filed **before** the patent is granted.
- Any person may file on the basis of **Sections 25(1)(a)–(k)** grounds (e.g., lack of novelty, prior claiming, non-patentability under Sections 3 & 4).
- No locus standi requirement — a simple "any person" can oppose.

**B. Post-Grant Opposition (Patents)**
- Filed **within 12 months** from the date of grant of patent.
- Restricted to "**person interested**" — a higher threshold than pre-grant.
- Grounds include non-patentability, non-disclosure of information, wrongful obtaining, etc.

**C. Trademark Opposition (Section 21, TM Act)**
- Filed within **4 months** from the date of advertisement in the Trade Marks Journal.
- Available to **"any person"** (broader than the "person aggrieved" standard for rectification).
- Grounds enumerated under **Section 21(1)**.

### 2.2 Grounds for Opposition — Detailed Logic Steps

The following are the structured legal grounds on which opposition may be filed. Engineers should memorise these as a **checklist** for exam answers.

**Under Section 21(1) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999:**
1. The mark does not satisfy the **definition of trademark** (Section 2(1)(zb)).
2. The mark is **deceptively similar** to an earlier trademark or well-known mark.
3. The mark is **descriptive** (Section 9(1)), **generic**, or customary in established trade practice.
4. The mark is **devoid of distinctive character**.
5. Use of the mark is **likely to cause confusion** or deception under Section 2(1)(zb) read with Section 10.
6. Use of the mark would be **contrary to law** or public policy, or scandalous/contemptuous.
7. Use of the mark would **hurt the religious susceptibilities** of any class or section of citizens.
8. The mark is **disqualified** under Sections 9, 11, or 13.
9. The applicant is **not the bona fide proprietor** of the mark, and another person has prior use.
10. Use of the mark would amount to **passing off** of goods/services.

**Under Section 25(1) of the Patents Act (Pre-Grant):**
1. Wrongful obtainment of the invention.
2. Prior publication in India or abroad before the priority date.
3. Lack of novelty or inventive step.
4. Non-patentability under Sections 3 and 4.
5. Insufficient disclosure of the invention.
6. Non-furnishing of information regarding foreign applications.
7. The applicant falsely mentions the source of biological material.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Value / Reference | Statutory Source |
|---|---|---|
| TM Opposition window | **4 months** | Section 21(1), TM Act |
| Patent Pre-grant opposition window | **6 months** from publication | Section 25(1), Patents Act |
| Patent Post-grant opposition window | **12 months** from grant | Section 25(2), Patents Act |
| TM Opposition Form | **Form TM-O** (Rule 47) | TM Rules, 2017 |
| Patent Opposition Form | **Form 7A** | Patent Rules, 2003 |
| Statutory fee for TM opposition | **₹ 2,500** (online) | Schedule I, TM Rules 2017 |
| Counter-statement deadline (TM) | **2 months** from notice | Section 21(2) |
| Evidence-in-support deadline | **2 months** from counter-statement or 2 months from copy | Rule 50 |
| Hearing notice | At least **1 month** advance | Section 21(4) |
| Appeal from Registrar's decision | **High Court / Commercial Court** | Section 91, TM Act |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility for Engineers

- **Brand Strategy:** A startup filing "NeuroCore" for an AI chip must conduct a trademark search and anticipate opposition from existing "Neuro-" prefixed tech companies.
- **Patent Strategy:** A researcher filing a patent on CRISPR-Cas9 variants must anticipate pre-grant opposition from competing research groups citing prior art.
- **Risk Management:** Opposition mechanisms reduce litigation costs by providing an administrative alternative to infringement suits.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedural Flow — Trademark Opposition (Section 21)

The following is the exhaustive procedural chain. Each step is numbered for KTU answer-writing convenience.

**Step 1: Advertisement in the Trade Marks Journal**
- After examination (Section 18), the Registrar causes the application to be advertised in the **Trade Marks Journal**.
- The four-month opposition window begins running from this date.
- This date is **t = 0** for the opposition timeline.

**Step 2: Filing of Notice of Opposition (Form TM-O)**
- The opponent files **Form TM-O** within **4 months** from the date of advertisement.
- Contents: statement of grounds, details of the opponent, the application number being opposed, and the goods/services affected.
- Statutory fee: **₹ 2,500** (online filing via IP India portal).

**Step 3: Service of Notice on the Applicant**
- The Registrar serves a copy of the opposition notice on the applicant.
- This triggers Step 4.

**Step 4: Filing of Counter-Statement by the Applicant**
- The applicant must file a **counter-statement** within **2 months** of receipt of the opposition copy.
- If no counter-statement is filed, the mark is treated as **abandoned**.
- This is a **mandatory step** — failure to comply results in automatic forfeiture of the application.

**Step 5: Filing of Evidence in Support of Opposition**
- The opponent files **evidence-in-chief** (affidavits, documents) within **2 months** from receipt of the counter-statement (or within **2 months** of the deadline for counter-statement if none is filed).

**Step 6: Filing of Evidence in Reply by the Applicant**
- The applicant files **evidence-in-reply** within **2 months** of receiving the opponent's evidence.
- No further evidence is permitted except by leave of the Registrar.

**Step 7: Hearing**
- The Registrar issues a notice of hearing, giving at least **1 month** advance intimation.
- Both parties are heard in **inter partes** proceedings.

**Step 8: Decision and Order**
- The Registrar passes a reasoned order: either **dismissing the opposition** (and proceeding to registration) or **allowing the opposition** (refusing registration or imposing conditions).

**Step 9: Appeal**
- An appeal lies before the **High Court** (or Commercial Court of appropriate jurisdiction) under **Section 91** of the TM Act within **3 months** of the order.

### 3.2 Timeline Calculation — Worked Example

Suppose a trademark application is advertised in the **Trade Marks Journal** dated **15 January 2025**. Compute the key deadlines.

**Step 1: Opposition window closes**
$$\text{Last date of opposition} = 15 \text{ Jan 2025} + 4 \text{ months}$$

Calculating month-wise: 15 Jan + 4 months → **15 May 2025**.

> [!NOTE]
> Under the **Indian Limitation Act, 1963** (read with Section 21, TM Act), if the last day falls on a day the IP India office is closed (holiday/Sunday), the period is extended to the next working day.

**Step 2: Counter-statement deadline (assuming opposition filed on the last day)**
$$\text{Counter-statement due} = \text{Service date} + 2 \text{ months}$$

If service is effected on **20 May 2025**, the applicant must counter by **20 July 2025**.

**Step 3: Evidence-in-support deadline**
$$\text{Evidence due} = 20 \text{ July 2025} + 2 \text{ months} = \text{20 September 2025}$$

**Step 4: Evidence-in-reply deadline**
$$\text{Evidence-in-reply due} = \text{20 September 2025} + 2 \text{ months} = \text{20 November 2025}$$

**Step 5: Hearing**
- Registrar issues notice at least **1 month** before the hearing date.
- If the Registrar fixes the hearing for **15 January 2026**, the notice must be issued by **15 December 2025**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Opposition Across IP Regimes

| Parameter | Trademark | Patent (Pre-Grant) | Patent (Post-Grant) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Window | 4 months | 6 months | 12 months |
| From when | Journal publication | Application publication | Grant date |
| Locus standi | "Any person" | "Any person" | "Person interested" |
| Form | TM-O | 7A | 7A |
| Counter-statement | Yes (2 months) | No formal counter | Yes |
| Hearing | Yes | Yes (representation) | Yes |
| Appeal court | High Court / Commercial Court | High Court | High Court |

### 3.4 Distinction — Opposition vs. Rectification

A frequently examined distinction in KTU papers:

| Aspect | Opposition (Section 21) | Rectification (Section 22) |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | **Before** registration | **After** registration |
| Who can file | "Any person" | "Any person aggrieved" |
| Purpose | Prevent registration | Cancel existing registration |
| Forum | Registrar | IPAB / High Court (post-2021) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Procedural Flow — Trademark Opposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Advertised in TM Journal] --> B[4-Month Window Begins]
    B --> C{Opposition Filed in Form TM-O?}
    C -->|No| D[Application Proceeds to Registration]
    C -->|Yes| E[Registrar Serves Notice on Applicant]
    E --> F[Applicant Files Counter-Statement within 2 Months]
    F --> G{Counter-Statement Filed?}
    G -->|No| H[Application Treated as Abandoned]
    G -->|Yes| I[Opponent Files Evidence-in-Support within 2 Months]
    I --> J[Applicant Files Evidence-in-Reply within 2 Months]
    J --> K[Registrar Issues Hearing Notice - 1 Month Advance]
    K --> L[Inter Partes Hearing Before Registrar]
    L --> M{Decision}
    M -->|Opposition Allowed| N[Mark Refused Registration]
    M -->|Opposition Dismissed| O[Mark Proceeds to Registration]
    N --> P[Appeal to High Court under Section 91]
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Comparative Architecture — Opposition Across IP Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IP1[TRADEMARK OPPOSITION]
        T1[Journal Advertisement] --> T2[4-Month Window]
        T2 --> T3[Form TM-O]
        T3 --> T4[Section 21 TM Act 1999]
    end
    subgraph IP2[PATENT PRE-GRANT OPPOSITION]
        P1[Application Publication] --> P2[6-Month Window]
        P2 --> P3[Form 7A]
        P3 --> P4[Section 25 1 Patents Act]
    end
    subgraph IP3[PATENT POST-GRANT OPPOSITION]
        Q1[Grant of Patent] --> Q2[12-Month Window]
        Q2 --> Q3[Form 7A]
        Q3 --> Q4[Section 25 2 Patents Act]
    end
```

### 4.3 Grounds for Opposition — Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Grounds for Opposition))
    Section 21 TM Act
      Relative Grounds
        Deceptively Similar Mark
        Confusion with Prior Mark
        Passing Off Risk
      Absolute Grounds
        Descriptive Marks
        Generic Terms
        Public Policy
        Religious Sensitivities
    Section 25 1 Patents Act

      Non Patentable Subject Matter

      Wrongful Obtainment
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. Define "opposition of registration" under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. State the relevant statutory provision, the form to be used, and the prescribed time limit.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Opposition of registration is a statutory mechanism under **Section 21 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**, whereby any person may challenge the registration of a trademark after its advertisement in the Trade Marks Journal but before it is actually registered. The opposition is filed in **Form TM-O** prescribed under the Trade Marks Rules, 2017, within a period of **four months** from the date of advertisement of the application in the Journal. *(Award 1 mark each for: definition, Section 21 + Form TM-O, and 4-month period.)*

---

**Question 2. Distinguish between pre-grant opposition and post-grant opposition under the Patents Act, 1970.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pre-Grant | Post-Grant |
|---|---|---|
| Section | 25(1) | 25(2) |
| When filed | Before patent is granted | Within 12 months of grant |
| Who may file | Any person | Person interested |
| Window | 6 months from publication | 12 months from grant |

*(Award 1 mark for each correct distinction across Section reference, timing, and locus standi.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. (a)** Explain in detail the **procedure for filing an opposition** to a trademark application under Section 21 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. Cite the relevant forms, time limits, and statutory provisions. *(7 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The procedure for trademark opposition is a multi-stage statutory process codified under **Section 21** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, read with **Rules 47 to 57** of the Trade Marks Rules, 2017. The stages are:

**(i) Advertisement in the Trade Marks Journal (1 mark):**
Once the Registrar is satisfied that the application should proceed after examination, the application is advertised in the **Trade Marks Journal**. The advertisement triggers the opposition clock.

**(ii) Filing of Notice of Opposition (2 marks):**
Any person may file a Notice of Opposition in **Form TM-O** within **four months** from the date of advertisement. The notice must contain:
- The application number being opposed.
- Statement of grounds (Section 21(1) grounds).
- Particulars of goods/services affected.
- Statutory fee of ₹ 2,500 (online).

**(iii) Service on Applicant (1 mark):**
The Registrar serves a copy of the opposition on the trademark applicant.

**(iv) Counter-Statement (1 mark):**
The applicant must file a counter-statement within **two months** of service. Failure to file results in the application being treated as **abandoned**.

**(v) Evidence Stage (1 mark):**
- Opponent's evidence-in-chief: 2 months from receipt of counter-statement (or from deadline if not filed).
- Applicant's evidence-in-reply: 2 months from receipt of opponent's evidence.

**(vi) Hearing (1 mark):**
The Registrar gives at least **1 month** notice and hears both parties in inter partes proceedings before passing a reasoned order either dismissing or allowing the opposition.

---

**Q.A. (b)** Discuss the **various grounds on which a trademark application may be opposed** under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. Why are these grounds important from an engineering startup's perspective? *(7 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Statutory Grounds (Section 21(1), 4 marks):**

The grounds are categorised as **relative** and **absolute**:

**Absolute Grounds (Section 9):**
- Marks devoid of distinctive character.
- Descriptive marks (generic/geographical/descriptive of quality).
- Marks customary in established trade practice.
- Marks contrary to law, public policy, morality, or scandalous.
- Marks hurtful to religious susceptibilities.

**Relative Grounds (Section 11):**
- Identity with earlier trademark.
- Deceptive similarity causing likelihood of confusion.
- Use that amounts to passing off.
- Conflict with well-known marks (Section 13).

**Procedural Ground (1 mark):**
- Applicant is not the bona fide proprietor.

**Engineering Startup Perspective (2 marks):**
For a tech startup launching a new product (say, "VoltEdge" EV controllers), opposition can:
- Block confusingly similar marks filed by competitors (brand protection).
- Allow the startup to oppose older marks that threaten its brand identity.
- Reduce litigation costs by resolving disputes administratively.
- Highlight the importance of **clearance searches** before filing.

**Conclusion:** Opposition grounds serve as a vital pre-registration filter that balances the rights of trademark owners against public interest and fair competition.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. (a)** Compare and contrast the **opposition mechanisms under the Trade Marks Act, 1999** and the **Patents Act, 1970**. Highlight the locus standi, time limits, forms, and grounds. *(7 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Statutory Basis (1 mark):**
- Trademark: Section 21 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999.
- Patent: Section 25 of the Patents Act, 1970.

**(ii) Locus Standi (1 mark):**
- Trademark: "Any person" can oppose.
- Patent Pre-Grant: "Any person" can oppose.
- Patent Post-Grant: "Person interested" — a stricter threshold.

**(iii) Time Limits (2 marks):**
- Trademark: **4 months** from advertisement.
- Patent Pre-Grant: **6 months** from publication.
- Patent Post-Grant: **12 months** from grant of patent.

**(iv) Forms (1 mark):**
- Trademark: **Form TM-O** (Rule 47, TM Rules 2017).
- Patent: **Form 7A** (Patent Rules 2003).

**(v) Grounds — Key Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Trademark | Patent |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Distinctiveness, similarity, confusion | Novelty, inventive step, prior art |
| Public morality | Explicit ground | Not a ground |
| Disclosure | Not applicable | Insufficient disclosure is a ground |
| Bona fide proprietor | Yes | Wrongful obtainment |

**Conclusion:** While both regimes share the *concept* of opposition, trademark opposition protects **brand identity** and consumer interest, whereas patent opposition safeguards **technological innovation** and prior-art integrity.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Critically analyse the **role of opposition proceedings as an administrative safeguard** against wrongful registration in intellectual property law. *(7 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Introduction (1 mark):**
Opposition proceedings represent a **critical pre-grant/post-grant administrative filter** in IP law, ensuring that only meritorious applications proceed to registration. They reflect the principle that IP rights, while private in nature, are granted for public benefit.

**(ii) Procedural Economy (2 marks):**
- Opposition resolves disputes at the **administrative level**, avoiding costly court litigation.
- Cheaper and faster than infringement or revocation suits.
- Provides an early-stage corrective mechanism.

**(iii) Multi-Stakeholder Protection (2 marks):**
- **Competitors** are protected from confusingly similar marks or patents.
- **Consumers** are protected from deceptive practices.
- **Prior users** retain their common-law rights.
- **Public interest** is safeguarded through scrutiny.

**(iv) Critical Evaluation (2 marks):**
**Strengths:**
- Democratises the IP grant process by allowing third-party participation.
- Corrects examination errors.

**Weaknesses:**
- Open to **abuse by trolls** who file spurious oppositions to extract settlements.
- **Procedural delays** can extend the timeline to years.
- Heavy reliance on documentary evidence may disadvantage pro se applicants.

**(v) Conclusion (closing remark):**
Despite limitations, opposition proceedings remain an indispensable safeguard, balancing private rights with public interest in the IP ecosystem.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong time limit citation** — Trademark opposition is **4 months**, NOT 3 months. Confusing with patent windows is the single most common error.
> 2. **Wrong form reference** — Students often write "Form 1" or "Form 5" for trademark opposition. The correct form is **TM-O**.
> 3. **Omitting Section reference** — Always cite **Section 21 of the TM Act, 1999** and **Section 25 of the Patents Act, 1970**.
> 4. **Confusing opposition with rectification** — Opposition is **pre-registration** (Section 21, TM Act); rectification is **post-registration** (Section 22). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Missing appeal provision** — Many answers stop at the Registrar's order and forget **Section 91** (appeal to High Court).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Trademark opposition** is governed by **Section 21 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**, with a **4-month window** from Journal advertisement.
- ✅ The opposition is filed in **Form TM-O** (Rule 47, TM Rules 2017) with a fee of **₹ 2,500** (online).
- ✅ **Patent pre-grant opposition** (Section 25(1)) runs for **6 months** from publication; **post-grant opposition** (Section 25(2)) runs for **12 months** from grant.
- ✅ Locus standi for pre-grant opposition (any person) is broader than post-grant (person interested).
- ✅ Counter-statement must be filed within **2 months** of service — failure leads to abandonment.
- ✅ Evidence stages: **2 months** for opponent's evidence-in-chief, **2 months** for applicant's evidence-in-reply.
- ✅ Hearing notice: minimum **1 month** in advance.
- ✅ Appeal lies to the **High Court / Commercial Court** under **Section 91** of the TM Act.
- ✅ Opposition is **administrative** (before the Registrar), not judicial.
- ✅ Key distinction: **Opposition = pre-registration**, **Rectification = post-registration**.
- ✅ Grounds are categorised into **absolute (Section 9)** and **relative (Section 11)** under the TM Act.
- ✅ For patents, grounds include **novelty, inventive step, prior publication, wrongful obtainment, and insufficient disclosure**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Effect of Registration** refers to the bundle of statutory rights, privileges, presumptions of validity, and legal remedies that flow to an intellectual property (IP) owner once their IP right (trademark, patent, industrial design, or geographical indication) has been formally registered with the appropriate government registry under the relevant IP statute. In India, these effects are codified in:

- The **Trade Marks Act, 1999** (Sections 27–29, 134–135)
- The **Patents Act, 1970** (Sections 11A, 48, 104–114)
- The **Designs Act, 2000** (Sections 11, 22, 30)
- The **Copyright Act, 1957** (Sections 13, 51, 55–62)
- The **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999** (Sections 20–22, 67)

Registration acts as a **statutory notification to the world** that the registered proprietor holds an exclusive, transferable, licensable, and enforceable monopoly over the use of the subject matter for a defined term.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Registration is a *condition precedent* for the grant of statutory rights in Patents, Trademarks, Designs, and GI. For **Copyright**, registration is voluntary and only serves as prima facie evidence (Section 48, Copyright Act, 1957).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine the IP registry as a **Government Land Records Office (like the Kerala *Pattayam* registry)**. Just as recording a property deed in your name does not *create* your ownership — you acquired it the moment you bought the land — but it does give the world **public notice**, allows you to **evict squatters**, and lets you **sell, lease, or mortgage** the property, **registration of an IP right does not create the IP itself**, but:

- It converts a *defensible* right into an *enforceable* right,
- It shifts the **burden of proof** onto any challenger,
- It allows the proprietor to sue for **infringement, damages, and account of profits** in courts of competent jurisdiction,
- It permits the proprietor to **license, assign, or commercially exploit** the right with legal certainty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** *Registration = Official Government Stamp of Ownership + Exclusive Use + Power to Sue + Right to Earn from the IP.*

## 1.3 Three Core Legal Effects (Common to All IP Registries)

| # | Legal Effect | Practical Outcome |
|---|--------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Exclusive Right** | The proprietor alone may use, license, or assign the IP. |
| 2 | **Right to Sue for Infringement** | Statutory cause of action arises the moment a third party uses the IP without consent. |
| 3 | **Presumption of Validity** | The registration certificate is *prima facie* evidence of ownership and validity. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Term Reminder (BOLD Constants):**
> - **Patent: 20 years** from the date of filing.
> - **Trademark: 10 years**, renewable indefinitely.
> - **Design: 10 years**, extendable by 5 years (total 15 years).
> - **Geographical Indication: 10 years**, renewable.
> - **Copyright:** Life of the author + **60 years** (post-2012 amendment).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Effect of Registration under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**

### 2.1.1 Statutory Framework (Sections 27–29)
- **Section 27(1):** No person shall be entitled to institute any proceeding to prevent, or to recover damages for, the infringement of an **unregistered trademark**.
- **Section 27(2):** Nothing in this Act shall be deemed to affect the right of action against any person for passing off goods or services as the goods of another person or as services provided by another person.
- **Section 28:** Registration confers on the registered proprietor the **exclusive right to use the mark** in relation to the goods or services for which it is registered.
- **Section 29:** Defines infringement — use of an identical or deceptively similar mark in the course of trade without the proprietor's consent.

### 2.1.2 Why This Matters
Registration transforms a **common law right of "passing off"** (which requires proof of reputation, goodwill, and deception) into a **statutory right of infringement** (which is easier to prove, as the registration itself proves ownership).

> [!NOTE]
> **Passing Off vs. Infringement (KTU 2-Marker Favorite):**
> - *Passing Off:* Common law remedy, requires proof of goodwill + misrepresentation + damage.
> - *Infringement:* Statutory remedy, registration is conclusive proof of ownership.

## 2.2 Effect of Registration under the **Patents Act, 1970**

### 2.2.1 Statutory Framework (Section 48)
Where a patent is granted, the patentee shall have, subject to the provisions of the Act, the following rights, namely:
1. Where the invention is a **product**, the exclusive right to **make, use, sell, offer for sale, and import** the product.
2. Where the invention is a **process**, the exclusive right to **use the process** and to **use, sell, offer for sale, and import** the product obtained directly by that process.

### 2.2.2 The Exclusive Right — "Five Pillars"
| # | Right | Meaning |
|---|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Make** | Manufacturing the patented product. |
| 2 | **Use** | Employing the patented process/product. |
| 3 | **Sell** | Commercial transfer. |
| 4 | **Offer for Sale** | Even displaying it for sale constitutes infringement. |
| 5 | **Import** | Bringing into India. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working of Patents (Section 83):** Patents are granted "to encourage inventions and to secure that the inventions are worked in India on a commercial scale." Failure to work the patent (3 years after grant) can trigger **compulsory licensing** under Section 84.

## 2.3 Effect of Registration under the **Designs Act, 2000**

### 2.3.1 Section 11 — Piracy of Registered Design
- During the existence of copyright in a registered design, **no person shall apply the design or any fraudulent or obvious imitation thereof** to any article.
- The registered proprietor has the exclusive right to apply the design to articles in the class in which it is registered.

### 2.3.2 Section 22 — Cancellation
- Any person interested may apply for cancellation of the registration on grounds of prior publication, lack of novelty, or fraud.

## 2.4 Effect of Registration under the **GI Act, 1999**

### 2.4.1 Statutory Framework (Section 20)
- The registration of a GI confers on the registered users the **exclusive right to use the GI** in relation to the goods specified.
- The right is **collective** — it belongs to the registered proprietors *and* authorized users.
- Authorized users can sue for infringement (Section 67).

### 2.4.2 Famous KTU Case Study
- **"Darjeeling Tea"** (GI No. 1) — only registered users in the Darjeeling district can use the mark.
- **"Mysore Sandal Soap"** — Government of Karnataka is the registered proprietor.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Effect of Registration Matrix

| Statute | Registration is | Exclusive Rights Conferred | Infringement Provision | Term of Protection |
|---------|-----------------|----------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Trade Marks Act, 1999** | Mandatory for statutory action | Use of mark in commerce | Section 29 | **10 years** (renewable) |
| **Patents Act, 1970** | Mandatory for patent grant | Make, Use, Sell, Offer for Sale, Import | Section 104 | **20 years** (non-renewable) |
| **Designs Act, 2000** | Mandatory for design protection | Apply design to articles | Section 22 | **10 + 5 = 15 years** |
| **GI Act, 1999** | Mandatory for GI protection | Collective right to use GI name | Section 67 | **10 years** (renewable) |
| **Copyright Act, 1957** | **Voluntary** (facultative) | Reproduce, distribute, perform, communicate | Section 51 | Life + **60 years** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Copyright Registration** with mandatory registration. In India, **copyright arises automatically upon creation** of the work. Registration only serves as **prima facie evidence** in court under Section 48.

## 2.6 Civil and Criminal Remedies upon Infringement

### 2.6.1 Civil Remedies (Available Across All IP Statutes)
- **Injunctions** (temporary, permanent, interlocutory)
- **Damages** (compensatory)
- **Account of Profits** (the infringer's gain)
- **Delivery up / Destruction** of infringing goods
- **Anton Piller Order** (civil search order)

### 2.6.2 Criminal Remedies
| Statute | Criminal Provision | Punishment |
|---------|-------------------|------------|
| **Trade Marks Act** | Section 103 | 6 months to 3 years + fine ₹50,000–₹2,00,000 |
| **Copyright Act** | Section 63 | Min. 6 months, Max. 3 years + fine (first offense); 1–3 years for second offense |
| **Patents Act** | No criminal provision | Only civil remedies |
| **Designs Act** | Section 22 + IPC 478 | Cognizable, bailable offense |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favorite:** The remedies under IP law are *cumulative*, not exclusive. The plaintiff may seek civil, criminal, and administrative remedies simultaneously.

## 2.7 Defenses Available to Alleged Infringers

1. **Prior Use / Prior Publication** — the defendant was using it before the registration date.
2. **Invalidity of Registration** — challenging the registration itself.
3. **Fair Use / Experimental Use** — for patents, use solely for experimental purposes is non-infringing.
4. **Exhaustion of Rights** — once a product is sold by the proprietor, the rights are "exhausted" (parallel imports defense, *Kirby v. Motorola* doctrine).
5. **Bona fide Description** — use of one's own name or geographical description in good faith.
6. **Government Use** — use by the Government under Section 47 of the Patents Act.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Working Through an Effect of Registration Problem

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is **legal-conceptual** rather than mathematical, the "derivations" below take the form of **exhaustive legal reasoning chains**. KTU examiners award marks for *structured, citation-backed legal logic*.

## 3.1 The Legal Logic Chain — How a Right Becomes Enforceable

### Stage 1: Acquisition of the IP
The IP is created by the act of invention (patent), use in commerce (trademark), creation of the work (copyright), or production in the GI region (GI).

### Stage 2: Application to the Registry
The applicant files a prescribed application with fees, specifications, and claims. (Patent) or logo + class (Trademark).

### Stage 3: Examination
The Registry examines for novelty (patent), distinctiveness (TM), novelty and originality (design), or geographical origin (GI).

### Stage 4: Acceptance / Grant
The Registrar:
- For **Trademarks:** Publishes in the Trade Marks Journal → 4-month opposition window → Registration.
- For **Patents:** Examination Report → Reply by applicant → Hearing → Grant → Publication.
- For **Designs:** Examination → Acceptance → Registration.
- For **GIs:** Examination → Publication in GI Journal → Opposition → Registration.

### Stage 5: Entry on the Register
This is the **constitutive moment** — registration in the public register.

### Stage 6: Statutory Effects Flow
- **Section 28 TM Act:** Exclusive right to use arises.
- **Section 48 Patents Act:** Right to make, use, sell, offer for sale, import arises.
- **Section 11 Designs Act:** Right to apply design to articles arises.
- **Section 20 GI Act:** Right to use the GI name in commerce arises.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU 14-Mark Style Question

**Hypothetical:** *Mr. A invents a novel water-purification device in 2020. He files a patent application in 2021, and the patent is granted in 2024. In 2025, Mr. B starts manufacturing and selling an identical device without permission. Advise Mr. A on his rights and remedies.*

### Step-by-Step Legal Analysis:

**Step 1: Identify the IP Right**
The IP is a **patent** for a water-purification device (a *product* patent). The governing law is the **Patents Act, 1970**.

**Step 2: Confirm the Patent is in Force**
- Patent was granted in 2024.
- Term: **20 years** from the date of filing (Section 53(1)).
- Filing date: 2021 → Patent expires in 2041.
- Conclusion: Patent is **in force** in 2025.

**Step 3: Identify the Rights Conferred by Registration**
Under **Section 48(a) Patents Act, 1970**, the patentee has the exclusive right to:
- **Make** the product
- **Use** the product
- **Sell** the product
- **Offer for sale** the product
- **Import** the product

**Step 4: Determine if Infringement Has Occurred**
Mr. B is *manufacturing* and *selling* an identical device **without Mr. A's consent**.
→ This falls squarely within Section 48(a) and constitutes **infringement** under **Section 104 of the Patents Act**.

**Step 5: Identify the Available Remedies**

*Civil Remedies (Section 108 of the Patents Act):*
- **Relief in suits for infringement:** Injunction, damages or account of profits, delivery up of infringing products.
- **Order of the court for seizure of goods** likely to be imported (Section 114).
- **Anton Piller / John Doe Order** to prevent destruction of evidence.

*Criminal Remedies:*
- The Patents Act **does not provide for criminal prosecution** for patent infringement. Mr. A must pursue civil remedies.

*Administrative Remedies:*
- File a complaint with the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks** for intervention, though the Controller's jurisdiction is limited.

**Step 6: Defenses Mr. B May Raise**
- **Prior Publication (Section 64):** If Mr. B can prove the invention was published before the priority date, he can seek revocation.
- **Working Defense (Section 83–84):** Only relevant for compulsory licensing, not as a defense to infringement.
- **Experimental Use (Section 47):** If Mr. B's use is for educational or experimental purposes only, it is non-infringing. However, *commercial sale* defeats this defense.

**Step 7: Conclusion and Advice**
Mr. A should:
1. File a **civil suit for infringement** in the **District Court** (where the defendant resides or where the infringement occurred).
2. Seek a **temporary/permanent injunction** to stop Mr. B's sales.
3. Claim **damages** (lump sum or assessed) **or account of profits**.
4. Seek an **Anton Piller order** to preserve evidence.
5. Apply for an **ex parte interim injunction** pending suit, if the infringement is blatant and continuing.

## 3.3 Quantitative Logic — Compulsory Licensing under Section 84

A registered patent confers exclusive rights — but the law permits revocation of those rights under specific, mathematically testable conditions.

$$
T_{reversion} = T_{grant} + 3 \text{ years}
$$

After this $3$-year period, **any person interested** may apply for a compulsory license if:
1. The reasonable requirements of the public with respect to the patented invention have not been satisfied, **OR**
2. The patented invention is not available to the public at a reasonably affordable price, **OR**
3. The patented invention is not worked in the territory of India.

The **reasonable requirements of the public** are deemed not satisfied unless:
- The patented invention is being worked to the fullest extent that is reasonably practicable.
- The patent proprietor is manufacturing the invention in India (or granting licenses on reasonable terms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Application:** If a patent was granted on **01-Jan-2024**, a compulsory license application can be filed on or after **02-Jan-2027**. This is a frequently tested 3-mark question.

## 3.4 Section-by-Section Infringement Logic

| Stage | Statutory Section | Effect |
|-------|------------------|--------|
| 1 | Application filed | No right conferred (only "patent pending" status) |
| 2 | Application published (Sec 11A) | Provisional rights; damages can be claimed from publication date, *if* patent is eventually granted |
| 3 | Examination request | No new right |
| 4 | Grant of patent | Exclusive rights under Section 48 arise *retrospectively* from the date of publication of the application |
| 5 | Post-grant opposition (Sec 25(2)) | 12-month window; rights are enforceable but defeasible |
| 6 | Renewal every year | Non-payment leads to lapse |
| 7 | Expiry at 20 years | Rights revert to public domain |

$$
\text{Retroactive Effect Date} = \text{Date of Publication of Application}
$$

$$
\text{Statutory Damages Period} = [\text{Retroactive Date}, \text{Date of Grant}]
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the effect of registration of a trademark under the Trade Marks Act, 1999?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under **Section 28 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**, the registration of a trademark confers upon the registered proprietor the **exclusive right to use the mark** in relation to the goods or services for which it is registered and to **obtain relief in respect of infringement** of the trademark. The certificate of registration issued by the Registrar is **prima facie evidence** of the validity of the registration. Importantly, **Section 27(1)** provides that no person is entitled to institute proceedings to prevent infringement of an *unregistered* trademark — this means registration is **mandatory** for statutory action. However, **Section 27(2)** preserves the common law remedy of *passing off*, which is available even for unregistered marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write that registration "creates" the right. **Correction:** Registration does not create the right — it *constitutes* and *evidences* the right for statutory enforcement purposes. Marks off **0.5–1 mark** for this error.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State the exclusive rights conferred on a patentee under Section 48 of the Patents Act, 1970.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under **Section 48 of the Patents Act, 1970**, where a patent is granted, the patentee has the exclusive right to:
1. **Make** the patented product,
2. **Use** the patented product or process,
3. **Sell** the patented product,
4. **Offer for sale** the patented product, and
5. **Import** the patented product.

These rights are collectively called the "**Five Pillars of Patent Exclusivity**." For a *process patent*, the patentee additionally has the right to use, sell, offer for sale, and import the **product obtained directly by the patented process** (Section 48(b)).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often miss "Offer for Sale" or "Import." **Correction:** Both are independent heads of infringement. Missing one costs **0.5–1 mark**.

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Adapted]** *Discuss in detail the civil and criminal remedies available for infringement of a registered trademark under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. Also, explain the defenses available to an alleged infringer.*

#### Part (a) — Civil and Criminal Remedies [7 marks]

**Step 1: Civil Remedies under the Trade Marks Act, 1999**

Under **Section 134** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, the reliefs available in a suit for infringement of a registered trademark include:
1. **Injunction** — temporary, interlocutory, or permanent; restraining the defendant from using the infringing mark.
2. **Damages** — compensatory damages to compensate the plaintiff for loss suffered.
3. **Account of Profits** — an alternative to damages, requiring the infringer to surrender profits earned from the infringement.
4. **Delivery up of Infringing Goods** — the court may order the destruction or delivery up of infringing labels, marks, or articles.
5. **Anton Piller Order** — a civil search order permitting the plaintiff to enter the defendant's premises to preserve evidence.
6. **Mareva Injunction** — to prevent the defendant from disposing of assets.

**Step 2: Criminal Remedies**

Under **Section 103 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**, any person who:
- Falsely applies a registered trademark,
- Falsely describes goods or services as being those of another, or
- Falsely applies a false trade description,

is punishable with:
- **Imprisonment** of not less than **6 months, extendable to 3 years**, AND
- **Fine** of not less than ₹50,000, extendable to ₹2,00,000.

The offense is **cognizable, bailable, and non-compoundable** (although the Trademark Rules allow compounding in certain cases).

#### Part (b) — Defenses Available to the Alleged Infringer [7 marks]

**Step 1: Statutory Defenses under the Trade Marks Act, 1999**

1. **Section 30(1): Bona fide description** — use of one's own name, the name of a predecessor, or the geographical place of business, in good faith, is a valid defense.
2. **Section 30(2): Continuous prior use** — continuous use of a similar mark from a date prior to the use of the registered mark by the registered proprietor, in the same locality, is a defense.
3. **Section 35:** A registered trademark is not infringed by use of any matter likely to be taken as a sign of the kind, quality, quantity, intended purpose, value, geographical origin, etc.
4. **Section 31(1): Rectification / Cancellation** — the defendant may file a rectification application (Section 47) challenging the registration itself on grounds of non-distinctiveness, fraud, or prior use.

**Step 2: Common Law Defenses**

1. **Passing-off defense** — the defendant can argue that the plaintiff's mark lacks goodwill in the defendant's market.
2. **Acquiescence / Laches** — if the plaintiff delayed in taking action, the court may refuse relief.
3. **Estoppel** — the plaintiff cannot deny a representation on which the defendant relied.
4. **Parallel Imports / Exhaustion** — once the proprietor (or licensee) sells goods in India, the rights are "exhausted," and the buyer can resell under Section 30(3).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse trademark remedies with patent remedies.** Patent infringement has **no criminal sanction** in India.
> 2. **Damages vs. Account of Profits:** They are *alternatives*, not cumulative. The plaintiff must elect one.
> 3. **Section 27(2) preservation:** The "passing off" remedy survives even for unregistered marks — a common omission.
> 4. **The "10 km radius rule" of Section 30(2):** Prior use defense applies only in the *same locality*, not nationwide.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Adapted]** *Explain the effect of registration under the Patents Act, 1970. Discuss the rights conferred on a patentee and the grounds on which a patent can be revoked.*

#### Part (a) — Effect of Registration and Rights Conferred [7 marks]

**Step 1: Registration Procedure**
- Filing of application under **Section 7** (provisional + complete specification).
- Publication of application under **Section 11A** after 18 months.
- Examination on request under **Section 12**.
- Grant of patent under **Section 43** in the **Patent Journal**.

**Step 2: Effects under Section 48**
As discussed in Part A's Question 1 model answer above, the patentee gets the "**Five Pillars**" of exclusivity:
1. Make
2. Use
3. Sell
4. Offer for Sale
5. Import

These rights are **personal property** (Section 51) and may be assigned, mortgaged, or licensed.

**Step 3: Term of the Patent**
Under **Section 53(1)**, the term is **20 years** from the **date of filing** of the application. The patent must be renewed annually (Section 53(3)).

**Step 4: Relief for Infringement (Section 108)**
- Injunction
- Damages or account of profits
- Order for seizure (Section 114)
- Declaration of non-infringement (Section 105)

#### Part (b) — Grounds for Revocation of a Patent [7 marks]

Under **Section 64 of the Patents Act, 1970**, the following are grounds for revocation:

1. **Section 64(1)(a): Lack of novelty** — the invention was published or used in India before the priority date.
2. **Section 64(1)(b): Lack of inventive step** — the invention would be obvious to a person skilled in the art.
3. **Section 64(1)(c): Not capable of industrial application.**
4. **Section 64(1)(d): Insufficient disclosure** — the complete specification does not sufficiently describe the invention.
5. **Section 64(1)(e): Wrongful obtaining** — the patent was obtained by fraud, false suggestion, or misrepresentation.
6. **Section 64(1)(f): Non-patentable subject matter** — the invention falls within Section 3 or Section 4 of the Act (e.g., atomic energy, frivolous inventions).
7. **Section 64(1)(g): Secret use** — the invention was secretly used in India prior to the priority date.
8. **Section 64(1)(h): Prior claiming** — the invention was already claimed in a valid claim of a prior Indian application.
9. **Section 64(1)(i): Non-working in India** — failure to work the patent on a commercial scale without valid reason.
10. **Section 64(1)(k): Double patenting.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Revocation is a "shield and sword":** It is both a defense in an infringement suit (Section 64(2)) and a separate action before the Intellectual Property Appellate Board (now Commercial Courts).
> 2. **Time limit:** Revocation on certain grounds (e.g., 64(1)(d) and (e)) has a 12-month limitation period from grant.
> 3. **Partial Revocation:** A patent can be partially revoked — one bad claim does not invalidate the entire patent.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Exam-Final High-Density Revision Notes — Effect of Registration**

- ☐ **Trademark Registration → Statutory Monopoly** (Section 28, TM Act, 1999). Term: **10 years** renewable.
- ☐ **Patent Registration → Five Pillars of Exclusivity** (Section 48, Patents Act, 1970). Term: **20 years**, non-renewable.
- ☐ **Design Registration → Right to Apply to Articles** (Section 11, Designs Act, 2000). Term: **10 + 5 = 15 years**.
- ☐ **GI Registration → Collective Right of Authorized Users** (Section 20, GI Act, 1999). Term: **10 years** renewable.
- ☐ **Copyright Registration → Voluntary, prima facie evidence only** (Section 48, Copyright Act, 1957). Term: **Life + 60 years**.
- ☐ **Common Law Remedies** (Passing Off) survive for *unregistered* marks under Section 27(2), TM Act.
- ☐ **Patent = Civil remedies only.** TM, Design, GI, Copyright = Civil + Criminal.
- ☐ **Compulsory Licensing** can be sought **3 years** after grant (Section 84, Patents Act).
- ☐ **Defenses** — Prior use, bona fide description, exhaustion, experimental use, invalidity of registration.
- ☐ **Burden of Proof** shifts to challenger after registration.
- ☐ **Damages or Account of Profits** — plaintiff must **elect**, not take both.
- ☐ **Section 11A Patents Act** — publication of application creates **provisional rights**.
- ☐ **Civil Court Jurisdiction:** District Court (where defendant resides) for TM, Copyright, Design; Commercial Court / High Court for patents.
- ☐ **Customs Recordal:** Registration enables border seizure under the **Intellectual Property Rights (Imported Goods) Enforcement Rules, 2007**.
- ☐ **Examiner's 5-Mark Mnemonic** — *"**MUSIC**I*" — **M**ake, **U**se, **S**ell, **I**mport, plus **O**ffer for sale (Section 48, Patents Act).

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — EFFECT OF REGISTRATION**
