<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Infringement of Intellectual Property Rights

## 1.1 Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Alignment):**
> *Infringement* is the unauthorized use, reproduction, distribution, performance, display, or sale of a protected intellectual property (IP) asset — covered under Copyright, Patent, Trademark, Design, or Trade Secret law — without the prior permission (license) of the rights holder, thereby violating one or more of the exclusive economic and moral rights granted to the creator/owner by statute.

In simple terms, infringement is the **legal violation of an IP owner's exclusive bundle of rights**. When a creator (inventor, author, brand owner, designer) legally secures an IP right, the government grants them a temporary **monopoly** over the use of that creation. Any third party who steps into that monopoly zone without a license commits infringement.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Fenced Garden"

Imagine a homeowner builds a beautiful, fenced garden (the IP right). The fence is the **statutory right** granted by the government. The owner can invite guests (licensees) through the gate. If a stranger jumps the fence, picks flowers, and sells them at the market — that stranger has **trespassed** on the protected property. In legal language, that trespass is called **infringement**.

- The **fence height** = the scope of protection (claims in a patent, claims in a copyright, registered trademark classes, etc.)
- The **gate** = license/permission
- The **trespasser** = infringer
- The **penalty** = damages, injunction, account of profits, or even criminal prosecution

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Infringement is a High-Weightage KTU Topic:**
> Infringement is the *enforcement arm* of every IP right. Without it, registration is meaningless. KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests: (1) what constitutes infringement, (2) defences available, and (3) remedies the aggrieved party can claim.

### 1.2 Categories of IP Infringement (At a Glance)

| IP Right | What is Infringed? | Key Statutory Reference (India) |
|---|---|---|
| **Copyright** | Reproduction, communication, adaptation of literary/artistic/musical/cinematographic works | Copyright Act, **1957** |
| **Patent** | Making, using, selling, offering for sale, importing the patented invention without consent | Patents Act, **1970** |
| **Trademark** | Use of identical/similar mark in respect of identical/similar goods/services causing confusion | Trade Marks Act, **1999** |
| **Design** | Applying fraudulent imitation of a registered design to any article | Designs Act, **2000** |
| **Geographical Indication** | Misuse of a registered GI on goods not originating from the indicated territory | GI of Goods Act, **1999** |

### 1.3 The Statutory Foundation — Section-Wise Anchors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Infringement Action:**
> Every infringement action in Indian IP law rests on three questions:
> 1. **Existence** — Does a valid IP right subsist?
> 2. **Violation** — Has the defendant performed an act reserved exclusively to the rights holder?
> 3. **Authorization** — Did the defendant have the rights holder's consent (express or implied)?
> If the answer to (1) and (2) is *Yes* and (3) is *No*, infringement is made out.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Anatomy of Infringement — A Universal Test

Although the specific tests differ per IP type, the **universal analytical structure** for proving infringement consists of **four cumulative elements**. Every KTU answer on this topic should explicitly state these.

1. **Valid Subsisting Right** — The plaintiff must own a valid, enforceable IP right at the time of the alleged act.
2. **Causal Act by the Defendant** — The defendant must have performed one of the *acts exclusively reserved* to the rights holder.
3. **No Authorization** — There must be no license, assignment, or statutory exception covering the defendant's act.
4. **Territorial & Temporal Nexus** — The infringing act must have occurred within the territory and duration of protection.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Exclusive Rights" Rule:**
> The list of infringing acts is *closed* — meaning infringement can occur only if the defendant's act falls within the statutory list of reserved rights. Anything outside the list (e.g., independent creation in patents) is **not** infringement.

## 2.2 Infringement of Copyright (Sections 51–62, Copyright Act 1957)

### 2.2.1 The Copyright Infringement Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 51 of the Copyright Act, 1957** lists the acts that constitute copyright infringement:
> - Reproducing the work in any material form (including storing in electronic memory)
> - Issuing copies to the public (distribution right)
> - Performing the work in public
> - Communicating the work to the public (broadcasting/streaming)
> - Making any translation, adaptation, or arrangement
> - Selling, hiring, or commercial dealing in infringing copies

### 2.2.2 The "Substantial Similarity" Test

Indian courts (following the UK and US tradition) apply the **Substantial Similarity Test**:

$$
\text{Infringement} \iff \big(\text{Access to the plaintiff's work}\big) \;\land\; \big(\text{Substantial Similarity in protectable expression}\big)
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

- **Step 1 — Access:** The defendant had a *reasonable opportunity* to access the original work (e.g., widely published, publicly performed, or copied from).
- **Step 2 — Substantial Similarity:** A *lay observer* (ordinary, impartial viewer/reader) would consider the defendant's work substantially similar to the plaintiff's protectable expression.
- **Step 3 — Idea vs. Expression Dichotomy:** Only the **expression** of an idea is protected, not the idea itself. Two works can share the same idea but be protected differently if the expression is independently created.

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Copyright Infringement

| Element | Legal Test | Burden on | Key Precedent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Valid copyright subsists | Ownership + originality + fixation | Plaintiff | *Eastern Book Co. v. D.B. Modak* (2008) |
| Copying occurred | Access + Substantial Similarity | Plaintiff (rebuttable presumption) | *R.G. Anand v. M/s Delux Films* (1978) |
| Idea–Expression dichotomy | Only expression is protected | Defendant (defence) | *Baker v. Selden* (US doctrine adopted) |
| Fair dealing exception | Section 52 carve-outs | Defendant | *Civic Chandran v. Ammini Amma* (1996) |

## 2.3 Infringement of Patents (Sections 104–114, Patents Act 1970)

### 2.3.1 The Patent Infringement Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 48 of the Patents Act, 1970** grants the patentee the exclusive right to:
> - **Make** the patented product
> - **Use** the patented product
> - **Sell** or offer for sale the patented product
> - **Import** the patented product
> - And where the patent is for a **process**, the right to **use** or **offer for sale** the product obtained directly by that process
> Any third party performing any of these acts **without consent** infringes the patent.

### 2.3.2 The Doctrine of Equivalents (DoE)

Literal claim interpretation is rarely sufficient in modern patent law. Courts apply the **Doctrine of Equivalents**:

$$
\text{Equivalence exists} \iff \big(\text{Same function}\big) \lor \big(\text{Same way}\big) \lor \big(\text{Same result}\big)
$$

*(Function–Way–Result Test — also called the "Triple Identity Test")*

In India, the DoE is applied through the judicial precedent of *F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd. v. Cipla Ltd.* (delhi HC, 2008), where the court held that infringement may exist even if the accused product does not literally fall within the claim language, but performs substantially the same function in substantially the same way to obtain substantially the same result.

### 2.3.3 Direct vs. Indirect Infringement

| Type | Definition | Liability |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Infringement** | Defendant personally performs a reserved act | Strict liability — no intent required |
| **Indirect (Contributory) Infringement** | Defendant supplies a component/know-how that the user combines to infringe | Knowledge-based liability |
| **Induced Infringement** | Defendant actively induces another to infringe | Knowledge + intent required |

### 2.3.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Patent Infringement

| Element | Legal Test | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Valid patent subsists | Granted + in force + renewal fees paid | Revocation is a defence |
| Claim construction | Claims interpreted through *specification + drawings* | Purposive rule (UK tradition) |
| Infringing act performed | Any act under **Section 48** without consent | Strict liability |
| Equivalence | Function-Way-Result Test | Applied to prevent design-around |
| Defences | Section 107 — innocent infringement, experimental use, Bolar exemption | Defendant's burden |

## 2.4 Infringement of Trademarks (Sections 29–30, Trade Marks Act 1999)

### 2.4.1 The Trademark Infringement Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 29** declares a mark infringed when a person uses, in the course of trade, a mark that is **identical with or deceptively similar to** a registered trademark, in relation to identical or similar goods or services, where such use is likely to cause confusion or deception.

### 2.4.2 The "Likelihood of Confusion" Test

$$
\text{Trademark Infringement} \iff \big(\text{Identical/Similar mark}\big) \;\land\; \big(\text{Identical/Similar goods}\big) \;\land\; \big(\text{Likelihood of confusion}\big)
$$

**Global Factors Considered by Courts:**

- Similarity of the **marks** (visual, phonetic, conceptual)
- Similarity of the **goods/services**
- Strength of the registered mark's reputation
- Evidence of **actual confusion** in the market
- Channels of trade and class of purchasers
- Defendant's intent (goodwill/dilution)

### 2.4.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Trademark Infringement

| Factor | Assessment | Indian Case Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Phonetic similarity | Pronounced similarly → confusion likely | *S.M. Dyechem Ltd. v. Cadbury (India) Ltd.* (2000) |
| Visual similarity | Look-alike marks | *Amritdhara Pharmacy v. Satya Deo Gupta* (1963) |
| Conceptual similarity | Same idea or meaning conveyed | *Laxmikant Vyas v. Chandramohan* (1968) |
| Class of goods | Identical or allied class | Schedule 4 (Nice Classification) |
| Customer profile | Ordinary consumer of average intelligence | *Whirlpool v. NRD Industries* (2014) |

## 2.5 Defences Against Infringement (Universal & Specific)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always pair an infringement question with a defence question. Examiners award marks for recognizing that even proven infringement may be excused by a valid statutory defence.

### 2.5.1 Common Defences Across IP Types

| Defence | Applicable to | Essence |
|---|---|---|
| **Fair Use / Fair Dealing** | Copyright | Use for criticism, review, news reporting, education, research |
| **Prior User Right** | Patent | Person using the invention before priority date |
| **Honest Concurrent Use** | Trademark | Continuous, honest use before registration |
| **Independent Creation** | Copyright, Patent | Work/invention created independently without copying |
| **Statutory Exception** | All | Research, experimentation, private use, de minimis |
| **Exhaustion of Rights (First Sale)** | Copyright, Trademark | Rights exhausted after first lawful sale |
| **Parody / Criticism** | Copyright, Trademark | Transformative use for commentary |
| **Laches / Acquiescence** | All | Delay in bringing suit defeats equitable relief |

### 2.5.2 Section 52 of the Copyright Act — The Fair Dealing Charter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Nine Statutory Fair Dealing Exceptions:**
> 1. Private or personal use (including research)
> 2. Criticism or review (with attribution)
> 3. Reporting of current events (with attribution)
> 4. Judicial proceedings or legal advice
> 5. Library, archive, museum preservation
> 6. Educational use in non-profit institutions
> 7. Sound recording for bona fide purposes
> 8. Reading/literacy in public for non-commercial use
> 9. Reproduction for the visually/print disabled

## 2.6 Remedies for Infringement

### 2.6.1 Civil Remedies

| Remedy | Nature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Interlocutory Injunction** | Temporary | Maintain *status quo* during trial |
| **Permanent Injunction** | Final | Stop future infringing acts |
| **Damages** | Monetary | Compensate actual loss |
| **Account of Profits** | Monetary | Surrender of infringer's profit |
| **Anton Piller Order** | Pre-trial | Surprise search of infringer's premises |
| **Mareva Injunction** | Pre-trial | Freeze infringer's assets |
| **Delivery Up / Destruction** | In rem | Seize infringing goods |

### 2.6.2 Criminal Remedies

- **Copyright Act:** Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹2,00,000** (repeat offences: up to **6 years** + fine up to **₹5,00,000**)
- **Trademarks Act:** Imprisonment up to **3 years** + fine up to **₹2,00,000**
- **Patents Act:** No criminal remedy — only civil

### 2.6.3 Custom Remedies (Border Enforcement)

The **Customs Act, 1962** (read with IP Rules) empowers the Customs Commissioner to **suspend clearance** and **seize infringing imports/exports** at Indian ports.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — How to Solve an Infringement Problem

## 3.1 The Master Infringement Analysis Framework

This is the *only* analytical scaffold you need for any KTU infringement question, regardless of IP type.

### Step 1 — Identify the IP Right Subsisting

Determine *which* statutory right is being asserted:

$$
\text{Identify the IP Right} = \begin{cases} \text{Copyright} & \text{if the asset is a literary/artistic/musical work} \\ \text{Patent} & \text{if the asset is a functional invention} \\ \text{Trademark} & \text{if the asset is a brand identifier} \\ \text{Design} & \text{if the asset is an ornamental/aesthetic shape} \end{cases}
$$

**Reasoning logic:** Each IP has a distinct statutory regime. Mis-classifying the asset leads to applying the wrong test and losing marks.

### Step 2 — Locate the Reserved Rights

For each IP type, identify the *closed list* of acts reserved to the rights holder:

| IP Type | Reserved Acts (Statutory Anchor) |
|---|---|
| Copyright | Reproduce, distribute, perform, communicate, adapt — **Section 14** |
| Patent | Make, use, sell, offer for sale, import — **Section 48** |
| Trademark | Use of identical/similar mark on identical/similar goods — **Section 29** |
| Design | Apply, import, sell fraudulent imitation — **Section 22** |

### Step 3 — Match the Defendant's Act to the Reserved List

$$
\text{Is defendant's act} \in \text{Reserved Acts?} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{Potential Infringement}
$$

$$
\text{Is defendant's act} \notin \text{Reserved Acts?} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{No Infringement}
$$

### Step 4 — Check for Authorization

If the act is reserved, ask: **Did the rights holder grant permission?**

$$
\text{Infringement Confirmed} = (\text{Reserved Act}) \land \lnot (\text{Authorization})
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Defences

Even if infringement is technically made out, examine statutory defences:

| Defences for Copyright | Defences for Patent | Defences for Trademark |
|---|---|---|
| Fair dealing (Sec 52) | Prior user (Sec 11A) | Honest concurrent use |
| Independent creation | Bolar exemption | Descriptive fair use |
| First-sale exhaustion | Experimental use | Parody/nominative use |
| Statute of limitations | Invalidation of patent | Acquiescence |

### Step 6 — Determine Remedies

If defences fail, catalogue the remedies:

$$
\text{Remedies Available} = \{\text{Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits, Delivery Up, Criminal Prosecution}\}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — A Copyright Infringement Problem

**Scenario (from a typical KTU paper):**

> *Rohit writes a novel "Midnight Echo" and registers copyright. Without permission, a film company "Starflix Productions" makes a web series with the same plot, character names, and several identical dialogue sequences. They argue they used only the "idea" of a haunted mansion mystery. Rohit sues.*

**Applying the Analytical Framework:**

**Step 1:** The IP right is **Copyright** in a literary work (novel).

**Step 2:** Reserved acts under **Section 14** include *reproduction*, *adaptation*, and *communication to the public*. A web series based on a novel is a *dramatic adaptation* and *communication to the public*.

**Step 3:** The film company's act of *adapting the novel into a web series* falls squarely within the reserved list — specifically the *right to make a cinematograph film or sound recording of the literary work* and *to communicate the cinematograph film to the public*.

**Step 4:** No license or consent was obtained from Rohit. Therefore:

$$
(\text{Reserved Act} = \text{True}) \land (\text{Authorization} = \text{False}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Infringement Confirmed}
$$

**Step 5:** Starflix raises the *Idea-Expression Dichotomy* defence — claiming only the "idea" of a haunted mansion was borrowed. Counter-analysis:

- The **plot details**, **character names**, and **identical dialogue sequences** are *expression*, not *idea*.
- Under *R.G. Anand v. Delux Films* (1978), substantial similarity in protectable expression overrides the idea-expression defence.

**Step 6:** Remedies available to Rohit:

- Permanent injunction to stop streaming
- Damages calculated by **actual loss + statutory damages**
- Account of Starflix's profits from the series
- Delivery up of infringing copies

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Reasoning Tip:** Always demonstrate the **dual-arm test** — (1) Plaintiff owns the right, (2) Defendant violated the right without authorization. Phrases like *"the defendant's act falls within the closed class of Section 14"* earn full marks.

## 3.3 Worked Example — A Patent Infringement Problem

**Scenario:**

> *Pharma Inc. holds a patented process for synthesizing Drug-X. Generic Co. manufactures Drug-X using a *modified* process that achieves the same end-product by substituting one reagent. Pharma Inc. sues for patent infringement.*

**Step-by-step Application:**

**Step 1:** IP right is **Patent** (process patent under Section 48).

**Step 2:** Under Section 48, a process patent grants exclusive right to **use the process** and to **use/sell/offer for sale the product obtained directly by that process**.

**Step 3:** Generic Co.'s substituted process:

- Achieves the *same final product* (Drug-X)
- Uses a *different reagent* but same underlying chemistry
- Therefore, the *product obtained directly* by the modified process is the same as the patented product

**Step 4:** No license. Hence the act of *selling the product* (which is "obtained directly by that process") is reserved.

**Step 5:** Apply the **Doctrine of Equivalents** (Function-Way-Result Test):

$$
\text{Function: Synthesise Drug-X} \;\;\text{Same} \;\;\text{Result: Same molecular compound}
$$

Even if *Way* (the reagent) differs, the Function and Result identity triggers equivalence.

**Defence Rebuttal (Generic Co.):** Generic Co. might invoke **Section 107(b)** — *Bolar exemption* — if the acts were done *solely for regulatory approval*. Otherwise, infringement stands.

**Step 6:** Remedies — injunction, damages or account of profits, delivery up of infringing stock.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Infringement Decision Tree

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the universal analytical pathway for determining whether infringement has occurred. It captures the decision logic from the framework in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify the IP Right Subsisting] --> B[Is the IP Right Valid and In Force?]
    B -- No --> Z1[No Infringement - Right Does Not Subsist]
    B -- Yes --> C[List the Reserved Acts for that IP Type]
    C --> D[Did the Defendant Perform a Reserved Act?]
    D -- No --> Z2[No Infringement - Act Outside Reserved List]
    D -- Yes --> E[Did the Plaintiff Authorize the Act?]
    E -- Yes --> Z3[No Infringement - Authorized Use]
    E -- No --> F[Does the Defendant Raise a Statutory Defence?]
    F -- Yes, Valid Defence --> Z4[No Infringement - Defence Succeeds]
    F -- No --> G[Infringement Confirmed]
    G --> H[Determine Available Remedies]
    H --> H1[Civil Remedies]
    H --> H2[Criminal Remedies]
    H --> H3[Custom Remedies]
    H1 --> H1a[Injunction]
    H1 --> H1b[Damages]
    H1 --> H1c[Account of Profits]
    H2 --> H2a[Imprisonment]
    H2 --> H2b[Fines]
    H3 --> H3a[Border Seizure]
    H3 --> H3b[Confiscation]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Block — Infringement Tests Across IP Types

This diagram compares the infringement test for each major IP type side by side, highlighting the statutory anchor and the key judicial test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IP1[Copyright Infringement]
        C1[Statutory Anchor Section 14 and 51] --> C2[Test Substantial Similarity]
        C2 --> C3[Burden on Plaintiff]
        C3 --> C4[Defence Fair Dealing Section 52]
    end
    subgraph IP2[Patent Infringement]
        P1[Statutory Anchor Section 48] --> P2[Test Literal Plus Equivalents]
        P2 --> P3[Burden on Plaintiff]
        P3 --> P4[Defence Section 107]
    end
    subgraph IP3[Trademark Infringement]
        T1[Statutory Anchor Section 29] --> T2[Test Likelihood of Confusion]
        T2 --> T3[Burden on Plaintiff]
        T3 --> T4[Defence Honest Concurrent Use]
    end
    subgraph IP4[Design Infringement]
        D1[Statutory Anchor Section 22] --> D2[Test Fraudulent Imitation]
        D2 --> D3[Burden on Plaintiff]
        D3 --> D4[Defence Prior Publication]
    end
```

## 4.3 Remedies Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following block diagram depicts the *remedies ecosystem* and how civil, criminal, and administrative remedies interlock to enforce an IP right.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Plaintiff Establishes Infringement]
        A1[Proves Ownership of Valid IP]
        A2[Proves Defendant Performed Reserved Act]
        A3[Proves No Authorization]
    end
    subgraph B[Plaintiff Elects Remedy]
        B1[Civil Track]
        B2[Criminal Track]
        B3[Administrative Track]
    end
    subgraph C1[Civil Track]
        C1a[Interlocutory Injunction]
        C1b[Permanent Injunction]
        C1c[Damages or Account of Profits]
        C1d[Delivery Up or Destruction]
    end
    subgraph C2[Criminal Track]
        C2a[FIR and Prosecution]
        C2b[Imprisonment and Fine]
    end
    subgraph C3[Administrative Track]
        C3a[Customs Border Seizure]
        C3b[Domain Name Dispute Resolution]
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C1
    B --> C2
    B --> C3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define infringement of intellectual property rights. List any four statutory remedies available to the aggrieved rights holder.**

**Model Answer:**
Infringement of IP rights is the unauthorized use, reproduction, distribution, or sale of a protected IP asset (patent, copyright, trademark, design) in violation of the exclusive rights granted by statute to the owner.
**Four remedies:**
1. **Injunction** (temporary or permanent) to restrain further infringement.
2. **Damages** to compensate the rights holder for actual loss.
3. **Account of profits** to disgorge the infringer's unjust gains.
4. **Delivery up / destruction** of infringing goods.
*(Acceptable 5th remedy: criminal prosecution with imprisonment and fine.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between "direct infringement" and "indirect (contributory) infringement" in patent law with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Direct Infringement | Indirect Infringement |
|---|---|---|
| Who acts | The infringer personally performs a reserved act | A third party supplies a component or know-how that another uses to infringe |
| Liability | Strict liability — no intent required | Knowledge-based liability |
| Example | Company A manufactures the patented product without consent | Company B supplies a key component knowing it will be assembled into the patented product |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | ESE Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the essential elements that the plaintiff must establish to prove infringement of a registered patent under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Cite the relevant statutory provisions.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the Doctrine of Equivalents and its application by Indian courts. How does it expand the scope of patent protection beyond literal claim language?*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Existence of a valid patent:** The plaintiff must prove ownership of a patent that is currently in force, granted by the Indian Patent Office, with all renewal fees paid up to date. *Statutory Anchor: Section 48 of the Patents Act, 1970.*

**Step 2 — Performance of a reserved act:** The plaintiff must show that the defendant has, without consent, performed any of the following acts enumerated under Section 48:
- **Making** the patented product
- **Using** the patented product
- **Selling or offering for sale** the patented product
- **Importing** the patented product
- For a process patent, **using or selling the product obtained directly** by the patented process

**Step 3 — Absence of authorization:** The plaintiff must demonstrate that no express or implied license, assignment, or compulsory licence covers the defendant's act.

**Step 4 — Territorial and temporal nexus:** The act must have occurred in India during the patent's term (20 years from filing).

**Step 5 — No statutory defence applies:** The plaintiff should anticipate defences such as *prior user right*, *Bolar exemption*, or *experimental use* under Section 107, and rebut them in evidence.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **Doctrine of Equivalents (DoE)** is a judicial principle that prevents an infringer from escaping liability by making insubstantial modifications to a patented invention.

**Triple Identity Test (Function–Way–Result):**
A substitute element is "equivalent" if it performs substantially the *same function*, in substantially the *same way*, to achieve substantially the *same result* as the claimed element.

**Indian Application:**
In *F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd. v. Cipla Ltd.* (Delhi HC, 2008), the court applied the DoE to hold that a generic drug using a *different polymorph* of the same active ingredient still infringed the process patent because the underlying chemistry, function, and result were identical.

**Expansion of Protection:**
The DoE prevents "design-around" infringement where a defendant copies the substance of the invention while altering non-essential features. It ensures the patent's *inventive contribution* — not merely its literal language — is protected, consistent with the constitutional mandate under **Article 27** of TRIPS to provide effective patent protection.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | ESE Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Examine the test of "substantial similarity" applied by Indian courts in copyright infringement cases. How does the "idea-expression dichotomy" operate as a defence?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the defences available under Section 52 of the Copyright Act, 1957. Why is "fair dealing" considered a users' right rather than a mere exception?*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Substantial Similarity Test** is the touchstone of copyright infringement in India. It is applied in two stages:

**Stage 1 — Access:** The plaintiff must show that the defendant had a *reasonable opportunity* to access the plaintiff's work. Direct proof of copying is rare; access is inferred where the work was widely published, broadcast, or accessible to the defendant.

**Stage 2 — Substantial Similarity in Protectable Expression:** The court asks: *Would an ordinary, impartial observer, after viewing both works side by side, conclude that the defendant has substantially copied protectable expression?*

If both stages are satisfied, copying is presumed. The burden shifts to the defendant to prove *independent creation* or *coincidental similarity*.

**Idea-Expression Dichotomy:**
This is a fundamental copyright principle stating that **ideas** are not protected — only the **expression** of ideas is. Established in *Baker v. Selden* (US) and adopted in India (*R.G. Anand v. Delux Films*).

- **Idea:** The *concept* of a haunted mansion mystery
- **Expression:** The *specific plot*, character names, dialogue, and scene sequences

The defendant often argues: "I borrowed only the idea, not the expression." The court distinguishes by asking whether the *copying extends to concrete details of expression* rather than general themes. Borrowing themes, plots, or stock elements (e.g., the *scènes à faire* doctrine) is permissible; borrowing dialogue, distinctive character portrayals, or scene-by-scene adaptation is infringement.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Section 52 — Fair Dealing Exceptions:**
The Copyright Act, 1957 enumerates **nine statutory fair dealing purposes** (see Section 2.5.2 above for the complete list). For a use to qualify as "fair dealing," the following cumulative conditions must be satisfied:

1. The purpose must fall within one of the nine enumerated categories.
2. The use must be **fair** — assessed by quantum of copying, market effect, and transformative nature.
3. Sufficient **acknowledgement** must be given (for criticism, review, news reporting).

**Why Fair Dealing is a User's Right:**
Modern Indian jurisprudence treats fair dealing not as a privilege that the rights holder may *choose* to waive, but as a **constitutionally protected users' right** that flows from the right to education, freedom of speech, and access to knowledge under **Articles 19(1)(a)** and **21**. The Supreme Court in *Super Cassettes Industries v. Yahoo India Pvt. Ltd.* (2009) and the *Eastern Book Company* line of cases emphasizes that fair dealing promotes the constitutional goal of *dissemination of knowledge*. Accordingly, fair dealing must be construed *liberally* in favour of the user, while the rights holder bears the burden of proving that the use is *unfair*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse "fair use" with "fair dealing".** Indian law recognizes only **"fair dealing"** (Section 52). The US "fair use" doctrine is *not* applicable in India. Writing "fair use" is a guaranteed **−2 mark penalty**.
> 2. **Always cite the section number.** Vague references like "copyright exceptions" without **Section 52** lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mention the burden of proof.** The infringer bears the burden of establishing the defence; the plaintiff does not need to *disprove* it upfront.
> 4. **Distinguish civil from criminal remedies.** Criminal remedies exist under Copyright and Trademark Acts but **not under the Patents Act**. Many students erroneously mention imprisonment for patent infringement.
> 5. **Trademark test = "Likelihood of Confusion"** — not "identical marks." Mark similarity is assessed through *visual, phonetic, and conceptual* lenses.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Infringement Module 4**

- **Definition:** Infringement = Unauthorized exercise of any *exclusive right* reserved to the IP owner under statute.
- **Patent Infringement:** **Section 48** enumerates reserved acts; **Section 107** lists defences. The *Doctrine of Equivalents* (Function–Way–Result Test) prevents design-around.
- **Copyright Infringement:** **Section 14** (rights) + **Section 51** (acts of infringement). The *Substantial Similarity Test* (access + similarity) is the judicial standard. The *Idea-Expression Dichotomy* is the principal defence.
- **Trademark Infringement:** **Section 29** — requires *identical/similar mark* on *identical/similar goods* with *likelihood of confusion*. Tri-factor test: phonetic, visual, conceptual similarity.
- **Design Infringement:** **Section 22** — fraudulent imitation of a registered design.
- **Fair Dealing:** Always cite **Section 52**, Copyright Act. Nine statutory purposes. It is a *user's right*, construed liberally.
- **Independent Creation** is a defence across copyright and patent law.
- **Exhaustion of Rights / First Sale Doctrine:** Rights are exhausted after the first lawful sale — the owner cannot control downstream resale.
- **Custom Remedies:** Border enforcement under the **Customs Act, 1962** is a powerful administrative tool against infringing imports.
- **Burden of Proof:** Plaintiff must prove infringement; defendant must prove defences.
- **No Criminal Remedy for Patents:** Patents Act provides only civil remedies.
- **Universal Remedies Structure:** Injunction → Damages → Account of Profits → Delivery Up → (Criminal) Imprisonment + Fine.
- **Exam Keywords to Use:** "Exclusive right", "Reserved acts", "Substantial similarity", "Likelihood of confusion", "Doctrine of Equivalents", "Idea-Expression Dichotomy", "Fair dealing", "Function-Way-Result Test", "Injunction", "Damages".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assignment and Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assignment and Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Assignment of Intellectual Property?

**Assignment** is the act of transferring the **ownership** of intellectual property rights from one person (the **assignor**) to another person (the **assignee**). It is a contractual, voluntary, and complete transfer of all or specified rights, title, and interest in the IP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Legal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard)**: *"Assignment means a transfer by act of the parties of the whole or any part of the right, title, and interest in a patent, trademark, copyright, or design from the proprietor (assignor) to another person (assignee), in consideration of valuable consideration or otherwise."*

## 1.2 What is Transmission of Intellectual Property?

**Transmission** is the transfer of IP rights by **operation of law** rather than by an act of the parties. It occurs automatically through events such as death, inheritance, succession, insolvency, corporate merger, or by the execution of a will.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction**: Assignment = **Voluntary + Contractual** ; Transmission = **Involuntary / Automatic + By Law**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The House Ownership Model"

To make this crystal clear, imagine you own a beautiful villa (your IP):

| Action | Real Estate Analogy | IP Equivalent | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Selling** the villa to your friend | You sign a sale deed; your friend becomes the new owner | **Assignment** of the IP | Voluntary, Contractual, Permanent |
| **Inheritance** by your children after death | The villa automatically passes to legal heirs | **Transmission** of the IP | Automatic, By Law |
| **Renting** the villa to a tenant | You give usage rights, but retain ownership | **Licensing** of the IP | Voluntary, Contractual, Limited |

## 1.4 Core Terminology

- **Assignor** — The original IP owner who transfers the right.
- **Assignee** — The new owner who receives the right.
- **Transmission** — Non-contractual transfer (death, succession, merger, insolvency).
- **Consideration** — Valuable payment in exchange for assignment.
- **Goodwill (Trademarks)** — The reputation and trust attached to a mark; often transfers with the mark.
- **License** — Permission to use IP without transferring ownership.
- **Merger** — A corporate event by which transmission can occur automatically.
- **Devolution** — Passing of rights by inheritance or will.

## 1.5 Why Does This Matter in KTU Examinations?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Assignment & Transmission is a **high-frequency 14-mark question** in PESFT757. Students often confuse licensing with assignment — examiners award partial marks to such answers, and full marks require clearly differentiating the three with statutory backing (specific sections of the Patents Act, 1970 / Trademarks Act, 1999 / Copyright Act, 1957).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Transfer of Intellectual Property

There are **three principal modes** by which IP rights can be transferred:

### 2.1.1 Assignment (Voluntary)
- A contract between assignor and assignee.
- May be **complete** (all rights) or **partial** (some rights).
- Requires **written instrument, signatures, and consideration**.
- Must be **registered** (mandatory in some statutes to be enforceable).

### 2.1.2 Transmission (Involuntary / By Operation of Law)
- Occurs through:
  - **Inheritance** (death of the proprietor)
  - **Succession** (statutory heirs)
  - **Insolvency** (bankruptcy of proprietor)
  - **Merger / Amalgamation** (corporate law)
  - **Execution of a Will** (testamentary succession)
- Generally **does not require a fresh contract** but may require registration.
- Once registered, the receiver has the same rights as the original proprietor.

### 2.1.3 Licensing
- The proprietor grants a **right to use** the IP, retaining ownership.
- May be **exclusive, non-exclusive, or sole**.
- Governed separately under each statute (e.g., Section 30 of Copyright Act, 1957).

## 2.2 Legal Framework Across Major IP Statutes

### 2.2.1 The Patents Act, 1970

| Section | Provision | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 6** | Reliefs in suits for infringement | Allows the Court to order assignment of patent |
| **Section 44** | Form of assignment | Assignability and registration of patent rights |
| **Section 68** | Register of assignments | Maintains record of assignments and transmissions |
| **Section 69** | Registration of assignments | Assignment must be registered to be valid against third parties |
| **Section 70** | Rectification of register | Corrections to assignment records |
| **Section 78** | Co-owners of patents | Joint ownership and rights of co-owners |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight**: Under Section 6 of the Patents Act, the Court has the power to order the assignment of a patent when the defendant proves the validity of the plaintiff's earlier patent in a counter-claim. This is a **judicial assignment**.

### 2.2.2 The Trademarks Act, 1999

| Section | Provision | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 37** | Assignment and transmission of trademarks | General framework for transfer |
| **Section 38** | Power of registered user to assign | Limits assignments to associated trademarks |
| **Section 39** | Assignability of trademarks | A trademark can be assigned WITH or WITHOUT goodwill |
| **Section 40** | Assignability of trademark applications | Even pending applications can be assigned |
| **Section 41** | Form of assignment | Must be in writing, with goodwill (if used) |
| **Section 42** | Power to require assignment to be recorded | Registrar's power |
| **Section 44** | Registration of assignment | Compulsory registration to be valid |
| **Section 45** | Transmission of trademark | On death, merger, or insolvency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trademark + Goodwill**: A trademark used in relation to goods/services carries the **goodwill of the business**. As per Section 39, a trademark is assignable **only along with the goodwill** of the business concerned in such goods/services, except in cases of honest concurrent use or special circumstances.

### 2.2.3 The Copyright Act, 1957

| Section | Provision | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 18** | Assignment of copyright | Owner may assign the copyright |
| **Section 19** | Mode of assignment | Must be in writing, signed, specify rights, term, territory, consideration |
| **Section 19(3)** | Compulsory requirements | Assignment must be in **writing, signed, and specify the rights and duration** |
| **Section 30** | Licenses by owners of copyright | Licensing framework |
| **Section 19A** | Assignment of future works | Future copyright may be assigned |
| **Section 21** | Relinquishment of copyright | Owner may relinquish rights |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 19(1)** of the Copyright Act mandates that no assignment of copyright shall be valid unless it is in writing signed by the assignor or his duly authorized agent. The assignment must specify the **duration, territory, and the rights assigned**.

### 2.2.4 The Designs Act, 2000

| Section | Provision | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Section 30** | Assignment of registered designs | Design rights are assignable by written instrument |
| **Section 31** | Registration of assignment | Assignment must be registered to be valid |
| **Section 32** | Transmission of registered designs | Transmission on death, insolvency, etc. |

## 2.3 KTU Cheat Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| Feature | Assignment | Transmission | Licensing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Voluntary | Involuntary / By Law | Voluntary |
| **Mode** | Contract | Operation of law (death, merger) | Contract |
| **Ownership Transfer** | Yes (full or partial) | Yes (automatic) | No |
| **Writing Required** | Yes | May not be | Yes |
| **Registration Required** | Yes (for enforceability) | Yes (recommended) | Sometimes |
| **Consideration** | Yes | No (automatic) | Yes |
| **Reversibility** | No | No | Yes (revocable) |
| **Statutory Backing (Patents)** | Sec. 44, 68, 69 | Sec. 6, 45 | Sec. 70–71 |
| **Statutory Backing (TM)** | Sec. 37, 39, 44 | Sec. 45 | Sec. 49–50 |
| **Statutory Backing (Copyright)** | Sec. 18, 19 | Sec. 21, 19(3) | Sec. 30 |
| **Goodwill (Trademark)** | Transfers with mark | Transfers with mark | Does not transfer |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, IP assignment and transmission are the **economic backbone** of the innovation economy:

- **Tech Mergers & Acquisitions**: When a company acquires a startup, all its patents, trademarks, and copyrights are **transmitted** to the new entity by operation of corporate law.
- **University-Industry Transfer**: Researchers assign their patent rights to the university (TLO - Technology Licensing Office).
- **Brand Franchising**: Trademarks are *licensed* (not assigned) to franchisees, retaining the parent brand's ownership.
- **Estate Planning**: IP assets are included in the **will** of inventors/creators, and transmitted to heirs on death.
- **Insolvency Proceedings**: An IP owner's patents become part of the **bankruptcy estate** and may be transmitted to creditors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix (Legal Analysis Framework)

Since this is a legal-IPR topic, the "derivations" are replaced with **structured analytical legal reasoning** — the same approach KTU Board examiners expect for 14-mark descriptive answers.

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: How Assignment Becomes Legally Valid

For an assignment to be **legally enforceable** under Indian IP law, the following sequential test must be satisfied:

### Step 1: Existence of an Assignable Right
- The assignor must be the **lawful owner** of the IP at the time of assignment.
- The IP must be a **legally recognized right** (registered or arising from statute).

### Step 2: Capacity of the Assignor
- The assignor must be **legally competent** to enter into a contract.
- In the case of minors or persons of unsound mind, the assignment is **voidable**.

### Step 3: Written Instrument
- A **written agreement** is mandatory.
- For copyright (Section 19(1)) and patents (Section 44), this is a **statutory requirement**.
- Oral assignments are **invalid** in IP law.

### Step 4: Signature of the Assignor
- The instrument must be **signed by the assignor** or their duly authorized agent.
- For copyright, this is mandatory under Section 19(1).

### Step 5: Specificity of Rights Transferred
The agreement must clearly state:
- The **specific rights** being assigned (e.g., right to make, use, sell, license).
- The **duration** of the assignment.
- The **territory** (e.g., India, worldwide, specific countries).
- The **consideration** (royalty, lump sum, etc.).

### Step 6: Consideration
- A lawful, valuable consideration must be present.
- Inadequate consideration does not void the assignment, but may invite judicial scrutiny.

### Step 7: Registration (where required)
- **Patents Act, Section 69**: Registration of assignment is **compulsory** to be valid against third parties.
- **Trademarks Act, Section 44**: The Registrar must be notified; failure may affect enforceability.
- **Copyright Act**: There is no compulsory registration of assignment, but the Copyright Board may be informed.
- **Designs Act, Section 31**: Registration is mandatory.

### Step 8: Recording in the Statutory Register
- The assignment is recorded in the **Register of Assignments/Patents/Trademarks** maintained by the Controller / Registrar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight**: Even if all other steps are followed, an unregistered assignment is **not invalid between the parties** (i.e., the assignor and assignee) but is **ineffective against third parties** who have no notice of the assignment. This is the **Section 69 trap** that loses marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Framework: How Transmission Operates

Transmission operates **automatically by operation of law**. The analytical sequence is:

### Step 1: Triggering Event
A triggering event must occur:
- **Death** of the IP owner → Succession opens
- **Insolvency** → IP becomes part of the bankruptcy estate
- **Merger / Am amalgamation** → IP vests in the surviving entity
- **Execution of a Will** → Testator bequeaths the IP

### Step 2: Application of Relevant Law
- **Indian Succession Act, 1925** governs inheritance.
- **Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016** governs insolvency transmission.
- **Companies Act, 2013, Sections 230–232** governs mergers.
- **Indian Contract Act, 1872** applies to testamentary capacity.

### Step 3: Identification of Successor / Transmittee
- The legal heir, administrator, executor, or successor company is identified.
- Documentary evidence (death certificate, succession certificate, court order, merger scheme) is required.

### Step 4: Notification to the Registrar / Controller
- The transmittee must notify the Registrar of Trademarks or the Controller of Patents of the transmission.
- **Section 45 of Trademarks Act** and **Section 45 of Patents Act** are relevant.

### Step 5: Recording in the Statutory Register
- Once recorded, the transmittee stands in the **shoes of the original proprietor** and enjoys the same rights, including the right to further assign, license, or sue for infringement.

## 3.3 Legal Distinction: Assignment vs. License (Detailed Analysis)

This is the **single most important distinction** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE answers.

| Parameter | Assignment | License |
|---|---|---|
| **Transfer of Ownership** | Yes — assignee becomes new owner | No — licensor remains owner |
| **Right to Sue for Infringement** | Yes — assignee can sue | Generally no (unless exclusive licensee) |
| **Right to Further Transfer** | Yes — assignee can assign to third party | No (or limited by license terms) |
| **Consideration** | One-time or staggered payment | Usually royalty-based |
| **Term** | Usually permanent (or as specified) | Limited (term-bound) |
| **Revocability** | Generally irrevocable | Revocable / non-revocable as per terms |
| **Statutory Reference** | Patents Sec. 44, TM Sec. 37, Copyright Sec. 19 | Patents Sec. 70–71, TM Sec. 49–50, Copyright Sec. 30 |
| **Registration** | Mandatory (for enforceability against third parties) | Optional (depends on statute) |

## 3.4 Case Law Spotlight (KTU Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak (2008)** — The Supreme Court held that the **Copyright Registrar has the power** to receive and act upon rectification applications regarding the Register of Copyrights, including the correction of assignment entries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sundarajan K. (Dr.) v. Indian Statistical Institute** — Demonstrated the doctrine of **equitable assignment** in copyright, where failure to register the assignment was held to be a procedural lapse, not a substantive defect, where the assignee was in possession.

> [!NOTE]
> **Parker & Son (Reading) Ltd. v. Parker (1969)** — A landmark English precedent often cited in KTU answers for the principle that **trademarks cannot be assigned in gross** (i.e., separated from the goodwill of the business) — the assignment must carry the goodwill.

## 3.5 Common Procedural Errors in KTU Answers

> [!WARNING]
> - Failing to mention that assignment of trademark **must include goodwill** under Section 39.
> - Conflating **transmission of patent** (Sec. 45) with **assignment of patent** (Sec. 44).
> - Forgetting that **future works/inventions** can be assigned under Section 19A (Copyright) and Section 44 (Patents).
> - Not specifying **territorial limits and duration** in assignment (mandatory under Section 19 of Copyright Act).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Modes of Transfer of IP Rights

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Intellectual Property Rights of Owner] --> B{How can rights be transferred?}
    B --> C[Assignment]
    B --> D[Transmission]
    B --> E[Licensing]
    C --> C1[Voluntary Contract]
    C --> C2[Complete or Partial Transfer of Ownership]
    C --> C3[Written Instrument Required]
    C --> C4[Registration Mandatory for Enforceability]
    C --> C5[Patents Act Sec 44, TM Act Sec 37, Copyright Act Sec 19, Designs Act Sec 30]
    D --> D1[By Operation of Law]
    D --> D2[Trigger: Death, Insolvency, Merger, Will]
    D --> D3[Automatic Vesting in Successor]
    D --> D4[Notification to Registrar Required]
    D --> D5[Patents Act Sec 45, TM Act Sec 45]
    E --> E1[Voluntary Permission to Use]
    E --> E2[Ownership Remains with Licensor]
    E --> E3[Exclusive or Non-Exclusive]
    E --> E4[Royalties Paid by Licensee]
    E --> E5[Copyright Act Sec 30, Patents Act Sec 70, TM Act Sec 49]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Assignment vs Transmission vs License

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ASSIGN[ASSIGNMENT]
        A1[Assignor] -->|Contract| A2[Assignee]
        A1 -.->|No More Owner| A1
        A2 -->|Becomes New Owner| A2
    end
    subgraph TRANSMIT[TRANSMISSION]
        T1[Original Owner] -->|Death / Insolvency / Merger| T2[Successor / Heir]
        T1 -.->|Automatic| T1
        T2 -->|Same Rights| T2
    end
    subgraph LICEN[LICENSING]
        L1[Licensor] -->|Permission to Use| L2[Licensee]
        L1 -->|Retains Ownership| L1
        L2 -->|Pays Royalty| L2
    end
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Stages of a Valid Assignment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Owner has valid IP] --> S2[Step 2: Drafting of Assignment Deed]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Mentioning Rights, Duration, Territory, Consideration]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Signature of Assignor]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Execution of the Deed]
    S5 --> S6{Is Registration Required?}
    S6 -->|Yes, Patents| S7[Register with Controller of Patents Sec 69]
    S6 -->|Yes, Trademarks| S8[Register with Registrar of TM Sec 44]
    S6 -->|Yes, Designs| S9[Register with Controller of Designs Sec 31]
    S6 -->|No, Copyright| S10[Record with Copyright Board]
    S7 --> S11[Valid Assignment]
    S8 --> S11
    S9 --> S11
    S10 --> S11
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Legal Framework Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IP[IP Owner] --> OWNS{Owns which IP?}
    OWNS -->|Patent| PA[Patents Act 1970]
    OWNS -->|Trademark| TA[Trademarks Act 1999]
    OWNS -->|Copyright| CA[Copyright Act 1957]
    OWNS -->|Design| DA[Designs Act 2000]
    PA --> PASEC[Sec 44 Assignment / Sec 45 Transmission]
    TA --> TASEC[Sec 37 39 Assignment / Sec 45 Transmission]
    CA --> CASEC[Sec 19 Assignment / Sec 21 Relinquishment]
    DA --> DASEC[Sec 30 Assignment / Sec 32 Transmission]
    PASEC --> REG[Register with Authority]
    TASEC --> REG
    CASEC --> REG
    DASEC --> REG
    REG --> NEWOWN[New Owner / Transmittee]
    NEWOWN --> ENF[Right to Enforce and Further Transfer]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Distinguish between assignment and transmission of intellectual property rights.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Assignment** is the **voluntary transfer** of IP rights by a contract between the assignor and assignee, requiring a written instrument, consideration, and registration. Example: Inventor A sells his patent to Company B via an assignment deed.
2. **Transmission** is the **involuntary / automatic transfer** of IP rights by operation of law upon death, insolvency, merger, or execution of a will. Example: On the death of the trademark owner, the rights pass to legal heirs under succession law.
3. **Key Difference**: Assignment requires consent and a contract; transmission occurs automatically by law.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Is it mandatory to register an assignment of a patent? Cite the relevant section.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Yes, registration of patent assignment is mandatory under **Section 69 of the Patents Act, 1970**. An unregistered assignment is valid between the parties but **ineffective against third parties** who have no notice of the assignment. Registration ensures the assignee gets the full statutory protection of being recognized as the patent holder for enforcement and licensing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **legal provisions for assignment and transmission of patents** under the Patents Act, 1970. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **differences between assignment and licensing** of intellectual property. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assignment of Patents**

1. **Statutory Basis**:
   - **Section 6** of the Patents Act allows the Court to order assignment of a patent in infringement suits.
   - **Section 44** deals with the form and register of assignments.
   - **Section 68** mandates the maintenance of the Register of Assignments at the Patent Office.
   - **Section 69** makes registration of assignment compulsory to be effective against third parties.
   - **Section 70** provides for rectification of the register.

2. **Conditions for Valid Assignment of Patent**:
   - The patent must be in **existence** (or be a future patent application) at the time of assignment.
   - Assignment must be by a **written instrument** signed by the assignor.
   - The instrument must specify the **scope of rights** assigned (e.g., manufacture, sale, import).
   - **Consideration** must be present.
   - The assignment must be **registered with the Controller of Patents** (Section 69) for enforceability against third parties.
   - **Co-owners** of patents may assign their share subject to the rights of other co-owners (Section 78).

3. **Transmission of Patents**:
   - Occurs on **death, insolvency, or merger** of the patent proprietor.
   - Governed by the **Indian Succession Act, 1925** (for death) and the **Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016** (for insolvency).
   - The **Section 45** of the Patents Act requires the **transmittee to apply to the Controller** for the registration of transmission in the register.
   - The successor enjoys the same rights as the original proprietor, including the right to sue for infringement and to further assign the patent.

4. **Assignment of Patent Applications**:
   - A **patent application can be assigned** even before the patent is granted (Section 6, Rule 10).
   - The application is transferred to the assignee, who prosecutes the application in his own name.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Differences between Assignment and Licensing of IP**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Assignment | Licensing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nature | Permanent transfer of ownership | Permission to use, ownership retained |
| 2 | Right to sue | Assignee can sue in his own name | Generally only licensor can sue; exclusive licensee may sue |
| 3 | Consideration | Lump sum or structured payment | Royalty (recurring) |
| 4 | Duration | Permanent or as specified | Time-bound |
| 5 | Right to sub-license | Yes, assignee may further transfer | Generally no, unless expressly permitted |
| 6 | Revocability | Generally irrevocable | May be revocable |
| 7 | Statutory Reference (Patents) | Sec. 44 | Sec. 70–71 |
| 8 | Statutory Reference (Copyright) | Sec. 19 | Sec. 30 |
| 9 | Statutory Reference (Trademark) | Sec. 37, 39 | Sec. 49–50 |
| 10 | Goods in Trademark | Transfers with goodwill | Goodwill not transferred |

**Conclusion**: While both assignment and licensing are contractual, assignment is a complete transfer of rights, while licensing is a limited grant to use. For KTU 2024, examiners expect students to highlight these distinctions with statutory backing.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **legal framework for assignment and transmission of trademarks** under the Trademarks Act, 1999. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically examine the role of **goodwill in the assignment of trademarks** with relevant case law. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assignment of Trademarks (Statutory Framework)**

1. **Section 37** of the Trademarks Act, 1999 is the **umbrella provision** that allows a registered trademark or a trademark application to be assigned.
2. **Section 38** provides for the **assignment of associated trademarks** — i.e., trademarks used on related goods/services can be assigned together.
3. **Section 39** is the **most critical** provision. It states:
   - A trademark is assignable **with or without the goodwill of the business**.
   - However, when assigned **with goodwill**, the assignee can use the trademark for all goods/services connected to the business.
   - When assigned **without goodwill**, the use is limited to the specific goods/services for which it was registered (avoiding deception).
4. **Section 40** — An **application for registration of a trademark** can also be assigned.
5. **Section 41** — The assignment must be in **writing**, with signatures, and where goodwill is involved, the goodwill must be expressly mentioned.
6. **Section 44** — Registration of assignment with the Registrar of Trademarks is **compulsory** to be valid.

**Transmission of Trademarks**

1. **Section 45** of the Trademarks Act deals with **transmission** of trademarks upon:
   - **Death** of the registered proprietor,
   - **Insolvency** of the proprietor,
   - **Merger / Amalgamation** of the company holding the mark.
2. The **transmittee** must apply to the Registrar to record the transmission.
3. The transmittee enjoys the **same rights** as the original proprietor.

**Distinctive Features of Trademark Assignment**
- The principle of **territoriality** applies — assignment is valid only in the territory covered.
- A **trademark cannot be assigned in gross** (i.e., separated from the goodwill of the business, except in special cases).
- The Registrar can refuse to register an assignment that is **likely to deceive the public**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Role of Goodwill in Trademark Assignment**

**Concept of Goodwill**:
- **Goodwill** is the **attractive force** that brings in custom. It is the established reputation of a business and the name, mark, or symbol associated with it.
- In trademark law, goodwill is **inseparably linked** to the mark.

**Legal Position**:
1. **Section 39 of the Trademarks Act, 1999** codifies the principle that a trademark is **assignable along with the goodwill** of the business concerned in such goods/services.
2. The rationale is to **prevent deception of the public**. If goodwill does not travel with the mark, the public may be misled into thinking the goods come from the same source.

**Case Law — Parker & Son (Reading) Ltd. v. Parker (1969) (English Precedent)**
- Held that a trademark is an **integral part of the goodwill** of a business and cannot be assigned in gross.
- A trademark assigned without the business attached to it creates a **false suggestion of connection** and is invalid.

**Honest Concurrent Use Exception**:
- Section 39(1) provides that a trademark can be assigned **without goodwill** if the assignment is accompanied by an honest concurrent use of the trademark or in special circumstances.

**Indian Application**:
- In **South India Beverages Pvt. Ltd. v. General Bottling Co. (2004)**, the Court emphasized that the assignment of trademark without goodwill was a ground for **rectification** if it created confusion.

**Critical Evaluation**:
- The goodwill doctrine balances **two competing interests**:
  1. The **proprietor's right** to monetize the trademark.
  2. The **consumer's right** not to be deceived.
- Modern Indian law permits assignment without goodwill in **specific categories of goods** registered, but the Registrar's scrutiny is strict.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Trap**: Students often write that a trademark is **freely assignable without goodwill** — this is **wrong**. The general rule is that goodwill travels with the mark; assignment without goodwill is an exception. Writing the opposite will cost you at least **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in PESFT757 Assignment & Transmission Answers**:
> 1. **Failing to cite specific sections** (e.g., Section 44 of Patents Act, Section 39 of TM Act, Section 19 of Copyright Act). Examiners expect statutory references.
> 2. **Confusing transmission with assignment** — using them interchangeably in answers.
> 3. **Ignoring the role of goodwill** in trademark assignment.
> 4. **Writing only definitions** without analysis of the legal procedure (Step-by-Step Framework in Section 3 is a model).
> 5. **Not distinguishing** between assignment of patent **application** vs assignment of granted **patent**.
> 6. **Forgetting registration requirements** under Section 69 (Patents) and Section 44 (Trademarks).
> 7. **Missing case laws** — at least 1 case law per 14-mark answer carries 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Assignment and Transmission (PESFT757 Module 4)**

- ✅ **Assignment** = Voluntary + Contractual + Permanent (or specified duration) transfer of ownership.
- ✅ **Transmission** = Involuntary + Automatic (by law) — death, insolvency, merger, will.
- ✅ **Licensing** = Permission to use; ownership remains with licensor.
- ✅ **Patents Act** — Sections 6, 44, 68, 69, 70, 78 govern assignment/transmission.
- ✅ **Trademarks Act** — Sections 37, 38, 39, 40, 41, 44, 45 govern assignment/transmission.
- ✅ **Copyright Act** — Sections 18, 19, 19A, 30, 21 govern assignment/licensing.
- ✅ **Designs Act** — Sections 30, 31, 32 govern assignment/transmission.
- ✅ **Section 19(1) Copyright Act** — Assignment must be **in writing, signed, specify rights, duration, and territory**.
- ✅ **Section 39 Trademarks Act** — Trademark assignable **with goodwill** (general rule).
- ✅ **Section 69 Patents Act** — Registration of assignment **mandatory** for enforceability against third parties.
- ✅ **Parker v. Parker (1969)** — Trademark cannot be assigned in gross (without goodwill).
- ✅ **Section 19A** — Future copyright works can be assigned.
- ✅ **Section 45 Patents / TM Act** — Transmission requires **application to the Registrar/Controller** for recording.
- ✅ **Distinction table** between assignment, transmission, and licensing is a **high-yield KTU 2024 answer component**.
- ✅ **Goodwill** is the most important concept in trademark assignment.
- ✅ **Co-ownership** of patents is governed by Section 78.
- ✅ **Future inventions** can be assigned under Section 44 of the Patents Act.

> **End of Module 4 — Assignment and Transmission Notes (PESFT757, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Appeal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT757 - INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Appeal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)**, an **Appeal** is a legal procedural remedy by which an aggrieved party (applicant, opponent, or any person affected by an order) challenges a decision, direction, or order passed by a quasi-judicial or administrative authority (such as the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks**, or the **Registrar of Copyrights / Trade Marks / Geographical Indications**) before a higher judicial or quasi-judicial forum empowered to review, modify, or reverse such decision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Basis of Appeals in Indian IP Law**
> Appeals are **statutorily defined** under specific sections of the relevant IP legislation:
> - **Patents Act, 1970** — Sections 117A to 117D
> - **Trade Marks Act, 1999** — Sections 83 to 100
> - **Copyright Act, 1957** — Sections 72 to 75
> - **Designs Act, 2000** — Section 31
> - **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999** — Sections 36 to 39

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Courtroom Ladder"**
> Imagine you applied for a patent for your invention, and the **Patent Officer** (a junior examiner) rejects it, saying your idea is not novel. You strongly believe the officer made a mistake. You cannot argue forever with the same officer — the law gives you a **"ladder to climb"**. That ladder is called an **Appeal**. You move to a **higher, more experienced authority** (earlier the **IPAB**, now the **High Court / Commercial Court**) and ask them to re-examine the decision. If the higher authority agrees with you, the rejection is reversed; if not, your last resort is the **Supreme Court of India**.
> 
> In simple words: **Appeal = Asking a senior authority to overturn an unfavourable order of a junior authority.**

## 1.3 Who Can File an Appeal? (Aggrieved Person)

The following categories of persons are generally entitled to file an appeal under various IP statutes:

| S.No | Person Entitled to Appeal | Source |
|------|---------------------------|--------|
| 1 | The **applicant** for IP registration | All IP Acts |
| 2 | Any person **opposing** the registration | All IP Acts |
| 3 | Any person **aggrieved** by the order | Trade Marks Act, Copyright Act |
| 4 | The **Central Government** (in certain matters) | Patents Act, GI Act |

## 1.4 Key Statutory Forums (Pre and Post IPAB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical 2021 Update — Abolition of IPAB**
> The **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** established in 2003 under Section 84 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999 was **abolished on 4th April 2021** by the *Tribunals Reforms (Rationalisation and Conditions of Service) Ordinance, 2021*. Post-abolition, the appellate jurisdiction of IPAB has been transferred to the **Commercial Courts** (where pecuniary jurisdiction is met) and the **High Courts** under Articles 226/227 of the Constitution.

| Period | Appellate Forum |
|--------|-----------------|
| **2003 – 4 April 2021** | Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB), Chennai |
| **Post 5 April 2021 onwards** | Commercial Court / High Court (jurisdiction depends on subject-matter and pecuniary value) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Appeals in Indian IP Law

Indian IP law provides a **three-tier appellate structure** for most IP disputes:

$$\text{Tier 1: Lower Authority} \rightarrow \text{Tier 2: First Appellate Authority} \rightarrow \text{Tier 3: Supreme Court of India}$$

### Tier 1: Original (Lower) Authority
- **Controller of Patents** (Patents Act, 1970)
- **Registrar of Trade Marks** (Trade Marks Act, 1999)
- **Registrar of Copyrights** (Copyright Act, 1957)
- **Controller of Designs** (Designs Act, 2000)
- **Registrar of Geographical Indications** (GI Act, 1999)

### Tier 2: First Appellate Authority
- **Until April 2021:** Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)
- **Post April 2021:** Commercial Court / High Court

### Tier 3: Apex Forum
- **Supreme Court of India** — under **Article 136** of the Constitution (Special Leave Petition) or under the relevant statutory provisions of each IP Act.

## 2.2 Grounds on Which an Appeal May Be Filed

An appeal can be filed on the following substantive grounds:

1. **Question of Law** — the lower authority misinterpreted or misapplied a legal provision.
2. **Question of Fact** — the lower authority's findings of fact are erroneous.
3. **Excess of Jurisdiction** — the lower authority acted beyond its statutory powers.
4. **Error Apparent on the Face of the Record** — a clear mistake in the order.
5. **Violation of Principles of Natural Justice** — denial of fair hearing, bias, or non-supply of relevant documents.
6. **Disproportionate / Unreasonable Order** — the order shocks the conscience of the court.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Appellate authorities are generally **not bound by technical rules of evidence** (Section 92 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 applies). They function as **courts of justice** and not merely courts of law.

## 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Appeal Provisions Across IP Statutes

| Statute | Section | Time Limit for Appeal | Appellate Forum (Pre-2021) | Appellate Forum (Post-2021) | Court Fees & Stamp |
|---------|---------|----------------------|---------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| **Patents Act, 1970** | **117A** | **3 months** from date of decision (condonable up to 6 months under **117A(2)**) | IPAB | High Court / Commercial Court | As per Court Fees Act of State |
| **Trade Marks Act, 1999** | **91** | **3 months** from date of order (condonable for further 1 month under **91(2)**) | IPAB | High Court / Commercial Court | As per Court Fees Act |
| **Copyright Act, 1957** | **72** | **3 months** from date of order (condonable for further 1 month) | High Court | High Court | As per Court Fees Act |
| **Designs Act, 2000** | **31** | **1 month** from date of order (condonable for further 1 month) | High Court / IPAB | High Court | As per Court Fees Act |
| **GI Act, 1999** | **36** | **3 months** from date of order | IPAB | High Court | As per Court Fees Act |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Note on Limitation:** All IP statutes provide a **limitation period of 3 months** (1 month for Designs) for filing appeals. The appellate authority has discretion to admit appeals after the limitation period if sufficient cause is shown — but this discretion must be exercised within the maximum additional time prescribed.

## 2.4 Powers of the Appellate Authority

The appellate forum, whether IPAB or High Court, possesses the following powers:

1. **Power to call for records** from the lower authority.
2. **Power to issue interim orders** (stay, injunction, etc.) during pendency of appeal.
3. **Power to take additional evidence** under Order XLI Rule 27 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
4. **Power to confirm, modify, or set aside** the order of the lower authority.
5. **Power to remand** the matter back to the lower authority for fresh consideration.
6. **Power to award costs** of the appeal.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

The appeal mechanism in IP law is **critically important** for:

- **Pharmaceutical & Biotech industries** — to challenge refusal of patent applications for drugs/biologics.
- **Software & IT firms** — to defend trademark oppositions and patent claims.
- **Automobile & Manufacturing companies** — to protect design patents from infringement disputes.
- **Creative industries (film, music, publishing)** — to protect copyrights.
- **Agricultural & GI-product producers** (e.g., Darjeeling Tea, Basmati Rice) — to defend GI registrations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** Most patent appeals in India pertain to **pharmaceutical patents**, as the cost of R&D in drug development is enormous, and any erroneous refusal by the Patent Office can have commercial implications running into **billions of dollars**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Framework & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure for Filing an IP Appeal

Since this is a **legal-procedural topic**, the "derivation" here is a structured **step-by-step analytical matrix** of the appellate procedure:

### Step 1: Receipt of Impugned Order
The aggrieved party first receives a **written, reasoned order** from the lower authority (Controller / Registrar). The date of this order is the **starting point of limitation**.

> **Legal Principle:** *Audi Alteram Partem* (hear the other side) — The lower authority must have given reasonable opportunity of being heard before passing the order; otherwise, the order is *void ab initio*.

### Step 2: Determination of Limitation Period
Compute the statutory limitation from the date of the order:

$$\text{Limitation} = \text{Date of Order} + \text{Statutory Period}$$

- For Patents, Trade Marks, Copyright, GI → **3 months**
- For Designs → **1 month**

> **Example Calculation (Patents Act):** If the Controller passes an order on **15th January**, the appeal must be filed by **15th April** (3 months). If filed on **20th April**, the applicant must file a **condonation of delay application** under **Section 117A(2)** stating sufficient cause. The maximum delay that can be condoned is **6 months from the date of order** (i.e., appeal can be filed at the latest by **15th July**).

### Step 3: Drafting of Memorandum of Appeal
The appeal must be in the form of a **Memorandum of Appeal** containing:

- **Parties:** Name, description, and address of the appellant and respondent.
- **Impugned Order:** Date, nature, and particulars of the order appealed against.
- **Grounds of Appeal:** Each ground must be set out distinctly and concisely, without any argument or narrative.
- **Prayers / Reliefs Sought:** Specific reliefs such as setting aside, modification, remand, etc.
- **Verification:** Signed and verified by the appellant or his authorised agent.

### Step 4: Filing and Fee Payment
- File the appeal in the prescribed form (**Form 1** for most IP Acts).
- Pay the **prescribed court fees** as per the Court Fees Act of the relevant state.
- Submit **certified copies** of the impugned order.

### Step 5: Service of Notice on Respondent
The appellate authority issues **notice of the appeal** to the respondent (the lower authority or the opposing party), calling for their **reply / counter-affidavit** within the prescribed time (usually 30–45 days).

### Step 6: Interim Stay Application
The appellant may file an **application for stay** of the impugned order, requesting the appellate authority to suspend the operation of the order pending disposal of the appeal. The appellate authority considers:

- **Prima facie case** in favour of the appellant.
- **Balance of convenience** between the parties.
- **Irreparable injury** that would be caused if stay is refused.

> **Legal Maxim:** *"Justice delayed is justice denied"* — Hence, stay applications are given priority and disposed of on first hearing.

### Step 7: Final Hearing
- The appellate authority hears arguments from both sides.
- It may call for **additional evidence** under Order XLI Rule 27 CPC if it deems fit.
- The lower authority's record is perused.

### Step 8: Pronouncement of Final Order
The appellate authority passes a final order, which may:

$$\text{Order} = \begin{cases} \text{Confirm} \rightarrow \text{Appeal dismissed} \\ \text{Modify} \rightarrow \text{Partially allow appeal} \\ \text{Set Aside} \rightarrow \text{Appeal allowed in full} \\ \text{Remand} \rightarrow \text{Send back to lower authority} \end{cases}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Appeal Provisions

| Parameter | Patents Act | Trade Marks Act | Copyright Act | Designs Act | GI Act |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|---------------|-------------|--------|
| **Section** | 117A | 91 | 72 | 31 | 36 |
| **Time Limit** | 3 months | 3 months | 3 months | 1 month | 3 months |
| **Maximum Condonation** | 6 months | 4 months | 4 months | 2 months | 6 months |
| **Original Authority** | Controller of Patents | Registrar of TM | Registrar of Copyrights | Controller of Designs | Registrar of GI |
| **Pre-2021 Forum** | IPAB | IPAB | High Court | IPAB / High Court | IPAB |
| **Post-2021 Forum** | HC / Comm. Court | HC / Comm. Court | High Court | High Court | High Court |
| **Second Appeal** | Art. 136 SC | Art. 136 SC | Art. 136 SC | Art. 136 SC | Art. 136 SC |
