# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Appeal" in the context of Intellectual Property Rights. Mention the statutory provision governing appeals under the Patents Act, 1970.**

**Model Answer:**
An appeal is a legal remedy available to an aggrieved party to challenge an adverse order passed by a lower quasi-judicial or administrative authority before a higher judicial forum, seeking modification, reversal, or setting aside of such order.

Under the **Patents Act, 1970**, appeals are governed by **Sections 117A to 117D**. Specifically, **Section 117A** provides that any person aggrieved by an order or decision of the Controller of Patents may file an appeal to the **High Court (or Commercial Court post-2021)** within a period of **three months** from the date of the order, extendable by a further period on sufficient cause being shown, subject to a maximum of six months.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)? Why was it abolished?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** was a quasi-judicial body constituted under **Section 84 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999**, with its headquarters in Chennai. It was established in **2003** to hear appeals against orders of the Registrar of Trade Marks, and subsequently given jurisdiction over patents, designs, geographical indications, and copyright matters.

The IPAB was **abolished on 4th April 2021** by the **Tribunals Reforms (Rationalisation and Conditions of Service) Ordinance, 2021**, citing reasons of **reducing judicial burden, eliminating multiplicity of forums, and improving efficiency**. Post-abolition, the appellate jurisdiction of IPAB stands transferred to the **Commercial Courts and High Courts** under Articles 226/227 of the Constitution.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Modeled on KTU ESE)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]
**(a)** Discuss in detail the provisions relating to appeals under the **Patents Act, 1970**. Explain the **limitation period**, **grounds of appeal**, and the **powers of the appellate authority**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyse the **abolition of the Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** in April 2021 and its impact on the Indian IP litigation landscape. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a) — Appeals under the Patents Act, 1970:**

**1. Statutory Provision:** Appeals under the Patents Act, 1970 are governed by **Sections 117A to 117D**. Section 117A is the operative provision, while Sections 117B, 117C, and 117D deal with procedural and consequential matters.

**2. Who May Appeal:** Any person aggrieved by an order or decision of the Controller of Patents may file an appeal under Section 117A. The term "person aggrieved" has been interpreted liberally by the Supreme Court to include any person who has suffered a legal grievance or whose interests are adversely affected by the order.

**3. Limitation Period:** As per Section 117A(1), the appeal must be filed within **three months** from the date of the impugned order. Under Section 117A(2), the appellate authority may admit an appeal filed beyond three months if sufficient cause is shown, but the maximum period of condonation cannot exceed **six months** from the date of order.

**4. Appellate Forum:** Prior to April 2021, the appeal lay to the **IPAB**. Post-abolition of IPAB, the appeal now lies to the **High Court** exercising jurisdiction over the area where the applicant resides or has his principal place of business, or where the Controller's order was passed.

**5. Grounds of Appeal:** The appellant may challenge the Controller's order on grounds including:
   - Error of law in interpreting patentability criteria (novelty, inventive step, industrial application).
   - Erroneous findings of fact.
   - Non-consideration of relevant documents.
   - Violation of principles of natural justice.
   - Improper exercise of discretion.

**6. Powers of the Appellate Authority:**
   - To **confirm, modify, or set aside** the Controller's order.
   - To **remand** the matter for fresh consideration.
   - To **take additional evidence** under Order XLI Rule 27 of CPC.
   - To **pass interim orders** of stay or injunction.
   - To **award costs** of the proceedings.

**7. Second Appeal:** Any party dissatisfied with the order of the High Court may approach the **Supreme Court of India** under **Article 136** of the Constitution by way of a Special Leave Petition.

---

**Model Answer for (b) — Critical Analysis of IPAB Abolition:**

**1. Background:** The IPAB was established in 2003 under Section 84 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. It had jurisdiction over appeals and rectification matters relating to trademarks, patents, designs, GI, and copyright.

**2. Reasons for Abolition:**
   - **Backlog of cases:** Despite years of operation, IPAB had a massive case backlog, with some matters pending for over a decade.
   - **Vacancies in IPAB:** Several posts of Technical and Judicial Members remained vacant for prolonged periods, crippling its functioning.
   - **Constitutional mandate:** The Supreme Court in *Rohitashwa Kumar Modi v. Prem Prakash* (2014) and *Srinivasa Ramanujam v. Bar Council* (2014) emphasised the need for rationalisation of tribunals.
   - **Tribunals Reforms Ordinance 2021:** The Government abolished IPAB as part of a larger exercise to abolish multiple tribunals (Article 323A and 323B) and merge their functions with the High Courts.

**3. Impact on IP Litigation Landscape:**
   - **Increased burden on High Courts:** The abolition transferred a vast jurisdiction to High Courts, which are already overburdened.
   - **Loss of technical expertise:** IPAB had both judicial and technical members (patent examiners/scientists) — High Courts lack such domain-specific technical expertise.
   - **Delay in resolution:** Procedural complexity in High Courts (e.g., long listing dates) may increase the time for disposal of IP appeals.
   - **Increase in litigation costs:** Filing appeals in High Courts is more expensive than filing in IPAB.
   - **Positive impact:** Decisions of High Courts carry greater precedent value, and the merger is expected to reduce forum shopping and conflicting orders.

**4. Conclusion:** The abolition of IPAB is a **double-edged sword** — while it consolidates IP jurisdiction in constitutional courts, it has raised concerns about delay and loss of technical expertise. The establishment of dedicated **IP Commercial Courts** at the district level and the appointment of technical advisors in High Courts are possible remedies to address these concerns.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Alternative Choice]
**(a)** Explain the **procedure for filing an appeal** under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999**. State the relevant sections, limitation period, and the appellate authority. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **appeal provisions** under the **Patents Act, 1970 and the Copyright Act, 1957**. Highlight at least four points of difference. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a) — Procedure for Filing Appeal under Trade Marks Act, 1999:**

**1. Relevant Sections:** Appeals under the Trade Marks Act, 1999 are governed by **Sections 91 to 100** of the Act.

**2. Who May Appeal (Section 91):** Any person aggrieved by an order or decision of the Registrar of Trade Marks may file an appeal.

**3. Limitation Period:** The appeal must be filed within **three months** from the date of the order, with a maximum condonation period of **four months** from the date of order (Section 91(2)).

**4. Appellate Forum:** Pre-2021 → IPAB. Post-2021 → Commercial Court / High Court.

**5. Procedure:**
   - **Step 1:** Obtain certified copy of the impugned order from the Registry of Trade Marks.
   - **Step 2:** Draft a Memorandum of Appeal in Form 1, setting out parties, the impugned order, grounds of appeal, and the reliefs sought.
   - **Step 3:** Pay the prescribed court fees as per the Court Fees Act of the relevant State.
   - **Step 4:** File the appeal with the High Court / Commercial Court.
   - **Step 5:** Apply for **stay of the impugned order**, citing the three-pronged test (prima facie case, balance of convenience, irreparable injury).
   - **Step 6:** Serve notice on the Registrar and the contesting opposite party.
   - **Step 7:** The opposite party files counter-affidavit; the appellant files rejoinder.
   - **Step 8:** Final hearing; appellate authority passes final order.

**6. Finality:** Orders of the High Court are appealable to the Supreme Court under **Article 136** of the Constitution.

---

**Model Answer for (b) — Comparison of Appeal Provisions:**

| S.No | Parameter | Patents Act, 1970 | Copyright Act, 1957 |
|------|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Relevant Section** | Section 117A | Section 72 |
| 2 | **Original Authority** | Controller of Patents | Registrar of Copyrights / Copyright Board |
| 3 | **Limitation Period** | 3 months | 3 months |
| 4 | **Maximum Condonation** | 6 months | 4 months |
| 5 | **Appellate Forum (Pre-2021)** | IPAB | High Court (No IPAB jurisdiction) |
| 6 | **Appellate Forum (Post-2021)** | High Court / Commercial Court | High Court |
| 7 | **Nature of Disputes** | Technical (involves scientific expertise) | Cultural / Creative (literary, artistic, musical works) |
| 8 | **Special Feature** | Technical members assist in deciding patentability | Copyright Board of India constituted for disputes |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT cite "IPAB" as the current appellate forum.** IPAB was abolished in **April 2021**. Always mention the **High Court / Commercial Court** as the present forum.
> 2. Students often confuse the **limitation period for filing appeal** with the **maximum condonation period**. Remember: **3 months + 3 months = 6 months maximum** for Patents, while for Trade Marks it is **3 months + 1 month = 4 months maximum**.
> 3. **Do not write** that the Registrar of Copyrights is the original authority for ALL copyright matters — appeals under Section 72 lie against orders of the **Registrar of Copyrights**, but appeals against orders of the **Copyright Board** lie under **Section 73** directly to the High Court.
> 4. Avoid using the term "IPAB" without context; if you do, immediately clarify that it was abolished in 2021.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Appeal" in IPR**
> 
> ✅ **Definition:** Appeal is a statutory right of an aggrieved party to challenge an adverse order of a lower authority before a higher forum.
> 
> ✅ **Statutory Provisions (must remember):** Patents Act → **S.117A**; Trade Marks Act → **S.91**; Copyright Act → **S.72**; Designs Act → **S.31**; GI Act → **S.36**.
> 
> ✅ **Limitation Periods:** **3 months** (Patents, TM, Copyright, GI) and **1 month** (Designs).
> 
> ✅ **Maximum Condonation:** Patents & GI → 6 months; TM & Copyright → 4 months; Designs → 2 months.
> 
> ✅ **Original Authorities:** Controller (Patents/Designs), Registrar (TM/Copyrights/GI).
> 
> ✅ **Appellate Forum (Current):** **High Court / Commercial Court** (since April 2021).
> 
> ✅ **Abolition of IPAB:** **4th April 2021** by Tribunals Reforms Ordinance 2021.
> 
> ✅ **Powers of Appellate Authority:** Confirm, modify, set aside, remand, take additional evidence, grant stay.
> 
> ✅ **Final Forum:** **Supreme Court of India** under **Article 136** (Special Leave Petition).
> 
> ✅ **Procedure:** Drafting → Filing → Service of Notice → Counter-Affidavit → Stay Application → Final Hearing → Order.
> 
> ✅ **Three-Pronged Test for Stay:** Prima facie case + Balance of convenience + Irreparable injury.
> 
> ✅ **For Examinations:** Always mention the **current forum** (HC/Commercial Court) — outdated references to IPAB will cost marks.
